headline: KOBE BRYANT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The best high school basketball player in America plays in a Philadelphia suburb. This summer, he was so dazzling in venues around the country, from camp to tournament to camp, he zoomed right to the top of the recruiting gurus' charts. Kobe Bryant , the 6-7, do-it-all senior from Lower Merion High, could play in the city next year. Or he could play in just about any big city in America and two in Canada. According to his father, Joe Bryant, the La Salle assistant coach and former La Salle and 76ers player, Kobe is accepting no home visits from college coaches and visiting no college campuses. That makes it easy to deduce that next year, Kobe Bryant will play at La Salle or he will play in the NBA. "I think La Salle is in good shape," Joe Bryant said. "At the same time, he's been contacted by just about every big school." All the big boys have called - Duke, Kentucky, Michigan, North Carolina. "You name one," Joe Bryant said. "It's just amazing." It's all because Kobe Bryant is an amazing player with the rebounding ability of a power forward, the finesse of a small forward, the shooting range of an off guard and the ballhandling skill of a point guard. Kobe Bryant gets all those calls, but he is not biting. No visits by coaches. No visits to coaches. Kobe knows the La Salle coaches. His dad has been an assistant there for two years. The program has treaded water the last few years. However, it's no secret La Salle knows how to showcase great talents. But . . . "He's been working out with the Sixers," Joe Bryant said. "People tell me when you watch him playing with the pros, you can't tell the difference." But . . . Kobe Bryant just turned 17 at the end of August. He certainly looks like a future NBA player, maybe a star. His dad figures he'll be a point guard at that level. But . . . Kobe Bryant is just 17. "I just have to look at all the different options as a parent," Joe Bryant said. "It would be difficult for him to leave home right now. He wouldn't be in college that long in any case." Two years seems about the average stay for the college superstars anymore. The first four players taken in last June's NBA draft - Joe Smith, Antonio McDyess, Jerry Stackhouse and Rasheed Wallace - were college sophomores. The fifth selection was Kevin Garnett, a high school senior. "He's just taking it easy, looking forward to his high school season," Joe Bryant said of his son. "He's just going to wait (to decide). But Kobe wants to keep all his options open. We'll see." headline: BRYANT STILL DECIDING
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 There have been rumors of a Kobe Bryant press conference for weeks, a press conference to announce the Lower Merion High senior was committing to play basketball at La Salle. Well, there is no press conference - yet. Bryant, the 6-7 phenom generally considered the best high school basketball player in the country, is not ready to make a decision. His father, Joe Bryant, a La Salle assistant coach, said on Tuesday that Kobe will not be signing a national letter of intent during the early signing period, which began yesterday. It's not about La Salle. Although Joe Bryant won't say it publicly, wanting to keep his son's options open, it's clear that if Kobe Bryant goes to college, he will be going to La Salle. Kobe's decision is the same as it was weeks ago. Will he go to La Salle or the NBA? Joe Bryant doesn't want his son signing with La Salle and then opting for the NBA. He wants his son to be certain of his decision and then stick with it. To do otherwise, he said, wouldn't be fair to anybody. So, The Kobe Watch continues. CAOUETTE TO 'NOVA? T.J. Caouette, a 6-8 forward from Winthrop (Maine) High, will make his decision on a college known today. Most recruiting experts think Villanova is the favorite over Vanderbilt, Notre Dame and Maryland. But it's not a definite. Recruiting guru Bob Gibbons described Caouette as the "sleeper of the summer" after a dazzling performance at the Nike Camp. Caouette was a virtual unknown before his Nike performance. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Bryant will jump from preps to pros With designer sunglasses perched atop his head and an expensive blazer stretched across his shoulders, Kobe Bryant looked every bit like the multimillionaire NBA player he expects to become. But Bryant was standing inside the high school gymnasium where he played his final game less than three months ago as he announced Monday that he will become the sixth player in history to skip college and enter the NBA draft. "I know that I'll have to work extra hard and I know that it's a big step," he said at a news conference in the gym where he led Lower Merion High School to the state class AAAA title. "I can do it." Experts like high school recruiting guru Tom Konchalski and NBA draft specialist Don Leventhal have said that Bryant is ready for the next level without putting in any time in a college program. The 6-foot-6 Bryant, 17, is expected to be among the top 10 prospects in the June NBA draft. Leventhal, however, said that Bryant's decision could add to a disturbing trend. Last year, Kevin Garnett went directly to the NBA draft from Farragut Academy in Chicago and was picked fifth by the Minnesota Timberwolves. Garnett won a starting position midway through this season. "It is disappointing as a college basketball fan," said Leventhal, who publishes an annual draft report. "The better (Bryant) does, the more it will probably encourage other high school players to try the same thing." A strong voice against Bryant's turning pro has been Marty Blake, director of scouting for the NBA. "He's a very good high school basketball player - with the emphasis on high school," Blake said in an interview before the decision was made. "Garnett was an exception. He was 19 and he was 6-11. Kobe's just not ready yet." Bryant's decision drew a cool reaction from NBA commissioner David Stern, who said in an interview in Portland, Ore., that he believes it's better for players to go to college for a few years, but there's nothing the league can do legally to stop a teen-ager from going directly from high school to the NBA. Bryant was recognized as the national player of the year by Naismith, Gatorade, USA Today and Parade magazine. In selecting the pros, Bryant decided against playing for La Salle, where his father, former Philadelphia 76er Joe Bryant, is an assistant basketball coach. His father believes the move is a good one. "Kobe has the burning desire and the kid's got heart," the father said. " Plus, he's a very intelligent player. I don't think that a lot of people realize that." La Salle, Kentucky, North Carolina, Arkansas, Duke, Michigan and Villanova, were said to be among the college programs Bryant was considering. Kobe Bryant was the leading scorer in southeastern Pennsylvania history with 2,883 points. His senior year, Bryant averaged 31 points, 10 rebounds, five assists, five blocked shots and four steals a game. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 IT was inevitable. In a big-city schoolyard, a 9-year-old boy bouncing a basketball while surrounded by microphones and cameras was announcing his intention to bypass junior high school and declare his availability in the International Basketball Association draft. "I want to try my game in the pros," he said. "I averaged 32 points and 20 rebounds this season." "But that's in a Police Athletic League," a voice said. "Don't you need to develop your skills in high school, or at least junior high?" "What can I do there that I haven't done already," the kid said. "The I.B.A. scouts think I'm ready." "But you're only 4 feet 11 inches tall," a voice said. "What makes you think that you're ready?" "My father is 6-4; my mother is 6-2," the kid said. "The scouts like my genes. They project me at 7-8." The night of the draft, the Madrid Matadors selected the 9-year-old kid with the seventh choice that was obtained from the Knicks, who had acquired it earlier from the Athens Olympians. Madrid? Athens? The I.B.A.? Didn't you know? When the National Basketball Association expanded by adding a 28-team European Division in 2002 while promising another 28-team Asian-Australian Division in 2008, it changed its name to the International Basketball Association. But the night of the draft, the kid was asked another question. "If Kobe Bryant had trouble coming out of high school," a voice said, "what makes you think you'll make it?" "All I know is, Moses Malone made it," the kid replied. "He was before my time, but I'm told he was great." With a wave, the kid walked away with his agent, who stopped to talk to the notebooks and microphones. "We'll be asking for a $100 million signing bonus," the agent said. "Ever since I.B.A. Properties signed a new $10 billion deal with that new sneaker company, it's only fair that this fine young man gets his share. He's worked hard to get here. And he wants his parents to have a better life." That scenario, which may or may not turn out to be silly, is what 17-year-old Kobe Bryant has wrought. In declaring for the N.B.A. draft out of Lower Merion High School in Ardmore, Pa., the son of Joe (Jellybean) Bryant -- once a pro himself -- will join five others who entered the N.B.A. without a college basketball resume: Malone, Shawn Kemp, Kevin Garnett, Darryl Dawkins and Bill Willoughby. Of those five, Malone was by far the most productive, a three-time N.B.A. most valuable player with the Rockets and the 76ers and arguably the game's premier offensive rebounder. Although volatile, Kemp is an All-Star for the SuperSonics and Garnett might be an All-Star some day for the Timberwolves. Dawkins is remembered mostly for smashing glass backboards, Willoughby for bouncing around with six teams. Malone, out of Petersburg, Va., was the first, joining the Utah Stars, then in the American Basketball Association, in 1974. Willoughby, out of Dwight Morrow High in Englewood N.J., was drafted by the Hawks and signed a five-year $1.1 million contract, but over eight seasons he averaged only 6.0 points a game. At age 19, Garnett averaged 10.4 points and 6.3 rebounds, good numbers for an average of 28.7 minutes this season, but he's 6-11, as were Malone and Dawkins. Kemp is 6-10. Willoughby went 6-8. As a rookie, each of those five were taller and older than Bryant, who is only 6-6. Maybe he's still growing; David Robinson, now the Spurs' center, sprouted 6 inches while at the Naval Academy. But if Bryant doesn't grow, he better be a great shooter. And at 17, he might grow up too soon in the N.B.A. life style. Marty Blake, the N.B.A.'s draft guru, has "no idea" how high Bryant will go in the draft. But no matter when he is chosen, the situation will work against him. If Bryant is taken quickly, he will probably be on a bad team. If he is taken late in the first round, he should be on a good team but won't get much playing time. That same dilemma would also confront Tim Thomas, the Paterson (N.J.) Catholic center, who is contemplating the N.B.A. rather than college. At 6-10, Thomas is considered a better prospect than Bryant. But someday a 9-year-old will be a better prospect than both of them. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Ending months of speculation, Kobe Bryant , the 6-foot-6, 17-year-old basketball phenom at Lower Merion High School, outside Philadelphia, announced to a cheering crowd of fellow students in the school gym this week that he had decided to bypass college and make himself available for the NBA draft in June. "You have to stay mentally tough and stay focused," he told reporters afterward. "If I can stay focused on my goals, I should be all right." This should not be difficult for Bryant, whose father, Joe, played in the NBA, now coaches college basketball , and plans to serve as his son's agent. "There's a lot of players that are skilled," said the elder Bryant, "but mentally, he's really ahead of his time." Indeed, Bryant the younger explained that he had vowed to bypass higher education as long ago as the ninth grade, when he told incredulous friends that he was determined to buck the system. Of course, while his classmates now praise his decision - assuming, not without reason, that their chum will soon be earning millions - other observers were not so sanguine. His coach is worried that Bryant is not physically prepared for life as a professional basketball player, and journalists have lamented the loss of a college education. This would be lamentable, I suppose, if Bryant had intended to pursue something resembling a college education. But it is fairer, and certainly more accurate, to say that colleges were pursuing him, not the other way around; and education was not foremost in their mind. Among the now-spurned suitors were such institutions as the universities of Michigan, Duke, North Carolina and Villanova. Kobe Bryant appears to be wiser - or, perhaps, more candid - than his critics: "A lot of people now are starting to think that college basketball is starting to die down," he said. "All the high school all-Americans are going to college for a year or two and then going to the NBA." At long last, an encouraging trend. Twenty years ago, three high school players (Moses Malone, Darryl Hawkins and Bill Willoughby) skipped college for the NBA, and so far as I know, did well enough. But for two decades they were lonely pioneers. Then, last spring, a Chicago high school player named Kevin Garnett quit classes to be drafted by a professional team, the Minnesota Timberwolves, and he has done quite well. Now the floodgates are prepared to open. Other high school players are reportedly ready to follow Bryant's example next month, and a record number of college scholar-athletes are dropping out to join the throng. May the "dying-down" continue. To be sure, this is all very disturbing to some observers, although it's hard to see why. Twenty years ago, among journalists, it was assumed that Moses Malone, by snubbing the University of Maryland and joining the Utah Stars of the ABA, was penalizing himself for life. He could always play basketball , and probably would, they said; but the value of a Maryland bachelor's degree - earned under the tutelage of Coach Lefty Dreisell, the late Len Bias' mentor - could scarcely be measured. It's nice to think that, once upon a time, people really believed such things. And maybe they did. But the idea that Moses Malone or Kobe Bryant or any player hired by a basketball powerhouse is anything other than a mercenary jock is simple nonsense. It is a cynical arrangement between the athletes and their schools; and it degrades the institutions that play such games. The much-overpraised John Thompson of Georgetown is not above putting players on his team as favors to business partners or team patrons. Indeed, one of his scholar-athletes, sophomore Allen Iverson, who Wednesday announced he was dropping out of school for the NBA draft, may have had no choice: Since he drives a $ 132,000 "free loaner" Mercedes Benz, courtesy of a local Washington dealer, he may have been forbidden to play next year, under NCAA rules. Why bother enforcing such provisions? At the University of Kentucky, players live in their own lavishly appointed dorm and are showered with thanks from worshipful alumni: Few books, I imagine, are among the bounty. Scarcely a day goes by, it seems, that some scholar-athlete isn't arrested somewhere for gross misconduct, or plain old criminal behavior, while his coach floats excuses. Watching these semi-professional entertainers take the court, or the field, one asks oneself: What have these players to do with Duke or UCLA or Villanova? Not much, except for this: The notion of universities serving as minor-league farm teams for the NBA - or the NFL or whatever - is awkward for players, demeaning to the schools, and a perversion of college athletics. More power to Kobe Bryant . Philip Terzian is the associate editor of the Providence Journal-Bulletin. headline: COACH SAYS KOBE READY TO GRADUATE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Gregg Downer says the NBA-issue pacifiers can be kept in their boxes. Kobe Bryant won't need one. Downer, who coached the prep phenomenon for four years at Lower Merion High, in the suburbs of Philadelphia, is telling Bryant's detractors the 17-year-old will do just fine in the NBA. "Kobe has a presence and maturity beyond his years and that should make the transition a little smoother," Downer said. "He's the best high school player I've ever seen. I can't help but think in three to five years he's going to be an NBA All-Star." The 6-6, 200-pound Bryant some say he is actually 6-4 has others, including the Knicks, convinced that he is worth a chance. But history suggests Bryant, son of former Sixer Joe (Jelly Bean) Bryant, is in over his head. The players who have made the jump from preps to the pros Moses Malone, Bill Willoughby, Darryl Dawkins and, last season, Kevin Garnett were all at least 6-10. However, Bryant's numbers, although compiled against high school opposition, are hard to ignore. He finished his prep career as the all-time leading scorer in southeastern Pennsylvania history, passing Wilt Chamberlain (2,359) and former St. Joseph's star Carlin Warley (2,441) with a mark of 2,883. He averaged 33 points, 12 rebounds, eight assists, 6.5 steals and nearly four blocks per game. He capped it by helping Lower Merion win AAAA state title, its championship in more than 50 years. Still, many remain unconvinced Bryant can immediately thrive in a man's game. "He can rebound, pass and shoot. He's got a lot of basketball skills," said Billy McKinney, vice president of basketball operations for the Seattle Sonics. "But there's a world of difference between the competition of high school basketball and the NBA. There's a world of difference between college and the NBA." Bryant does have supporters. Nets coach John Calipari noted Bryant is just one year younger than Stephon Marbury. Downer said Bryant has been through workouts with eight or nine teams and the reviews have all been positive. "I've been hearing he's in the 10-15 range, but I wouldn't be surprised if he went before 10th. I think 10 years from now when we look back on this draft, he will be by far the dominant pick of this crop. I have a feeling he's a special one." GRAPHIC: BRYANT FILE NAME: Kobe Bryant . SCHOOL: Lower Merion HS. HEIGHT: 6-6. WEIGHT: 200. STRENGTHS: Raw athletic ability, quickness. Wingspan of a 6-11 player should help him defensively. WEAKNESSES: Age and his fragile frame. Got banged up over 30 games in high school. SCOUT'S COMMENT: David Fredman, Utah Jazz assistant: "It looks to me like he hasn't done a lot of weight training. I think that would be one of the first things he needs to work on. I think he'll be very good in the long run." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Chosen one. Just when Michael Jordan thought he could retire unchallenged, along came Kobe Bryant . YOU know things are hotting up in American basketball when they start talking about Air, you-know-who Jordan, being under threat from Heir, a 19-year-old by the name of Kobe Bryant . Just when it seemed Jordan might head into retirement with no obvious successor in sight, Bryant has produced a string of consistent performances that have not only LA Lakers fans singing his praises but hard-bitten critics as well. It is not as if Bryant is a rookie either. He played his first season of NBA as an 18-year-old and for a while was remembered as the player who missed the crucial shot of the Lakers' season which would have seen his side advance in last year's playoffs. Utah went through instead. Jordan, the NBA sorcerer for so long now, faced his potential apprentice in the recent All-Stars game, Bryant being the youngest All-Star in the 51-year history of the NBA. The clash was touted as a battle between Air and Heir. An ill Jordan made it plain where his intentions lay. "I just wanted to fit in, to make sure Kobe didn't dominate me. The hype was against him. I was just glad that I was able to fight him off," he said. Jordan's experience was reflected in East's 135-114 win over West and he was named Most Valuable Player (MVP). He top-scored with 23 points for his team while Bryant top-scored with 18 for the Wests. Bryant admitted to nervousness before the game. "I had butterflies because I wanted this moment to come. Once I went out there, I was comfortable," Bryant said. Jordan, who has spent the latter part of his career putting down pretenders to his throne by outplaying them whenever the chance arose, acknowledged Bryant. "He came at me early, which I would have done if I were him. If you see someone who's sick, or whatever, you've got to attack him. "He attacked. I like his attitude," Jordan said. The impact Bryant is making is summed up by veteran NBA watcher and LA Times columnist Jim Murray. "You hear about Kobe Bryant , the Lakers' 19-year-old basketball whiz, and your first reaction is, the last time anyone this good appeared there was a star in the East. "It gets to the point where, when you meet him the first time, you want to ask him what he did with the halo. Did he fly in or just walk across Santa Monica Bay? "You don't know whether to get his autograph or his blessing. Or just touch the hem of his warm-up suit. You feel inadequate interviewing someone so perfect. It's a job for Matthew, Mark, Luke and John, not a mere sportswriter." Murray quoted long-serving broadcaster Chick Hearn, a basketball hall-of-famer, on Bryant: "Kobe is a 28-year-old in a 19-year-old body. The things he does with no college experience is beyond belief. He has total confidence. He'll walk up to a Michael Jordan or a backup guard with the same degree of skill and enthusiasm and expectations of success. "He's a star on and off the court. I've seen lots of them come and go but none with the potential of Kobe Bryant . Someday, we'll be able to brag We knew him when!'" -------------------- CAPTION: SLAM TIME - LA Laker Kobe Bryant buries the ball in a NBA match against the Atlanta Hawks. Bryant is seen as the heir-apparent to the throne of Chicago Bulls superstar Michael Jordan. headline: BRANDS IN TROUBLE- IN DEMAND: AFTER FIRST WAVE OF HYPE, BRYANT GATHERS STRENGTH
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 When he was 17, Kobe Bryant was held up as an example of everything wrong with the National Basketball Association, the latest in an alarming series of wannabe-like-Mike superteens, impatiently bypassing college for NBA riches. That was then. Now Kobe Bryant is 19. He's a Los Angeles Laker. He's an all-star starter who doesn't start for his own team. And he's being molded by the NBA's media machine into the league's next-generation superman. This year, he will star in the largest ad efforts to come out of Spalding Sports Worldwide and Adidas America. Last week, Nintendo of America inked a deal to rebrand an existing basketball videogame as '' Kobe Bryant 's NBA Courtside.'' He is 1998's athlete brand in demand. And if his agents have their way, Mr. Bryant won't be stilling that demand anytime soon. NEEDS LONG-TERM DEALS If his agents can resist saying yes for now (a one-time appearance in a spot for Coca-Cola Co.'s Sprite last fall and a rumored McDonald's Corp. deal suggests they can't), when he does say yes, he will say it to long-term deals with hungry, world-class marketers. ''I promised his family I wouldn't oversaturate him,'' said Sonny Vaccaro, Adidas' legendary director of sports promotions who's known Kobe all his life through a relationship with his father, and who is involved in the star's marketing decisions. ''You can't give the public a Cracker Jack box. You lose credibility when everything becomes a payday.'' Yes, Kobe Bryant , a hip and handsome (yet humble), spectacular (but still learning) athlete icon is being deliberately cultivated. But save for last month's all-star game, where NBC's fawning drew media criticism and Kobe's hot-dogging irked NBA veterans, he seems to be avoiding the appearance of being manufactured. ''Our society has become more sophisticated and can see through a contrived strategy to develop an icon,'' said Dean Bonham, president-CEO of Bonham Group. '' Kobe Bryant comes across fresh and natural and not contrived. He's also extraordinarily talented, which is the critical ingredient.'' ''Kobe basically embodies what the NBA is about right now: youth, excitement, charisma and charm,'' said Dan Touhey, Spalding's product manager of retail basketball . ''We want to lend his superstardom to our authenticity as the NBA's official basketball .'' FIRST SPALDING CAMPAIGN Spalding's first Kobe Bryant ad campaign, hitting this spring, supports a new brand positioning. Mr. Bryant will benefit from Spalding's plan to increase media spending by 50% this year; TV spots will run almost exclusively during NBA broadcasts. Mr. Bryant also will get a signature basketball product line. Those products will be more sophisticated and upscale than the usual graphics-laden, player-endorsed ball no serious hoops player would use. Adidas has employed Mr. Bryant in two global ad pushes to date. The third arrives in late summer to promote the KB8 3 basketball shoe. But Adidas hopes Kobe's mojo can influence its entire line of products. Despite a recent slump, Mr. Vaccaro is confident Kobe is no flash in the pan. ''He's show time,'' he said. ''Signing Kobe was a major step for Adidas, the boldest step it's ever taken in this new era of Adidas. If we've guessed wrong, then I don't know what's right.'' headline: GAMERS' REVENGE VIDEO GAMES CAN PUT YOUR TEAM BACK IN THE PLAYOFFS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Unhappy with the way the your favorite basketball or hockey teams are doing in the championships? If you own a video game system, you can get another chance to win by replaying those match-ups. Just for fun, I played as the Miami Heat in four NBA video games. The result: in three out of four, the Heat won the NBA crown. But the winner of the fun quotient was " Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside" it's so realistic! For hockey games, it wasn't as easy for this Florida Panthers fan. They are out of the playoffs, and since these games are based on current rosters and abilities, the result was 1-3, with the Panthers losing in the second round. But when I re-created the Stanley Cup finalist team of 1996 using "NHL '98," I won two out of four games for the Cup, proving that anything is possible in a video game. Like the real thing, every game is unpredictable. You call the plays, the formations, the timeouts. And you control the player with the ball (highlighted by a star or circle), with the computer controlling the others based on the play called. You can play against the computer or head-to-head against a friend, or the two of you can play against the computer. Unlike the real thing, games can take anywhere from 15 minutes to an hour to play, based on the time limits you set for the quarters. Here are the best of the best, based on my tests, for play on the following systems: Sony PlayStation (PSX), Nintendo 64 (N64) and personal computer (PC). Games are available at most video outlets, with an estimated selling price of $ 49 to $ 79. BASKETBALL " Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside" is from the latest generation of sports games. Like "Madden 64" and "NFL Quarterback Club 64," Kobe's game sets new standards for lifelike play. All the real players are in the game, the pace is fast and the dunks and shots never stop. Nintendo opted not to use real signature moves for each player, but all the goodies are still there behind-the-back passes, fakes, rim-hanging dunks. Smart computer opponents actually guard players and go for the ball, as opposed to just sitting there and waiting to be taken advantage of. There is absolutely no slowdown in gameplay or graphics. Best sports title on Nintendo 64. One to four players. By Nintendo Sports, (N64). "NBA Live '98": This new version combines the fast action, strategies and realism of five-on-five basketball . It contains all the NBA teams, two All-Star teams, a custom team and a trade option. The best feature is a new 30-degree perspective that opens up the court like never before. A slow-motion feature allows you to view slam-dunks. One to five players. By EA Sports, the sports division of Electronics Arts, (PSX, Saturn, PC). "NBA Shootout '98": Like "NBA Live '98," this version offers fast action, strategies and realism of five-on-five basketball for the PlayStation, but with better graphics. It contains all the NBA teams, two All-Star teams, a custom team and a trade option. One to four players. By Sony Sports, (PSX). HOCKEY "NHL '98": This new version combines the action, strategies and realism of hockey with new CD sound effects that bring the arena to life. The checking, power plays and slap shots will send you into next week. The puck travels at real-time speed, making it a game that takes a lot of practice. One to four players. By EA Sports (PSX, PC). "NHL FaceOff '98": This challenger to "NHL '98" adds in-your-face graphics with arena sound effects that make you feel that you're sitting right behind the glass. One to four players. By Sony Sports (PSX) WEEKEND MAG Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PHOTO, PHOTO: A scene from " Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 TITLE: Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside FORMAT: N64 PUBLISHER: Nintendo PRICE: \'a359.99 BASKETBALL fans have suffered from a lack of courtside video game entertainment on the N64 - until now. Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside has changed everything - high quality graphics, easy controls and a host of gizmos, including playing God and making your own player, puts this game streets ahead of its rivals. In just a few minutes you will be slam dunking, intercepting passes and turning on a six pence as you battle it out with the best to become NBA king of the court. I approached the game with a touch of cynicism - firstly I am not a basketball fan and secondly the instruction book is four times thicker than usual. But once I plugged in and started fiddling around I quickly became hooked. There are a choice of 29 teams from all the leagues across the States and as it is officially endorsed by the NBA, the players bear an uncanny resemblance to their human counter parts. Once you have picked your team you are ready to go - the action is fast moving and there are a choice of seven different camera angles with zoom to help you see exactly what you are doing. One advantage of basketball games to football is the action is non-stop, which makes it fast and a lot more entertaining. Apart from great handling, the option to hire and fire different players gives you a bit of fun in between games. The game really comes in to its own with the multi-player function, which allows up to four people to play against each other or even all together. If you choose to make your own player, you can decide his height, weight, hair colour, even the colour of his shirt - you can also make him into a superhuman, meaning he is almost certain to score whenever he gets the ball. Basketball nuts will love this game but even if you don't it offers a good challenge and plenty of fun. headline: KOBE BRYANT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 How do you market the ''next'' Michael Jordan? The answer to that question was answered quite definitively over the past year by Arn Tellem, 44, agent for budding superstar Kobe Bryant : You can't. That isn't to say the image of the red-hot 19-year-old guard for the Los Angeles Lakers hasn't been manufactured. The difference is in who's doing the talking. Mr. Bryant has let the media, the National Basketball Association and NBC crown him Air Apparent. He's responded with modesty, sometimes with the chestnut ''There can be no next Michael Jordan, I just wanna be Kobe Bryant .'' But he has done nothing to discourage such talk. When USA Today wanted to track his every move during the NBA playoffs after naming him as one of five candidates to be the next Michael Jordan, Mr. Bryant didn't turn the newspaper down. Mr. Bryant's marketing team includes Mr. Tellem, his parents, with whom he still lives, and Sonny Vaccaro, director of sports promotions at Adidas. They told Advertising Age in March that Mr. Bryant won't rush into endorsements. But there he was during the commercial breaks of the May/June NBA playoff games, hawking Nintendo videogames, Spalding Sports Worldwide basketballs and Coca-Cola Co.'s Sprite. Yet the spots smartly lay a foundation of trust and humility. An Adidas spot from last fall has Mr. Bryant telling consumers to just be themselves. Irony is deftly used to both defuse and fuel Kobemania. Nintendo uses a crazed sportscaster to sing Kobe's praises, leaving him confused. Sprite has employed him in two spots for the ''Image is nothing. Thirst is everything'' campaign. Mr. Bryant's marketing partners are banking on the youngster to grow "Michael Jordan big." Spalding abandoned Shaquille O'Neal to build a future around Mr. Bryant. Adidas, the hottest athletic brand at retail, is counting on Mr. Bryant. But that's a big if. Mr. Bryant now has to fulfill his promise and play like Michael Jordan. The jury is still out on that. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Blow the World Cup. Soon you will be able to steer a team to victory in the Premiership, with Premier Manager 98 on the Playstation. Released tomorrow, it will give you varying levels of control, from humble accountant to complete dictator. ...Virgin Interactive has set up a helpline for Resident Evil 2. They suggest it will be particularly useful if you are distracted by flesh and meat when you walk past a butchers shop, or get edgy walking past a cemetery. Call 0891 600321 if you can't handle the Playstation gorefest without assistance. ...Nintendo is hitting the big screen with a cunning bit of product placement in a forthcoming British movie. Virtual Sexuality tells the story of a girl who finds herself in the body of her ideal man after an accident with a virtual reality machine. Mario's set for a cameo role walking around a funfair. England have bowed out of the World Cup, except on EA World Cup 98, where we consistently thrash Argentina 3-1. Now all thoughts turn to basketball , with Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside on the N64, and Total NBA 98 on the PSX. If the merest mention of sport brings you out in a rash, then check out our game of the week, the madcap midget motor racer, Circuit Breakers. Circuit Breakers (Mindscape) For Sony Playstation Price: GBP 39.99 Out Friday Micro Machines has a pretender to its position as the wacky racer of choice on the Playstation. Circuit Breakers takes the concept, adds a third dimension to the tracks, litters the courses with weapons and challenges you to stop playing. Choose from the eight cars available and head on to one of the four courses open to you at the start. Keep winning and more courses become available. There are 32 in the game, each with its own traps and attributes. Egypt is a dustbowl littered with ramps and fires. Ramps are fun, but fires blow you up. That's where realism ends and insanity begins. While you tear around the tracks you can pick up anything from oil-slicks to shrinkers, handy for squeezing through the frequent tight gaps. The gameplay is frenetic, and difficult. Because the races are so short, one crash is usually enough to kiss goodbye to victory, unless you find a secret shortcut. Contact with other cars slows you dramatically, but with time you learn to use it to your advantage. In case you hadn't realised, this isn't Gran Turismo. The cars handle like dodgems and you won't need to get a racing licence. The graphics are excellent, with well-drawn tracks, cute cars and good camera angles. The sound effects are average, with bog-standard engine revving and tinny tyre screeching, but the dancey soundtrack makes up for it. Circuit Breakers has a limited life as a one-player game, but if you race with up to three human competitors you'll not switch off. Micro Machines manages to hold its crown, but only because it's GBP 20 cheaper. Circuit Breakers is technically better and definitely worth a look. RATING: 85 per cent (RA) Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside (Nintendo) For Nintendo 64 Price: About GBP 45 Out now The intensity and pace of basketball makes the sport a natural choice for conversion into a video game, and Nintendo have pulled out all the stops to make Kobe Bryant a winner. Thanks to the NBA licence, all 29 teams are featured, along with their players from last season. And we're not just talking names. Nintendo have even grafted their faces on to the game characters. You and up to three other players can participate in single games or an NBA season. The rules can be customised, taking the difficulty level from schoolyard to All Star and, because there are so many options and moves available, the control method takes time to learn, but once you're there Kobe Bryant plays well. Graphically, there is only so much you can do to make a basketball court look interesting, but some nice touches like reflections on the waxed surface highlight the care that has gone into putting the game together. The myriad of camera options let you play from every conceivable angle with a variable zoom and, although the players look great in close-up, don't expect to win with a single player filling the screen. The sound effects are faultless. Trainers squeak as the players turn and pivot, while the ball sounds like, well, a bouncing basketball . The crowd sound is streets ahead of the competition. Background noise in sports games usually sounds like it's being played on a badly-tuned AM radio, but in Kobe Bryant you can almost pick out individual conversations. As you would expect, you can play single games or NBA seasons with one or two players. Total NBA lacks the graphic flare of Kobe Bryant , but it's not fair to compare games for different consoles in that department. The players lack definition but move well, and the court looks realistic enough. The Playstation game does win on instant playability. You can choose your own teams and tinker with the rules, or just leap into the action in arcade mode. The players move well on the court and a simple control layout keeps you on top of the game. The sound is bang on - shut your eyes and you could be watching it live on Channel Four at three in the morning. The commentary is intelligent, by basketball standards, but as with most sports games you'll be reaching for the off switch soon enough. Total NBA 98 is good fun, and where it lacks in polish there's bags of gameplay. headline: LATEST GAME REVIEWS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 COLOMBIAN drug barons and Russian rebels give you problems in Team Apache (Mindscape, GBP 39.99) on the PC. This is the closest most people will get to flying an awesome AH64-A chopper - which costs $14 million in real life! But hours of painstaking work have been invested in this game. The attention to detail is superb - from the rippling grass as you take off, to the forked lightning as you battle through a storm. The missions are challenging and there's a good tutorial too. But the 300-page manual makes this a game for serious propeller-heads only! VERDICT: 5/5 (excellent) AGE RATING: Age 11 plus YOU may have heard of Michael Jordan but how about Kobe Bryant ? To the uninitiated, Kobe is the latest big name in American basketball to endorse a video game - this time for the Nintendo 64. Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside (Nintendo, GBP 39.99) is packed with other US stars too. The game even animates them wearing their trademark arm bands, knee supports and favourite socks! All this may mean little to UK video games fans. The action is all -important - and it's here where Kobe scores a spectacular slam-dunk. The game is slick and stylish, offering end-to-end thrills of the highest quality and can be viewed from seven different camera angles. VERDICT: 4/5 (good) AGE RATING: All ages AS the name suggests, Everybody's Golf (Sony, around GBP 34.99) will appeal to the masses. Cute and colourful, this new PlayStation game has already sold more than 1.5 million copies in Japan. Cartoon players swipe their way through five themed courses. It's fun - and you don't need to understand the jargon. headline: KOBE BRYANT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 HEART OF DARKNESS (G) Sony PlayStation TAKING the whole 3-D adventure genre a step further, Roadshow has developed a game that is innovative and highly-detailed, yet simple enough to cater for the whole family. It's set in another world at another time. You play the part of Andy, a young lad whose best mate is his dog Whiskey, who has been dognapped by the Forces of Darkness. (At least in Andy's world they don't even pretend to like city council inspectors). QNP You must enter the Secret Kingdom and battle soul-hungry phantoms and demons (trainee inspectors) in your quest for Whiskey. (I know a few old journos who go through that experience each and every day _ and they'll even settle for beer.) Along the way, various obstacles and foes will try to make life difficult, but these can be overcome with a burst of laser, a sidestep and jump in the air, or a bit of a surf on the Internet to look for cheats (but that's Generation X for you). The fact that this game is aimed at children from eight years of age means it's very user-freindly _ to the extent that it will bring up hints on the screen if you seem to be stuck at a certain level. This comes in extremely handy _ and seems to be timed to the exact second your frustration moves you toward the Off button. The promo says there's more than 30 hours of gameplay here _ and the 3D glasses included are more than just a gimmick. MICHAEL WILSON KOBE BRYANT IN NBA COURTSIDE (G) Nintendo 64 YOUNG basketball legend Kobe Bryant stars in and presents this excellently rendered 3D basketball action game. All the top American teams have been bought and the line-ups are authentic, but you can swap and buy other players along the way. The players' faces are texture-mapped to display their real characteristics but the place where the game's graphics really give more is in the movements of players _ about as far removed from fussball and as real as you can get. This is achieved through the proprietary animation system, which uses character motion blending to create the smoothest moves. The game gives you the choice of playing a one-off pre-season game, relive the season's matches or indulge in any number of play-offs. It takes a little time to get used to controlling the moves. At the start, it seems like you can just let the players go about their business, alley-oop slamdunking and taking throw-ins without any input. After a while you'll learn how to get the advantage with fancy moves such as ball stealing and defensive shuffles. When you get the game down pat, you realise how accurately the players moves have been callibrated and appreciate the potential for finesse in championship gaming action. This game is bound to rank with the all-time great sporting games such as NHL Breakaway 98. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KOBE BRYANT IN NBA COURTSIDE Runs on: Nintendo 64 Category: Sports Players: 1-4 Publisher: Nintendo Distributor: Nintendo Price: $79.95 Classification: G Rating: 4 IF the only dribbling you're able to manage is onto your pillow at night, then maybe it's time to get into the virtual NBA, where you can be a basketball star regardless of your height or athletic ability. And who better to help out than Kobe Bryant , star player with the LA Lakers, who was in Sydney earlier this month to help launch the adidas basketball camp for young Australian players. Many believe Bryant will be "the next Michael Jordan". Apart from being up in lights on the title, Bryant doesn't actually play a big role in Kobe Bryant in NBA Courtside. It features the full NBA roster with all 29 teams, and all players (except Michael Jordan, who is too expensive) are represented. The Nintendo 64 doesn't have too many star b-ball games, and this five-on-five simulation wipes away most of the competition (although it still doesn't beat NBA Live 98 or Total NBA 98 on the PlayStation). The graphics are sharp and the animations smooth and realistic. There are seven camera angles, and the distance of the camera from the players can be changed. The controls seem simple but take a while to come to grips with. After an hour of mucking around, you should get a feel for the game, and when you do there's a lot of fun waiting. The computer AI is great and, with three levels of difficulty (rookie, pro and all-star), it should please players of all skill levels. Like all decent sports games, NBA Courtside allows for four-player simultaneous action, making it a perfect party game. To spice up the on-screen events, there's a play-by-play call from the Seattle Sonics announcer Vic Orlando. The call can get repetitive but you've got the crowd noise, sneaker squeaks and players' grunts to keep things atmospheric. NBA Courtside is neither groundbreaking nor brilliant, but it is very good fun for basketball fans willing to put in some time and master it. If you've got a N64 console, this is the b-ball sim you want. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 OVER the next few years you'll be reading endless comparisons between Kobe Bryant and Michael Jordan. You'll probably see Kobe on your television screens several times a night advertising some product. And you'll get sick of your kids asking time and again for a "pair of Adidas joggers like Kobe wears". So who is Kobe Bryant ? On a special tour of the Homebush Olympic Games site in Sydney recently, he was just like any aspiring young Olympian checking out a venue that promises to fulfil so many of his dreams. "My ultimate goal is an NBA championship but I'd love to be back at this place for the Olympics," he said from a suite overlooking the main Olympic Stadium. Bryant has just turned 20 and he doesn't wan't anything for his birthday. He already has it all _ a $3 million contract with the LA Lakers and another few million dollars on top of that through corporate deals. He lives with his mother Pam and father Joe, a former NBA star, in his own house in Los Angeles. He is worshipped by basketball fans the world over and has pages of Internet sites dedicated to him. But for all that, he is just a typical 20-year-old who, at times, laments the sacrifices he has to make in order to be a sporting superstar. Which is what separates Bryant from his ego-driven rivals. We might get sick of hearing his name over the next few years but we won't tire of watching him play _ not even when he returns to Homebush in two years and slam dunks his way to a gold medal with the American Dream Team. After the Stadium tour, Bryant plonked himself on a chair and explained why, sometimes, it's hard being Kobe Bryant . "Not being able to hang out with my friends and go to parties and to the movies with them is hard, but it's all part of the sacrifice if you want to be a star," Bryant said. In a sport which breeds egos bigger than teammate Shaquille O'Neal, he is surprisingly level headed for someone so young and talented. Bryant's mother said he'd always acted older than his age. "Ever since he was young he was always a very serious person _ he always wanted to be the best in whatever he did," Pam said. "Whether it was basketball or karate, Kobe was good at anything and I knew he was going to be a star. "Kobe is a good boy, he has two older sisters who keep his feet on the ground." But when he steps on to the basketball court, his feet are anything but on the ground. Bryant is the current NBA slamdunk Champion and has a list of other achievements as long as his 6ft 9in (202cm) frame. He has played in two NBA playoff series and scored a career-high 33 points against Michael Jordan's Chicago Bulls last December. Bryant was the youngest ever player to appear in an NBA game when he made his debut at 18 years and two months against the Minnesota Timberwolves in 1996. He is the youngest All Star in NBA history. What is even more amazing is he skipped college and went straight into the NBA as a first-round draft pick. "Ever since I was six I always knew I wanted to be a basketballer . . . that was my dream and I'm not worried about all of the hype that has surrounded me," he said. "A basketball career has been my destiny and I really don't think it's any big deal going straight into the NBA and not playing college ball." Bryant admits he had two heroes when he was growing up _ his father Joe and LA Laker's champion "Magic" Johnson. "My dad played in the NBA but he's not my hero because of basketball _ he's my hero because he is my dad and he taught me about life . . . . about growing up," he said. "Magic is my hero and because of him I always wanted to play for the LA Lakers." Basketball is his life now but it won't necessarily always be the ease. "If I'm still playing basketball and I have a family who needs me _ if my children need me _ than they would come first no matter what," he said. Even if he is at the height of his career? "Definitely. I don't ever want to miss seeing my kids grow up." But that is long time away. At the moment Bryant's basketball commitments don't allow him enough time to have a girlfriend. "He goes out with girls sometimes but they are just his friends _ even though they probably think they're Kobe's girlfriend," Pam said. Bryant is too focused to let a girl get in the way of his career, "I know what I want and I know the sacrifices I have to make," he said. "When I finish playing, I'd like to be remembered as the most dedicated basketball player ever, but someone who you can tell really enjoyed the game. "But at the moment, I've got plenty of years left in the game and I'll play until I'm no longer enjoying And until that time, Kobe Bryant will keep breaking records until there are none left to be broken. When he does retire, Kobe Bryant may just be the best ever. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Shaquille O'Neal yelled as he walked past Los Angeles Lakers teammate Kobe Bryant , "Trade me to Kobe's team!" Bryant, new half-owner of a basketball team in Italy, grinned and said, "I don't think Jerry West would like that." The Associated Press Both returning to his roots and making -- at age 21 -- an investment for his retirement, Bryant has purchased a 50-per-cent share of Olimpia Milano, the storied Milan team for which his father once played. "That's my home. That's where I developed a lot of my love for the game. This is something I've always wanted to do, and it's just great that it has happened," Bryant said yesterday. "I've always wanted to raise my family in Italy. When my NBA career is over, hopefully I'll be there," Bryant added. He spent eight years of his childhood in Italy while his father, Joe, was establishing himself as a star in the Italian professional league. Joe Bryant earlier played eight seasons in the NBA, with Philadelphia, San Diego and Houston. The elder Bryant will be executive vice-president of the Italian team, with the Bryant family responsible for running the operation. Pasquale Caputo, who bought the team earlier this year, retains 50-per-cent ownership. It is believed to be the first time a professional basketball player has acquired a substantial interest in another pro team. NBA players are prohibited from owning a piece of an NBA team. Kobe Bryant , who chipped in more than $1 million of the $4 million paid for the half interest, plans to spend several weeks each summer in Milan working with his father and with corporate partners, and making appearances. He speaks Italian fluently and has returned to Italy many times since he and his family left in 1992. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The votes aren't in, but Kobe Bryant will definitely perform -- off the court -- during the NBA All-Star Weekend next month. The Los Angeles Lakers star, who probably also will play a little hoops that weekend, will make his concert debut, performing his upcoming single K.O.B.E. with supermodel Tyra Bankson the NBA TeamUp Celebration TV special. TeamUp recognizes the volunteer efforts of thousands of teens. The two collaborated on Bryant's first album, Visions , expected to be released by Columbia records in April. K.O.B.E. will be released Tuesday. "I'm not nervous," says Bryant, 21. "It's going to be fun." But what about Banks, was this a ploy just to have her in the video? "No, no no," says Bryant, laughing. "She can sing; she did great." Bryant, who has known Banks, 26, since he was 17, says she heard he was doing an album and at an awards show, she asked if she could sing with him. "I didn't jump at the offer at first," Bryant jokes, "then she said, 'I'll do it for free.' " USA TODAY music critic Steve Jones says, " Kobe Bryant probably couldn't be called for traveling on K.O.B.E. ( * * out of four), but he's a lot more creative flowing to the hoop than he is flowing on the mike. "He raps with a cool confidence. At the same time, his deliveries aren't likely to make anybody's highlight reels, and his derivative lyrics would keep him on the bench in the hip-hop big leagues." Teammate Shaquille O'Neal, who has his own label, has released five rap albums. "We talk music a lot," says Bryant, who has been writing music since he was 9 and rhyming since he began playing basketball . fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 IN the old days, Kobe Bryant would have been a college senior. In these new days, he is a co-owner of a basketball team. Bryant recently spent a few of Jerry Buss's lire to buy half of Olimpia Milano, his father's old team when the Bryant family was based in Italy. He may be the first active player to own a major piece of another team, albeit in another country. Although he insisted his purchase of Olimpia was "mostly business," Bryant talks of living in Italy again some day. It will not be any time soon. With the National Basketball Association rooting for the next generation of marketable stars to coalesce, Bryant shows signs of becoming positively Pippenesque. At the age of 21, he is already an explosive star with glorious moves, linked, as Scottie Pippen once was, to a superstar who makes him all the more lethal. "I'm the big brother and he's the little brother," said Shaquille O'Neal, who may be playing the role of Michael Jordan in this West Coast performance. Bryant may not love that viewpoint, but with the big man clogging up the middle, Bryant has become a significant offensive and defensive force. In four seasons with the Lakers, his average has risen from 7.6 to 15.4 to 19.9 and now to 22 after Friday night's game with the Celtics, as the Lakers won at home for their 10th straight victory. With his long arms, Bryant has also become a defensive power. Last Sunday in Philadelphia -- another of his homes -- Bryant stuck his willowy 6-foot-7-inch frame in the way of Allen Iverson, helping to hold the league's leading scorer without a point in the second half. Bryant never claimed to have personally shut down Iverson, but that's how it came across in the accounts of the game. "He did a good job of staying in front of Iverson and bringing him to me," O'Neal said last week in New Jersey. On coaching instructions, Bryant had planted himself to Iverson's side, denying him the middle-range shot, and forcing him to move in toward O'Neal, who is more than a full foot taller than the 6-0 Sixer guard. "What I tried to do was put myself in his shoes," Bryant said. "In the past when I've had games where I'm struggling, any moment I could go off. I'm sure he was thinking the same way, so I just played him." Iverson shot 0 for 11 in the second half, and later he gave some credit to Bryant, saying, "It's always tough playing against somebody quick and tall because they can kind of back up off you and get a hand in your face." Bryant is a joy to watch, with his quick steals, his pogo-stick jump shots and electric drives, and an instinct for jamming home rebounds from far above the basket. He missed the first 15 games this season with a broken right hand, but is fourth on his team in total assists and tied for fourth in rebounds -- and also was second on the team in turnovers with an average of nearly three a game. "He has his moments when he tries to penetrate too much," Coach Phil Jackson said the other night. "I'd say it's two steps forward and half a step backward." O'Neal acknowledged that Bryant, who passed up college, is learning more all the time. The veteran rap artist and the aspiring young rapper, about to release his first CD, may not be close, but they put it together on the court. "He and Coach have a good relationship," O'Neal said. "Coach gives him a plan and he executes it." With his exotic name -- which stems from an obviously unforgettable Japanese steak dinner his parents enjoyed during the gestation period -- Kobe Bryant is a fixture with the Lakers. He is currently in the second year of a six-year contract extension, believed to be worth nearly $71 million. Bryant is in the very top of the line of players who went directly from high school to the N.B.A.: Kevin Garnett, Shawn Kemp, Moses Malone, Darryl Dawkins. Dozens of others have come out way too young, and never adjusted. Then again, Kobe Bryant was not the normal teenager even when he was a senior at Lower Merion High outside Philadelphia. He had observed basketball up close in the friendly environment of Italy, where his father, Joe (Jelly Bean) Bryant, played for eight seasons after an eight-year career in the N.B.A. "See, the kids in America, they don't do the work that Kobe did," Magic Johnson once said. "That's the problem with the young people now. They don't have the fundamentals." Kobe Bryant absorbed the game in Italy, going to practice with his dad, getting on the court, listening to his father talk about the game on the leisurely afternoons off. The family went home when it was time for Kobe to play high school ball, but he still speaks Italian, and talks with reverence about the three imported Dutch stars, Marco Von Basten, Ruud Gullit and Frank Rijkaard, who made AC Milan the soccer powerhouse when he was living there. "My mother wouldn't let me go to the games," he said with a sigh. "She said it was too dangerous." He could surely go back now a

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The Los Angeles Lakers' 19-game winning streak ended tonight against an unlikely opponent, the Washington Wizards. In their most spirited performance in two years -- before a crowd of 20,674 fans who frequently cheered wildly for the visitors -- the Wizards ended the fourth-longest winning streak in National Basketball Association history, beating the Lakers, 109-102. Washington, led by Mitch Richmond who scored 32 points, won for only the 21st time this season. The streak did not end passively as the Lakers rallied from a 21-point deficit in the second quarter. The Lakers' Shaquille O'Neal had 40 points, 12 rebounds and made 10 of 16 free throws. But he also was plagued by foul trouble, as was Kobe Bryant , who fouled out in the game's final second with 7 points on 2-for-10 shooting. The Wizards also needed some luck in the final minute. Leading, 101-98, Richmond forced a jumper that barely scraped the rim. The ball fell right into Juwan Howard's hands, giving the Wizards another 24 seconds on the clock. Richmond was then fouled and made two free throws with 17.1 seconds remaining. The Wizards went 6 for 6 from the line after that to keep the margin safe. The Lakers' 19 consecutive victories trail only the 1971-72 Lakers (33), the 1970-71 Milwaukee Bucks (20) and the 1947-48 and the 1948-49 Washington Capitols (20) for the N.B.A.'s longest winning streak. Despite their struggles, the Wizards recently took the Knicks and the Spurs to overtime, prompting Lakers Coach Phil Jackson to warn his players before the game that the Wizards were a "team that plays up to the level of the competition." The Washington fans cheered more for the visiting Lakers than the Wizards early on, then roared for the underdogs when the Wizards took a 21-point lead in the second quarter. Then, as Los Angeles rallied in the fourth quarter, the MCI Center reverberated with the chant: "Let's go Lakers!" In the final minute, the chant became "Beat L.A." The game also featured the rivalry of Michael Jordan against Jackson, his former coach in Chicago, for the first time since Jordan became a part-owner of the Wizards and the president of basketball operations. Jordan was at the game, making his first appearance since the Feb. 24 trading deadline, but he did not arrive until the second quarter. headline: LAKERS' RICE CONSPICUOUS BY HIS ABSENCE OF OFFENCE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 to fill void left by injured Bryant BASKETBALL COLUMNIST INDIANAPOLIS - The Lakers have a problem with their missing in action. Or inaction, as it were. First, there is Kobe Bryant , still sore yesterday with that left ankle injury suffered in Game 3 of these NBA Finals. Sore enough that he wasn't able to do much of anything in practice and will likely be a game-time decision to play tomorrow night's Game 4, with the Lakers leading the Pacers in the series 2-1. Then there is Glen Rice. The catch-and-shoot artist and purported second option with Bryant out of action Sunday wasn't able to do much of either. Rice played but 11 minutes of the second half, missing all four of his shots during that period. ''Any time we have Kobe Bryant out of the game, a key player, I feel the opportunity should have been given," said Rice, who has averaged just 10.3 points this series. ''It's just a matter of being involved in the offence. I wish I could have been more involved." Phil Jackson said he replaced Rice with Rick Fox in the third quarter for defensive purposes. Rice didn't return until 1:29 remained in the game, when the Lakers needed offence to close a six-point Pacers lead. ''We needed some defence and change of venue, and with that group out there on the floor we got back in the game," said Jackson. When told of Rice's complaints, Jackson said: ''I have no comment for that. That's the way he saw the game." This has been an ongoing problem for Rice in his first and probably only season with the Lakers, with free agency beckoning this summer. The Lakers' triangle offence doesn't include set plays, and Rice doesn't get the chance to come off screens and get shots on statagems designed for him. ''I was very surprised," Rice said of his benching. ''When you got a guy who can get the job done sitting there on the bench . . . Definitely, come Wednesday night I'm going to try to be more aggressive." As for Bryant, he spent Sunday getting treatment in the dressing room while watching his side lose 100-91. He has yet to practise since the injury. ''I'll probably have to push it on Wednesday," he said. ''So we'll just see how it feels." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 To the Sports Editor: I remember the first time I saw Kobe Bryant play basketball . It was a breezy Friday night in the fall of 1994, and he led Lower Merion High School to a romp over Haverford High, another suburban Philadelphia school. At the time, I was a fledgling freelance reporter for The Main Line Times, and watching Kobe, I felt energized, my senses sharp. He reminded me of Michael Jordan -- the leaping ability, the agility, the swagger, the style -- and I knew I had just seen a prodigy. Six years later, after watching Kobe carry the Los Angeles Lakers to an overtime victory over the Indiana Pacers in Game 4 of the N.B.A. finals, this time on television in my apartment, I was energized again. I turned to my friend Ted, a Lower Merion native as well, and said: "Do you believe this? He's just become a legend. And he grew up right around the corner from us on Remington Road. And he's just 21." I recently had lunch with an old neighborhood friend's mother who, like me, lived in the town of Wynnewood, Pa. She had crossed paths with the young Kobe, too, because her youngest son had caught the basketball bug at Lower Merion High. Some Saturdays she would take her son to practice and pick up Kobe, who lived up the street, on the way. She told me how one day she joined the team at its annual fund-raising car wash. She remembers Kobe standing off to the side, holding a sign advertising the car wash. "You've got to go out near the street and hold your sign up, Kobe," she told him. "No one's going to see you if you stand back here." Hard to believe that the shy 10th-grader's face popped up on a video screen in a Times Square store-front window as I walked home from work one night. For all of us who ever played basketball growing up in the neighborhood, Kobe represents a dream. He dreamed big and followed his dream. And that's what we should all do: follow our dreams. ANDREW SHERMAN New York HYPERLINK http://www.nytimes.com 00 http://www.nytimes.com Classification_105 Classification_105 Classification fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Language: ENGLISH Subject: BASKETBALL (91%); FUNDRAISING (89%); FREELANCE EMPLOYMENT (73%); SUBURBAN SCHOOLS (72%) Company: KOBE BRYANT (96%); LOS ANGELES LAKERS KOBE BRYANT (96%); LOS ANGELES LAKERS (83%); LOS ANGELES LAKERS (83%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (56%); INDIANA PACERS (56%); INDIANA PACERS (56%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (56%) Organization: LOS ANGELES LAKERS (83%); LOS ANGELES LAKERS (83%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (56%); INDIANA PACERS (56%); INDIANA PACERS (56%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (56%); LOS ANGELES LAKERS LOS ANGELES LAKERS (83%); LOS ANGELES LAKERS (83%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (56%); INDIANA PACERS (56%); INDIANA PACERS (56%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (56%) Industry: SUBURBAN SCHOOLS (72%) Person: KOBE BRYANT (90%); MICHAEL JORDAN (79%) Geographic: LOS ANGELES, CA, USA (76%) Load-Date: July 2, 2000 fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor 0 lineWidth 12700 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 End of Document Page PAGE of NUMPAGES Sex saga top sports story of '03; Alleged sexual assault by Kobe Bryant shocks sports world HYPERLINK https://advance.lexis.com/api/document?collection=news&id=urn:contentItem:4BBN-62N0-018S-3380-00000-00&context=1519360 00 Sex saga top sports story of '03; Alleged sexual assault by HYPERLINK https://advance.lexis.com/api/document?collection=news&id=urn:contentItem:4BBN-62N0-018S-3380-00000-00&context=1519360 00 Kobe Bryant HYPERLINK https://advance.lexis.com/api/document?collection=news&id=urn:contentItem:4BBN-62N0-018S-3380-00000-00&context=1519360 00 shocks sports world Hamilton Spectator (Ontario, Canada) December 30, 2003 Tuesday Final Edition Bookmark_108 Bookmark_108

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant winks, and everybody's in. It's his left eye, usually. And it is disarming. He winks, he grins, and you're in. So when he takes the basketball along the baseline, three-point line to rim, in about three-quarters of a second and rises up and everybody's screaming and he's descending, you knew it all along. You've been right there with him, your heart on a clumsy drum roll. Los Angeles Times Because he winked. He invited you along, asked you to feel it with him, to look down -- yes, down -- and see net, and in the eyes of the defender, desperation and surrender. He asked you to stand with him in that moment when all is still, when no one knows what the next move might be, least of all him. With him, you looked into the eyes of a man on his heels, 20 years of basketball in those eyes, more probably, but he hasn't the slightest notion of what the next second, two, three might bring. He only knows that it probably won't be kind to him, but that it'll be some other poor sap's problem tomorrow night, and if it has to be bad, it might as well be quick. And, then, you're beside that defender, the first move so startling, and then past him, on to the next, then the next, and they fall away so fast you can't remember their faces. The flashes from the cameras mounted to the backboard like barnacles on a hull crackle and every colour bleeds into the next until there is only light and dark and the shrill sound of 16,000 people at the very end of a breath that quit on them too abruptly. Then he lets you off. Kobe Bryant knows. He knows what it's like to have it all go faster than is entirely pleasant. He is 22, beginning his fifth NBA season, a championship immediately behind him, a career immediately ahead. Bryant walks with an elegant stride. He peers out from behind the coolest eyes. He winks. He knows. It's fast. But it's OK. He does this kind of thing every day, every night. "I'm very blessed to have a family that's caring and loving," he says. "I've found the woman I'm going to spend the rest of my life with at an early age. I'm very at peace with myself. There can be a lot of things going on around me. People talking. All this stuff going on, and I'll just sit in the middle, very calm. "Sometimes it feels like things are moving a million miles a second. You tend to get caught up. You might get a little frustrated. That's when you detach yourself from the situation. That's when you're back to your basic self." Those are the moments when Bryant retreats to the basketball court, where the things that matter are family and team and that's it. Or, to the deck in the Pacific Palisades, where the mountains grow from the right, the ocean lazes ahead, then Catalina and the pier, where his mind is cleared simply by the spectacle. In every season since he arrived from Lower Merion High School in suburban Philadelphia, Bryant has raised his averages in points, rebounds, assists and shooting. And, although that matters over the course of a season, put him on the deck off the back of his house, and the numbers wash away. "I think in learning from the past, learning from other successful people, some of them haven't been able to detach themselves," he says. "They become so driven and so focused that it actually hurts them. "There is a middle area. There is a point where you can be driven so that it gets you the ultimate goal and beyond, and you can still detach yourself and still have a personal life, and not letting it get too mixed up. That's what's fun about doing it, because it's a constant challenge trying to measure out the two. "When I can, I've found that my life will bring me more success than basketball . My basketball will bring me more success with my life. It's about being in the present." Bryant appears to be changing. Always unusually adult and famously guarded, he has begun to let people in. There is the wink, often to total strangers. There is Vanessa Laine, the recent high-school graduate whom he will marry, he says, during this basketball season. They live together in Pacific Palisades. And, Bryant says, he is ready to lead this basketball team, because he thinks he's earned it. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Shaquille O'Neal and Kobe Bryant , the best players on the NBA's best team, have decided that sharing is a bad idea. Eddie Griffin, the best freshman in college basketball , punched a Seton Hall teammate after a game for the sin of not sharing. The Denver Nuggets, not among the NBA's best teams, boycotted practice because they decided their coach was being a big meanie.CONSTITUTION Question: Why do big-time basketball players act like spoiled children? Answer: Because most every big-time basketball player was spoiled, if not in childhood then surely in adolescence. We on the periphery tend to lump all famous athletes under the same heading, but there are levels within. Football players learn early that they must work within a system if anyone is to profit, and a baseball pitcher, no matter how much Greg Maddux would prefer it, cannot throw to himself. Only in basketball is selfishness elevated to an art form, for only basketball hews to the doctrine that One Guy Can Change Everything. Kansas hired Danny Manning's dad as an assistant coach, signed Danny Manning and won the NCAA title. The San Antonio Spurs won the lottery, drafted Tim Duncan and won a championship. One guy changed everything. A similar hope exists within every front office, within every college program. The right draftee or the right recruit will turn a loser into a winner, or a winner into a champion. Only in basketball are the whims of a player tantamount to policy decrees. Only in basketball can Magic Johnson, player, fire Paul Westhead, coach. Only in basketball is the path to glory scouted by sneaker salesmen and AAU coaches and street agents who whisper to high school juniors that college isn't necessary. Only in basketball is an adolescent buffeted with so much stimulation and temptation. Only in basketball does the recruiting begin before a player is old enough to shave. On the day Tony Akins signed with Georgia Tech, Berkmar coach David Boyd thanked him for staying at the Gwinnett school. Staying? Where else would Akins have gone? "St. John wanted me last year," he said. "Oak Hill wanted me before my junior year. Mt. Zion wanted me before my sophomore year." These are nationally known prep schools. And Akins, lest we forget, wasn't one of the nation's top 20 recruits. O'Neal and Bryant aren't even among the most self-indulgent of their generation, but they cannot divorce themselves from their I-before-we times. Players play. Players score. Players win. The coach sits over there and calls the occasional timeout. The team is a byproduct not of personal sacrifice but of individual brilliance. And if you try to convince a big-time player otherwise --- well, there are agents and marketing reps and image consultants standing by, cell phones at the ready, to shout you down. "Sandbox stuff" is what Phil Jackson, the Lakers' titular coach, calls the Shaq-Kobe tiff. But to expect anything different is to ignore the process. Big-time basketball players don't spoil themselves. The system spoils them. The wonder isn't that so many fall victim to it. The wonder is that anyone ever escapes. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 With apologies to the Eastern Conference, the NBA playoff matchup most everyone wants to see is finally upon us. The Western Conference's big boys -- the defending champion Los Angeles Lakers and the 1999 winners, the San Antonio Spurs -- begin their best-of-seven match of wits, styles and stars Saturday at the Alamodome. It has all the anticipation, intrigue and subplots of a Hollywood blockbuster. The Spurs had the league's best record during the regular season and have the home-court advantage for as long as they last in the playoffs. The Lakers tied the Philadelphia 76ers for the league's second-best record and haven't lost a game since April 1 -- that's a 15-game winning streak, seven in the playoffs. What makes the matchup even more appealing than Lakers stars Kobe Bryant and Shaquille O'Neal going against Spurs stars Tim Duncan and David Robinson is the extremes of the teams and their cities in so many areas. Whatever scale is used, the Lakers and Spurs are seemingly at opposite ends. It's the Lakers' glitz against the Spurs' grit, the Lakers' flair against the Spurs' no-frills approach. Every bit of Lakers business is fodder for tabloids and sports talk shows, and all of their laundry is aired out almost daily. The Spurs keep all of their business in-house; if they have internal conflicts, no one outside their family hears about them. As Miami coach Pat Riley says, "The Spurs are the most emotionally stable team in the league. They don't get caught up in anything else." They are caught up now in one of the NBA's most eagerly anticipated playoff series in recent seasons. Here's a side-by-side comparison: Team origins Lakers Joined NBA as Minneapolis Lakers in the league's second season Spurs Joined American Basketball Association as Dallas Chaparrals Celebrity courtside regulars Lakers Movie stars Jack Nicholson, Denzel Washington and Dyan Cannon, director Penny Marshall Spurs Country music star George Strait, former junior lightweight boxing champ Jesse James Leija, Vikings owner and Spurs former owner Red McCombs Price of courtside seat Laker $ 1,350 Spurs $ 400 Fans' beverage of choice Lakers Chardonnay Spurs Lone Star The coach Lakers Zen master, psychology guru and former hippie Phil Jackson Spurs Air Force Academy graduate with a degree in Soviet studies Gregg Popovich "Coachspeak" Lakers "They play a 1-2-2 zone. Nobody calls it." Spurs "It's more of a 1-1-3, but it's pretty tricky." Home court Lakers Staples Center, an 18,997-seat palace that opened last year; also home court of Los Angeles Clippers Spurs The Alamodome holds 34,215 for basketball but is really a football stadium; new home, the SBC Center, will be ready for the 2002-03 season Former home court Lakers Fabulous Forum Spurs HemisFair Arena Homecoming Lakers Shaquille O'Neal graduated from Cole High in San Antonio Spurs Gregg Popovich's only other head coaching job was at Pomona-Pitzer Colleges in Claremont, Calif., near L.A.
