fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE high school coach stayed home Sunday night and was not sorry he did, though Kobe Bryant scored the late runner in the lane and the Lakers escaped with a 2-1 N.B.A. finals lead. Gregg Downer just couldn't be party to his city chanting vulgarities at one of its own, and his guy at that. He didn't need to have a compelling showdown between his hometown team and a special player Philadelphia suddenly and sadly loves to hate reduced to a contemptuous primal scream. "Nasty and personal and not good for my blood pressure," Downer called the treatment of Bryant at the First Union Center as the finals shifted east. He is only 38 and a product of a basketball -crazed city that can be as provincial as a hamlet. "I grew up in this league," Downer said yesterday in a quiet gymnasium, pointing to the Penncrest banner on the far wall, next to those of Upper Darby and Haverford. Eleven years ago, he returned to the Central League as coach at Lower Merion High, sitting handsomely amid tree-lined streets of brick colonial-style homes. Here, Downer would direct players much like himself, kids with modest basketball dreams, until an eighth grader relocated from Italy walked into the gym one day to scrimmage with the varsity. Soon Downer would turn wide-eyed to an associate and say, "The kid's a pro." As Kobe Bryant 's father was standing in the corner at 6 feet 10 inches and with a journeyman's career behind him, this was no staggering prophecy. And even if Downer sensed something special in the making, he could not have imagined how much so and how soon. The Lakers' stylish superstar began in a gymnasium a 20-minute drive from where his father, Joe, once played as a Sixer. By the time the son was gone, straight to the National Basketball Association, he'd scored more points than the Philadelphia legend Wilt Chamberlain and tripled the school scoring record, an achievement somewhat understated on a list of the school's leading scorers high on the gymnasium wall. "Alex Goodman . . . Ali Cohen . . . Kobe Bryant ." "There's not a lot else to show," Downer said, apologetically. A dusty trophy or two encased in the hall. A few clippings and keepsakes packed away in a cramped athletic office. It is as if, Downer said, "People want it to seem like he was one of the many who passed through here." This was more the result of the school's commitment to academics than anything else. Bryant was an excellent student who could have gone to Duke. No one, Downer hopes, will object to the inevitable retiring of his No. 33. "It's not like people aren't proud," Downer said, while admitting the series has inflamed everyone's emotions and twisted old ties. Kobe's on-court mannerisms are arrogant, he hears around school. Kobe's not the fighter Allen Iverson is. He's turned his back on Philadelphia and become a pampered Hollywood star. Downer absorbs this at school and around town and he wants to scream, "Do you understand this kid is only 22?" He would be a rookie now had he gone four years to college. Philadelphia might also want to remember that the 76ers could have drafted Bryant and instead took Iverson with the first pick in 1996. Downer almost called Pat Croce that spring to say, "Your guy is right here." They should have known for themselves after he'd scrimmaged with the then-woeful 76ers and put on a show. Downer laughs ruefully at the L.A.-soft stereotype, as if "Kobe isn't one of the top five guys in the N.B.A. at sticking a knife in your heart." God only knows what Phil Jackson was talking about during the Lakers' descent into dysfunction earlier this season, saying he'd heard Bryant used to sabotage high school games to pad his statistics. "I was fuming about that," Downer said. If Jackson had asked, he would have told him how Bryant, two days before the 1996 state title game against Chester, had had his nose shattered at practice by a 5-foot-4-inch bench player diving for a loose ball. "Wear a mask," the doctor ordered. Bryant tore it off and rang up 39 points as Lower Merion reversed a 27-point defeat from the previous year. "That was pure Kobe," he said. By then, Lower Merion's games were a circus of scouts, which Downer had seen coming and tried to prepare the school for. "It was still overwhelming for everyone," he said. "The only one who never seemed flustered by it was Kobe. He just played harder." The First Union fans may want to consider this before they begin chanting in Game 4. headline: BRYANT'S OLD COACH SAW HIS TALENT RIGHT AWAY
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 * When he was 13, it was obvious his skill level was amazing. The tree-lined streets and landscaped lawns identify this quiet community as a typical American suburb. In the middle of it stands Lower Merion High, where Gregg Downer coaches basketball and Kobe Bryant once played. Downer grew up playing ball in the area, an educator who enjoys explaining dribbles and dunks to kids. One day, fate dropped an eighth-grader with a special talent into his midst. Kobe Bryant had spent eight years in Italy where his father was closing out a pro career that had taken him all over the basketball map. When it was time to come home, the family settled on the outskirts of Philadelphia, where Joe "Jellybean" Bryant had played for the 76ers. Bryant's youngest child, his only son, drew attention immediately; Downer heard there was a special player in the neighborhood. "There was a buzz about a 13-year-old who was playing on the junior high eighth grade team," Downer said. "I went to see him once. I do that rarely. I was so impressed that I invited him to practice with our varsity. After five minutes, I turned to somebody and said, 'That kid's a pro.'" There was a continental flare about the youngster, who spoke fluent Italian and played fluid basketball . He could drive and he could dunk. He could rebound and he could pass. He had all the tools. "I knew right away I had something unbelievable, something special," Downer said. "He was 6-2 and 140 pounds. Physically, he was not very mature, but there was a fundamental foundation to his play. "And there was his father, Jellybean, standing in a corner. He was 6-10. I had an idea what the genetic equation was." Lower Merion had been home to some good players over the years. Their names are on the gymnasium wall, six members of the school's 1,000-point club, from Mitch McDaniel in 1963 to Carli Brown in 1994. Next to them, in a column all his own, is Kobe Bryant , who not only broke the school scoring record but nearly tripled it with a career total of 2,883 points. When he was through, Bryant had scored more points than anybody in Philadelphia high school history, and this, remember, is a city where Wilt Chamberlain grew up. "He was a hard-nosed player," Downer said. And a broken-nosed one, too. Downer remembers the 1996 state championship game against Chester. Br yant's nose was rearranged the day before the semifinal game in a loose ball collision during practice. There was talk about him wearing a protective mask but Bryant refused. He scored 39 points in the title game and Lower Merion won its first state championship in 53 years. That was a dramatic turnaround from Bryant's freshman season when the team went 4-20, and the district finals in his junior year when Chester beat Lower Merion by 27. Downer remembers Bryant's drive. "He had a hatred of losing like I've never seen," the coach said. "He'd chase kids around if he thought they were responsible for losing a drill." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 ARDMORE, PA. -- The tree-lined streets and landscaped lawns identify this quiet community as a typical American suburb. In the middle of it stands Lower Merion High School, where Gregg Downer coaches basketball and Kobe Bryant once played. Downer grew up playing ball in the area, an educator who enjoys explaining dribbles and dunks to kids. One day, fate dropped an eighth-grader with a special talent into his midst. Associated Press Kobe Bryant had spent eight years in Italy where his father was closing out a pro career that had taken him all over the basketball map. When it was time to come home, the family settled on the outskirts of Philadelphia, where Joe (Jellybean) Bryant had played for the 76ers. Bryant's youngest child, his only son, drew attention immediately; Downer heard there was a special player in the neighbourhood. "There was a buzz about a 13-year-old who was playing on the junior high eighth-grade team," Downer said. "I went to see him once. I do that rarely. I was so impressed that I invited him to practise with our varsity. After five minutes, I turned to somebody and said, 'That kid's a pro."' There was a continental flare about the youngster, who spoke fluent Italian and played fluid basketball . He could drive and he could dunk. He could rebound and he could pass. He had all the tools. "I knew right away I had something unbelievable, something special," Downer said. "He was 6-2 and 140 pounds. Physically, he was not very mature, but there was a fundamental foundation to his play. "And there was his father, Jellybean, standing in a corner. He was 6-10. I had an idea what the genetic equation was." Lower Merion had been home to some good players over the years. Their names are on the gymnasium wall, six members of the school's 1,000-point club, from Mitch McDaniel in 1963 to Carli Brown in 1994. Next to them, in a column all his own, is Kobe Bryant , who not only broke the school scoring record but nearly tripled it with a career total of 2,883 points. When he was through, Bryant had scored more points than anybody in Philadelphia high school history, and this, remember, is a city where Wilt Chamberlain grew up. "He was a hard-nosed player," Downer said. And a broken-nosed one, too. Downer remembers the 1996 state championship game against Chester. Bryant's nose was rearranged the day before the semifinal game in a loose ball collision during practice. There was talk about him wearing a protective mask but Bryant refused. He scored 39 points in the title game and Lower Merion won its first state championship in 53 years. That was a dramatic turnaround from Bryant's freshman season when the team went 4-20, and the district finals in his junior year when Chester beat Lower Merion by 27. Downer remembers Bryant's drive. "He had a hatred of losing like I've never seen," the coach said. "He'd chase kids around if he thought they were responsible for losing a drill." By the time he was a senior, the word was out about Bryant. Lower Merion became a magnet for college recruiters. In a school where academics are important and college is the logical next step, the multi-lingual Bryant did well. But his agenda did not include college. He was headed for what Downer had seen as his destiny all along. He declared for the NBA draft. "I was neutral about that," Downer said. "I knew he'd be a good pro in time. I didn't know how quickly, how mentally healthy it is for an 18-year-old to hang out in an adult world." Pro scouts viewed him as the top high school player in the country but there is always some trepidation about drafting teenagers. This teenager was special, though. "He was ready," Downer said. Philadelphia owned the first pick and the Sixers knew all about the kid from Lower Merion. They opted instead for Allen Iverson. Bryant went to Charlotte with the 13th pick and immediately was traded to the Los Angeles Lakers for Vlade Divac. There had been rumours of the trade even before the draft. There also was talk of New Jersey picking Bryant. The Nets decided on Kerry Kittles instead and Bryant wound up with the Lakers. He handled all that turmoil with admirable maturity. "Anybody that knows me knows I'll play anywhere," he said. "I don't care where it is. I'll even play here in Philly." No one can quibble with the 76ers pick. Iverson, after all, was the rookie of the year that season, has twice led the league in scoring and is this season's MVP. But Downer thinks they would have done just as well with the kid from his gym, the one who, at 22, already owns an NBA championship ring and is one win away from getting another, right here in his old backyard. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 More than a half-million fans celebrated the Los Angeles Lakers' second straight NBA championship Monday as Shaquille O'Neal and Kobe Bryant promised to do it again next year. "Three-peat, three-peat, three-peat," rap master Shaq exhorted to a purple-and-gold throng massed outside Staples Center in a rousing hip-hop performance that capped the festivities. Windsor Star News Services Bryant put it another way: "We're going to get another one next year, again -- back to back to back." Baking heat couldn't keep fans from flocking downtown to celebrate the Lakers' out-of-town victory over the Eastern Conference-champion Phil-adelphia 76ers. About 550,000 people turned out for the parade and rally, police spokesman Lieut. Horace Frank said. A TV vehicle was vandalized, but there were no major problems, he said. Paramedics treated 95 people, mostly for heat-related problems, Fire Department spokesman Brian Humphrey said. The most serious injury was a leg fracture suffered by a 43-year-old woman who was hit by a car near the parade route. On stage, Bryant squirted teammates with a giant water gun. "Man, it's hot out here, but my brothers and I just had to be here," said Julio Rodriguez, 13, of Los Angeles. "What a lot of people. What a lot of fun." Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Laker Kobe Bryant , left, holds a water gun as Bryant and Brian Shaw dance back-to-back Monday during the Lakers' victory celebration at Staples Center in Los Angeles. The Lakers defeated the Philadelphia 76ers to win their second consecutive NBA championship. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Los Angeles Lakers superstar Kobe Bryant nearly sparked a riot yesterday in China, where about 1,500 fans of the basketball hero got mad when he didn't make it to a scheduled appearance. Bryant, a key player in the Lakers' back-to-back championship seasons, has taken China by storm during a visit there, fans treating him more like a rock superstar than an NBA star. When the 22-year-old got stuck in traffic caused by his own event yesterday, a crowd that had waited 1 1/2 hours under the blistering Beijing sun began to grow agitated and restless. Some hurled bottles, pushed barricades and criticized the event sponsor, Adidas. Police dragged off one offender by his hair and quickly dispersed the crowd, although it wasn't immediately clear whether anyone was detained. Beijing police would not confirm whether arrests were made, and Adidas said it knew of none. Bryant's scheduled appearance at the Millennium Monument in Beijing was then abruptly canceled - with authorities saying the fanfare outside the venue caused so much congestion they couldn't allow his car to enter. "The police said there were too many fans," said a spokeswoman for Adidas, which invited Bryant to China to meet young basketball players. The slam-dunking guard blamed the unrest on "circumstances that were out of my control." "I was informed that the conditions around the venue [where] I was to appear were unsafe for my fans," Bryant told a cheering crowd at another event last night. "I so badly wanted to meet those fans that waited with such loyalty and patience." Chinese fans have fallen in love with Bryant, taking to calling him "Ke bi" at other jam-packed events. During a children's basketball clinic Saturday, Bryant became the first known American to do a reverse slam dunk at the Great Wall. "It's an experience that's very difficult to capture in words," Bryant said. The Great Wall event was so spectacular to the Chinese that the Guangzhou Daily News put Bryant on the front of its sports page yesterday with the headline "Kobe's Big Great Wall Slam Dunk." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Traffic and surging crowds prevented Kobe Bryant from showing up for a promotional appearance in Beijing, China. Fans who had been waiting hours in blistering temperatures grew agitated when the Los Angeles Lakers guard was more than 1 1/2 hours late to arrive at the Millennium Monument in western Beijing. Windsor Star News ServicesSome hurled bottles, pushed at barricades and criticized Adidas, the sponsor. Police quickly dispersed the crowd of about 1,500, though it wasn't immediately clear if anyone was detained. The disturbance caused so much congestion that authorities barred Bryant's car from entering, Adidas representatives said. "I was informed that the conditions around the venue I was to appear at were unsafe for my fans," Bryant told cheering fans Sunday night at another appearance. Fever 83 Shock 66 Rita Williams scored 20 points as Indiana beat visiting Detroit for its first season series sweep since joining the WNBA last year. With one game to go, the Fever (10-21) topped their expansion season total of nine victories. The Shock fell to 9-22, their worst record in four seasons in the league. Williams, who also had 10 assists, scored 12 points in the first half, when Indiana led by as many as 10. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 If this week s display of enthusiasm in Beijing for NBA star Kobe Bryant (left) is anything to go by, God help the next version of the American Dream Team by the time the 2008 Olympics rolls around. Bryant labelled his visit to the Great Wall of China as one of the most memorable events of his life. But some fans felt otherwise when the NBA star didn t show for an appearance in the city yesterday. Fans who had been waiting for hours in blistering heat grew agitated when the Los Angeles Lakers guard was more than 90 minutes late for a gig at the Millennium Monument in western Beijing. Some hurled bottles at organisers, pushed at barricades and criticised sponsor, adidas. Police dragged off one offender by his hair and quickly dispersed the crowd of about 1500. The event was abruptly cancelled, apparently because the fanfare caused so much traffic that authorities wouldn t allow Bryant s car to enter. " Kobe Bryant was not allowed to come to the site by China police. He was blocked on the way," said a spokeswoman, adding she did not know of any arrests. She said Bryant s appearance may be rescheduled for tomorrow. Earlier, at a news conference, Bryant rhapsodised about playing hoops at the Great Wall. "It s an experience that s very difficult to capture in words," he said. The 22-year-old Bryant, whom Chinese fans call 'Ke Bi," also joined kids at a basketball clinic near the Great Wall, north of Beijing. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The two-time defending NBA champion Los Angeles Lakers, matching their franchise-best 12-1 start from 1997, suddenly look even stronger. Derek Fisher returned from offseason foot surgery and scored 14 points, 11 in the fourth quarter, as the Lakers beat the visiting Denver Nuggets 105-98 Sunday night. Forward Mark Madsen, who had wrist surgery, also was activated earlier in the day, although he did not score in what was his first appearance of the season. Fisher hit his first shot, a 3-pointer in the first quarter after he'd been on the floor just 57 seconds, and finished four of four from 3-point range. He made three in the fourth quarter, big baskets for the Lakers, who led by up to 16 points late in the first half but fell behind in the third quarter. Los Angeles broke away from a 96-96 tie by outscoring the Nuggets 9-2 in the final minute. Fisher's teammates were glad to have him back. "I told him I started having some flashbacks about penetrating and kicking it back out to him for those 3's," said Kobe Bryant , who had 25 points, seven assists and seven rebounds after missing the morning shootaround because of an upper respiratory infection. Fisher played 20 minutes and finished five of eight from the floor, with three rebounds and one assist. "I didn't have many expectations, in terms of how I would play," he said. "I really felt kind of awkward when I first got out there, but things came together for me." Kobe Bryant was fined $7,500 by the NBA on Monday for verbally abusing a game official and failing to leave the court promptly following his ejection. Bryant was ejected with 8:04 remaining in the Los Angeles Lakers' 106-90 victory over Golden State at Staples Center on Friday. The fine was announced Monday by Stu Jackson, NBA senior vice president of basketball operations. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KOBE BRYANT was the villain in his hometown last June. He asked for no favors and gave none. He spoke about "cutting out the hearts" of the 76ers after Game 3 of the NBA Finals. And then he did. He did not feel badly about winning the NBA title for the Los Angeles Lakers against his hometown team. Nor should he have felt badly. He did not even mind getting booed. He understood. Bryant, however, did not want to hear that he had forgotten where he came from. So he arranged to call Daily News columnist John Smallwood at home to explain that he would always be a Philadelphia basketball player, that he had not lost his roots. When Bryant came home two weeks ago to play the Sixers, he was honored the night before the game at Lower Merion High, where his No. 33 jersey was retired. "It's always good to come home," Bryant said. "I usually stop by the gym whenever I come home to see if it smells the same." Kobe will be home again this weekend. He will start for the Western Conference in Sunday's NBA All-Star Game at the First Union Center. "I won't be beating the Sixers or anything like that," he said after the Sixers beat the Lakers on Jan. 27. "I'll just come in here as a basketball player, showcasing my talent, along with the other players. Hopefully, I can bring back some of that Philly flavor, being that we're not going to run the triangle offense. I might be able to go between my legs and around my back a couple times." The NBA is, first and foremost, a show. And Kobe is one of its greatest entertainers. He understands what sells. He also knows the scoreboard is there for a reason. It's the rare player who can entertain and win. Kobe Bryant is one of those players. He never went to college. "Lower Merion is the closest thing to it," Bryant said. "That's my university." In his last year at that "university," his team became a happening so big that Montgomery Avenue often was clogged before and after Aces games. Getting in the doors was always problematic. "Will there ever be another Kobe Bryant ?" Lower Merion coach Gregg Downer asked. "The answer is no." People forget now, but Bryant was in uncertain territory when he declared for the draft. Kevin Garnett had done the high-school-to-the-NBA thing in 1995, but it was still sort of novel. It had been so many years since Moses Malone, Darrell Dawkins and Bill Willoughby did it that nobody really thought about it. Garnett thought about and did it. So did Bryant. There were doubters, including many in his hometown. Who is this kid? What is he up to? Why is he wearing shades on his bald head at the press conference to announce that "he was taking his talents to the NBA?" Words like arrogance were tossed around freely. Was it fair? Probably not. Bryant was just a teenager who wanted to play basketball . The best league in the world was interested in him. It seemed only natural that he play in the NBA. So he did, being drafted by Charlotte 13th overall and dealt to the Lakers a couple of weeks later. He became the youngest ever to play in an NBA game when he took the court against Minnesota on Nov. 3, 1996. He was 18 years, 2 months, 11 days old. Bryant needed a few years to become a major star. Many were impatient, unwilling to let the teenager become a man. Well, Bryant is a man now. He is married. He has houses. He does commercials. He already has won two NBA titles. He is clearly one of the world's best players. Sunday, his hometown might even find it possible to welcome him. Winning and losing won't be all that relevant. It will be all about the show. Kobe did show at Lower Merion. He does it now in arenas all around the country. "The All-Star Game is having a good time," Bryant said. "It's all about enjoying the moment. Being from Philadelphia, it's definitely going to be nice to come back home and play. "It's going to be easy. I'm looking forward to it. It's going to be easy for me to be in Philadelphia and just play. I'm going to be around family and friends. I'll be able to enjoy myself." The 2001 Finals were not really a joy for Bryant. It was strictly business. Teammate Rick Fox understood the attitude. It's that attitude that separates players with great talent from players with great records. "I don't think everybody in Philadelphia is happy to see him, especially after the way he went about his business last year in the Finals, focusing on the task at hand as opposed to treating it as a homecoming," Fox said. Bryant played in the suburbs, so he's not, in the truest sense, a Philadelphia player. But he honed his skills in the city's summer leagues. He always had a city game. He wants everybody to know this is where he really learned how to play. In a town that loves its basketball , Kobe Bryant deserves a special place on the wall of memories. He won a state title for Lower Merion in 1996 and NBA titles for Los Angeles in 2000 and 2001. That first pro title came in the yea

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 During the NBA All-Star Game in Philadelphia on Feb. 10, the fans there proved to the world that "The City of Brotherly Love" is a misnomer. Only Philadelphians would boo one of their own -- in this case, Los Angeles Lakers guard Kobe Bryant ("Kobe not of good cheer: Hometown Philly fans boo All-Star MVP in 135-120 West win," Sports, Monday). Bryant is a magnificent talent. He gives the crowd what it pays for -- a fantastic display of basketball prowess. This is a young man with strong ties to Philadelphia: His father was a player for the NBA's Philadelphia 76ers, and Kobe grew up in that city. Some may view him as arrogant for choosing to turn pro instead of attending college. But how many, if faced with the same option, would not make the same decision? Bryant may be a player for the Los Angeles Lakers, a team that is a fierce rival of the local 76ers, but he was not in Philadelphia on Feb. 10 as a player for the Lakers. He was there as an NBA All-Star. When interviewed after the game, Bryant seemed somewhat puzzled by the boos, and one could see that he was hurt. At a time when the world is focused on the Olympics in Salt Lake City, Philadelphia displayed to the world that it is a city of poor sports. It was an appalling display at a time when we should be reaching out to each other. Judy Dolan Reading, Pa. Bryant's fault: He's too good As a fan of the NBA, the Los Angeles Lakers and Kobe Bryant , I cannot begin to describe how saddened and sickened I was by one of the rudest displays from fans I have ever seen. I would like to know what Bryant has done to his hometown of Philadelphia that makes its fans show so much lack of respect toward him? The only things I can possibly think of are: * Going straight from high school to the NBA. * Joining the ranks of a legendary winning team. * Being compared to basketball legend Michael Jordan. * Winning two NBA championship rings. * Being named MVP of the NBA All-Star Game. Shame on Bryant for succeeding! I would think that fans of any sport would have more appreciation for someone of such caliber. He is considered a great now and is still in his prime. Just think of what this young man will have to offer within the next five years. Even after the horrible behavior of Philadelphia fans, Bryant continues to say nothing but kind words about that city. This kind of conduct only makes me appreciate him more as a person and as a player. Felecia C. Moore Hattiesburg, Miss. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Thursday is the 25th anniversary of the McDonald's All American Boys Game. Some of basketball 's greats played in the game, among them Magic Johnson, Michael Jordan, Shaquille O'Neal and Kobe Bryant .Five memorable moments: * 1979, Charlotte: Perhaps the greatest high school senior class of all time gathers for the second annual game. Players include Isiah Thomas, James Worthy, Dominique Wilkins and Ralph Sampson. * 1989, Kansas City, Mo.: Shaquille O'Neal has 18 points, 16 rebounds and six blocks in the East's 112-103 victory. Shaq, who shares MVP with Bobby Hurley, has a monster dunk at the end of a coast-to-coast rebound and sprint. * 1991, Springfield, Mass.: Played at the birthplace of basketball as part of its centennial celebration, the world gets its first look at the potential of Michigan's "Fab Five" as four members -- Chris Webber, Juwan Howard, Jalen Rose and Jimmy King -- take the court together for the first time. * 1999, Ames, Iowa: Another high schooler headed to the NBA, Jonathan Bender of Picayune, Miss., who will be selected by the Indiana Pacers, wins MVP honors by breaking Jordan's 18-year-old record with 31 points. * 1996, Pittsburgh: Kobe Bryant has 13 points and three assists, but the East's 120-105 victory features 27 dunks and perhaps the fastest-paced, highest-flying action of any of the games. The jaw-dropper is MVP Shaheen Holloway's fast-break, between-the-legs bounce pass to Vassil Evtimov, who finishes with a thunderous dunk. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Shaquille O'Neal, returning from a wrist injury that sidelined him for two games, scored 40 points Sunday to lead the Los Angeles Lakers to a 96-88 victory over the Miami Heat. The Heat lost for the eighth time in 10 games and moved closer to elimination from the playoff race. The Lakers, who have five games remaining, moved a game ahead of third-place Dallas in the Western Conference. Windsor Star News Services O'Neal made 14 of 22 shots, grabbed 11 rebounds in 41 minutes and reached 40 points for the sixth time this season when he sank a free throw with 31 seconds left. His baskets included a fast-break layup when Robert Horry fed him with a behind-the-back pass. The Lakers lost both games while O'Neal was sidelined, scoring 81 points and shooting 34 per cent Friday against Boston, both season lows. Kobe Bryant , who missed a morning shootaround with flulike symptoms, had 19 points and 11 rebounds in 38 minutes. Rod Strickland led the Heat with 21 points and nine assists. Valiants champs The Windsor Valiants downed Hamilton Blessed Sacrament 34-31 to win the Ontario Basketball Association Division II championship on the weekend in Kitchener. Windsor's Jillian Kiviosoto was named tournament MVP. The Valiants posted a 6-0 mark in the tournament. Wooden Award Jason Williams of Duke, last year's runner-up, won the 26th John R. Wooden Award Sunday as U.S. college basketball 's top male player. "Wow. This is a great award," Williams said, accepting the trophy from Wooden in a presentation at the Los Angeles Athletic Club. "This one just tops them all. Coach Wooden represents everything I've always stood for -- pride, integrity and trust." Williams will graduate in May with a sociology degree in just three years. He is the third Duke player in four years to win. Shane Battier beat out Williams last year and Elton Brand won in 1999. The Lakers won 96-88. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LOS ANGELES -- Kobe Bryant was just 17 when he arrived in Los Angeles, a city that couldn't wait for him to grow up and just maybe help Shaquille O'Neal forge another Lakers dynasty. Now Bryant is all of 23, and what's that on his fingers? Right, two championship rings with perhaps a third soon on the way. If Bryant didn't play another game in L.A. -- though he figures to play many, many more -- the City of Angels would sing his praises for a long time. At Lower Merion High School in Ardmore, Pa., they know the feeling. In Bryant's freshman season, the team was a pushover. His senior year, 1996, the Aces were state champions. "That's my most lasting memory from high school -- winning the state championship," Bryant says. "The high school hadn't won it in over 50 years. So it was big, really big, especially going from 4-20 my freshman year to winning the state title." The turnaround he led at Lower Merion brought attention. As did the fact that he was the son of Joe "Jelly Bean" Bryant, who played eight seasons in the NBA and later in Italy, where Kobe spent eight years of his childhood. Mostly, though, what caused all the buzz was Bryant's jaw-dropping skills. "A complete player who dominates," says his high school coach, Gregg Downer. As a 6-6, 190-pounder his senior year, he averaged 30.8 points, 12 rebounds, 6.5 assists and 3.8 blocked shots in leading the Aces to a 31-3 record. Because of his accomplishments at Lower Merion, and partly because of all he has done in a six-year NBA career, Bryant has been chosen as the outstanding high school boys player of the last 20 years. Bryant skipped college, was taken 13th in the NBA draft by Charlotte and traded to L.A. for center Vlade Divac, a move that cleared the way for the Lakers to sign O'Neal. Those moves were the building blocks of a potentially long dynasty. Glory didn't come without adversity, though. Bryant's first season ended as he threw up air balls in overtime in a season-ending playoff loss in Utah. The next two seasons, the Lakers were swept in the playoffs by Utah and San Antonio. In 2000, Bryant's fourth season, the Lakers won their first NBA title since the Magic Johnson-fueled Showtime era, and Bryant's star status was cemented. Last season, however, Bryant's tendency to force his offense and ignore his teammates caused a rift between him and O'Neal. But in the playoffs, Bryant played an unselfish and superlative brand of basketball that helped the Lakers to a 15-1 waltz through the postseason. "There have been ups, and there have been downs," Bryant says of his six years since high school. "Good times, bad times. Surprises. But I'm very happy with where I am right now. You have to go through those periods. "It's those struggles and those good times and bad times you go through that determine the person you're going to become. And I wouldn't trade that for the world." Nor would he change the path he took, bypassing college. He concedes, however, that he has daydreamed about the NCAA career he might have had. "Sometimes, out of curiosity, I think about that. My mind starts drifting a little bit, wondering what I'd look like in a Duke uniform." No doubt Duke coach Mike Krzyzewski has thought about it, too. The Blue Devils failed to win an NCAA title during what would have been Bryant's college years. And, as Lower Merion High School and the Lakers have found out, Kobe Bryant and championships go together. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Wilt Chamberlain, Kareem Abdul Jabbar and Shaquille O'Neal all ended up playing for the L.A. Lakers. One plays with Jerry West, the other Magic Johnson, and now Kobe Bryant and O'Neal. Coincidence? Not hardly ("Lakers deny Kings crown," Sports, Monday). In the 1980s it was Jabbar and Johnson playing in eight NBA Finals. In the '90s it was Michael Jordan and the Chicago Bulls winning six rings. And now the current decade dynasty again appears to be the Lakers. The NBA needs its best players in big markets to sell its game. This is unlike the NFL, where the game sells itself. After watching Game 6 of this year's Western Conference Finals, I'm convinced, the Lakers are unbeatable. It's like watching the Harlem Globetrotters play the Washington Generals. Mark Pitassi Portland Ore. Real basketball ? In response to USA TODAY letter writer Harold Crawford, who complained that when NBA teams play zone defense, it's not "real" basketball : Harold, your first mistake was watching the NBA and expecting to see a real basketball game ("Zoned," Keeping Score, Sports, Tuesday). OK, it's not quite World Wrestling Entertainment, but Kobe Bryant takes on drives to the basket with the ball clinched in both hands. Laugh loudly and often at the either hopelessly pathetic or heavily biased refereeing. The NCAA has more than its share of problems, to be sure. But even though the zone defense you don't care for is a mainstay of the game, you're more likely to find a purer game of basketball between two average college teams than you'll ever find at an NBA game. NBA players are obviously the most talented players in the world. But instead of being about basketball games, the NBA is all about watching top stars put on exhibitions. Sure, I still watch at times, and will continue to do so. However, never do I expect anything more than an occasional flash of "real" basketball . I take it for what it is: a couple hours of entertainment -- awful refereeing and all. Brian R. Harris Beach Park, Ill. Instant replay says differently Would the outcome of the L.A. Lakers-Sacramento Kings playoff games have been the same if there was instant replay in the last minute of the fourth game in which L.A. Laker Robert Horry made a three-point winning goal? I think not. It was obvious to me that on the TV replay the ball was still in the palm of his hand when the whistle blew. When important games are played, instant replay should be part of the package. Too much is riding on these games to bypass available technology -- and increase the chance of human error -- during playoff games. John E. Palmeri Bradenton, Fla. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Los Angeles --- The Nets, down 0-2, are holding on to faint hope. They know only two teams in history have come from that deficit in the NBA finals, and they know the Lakers are the superior team. AJC "But we had a lot of fight left," forward Kenyon Martin said. "We showed that again when we got down that we kept battling, kept fighting." It would help if the Nets made some shots. They were just 35 percent from the field (30-for-86, 6-for-22 from 3-point range). " Basketball is about making shots, and we didn't," guard Jason Kidd (6-for-17) said. "We got plenty of them." Worse, Kidd said: "The Lakers probably didn't play their best, and they still held serve. If we can win Sunday [in Game 3], maybe we might give them a scare." Odds and ends Key reserve guard Lucious Harris was 0-for-9 Friday and is 0-for-14 for the finals. . . . The Nets had 20 offensive rebounds. . . . Rick Fox had another solid game with 10 points, 8 rebounds and 6 assists. If the Lakers dispense of the Nets next week, Fox, a part-time actor, likely will keep a part in a movie. Praise for Bryant Kobe Bryant , subject of much criticism from coach Phil Jackson over the years, now is the object of much praise. And not just for his remarkable skill and production, either. Said Jackson: "This year, Kobe's leadership had a major impact on this team. His game-to-game demeanor . . . he wanted to play all 82 games this year. He was the guy that was there every night for us, pushing the team, driving the team. That influence was tremendous, especially for some of the young players. . . . We had a number of games without Shaquille [O'Neal] where Kobe had to carry that torch for the champions." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 National Basketball Association fans have been busy dissing each other during the playoffs. But NBA.com has offered a whole new way to brag.The Ottawa CitizenThe site has been getting record hits since it added an e-mail feature in April that gives fans a novel way to rub in a team's loss: sending NBA photos to friends and family with a told-you-so message attached. Fans have sent more than 350,000 picture e-mails so far, and the NBA expects another 100,000 to have been sent during the NBA Finals. The photo feature has increased NBA.com's traffic, helping the site break its record seven times since the playoffs started, says Brenda Spoonemore, the NBA's vice president of interactive services. The in-your-face e-mails tend to be sent in the heat of a close game and in the emotional aftermath. "We see spikes generally starting at the middle of the fourth quarter to about an hour after the game," Spoonemore says. The most-popular photo is one of the Sacramento Kings' bobble-head dolls. But that is changing with the Kings' elimination Friday at the hands of their intrastate rival, the Los Angeles Lakers. Pictures of Lakers' star Kobe Bryant (shown here) already hold three of the top five spots. Another of the site's features allows fans to control robotic cameras during the games and to take pictures, which they can then e-mail to friends. "They can take shots of the guys in the huddle or of celebrity row," Spoonemore says. "The dance squads seem to be really popular." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NBA REFEREEING controversy strikes again. Dee Kantner, one of two female officials on the 59-member crew, was quietly canned last week. Kantner's whistle blowing a foul can be heard all the way from her home in Charlotte. "I'm completely stunned," she told the Associated Press. "I had no idea I was in trouble. I would have expected management to have said something, yet there was nothing in any of my reviews. I am completely blown away." Kantner was widely recognized as the best referee in women's college basketball when she was hired by the NBA 5 years ago. She questions the validity of the league's season-ending referee ratings that place her last, among other things. NBA vice president Stu Jackson and officiating supervisor Ed Rush contend that Kantner failed to progress sufficiently in her 5 seasons. The other female referee, Violet Palmer, remains employed by the NBA. Kantner's firing has sparked criticism from several NBA general managers and coaches who respected her work, personality and professionalism. Of course, the praise was anonymous. One coach said that he could name 10 men who were worse and more deserving of being fired. This could really get ugly should Kantner file a lawsuit. Several coaches have said they would be in line to support her if that happens. Kantner, an engineer before her NBA career, said that she's "pursuing all options." Translation: See you in court, NBA. Shoeless Kobe Bryant Basketball superstar Kobe Bryant is now a free agent - for footwear endorsements. Adidas said in a joint statement with the Lakers' All-Star that their 6-year relationship ended this summer. Bryant said he will wear a variety of shoes next season while evaluating what should be his many opportunities. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant and Adidas-Salomon said yesterday that they had ended a six-year relationship by deciding not to extend Mr. Bryant's multimillion-dollar contract for two more years. The decision came after speculation about a possible parting of Mr. Bryant, the 23-year-old star of the Los Angeles Lakers, and Adidas footwear and apparel products after he wore an older-model sneaker during the most recent National Basketball Association playoffs rather than the current one. "We have reached a point where we see things differently, and we prefer to move on and seek new paths," said Nicole Vollebregt, a spokeswoman for the American operations of Adidas, based in Portland, Ore. A statement from Mr. Bryant, who also endorses brands like McDonald's, Spalding and Sprite, said that he would play next season in a variety of footwear brands while considering another endorsement deal. While an athlete of Mr. Bryant's caliber could be wooed by many apparel and footwear marketers, his image may be too closely tied to the Adidas label, said Steve Rosner, a partner at 16W Marketing in Rutherford, N.J., who worked at the SFX Sports Group, which now represents Mr. Bryant. "In the industry he is definitely known as an Adidas guy," Mr. Rosner said. "From the Kobe Bryant standpoint, I think it's going to be somewhat of a challenge to align himself with a company that is willing to market him." SFX is part of Clear Channel Entertainment. headline: OHIO PREP STANDOUT SET TO PLAY AT ARENA
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PREP FROM PAGE D-1 or at least not have to miss watching the Steelers on television. But we also have to make the game at a time when St. Vincent-St. Mary can get back home at a decent hour." Brian Chiera, director of marketing at Mellon, said, "For this event, we don't think a crowd of nine or 10 thousand would be out of the question." Huge crowds have come to watch "King James" in the past. Last season, a sellout crowd of more than 20,000 watched him play a game at Cleveland's Gund Arena. Ticket demand was so high that St. Vincent-St. Mary moved its games to JAR Arena on the campus of the University of Akron. St. Vincent-St. Mary also played George Jr. Republic of Grove City at Youngstown State University. The game was sold out (capacity 6,000) more than a week ahead of time. "The kid is a tremendous draw, no matter where he plays," said Bob Gibbons, a nationally known high school basketball talent scout from Lenoir, N.C. "People want to see the so-called next Kobe Bryant , the next Michael Jordan, the next great one that he's projected to be in professional basketball ." Some NBA scouts said James would have been an NBA lottery pick after his junior year at St. Vincent-St. Mary. He seems certain to be one of the first picks next year. "He will not go to college. There is no pretense that he will," Gibbons said. "It will be an interesting evening for Pittsburgh people to see him, I'm sure." Gibbons first saw James play at the USA Basketball Development Festival in Colorado Springs when James was a sophomore. "At that age, he was the most talented player I ever saw," Gibbons said. Nike and adidas already are in a bidding war to land James' services once he uses up his college eligibility. They are reportedly offering $20 million. Sonny Vaccaro, a well-known adidas representative who used to run the Dapper Dan Roundball Classic, has said James is the best high school player he has seen, and that includes Kobe Bryant , Kevin Garnett and Tracy McGrady. Gibbons said: "[James] went to the Nike basketball camp this summer and then came right to the adidas camp. He came to the adidas camp in Nike gear. He caused quite a stir." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 ATLANTA -- Kobe was good. Coke, as it turns out, was lucky. Back in 1996, when Kobe Bryant jumped from high school to the National Basketball Association's Los Angeles Lakers, Coca-Cola signed the promising player to an endorsement deal for Sprite. In the seven years since, Bryant has made himself one of the league's most popular players. He even was the highest vote-getter in balloting for the NBA All-Star Game on Sunday (Feb. 9) in Atlanta. As a business move, linking Sprite with Bryant was fortunate, especially for a struggling brand that has been trying to make a sales turnaround, partly via its close ties with the NBA. "We bet on the right horse," said Jeff Crow, senior manager of sports marketing for Coca-Cola North America. The approach of the All-Star Game is a reminder of how pro basketball remains a coveted marketing tool, even though sponsorship money is tight now and the league lacks the kind of larger-than-life star that Michael Jordan was in his prime. "It's still a very valuable property," said Rick Dudley, president and chief executive officer of Octagon Worldwide, a major sports and event marketing company. "It delivers a great audience." Bryant, for example, gleans an estimated $11 million to $13 million a year in endorsement deals, with Sprite being just one source of that income. Using sports celebrities as promoters, however, is partly a guessing game, requiring planning, skill and more than a little bit of luck. Bob Williams, president of Burns Sports & Celebrities, said Sprite's early accord with Bryant was an unusual move, given that he was an unproven player. "The risk was higher than your typical sports endorsement deal," Williams said. "It was a monetary risk, albeit a small one for a company the size of Coca-Cola." For businesses, dealing with sports celebrities can be tricky, especially if they're depending on the performance of a single player. Coke's Sprite hedges its bets by having deals with 14 NBAers, ranging from bonafide stars like Tim Duncan of the San Antonio Spurs and Bryant to lesser-known players like Jason Terry of the Atlanta Hawks. Some of Sprite's players are relatively low-profile in the United States but are used for promotions in other markets. Tony Parker, for example, is a Frenchman who plays for the San Antonio Spurs and is featured as a Sprite drinker in his home country. Robert Tuchman, president of Tuchman Sports & Entertainment, said the NBA's growing international flavor is one of the upsides to what will soon be a Jordanless league. Jordan is retiring this year. Linking a company logo to a hot player can help a brand. Among the best-known examples are Nike and Gatorade, which both signed Jordan and rode his fame. But for every Michael Jordan or Kobe Bryant , there also is a Kwame Brown. Brown, a high schooler who went straight to the NBA, plays for the Washington Wizards. The Georgia native -- of Glynn Academy in Brunswick, Ga. -- was the top draft pick in 2001 but has never come close to being an NBA star. Nevertheless, he still has a deal with Sprite. "You've got to roll the dice sometimes," Crow said. There are limits, however, in what a company will do. Some NBA players have an edgy image -- the retired Dennis Rodman comes to mind, among many others -- and companies often don't want an unpredictable spokesman. "We are an edgy brand," Crow said about Sprite. "But we are also owned by the Coca-Cola Co." Thus, it prefers to sign clean-cut players such as Bryant, Duncan and Orlando's Grant Hill. But there is a market for many kinds of players, including controversial figures such as Philadelphia's Allen Iverson. Jonathan Press, the NBA's vice president for marketing partnerships, said 70 NBA players have endorsement deals of some sort on a range of products, out of roughly 350 men in the league. There is a track record of success, too. For years, Sprite's NBA tie-in was a key factor in the brand's growth. The NBA was part of Sprite's repositioning in 1994 under the "Obey your thirst" theme. Coke successfully used the approach to attract an urban, African-American audience. Sprite's rise helped cement the brand as Coke's third-largest seller in the United States, behind Coke Classic and Diet Coke. Sprite's strong run has faded, however. Sales have slumped three years in a row. Coke is looking for ways to revive Sprite, including a possible line extension that could debut this summer. Nonetheless, Coke has no plans to shift away from the NBA when it comes to promoting Sprite, Crow said. Instead, the company plans to do more to tie the brand to pro basketball . Already, Coke has sold special cans and bottles with NBA graphics in the Atlanta area. The company will run a national TV ad. Locally, there are about 50 billboards touting Sprite. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 SEATTLE -- Ray Allen has a chip on his shoulder. He's still trying to prove he's more than just a shooter. "I've taken so much criticism coming out of college," Allen said after getting his second career triple-double as the Seattle SuperSonics snapped the Los Angeles Lakers' six-game winning streak with a 107-90 victory last night. "People try to say what you do and who you are," said Allen, a three-time All-Star who played at Connecticut. "I can just shoot the ball and I can just score. Having a triple-double is saying that you're doing everything." The Sonics won their fifth game in a row at home and improved to 4-1 since acquiring Allen last week in a big trade that saw Gary Payton sent to the Milwaukee Bucks. The Lakers had won 13 of their last 15 coming in. The Sonics' only loss since the trade came to the Lakers in Los Angeles on Sunday night. "Every night I like to rebound the ball and I like to pass the ball," said Allen, who, at 27, is seven years younger than Payton. "The stats prove it." Asked what he thought Milwaukee coach George Karl, who was critical of Allen's defense, would say when he saw Allen's line in the box score, Allen smiled and said: "George is going to say . . . 'He's rebounding the basketball ?' " Kobe Bryant scored 34 points on 10-for-27 shooting and Shaquille O'Neal had 18 points and 11 rebounds for the Lakers, who looked tired after beating Detroit in Los Angeles on Thursday night. Bryant said he thought he had the flu. "I feel terrible," Bryant said. "I think I've been going nonstop since before the All-Star break and it's just caught up with me. I've got aches and chills." Allen was particularly motivated to be matched against Bryant, generally regarded as one of the best players, if not the best, in the NBA. "So much is said about what Kobe Bryant is and what Kobe Bryant does," Allen said. "I don't know if a lot of people know it, but I was drafted No. 5 and he was drafted like No. 8 (actually 13th in 1996). Not that that means anything. But I think I've always ranked right there along with what Kobe has done." The Lakers were missing their head coach, Phil Jackson, for the third game in a row. Jackson hasn't completely recovered from having a kidney stone removed Monday. Assistant Jim Cleamons coached the Lakers in Jackson's absence. Predrag Drobnjak and Vladimir Radmanovic each scored 18 points for Seattle, which outrebounded the Lakers 57-33. "We just didn't box out," O'Neal said. The Sonics broke the game open in the third quarter, and took an 83-61 lead into the fourth. After Brent Barry made a 3-pointer and Drobnak hit a 16-footer, the Sonics led 67-51 with 7:38 left in the period. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The Shaq & Kobe show came to Atlanta and turned Philips Arena into Staples Center East on Tuesday. A Shaquille O'Neal dunk. Cheers. A Kobe Bryant turnaround jumper. More cheers. Jason Terry jumper. Silence. AJC The Los Angeles Lakers brought out the first purely basketball sellout of the season --- the March 9 number was more a credit to a postgame concert by gospel group Mary Mary. And most of the fans came to see the team pursuing a fourth championship in a row, not the team that's headed for its fourth draft lottery in a row. It wasn't exactly a star-studded affair like the All-Star Game, but this was the game on the schedule for most of these fans. The game to see and be seen --- in your coolest Lakers jersey. Fans painted the place purple and gold. O'Neal had five dunks in the first quarter and people erupted and rose to their feet every time Shaq rocked the rim. A fan offered pointers when Shaq couldn't. "Throw it down, big man," said Lakers fan Geoffrey Brooks, sporting a white Kobe Bryant jersey and doing his best imitation of ESPN commentator Bill Walton when O'Neal attempted a dunk that was blocked by the rim in the first quarter. The Hawks are 7-2 this season with crowds of 15,000 or more, so they were fired up. Reserve forward Darvin Ham was able to get some converts with a reverse baseline slam, where he took off from one side of the rim and jammed on the other. "When I was a player, I used to take it personal whenever fans for the Lakers or Boston came into our place," Hawks player development assistant Dominique Wilkins said before the game. "That used to fire me up." It was like a playoff game for the Hawks. It meant more to the team on the bottom than the guys on the top. It meant even more to fans like Brooks to see Shaq & Kobe up close. In the middle of the fourth quarter, after Bryant hit his second consecutive 3-pointer in Ira Newble's face, the Hawks, er, Los Angeles Lakers fans in the building began chanting, "MVP! MVP!" for Bryant. AJC "I've never heard that," Hawks point guard Jason Terry said. Neither has Kobe or the Lakers. The MVP chant is a staple in Staples, but never on the road. This was a first. "It's a weird feeling," Bryant said afterward, "a good feeling." Shaq & Kobe brought out the first purely basketball sellout of the season --- the March 8 number was more a credit to a postgame concert by gospel group Mary Mary. And most of the fans came to see the team pursuing a fourth consecutive championship, not the team that's headed for its fourth consecutive draft lottery. "Maybe the Hawks fans will be happy when they get back their money," Bryant said, laughing, after getting 28 points and 10 rebounds. It wasn't exactly a star-studded affair like the All-Star Game, but this was the game on the schedule for most of these fans. The game to see and be seen at --- in your coolest Lakers jersey. Fans painted the place purple and gold. O'Neal had five dunks in the first quarter, and people erupted every time he rocked the rim. One fan offered pointers when Shaq couldn't. "Throw it down, big man," Lakers fan Geoffrey Brooks, sporting a white Kobe Bryant jersey, said after O'Neal had a dunk blocked by the rim in the first quarter. But the big fella got it together, scoring 31 points in three quarters of work. The Hawks are 7-3 this season with crowds of 15,000 or more. But this night, there were 15,000 rooting against them. "If the Hawks get it together, I'm sure the city will support them," O'Neal said. "If I was here, it wouldn't be a problem." ajc.com/sports * Go online for a photo gallery from the Lakers-Hawks game. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE addition of NBA superstars Kobe Bryant and Allen Iverson to the US Olympic basketball team yesterday has put credibility back in the Dream Team concept. Bryant, a member of the three-time defending NBA champions Los Angeles Lakers and Iverson, the Philadelphia 76ers' controversial point guard, along with Jermaine O'Neal from the Indiana Pacers, were the last of the nine "core" members of the team picked by US Basketball . All of the core members will play the Americas qualifying tournament which will be held in Puerto Rico from August 20-31 and, should the US qualify, at the Athens Olympics in 2004. MATP American basketball stocks hit rock bottom after a disastrous world championships last year when they finished sixth and were forced to enter the qualifying tournament for the 2004 Games. Bryant yesterday scored 32 points with Shaquille O'Neal adding 27 points as the Lakers brushed aside the Minnesota Timberwolves 120-90 to lead the best-of-seven Western Conference series 3-2. The Indiana Pacers avoided playoff elimination with a 93-88 overtime win over the Boston Celtics, who hold on to a 3-2 series lead. The New Jersey Nets beat the Milwaukee Bucks 89-82 to take a 3-2 series lead, as did the San Antonio Spurs with a 94-82 win over the Phoenix Suns. US squad: Kobe Bryant (LA Lakers), Allen Iverson, (Philadelphia 76ers), Jermaine O'Neal (Indiana Pacers), Karl Malone (Utah Jazz), Ray Allen (Seattle Supersonics), Jason Kidd (New Jersey Nets), Tim Duncan (San Antonio Spurs), Tracy McGrady (Orlando Magic), Mike Bibby (Sacramento Kings). Three more players to be named. * AUSTRALIA'S Sandy Brondello and Tully Bevilaqua are to resume their WNBA careers alongside Opals superstar Lauren Jackson after the Seattle Storm threw the pair a lifeline yesterday. Brondello and Bevilaqua have verbally agreed to join the Storm for the 2003 WNBA season, bringing Seattle's Aussie content to four after they also drafted Opals centre Suzy Batkovic, 22, last weekend. Until the Seattle offer came, Brondello and Bevilaqua appeared as though they wouldn't play in the WNBA this season after their respective clubs, the Miami Sol and Portland Fire, folded last year. * THERE will be no more bad hair days for the Women's National Basketball League and the Opals after they announced a joint four-year naming rights sponsorship deal worth $750,000 with budget haircare chain Price Attack. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant , the Los Angeles Lakers' All-Star guard, turned himself in to the police Friday and posted a $25,000 bond on a felony count of sexual assault, the Eagle County sheriff'soffice said today. Bryant, 24, was released the same day. The sheriff's office said an arrest warrant was issued after deputies investigated a woman's claims of sexual misconduct by Bryant. The claims were made Tuesday, a day after the woman said she was assaulted in a hotel in the Edwards area of Eagle County, near Vail. After interviews and a review of the physical evidence, investigators met with representatives for District Attorney Mark Hurlbert and concluded that they had enough evidence for the felony sexual assault charge, the sheriff's office said in a statement. Hurlbert said tonight that he had not decided whether to file charges and had not seen the arrest report. He said the sheriff's office asked a judge for an arrest warrant instead of requesting one through his office, the usual practice. Attempts to reach Bryant for comment were not successful. His agent, Arn Tellem, declined to comment through a spokeswoman for SFX Sports, Bloomberg News said. Bryant has cooperated with investigators, the sheriff's office said. "These allegations are completely out of character of the Kobe Bryant we know," Mitch Kupchak, the Lakers' general manager, said in a statement. "For the seven years he's been with us, he has been one of the finest young men we've known and a wonderful asset to both our team and our community. "However, since this is a legal matter being handled by the authorities in Colorado, we must refrain from further comment at this time." Bryant married Vanessa Laine in April 2001. He and his parents recently mended a rift that developed when he became engaged to Laine when she was an 18-year-old high school student. Vanessa Bryant gave birth to their first child, Natalia Diamante Bryant, in January. Bryant was the N.B.A.'s second-leading scorer last season, averaging a career-best 30 points a game. He scored 40 or more points in nine straight games in February and also had career-high averages of 6.9 rebounds and 5.9 assists while playing in all of the Lakers' 82 games. The Lakers were eliminated from the Western Conference semifinals by the San Antonio Spurs, ending the Lakers' run of three straight titles. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LOS ANGELES Lakers basketball star Kobe Bryant has given himself up to police in Colorado, after a woman accused him of sexual assault in a hotel. A statement from the Eagle County sheriff 's office said the woman went to deputies last Tuesday and told them the "sexual misconduct" occurred in Denver the previous night. "Upon review of interviews and physical evidence, investigators met with the District Attorney's office and concluded they had enough evidence to charge Kobe Bryant with felony sexual assault," the statement said. It added that Bryant, 24, was co-operating with police and had voluntarily complied with an arrest warrant by turning himself in. Bryant has been the cornerstone, along with Shaquille O'Neal, of the Lakers, the three-time NBA champions between 2000 and 2002. Bryant joined the Lakers in the 1996-97 season and the following year was the youngest player, at 19, to be selected in an All-Star team. He has been an All-Star ever since and was MVP in 2002. He was the NBA's second-leading scorer last season, averaging a career-best 30 points while playing in all 82 games for the Lakers. Bryant also had career-high averages of 6.9 rebounds and 5.9 assists last season. The 24-year-old has been married to Vanessa Laine since April 2001. The couple had their first child, Natalia Diamante, in January. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WITH THE arrest of one of the National Basketball Association's most glittering stars, allegations of sexual violence are again threatening to sully the reputation of American sport. Kobe Bryant , the driving force behind the Los Angeles Lakers and spoken of by many as the heir to Michael Jordan, was arrested on suspicion of sexual assault after a woman accused him of an incident at a hotel near the Colorado ski resort of Vail. The 24-year-old star turned himself in last Friday, and was released on $ 25,000 (pounds 15,000) bail as prosecutors considered whether to file formal charges. But in Los Angeles, Mr Bryant's team-mates and Lakers officials expressed disbelief at the allegations. "He's married, he has a young kid, he talks about his wife all the time," one said. Another Lakers official says Bryant insists the charges are bogus. The club's general manager, Mitch Kupchak, called the claims "completely out of character of the Kobe Bryant we know". The player, who was the first in NBA history to be signed straight from high school when he joined the Lakers in 1996 as the league's youngest player, has lived up to his early promise. He starred when Los Angeles won three titles from 2000-02 and was named most valuable player in the 2002 All-Star game. Last season he was the NBA's second-highest scoring player, averaging 30 points a game and signing a $ 45m endorsement deal with Nike. Bryant was in Colorado for surgery on his right knee to relieve pain caused by persistent swelling. The woman who brought the complaint said the alleged incident happened on 30 June, a day before Bryant underwent surgery. Prosecutors said yesterday that they needed more time before deciding whether to bring charges against him. The District Attorney, Mark Hurlbert, said: "It may be beyond the end of the week." headline: KOBE CRY: I'M INNOCENT FOULING OUT AS PITCHMAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Whether he beats the rap or not, basketball superstar Kobe Bryant can kiss millions of dollars goodbye, image experts said yesterday. Bryant's mere admission yesterday that he had sex with a 19-year-old Colorado woman shattered his squeaky-clean image and made him damaged goods to top-shelf companies whose products he endorses, they said. A conviction would almost certainly trigger morals clauses in Bryant's contracts and send an estimated $10 million to $12 million a year in endorsement bucks down the drain. Nike, which recently signed a 10-year, $45 million contract with Bryant, stood by the star yesterday - delicately. "We are pleased to have a relationship with Kobe Bryant ," the sport shoe company said in a statement. "He has stated that he is innocent of the charges." The company declined further comment. But Mike Paul, whose Manhattan-based MPG & Associates specializes in "reputation management," said, "Kobe is certainly in trouble." "As we speak, you can bet the lawyers, public relations people and marketers at Nike and the other companies are fighting over what direction to go. And I guarantee the lawyers are saying, 'Pull out now.' " Some of the other products Bryant endorses are Coca-Cola's Sprite, Upper Deck trading cards and Spalding sporting goods. Ronn Torossian, president of 5W Public Relations in Manhattan, said he'd advise companies that use Bryant to "sprint, not jog, away from him immediately." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant was charged Friday with sexually assaulting a 19-year-old woman in a case that is bound to tarnish the career of the NBA superstar. The 24-year-old Bryant faces four years to life in prison if convicted on the single felony count, Eagle County District Attorney Mark Hurlbert said during a news conference. The woman worked the front desk at the resort where Bryant was staying while in the state for knee surgery. Associated Press The Los Angeles Lakers' guard insisted he was innocent of the charge, saying he was guilty only of adultery. "Nothing that happened June 30th was against the will of the woman who now falsely accuses me," Bryant said in a statement. His wife, Vanessa, also released a statement, saying she stood by her husband. "He is not a criminal," she said. Bryant's lawyer, Pamela Mackey, scheduled a news conference for later Friday. Hurlbert refused to talk about the details of the case. He said he reviewed all evidence during the last two weeks and consulted prosecutors around the state before making his decision. "This did not come easily," he said. Bryant, free on $25,000 bail, must return to court in Eagle on Aug. 6 for an advisement hearing. The case has riveted the sports world for nearly two weeks because it involves one of the NBA's brightest and most magnetic young stars. Bryant, a father of a baby girl, has also used his clean image to land multimillion-dollar endorsement deals. Documents in the case, including the arrest warrant, have been sealed. Authorities have said Bryant assaulted the woman June 30 at the gated Lodge & Spa at Cordillera in nearby Edwards. He was in Colorado for knee surgery at a Vail clinic, and was booked at the lodge from June 30 to July 2. Eagle County sheriff's deputies took Bryant to Valley View Hospital in Glenwood Springs for tests early July 2, later letting him leave in a taxi with his bodyguards. The woman also underwent tests, and Hurlbert has said he needed information from the state crime lab before making his decision. Bryant surrendered to authorities July 4 and was released an hour later after posting bond. The sheriff refused to release Bryant's booking photo for days. The arrest was announced July 6, prompting a firestorm of media attention on this small mountain town 160 kilometres west of Denver. Reporters swamped the cul-de-sac where the woman lives with her parents, but she has said nothing publicly. Her family has consulted an attorney. The news stunned basketball fans: Bryant, a five-time all-star who has already won three titles with the Lakers, is by most accounts a quiet man who prefers dinner with a few friends to joining teammates for a night on the town. Sports marketing experts say the case will be a permanent blow to Bryant's image, and could cost him millions in lost endorsements. The basketball superstar has been charged with sexual assault. headline: LEBRON CAN LEARN FROM KOBE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant has put on a lot of great performances in the brief history of Staples Center, but Friday night wasn't one of them. The circumstances were all wrong, for obvious reasons. "If I could just turn back the hands of time," he said during his news conference, where the topics of rape and adultery were front and center, and basketball was mentioned only in passing. But he can never again go back to June 29, when he was the other Kobe Bryant , the one who handled fame and fortune so easily. You really had to admire that Kobe Bryant . On the court, he was the closest thing we had to Michael Jordan, and getting better all the time. Off the court, he led an exemplary life and still had not celebrated his 25th birthday. He had it all. We don't know him off the court, no more than many of his teammates. But we felt fairly certain that if there were ever any rehabbing he had to do in his career, it would always be for a medical problem. Never for his image. That's where he separated himself from his peers, who seem to gravitate toward trouble as if it's a loose ball. But that Kobe Bryant disappeared forever on June 30 in Colorado, when he put himself in a position where he could be charged with a felony offense. It doesn't matter if he wins his case and is found innocent of sexually assaulting a 19-year-old woman. Everything he had done during his career to craft his image, he just threw it all away that one night. What a shame. LeBron James certainly shouldn't be painted with the same brush as Bryant, but it would be a shame if James didn't learn something from Bryant's stunning fall. James has seen Bryant do some magical things with a basketball . But if he tuned into the news conference and listened closely, he would have gotten more out of it than any move Bryant ever executed on his way to the rim. Perhaps James already knows how he has to handle himself as he continues to be one of the most scrutinized people on the planet. "I don't want to disrespect Cleveland and I don't want to disrespect my team," he said on the eve of the draft. Let's hope that he means that. Because James does need to remember those words as he goes about building a career, like Bryant, right out of high school. Better still, he should heed the sad words of Bryant from Friday night, when there was no turning back the hands of time. Zo long, Pat On his way out of Miami to start his new career with the Nets, Alonzo Mourning had a talk with Pat Riley. "I told Pat, 'One of the reasons I want to go to New Jersey is that Byron is an extension of you,' " Mourning said, referring to Byron Scott. "Along with Jason Kidd, that's why I wanted to come here. Pat taught me a lot about winning and competing. Byron is the same way." Which might not be the best thing for a player who missed all of last season because of kidney disease. While Riley drives his players with long, taxing practices from training camp right through the end of the season, Scott is perhaps even more of a taskmaster. In fact, Kidd told friends before he re-upped in Jersey that he does not like Scott's rigorous workouts, the few off days he gives his team, his "Riley act." As Kidd probably knows, Scott isn't likely to change his ways. He may have to, however, considering the many questions surrounding Mourning's health. "It's a complete unknown as to what will happen with Zo," says one NBA executive. "He could last one day, or four years." A lot could depend on Scott's practices. "He kills people in practice and never gives them many days off," the exec says. "Kidd and Zo hate that. More importantly, his practices could wear down Zo. But if he doesn't practice, he can't get in NBA shape." Rim shots Clippers owner Donald Sterling, known for renting players on the cheap, has taken on his biggest contract. The Clippers matched Miami's $82 million offer to Elton Brand. The Clips now expect Riley to sign Lamar Odom to an offer sheet worth up to $50 mil. With Elgin Baylor still among Odom's biggest supporters, the Clips might match that one, too. He is ready to let Denver have Andre Miller for $51 million and Utah have Corey Maggette for $42 mil. . . . One scout's assessment of Knicks' Mike Sweetney during Boston games: "Nothing comes easy for him. He plays below the rim." Troubling news, but not surprising in the least. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LOS ANGELES: One of America's biggest basketball stars was yesterday charged with the sexual assault of a young woman. Los Angeles Laker Kobe Bryant , the youngest player to be selected in the NBA, faces a life sentence for allegedly raping a 19-year-old hotel employee in the ski town of Vail, Colorado. MATP A married father-of-one, Bryant is expected to plead not guilty and has issued a statement saying he had consensual sex with the woman after a night of partying on June 30. Nicknamed the "King of Hollywood," because of friends like Jack Nicholson, Bryant now faces the highest profile trial in the US since the O.J. Simpson case. "I did not assault the woman. I made the mistake of adultery," Bryant said. But prosecutors said a two week investigation led them to charge the 24-year-old with rape. Bryant has become one of the most respected players on and off the court since making his professional debut in 1996 straight out of high school. The son of former NBA player Joe Bryant, the star Laker was raised in a rich neighbourhood of Philadelphia, in contrast to the vast majority of professional players, who grew up in poverty. A tearful Bryant yesterday presented a dignified front: "There are a million and a half excuses, but we are not going to make any." Bryant's wife, Vanessa, jumped to the defence of her husband with whom she had a daughter in January. "I know that my husband has made a mistake -- the mistake of adultery," she said. "He and I will have to deal with that within our marriage." The alleged victim is a former high school cheerleader who friends describe as honest and ambitious.
