fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The good news for the marketers who employ Kobe Bryant as a celebrity endorser is that the repercussions of his arrest on charge of sexual assault, based on the experience of other stars and scandals, is unlikely to tar their brands. In the past, at least, consumers have been willing to forgive the transgressions of heroes like the Los Angeles Lakers' player -- and forget. Mr. Bryant, who says he is innocent, has lucrative endorsement deals with Nike Inc., Coca-Cola Co.'s Sprite beverage, and McDonald's Corp. The Wall Street Journal Burns Sports & Celebrities Inc., an Evanston, Ill., company that helps match athletes with advertisers estimates he will earn $11 million to $13 million from his signed endorsements deals in 2003. Last month, Nike signed Mr. Bryant to a four-year, $40 million deal. Both Coke and McDonald's say their relationships with Mr. Bryant are still current. In a statement, Nike said it is "pleased to have a relationship with Kobe Bryant ." Last summer, another sneaker company, Reebok International Ltd., stood by another troubled basketball player, Allen Iverson of the Philadelphia 76ers, to their mutual profit. Mr. Iverson was alleged to have barged into his cousin's apartment with a gun w and was charged with weapons violations. Reebok issued a news release stating he was his innocent. As it turned out, "sales pretty much stayed consistent during and after the incident," says Ron Rogers. Charges against Mr. Iverson were later dropped. Reebok's first contract with Mr. Iverson incorporated a morals clause. Common to all National Basketball Association player contracts, such a clause allows marketers to distance themselves from a tarnished endorser. After signing up celebrity endorsers, smart marketers have learned that reacting too quickly to bad news can boomerang. A unit of Fila Holding SpA, an Italian apparel maker, dropped Sacramento Kings forward Chris Webber as an endorser in 1998, only to be sued because he had only been charged with -- but not convicted of -- marijuana possession. Fila was ordered in 1999 to pay Mr. Webber $2.61 million in lost endorsement fees, and Mr. Webber went on to become the basketball division president at LL International Shoe Co., which makes Dada branded athletic footwear. Another axiom of the game is that consumers decide when and where to give their loyalties, and it can be a mistake to sell those affections short. "People are already used to factoring in information that would tarnish athletic accomplishment," says Michael Markowitz, a branding consultant. "People have become accustomed to hearing bad stuff." headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 IMAGINE IF David Beckham were involved. No, on second thoughts not David Beckham, since even hype-drenched America has not quite contrived an equivalent to Beckham-mania. Michael Owen or Paul Scholes would be a better parallel, but that is the measure of the sensation here that Kobe Bryant , guard of the Los Angeles Lakers and one of the NBA's most glittering stars, has been charged with sexual assault. The incident in question took place late in the evening of 30 June, after Bryant had arrived in his hotel at a Rockie Mountains resort near Vail, where he was due to undergo knee surgery the next day. The alleged victim who bought the complaint is a 19-year-old college student who was working at the hotel. According to media accounts, she went to Bryant's room, for reasons yet to be explained. She claims she was raped; he insists the sex was consensual, and that his only offence was "the mistake of adultery". On 2 July, Bryant was arrested and released on $ 25,000 bail. On 18 July, after more than a fortnight of gathering physical evidence, the local district attorney, Mark Hurlbert, charged Bryant with a single count of felony sexual assault. If convicted, the player faces from four years to life in prison. That however is for a court to decide. Basketball and American sport, meanwhile, are still struggling to come to terms with what has happened. But, one might ask, why? Star athletes and accusations of violence and sexual misbehaviour are a depressingly familiar mix. Mike Tyson is but the most notorious example. Each year brings a new crop of famous football, basketball and baseball players caught up in such allegations. Some are genuine, others are the product of a celebrity-seeker's fantasy. But Kobe Bryant 's case is different. He one of the NBA's biggest names, the first player to enter the big leagues straight from high school, a spectacular and graceful athlete who averaged 30 points a game last season and is frequently spoken of as the next Michael Jordan. Thanks in part to Bryant, the Lakers - winners of three straight NBA championships between 2000 and 2002 - have replaced Jordan's Chicago Bulls as basketball 's most glamorous franchise. Bryant already plays alongside fellow superstar Shaquille O'Neal; next season the Lakers will boast two more huge acquisitions, Karl Malone and Gary Payton. Even more important, Bryant has been been a poster boy for the NBA. His purple and yellow Lakers shirt, bearing the No 8, is one of the hottest selling replica jerseys in any sport. He has a beautiful wife, his high school sweetheart Vanessa, to whom he is utterly devoted. In January the couple had their first child, and a day after the birth, Bryant wore his hospital ID bracelet in a game. He is handsome, with a winning smile, and nicely spoken - even to reporters. He may also be the only NBA player fluent in Italian, the result of having lived there for eight years while his father, Joe "Jellybean" Bryant, played basketball in the Italian leagues. All this makes Bryant one of American sport's most marketable properties. He has endorsement deals with Coca-Cola and McDonalds, capped by a $ 45m tie-up with Nike. Not surprisingly, when news of Bryant's arrest hit the Lakers, the reaction of his team-mates was stunned disbelief: "This is completely out of character from the Kobe Bryant we know," the club's general manager, Mitch Kupchak, declared. Both the squeaky-clean image and the massive endorsements are now in serious jeopardy. Thus far, Nike has said nothing, but it is inconceivable the company would persist with the arrangement if Bryant is found guilty. Endorsement contracts invariably have a public disrepute clause allowing a sponsor to pull out if the principal is charged with a crime, let alone convicted. And for the next few weeks, the headlines swirling around Bryant will be the opposite of wholesome. Assuming it is televised, the trial this autumn bodes fair to be a linear, if miniaturised, descendant of the O J Simpson jamboree. All the ingredients are there: a sporting superstar facing disgrace, a doughty smalltown district attorney who brought the charge, pitted against the bevy of star defence lawyers sure to be hired by Bryant and - of course - plenty of sordid sexual detail. The fight will be down and dirty, as the media rake over every detail of the alleged victim. Officially, her name is being withheld, but that and much more is purportedly available on the internet. The familiar rows of TV trucks with their satellite dishes are lined up outside the courthouse in remote Eagle County. Already unflattering leaks about her past are appearing in California and Colorado newspapers avid for every titbit about the case, although the past sexual activity of a plaintiff is inadmissible as evidence in a rape case under Colorado state law. But as the O J case showed, such niceties tend to be trampled in the media stam

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Woe is the NBA. Woe is the state of American basketball . Oh, my. How will they survive? How will we get through this? Should we all hyperventilate at once or simply run around the streets screaming with our hands over our heads? According to what I've read and heard, there's not much left of the NBA or American basketball now that Kobe Bryant has been charged with the sexual assault of a 19-year-old woman. South Florida Sun-Sentinel Let's see, according to reports, Bryant's arrest is going to cause "massive damage" to the NBA. The ripple effect of his arrest could be "devastating." And it could show the lack of fundamentals (morals?) by American basketball players. Wait a minute. I have to breathe into this paper bag to catch my breath. The intention here is not to demean the seriousness of the allegations against Bryant, or to pontificate on how we shouldn't rush to judge. But to act as if Bryant's arrest is going to cause irreparable damage to the NBA or American basketball is ludicrous and perhaps racist. Since he's innocent until proven guilty, all we know is that Bryant cheated on his wife. Now, if I'm not mistaken, he's not the first person who has cheated. But if Bryant's only transgression is thinking with a body part other than his brain, I believe the NBA can pull through this nasty episode. Several such episodes didn't seem to destroy the Oval Office. But why would anyone believe Bryant's arrest would damage the NBA beyond repair? The argument is that since Kobe was the league's golden boy he was the image the league most wanted to project. Why wasn't anyone questioning whether Major League Baseball would survive when Kirby Puckett was charged with groping a woman, or John Rocker was spewing insensitive remarks? Where were the NFL doomsayers when Michael Irvin was getting arrested or NFL Man of the Year Eugene Robinson was getting tagged for solicitation on Super Bowl Eve? Are such outlandish charges more credible about the NBA because some are uncomfortable with the image of its players, from their tattoos to what we consider their thug representation? Is it because a larger percentage of athletes are black in the NBA than Major League Baseball? And why are some critics asking if fans will begin wondering if the "fundamentals" European players bring to the NBA are above those of American players in the wake of Bryant's arrest? They wonder if Bryant's arrest highlights the demise of American basketball 's morale. But I didn't hear such an outcry when Pete Rose was bounced from baseball or the NFL's Ray Lewis was charged with murder. But what's really confusing is how Bryant is being portrayed in regard to the rest of the NBA. Bryant's arrest appears for many to be "shocking" because they believe he was the last of the good guys in the NBA. Sure, some players have had their share of problems in the league, but what about Shaq going back to school? The generosity of David Robinson or Alonzo Mourning? There's Tim Duncan and Kevin Garnett. There are plenty of good guys in the NBA. You see, the game will go on. It will not crumble. It will not be devastated. As far as Bryant's life, marriage and career? That's to be determined. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Note to would-be wives of sports/political superstars: Make sure the pre-nuptial agreement says you won't be a public-relations prop in the event of a sex scandal. Yes, I'm talking about that sickening display at Friday's press conference when basketball superstar Kobe Bryant slobbered adoring sentiment all over his wife, Vanessa, after he confessed to committing adultery. "And I love my wife with all my heart. She's my backbone," he said, gripping her hand. Then he spoke to her directly. Knight Ridder Newspapers "You're a piece of my heart; you're the air I breathe," he oozed. "You're the strongest person I know, and I'm so sorry for having to put you through this, and having to put our family through this." Barf bag, please. Whatever credibility Bryant had with me was obliterated by that self-indulgent display of suspicious spousal devotion. Bryant's clean public image suggests he avoided the pitfalls of sports stardom before this tragic incident. But when the chips were down, he resorted to a familiar sleazeball public-relations tactic to stem the damage. And this was one of the worst cases of exploiting a spouse I've ever seen. Usually, the chastened husband just parades his wife in front of the cameras, ostentatiously holding her hand, satisfied to have her as a visual prop to redeem his wrongdoing. But Bryant subjected us to embarrassing protestations of love, quite hollow under the circumstances. Such words are meant to be spoken in private, not uttered in public for personal gain. Why do powerful men have to hide behind their wives' skirts when they find themselves in trouble? From Gary Hart to Bill Clinton to Kobe Bryant and all the others in between. It's bad enough to commit adultery - never mind allegedly committing rape - when your high profile invites exposure. Why add humiliation to betrayal by forcing your spouse to exhibit wounded forbearance in public rather than suffer in private? Of course, Bryant's wife of two years has a stake in this, too. His plummet from the pantheon of superstars would have a profound impact on her extraordinary life. Surely, she's willing to do whatever it takes to bolster his credibility when he insists he didn't rape anyone. And surely, she believes him - along with the rest of the world, from what I can tell. It certainly seems out of character for the Kobe Bryant the world knows to have done such a thing. But power can beget arrogance, and arrogance can beget recklessness. He wouldn't be the first person to be revealed as something less than he seems to be. But this is about what Bryant did to his wife, not what he did or didn't do to the alleged victim of sexual assault. The public display at Friday's press conference just made me gag. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NBA players not behaving well is nothing new to professional basketball -- just ask the Portland Trailblazers who seem to have a new player in the slammer each week. But Kobe Bryant is supposed to stand for everything that is still good in the NBA. He comes from a family with a strong basketball background, works hard to be the best and has a very supportive wife. During the past several years, Bryant has proven himself a model citizen in the NBA. Ohio State U. Or so we thought. Over the last couple of days, Bryant has proven to be no different then any other athlete who has had his mug shot flashed on ESPN. Now I know Bryant has not been found guilty of anything as of yet, but there is no doubt that Bryant's relationship with his teammates, major companies and the Lakers franchise, not to mention his wife, have become severely damaged. It is hard to believe how fast an individual athlete can fall these days. Since the alleged claim that Bryant sexually assaulted a 19-year-old woman in Colorado, his character has been brought into serious question. Bryant's legal issues are just another example of an athlete who perceives himself to be above the law. But when you hear of an NBA basketball player who has found himself in trouble with the law, names such as Allen Iverson, Ron Artest and Rasheed Wallace immediately come to mind, not Kobe Bryant . Never in a million years would anybody associate Bryant's name with someone who represents an NBA bad boy. When we think of Kobe Bryant we think of his acrobatic dunks, three championship rings and cheesy Sprite commercials. Now, when people see Bryant flying through the air for another acrobatic dunk on "SportsCenter," they will immediately associate Bryant's name with crime. Even if Bryant is found innocent in the case that could potentially go to trial, Bryant has associated himself with everything that is wrong in the NBA today. Earlier this season, Bryant responded to a charge laid upon Allen Iverson for possession of marijuana. In an article in The Los Angeles Times, Bryant was quoted as saying NBA players have a responsibility to kids who look up to them. You know things have gone down hill for Bryant when reporters ask Iverson, of all people, to comment on Kobe and his legal troubles. Oh, how the tables can turn. Although he may avoid any sort of jail time, Bryant could still miss a good part of the season, not to mention that a possible trial could take away from Bryant's ability to train for the upcoming season. The rehab on his surgically repaired shoulder, which is why Bryant was in Colorado in the first place, could also be affected. Lost in all the hoopla of the Bryant scandal is the strained relationship he could have with his teammates, who have remained silent throughout his legal troubles. After all, it was Bryant who questioned the team's focus, along with the direction the Lakers franchise was headed, after their second round exit from the 2003 NBA Playoffs. So I find it difficult to believe Bryant's teammates will welcome him back with open arms upon his return. Although it is uncertain what the upcoming months have in store for Kobe, one thing is for certain -- Bryant's name will be tainted for the rest of his NBA career. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The office of the district attorney who plans to prosecute basketball star Kobe Bryant for felony sexual assault was locked down yesterday afternoon after a series of telephoned death threats. District Attorney Mark Hurlbert was not in his office, but several members of his staff were. The FBI said it was investigating the calls. "Everyone in the office got some [of the calls ]," spokeswoman Krista Flannigan said. "The accumulation of the calls rose to a level today where those in the office felt it was necessary to lock the doors." Flannigan said Hurlbert has been receiving threats since July 18, when he announced his decision to file criminal charges against the Los Angeles Lakers guard for an alleged attack June 30 on a 19-year-old Eagle woman. Bryant, who is free on bail, has said the sex was consensual. "This is part of the controversy related to a high-profile case, but there was a high level of concern in the office," Flannigan said. "Still, those who were working inside carried on about their business professionally." Flannigan confirmed FBI involvement but declined to identify how many calls were received, from what area code the calls originated or what was said. She said the FBI "will investigate whatever the FBI investigates." A Denver FBI headquarters spokeswoman declined to discuss the investigation. Eagle County Sheriff Joseph Hoy, who oversaw Bryant's arrest July 4, said his office was not threatened yesterday. Bryant's accuser also reportedly has received telephone and e-mail threats. Also yesterday, Eagle County judge Frederick Gannett scheduled a July 31 hearing on the attempt of news organizations to unseal legal documents in the Bryant case. Gannett also ruled that news cameras will be allowed inside the courtroom at Kobe Bryant 's hearing Aug. 6 on the sexual assault charge. A live audio feed will also be permitted, the judge said. He placed no restrictions on what the cameras could record. Gannett's order allows one pool TV camera and one pool still photographer in the courtroom. A second judge who oversees the courthouse has not ruled on a media request to station pool cameras in a hallway outside the courtroom and at the courthouse entrance. Additionally, the attorney for a Vail, Colo., newspaper seeking the release of two noncriminal police visits to the home of Bryant's accuser within the last year said he expected a district judge to withhold his decision until Gannett makes his decision. The police responses are part of the sealed case. Notes fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The Associated Press contributed to this report. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 I've read only two kinds of stories about Kobe Bryant since the NBA superstar was arrested and subsequently charged with sexual assault at a Colorado resort earlier this month. Of course, thousands of stories have appeared in newspapers and Web sites around the world, but most of what I have read falls into just one of two categories. First are the stories, columns and editorials expressing shock that a guy like Kobe could find himself in a bind like this. Kobe, after all, is a three-time NBA champion with the Los Angeles Lakers and he's one of the league's ambassadors. He's a nice guy with no earrings and no tattoos and no criminal record. These aren't huge accomplishments for most of us, but they make Bryant look like an angel compared to the increasing number of NBA players we see in handcuffs. Kobe's afro became a halo. According to these stories, Kobe Bryant is as close as any pro athlete can come to being perfect. "So how could this have happened?" the writers cry. Second are the stories in which a writer sifts through the shards of Kobe's shattered public image and speculates about the basketball star's future. The usual conclusion is that, regardless of the result of the upcoming trial, Bryant will have to pay for his actions that night. "Guilty or not, we will never look at Kobe the same," is the refrain. He'll pay with his reputation, which many pundits feel has suffered permanent damage, and he'll pay when the corporations he endorses distance themselves from him. The scenario makes some sense. It happened to O.J. Simpson nine years ago when he was arrested after his ex-wife and her friend were murdered. Hertz Rent-a-Car fired him as an endorser and NBC dumped him as an analyst. Neither company offered to take Simpson back after he was acquitted in 1995. But I don't think Kobe Bryant 's story will end the same way. Unlike Simpson, who was an ex-jock, B-movie actor at the time of his arrest, Bryant is at the peak of his game and his popularity. He has a legion of young fans with deep allegiances and short memories. As long as he is handsome, charismatic and capable of scoring 25 points a game he'll never have to worry about losing popularity. These days fame equals forgiveness. Just ask R. Kelly. In the winter of 2002 the R & B star was arrested and charged with several counts of child pornography after videotapes were released of him having sex with teenage girls. Kelly then suffered through a rough couple of months. Ministers and radio stations in his hometown of Chicago boycotted his music, and multi-platinum rapper Jay-Z refused to tour with Kelly in support of their album, Best of Both Worlds. But that was last year. This year, if you're younger than 30 you probably know some of the words to Kelly's hit "Ignition/Remix." Watch BET for an hour and you'll probably see Kelly singing a duet with R&B legend Ron Isley or producing singles for the teen group B2K. Another summer, another string of hits. And Kelly's not the only one. In 1988 videotapes were released of actor Rob Lowe having sex with a teenage girl. He was vilified back then, but now, after a Wayne's World comeback and an eventual stint on the NBC drama The West Wing, Lowe is preparing to star in his own series, Lyon's Den. If Kobe doesn't go to jail, I see his case playing out the same way. He might miss some games while standing trial but he'll return to the NBA without losing any popularity. And if he does go to jail, his fans won't love him any less. His most ardent supporters will think he's innocent, no matter what 12 jurors say. Like fans of former heavyweight boxing champion Mike Tyson, they'll revere Bryant as a martyr if he's convicted. It may boggle the mind that fans would continue to cheer for a rapist, but Tyson still sells tickets. That's the point. In time, like Tyson, Bryant would become cast as the victim. The crime forgotten, his fans would focus on the cruel interruption of a Hall of Fame career. And, however inappropriate the perspective might be, his return to the NBA would still invite comparisons to former heavyweight champ Muhammad Ali, when Ali was jailed briefly and lost his boxing licence for three years after refusing induction into the U.S. Army in 1967. This legal trouble may even make Bryant more popular than he is right now. Darren Rovell of ESPN.com suggested as much in a story he wrote shortly after Bryant was arrested. In his story Rovell proposed that Bryant's new rap sheet would win him new fans by giving him the "street cred" some people feel he lacks. I don't know about all that. If Kobe were accused of smashing a bottle of Cisco over somebody's head after a craps game gone bad, he might win some "street cred." But he's not. He's accused of rape. This incident didn't occur on a ghetto street corner, but in an exclusive Rocky Mountain resort. There's nothing "street" about Eagle, Colo., and no "cred" to be earned.

headline: MEDIA SCRUM CIRCLES RAPE-CASE SPORTS STAR
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 HOT ON the trail of the latest celebrity crime scandal, the American media will descend today on the tiny town of Eagle, Colorado, when one of the country's top basketball players makes his first court appearance on charges of raping a female employee at a mountain resort hotel. Kobe Bryant , the 24-year-old star of the Los Angeles Lakers, will not have to do much more than answer "yes" or "no" as the charges are read out and the judge sets a timetable of preliminary hearings pending a trial that might not start until next summer. But the media will be poring over every detail, as they did in the O J Simpson trial, to keep the story rolling through the dog days of summer. Little is known of the accusations beyond the bald charge, filed last month, that Mr Bryant "unlawfully, feloniously and knowingly inflicted sexual intrusion or sexual penetration". The victim has not been named, and lawyers on both sides are subject to a gagging order. If convicted, Mr Bryant faces a possible prison sentence of four years to life. Mr Bryant, who is married with a young child, has admitted having sex with the woman but insists it was consensual. Having publicly apologised for his adultery, he bought his wife a $ 4m (pounds 2.5m) purple diamond ring. The couple appeared at a sports awards ceremony at the weekend, receiving a standing ovation from fans. headline: ALL ALONE IN A HOSTILE ARENA
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 He has been a great star in all the great theaters of his sport. Kobe Bryant helped win the Lakers their third championship in a row just across the Hudson River in 2002, beating the Nets in Game 4 of the NBA Finals to complete a four-game sweep. And even with Shaquille O'Neal on the court that night, there were times when the game belonged to Bryant, when he was the only one out there worth watching, when it was his ball and his game. So he has owned the Meadowlands, and he has owned the Garden, where people came to see him play once a year the way they did once with Magic Johnson, a Lakers star who had a different fall from grace. "I love New York City," Bryant said in February when he came here to play the Knicks. "I love coming here to play. It's a beautiful gym." He has been having a beautiful basketball career. Until now. His home court is the Staples Center in L.A., where he and Shaq made their team the biggest and most glamorous sports show in Hollywood. Bryant is 24 and has won his first three championships a lot faster than Michael Jordan. In L.A., he was a star in a town filled with stars. Now, yesterday, he was in an Eagle, Colo., courtroom, made to stand and listen to his lawyers waive his right to hear the charge of sexual assault against him. There has not been a day like this for a sports star as big as Bryant since Mike Tyson was charged with raping a young woman named Desiree Washington 11 years ago. So there was no basketball theater filled with 20,000 people yesterday, there to watch Bryant's brilliance with a basketball in his hands, on the floor and flying through the air. This was no beautiful gym like the Garden. Here was Kobe Bryant 's arena yesterday at the Eagle County Justice Center: Judge Frederick Gannett's courtroom, Courtroom 1, on the first floor of the Justice Center, on E. Chambers Ave. The capacity is usually 70 or so, not including the jury. That happens to be the same capacity for the jails at the Eagle Country Justice Center, located above Courtroom 1, on the second floor. When you called the extension for the jails yesterday, a young policeman who identified himself as Chad answered. He was asked how many people were in his jail while Bryant was in the courtroom. "I'm not able to reveal that," he said. "Have you arrested any media?" he was asked. "No comment," he said. Then he was asked if he had ever seen anything on E. Chambers and the surrounding streets like yesterday, with media people and media trucks in the parking lot across the street and a giant television screen set up for people who couldn't get into Courtroom 1. "It's a little crowded," he said. It was crowded on the streets and in the courtroom with its fold-back theater seats and limited gallery seating. Gannett had been there long before Bryant showed up, since 8:30 yesterday morning, doing criminal case reviews. Before Bryant and his people showed up in Courtroom 1, there had been a sentencing hearing in the afternoon for a guy named Jay Darrell Brown. After that came a hearing for an Anastascio Moreno. Through Moreno's appearance, it was business as usual. Then the Eagle County Justice Center belonged to Bryant in a way that the Staples Center and all the other arenas have in his remarkable basketball career, a career that took him straight from high school to the pros and then to championships, fame and fortune. This was not the O.J. Simpson trial yesterday in Colorado. This was not about a double homicide. Simpson was long retired from his storied pro football career when he became the defendant in what was known at the time as the Trial of the Century. Bryant is being charged with sexual assault. But he is being charged at the height of his fame and, at the age of 24, in his basketball prime. Far from ordinary So it was not business as usual for him yesterday anymore than it was in Courtroom 1. They still wanted to get into the arena to see Kobe Bryant . Just not in a way he ever could have imagined. He went to Colorado to have minor knee surgery. He stayed at a place called the Lodge & Spa at Cordillera. He invited a 19-year-old hotel employee to his room. That young woman's version of what happened next has brought Bryant, and the world, to E. Chambers Ave. in Eagle. Either he raped that young woman or he did not. For now, Bryant has gone from loud crazy arenas filled with 20,000 people to one that seats 70 and must have seemed as quiet to Kobe Bryant yesterday as the middle of the night. For the next part of his life, for as long as this takes, he does everything he can to do to stay out of an even smaller arena than Courtroom 1, the kind they have on the second floor of the Eagle County Justice Center. These are known as cells. headline: SEX CHARGE SPORT STAR ANGRY AT 'POLICE LEAKS'
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Lawyers for a US basketball star accused of sexual assault want any police officers who discussed the case with the media to be punished. Kobe Bryant , a multimillionaire player for the Los Angeles Lakers, was due to make his first court appearance in Colorado yesterday. His legal team are upset about press reports quoting anonymous sources who described reported injuries to the accuser and the amount of time she spent in Bryant's room. The sources also said Bryant had given inconsistent statements to the police. The star's lawyers want officers to take the stand in a separate hearing to be questioned under oath about whether they leaked information. At the court appearance Bryant was due to be advised of his rights, the charge against him and the possible penalty, which ranges from probation to life in prison. The player, who is currently free on bail, is charged with sexually assaulting a 19-year-old woman who worked at an exclusive resort hotel where Bryant stayed at the end of June. He has said he had consensual sex with her. Last month, the 24-year-old star appeared at a press conference alongside his wife and fought back tears as he denied the charge. He said he was 'disgusted' at himself for cheating on his spouse. But he strongly denied assaulting the woman: 'I didn't force her to do anything against her will. I'm innocent,' he said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NBA superstar Kobe Bryant yesterday held court at his first media-frenzied appearance before a judge on charges he raped a 19-year-old woman employee at a luxury resort. Bryant, 24, smartly-dressed in a pale grey summer suit and white shirt, turned up at court in the secluded Rocky Mountain hamlet of Eagle without his wife Vanessa, who has vowed to stand by her husband through the ordeal. Through his attorney Pamela Mackey, Bryant, one of the biggest and wealthiest US sports stars, waived his right to hear the single charge of felony sexual assault read to him in court and was not asked to plead. MATP The Los Angeles Lakers guard spoke only to indicate he had seen and understood legal documents. The alleged victim was not present at the hearing, which Bryant left without stopping to talk to fans or reporters. Judge Frederick Gannett schduled a pre-trial hearing for October 9 in a case billed as a celebrity trial comparable to those of O.J. Simpson or Mike Tyson. The judge also warned lawyers and police to stop leaking information to the media in contravention of a gag order. "I am concerned with some of reports that I have seen in the press," he told the court. "They appear to me to address issues that are not generally available to the public," he said warning those leaking salacious details they would be "dealt with". Eagle, population 3500, was besieged by TV crews, journalists and fans, angering locals. Bryant's appearance followed failed bids by his lawyers to excuse him from the hearing and to bar cameras from the court. The all-star player was the often scandal-wracked National Basketball Association's clean-cut pin-up boy until his July 4 arrest. He denies assaulting the woman on June 30 in Edwards, Colorado, where he was staying while undergoing knee surgery in nearby Vail, but has admitted cheating on his wife with the woman. He is free on $38,700 bail after turning himself in on July 4. If convicted, the good-looking hero could face between four years to life in jail or probation of up to 20 years. His once pristine reputation made him a top pitchman and won him multi-year contracts worth up to $23 million a year with McDonald's, the Coca-Cola company and game firm Nintendo. In June, he signed a five-year, $70 million deal with Nike. All that is now at risk. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Dallas Mavericks owner Mark Cuban reflects the self-serving, arrogant attitude that has dribbled the NBA to new lows ("Mavs boss: NBA gains from Bryant," Sports, Tuesday). A rape case is not good for anything, and the NBA likely will suffer further. At the very least, Kobe Bryant is an adulterer. If he is judged to be a rapist, the NBA loses big-time. Professional basketball has been the chosen sport for me and my wife for years. We annually renew our subscription to the NBA League Pass cable-TV package and frequently attend games, including courtside, Kobe-featured Los Angeles Lakers games. After a season-shortened strike a few years ago, our passion for the game diminished even further as more NBA misfits emerged. The Kobe case seals our disappointment with the game. We will not be renewing our NBA League Pass subscription or attending any more NBA games. Nor will we be purchasing any products that sponsor "glorified adulterers." Wayne Massey Livonia, N.Y. Great for NBA business? I have been a fan of Mark Cuban and his sometimes flamboyant style of taking on the league referees and the NBA hierarchy. But his comments Monday on the Kobe Bryant situation have crossed the line. How can Cuban assert that the charges against Bryant are great for NBA business? I don't see how he thinks this story -- whatever the outcome -- can be good for any business. If he thinks the notoriety of this issue and the train-wreck type of reality TV it generates will help sell the NBA, what does that say about the product? George Watts Howell, Mich. NBA, media stand to gain NBA executive Mark Cuban is probably far more honest than the media who cover him. The Dallas Mavericks owner knows that the TV talk-show and tabloid-news audience will tune in to the NBA -- and when they do, ratings will go up, and the NBA and networks will be able to charge more for games. A question could even be asked of USA TODAY: Why put information about Kobe Bryant on the front page if you don't think it will help sell more newspapers? There are also the TV programs that provide daily updates and live coverage. Many folks see there's money to be gained from the Kobe Bryant case, including members of the media. USA TODAY is lying to itself and its readers if it criticizes Cuban's view. Dan Cotto Naperville, Ill. No more financing basketball Shame on you, Mark Cuban! I used to think that professional basketball was about the outstanding abilities of the athletes and the teamwork being displayed, but apparently the sport now involves facing felony charges and cheating on your wife. As a high school coach in a small school, I try to use this sport to prepare young people for their lives in the "real world." I understand that it is a business, but we must still try to find some moral integrity in what we are teaching our young people. To think that I once enjoyed watching basketball . I will now make sure that I do nothing to support professional basketball in a financial way. Michael Wright Ladysmith, Wis. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Nothing excites the American media more than a celebrity in trouble. And they've certainly got their man now. Kobe Bryant , 24-year-old guard for the Los Angeles Lakers, the most celebrated player in basketball today, stands accused of raping a 19-year-old receptionist who came to his room at a luxury spa hotel in the Colorado Rockies. The cable news stations are in very heaven. Not for the first time, a prominent black athlete has found himself under indictment for offences that scarcely seem to square with his squeaky- clean public image. A few years ago, of course, it was OJ Simpson, the football star and occasional Hollywood actor put on trial for the murder of his wife and her paramour, and we never heard the end of it. This time the alleged offence is considerably less grave but the star in question is altogether larger. And, the chances are, we're never going to hear the end of it, all over again. Bryant isn't just very good at what he does. He gets spoken about in the same breath as Michael Jordan, the legendary star of the Chicago Bulls and the Washington Wizards, who not only dominated the game for much of the 1990s but also acted as an ambassador and role model for America's much misunderstood black youth, who are among the sport's greatest fans. Clearly, we are deep into who-woulda-thunk-it territory here. Americans love their heroes, have something of a hero fixation, and therefore love to shake with righteous indignation when their heroes don't live up to expectations. Bryant, a rapist? The reaction of Leonard Pitts, columnist with the Miami Herald, perhaps epitomises the response of many of his fans: "No way in hell." And Kobe Bryant really does seem an unlikely villain, unlikely, that is, given the way he is perceived. This is a guy who has religiously shunned the celebrity glam circuit in the seven years since he first turned professional. He doesn't party, doesn't go out with the other members of the team and, in the days before he met his wife Vanessa, was so shy of the girls who flocked around him that he was forced to explain: " Basketball is my girlfriend." What Kobe is known for is his tireless devotion to his skills on the court, his extraordinary work ethic, and his rugged individualism as a player - something that has got him into trouble with both his coach, the legendary Phil Jackson, and his gentle-giant co-star on the Lakers, Shaquille O'Neal. In fact, Kobe is considered so much the opposite of a bad boy that in some quarters he is thought to be "not cool". It is said that Allan Iverson, the star guard for the Philadelphia 76-ers whose upper body is festooned with gang tattoos, sells more sports shoes than Bryant; clearly, that whiff of danger is appealing to young basketball fans. So what are we supposed to think now? Television news stations are always looking for a master narrative line to drive the plot forward, but even they don't seem too sure. Goody-two-shoes corrupted by system? Family man with a double life? Icon of virtue wronged by conniving Colorado teenager? Twenty-five years ago, basketball was a minor spectator sport, but by the early 1990s, thanks to stars such as Michael Jordan, it had gained a following and a mystique. It was as popular as baseball and as hip with young black kids as the music they listened to. In 1996, Sports Illustrated sent a writer to a high school gym in Philadelphia where, to the cheers of classmates, Bryant announced that he would forsake college and dedicate himself to basketball . He was the son of a professional basketball father, Joe Bryant, and dreamt of stardom from an early age, first in Italy, where the family lived for eight years, and then back in Philadelphia. According to his uncle, Chubby Cox, he was a smart kid whose worst mistake was to "put his feet on my sister's sofa". The next season he joined the LA Lakers, the glamorous team followed by some of Hollywood's biggest stars. He was 6ft 6in, presentable, articulate and athletic. He was not only a sports fan's dream, but also a corporate dream. Big business, including Nike, Sprite and McDonald's queued up to sign him. Nutella, the chocolate spread, put his face on the jars. In the unofficial version of Kobe's life, the blameless kid is a little less blameless. He does not hold a California driving licence, for reasons that have not been explained, and when he received a speeding ticket in 2001 he did not pay it. He's been spotted elbowing or punching adversaries on the basketball court, and was once caught fighting with a teammate on the Lakers bus. When he married his straight-out-of-high-school wife, Vanessa, three years ago, his parents were so upset they broke off all communication with him. The reasons have never been entirely clear. Did they think he was too young, or that Vanessa was a gold-digger, or that race had something to do with it? (She is Latina.) But communication has sin

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THERE IS NO WAY of predicting how the erratic American cable television news networks will respond to a breaking story, but no one has come closer to a plausible mathematical formula than the TV pundit who said of the appearance last week by Kobe Bryant in a Colorado courtroom: when the number of journalists seeking entry to an event exceeds the length of the event itself (in seconds) then brace yourself for blanket coverage. Last Wednesday, Bryant took a private jet from Los Angeles where he stars for one of the most glamorous sports franchises in the United States, the LA Lakers basketball team, to the small but affluent Eagle County where he appeared before Judge Frederick Gannett, accused of raping a 19-year-old hotel concierge. Legally, the hearing was a formality. In media terms, it was cataclysmic. The traffic jam of satellite trucks outside the court - 40, stretching the length of a dozen basketball courts - gave a sense of how big this story will play across American in the coming months, but for anyone seeking confirmation there was always the formula; the number of journalists who had applied for the 22 seats in the courtroom - 540; the length of Wednesday's hearing (in seconds) - 426. During those 426 seconds, Bryant, dressed in an expensive linen suit, flanked by his expensive lawyers, said just two words ('No sir') before flying back to his LA home, leaving the TV anchors, legal analysts and social commentators to dissect and speculate for hour upon end. Seldom have so many words been expended on so little action, although some credit is due to Bob Jarvis, a sports law lecturer, who when asked why there was so much interest in this story, matched Bryant's courtroom brevity with: 'This case has it all.' He's right. This case covers modern America's every obsession: celebrity, violence, sport, culture, crime, sex and money. Then there is, as always in America, race. Already, comparisons are being made between Bryant and OJ Simpson, the former NFL star who was acquitted of murdering his wife in 1995 after a long-running court case that divided public opinion along racial lines. This case shows no signs of similarly polarising the country; not yet. That could change if Bryant's legal team fails to move the case from Eagle County, where the potential jury pool is 96 per cent white and - if some reports are to be believed - the alleged victim has friends, colleagues and relations on every street. The accused, on the other hand, is known in Eagle County only as he is known elsewhere in America; a sporting superstar. Like David Beckham in this country, Bryant is a marketing icon in his homeland. Only Tiger Woods, Michael Jordan and now LeBron James can outstrip his off-the-field earnings, said last year to have exceeded Dollars 15 million. A month before his fateful visit to Colorado, he signed a Dollars 40m shoe contract with Nike. All is now at risk. BARELY A WEEK goes by in America without an accusation of criminal behaviour being made against a professional athlete. Countless books have been written on the subject by authors who never dreamed they might one day have to include a chapter on Bryant. He was a template against whom up-and-coming sportsmen in the US were measured; a brilliant athlete adored by millions but quietly spoken and respectful, a multi-linguist, a happily married man with a young child, whose only previous scrape with the law was a ticket for speeding. The accusation of rape, which carries a maximum sentence in Colorado of life, is shocking enough but it is the dissonance between Bryant's public persona and the violence of the alleged crime that has so stunned the country. It is also what has fuelled a desperate media campaign by the 24-year-old to shore up that persona in the period before the case comes to court. The defence began early last month, when rumours surfaced that Bryant was being investigated for sexual assault on a woman during a visit to Colorado for knee surgery. Initially, he denied ever meeting his accuser. But when the rumours of a police investigation were confirmed, he changed his story. Flanked by his lawyers and his wife, Vanessa - whom he described as 'the air that I breath' - he tearfully admitted to adultery, but said he was innocent of any crime. A story leaked that he had bought Vanessa a Dollars 4m diamond ring as a peace offering. Finally, there was a press conference during which he said: 'You know me guys. You know I wouldn't do this.' For many Americans watching at home, particularly those who deify their sporting heroes, this was all the convincing they needed. But the journalists in the room were less impressed. Many had covered Bryant's basketball exploits for years and had come to think of him as obsessive and aloof. Instead of answering the desperate superstar in the affirmative, they responded with: But do we know the real Kobe Bryant ? Joe 'Jelly Bean'

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 EAGLE, Colo. - On the day he got a warrant to arrest Kobe Bryant , Sheriff Joseph Hoy had no idea how big a case he had. He didn't even know who Kobe Bryant was. "When they told me that the case involved Kobe Bryant , I had a blank look on my face," Hoy said. "The name didn't mean anything to me." One of his cops offered a hint - he dribbled an invisible basketball . "My investigator said to me, 'Sheriff, basketball ! NBA. Superstar. L.A. Lakers. That Kobe Bryant .' I looked at him and said, 'So?' This case had nothing to do with his celebrity status." It has been a brutal month for Hoy since he persuaded a judge to sign the warrant July 3 - three days after Bryant was accused of raping a 19-year-old college student at the Lodge & Spa at Cordillera in nearby Edwards, where she worked as a concierge. The sheriff sparked controversy when he skipped the usual step of consulting with the county district attorney first. Under pressure Accused of moving against his celebrity suspect too quickly, Hoy - on the job barely six months then - was pilloried in the press and called everything from a "dumb country hick" to an "incompetent jackass" in a slew of phone calls and E-mails that flooded his office. "It was a surprise to me the amount of backlash we got right away," he said in an interview last week. "This past month has been a challenge, a real challenge." Eagle County Judge Frederick Gannett has imposed a gag order barring all parties from discussing the case. This week, at the request of Bryant's lawyers, he appointed a special investigator to determine whether Hoy's office leaked details of the case to the media. Hoy denies it. He also denied a rift with the district attorney, Mark Hurlbert, who didn't file a sexual-assault charge against Bryant until 15 days after Hoy got the arrest warrant. "There is no feud. I do not work for the DA. He does not work for me. . . . I know we did the right thing, no doubt about it in my mind," Hoy said. Hurlbert's office agrees there is no feud between the two lawmen. "He is in no way at odds with the sheriff," Hurlbert's spokeswoman Christa Flanagan said. Hardly the image of the leathered, rough-and-tumble Western sheriff, the 56-year-old Hoy is more a "granola tree hugger," as one patron at the Bush Creek Saloon - the town's only bar - put it. The lanky, gray-haired sheriff does not carry a gun, prefers shorts and sandals to his uniform and spends his lunch hour bicycling 22 miles through the mountains. "I don't like to carry a weapon when I'm not in uniform, and I only wear my uniform to events," Hoy said. He moved to the tiny upscale town of Eagle in the early 1980s, after a long stint in the Army as a helicopter pilot that included a tour in Vietnam. For 14 years, he was the town's deputy, running the drug and alcohol awareness program for a dozen years. Hoy was elected sheriff in November and took office Jan. 14. Six months later, he was at the center of the most notorious rape case in the country. Eagle County, Colo., Sheriff Joseph Hoy (inset) says Bryant's name didn't ring a bell when he asked a judge for an arrest warrant last month. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Mostly we like Mark Cuban, the Dallas Mavericks owner. True, he's addicted to the spotlight and the sound of his own voice, one of those guys who always knows exactly where the camera is. But he is an authentic and admirable American success story. He wanted to listen to his beloved Indiana Hoosiers' games on the radio while living in Dallas. He figured a lot of other people might like something like that. So he figured out how to do it, on the Internet, and became a billionaire. #KOBEMESS.A10# As an owner he's been a rabble-rouser for mostly the right reasons. He's largely right about the NBA officiating, for example. He is in a crucial way exactly what a modern pro sports owner should be: rich enough to regard his team as a toy, and to care only about making it the best toy possible. He doesn't think he deserves a profit from the Mavericks any more than he would from his airplane or yacht. Now Cuban says the Kobe Bryant thing is good for the NBA's business. Boy, is he wrong about that. Here's exactly what he said: "From a business perspective, it's great for the NBA. It's reality television, people love train-wreck television and you hate to admit it, but that's the truth, that's the reality today." Later, he added that, "You need only look at Mike Tyson as the number one draw in boxing as proof. I went back and tried to find examples in the entertainment business where it hurt. I couldn't." You didn't look hard enough, Mark. Mike Tyson is generally perceived to be a loon and a thug. Said loon/thug is indeed the number one draw in boxing. In the public mindset, boxing has fallen to about a half-step above kiddie porn. Curling has a bigger day-in, day-out hold on the public imagination. So how has Mike Tyson been good for boxing? In fact he's been a disaster for boxing, perfect evidence of what a mess it's in. Saying Tyson is good for boxing is like saying Rush Limbaugh is good for rhetoric. The NBA's problem is the masses don't respect it. Pro basketball players are seen as sullen humanoid genetic-freaks and thugs who've been babied since third grade, who don't want to be coached or practice or set a screen or learn fundamentals or play by the rules, who spend their on hours working on nothing but dunks and their off hours trolling hotel lobbies for groupies (who would be called stalkers if they were male) and ducking autograph-seekers. It's not clear what the NBA can do about all that. Some of it is baloney. Almost all of it is overstated, based on the view through racist/elitist-colored glasses. It's not entirely clear why the NFL mostly gets a pass on all this. Certainly not because its players are better people, as far as we can know. The guess here is it's because football players' uniforms shield them from the public, because people honor the way they sacrifice their bodies to good ol' American violence, because basketball is seen as being about players, football about teams, and because football holds the all-time, all-purpose trump card: it's so easy to bet on and party over that nothing else matters. But whether the NBA can solve this problem or not, it is a problem. And now one of the very, very few players with anything like a wholesome public image is up for rape. We are about to see debunked the absurd notion that there is no such thing as bad press. If nothing else, Kobe Bryant could very well go to jail, the NBA thus losing arguably its best player and most recognizable, media-friendly persona. Sure, the Lakers' first few games this season will be boffo TV, as the world watches Kobe try to squirm and hoop at the same time. Imagine what the fans will holler. Imagine the signs they'll hold up. Sure, the trial could be O.J., The Sequel. But that's good news for the ABA (American Bar Association), not the NBA. Mark Cuban is smart enough to know that. But, remember, the man does love to hear himself talk. Wednesday.. headline: IN OUTAGE, USA HOLDS COURT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The blackout of 2003 forced USA Basketball officials to postpone last night's exhibition game against Puerto Rico until noon tomorrow at Madison Square Garden. Team USA, which features Jason Kidd, Tim Duncan, Allen Iverson and Tracy McGrady, has been practicing in New York since Sunday in preparation for the Olympic Qualifying Tournament that begins Wednesday in San Juan. The 2004 Olympic Games are scheduled for next summer in Greece. The team's hotel on the upper West Side lost power Thursday afternoon, and many of the players were spotted in the lobby mingling with hotel guests. "People were great," said USA coach Larry Brown, who was raised in Long Beach, L.I. "People were courteous and helping each other. I was really impressed with the way New Yorkers were acting." Nick Collison, a first-round pick of the Seattle SuperSonics out of Kansas, walked through Times Square to see the neon lights out and streets filled with people. "I saw a lot of interesting people that had too much to drink," Collison said. "There were a few bars over there that were open, a lot of people coming in and out having a good time. We had fun with it despite the inconvenience." ANSWER MAN: Iverson weighs in on the Kobe Bryant case in an interview set to air on ESPN's "SportsCenter" tomorrow. "I feel like, in this situation, it has to be something, because you can go get Allen Iverson, because Allen Iverson's had brushes with the law before, but to go get Kobe Bryant , 'the squeaky clean, human being,' it's got to be something," Iverson tells ESPN's Jeremy Schaap. "I wasn't shocked at all. He's a human being. The media made Kobe Bryant who Kobe Bryant is. The media made him squeaky clean. Y'all say he's squeaky clean, but we say he's a human being." - With Christian Red and The Associated Press Classification_24 Classification_24 Classification fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Language: ENGLISH Subject: BASKETBALL (90%); SPORTS & RECREATION EVENTS (90%); STADIUMS & ARENAS (90%); SPORTS & RECREATION (89%); INTERVIEWS (79%); OLYMPICS (79%); SUMMER OLYMPICS (78%); TOURNAMENTS (78%) Company: ESPN INC (54%); ASSOCIATED PRESS (52%); ESPN INC (54%); ASSOCIATED PRESS (52%); USA BASKETBALL (84%); USA BASKETBALL (84%); SEATTLE SUPERSONICS (56%); SEATTLE SUPERSONICS (56%) Organization: USA BASKETBALL (84%); USA BASKETBALL (84%); SEATTLE SUPERSONICS (56%); SEATTLE SUPERSONICS (56%) Industry: SIC4841 CABLE & OTHER PAY TELEVISION SERVICES (54%); SIC7383 NEWS SYNDICATES (52%); HOTELS & MOTELS (76%) Person: KOBE BRYANT (89%) Geographic: SAN JUAN, PUERTO RICO (73%); NEW YORK, USA (93%); PUERTO RICO (79%); GREECE (57%) Load-Date: August 16, 2003 fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor 0 lineWidth 12700 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 End of Document Page PAGE of NUMPAGES KOBE'S BIG VICTORY HYPERLINK https://advance.lexis.com/api/document?collection=news&id=urn:contentItem:4H0R-FNC0-0191-1510-00000-00&context=1519360 00 KOBE'S BIG VICTORY Philadelphia Daily News SEPTEMBER 2, 2004 Thursday 4STAR EDITION Bookmark_27 Bookmark_27

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 IMAGINE if Michael Owen, Marcel Desailly or Juan Sebastian Veron had run out to start their new season at Anfield on Sunday while facing trial for rape. Not very likely, we are pleased to say. But then no one in America expected Kobe Bryant to be warming up for the resumption of major league basketball while police, prosecutors, defence lawyers, judge and jury are preparing to ascertain what happened in a hotel bedroom in Colorado one night this summer. In Los Angeles, where Bryant is the superstar of the Lakers, this has come as almost as big a shock as O. J. Simpson being charged with murdering his wife. Bryant, as heir to Michael Jordan's throne, is the golden boy of U.S. sport. Not only can he defy gravity but he was also perceived to be as squeaky clean as his Nike footwear. Now - after an initial admission Betrayed by the nice guy of adultery, his tacky display of repentance on TV and reports his wife needed paramedical attention on the night he was charged with sexually assaulting a female hotel employee - the idol has fallen. Where white-collar America derived sadistic satisfaction from the imprisonment of the scary Mike Tyson, it now feels betrayed by the apparent nice guy it embraced. This comes at a bad time for the NBA, for whom Bryant was the glamorous prop against slipping TV ratings. Nor is it good for his endorsements. What new line in sports shoes might Nike market in the name of their $35million-a-year basketball man? Bryant Escapes - for bounding over the prison wall and sprinting to freedom? Nevertheless, the Lakers expect him to play through his trial this autumn. O. J. Simpson, Mike Tyson, Kobe Bryant ? Come back Gazza. Come back George. All is forgiven. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Coca-Cola Co. signed a six-year endorsement contract with No. 1 NBA draft pick LeBron James, giving the world's largest soft-drink maker a high-profile basketball player after it stopped running commercials that featured Kobe Bryant . Coca-Cola didn't disclose financial terms of the agreement with James, an 18-year-old guard with the Cleveland Cavaliers. ESPN, citing people it didn't identify, said it's worth about $2 million a year. Bloomberg News Bryant, who helped the Los Angeles Lakers win three straight National Basketball Association championships from 2000-2002, is charged with the sexual assault of a 19-year-old Colorado woman. Coca-Cola has not run Bryant's commercials since his arrest last month. Bryant's contract runs through 2005, company representative Scott Williamson said. "This announcement is unrelated to the Kobe Bryant situation," Williamson said. James, who also has a $90-million endorsement contract with Nike Inc., will be a representative for Coca-Cola's Sprite and Powerade brands, the company said in a statement. Argus Research analyst David Kerans said the company paid too much and is taking too much of a risk on an unproven player. "It was a knee-jerk reaction to Kobe. They're putting a lot of eggs in one basket," said Kerans, who has a "hold" rating on Coca-Cola and who doesn't own shares. "The fact that they thought it over doesn't mean they're right." When doing ads for Powerade, James won't be allowed to wear his Cavaliers uniform because Gatorade, a brand of PepsiCo Inc., is an NBA sponsor, NBA spokesperson Matt Bourne said. He can wear the uniform when promoting Sprite, which is a league sponsor. "LeBron James has brought more excitement and anticipation to the game of basketball than any player in recent history, and we think he's just getting started," Jeff Dunn, president of Coca-Cola North America, said in the statement. James, drafted out of high school, has been compared to Michael Jordan and Magic Johnson. The 6-foot-8 James said in the statement he's been drinking Sprite and Powerade "for a long time." James was named the Gatorade player of the year after his junior and senior seasons in high school. Jordan, James's favourite player, is a representative for Gatorade. Officials at Gatorade broke off talks with James about two weeks ago. "We weren't willing to swallow the kind of dollars they were asking for," representative Andy Horrow said. Derek Jeter of the New York Yankees and Yao Ming of the Houston Rockets are among Gatorade's endorsers. The Cavaliers will make 13 national television appearances next season. They didn't play on national TV last season, when they won a league-worst 17 games. James, who appeared on the cover of Sports Illustrated magazine as a high-school junior, led St. Vincent-St. Mary High School in Akron, Ohio, to last season's state championship. Two of his games were shown on Walt Disney Co.'s ESPN network. Bryant, who is free on $25,000 bond, faces four years to life in prison if convicted. He could also be sentenced to 20 years to life on probation. A preliminary hearing is scheduled for Oct. 9. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LeBron James has yet to set foot on an NBA court, but the No. 1 draft pick has landed himself a multimillion-dollar en-dorsement deal with Sprite - benching former pitchman Kobe Bryant . Coca-Cola officials insisted they have not replaced Bryant with basketball 's teenage phenom, pointing out that Bryant remains under contract for Sprite until 2005. But the L.A. Lakers' star's ads have not been aired since early July, shortly after he was busted on a rape charge in Colorado. Coke spokesman Scott Williamson said yesterday that the Bryant ads were scheduled to end long before the star's legal troubles began. But the court case has hit Bryant's bottom line, big-time. The 24-year-old, who rakes in as much as $13 million a year in endorsements, was dumped this month by the Italian maker of Nutella chocolate spread. So far, Nike has not backed off its $45 million deal with Bryant. The Sprite deal was a big payday for 18-year-old James. Analysts said the Cleveland Cavaliers rookie will rake in between $12 million and $15 million for shilling for Sprite and Powerade, both Coca-Cola products. In Colorado, there was more bad news for Bryant when a judge ruled that his arrest warrant - which may contain potentially embarrassing details - can be unsealed. The judge delayed the release for 15 days to allow lawyers time to appeal. In Iowa, a 22-year-old man was arrested for leaving a death threat on the answering machine of the 19-year-old woman who accused Bryant of rape. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe isn't entirely out, but LeBron is definitely on his way in at Coca-Cola. On Thursday, the company announced an endorsement deal with the much-hyped LeBron James, a fledgling basketball star who jumped directly from high school to the National Basketball Association's Cleveland Cavaliers. James signed a six-year deal with Coke and will tout Sprite and Powerade. Terms were not disclosed. AJC James will be "a key spokesperson" for Sprite, Coke said. Throughout the previous season and in prior years, that role was held by Kobe Bryant of the Los Angeles Lakers. Bryant's image has taken a hit, however, in the wake of charges of sexual assault. Coke spokesman Scott Williamson said signing James was "unrelated to the Kobe Bryant situation." Coke hasn't made any changes in the company's contract with Bryant, which runs through 2005. With such serious charges against Bryant, however, he is no longer the coveted endorser he once was. His Sprite ads mostly disappeared from the air in early July, prior to his being formally charged. "Until Mr. Bryant's legal situation is resolved, it would be very surprising to see him in Sprite advertising," said John Sicher, editor and publisher of Beverage Digest. James visited Coke's Atlanta headquarters Thursday to finalize his deal. He met Jeff Dunn, president of Coke North America, along with several people from Coke's marketing staff. He also saw some of the plans that could be in store for him. James' agent, Aaron Goodwin, said Coke was aggressive in pursuing a deal, especially Bea Perez, vice president of sports marketing for Coca-Cola North America. "Coke got involved much after we started negotiating with Gatorade," Goodwin said. "Coke saw the vision, and we moved forward." Goodwin would not discuss the value of the agreement, nor would Coke. But it is certain to be a multimillion-dollar accord. James already has a seven-year, $90 million agreement with Nike. Bryant's current Sprite contract started at $1 million in its first year in 2001. Pepsico's Gatorade unit talked to James but dropped out of the running when prices got too high. "We're a brand that doesn't need to ride the coattails of one athlete," said Andy Horrow, a spokesman for Gatorade. "Those kind of dollars do not make any sense." That left an opening for Coke. Sprite is one of the company's top brands, while Powerade is a distant second-place rival to Gatorade. James is worth Coke's money in the eyes of Eric Wright, vice president for research and development at Joyce Julius & Associates, a firm that evaluates exposure generated by sponsorships. "He's a unique individual. It's almost a no-brainer decision," Wright said, due to the publicity James will generate in his rookie season. Marc Ippolito, senior vice president of Burns Sports & Celebrities, also believes James is a good catch. Ippolito thinks Bryant's legal problems aren't an issue. "Putting that aside, you have to look at who LeBron is," Ippolito said. "He is one of the hottest names in sports right now." Big names, however, aren't a guarantee of success. Coke has done relatively little lately to promote Powerade under a deal with Atlanta Falcons quarterback Mike Vick. And Sprite's sales have slid for years, despite the use of famous NBA endorsers. "Only time will tell if it's a good deal," said Jeff Chown, managing director of the Marketing Arm, an entertainment and sports consultancy. Nonetheless, Coke has high hopes for James. "LeBron James has brought more excitement and anticipation to the game of basketball than any player in recent history, and we think he's just getting started," Dunn said. James, 18, is from Akron, Ohio. He was the No. 1 player taken in the NBA draft. His contract with the Cavaliers is worth about $13 million over three years. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Coca-Cola has signed a six-year deal with basketball phenom LeBron James to represent the Sprite brand, but said the deal was unrelated to the legal woes of Sprite spokesman Kobe Bryant . James, 18, boasts more than $150million in endorsement deals with his first National Basketball Association game still to come. Under the deal announced yesterday by the soft drink giant, he will be a spokesman for Sprite and Powerade sports drink. James already had deals with Nike _ worth a reported $138million _ and Upper Deck. A high school star in Ohio, James was taken first overall by the ClevelandCavaliers in the 2003 NBA draft. Sprite considers James a "key spokesperson" for its youth brand, a role that will perhaps be especially true with Bryant facing charges that he sexually assaulted a 19-year-old Colorado woman. Bryant, who endorses Sprite, has a contract with Coca-Cola that runs through until 2005. Company spokesman Scott Williamson told ESPN.com yesterday that James's deal was "unrelated to Kobe Bryant 's situation". The agreement with James will include extensive marketing activitiesfor both brands, including advertising,promotions, appearances and other initiatives. "LeBron has not only achieved his NBA dream quickly, but he has done it on his own terms," Jeff Dunn, president of Coca-Cola North America, said. "Quite simply, he personifies the Sprite brand."TRACY McGrady scored 17 points, Vince Carter had 15, JermaineO'Neal 14 and Kenyon Martin 13 yesterday as the Americans used an early 18-0 run to take control in a 111-73 win against the Dominican Republic in the FIBA-Americans Olympic qualifying tournament. In earlier games, Argentina defeated Uruguay 91-60, Brazil beat the US Virgin Islands 100-74 and Canada downed Puerto Rico 89-79. It was the first time since the 2001 Goodwill Games that a team of American professionals surpassed 110 points. Last summer's US team at the world championships scored 110 twice but was held to 84 points or less in its final four games _ three of which were losses. The 1992 Dream Team scored fewer than 110 points only twice in 14 games, but subsequent editions of the national team have not been as dominant as that one. The 2000 Olympic team surpassed 110 points only once _ in a 47-point victory against China _ and the 1996 Olympic team also surpassed 110 just once, also against China. Nick Collison, the team's 12th man, put the United States over the 110-point mark on a driving scoop shot with 19 seconds left. headline: KOBE BEFORE THE FALL, WITH THE WORLD AHEAD OF HIM
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 SPORTCENTURY: KOBE BRYANT Monday, 7 p.m., ESPN Classic On the one hand, the last thing you might think we need right about now is another feature on Kobe Bryant . On the other hand, it's not a bad thing to be reminded of why we had such a strong response to the news that Kobe Bryant was charged with sexually assaulting a woman in a Colorado hotel room: because he's a really, really good basketball player. Tomorrow night's "Sportscentury" makes that point, though we're obviously looking at footage of his basketball feats with a different perspective today than we were back in, say, June. Before the charges were filed, we figured we knew the basic outlines of his story. Kobe is a certified whiz kid, a smiling, intelligent, well-spoken and well-mannered guy who also knows how to play the game off the court. He doesn't even behave like a jerk on the court, which is not something that can be said of all contemporary NBA players. He has accomplished remarkable things before the age of 25 years old, and we've always assumed that with even reasonable luck he would accomplish more before he's through. You figured he'd play another 10 or 12 years, win another fistful of championships, probably set a bunch of scoring records, and someday go out like Michael Jordan, in a blaze of adulation even from players he beat and fans whose teams he disappointed. But now suddenly he's a story without an ending, because we have absolutely no idea what will happen with this criminal case. It could get thrown out tomorrow. He could get convicted. More likely the result will be somewhere in between, which is how most court cases end up. Rarely do both sides find an outcome absolutely fair. When a defendant is convicted, someone almost always thinks there's some circumstance or fact that wasn't properly considered. When a defendant is acquitted, someone almost always thinks the accuser was victimized again. That isn't a particularly profound observation, but it's relevant in Bryant's case because he's the rare defendant who is also a household name, whose image and future resonate way beyond family and friends. This is not, mercifully, another O.J. Simpson case. No one is dead. But already some of the same questions are surfacing, like whether America is a little too eager to see a hero take a fall - and, if you listen to the black community, whether black heroes are especially vulnerable. It's complicated and a little tense already, and if the case goes to trial, it's 100% certain the discomfort and tension will escalate. But this "Sportscentury" reminds us of exactly what Kobe Bryant needs to win this case. He needs to emerge as a basketball player. When his picture comes on TV, or his name comes up in print, people need to think of his moves to the basket, or his points per game. If his name is always accompanied by a reference to this case, he has taken a hit even if he's acquitted. And right now we just don't know. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 An Iowa college student pleaded innocent Tuesday to making a death threat against Kobe Bryant 's accuser in a profanity-laced telephone message last month. John Roche said nothing in court and ignored reporters outside the courtroom. He remains free on a $$ @250,000 unsecured bond. U.S. Magistrate Patricia Coan barred Roche, 22, from drinking alcohol and limited his travel. She did not immediately set a trial date. Bryant, 25, is charged with sexually assaulting a 19-year-old Colorado resort employee June 30. The Los Angeles Lakers star has said the sex was consensual and an Oct. 9 hearing will determine whether there will be a trial. The case has drawn extraordinary attention. Some Web sites have disclosed the identity of Bryant's accuser, and the judge said some letters sent to authorities have included death threats. Roche's arrest was the first of its kind in the case. The University of Iowa student is accused of leaving a message on the woman's answering machine July 27. The caller threatened to assault the woman with a coat hanger and repeatedly vowed to kill her. Roche is charged with making a threatening telephone call across state lines. If convicted, he faces up to five years in prison and a $$ @250,000 fine. A friend of Roche, Elliot Schwind, has said Roche is a "sports fanatic" who had been drinking all day at a golf tournament when he allegedly made the call. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 John William Roche, 22, (right) leaves the federal courthouse in Denver on Tuesday with his attorney, Nathan Chambers. Roche, an Iowa college student, pleaded innocent to making a death threat against the woman accusing professional basketball player Kobe Bryant of sexual abuse. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Shortly after sexual assault charges were filed against basketball player Kobe Bryant , Dallas Mavericks owner Mark Cuban took some heat for comments he made about how the court proceedings would drum up interest in the National Basketball Association for the coming season. The NBA quickly distanced itself from his comments, but Cuban's words have a grain of truth in them. Publicity, be it good or bad, does seem to boost careers. Naturally, the NBA is not going to promote Kobe as a suspected rapist, with commercials stating, "You've seen him in the legal courts, now watch Kobe Bryant on the basketball court, taking on the Utah Jazz." But after hearing about Kobe Bryant day in and day out for months on the news, it's not unthinkable that some of those people watching the news, out of curiosity, may watch Kobe play when they didn't before. The crime may be bad, as sexual assault is, but the result still comes to pass. Several months ago, Latin singer Celia Cruz passed away. Shortly thereafter, she held the top three positions on the Billboard Latin Album Charts, with a couple other albums also among the most popular. Clearly, death is not a positive way to promote a career, but nonetheless people bought her albums after hearing of her passing. It's happened before, with artists ranging from Aaliyah to John Denver. Death seems to equal a sales spike. Brad Garrett of the TV series "Everybody Loves Raymond" recently refused to go back to work until his contract was renegotiated, giving him a bigger salary. After being written out of two episodes, the producers caved in and gave Garrett a raise. Coincidentally, the same week the dispute was resolved, "Everybody Loves Raymond" was the No. 1 show of the week. A work stoppage is not a good thing, but it looks like it improved ratings. Satirist Al Franken was sued by Fox News because he planned on releasing a book titled, "Lies and the Lying Liars Who Tell Them: A Fair and Balanced Look at the Right." Fox News claimed that the expression "fair and balanced" was a trademark of theirs, and that he should remove it from his title. The courts found in favor of Franken, and his book reached No. 1 on the New York Times Best Seller list, due in no small part to the controversy. Lawsuits aren't a positive thing, but I'm sure Franken doesn't mind. There have even been media pundits who felt Madonna's open-mouth kiss with singers Britney Spears and Christina Aguilera at the MTV Video Music Awards was designed to generate a buzz, as Madonna is getting ready to release a children's book. While it may be hard to see a connection, any publicity is apparently good publicity, even if it is an erotic act. Although his phrasing might have been off, I don't think Mark Cuban was so far off from the truth. There probably will be a boost in ratings for Lakers games in the short term, although I doubt anybody associated with the case wanted to get publicity this way. Feldman, a Dubuque free-lance writer and editor, wrote, "The Billboard Book Of No. 2 Singles." His e-mail address is HYPERLINK mailto:cgfeldman@mwci.net 00 cgfeldman@mwci.net .
