fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 As the sexual assault case against basketball star Kobe Bryant moves toward the preliminary hearing stage, it is increasingly evident that for too many people trapped in a time warp of race, the case has all the makings of a sequel to the O.J. Simpson trial. Because I am not a Los Angeles Lakers fan and do not follow professional basketball closely, Bryant was barely on my radar screen before this summer. Unfortunately, my first sustained sighting of him was not for his basketball skills, but for his sexual encounter with a 19-year-old woman in Colorado. Since then, he has become a household name. He is black. She is white. He says their sex was consensual; she says it was not. And, as evidenced in Aaron McGruder's syndicated comic strip "Boondocks" (which the Daily News carries), this is yet another racial Rorschach test. In a three-panel strip last week, the lead protagonist, Huey, an ultramilitant little boy uprooted from the mean streets of Chicago to one of its mostly white suburbs, asks his best friend, the Brooklyn-born Caesar: "What advice do you think O.J. Simpson has for Kobe Bryant ?" The middle panel serves as a pregnant pause. Then in the third, Caesar says: "Don't let all this trial business turn you off of white women." That's the funnies. In a real incident last month, a white supremacist group, the National Alliance, distributed flyers in Eagle, Colo., where Bryant's preliminary hearing is scheduled for Oct. 9, urging: "Don't sleep with blacks." The flyers were duly denounced by the town's mayor, but the issue is out there. It's also out there among blacks who gravitate toward media aimed at keeping them abreast of injustices against the race. Savoy magazine, which reaches a rather upscale audience of blacks, has dedicated its October cover and several articles to the Bryant story. The most provocative is an essay by author and academic Michael Eric Dyson, titled "Black Man's Kryptonite." The suggestion is that just as Superman can be brought down by exposure to kryptonite, black men can be - and have often been - brought down by exposure to white women. "Politics and history are never far away in the interracial bed," he notes. I can attest to his assertion that whites less virulent than the National Alliance and blacks less militant than black nationalists are made uneasy by this case. Dyson categorizes many of the troubled people as "blacks tired of our men tripping up with, and on, white women" and "whites with little experience of blacks, much like those in the Colorado town with a minuscule black population where Kobe Bryant may have sealed his fate." At parties, at the barbershop, at the beauty parlor, at church, at the supermarket checkout counter, in sports arenas, in bars, on the subway, on the Internet - you cannot really escape the Kobe Bryant conversations. But rich men - whatever their race - behaving badly, as the married Bryant acknowledged he did in having an adulterous encounter, have to work hard to convince me that they are victims because of their celebrity, their wealth or even their race. I didn't buy O.J. Simpson as, in civil rights terms, the equivalent of Emmett Till, the black youth killed in Mississippi in 1955 for allegedly getting fresh with a white woman, and the Scottsboro Boys convicted of raping two white women in Alabama in the '30s - in spite of the herculean efforts of Johnnie Cochran to portray him as such. And I wasn't exactly bowled over by Bryant's invoking the words of the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. on an injustice anywhere being an injustice everywhere while accepting Fox network's 2003 Teen Choice Award as favorite male sports figure in Los Angeles. This is ultimately a case about what went on in a hotel room between a man and a woman. If the rest of us need to read more into it, to weigh it down with the nation's historic baggage, then shame on us. To his indictment, add ours. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Less than a week into the Los Angeles Lakers' training camp, the off-court distraction involving Kobe Bryant grows. Bryant, who was scheduled to arrive here on Thursday, surprised the Lakers by telling them he was feeling "under the weather" and would be delayed. Team officials said that they did not expect him to take part in the team's first full practice on Friday. "We're obviously disappointed that Kobe is not here with the basketball club, with his teammates," Coach Phil Jackson said. "You know this is a team thing, it's about us as a basketball team and what the best is for our team. So we'll commence tonight and go on as usual. No one is bigger than the team or the game. That's the way it is." The club did not disclose the nature of Bryant's ailment. No timetable was given for his return to the team. The Lakers have two exhibition games here against the Golden State Warriors next Tuesday and Wednesday. The next day, Bryant must appear at a bail hearing in Eagle, Colo., in his sexual-assault case. Bryant is accused of raping a woman last June 30 at the mountain resort where the woman worked. Bryant, who was in Eagle to have knee surgery, claims the two had consensual sex. On Friday, Bryant is expected to make his first extended comments since he sat alongside his wife, Vanessa, at a Staples Center news conference in July, when he admitted committing adultery. "I wasn't told he was sick," Jackson said. "I was told he was under the weather." With Bryant absent, Jackson planned to speak to the Lakers on Thursday night about the distractions that will come their way this season. But he played down Bryant's absence from training camp. "We have to trust that he's doing the most responsible thing he can do for himself," Jackson said. "Ultimately at this point, I think Kobe has to think about himself before the team." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 HONOLULU -- Kobe Bryant never used to talk about his faith. Funny how fast a man can get religion when he's in trouble. Bryant floated through life as if he lived in a bubble. He had an effervescent personality, an affable smile, lively eyes. If he seemed oblivious to critics of his ball-hogging play and came across to some as a loner, he also could be genuinely gregarious. He was approachable, win or lose. He returned to the Los Angeles Lakers a few days ago a changed man, understandably so for someone facing a sexual assault charge and a possible sentence of four years to life in prison. He looked joyless, yanked from his cocoon at home with his wife Vanessa, baby daughter Natalia and his ever-present bodyguards. His priorities had changed, too, he said: Faith came first, family second, and basketball a distant third. Vanessa had sat holding his hand at a news conference in July when he denied raping his 19-year-old accuser and claimed he was guilty only of adultery for having consensual sex with her. On his first day back with the Lakers, amid the familiar drumming of basketballs in a gym, he appeared uncomfortable. The next day, he loosened up a little and managed a few smiles, joining teammates in a paintball fight . This was one of coach-Zen master Phil Jackson's brainstorms to help the team bond, fighting vigorous faux battles with guns and padded uniforms. The four horsemen of the Lakers -- Bryant, Shaquille O'Neal and newcomers Karl Malone and Gary Payton -- each captained teams of five. By the end of practice Sunday, Bryant was hugging Lakers owner Jerry Buss, shaking hands and rubbing shoulders with Malone and Payton, relaxing around all the players. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The woman who accused Kobe Bryant of rape told police a flirtatious encounter quickly turned ugly when he grabbed her by the neck, bent her over a chair and attacked her, repeatedly asking, "You're not going to tell anybody about this, right?" The 19-year-old woman was raped after agreeing to go to Bryant's suite at the resort where she worked, Eagle County Sheriff's Detective Doug Winters testified yesterday at a preliminary hearing to determine whether Bryant will stand trial. Winters described in graphic detail for a packed courtroom how, according to the woman, an exciting, chance meeting with the Los Angeles Lakers' star led to a nightmarish assault that left her shaken and in tears. She said she told Bryant "no" at least twice and he ignored her, pulling her dress up and her underwear down and raping her from behind. The Associated Press At one point, the woman told police, Bryant forced her to face him and say "no" when he asked if she was going to tell anyone. Bryant, 25, has insisted the sex was consensual. He sat at the defence table staring straight at Winters for much of the hearing, hands folded in front of him. Bryant occasionally clenched his jaw, but showed little other reaction. Though the testimony was graphic, the most explosive statement came from Bryant's own defence lawyer when she suggested under cross-examination that the woman's injuries would also be "consistent with a person who has had sex with three different men in three days." That led an angry Judge Frederick Gannett to empty the courtroom and summon the lawyers to his chambers. Gannett was also upset earlier when defence lawyer Pamela Mackey said the woman's name four times when asking questions. She apologized, saying she would write herself a big note not to say it. "Or I could get you a big muzzle," Gannett said. The hearing -- expected to last only an afternoon -- was finally adjourned after more than six hours, an indication the trial could be long and laborious for both sides. Gannett said it would continue next Wednesday, and the district attorney's office said Bryant had to appear. Winters, the only witness of the afternoon, recounted what the woman told him in an hour-long interview the day after she met Bryant at the resort. It all began with a tour of the hotel on June 30 that led to some flirting. She went back to Bryant's room and showed him a tattoo on her ankle, then turned down his request to join him in the hot tub, Winters said. Her shift at the front desk was ending and she wanted to go home, he said, and "she was starting to feel a bit uncomfortable." Winters said she stood up to leave and Bryant gave her a hug that led to some consensual kissing, Winters said. But when she turned to go, Bryant grabbed her by the neck, pulled up her black dress and raped her against a chair, Winters said. She told investigators she said "no" at least twice, before bursting into tears as the five-minute attack went on. Bryant wasn't holding her neck so tight she couldn't breathe, he said, but enough to control her movement. "She was afraid that he was going to choke her," he said. Afterward, Bryant told the woman to clean up, Winters said. She fixed her hair, wiped her face and left after again promising to remain silent. She went back to the front desk to finish up her work and finally left the resort with an unidentified bellman, Winters said. She told him what happened and he urged her to report it, later following her home. Winters testified that the woman's blood was found on the inside of Bryant's T-shirt, based on DNA tests. The woman told him she had bleeding from the attack, he said. The prosecution also presented photographs showing vaginal injuries and one of a bruise on the woman's jaw, and a rape nurse's statement that her injuries were not consistent with consensual sex. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Photo: Ed Andrieski, Associated Press; Kobe Bryant enters the Justice Center with his attorney Pamela Mackey yesterday. Bryant is charged with sexual assault. headline: SOME FEAR BRYANT CASE MAY BE KEEPING VICTIMS FROM COMING FORWARD
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Sexual assault reports have plummeted at a university once attended by the woman who accused Kobe Bryant of rape, prompting speculation that the high-profile case has made victims fearful of coming forward. Attorneys for the basketball star have attacked the woman's credibility by questioning her sexual history and bringing out evidence indicating she had sex with another man shortly before the alleged attack. "If I were a victim of sexual assault, and I heard and saw things going on in the media with Kobe Bryant , it would certainly make me think twice about reporting it," University of Northern Colorado's Police Chief Wendy Rich-Goldschmidt said last week. The woman who accused Bryant of rape attended the University of Northern Colorado earlier this year but did not return for the fall term. An Eagle County judge hopes to decide by Monday whether Bryant should stand trial. Bryant, 25, is accused of raping the woman June 30 at a resort where she worked. The Los Angeles Lakers guard has said the sex was consensual and remains free on a $25,000 bond. At the University of Northern Colorado, 12 women reported being sexually assaulted through mid-October last year, according to the assault survivors program. In 2001, the same number of women reported being assaulted during the same time period. This year, only three anonymous reports had been made in the same time period by women who said they were attacked but did not want to offer details or seek help. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The cover of the latest issue of the Globe, an American supermarket tabloid, features a pretty blonde smiling at the camera and provocatively lifting her skirt to reveal a gartered thigh. Alongside the picture (taken some years ago for the girl's high school prom) is her name and the caption: "Did she really say No?" She is basketball superstar Kobe Bryant 's accuser: the Globe's splash is timed to coincide with Bryant's court appearance this Thursday, on charges of rape. The paper justifies its violation of Colorado's "rape shield law" by the emperor's clothes argument. Every net-connected citizen in America has access to her identity. There are websites with photographs and intimate personal history. Bryant's attorney "accidentally" dropped the name six times in preliminary hearings, in between detailing the semen stains on her yellow pants. The Bryant case involves a power struggle between four of America's most powerful institutions: the law, the press, the internet, and Lakers' fans. The LA team is to basketball what Man Utd is to soccer, and Bryant is its Beckham. After only six years in the National Basketball Association, he earns $ 40m a year. But will he earn it next year? NBA players are traditionally tom cats. Hall-of-Famer Wilt Chamberlain claimed 20,000 women during his career. Shaquille O'Neal - the Lakers' other superstar - has children by multiple partners. Who cares, just so long as the team keeps on winning? Bryant was different - he is no ghetto kid. He was brought up in Europe by middle-class parents. He is fluent in a number of languages. He married early, didn't party, and clearly adored his wife Vanessa and their newborn daughter. If there were a role model for young African-Americans, he was it. The pounding of huge bodies like Bryant's (6ft 7in, 220 lbs) on hardwood floors wrecks legs. The 24-year-old star's knees were already wearing out. Before the start of the 2003 season, he went for secret surgery in distant Colorado. Not even the Lakers knew about it. He registered in a resort hotel under a false name, leaving Vanessa at home. On the night of June 30, Bryant flirted in the lobby with a 19-year-old college student who was working part-time in the hotel. Around 11pm, she snuck up to his room. She was, allegedly, willing to fool around but did not want sex. The charge is that Bryant bent her over a chair and raped her. The following day, she was examined at the same hospital where a blissfully unconcerned Bryant was having his surgery. Vaginal injuries and bruises were recorded. Charges were duly brought by the local police. Bryant admitted the sex but protested it was consensual. Contritely, he bought Vanessa (very "pissed") a $ 6m ring and had her name tattooed on his arm. If his lawyer, Pamela Mackey (small, white, pretty, smart), gets him off he should inscribe her name underneath it. In between court appearances, Bryant is leading the Lakers on a winning streak. Any court he plays on, he owns. But not the Colorado court. Legally it is a tough sell. A huge black man from out of town having sex with a little white girl, however consensually, ignites prejudice. The locals (predominantly white) are not Lakers fans. Sheriff's deputies have been reprimanded for wearing T-shirts with Bryant's name and a hangman's noose on them. Colorado is severe on rapists. A guilty verdict could mean life as the best basketball player in the exercise yard. Kobe has his legal "dream team" and 14m Lakers fans rooting for him. "Unconfirmed rumours" are being published on the web about the accuser's mental instability, her promiscuity, her insatiable appetite for "big" black men. And now a supermarket tabloid has made her a sexual fantasy. If this is press freedom, bring back the Star Chamber. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 I have been a Lakers fan since I started watching basketball back in 1987 at the age of five and I think Kobe Bryant is the most talented basketball player in the NBA. I grew up less than five miles from Great Western Forum and my father swears that he bleeds purple and gold. Nevertheless, my loyalty to the team and to my favorite player does not compel me to discredit the victim in this case and proclaim Kobe's innocence. While it is true that the accused is innocent until proven guilty, that only means that the burden of proof falls on the prosecution to convict Kobe Bryant . The jury must return a verdict of not guilty if the prosecution fails to adequately demonstrate the guilt of the defendant. It does not mean that we must assume that Kobe is innocent, and therefore the victim is dishonest. In fact, the principle does not even apply to the general public, but instead is restricted to the realm of the courtroom, and to the deliberation of the jury in particular. Georgetown U. Furthermore, the opinions of those not directly involved in the case have no influence on the verdict, and are therefore irrelevant except in one aspect. The fact that there are so many outspoken proponents for the innocence of Kobe Bryant makes it harder for rape victims to come forward and report their crimes. Sexual assault is already one of the most underreported crimes in the country because of its low conviction rate and the shame associated with being dominated and violated by another human being. The assumption of innocence at the societal level only serves to discourage rape victims from reporting because they feel that nobody will believe them. Why do so many people believe Kobe is innocent anyway? People claim to know that Kobe would not commit such a heinous crime and cite his record as an outstanding citizen and excellent sportsman. What do we really know about Kobe Bryant 's personal life? There was little available information before this past summer, but there are some intimate details that are now common knowledge. It is a well-known fact that he cheated on his wife who just gave birth to his only child and publicly humiliated her in the process. The truth is that there is so much more that is unknown about Kobe's character than what is known. Therefore, assumptions about his capacity or desire to commit such a crime are without foundation. While it is unfair to ask individuals to avoid making judgments about parties in a court case, it is not unreasonable to expect those decisions to be made after the facts have been presented. This case highlights the fact that rape victims are almost automatically discredited when they decide to report their case. The "supporters" of Kobe Bryant made their decision about his innocence and the dishonesty of the victim before the evidence was even available. The woman in question was labeled as a gold-digger, slut and manipulator and was crucified before the arraignment even took place. Why is it that this woman is at fault because she went to Kobe's room late at night? Should she have an obligation to have sex with him because she paid him a visit? Even if we assume that she found him attractive and wanted to have sex with him, she still has the right to change her mind. Many of us would not be where we are today were it not for the simple freedom of changing one's mind. The law upholds the right of individuals to change their minds, even in the case of consensual sex. There is legal precedent that states that if a woman consents to sex, but then changes her mind during intercourse, the act must stop. Failing to respect the choice of a woman in that circumstance is considered rape. This case along with all other high profile rape cases allow society the opportunity to show rape victims that we are receptive and supportive, yet more often than not we see the victim demonized which only further complicates the process of getting rapists convicted. If we truly feel that rape is a horrible crime, we must create a social climate that encourages rape victims to come forward and identify their attackers. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 It's hard to know which Lakers' team will show up at the Palace of Auburn Hills tonight. Will it be the one that charged out of the gate at the beginning of the season and tried to convince everyone that Kobe Bryant and Shaquille O'Neal were buddies again? Or will it be the one that lost to Memphis last week as Bryant went five-for-15 and basically froze out all of his teammates, not just Shaq? Windsor Star The Lakers' organization have supported Bry-ant in the fallout from his sexual assault case but Bryant upset some of his teammates with comments in ESPN The Magazine. Bryant was less than gracious about his teammates and how they've dealt with the case. "What bothers me is people who say my situation is hard for my teammates and hard for the organization and I should be mindful of that," Bryant said. "Be mindful of that? Why? Because you have to answer a couple of questions here and there? I'm living this. I am living this. "Everybody else is watching me live this. What's hard about that (for teammates)? . . . I don't see it as being difficult to go out and play a game of basketball and have to answer some questions about someone else's situation." Penalty reduced Maurice Taylor's suspension was reduced to six games Monday by the NBA and the players' union withdrew its grievance over the penalty. Taylor was originally suspended 10 games without pay for violating the league's substance abuse policy. Logjam at top The St. Clair Saints men's basketball team, in a four-way tie for first place in the west division, will be in London Friday to face the Fanshawe Falcons. St. Clair, Niagara, Lambton and Sault each have four points. In Saturday's 81-61 victory over Algoma, Brian Bradley had 18 points for the Saints. Jermaine Small had 17 points, Jerome Forde had 16 and Bode Olagundoye had 10. Lancers vs. Golden Hawks The University of Windsor's basketball teams will tip off Saturday against the Laurier Golden Hawks. The 2-2 Lancers men's team face the 2-2 Golden Hawks at 2 p.m. while the 1-3 women's team faces a Laurier squad with a 4-2 record at 4 p.m. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 TOWER OF POWER: Houston's Yao Ming, centre, is double-teamed by Philadelphia's Eric Snow, left and Marc Jackson. headline: KOBE ARRIVES UP IN AIR STAR OF STARS IN DIFFERENT LIGHT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 When Kobe Bryant was last in New York, he was months away from being a defendant. This was last February, when Bryant was still just the basketball player hardly anybody in this world could defend. Bryant scored 27 points in one electrifying 13-minute stretch at Madison Square Garden, scored 46 points in all, dominated the Knicks and the occasion and the stage the way Michael Jordan once did at the Garden, when nobody could defend him. For this one New York basketball night, every comparison ever made between Bryant and the young Jordan seemed to fit Kobe Bryant the way his celebrity always had, from high school on. This was his one night in New York for the whole NBA season. He made the most of it. And in the middle of it all, he gave the Garden and the crowd one signature moment, the way Jordan always did, going down the baseline against Latrell Sprewell and exploding past Sprewell and into the air and then twisting his body and throwing down a reverse dunk. "The crowd was still buzzing a couple of minutes later," Bryant would say. This was during that stretch last season when Bryant seemed to be able to score 40 points at will. Sprewell said after the game that the last time he had seen an NBA player on a roll like this was Jordan in the late 1980s. "We couldn't stop him," Knicks coach Don Chaney said. No one could stop Kobe Bryant then. Now he comes back to the Garden and back to New York famous in ways he never could have imagined, not as one of the bright stars of all sports, but as the most famous defendant - in a rape case in Colorado - his sport, pro basketball , has ever had. The basketball player the Garden will see tonight is the same one who had the Lakers ahead of the Knicks by 34 points in the third quarter last February. But everything else has changed since Bryant last played a game of basketball in New York: His image, his good name, his marriage. His life. Whatever happens in his court case in Eagle, Colo., the reversal of fortune for Bryant is as stunning as his body twisting in the air against Sprewell for that reverse dunk. "Astonishing," is the way Frank Isola described Bryant's dunk in the Daily News. The Knicks went to Los Angeles less than two weeks after that Knicks-Lakers game at the Garden. Allan Houston would go crazy for 53 points in that one. But going into it, everybody was still talking about trying to defend Bryant, who was on that Jordan roll. "If he gets by the double-team," Sprewell said at the time, "there's no last line of defense." Back when the word "defense" with Kobe Bryant was a world apart from what it is now. The Lakers come here tonight as much of an attraction as they have ever been, going all the way back to Magic and Kareem and the Showtime Lakers. They still have Kobe on the floor, and Shaquille O'Neal. Since last season, they have added Gary Payton and Karl (The Mailman) Malone. That means four future Hall of Famers in the same starting lineup. The last time the Garden really saw anything like that was when the Knicks started five Hall of Famers back in the glory days: Frazier, Monroe, Reed, Bradley, the late Dave DeBusschere. Once again, the Lakers are the favorites to win it all. On the court, they are once again the best basketball show on earth, keep winning around Bryant's court schedule in Eagle, Colo. Where he has been charged with raping a 19-year-old concierge last summer. They met when he checked into the Cordillera Lodge & Spa. She came to his room later. There, the married Bryant says, the two of them had consensual sex. The young woman says he raped her. Until she first made her accusation, Bryant had been one of the golden children of American sports. The Lakers' Derek Jeter. That was then. Now, because of that night in Eagle, Colo., Bryant has become a separate attraction from the Lakers, even as he is still part of the basketball attraction. The way Jordan was a separate attraction all by himself when the old Bulls would come to town. Just for completely different reasons. Only Bryant and the young woman know what really happened between them in that hotel room. Even when it is all over, when he has either been acquitted or convicted, only the two of them will know. We will just be left with his version, and her version. For now, we are left with this new version of Bryant, at least the one off the court. We watch him at his press conference with his wife, one that made smart women I know more angry at Bryant's wife, Vanessa, than at Bryant himself. We hear about him buying her diamond rings that cost millions. We listen to him talk about his newfound faith in God. Sometimes even the prospect of a jailhouse inspires a jailhouse conversion. A friend of mine once said this about Michael Jordan: "I believe everything he does on the court, and hardly anything away from it." No one knows what to believe about Bryant anymore. You always wonder who has been givin

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Everyone who knows of Kobe Bryant seems to have a theory about his sexual assault charge. It was the subject of much discussion among the SundayLife team this week as we were putting this issue together. His supporters claim that all he did was cheat on his wife with a basketball groupie (and although they were not condoning this, it's clearly the lesser of the two evils in our culture). Others think he's a "scumbag" (and worse) who deserves to have his once-glorious basketball career in tatters. A segment on American 60 Minutes, shown here a couple of months ago, shed some light on the world of the elite American basketballer. A couple of ex-wives were interviewed about the reality of being married to heroes of the game. Both admitted their marriages ended as the result of their husbands' infidelity while on tour. However, curiously, one of them suggested that it was hard for men in their situation to resist the hundreds of groupies who were constantly throwing themselves at them. Extraordinary, isn't it? Men who have the willpower to train to a gruelling schedule every day, to maintain a certain body weight and fitness level, to refrain from drinking/ smoking/drug-taking/pre-game sex with their partners, cannot be expected to resist the advances of a bunch of crush-struck girls. I'll be interested to read your thoughts on the Kobe Bryant saga. Perhaps you'll be able to break our in-house stalemate on the issue. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 EAGLE, Colo. -- Questions that could be critical in Kobe Bryant 's sexual assault case -- whether his accuser's medical and psychological records can be entered as evidence and whether they can be made public -- won't have answers until late next month. Bryant's lawyers argued here Friday that they need the records to bolster a defense in which they seek to portray the 19-year-old woman as mentally unstable and promiscuous. They suggest the woman filed a false report against the NBA basketball star as part of a pattern designed to get the attention of her former boyfriend. Prosecutors and lawyers for the woman maintain that the records should be kept secret to protect her privacy and dignity. They say any discussion of them should be conducted behind closed doors because Colorado's rape shield law offers victims in rape cases the same state constitutional protections as defendants. Arguments are further complicated because lawyers for the news media say medical evidence and discussion of it by nine witnesses should be in open court so the press can perform its watchdog role and inform the public. With no clear precedent to guide him, District Court Judge Terry Ruckriegle said he was not yet prepared to rule on how he will balance the three constitutional issues: the accuser's right to privacy, Bryant's right to a fair trial and the public's right to know what is going on in the case. 'No guiding authority' "I find no guiding authority" in previous rulings by either Colorado's Supreme Court or the U.S. Supreme Court, Ruckriegle said. He asked lawyers for Bryant, the woman, the prosecution and the news media to submit written arguments to him by Jan. 9 in preparation for his next pretrial hearing Jan. 23. That would likely be the soonest that Ruckriegle would rule on the issues, which could be critical to the outcome of the trial. No trial date has been set. Bryant, 25, is accused of sexually assaulting the woman June 30 at a spa near the resort town of Vail where he was staying while he recuperated from knee surgery. The woman was a concierge at the spa. Bryant says that he had sex with the woman but that it was consensual. The woman alleges that he forced her over a chair during a visit to his room and raped her. Hospitalization records sought Among the records sought by the defense are those related to the woman's hospitalization Feb. 3 in Greeley, Colo. The woman, who was a University of Northern Colorado student at the time, was taken to North Colorado Medical Center by campus police as a "danger to herself." Bryant's lawyers have said the woman had two "purported" suicide attempts before the incident with Bryant and that she was prescribed an anti-psychotic drug. Also at issue is whether notes that were taken by a rape victim advocate during an interview of the accuser by sheriff's deputies after the incident June 30 should be public. 'Unnecessary humiliation' A jury's determination of the woman's credibility could hinge on how much of her medical background is introduced as evidence. But discussions of her background in open court before the trial begins could violate Colorado's law. John Clune, a lawyer who represents the accuser, said a public hearing on the woman's medical, psychological and counseling history would subject her to "unnecessary humiliation and embarrassment." That is why so few rape victims come forward to report sexual crimes, Clune said. And that is the type of indignity the Colorado law is designed to prevent. Colorado voters adopted a constitutional amendment in 1992 that said crime victims should be treated with "dignity, fairness and respect." Legislators followed with a law requiring law enforcement agencies, prosecutors and judges to protect victims "in a manner no less vigorous than the protection afforded criminal defendants." But Tom Kelley, who represents 11 news media organizations covering the case, said so much about the woman has become public that the right to privacy no longer applies. Bryant missed the first quarter for a hearing in Eagle, Colo., earlier in the day. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Prosecutors in the sexual assault case against Kobe Bryant are being helped by a respected investigator who recently was hired to help solve the JonBenet Ramsey slaying. Tom Bennett, who retired from the Arvada Police Department in 2001 after 30 years, has interviewed the alleged victim in the Bryant case at least once, according to a recent court filing. The Associated Press "His involvement was for a very limited purpose, not as an ongoing part of the case," prosecution spokeswoman Krista Flannigan said yesterday. She would not elaborate. In the Dec. 12 filing, deputy prosecutor Ingrid Bakke said Bryant's 19-year-old accuser told Bennett the National Basketball Association player tightened his grip on her neck at one point during their encounter June 30 at the mountain resort where she worked. Prosecutors asked the judge to order Bryant to allow prosecutors to measure his hands. State District Judge Terry Ruckriegle has not ruled on the request. Bennett declined to comment on his work on the case, first reported in yesterday's Rocky Mountain News. Bryant, 25, faces four years to life in prison or 20 years to life on probation if convicted. No trial date has been set. Bryant has said he had consensual sex with the woman. The prosecution has also consulted with Henry Lee, the forensic scientist who worked on the O.J. Simpson and Ramsey cases. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The young hotel worker's accusation was shocking enough: Thrilled by a chance encounter with a celebrity, she went to his room only to be forced over a chair and sexually assaulted. More startling was the name of the accused: Kobe Bryant . Exactly what happened behind a locked door at a Colorado resort June 30 probably will be up to a jury to decide next year. But it didn't take long for the accusations to hurt the reputations of Bryant and his accuser. The Associated Press Reporters and Bryant's supporters and bashers flocked to the small mountain town of Eagle, Colo., over the next few months for a series of court appearances that brought out graphic details of the 19-year-old woman's charges along with revelations about her past. Even with a trial months away, it became the most-watched case against a celebrity sports figure since O.J. Simpson was charged with murder. It was chosen the sports story of the year by the newspaper and broadcast members of The Associated Press. In the AP voting announced yesterday, the Bryant saga received 30 first-place votes for 616 points, beating out Lance Armstrong's record-tying fifth straight Tour de France title (19 first-place votes, 540 points), the Florida Marlins' surprising World Series win (12 first-place votes, 536 points) and Annika Sorenstam becoming the first woman in 58 years to play on the PGA Tour (seven first-place votes, 473 points). Ten points were awarded for first place, down to one point for a 10th-place vote. Rounding out the top 10 were: Ohio State beating Miami in double overtime for the U.S. college football national championship; Sammy Sosa caught using a corked bat; steroid scandals; Tampa Bay's Super Bowl win over Oakland; Carmelo Anthony leading Syracuse to its first U.S. college basketball title; and Ben Curtis capturing the British Open in his first appearance at a major. Kobe Bryant isn't just any basketball player. He's an NBA superstar making millions a year on the court and just as much in endorsements. Advertisers saw him as the next Michael Jordan, and he enjoyed a pristine image as a likable player who was happily married and the father of a baby girl. A 2002 poll rated him the third-best product endorser in sports, behind Tiger Woods and Jordan. All that changed overnight when Bryant was arrested and charged with sexually assaulting the hotel worker who checked him into the Lodge & Spa at Cordillera in the mountains outside Eagle. The charges were so out of character with Bryant's public image that one noted television interviewer -- and friend of Bryant's -- immediately proclaimed on national TV that he would be "astonished" if the charges were true. The accuser told police she checked in Bryant -- who had gone to Colorado for arthroscopic surgery on his knee -- and two bodyguards, then showed him to his room. Bryant, she said, told her to return in a few minutes and give him a tour of the resort. She did, and they later began kissing in his room. The woman said she tried to leave, but Bryant pulled her dress up, forced her over a chair and had sex with her. Afterward, she was told to go clean up, she said, made to perform one final act, and ordered never to tell anyone about what happened. Bryant held a tearful news conference in Los Angeles to deny the charge, while admitting he had consensual sex with the woman. He held hands with his wife Vanessa, who was given a new $4-million US diamond ring after his arrest. "I'm a human being. I'm a man just like everybody else. I mourn. I cry. Just like everybody else," Bryant said. "And I sit here before you guys embarrassed and ashamed for committing adultery." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 EAGLE, Colo. -- Basketball star Kobe Bryant called in sick Monday for a court hearing in his sexual assault case. But his defense team went on without him, pressing for access to the medical records of his accuser. Eagle District Court Judge Terry Ruckriegle excused the Los Angeles Lakers guard from the day-long hearing, during which the judge heard from as many as nine witnesses. Among them were the alleged victim's mother, several friends and an ex-boyfriend. All testified in private in the judge's chambers to prevent publicity from influencing future jurors. Much of today's hearing also could be in private as lawyers debate over a secret audio recording that investigators made of Bryant after the alleged attack. In Monday's hearing, defense lawyers sought to convince the judge that the woman's sexual and mental health histories are important to Bryant's defense. They contend she falsely accused him to get her ex-boyfriend's attention. Such personal details normally are off-limits under Colorado's "shield" law to protect those who allege rape. But Bryant's lawyers contend that his accuser waived privacy rights by discussing the matters with family and friends. Bryant, 25, is accused of raping the 19-year-old woman June 30 at a mountaintop retreat near the Vail ski resort. He was staying at the Lodge & Spa at Cordillera while in Colorado for knee surgery at a Vail clinic. The woman was a concierge at the lodge. Bryant admits that they had sex but says she consented. The woman says he attacked her. He could face four years to life in prison or at least 20 years' probation if convicted. Bryant is expected back in court today to conclude two days of hearings. In today's hearing, Bryant's lawyers are expected to move ahead with their request that prosecutors be barred from using a secret recording of their client after the alleged attack. Sheriff's investigators made the 75-minute tape while they were questioning Bryant in his room at the resort. Secret taping is not illegal under Colorado law. But whether the recording can be used in Bryant's trial is unclear. His lawyers have argued that Bryant was virtually in police custody in his room during the July 2 interview without being advised of his legal rights or arrested. They also want Ruckriegle to bar other evidence gathered at the same time -- the clothing Bryant wore and physical samples he gave in a hospital exam after the interview. Bryant's absence Monday came as a former tabloid newspaper reporter, Jeffrey Scott Shapiro, went on cable TV to promote the first book about the case, Kobe Bryant : The Game of His Life . And with intense interest now in several high-profile criminal prosecutions, a new USA TODAY/CNN/Gallup Poll finds that 43% of respondents are interested in the Bryant case. Forty-two percent believe the sex-assault charge is either "probably" or "definitely" true. Bryant already is off the basketball court for at least 10 days and five games because of an injury. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 When news spread that Akiba Hebrew Academy had hired Joe Bryant, a number of alums began scratching their heads, trying to figure out why a former Philadelphia 76er would take a job coaching the Akiba girls' basketball team. As it turns out, Bryant had just returned from a successful post-NBA career playing in Italy, and he wanted his teenage son to play high school basketball in America. So Kobe Bryant was enrolled at Lower Merion High School in 1992, and Joe Bryant kept an eye on him - and kept busy - by coaching Akiba, which was only a few miles away in the Philadelphia suburbs. Joe made quite an impression on the girls over the course of his one season there. They played their hearts out for him, and the end result was the best season an Akiba girls team had ever had. Amy Malissa, a 6-foot sophomore at the time, said that she was a bit antsy when she first heard that Joe Bryant would be coaching her team. "It felt like, this is a real person who played basketball in the real world, and I want to be a good player for him," she said. "After one or two practices, though, you realized how warm and encouraging and nurturing he was, and you played better because you really wanted to please him, because he was so nice." Malissa said the girls immediately took to Joe. "He was like an uncle," she said, "a completely beloved mentor for all of us." That year, Akiba played in a league made up mostly of small Catholic schools. The girls figured out a way to use that to their advantage. They gave their plays Hebrew names, and had Coach Bryant call them out as they played. "We taught him the names of the plays, and it was hilarious to hear him screaming the plays out in Hebrew," she said. Joe left Akiba after one season to be an assistant coach at his alma mater, La Salle University in Philadelphia. As for Kobe, he would eventually lead Lower Merion to a state championship before heading straight to the NBA. Today, Kobe is an NBA all-star who also happens to be awaiting a trial on sexual-assault charges. But back when he was a standout freshman for Lower Merion, Kobe would come to watch the Akiba girls practice. On a few of those occasions, Kobe and Joe would scrimmage against the girls, two on five. "It was ridiculous," Malissa said, "they wiped the floor with us. But it was still really fun. Sometimes, I'll tell people, 'I scrimmaged against Kobe Bryant ,' and they'll say, 'No way!'" fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant has been booed in San Antonio. In Milwaukee, a fan held up a sign that read, "Kobe raped me." He has endured chants of "Guilty! Guilty!" in Denver. Bryant also has been cheered in Memphis. In New Orleans, a fan held up a sign that read, "Innocent 8." And, on his second trip to Denver last week, Nuggets general manager Kiki Vandeweghe urged fans to root for Bryant, a gesture the Los Angeles Lakers superstar said touched him during a season that he, his teammates and his coach could not have fully prepared for. AJC "You never knew what to anticipate, because there has never been anything like it before in basketball ," Lakers coach Phil Jackson said before his team practiced in Atlanta on Monday. The purple and gold spectacle that is the Lakers comes to town today, but Bryant won't have to worry about a hostile or jovial reception in Philips Arena --- he won't be there. Instead of being on the court, Bryant will be in an Eagle, Colo., courthouse for a hearing involving his sexual assault case. Tonight will also be the first game that Bryant was physically able to play but couldn't because he had to appear in court. Bryant, 25, had a court hearing in Colorado on Dec. 19, but he jetted back to Los Angeles in time to sink to game-winning bucket during a win against the Nuggets. . Bryant has missed 14 games because of various injuries, including one to his surgically repaired right shoulder, a lacerated finger and a sore left knee. But if you ask the Lakers, Bryant's absence will only mean that they will be without a splendid basketball player --- not a distraction. "We're going to miss him," Lakers reserve point guard Derek Fisher said. "People thought he was going to be a big distraction to our season. It's been a lot easier than we expected it would be, in terms of the travel, or any safety or security concerns." Bryant said his plans were to "just expect the worst" on the road this season. Lakers director of public relations John Black said the team has increased security on the road but added, "We haven't had any problems." "We've had to be defensive," Jackson said of Bryant. "He's gotten an onslaught of catcalls, thunderous . . . when he touches the ball. But it has abated as the season has gone on. It flares up every time there is another hearing and he's back in the news, but it will abate in a week or so." And the Lakers will move on to their next melodrama. They have had plenty this season --- ones that didn't involve one of the world's most recognizable athletes playing under the cloud of a possible jail sentence. From "Shaq vs. Kobe: The Feud Part III" to Bryant saying he will opt out of his contract and become a free agent after this season; to Bryant saying he wants "to be a Laker for life," then adding that he is considering other teams in free agency; to Gary Payton griping about minutes and shots and openly announcing two days ago his displeasure about joining the Lakers; to Karl Malone finally returning to practice Monday after missing the past two months with a sprained right knee. It never ceases. "It's not a circus," Lakers center Shaquille O'Neal said. "We live in Hollywood. Stuff like this goes on. It's always something with us. The media down there is very different. It's very National Enquirer-ish. They get stuff; they run with it. We're used to it." In many ways, they've gotten used to Bryant and his legal troubles. "We just want to make it a place where he can come and just work," O'Neal said. "Not be worried about what else is going on. I'm sure it's pretty hard for him." Bryant has kept most of his legal struggles to himself. "I don't think we've had any conversations about the crowd or his personal situation or anything other than basketball ," Fisher said. "We come to work, and we spend a lot of time together. We also respect each other's privacy in personal matters. If a guy wants to open up and share anything, that's all right, but Kobe has been a point of stability for us all season." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Responding to his accuser's plea for an early trial date, lawyers for the basketball star Kobe Bryant say he is suffering an ''incalculable'' toll and is also eager to proceed to trial in the rape case against him. The response follows by two weeks a motion in which the accuser asked the court to expedite the trial process, because, her mother wrote in an accompanying letter, her life was being torn apart by death threats, obscene messages and news-media hounding. Then, last Friday, District Attorney Mark D. Hurlbert filed a motion requesting that Mr. Bryant, who has yet to be arraigned, enter a plea. ''The people join in the victim's motion,'' Mr. Hurlbert wrote. ''The emotional and mental toll on her is truly devastating as this case lingers on.'' In court documents made public Tuesday, one of Mr. Bryant's lawyers, Harold A. Haddon, replied: '' Kobe Bryant has always wanted an early trial date that would accommodate the court's docket and scheduling requirements. The accuser's false accusation of rape has exacted a personal and professional toll on Mr. Bryant that is as incalculable as it is indescribable. No one looks forward to this case being over more than Mr. Bryant.'' But while the two sides agree on the goal of hastening the process, neither is taking responsibility for the delays. In the defense papers, filed with the state district court overseeing the case in the resort community of Eagle, Mr. Haddon said months had passed since the defense asked the prosecution to turn over physical evidence, including the accuser's underwear. He attacked the ''substantial and unjustified prosecution delay'' and said Mr. Bryant was following procedure by not entering a plea until ''substantial completion of the pretrial hearings.'' In his own motion, Mr. Hurlbert wrote: ''At this time, the people have not caused any delay in this case. The motions, although taking a long time, are in the process of being litigated and are important to ultimate issues of admissibility of evidence.'' The next court date is April 26, when the judge, W. Terry Ruckriegle, will continue hearing arguments on whether testimony about the accuser's sexual history can be admitted as evidence. headline: SHAQ'S STEPFATHER CRITICIZES BRYANT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Shaquille O'Neal's stepfather never has been one to mince words. If Philip Harrison doesn't like something, he will tell you -- in no uncertain terms. That's why it wasn't surprising Tuesday when Harrison vented about Kobe Bryant 's play in the Los Angeles Lakers' 88-78 loss Sunday to the Spurs in Game 1 of their Western Conference semifinal series. While expressing dismay with the Lakers' inability to get the ball to O'Neal consistently, Harrison blamed Bryant for a big part of their offensive woes. " Kobe Bryant could be one of the best players that ever played the game if he learned how to play team basketball ," Harrison said. "The game is played inside out, not outside in. You play team basketball , no problem. "You have to establish the inside game first. Shaquille is the most dominant player to ever play the game. You have to pass him the ball. Let's stop the drama and just play ball." O'Neal and Bryant have had a rocky relationship since they started playing together in 1996. Asked how he would describe Shaq's relationship with Bryant, Harrison said, "They just work together and that's all." Bryant led all scorers with 31 points in the series opener. He hit 10 of 26 field-goal attempts -- taking almost twice as many shots as O'Neal -- and was 9 of 10 from the free-throw line. " Kobe Bryant , you can hate him and love him in the same breath," Harrison said. O'Neal had 19 points in Game 1, hitting 8 of 14 field-goal attempts and only 3 of 13 free throws. Los Angeles led the Spurs, 65-62, after three quarters Sunday, but was done in by 11 turnovers in the fourth period, including six in a span of 3:47. "The Lakers gave the Spurs that game," Harrison said. "They didn't play up to their potential. They were in a fog." Harrison cut Lakers coach Phil Jackson some slack. "I'll give Phil credit," he said. "He has a lot on his mind. He's had to deal with all the personalities on the team and Kobe's situation. Talk about stress." Harrison declined to comment on whether he thinks the court case hanging over Bryant is a distraction to the team. "I ain't touching that," Harrison said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 EAGLE, Colorado: US basketball star Kobe Bryant , facing a possible sentence of life in prison, finally had his moment in court yesterday when he pleaded not guilty to raping a 19-year-old woman at a luxury Colorado resort last year. Dressed in a black suit, a white shirt and a gold tie, the 25-year-old Bryant said just four words in a courtroom in Eagle, Colorado, during his brief arraignment, which was televised nationwide. Asked by judge Terry Ruckriegle whether he understood the charges against him, the Lakers shooting guard replied: "Yes, sir." AFP Asked to enter his plea to the charges of felony sexual assault, which carries a sentence of between four years and life in prison, Bryant said "Not guilty", before sitting down between his lawyers, Pamela Mackey and Hal Haddon. Bryant, who is married and has a young daughter, admitted to having sex on June 30, 2003 with the female concierge at the Lodge and Spa at Cordillera in Edwards, Colorado, but said it was consensual and denied raping her. The alleged victim's parents sat impassively in court behind the prosecution table as the sports star entered his plea. Their daughter, who made a surprise visit to a pre-trial hearing yesterday, did not appear. The trial must begin within six months of Bryant's having entered his plea, under Colorado law. No date was set today for a trial. His next pretrial hearing was set for May 27. Bryant left immediately after the hearing for a flight to Los Angeles in order to play a National Basketball Association game for the Los Angeles Lakers. "As of this moment, the clock is running," said former Denver prosecutor Craig Silverman, who is following the case. "We have less than six months for a trial to begin." Bryant's lawyers contend the woman had had sexual relations with another man a few hours before and a few hours after her alleged rape, which would put into question the genital lesions that she had pointed to as evidence that force had been used. Bryant's lawyers have attempted to depict his accuser as unstable, asserting she had a turbulent sex life and had more than once attempted suicide. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The National Basketball Association denied the San Antonio Spurs' protest of Game 5 of the Western Conference semi-finals, which ended with a buzzer-beating shot by Derek Fisher of the Los Angeles Lakers. The Spurs contended the clock did not start quickly enough after Fisher caught an inbounds pass with 0.4 seconds remaining. The Associated PressFisher swished an 18-foot jumper to give the Lakers a 74-73 victory Thursday in San Antonio. The protest was denied yesterday by NBA commissioner David Stern. The league said a videotape review showed the game clock "started appropriately" and confirmed the determination of the referees, using instant replay, that the shot was released before time expired. One of the three officials triggers the start with a wireless device on his belt. A neutral scorekeeper does, too, as a backup. "I think it definitely started late," San Antonio head coach Gregg Popovich said. Fisher's shot came after Tim Duncan made an off-balance from the top of the key to give the Spurs the lead with 0.4 seconds left. After three timeouts, Gary Payton was ready to pass to Shaquille O'Neal or Kobe Bryant . The Spurs had them covered, leaving Fisher free. He caught the ball, sank the shot over Manu Ginobili, then ran off the court in celebration. "I just wanted to get out of there and not give them an opportunity to think that we didn't believe it went in," Fisher said. The Lakers lead the series 3-2, with Game 6 in Los Angeles today. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant has it. Jackie Robinson and Henry Aaron had it. So did Tony Gwynn. But you can't say all great athletes have -- or have had -- the uncanny ability to block out the bad in their lives and somehow focus on their jobs and achieve greatness in the arena. Some have the mental makeup to do it. Some do not. The Dodgers did not choose Robinson to become the first player to break through baseball's color barrier simply because he was a gifted black man. There were others just as skilled. Robinson was chosen because of his maturity and toughness. He was not a child. He had been in the armed forces. He was smart enough to know the road ahead, and he accepted the challenge that eventually changed the direction and thinking of sports in America. Through all the hatred piled upon him, he carved a Hall of Fame career. A proud man. A rock of ages. Aaron was a black man chasing a white legend -- Babe Ruth and his career home run record. It didn't matter that this journey came along nearly 30 years after Robinson's breakthrough, because there still was hate. There were death threats. Another rock, Aaron did it anyway. Roger Maris, pursuing Ruth's single-season home run mark, obviously had it, although he suffered and lost hair. His curse: He wasn't Mickey Mantle. Barry Bonds is going through it in another way, a victim of rumors surrounding the BALCO Laboratories drug scandal. Bonds is so technically perfect at the plate, he has become a machine (which has absolutely nothing to do with steroids). If they continue walking him and pitching around him, Bonds won't play long enough to break Aaron's record. And then there's Lakers basketball star Kobe Bryant , who is up on rape charges in Colorado. If he is found guilty, his career will be over and basically his life. Don't look at Bryant being guilty or innocent here. That will be up for a jury to decide, and we are not a part of it. But no jury is necessary to access Bryant's remarkable ability to focus. He isn't going through what Robinson went through. There are a lot of people who still like Kobe Bryant . But his personal achievements, in the wake of all this, only can be described as indescribable. On four occasions -- twice during the Lakers' current playoff run -- Bryant has spent the day in a Colorado courtroom and flown back to Los Angeles that evening not just to play, but to star. On Dec. 19 against Denver, he missed the first quarter and scored 13 points, but his 21-foot jumper at the buzzer won it for L.A. On March 24 against Sacramento, he scored 36 points. On April 28 in the playoffs against Houston, he scored 31. On May 11, he scored 42 to help even the series with San Antonio. The Lakers are 4-0 when Bryant goes to the other court. We see the doubters, like the pap from a Florida columnist about people making too big a deal of Bryant's toughness when all he does is appear in a Colorado courtroom and then hop a private jet for a "45-minute flight" to L.A. to play basketball . First of all, Bryant isn't flying to L.A. from Colorado in 45 minutes, unless he's riding NASA. And we can't even begin to think what's going through his mind. This is not a parking ticket he's fighting. How could this not be mentally exhausting? Among athletes I've been around, Gwynn, now baseball coach at San Diego State, was the best at blocking out the bad stuff in his life. In 1987, Gwynn declared bankruptcy. Somehow, the Padres' greatest player batted .370, the highest average by a National Leaguer since Stan Musial's .376 in 1948. Gwynn had 218 hits, scored 119 runs, had career highs in triples (13) and steals (56) and earned one of his five Gold Gloves. Yet even Gwynn, once an outstanding basketball player at State and an NBA fan, marvels at Bryant's achievements. "Oh, yeah," he says, "I can't even imagine what he's going through. Bankruptcy is one thing. But being charged with rape? "Only the best guys have that ability to block things out, but for him to play at this level with what's going on in his life, is incredible. People said there's no way he could handle this, and I was one of those skeptics. He's proved me wrong. He's proved everybody wrong." Of course, you can't compare what Gwynn went through with the Bryant case, which includes huge media attention. No way we can know what Bryant is going through. "For me, it was easy," Gwynn says. "In my case, I hadn't done anything wrong -- except pick a bad agent. I took a lot of grief then, and I got hate mail and there were racial slurs. It was a tough time in my life, but when I see this and the Bonds deal, that all seems relatively easy now. "Jackie's was tougher, because that wasn't dislike, it was pure hate, and he had to keep so much inside. With Kobe, I don't think it's so much hate. People take for granted that Kobe's a great player, but nobody realizes how tough a game of basketball is. At the end of Game 5 (vs. San Antonio), h

headline: SPORTS DIGEST
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KOBE BRYANT CASE Judge rules accuser is not a "victim" Prosecutors, witnesses and other participants in Kobe Bryant 's trial will be prohibited from using the term "victim" to describe the woman accusing the NBA star of rape. In a ruling made public Tuesday, state District Judge Terry Ruckriegle said he agreed with Bryant's lawyers that the term implies guilt. Ruckriegle said the 19-year-old woman must be referred to by name in the courtroom during the trial, and as a "person" in jury instructions. Bryant's attorneys had also asked the judge to prohibit use of the term "defendant" in reference to Bryant. Ruckriegle rejected that, saying the term is "an accurate reflection of his legal status." For his part, the judge said he will continue to refer to the woman as the alleged victim. BASKETBALL Former Irish star loses hand in Iraq Danielle Green, a former Notre Dame basketball player who lost a hand during an attack in Iraq, has returned to the United States for treatment. Green was guarding a Baghdad police station last week when she was hit by a rocket-propelled grenade, losing her left hand. "When I first realized my hand was blown off, I thought, dang, my wedding ring is gone forever," Green said. Her platoon sergeant went up on the roof and found the ring, she said. She now wears it on her right hand. She graduated from Notre Dame in 1999. She was an assistant girls basketball coach at Washington High School in her hometown of Chicago and at Chicago State University. She enlisted in the Army in 2003. OLYMPICS Two-thirds of tickets remain unsold Almost two-thirds of the tickets for the Athens Olympics remain unsold, with corporate sponsors buying far fewer seats than four years ago, organizers said. A total of 5.3 million tickets were set aside for Athens - 3 million to the public and 2.3 million for International Olympic Committee officials, sponsors and others. But just 1.83 million tickets have been sold so far. Officials would not give a breakdown of how many were for the public and the "Olympic family." MISCELLANEOUS Jockey loses bid to ride in Belmont Patrick Valenzuela lost his bid to ride Rock Hard Ten in the Belmont Stakes, denied by a judge who refused to postpone his suspension for failing to take a drug test. One of the horse's owners said Alex Solis will now ride the colt considered to have the best chance of thwarting Smarty Jones' Triple Crown bid. * The New York Jets submitted the necessary paperwork to the NFL on Tuesday to release Vinny Testaverde, 40. By cutting him, the Jets will save $1.5 million on this year's cap. In 2005, Testaverde will count for $4 million. * Mark Fox was hired as Nevada's basketball coach after working four years as an assistant under Trent Johnson. Johnson resigned a week ago to coach Stanford. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Dazed by the hardest punch thrown so far in the NBA Finals, the Detroit Pistons return home vowing to shake off the Kobe Bryant three-pointer that set up the Los Angeles Lakers' series-tying 99-91 overtime win in Game 2. "We have to expect what they did," Chauncey Billups said after Bryant's shot with 2.1 seconds left in regulation rallied Los Angeles from a six-point deficit with 35.9 seconds to play Tuesday. Bryant and Shaquille O'Neal scored all the Lakers' overtime points -- a 10-2 romp to victory. Star News Services "First game (an 87-75 Pistons victory), they said it was a fluke and what-not. Today, we came in and didn't play as good, but we hung in there," said Billups, who led Detroit with 27 points. The series shifts to The Palace of Auburn Hills today (9 p.m., ABC), Sunday and Tuesday. "They did their thing tonight," Rasheed Wallace said. "We've just got to go back to Detroit and do ours." Highly rated The ratings for Los Angeles' overtime win over Detroit in Game 2 of the NBA Finals Tuesday more than doubled the ratings for the second game of last year's series. NBC's broadcast got a 10.6 rating with a 19 share, 106 per cent higher than last year's Game 2 between New Jersey and San Antonio. It was the highest-rated prime-time network program of the night. No harm, no foul The NCAA has determined that infractions in Rhode Island's basketball program were either isolated or inadvertent, the university said Wednesday. The NCAA accepted the corrective and punitive measures Rhode Island has taken -- including the loss of one scholarship for three years. The violations occurred between 1997 and 2000 during the coaching tenures of Jim Harrick and Jerry DeGregorio. The NCAA the infractions provided only minimal advantages. BASKETBALL NOTES NEW DEAL FOR KNIGHT Bob Knight agreed to a three-year contract extension that will keep the basketball coach at Texas Tech until 2009. Athletic director Gerald Myers said Wednesday the agreement will likely be sign-ed next week. Knight's contract pays him $250,000 US a year through May 2006. Knight has led Texas Tech to three consecutive seasons of at least 20 wins and taken the Red Raiders to post-season play each year. -- Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Photo: CLUTCH SHOT: Los Angeles' Kobe Bryant , left, goes up for a three-pointer over Detroit's Rip Hamilton as the Pistons' bench looks on Tuesday in the final seconds of Game 2 of the NBA Finals in Los Angeles. Bryant send it into overtime, and the Lakers won 99-91. body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 COLLEGE BASKETBALL Kansas player may transfer to UNC Kansas has given North Carolina permission to talk to David Padgett, who announced unexpectedly in April that he was leaving the Jayhawks' basketball program after his freshman season. The 6-foot-11 Padgett had been recruited to Kansas by Roy Williams, who is now the coach at North Carolina. KOBE BRYANT CASE Judge upholds state's rape shield law Colorado's law protecting rape victims is constitutionally valid and will be applied in deciding what evidence is admissible in the sexual assault trial of the basketball star Kobe Bryant , the judge in the case ruled Thursday. The decision was a setback to Bryant's defense lawyers, who argued that the law violated their client's rights. CYCLING Armstrong struggles in Tour warm-up Lance Armstrong remained upbeat about his chances of winning an unprecedented sixth Tour de France title despite a poor showing in the fourth stage of the Dauphine Libere. "I'm not worried because the Tour does not start" until July 3, he said. NFL Hall of Fame lineman dies at age 71 Roosevelt Brown, a Hall of Fame offensive tackle who starred for the New York Giants in the 1950s and '60s, died, apparently of a heart attack. He was 71. Dallas Cowboys receiver Antonio Bryant did not practice Thursday and is in Miami. Bryant has missed three consecutive practices after being escorted out of practice by club security Tuesday after an altercation with coach Bill Parcells. Denver Broncos linebacker John Mobley should skip the 2004 season because of a bruised spinal cord, a neck specialist recommended. He will be re-examined in a month or so. Cleveland Browns rookie defensive back Sean Jones is likely to miss the season after tearing a ligament in his left knee. COLLEGE FOOTBALL Liaison says CU should replace AD A liaison hired to examine the University of Colorado athletics department because of a football recruiting scandal says he recommended hiring a new athletics director, advice that was ignored. John DiBiaggio said he stopped short of recommending that Dick Tharp be fired. Tharp declined to comment. AUTO RACING Franchitti takes pole, but slowlyDario Franchitti earned his first front-row start in the IndyCar series with a lap Thursday night at 209.609 - nearly 13 mph slower than the pole speed of last October at Texas Motor Speedway in Fort Worth. It's the slowest pole speed at Texas, and down from Gil de Ferran's 222.864 for last season's IRL finale. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Whether Kobe Bryant continues along his path as the NBA's Ray Lewis depends more on the rest of the Los Angeles Lakers than Kobe Bryant . He's mostly done his part. Which means we should, well, I really don't know. It's difficult to support greatness when it is tarnished with much help from itself. AJC Bryant has that rape thing, and Lewis had that murder thing. As was the case with Lewis, the rather gifted linebacker for the Baltimore Ravens, Bryant is pleading innocence. And, as was the case with Lewis, who eventually blitzed his way to freedom with a plea bargain, Bryant is using the controversy to spur himself and his teammates in dramatic ways. That's good. No, that's bad. The way the Lakers are stumbling in the NBA Finals against the Detroit Pistons, none of this may matter. While Lewis' Ravens reached the Super Bowl and dominated it, the Lakers have dropped two of three games to the Pistons in the race to win four. You can't fault Shaquille O'Neal or Bryant, the only consistently motivated players on their team. For instance: The Lakers should trail the Pistons 0-3, but Bryant continued his special play throughout the postseason with a miracle 3-pointer near the end of Game 2. Although Bryant vanished with the rest of his teammates for most of Game 3, he was efficient down the stretch. It was too late then. Such also will be the case for the Lakers overall if they drop tonight's Game 4 in Detroit. They'd sit just a loss away from ending their search for a fourth world championship in five seasons. They'd also move closer to watching Bryant do what he says he'll do. That is, dribble into the free-agent market. Here's the big thing: At season's end, Bryant will be left to ponder his upcoming trial on charges of raping a woman in Colorado. He admits that basketball is a welcome diversion from it all. He also admits to transferring his anger, frustration or whatever else he feels these days into his pursuit of a championship. The same was true for Lewis after he went to an Atlanta court three years ago. It involved a fatal double-stabbing at a post-Super Bowl party in Buckhead. Was Lewis guilty or innocent? Who knows? What is for sure is that Lewis had no business in that situation. Even he knew as much. He admitted to giving a false statement to police when he said he didn't know the other two men accused in the slayings. According to medical examiner's office, as many as 10 people likely jumped the victims during the fight that Lewis claimed he was trying to break up. Lewis wasn't convicted. Then, with loud and lengthy debate everywhere over his guilt or innocence, he won the NFL's Defensive Player of the Year award and led the Ravens to the Super Bowl, where he was named most valuable player. Not good. Not with Lewis suggesting that he was the ultimate victim. He was bold with those feelings throughout the week of the Super Bowl, and during the subsequent celebrations in downtown Baltimore, he got bolder. He whipped the cheering thousands who couldn't care less into a frenzy with the dancing he does before each game. What a contrast to the other guy. Bryant also knows he had no business in that situation. At the very least, he admits to adultery. He says he is "terrified." As a result, he has approached this season with a heavy dose of humility, vulnerability and fear. You get the feeling that Bryant has learned his lesson. So, maybe it is all right to celebrate the inspired play this season of the Lakers' No. 8, but only to a point. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Cool, calm and collected, the Detroit Pistons took care of business while the Lakers lost their tempers. Taking the lead early in the fourth quarter, holding it the rest of the way and repelling every Los Angeles rally, the Detroit Pistons moved one victory closer to their first championship in 14 years with a convincing 88-80 victory last night in Game 4 of the NBA final. The Associated Press Detroit now holds a 3-1 lead, and the Pistons have made one thing crystal clear -- they are the better of these two basketball teams despite lacking the edge in egos, superstars and problems. Game 5 is tomorrow, and the Pistons could become the first team to bring the title back to the Eastern Conference since Michael Jordan's Chicago Bulls won it in 1998. Ben Wallace scored 26, Chauncey Billups had 23 and Richard Hamilton 17 to lead the Pistons, who outscored the Lakers 32-24 in the fourth quarter. Shaquille O'Neal had 36 and Kobe Bryant 20 for the Lakers, whose dysfunctional two-man show isn't enough to keep up with Detroit's depth and determination. These were some of the scenes that Pistons' fans will cherish: Billups draining timely three-pointers, Rasheed Wallace backpedalling downcourt with a minute left after making a jumper that capped his best game of the playoffs; Richard Hamilton calmly knocking down free throws. As for the Lakers, the snapshots were these: Kobe Bryant screaming at the referees and picking up an untimely technical foul; Shaquille O'Neal yelling at someone in the Lakers' huddle. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 EAGLE, Colo. -- Two prominent forensic scientists hired by the prosecutors in the Kobe Bryant sexual-assault case testified behind closed doors Tuesday as a Colorado judge completed hearing evidence on whether the sexual history of the basketball star's accuser can be discussed at trial. DNA experts Henry Lee and Michael Baden, who had testified for O.J. Simpson during his murder trial nearly a decade ago, appeared before District Court Judge Terry Ruckriegle for about three hours. The judge is not expected to rule on the critical issue of the woman's sexual history until after lawyers submit written arguments June 29. No trial date has been set for the Los Angeles Lakers guard, but it likely could begin in late August or September. Bryant, 25, faces a maximum sentence of life in prison if convicted of sexually assaulting a then-19-year-old Colorado resort employee June 30. Bryant, who says they had consensual sex, has pleaded innocent. Ruckriegle's decision on whether to allow evidence about the woman's sexual history could be crucial to the trial. Bryant's attorneys claim in written filings that the accuser had sex with other men shortly before and after her encounter with Bryant, and those relations may have caused her minor injuries. Defense attorneys also claim the woman's encounter with Bryant was part of a pattern to win back an ex-boyfriend. Prosecutors argue that the woman's sexual past is irrelevant. Under Colorado's rape-shield law, such testimony is generally not admissible unless it bears directly on a defendant's guilt or innocence. Prosecutors could be helped by having Lee and Baden on their side, said Craig Silverman, a former Denver prosecutor and legal commentator. "If I was Kobe Bryant , I would be very worried about Henry Lee and Michael Baden testifying against me," Silverman said. "They are very formidable and have a history of success and connecting with jurors." But Silverman also said jurors might question Lee because of his role in the 1995 Simpson case, which resulted in an acquittal. Many Americans disagreed with the verdict. Eagle County court officials, meanwhile, are preparing to summon at least 1,000 people as potential jurors for the trial. Court spokeswoman Karen Salaz said the county typically calls about 250 potential jurors for a district court case, but she predicted it will be at least four times as many for the Bryant case. Potential jurors will fill out a form with 117 questions. A standard juror questionnaire has about 15.
