fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 After months of legal maneuvering and anguish about the delays by some people involved in the case, court officials said Friday that a date had finally been set for the opening of the sexual assault trial of the basketball star Kobe Bryant . The opening, in Eagle, Colo., will be Aug. 27 when lawyers will begin picking a jury, officials said. The question of a date has loomed over the case since March, when the mother of the woman who accused Mr. Bryant of rape at a Colorado resort in June 2003 came forward with an open letter to the judge saying that her daughter's life was being shattered by death threats and harassment. She begged that the matter be brought to a resolution as soon as possible. Court officials responded that the case was moving as quickly as it could, given the many legally technical questions, including whether the defense would be allowed to present evidence about the woman's sexual past. Chief Judge W. Terry Ruckriegle of District Court has still not ruled on how much information -- if any -- about her sexual past will be allowed under the Colorado law protecting rape victims. Mr. Bryant, who has pleaded not guilty, faces four years to life in prison or up to life on probation if he is convicted. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 After a week of serious deliberation, Duke men's basketball head coach Mike Krzyzewski turned down an offer from the Los Angeles Lakers that would have placed him among the highest paid coaches in all of professional sports. He announced his decision at a press conference Monday, relieving anxiety throughout the Duke basketball community and providing a major lift to college basketball . "I wanted to lead. Your heart has to be in whatever you lead," Krzyzewski said. "Duke has always taken up my whole heart." Krzyzewski made his final decision to remain at Duke late Sunday night and placed early morning calls to Lakers General Manager Mitch Kupchak and Duke President Richard Brodhead to inform them of his intentions. "I must say that I am enormously excited that, at the end of the day, that you decide that your place was in college basketball ," Brodhead told Krzyzewski. Last Thursday, Krzyzewski was offered a contract by the Lakers valued at $40 million over five years. Ultimately, his comfort level with the university and excitement about the challenges he finds in the college game compelled the 57-year-old coach to stay on for at least a 25th season. "The allure of coaching in college has no price," Krzyzewski said. "I have never made a decision based on what was going to just make me the most money. ... I have been really happy at Duke and fulfilled at Duke." The announcement Monday ended several days of tension for Duke players and fans. Members of the coaching staff called each of the players and committed recruits when news of discussions with the Lakers broke Thursday, telling them, "Just trust us and stick with us." Krzyzewski spoke with each player Monday morning, informing his team of his plans to stay. "I know I am breathing a huge sigh of relief that Coach K is staying at Duke," junior forward Shavlik Randolph said. "I am very grateful, as I know he just passed up an incredible opportunity to coach the Lakers. It shows how much he cares about Duke and our basketball program." Krzyzewski began conversations with Kupchak about the coaching vacancy in June, while the two discussed the potential placement of Duke players in the NBA draft. "I said, 'I am 57, maybe I should just look,'" Krzyzewski said. "I have kind of just dismissed things for at least the last 10 years. I said, 'Just take a look at it.' As it went on I took a closer and closer look." Many speculated that free agent Kobe Bryant played a large role in the Lakers' attempt to bring the Duke coach to Los Angeles. Krzyzewski had recruited Bryant to play at Duke before the Philadelphia high school star chose to jump directly to the NBA in 1996, and Monday the Hall of Fame coach dispelled rumors that Bryant played such an instrumental role. "Kobe and I are very close," he said. "We got close in recruiting. Obviously the Lakers are a big organization. This isn't a Kobe Bryant -led movement. I was considering the Lakers whether Kobe Bryant was going to be there or not." Flanked at Monday's press conference by Brodhead and Athletic Director Joe Alleva, Krzyzewski said he wanted to reach a decision quickly because of the fluidity of NBA free agency. The outcome for Krzyzewski never hinged on any possible shortcomings of Duke, where he has always felt very connected with the rest of the Duke community. The head coach has taken an active role in other facets of the university beyond basketball , serving as a special assistant to the president and holding a faculty position in the Fuqua School of Business. Krzyzewski said these "intangibles" contributed to his decision to remain at Duke. Krzyzewski has what amounts to a lifetime contract with Duke, and although the university could not come close to matching the Lakers' offer monetarily, his current deal has been reworked, Alleva said. In addition, Krzyzewski may have garnered further support for projects he considers important, including a separate practice facility for the team. "Everybody knows about the practice facility, and I think that was fairly important," said Chris Kennedy, senior associate director of athletics. "But that thing has been in the works for a long time." By turning down the Lakers' lucrative offer, Krzyzewski caught the nation's attention and may have gained an edge in future recruiting battles for top prospects. "I think that's just an unanticipated bonus. I don't think he went into it thinking, 'I'll acquire the reputation as the guy that turned down the Lakers,'" Kennedy said. Krzyzewski's departure would have been a major hit for college basketball , which has been coping with the loss of its most talented stars to the pro ranks in recent years. "It is a great day for Duke University," Alleva said. "It is a great day for college basketball ." -- Mike Corey contributed to this report. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Lakers star rules a hollow kingdom Kobe Bryant says it was consensual sex. The Colorado resort worker he is accused of raping says otherwise. And on Aug.27 a jury of Kobe Bryant 's peers will begin piecing together the evidence in this high-profile case of he said, she said. In the meantime, back in the fantasy world of pro basketball , Bryant stood guilty yesterday of a consensual assault on an NBA dynasty that easily could have contended for championships for years to come. Although fans of the L.A. Lakers were likely relieved to hear that their free-agent defendant had decided to remain with the only NBA club he has ever played for, announcing his intent to sign a seven-year contract worth some $136 million (all figures U.S.), it was a hollow victory in an off-season of irreplaceable losses. Before Bryant wielded his free-agent leverage as a franchise-wrecking bulldozer, after all, the Lakers had it all. They had the NBA title in three of the past five seasons, the prestige of being champs of the superior Western Conference in four of the past five. And if it wasn't for bad luck - including injuries to key cogs Karl Malone and Derek Fisher - the 2003-04 edition of the Lakers, for all their internal dysfunction, would have made it a series in an NBA final dominated by the Detroit Pistons. They had it all, indeed, but Bryant wanted it all for himself. He wanted the hall-of-fame-bound coach for whom he harboured a never-explained dislike ousted, and so Phil Jackson took his nine championship rings to his Montana ranch. Bryant wanted his hand-picked hall-of-fame coach hired, and so management put together a $40 million, five-year deal that would have made NCAA kingpin Mike Krzyzewski the highest-paid coach in sports even though he's never coached an NBA game, an offer Krzyzewski declined. Above all, Bryant wanted the hall-of-fame-bound centre with whom he so famously clashed bounced, and so general manager Mitch Kupchak - on the orders of sweet-on-Kobe owner Jerry Buss - was forced to trade the most dominant player in the game, Shaquille O'Neal, to the Miami Heat for a trio of players that did not include an all-star. Sports Illustrated has called it the Cirque de L.A., and it still reeks of elephant dung. Bryant's power has been asserted and his ego has been stroked. And the second-biggest loser in all of this, to be sure, was a bidding-war bridesmaid known as the L.A. Clippers, which even considered Bryant's request to move a handful of its games to Anaheim's Arrowhead Pond, which is closer to the player's Newport Beach home than downtown L.A.'s Staples Center, where the Lakers and Clippers are co-tenants. But the biggest loser is Bryant and the club he's been running this summer. He's now a muscled-and-tattooed version of the third-world dictator who pillages his land of its treasures for his own gratification. He's the ruler of all he surveys, but sits on a throne surrounded by squalor; by a team that won't win a championship anytime soon; by former teammates who are now likely to take their free-agent business elsewhere. Across the country in Miami there is ecstasy, because O'Neal is riding in as a saviour. Said Heat president Pat Riley the other day: "We went from basketball hell to basketball heaven ... because this man was available." The Heat are an instant contender. But neither protagonist, neither Bryant nor O'Neal, should be exalted. Their refusal to bury their differences amounted to sickening vanity. Their break-up of the game's most potent combination amounted to a sporting tragedy. If the basketball gods dole out any justice, if there is a basketball hell that's worse than playing for the pre-Shaq Heat, Bryant and O'Neal will be reunited as teammates in the unbearable inferno of eternal purgatory. Even with the hounds of Hades nipping at his Nikes, you'd have to assume Bryant only passes to the Big Luggage when the court freezes over. Lakers have agreed to a seven-year, $136.4 million deal with Kobe Bryant . The announcement comes in the wake of two big departures for the club, Shaquille O'Neill and Phil Jackson. headline: SPORTS DIGEST
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NBA Mavs acquire big man in eight-player deal Dallas Mavericks got a big man, completing an eight-player deal Tuesday that will bring Erick Dampier from Golden State. Dallas sent Christian Laettner, Eduardo Najera, two future first-round draft picks and the draft rights to guards Luis Flores and Mladen Sekularac to the Warriors for Dampier, Dan Dickau, Evan Eschmeyer and the draft rights to Steve Logan. Dampier averaged career highs of 12.3 points and 12 rebounds in 74 games last season for the Warriors. KOBE BRYANT CASE Judge restricts use of cameras at trial The judge in the Kobe Bryant rape case on Tuesday sharply restricted how the news media may cover the trial using television and still cameras, saying he was worried too much exposure could threaten the fairness of the proceedings. District Judge Terry Ruckriegle said no cameras will be allowed during witness testimony. Still photography will be allowed during opening statements and closing arguments. Video and audio coverage will be allowed only during closing arguments. Attorneys for news organizations had asked to be allowed to photograph and videotape the entire trial. Attorneys for the alleged victim joined prosecutors and defense attorneys in opposing the request. COLLEGE FOOTBALL Bloom loses final appeal to the NCAA Jeremy Bloom has lost his final plea to be allowed to play football for the University of Colorado while accepting commercial ski endorsements. The five-member NCAA Student-Athlete Reinstatement Committee turned down the appeal Tuesday after hearing presentations from Bloom, Colorado, and the NCAA in a conference call. Bloom has been fighting the NCAA over endorsements for two years. COLLEGE BASKETBALL Kansas suspends junior point guard Junior point guard Jeff Hawkins has been suspended indefinitely from the University of Kansas basketball team by coach Bill Self, who declined to give reasons for the discipline. Hawkins, who worked out on his own at Allen Fieldhouse on Monday, won't make the Labor Day weekend trip to Vancouver for four exhibition games. - University of Connecticut assistant basketball coach Clyde Vaughan, charged with patronizing a prostitute last week, will perform community service and take a special class for men, in a deal that could result in the charges being dismissed. AUTO RACING Darlington Raceway shows off new lights Darlington, S.C., Raceway gave up a half-century of tradition by installing lights, which they debuted on Tuesday night. Darlington spent $3 million for 286 poles of lights, which will provide 180 million lumens of light. That will give drivers the same view they would have on a sunny afternoon. Why race at night if they need to provide enough light to make it look as though it was day? "Racing at night just looks faster," said driver Bobby Hamilton. TENNIS Venus Williams is not so special any more Serena Williams is still special. Venus Williams is not. Serena received a No. 3 seed in the U.S. Open (behind Justine Henin-Hardenne and Amelie Mauresmo), eight spots above her No. 11 ranking. That's thanks to the "protected ranking" the WTA Tour gave both Williams sisters in January because they had missed time with injuries. But Venus' special ranking expired recently, so she was seeded No. 11 for the Open. MISCELLANEOUS Five now top Bryant in Internet searches According to Internet search engine Lycos, Kobe Bryant has lost the top spot on the list of most-searched athletes on the Internet. The new No. 1 is U.S. Olympic swimmer Amanda Beard. Bryant dropped to sixth. Nos. 2-5: Russian gymnast Svetlana Khorkina, U.S. high jumper Amy Acuff, U.S. gymnast Carly Patterson and tennis player Anna Kournikova. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Nine hundred and ninety-nine people received summonses to appear in court in Eagle, Colo., this Friday to answer the questions that could land them a seat on the jury that will pass judgment on Kobe Bryant . Yes. A thousand minus one. If anyone among this crowd is so unfamiliar with the case as to have no opinion on it, they must have a mailing address in some cave in the Rockies. After jury selection, the trial of the basketball star whose case has produced hardly a wink of worry among his employers - what message does it send having a man charged with rape on your marquee? - will go forward, media circus and all. Another message is being transmitted, though, and it is heard loud and clear by rape victims and those who would counsel them: When you go to court to point out your perpetrator, you and your sexual behavior could end up on trial. This deeply worries Donna Todd, from the Crisis Center of Tampa Bay, which counsels rape survivors and others. No matter the outcome, no matter the truth of the case - if it can ever be found - Bryant's trial will play to the feeling often held by rape victims that they won't be believed, but blamed instead. From cable to Web site, talk show to tabloid, Bryant's accuser has been trashed. She's said to be promiscuous, crazy, or, since she recently sued Bryant, just out for money. Todd put the message sadly and succinctly: "She's a slut; isn't that already true?" It is the main strategy of Kobe Bryant 's defense. It's terrible, predictable and sickeningly reasonable. Last month, the trial judge ruled that evidence of the woman's sexual conduct in the three days surrounding her encounter with Bryant and up to a rape exam can be admitted as evidence. The judge said testimony of her sexual relationships with two other men could be admitted. As for Bryant, there's no doubt she had sex with him - he admits to it - but tests also revealed the presence of semen from another man. Repeated acts of intercourse, not rape, could explain her minor abrasions. There is no easier way to spell "reasonable doubt." The judge hardly had a choice whether to let this evidence in. I usually think rape cases are open and shut. Not this time. I am pulled in two opposite directions by Colorado vs. Bryant. His accuser has the right to be treated with dignity and understanding, in line with the rape shield law that is supposed to keep a woman's sexual history out of bounds so she isn't raped, metaphorically, again. But she doesn't have the right to keep him from getting information about her that could properly help his defense. They say bad cases make bad law. This is Exhibit A. It takes a chink out of the armor that rape shield laws provide women. Women thought this protection was absolute. We believed - and still believe - that "no" means "no," no matter a woman's sexual history. We believe that even prostitutes can be raped. Now suddenly there's the Kobe Bryant exception. Maybe this win for him off the basketball court was inevitable. When you go up against a powerful, popular figure, you will not pass through untouched. Every rock will be hurled at you, every bucket of mud slung, and every word of every moment will be told and retold in the press. Maybe you are crazy for taking on a man like this. Because only one thing can happen: You're the one who gets destroyed. The Bryant case sometimes seems to be the only story in the world. And yet it may have some very personal and powerful effects. This is what troubles Todd. She hasn't been able to sort out what side she's on, what verdict she would render. But she fears rape victims will begin to think about the Kobe Bryant exception and not call 911. She thinks they will blame themselves and live in fear. Forget about guilt or innocence for a moment. This other thing, this risk that rape victims will go mute, would be the worst outcome of all. headline: SPORTS DIGEST
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 COLLEGE BASKETBALL Huggins returns A contrite Bob Huggins returned to work as Cincinnati's basketball coach Friday after university officials suspended him following his drunken driving conviction in June and told him to rehabilitate himself. Huggins said he spent his days fishing, reconnecting with his family and reflecting on how he had let down those close to him. "I made a terrible mistake, and what bothers me most is I hurt other people," Huggins said. "All I can do is work like crazy to be a better person, a better coach, be better at everything I do and make those people proud of me." Huggins, 50, has coached Cincinnati since 1989 and taken the Bearcats to 13 straight appearances in the NCAA Tournament. Eager to get back with his players and assistant coaches, Huggins telephoned recruits just after midnight and returned to Cincinnati's basketball arena later in day. KOBE BRYANT CASE Potential jurors are interviewed Kobe Bryant 's trial began Friday with 300 prospective jurors arriving at the county courthouse in Eagle, Colo., to fill out questionnaires, the first step toward choosing 12 people who will decide whether the NBA star is guilty of rape. Many arrived by car, though one rode her bicycle to the courthouse despite a chilly breeze and streets wet with overnight rain. Some appeared to know each other, chatting and laughing as they walked up the circular driveway. All are from Eagle County. Bryant was not at the courthouse. Jury selection will probably take most of next week. Opening statements are not expected until Sept. 7. TENNIS Dechy, Bovina will play for title Nathalie Dechy of France advanced to the final of the Pilot Pen tournament in New Haven, Conn, using quick, accurate play to beat Lisa Raymond 6-4, 6-0 Friday. The eighth-seeded Dechy will face doubles partner and seventh-seeded Elena Bovina in today's championship match. Each has won a match against the other this year. Bovina used her big serve to overpower fellow Russian Elena Dementieva 6-1, 6-1. MISCELLANEOUS Commercials will return to Masters Television advertising is back at the Masters. Martha Burk and her campaign against the all-male membership at Augusta National might not be too far behind. Ending two years of a commercial-free broadcast at golf's highest-rated event, Augusta National chairman Hootie Johnson said Friday that three corporate sponsors -- ExxonMobil, SBC Communications and IBM -- will provide four minutes of commercials every hour in a telecast that has been extended 90 minutes. Johnson dropped the previous TV sponsors two years ago when Burk and her National Council of Women's Organizations began to pressure companies. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The trial of Kobe Bryant , the American basketball mega-star accused of raping a young resort worker at a luxury spa outside Vail in Colorado in June last year, is finally under way amid the same kind of circus-like atmosphere that surrounded the O J Simpson drama almost a decade ago. More than 500 residents of Eagle, Colorado, where the trial taking place with as many as 600 reporters from around the world watching, filed into the main courthouse on Friday to answer preliminary questionnaires about a case that combines questions of race, sex and super-charged celebrity. Prosecutors and lawyers for the defence will begin direct questioning of potential jurors tomorrow in an effort to whittle them down to a panel of 12 who can sit in judgment on the 26-year-old member of the Los Angeles Lakers basketball team. Opening statements are expected on 7 September. Finding the right mix of jurors is fraught with risk for both sides. While attitudes about race and sexual promiscuity will be important, most experts say that his celebrity status will be of greatest concern. "Obviously for Kobe Bryant the challenge is that he's a black athlete," said Patricia Smith, a trial consultant, alluding to the 1995 trial of O J Simpson, the former football star who was acquitted of murdering his ex-wife and her friend. "A huge percentage of Americans think he got off because of who he was and think Kobe Bryant might get off because of who he is." Prosecutors will claim at trial that Mr Bryant met the woman, who has not been officially identified, at the Lodge and Spa Cordillera, a luxury resort in Edwards, Colorado, in June 2003. Nineteen years old at the time, she worked on the front desk. They will attempt to prove that he took her to his room and forcibly had sex with her over a chair. Mr Bryant has pleaded not guilty but has admitted to having consensual sex with the woman. If found guilty, the star, who remains a player with the Lakers, could face four years to life in prison or 20 years to life on probation, and a fine of up to $ 750,000. At stake also is Mr Bryant's formidable career, not to mention his future endorsing products. Measuring 6ft 6in, he became a role model for many young black Americans after turning professional on leaving high school and quickly transferring to the Lakers. Uncertainty lingers over proceedings in Eagle, however, following a last-minute objection last week by prosecutors. They claimed that DNA evidence from the defence, suggesting that the woman had had sex with someone else soon after seeing Mr Bryant, may have been contaminated. They want a separate hearing on the issue, which could delay the start of the trial itself. Judge Terry Ruckriegle gave the prosecution until Tuesday to present more evidence to back up its claim, and also chastised it for raising the DNA issue so late before the start of the trial. A defence lawyer, Hal Haddon, criticised prosecutors for their late intervention. "These motions are humbugs," he said, "designed to confuse the jury." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 A half-dozen Canadians who partied in Calgary with the woman who has accused basketball star Kobe Bryant of rape are expected testify in the Colorado trial. Testimony in the case is expected to begin next week. The witness list, obtained by Denver TV station KCNC, shows as many as 150 people may take the stand. Beyond the accuser, ex-boyfriends and the Cordillera bellman who was the first person the woman spoke to after the alleged attack, the witness list includes the Canadian group and a representative of a rehab facility the woman reportedly attended and a company that made controversial Kobe Bryant "hangman" T-shirts. Also on Tuesday, Sports Illustrated magazine reported that soon after Bryant was accused of rape, he told Colorado detectives he knew the accuser wanted to have sex with him because of the look on her face -- but he would pay her off to get the matter dropped, according to a new report. Bryant made the comments during a 75-minute interview with two Eagle County sheriff's detectives who taped the conversation surreptitiously. Lawyers for the L.A. Laker fought hard to get the statements barred, but the judge ruled prosecutors can use it against Bryant at trial, which began last week with jury selection. What Bryant told the sheriffs in the wee hours of July 2, 2003, has been kept secret by the court, but Sports Illustrated obtained a copy of the investigators' report. "Bryant acknowledged talking with the woman . . . but denied having sex with her. After the detectives said they had evidence to the contrary, Bryant admitted he did have sex with the woman but denied any use of force," the magazine reported. headline: KOBE BRYANT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE PROSECUTION of basketball superstar Kobe Bryant on rape charges was on the verge of collapse last night as prosecution sources told several media outlets they would ask the judge to drop the charges as early as today. The case, which caused a sensation when it first surfaced because of the Los Angeles Laker's hitherto squeaky-clean image, has been in trouble for months because of conflicting evidence surrounding last year's encounter between Mr Bryant and a young receptionist at a hotel in the Colorado Rockies. The accuser alleged she had medical evidence to show that Mr Bryant had forced himself on her in his room - contradicting the star's assertion that their encounter had been consensual. However, a DNA test traced hair and semen found in her underwear to someone other than Mr Bryant, indicating she had had more than one sexual partner around the time of the alleged assault. The prosecution fought doggedly to keep that evidence out of the courtroom, arguing that the alleged victim's sexual habits and history were her own private business and that airing them in public would merely compound her trauma. Judge Terry Ruckriegle, however, ruled in July that the usual "rape shield" provisions did not apply in this case because any sexual encounters in the period immediately preceding or following the alleged attack were too pertinent to ignore. The Colorado Supreme Court refused to hear a prosecution appeal on the rape shield question. Until yesterday, it appeared the case in the town of Eagle, Colorado would go ahead regardless. Jury selection began last Friday, and lawyers on both sides predicted that the trial proper would get under way early next week. The prosecution has been dogged by new problems, however. Potential jurors were asked in a questionnaire whether they thought Mr Bryant was guilty or innocent. So many of them answered either "definitely not guilty" or "probably not guilty" that the prosecution made a special plea to the judge to throw out everyone in those two categories. It is not clear what the judge told the prosecutors in response. A filing by the defence, meanwhile, alleged that a forensics expert consulted by the prosecution had information that "undermined the accuser's allegations and the prosecution's case, and corroborated Mr Bryant's defence on a central issue - the cause and significance of the accuser's alleged injuries". The filing said defence lawyers had only found out the extent of the witness's opinions after they spoke to him last Friday. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The Kobe Bryant case collapsed yesterday as prosecutors said they had no choice but to drop a sexual assault charge against the National Basketball Association star because the complainant did not want to testify. With the parents of the 20-year-old accuser looking on, District Judge Terry Ruckriegle dismissed the case under a deal that means no charges will be refiled. "The people have filed a motion to dismiss this case based on the fact the sole victim at this time is unable to go forward," District Attorney Mark Hurlbert told the judge. He said the complainant did not want to participate in the trial. The woman's lawyer, John Clune, said she had been through an extremely difficult time over the past 14 months and was disturbed by a series of courthouse mistakes that included the release of her name and medical history. "The difficulties that this case has imposed on this woman in the past year are unimaginable." Ruckriegle admitted mistakes had been made and took full responsibility. The prosecution dropped the case as jury selection was nearing an end. Opening statements had been expected next Tuesday. Bryant, 26, has said he had consensual sex with a then-19-year-old employee of a Vail-area resort where he stayed last summer. Had he been convicted, the Los Angeles Lakers star would have faced four years to life in prison or 20 years to life on probation, and a fine of up to US$750,000 ($1.15 million). Bryant still faces a federal civil lawsuit filed by the woman, who is seeking unspecified damages. Defence lawyers this week asked the judge to dismiss the assault charge, saying prosecutors had refused to turn over details that could suggest Bryant is innocent. Court rules require prosecutors and defence lawyers to exchange evidence and witness opinions before a trial. In a motion made public yesterday, the defence said a forensics expert prosecutors had planned to call as a witness had information that "undermined the accuser's allegations and the prosecution's case, and corroborated Mr Bryant's defence on a central issue - the cause and significance of the accuser's alleged injuries". The defence has said that injuries found on the woman could have been caused by sex with someone else close to the time of her encounter with Bryant.AP Classification Classification Classification fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Language: ENGLISH Subject: BASKETBALL (92%); fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LEGAL MOVES US basketball star Kobe Bryant , his criminal charge of raping a young woman dismissed, faces what could be a nasty civil lawsuit, lawyers and experts said today. "It's not going to be a cakewalk for Kobe Bryant , but it could be a mudslide for his accuser," according to Denver lawyer and former prosecutor Craig Silverman, who watched the criminal case. After weeks of speculation that the criminal charge against the Los Angeles Lakers player would be dropped because the case was weak, prosecutors gave up yesterday in the middle of jury selection. District Attorney Mark Hurlbert said the accuser, who had received death threats, did not want to testify. A statement from Bryant yesterday apologised for the pain suffered by the woman, who was 19 at the time. In the civil suit, 26-year-old Bryant's defence team will be able to delve into her sexual history, as well as reported suicide attempts and drug and alcohol abuse. Ready to ask Bryant the tough questions will be the woman's lawyers, John Clune and Lin Wood. "There are risks on both sides digging into the past of both of the parties," Arthur Hellman, a professor at the University of Pittsburgh School of Law, said. "It seems to me that both parties would find it advantageous to settle sooner rather than later," Hellman said. The burden of proof for a plaintiff in a civil lawsuit is lower than in criminal cases because a defendant faces money damages and not the risk of prison. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 They'll settle. That's the legal experts' prediction for the federal civil lawsuit filed against Kobe Bryant by the woman who has accused him of raping her. The sexual-assault charge against him was dropped last week. "And Kobe's going to pay her a bunch of money," said Denver lawyer Dan Recht. "And probably sooner rather than later," added Denver lawyer Scott Robinson. "Most do settle," Denver lawyer Craig Silverman said of civil lawsuits in general. Here, they said, are some factors affecting the federal civil case of Jane Doe vs. Kobe Bryant : 1. MONEY, FOR STARTERS Colorado law caps awards for non-economic damages -- such things as pain and suffering, humiliation and emotional distress -- at about $366,000. With some legal arguing and manoeuvering, that can be doubled in some cases. Economic damages -- for such things as medical expenses and possible lost wages -- likely would be small. Punitive damages aren't generally allowed in such cases, but if they were allowed in this case, they couldn't be higher than the amount of total damages, Robinson said. He said that puts the highest possible award in the lawsuit at about $1.5 million. Recht thought the maximum possible award would be closer to $1 million, and then only with difficulty. Settlements reached out of court, however, have no limits. "It seems to me she's wanting to be compensated in dollars," Recht said, "and Kobe Bryant has lots of dollars." Bryant's current contract with the Los Angeles Lakers will pay him $136 million to play basketball for the team. 2. OH! THE SHAME! Both sides have much to lose if the case goes to trial. "Amazingly, most of the world still doesn't know the dirty details of the sexual act, and I'm sure Kobe Bryant would prefer it remain that way," Silverman said. "He's an international celebrity." "He doesn't have clean hands in this," Recht said. "At a minimum, he was acting in an adulterous fashion. At worst, he forced a woman to have sex against her will." Bryant's alleged victim also would have to face the public revelation of some information about her that was excluded from the criminal case: her medical and psychiatric history, including alleged suicide attempts and possible drug and alcohol abuse. "And her entire social life becomes fair game for defence lawyers," Robinson said. "No case in Colorado applies the rape shield law to a civil case." The rape-shield law prohibits the use of evidence of an accuser's sexual history in criminal trials, unless it is found to be relevant to the defence. In a civil trial, though, much more information about the woman's personal life would be relevant than in the criminal case. "She's claiming that Kobe Bryant ruined her life," Silverman said, "and Kobe Bryant is entitled to show that she wasn't having that great a life before she ever met him." 3. MONEY, AGAIN Civil litigation is very expensive. Bryant can afford it. But the alleged victim's lawyers, L. Lin Wood and John Clune, "may have to make a business decision," Silverman said. He said they may have to pay all of the expenses on their client's behalf -- including such things as the cost of travelling to take depositions -- in the hope of getting that money back from any award she eventually wins from a jury. Unless Bryant's alleged victim loses at trial. Then her lawyers might get nothing. 4. HUMAN NATURE A trial could happen despite all the legal and financial obstacles if either Bryant or his alleged victim insists on going ahead. "The only thing that remains to be resolved is everybody's desire to vindicate his or her own interests," Robinson said. "Those are the wild cards." "Maybe Kobe wants to clear his name through a trial," Silverman said. In the end, the experts agreed, a settlement is likely because a civil trial would be a lot of trouble and expense and just isn't necessary. 5. ON THE OTHER HAND . . . The burden of proof in a civil case is easier to meet. Where a criminal charge must be proved beyond a reasonable doubt, a civil claim must be proved only by a preponderance of the evidence -- a slight tilt in favor of one side. In addition, Bryant couldn't invoke his Fifth Amendment rights against self-incrimination and refuse to answer questions. He would have to answer them. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Colour Photo: The Associated Press; Los Angeles Laker Kobe Bryant (right), seated next to his wife, Vanessa, during a news conference last year, no longer has to worry about criminal charges, but still has to deal with the threat of a civil lawsuit. Some experts expect he'll settle out of court. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 After his rape charge was dropped last week, Kobe Bryant stated that he "now" understands the accuser "feels that she did not consent to this encounter" ("Judge dismisses Bryant sex charge," Cover story, News, Thursday). What? Soon after, Rod Thorn, president of the New Jersey Nets basketball team, said, "Kobe has always had a very good reputation in everything he has done. Having these charges dropped says a lot about what actually happened" ("Bryant damaged as endorser," Sports, Thursday). Huh? I am dumbfounded by these statements. The only thing I know that really happened is that a judge's rulings consistently favored Bryant, and that fact, along with numerous media leaks, made prosecution virtually impossible. The accuser's sex life was obviously becoming the central issue, despite the fact that many men have been convicted of rape because they ignored the well-established premise that a "no" at any point must be respected. Women's rights advocates should hope that future plaintiffs in sexual-assault cases are not treated in the hostile manner that I feel Eagle County, Colo., District Court Judge Terry Ruckriegle treated Bryant's accuser. By the way, why have we not heard from those groups as this judge's questionable rulings were unfolding? William E. Lewis Marietta, Ga. Shield for the taxpayers Kobe Bryant 's accuser has decided that taking the stand in a criminal trial against the basketball player would be too much to bear, but going forward with a civil trial is OK. Isn't it time to enact a law called "the Taxpayer Shield"? The accuser in this case has decided that she cannot take the pressure of the witness stand for the sake of mere justice. But she likely will find the fortitude for a couple million dollars. How about she repay the hardworking taxpayers of Eagle, Colo., for the millions of dollars they have spent promoting her desire for justice during the past year? Let's start shielding the taxpayers, too. David Ruffley Mullineux Snohomish, Wash. Life lessons learned Regardless of whether justice was actually served in the Kobe Bryant rape-trial-that-never-happened, there are important and vital life lessons to be learned from the experiences of Bryant and other professional athletes in the news recently. Don't smoke illegal substances, and you won't be suspended from work (former Miami Dolphin Ricky Williams); don't cheat on your spouse, and you won't be accused of rape ( Kobe Bryant ); don't swindle the system, and you won't bear permanent defeat (Pete Rose). Live a life of honesty and integrity, and these unfortunate, sadly ubiquitous situations won't occur; they won't even be possible. Don't open the door to impropriety and invite it in. Lock the door, and turn a deaf ear to its incessant knocking. Eventually, it will tire and turn away -- and you will be able to look into the mirror with dignity and peace. Christine McGaugh Phoenix Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PHOTO, B/W, M. headline: PUT KOBE'S SEX LIFE OFF LIMITS - LAWYER
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NBA STAR Kobe Bryant 's lawyers, who pried into the sexual past of the woman who accused him of raping her, now are asking a judge to stop her lawyers from doing the same to him. "Plaintiff's counsel has indicated that he intends to depose Mr. Bryant on sexual matters having no possible relevance to the claims and defenses in this action," Bryant's attorneys wrote in court filings. The accuser was 19 last summer when she claimed Bryant raped her in a Colorado hotel room. She dropped her criminal case against the Los Angles Laker player after receiving death threats and being embarrassed by the release of her name and details of her sex life. Prosecutors complained Bryant's lawyers were intimidating the woman by delving into her sexual history. The woman since has filed a civil case against Bryant, and lawyers on both sides have been working out the details of Bryant's upcoming deposition. The woman's attorney has said he also wants to depose Bryant's wife, Vanessa, sometime in January. Bryant's attorneys want the courts to issue a confidentiality order that would prohibit the woman's lawyers from asking the NBA basketball star his sexual history and also limit his deposition to just one session. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant will miss at least five games with a severely sprained right ankle, but the Los Angeles Lakers said Friday that they would not know until Monday exactly how long he would be out. Bryant was injured in the first quarter of the Lakers' 98-94 victory over Cleveland on Thursday night. Coach Rudy Tomjanovich said Bryant would be placed on the injured list and replaced on the roster by the rookie guard Tony Bobbitt before the Lakers' game at Golden State on Saturday night. An M.R.I. exam Friday confirmed that Bryant had a severely sprained ankle. The Lakers said he would be re-evaluated on Monday and an estimate on how long he will be sidelined will be made at that time. Bryant, 26, is the N.B.A.'s second-leading scorer with a 27.5-point average. He was injured when he landed on Ira Newble's right foot while going for a rebound under Cleveland's basket. ''We will miss him greatly, but this is an opportunity to grow as a team,'' Tomjanovich said before the Lakers flew to Oakland for their game against the Warriors. Bryant, has played in every Lakers game this season despite having soreness in his left foot because of plantar fasciitis. He entered Thursday night's game averaging an N.B.A.-leading 43.1 minutes a game. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jackson's lawyer told a court yesterday he planned to call a star-studded list of witnesses to defend the singer against child molestation charges, including basketball 's Kobe Bryant , actress Elizabeth Taylor and Tonight Show host Jay Leno. Lead Jackson attorney Tom Meserau recited his list of proposed witnesses as jury selection resumed in the high-profile case in which the 46-year-old pop star is accused of sexually molesting a then 13-year-old boy who lived at his Neverland Valley Ranch. Meserau's list included the boy who accuses Jackson and members of the youth's family. He also said he would call singers Nick Carter, Stevie Wonder, Barry Gibb and Diana Ross, comedian Chris Tucker, talk-show hosts Larry King and Maury Povich and lifestyle guru Deepak Chopra. Bryant, a star player for the Los Angeles Lakers, has legal problems of his own. He has been sued in civil court for rape by a Colorado woman who says he attacked her in 2003 in a Vail-area hotel. Bryant has said the two had consensual sex, and charges against him were dropped last September after the woman decided she did not want to proceed. Jackson is charged in a 10-count Santa Barbara County Grand Jury indictment with molesting a boy at Neverland and with conspiring to commit child abduction, extortion and false imprisonment. He has pleaded not guilty. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE votes have been counted and the winner was a record-breaker: the Houston Rockets' Yao Ming. The Chinese was chosen as the starting centre for the West in today's National Basketball Association All-Star Game for the third year in a row, collecting more votes (2,558,278) than anyone in history. No surprise there, with voting open to fans worldwide. Shaquille O'Neal collected the second-highest total with 2,488,089, as he earned the starting centre spot for the East. How the two giants cope with one another in their first All-Star game on opposing sides will be one of the major plot lines in today's game in Denver. Yao, though, knew what he was waiting for. 'Shaq and Kobe,' he said, referring to the feud between O'Neal and former Lakers team-mate Kobe Bryant , who will start for the West. 'How about Yao v Shaq?', Yao was asked in return. 'I won't be using all six of my fouls against him,' he predicted. ALL-STAR ROSTERS East starters: Allen Iverson (Philadelphia), LeBron James (Cleveland), Vince Carter (New Jersey), Grant Hill (Orlando), Shaquille O'Neal (Miami). West starters: Tracy McGrady (Houston), Kobe Bryant (Los Angeles Lakers), Tim Duncan (San Antonio), Kevin Garnett (Minnesota), Yao Ming (Houston). fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Shaq and Kobe's icy relationship shows no signs of thawing The relationship between former teammates Kobe Bryant and Shaquille O'Neal continued its icy course at the weekend's all-star festivities in Denver. The feuding stars didn't talk all weekend and didn't make eye contact or shake hands at the opening tip of Sunday's game. By the time the weekend was over, Bryant was tired of talking about their strained relationship. "I'm really not going to make this weekend about myself and Shaquille. That's just not fair, that's not basketball ," Bryant said. "It's a great weekend with a lot of great basketball being played. Obviously, I respect Shaquille tremendously for what he can do on the basketball floor and vice versa. Even when we played together we weren't the best of buddies, but we won three titles so I think everybody has to kind of leave that in the past and move on from it." O'Neal was apparently tired of the conversation as well. When asked why he didn't talk to Bryant all weekend, the big fella just broke into laughter. Grandfather's death leaves Kobe in limbo Kobe Bryant 's status for the Lakers' game tonight against the visiting Celtics is up in the air after his grandfather died over the weekend. He was expected to miss practice yesterday and it was unclear whether he'd be back in time for the game. Bobcats expect Okafor to return tonight Charlotte Bobcats rookie Emeka Okafor is expected to return to the lineup tonight against Milwaukee, the team said. Okafor, the No. 2 pick in the June draft, is recovering from a sprained left ankle that forced him to miss the Rookie Challenge during the NBA all-star weekend. Averaging 14.8 points and 11 rebounds, Okafor was hurt Jan. 30 against the Los Angeles Lakers and spent five games on the injured list. He re-injured the ankle against Miami and missed the Bobcats' last three games. Clippers put Livingston on the disabled list The Los Angeles Clippers signed guard Maurice Baker to a 10-day contract and placed guard Shaun Livingston on the disabled list with torn cartilage in his right shoulder. Baker has averaged 16.9 points, 6.7 assists and 5.4 rebounds this season with the Dakota Wizards of the CBA. Livingston injured his shoulder in the game against the Orlando Magic on Feb. 15 and is expected to miss three weeks. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 A civil lawsuit against the basketball star Kobe Bryant brought by a woman who said he raped her in a Colorado resort hotel quietly folded yesterday with a one-paragraph statement saying that the parties had reached terms of settlement. No terms were disclosed. The end of the case, which had sputtered for months here in Federal District Court, with each side threatening to bring out damaging information about the other, puts a final coda on a story that for most of its arc produced much more smoke than fire - and ultimately no final answers about what really happened. Bryant said he and the woman had consensual sex after she came to his room at a hotel near Vail on June 30, 2003. She said they kissed and flirted, but that he then became violent and sexually assaulted her. A criminal charge of sexual assault against Bryant was dropped last September when the woman said she no longer wished to participate in the case. She had meanwhile sought unspecified monetary damages against him in the civil suit. The lawyers for Bryant and the woman, whose name is being withheld, issued a statement saying the matter had been "resolved to the satisfaction of both parties,'' and that "no further comments about the matter can or will be made.'' Legal experts said the inconclusive end to both of the cases stemmed from the same issue - one side or the other, or perhaps both, didn't want the matter to unfold in public. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The Carl's Jr. restaurant chain will sell a Kobe Bryant bobblehead doll next month, the first time the Los Angeles Lakers All-Star will be featured in a promotion since rape charges against him were dropped in September. The doll is part of a series of five Lakers bobbleheads that the fast-food chain will sell in its Los Angeles and Orange County stores for US$4.99 with a purchase of a combo meal, Carl's Jr. said in a press release. Rape charges against Bryant, 26, were dropped in September when the accuser, a 19-year-old woman, decided not to testify. Bryant last week settled a lawsuit the woman filed against him. He was accused of raping the woman in June 2003 at a resort in Colorado. Bryant, who is married and has a daughter, has admitted he committed adultery, but insists the sex was consensual. He signed a seven-year, US$136-million contract with the Lakers during the off-season. The seven-time National Basketball Association All-Star lost endorsements with McDonald's Corp. and Ferrero SpA, Italy's largest chocolate maker, after the encounter, while Coca-Cola Co. stopped making commercials with him. Carl's Jr. is a family-oriented eatery, and some parents may not be keen on the doll of the accused rapist. The chain's Web site features wholesome giveaway toys for kids, including one in which the Angelina Jolie character in Shark Tale wiggles her fins if you blow bubbles out the wand attached to her mouth. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The accolades continue to pile up for Louis Williams. The South Gwinnett star has been named the Naismith Award winner as the nation's best high school player. Williams, also a McDonald's All-American, confirmed he'd been chosen the winner by the Atlanta Tip-Off Club. He leaves today for South Bend, Ind., to play in next week's McDonald's game at Notre Dame. "It's just a tremendous accomplishment," said Williams, who is mulling a jump to the NBA. "I've been getting a lot of awards, but this is the award that really makes you excited about it, this and the [Georgia's] Mr. Basketball . This is one of those things I always wanted to do in my career." The formal announcement will come at the Naismith Awards banquet in Atlanta on April 8. Williams will be in Memphis that week preparing to play in the Nike Hoop Summit, but USA Basketball will fly him back to receive the award. The Naismith Prep Player of the Year Award was established in 1987 to honor the nation's top male and female players. Previous winners include such notables as LeBron James (2003), Raymond Felton (2002), Shane Battier (1997), Al Harrington (1998) and Kobe Bryant (1996). Williams, a 6-foot-2 guard, becomes the second consecutive Georgian to win the honor, joining former Southwest Atlanta Christian Academy star and eventual No. 1 NBA draft pick Dwight Howard. "I'm extremely happy for him," South coach Roger Fleetwood said. "He's had an unbelievable four years in high school. This kind of ends it the right way." Williams averaged 27.5 points, 6.5 rebounds, 6.0 assists and 3.0 steals per game as a senior to help lead the Comets to the Class AAAAA state quarterfinal and earn the state's Mr. Georgia Basketball honor. As a junior, he led his team to the school's first state championship. Williams finished his career as the leading scorer in both school and Gwinnett County history, with well over 3,000 points. He became the first Gwinnett player ever selected to the McDonald's All-American game. He will join 11 of the nation's top high school players against a strong international field for the Nike Hoop Summit on April 9 before joining former teammate Mike Mercer in New York for the Jordan Classic game at Madison Square Garden on April 16. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MIAMI -- Kobe Bryant is no longer one of the 10 best basketball players in the world. He remains exceptional, just not elite. He falls somewhere in the 11-to-20 range -- in the group with Paul Pierce, Allen Iverson and Vince Carter, but behind Shaquille O'Neal, Tim Duncan, Kevin Garnett, LeBron James, Dirk Nowitzki, Jermaine O'Neal, Amare Stoudemire, Steve Nash, Tracy McGrady and, as Chicago Bulls coach Scott Skiles said last week, Dwyane Wade. Basketball isn't like baseball or football. One player can very much alter the look of an entire team, and make it a winner by himself if he's good enough. That's why Garnett and Shaq and Duncan always have winning records, no matter the supporting cast. You'll find very few top-10 talents in the history of basketball with a record as bad as the one Bryant's Lakers have this season. L.A. is closer to the bottom of the standings than the top. It is going to finish the year out of the playoffs and about 10 games below .500. Bryant has no help? Wade took many of his teammates deep into the playoffs last season, didn't he? The Lakers have a worse record than the Clippers. Safe to say that wouldn't happen with any other player considered one of the 10 best in the world. Bryant has been exposed. Again, he still is very good. But also overrated. The next Michael? Please. Jordan shot more than 50 per cent with Bill Wennington as his centre. Bryant's shooting percentage has tumbled from 48 with Shaq to 43 per cent now that Shaq has left his side. There are a lot of guys in the NBA, Ricky Pierce included, who could be second in the league in scoring if they ran the entire team, got carte blanche on shots and turned the ball over as much as Bryant does. That's why Bryant is not unlike Iverson now, a gunner who can fill it up whenever he pleases without involving those around him. Bottom line: The teams of top 10 players don't have a record of 34-42. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Then the heavens parted, a mighty thunderclap was heard, Phil Jackson discovered his little vision quest ... and they all lived happily ever after? Who knew life could be so simple, bitterness so easily dissolved? Phil finally communed with the big chief in the sky and decided he really did want to return to the Lakers, Kobe Bryant and all. Like this little Kobe problem was nothing. Like it was this minor annoyance, effortlessly solved and made ancient history. Jackson returned yesterday to the Lakers, and absolutely, as he first stepped to the podium at Staples Center, it felt weird. Meet the new boss, and not just the same as the old boss. He really IS the old boss! "I never thought this could possibly happen,'' said Jackson, echoing just about every one in Los Angeles, including Kobe. Yes, the great soap opera has returned to the Lakers, only this time we sub the all that Shaq v. Kobe intrigue for the Phil v. Kobe. Sports reality TV, with a few basketball games thrown in. Phil is back, and bring on the subplots. But last year was a humbling experience for everyone associated with the Lakers, perhaps Kobe particularly, and it's not necessary they have each over for beers and hugs. Above all else, Kobe is an intense competitor. He wants to win, and he has to know his chances of doing so are better with Phil than without. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 You applaud when you see the names LeBron James and Dwyane Wade. You say "about time" when you see a shooter such as Michael Redd on the list. But once you analyze the preliminary roster for the U.S. national basketball team - and notice the omission of Allen Iverson - the nausea that instinctively kicks in should not result from the inclusion of Luke Ridnour or rookie Chris Paul over the 76ers' dynamic superstar. Try the name Kobe Bryant . It may sting a little bit, but deal with it. When you have Kobe Bryant , you don't need another shooter or scorer. You don't need a point guard who likes to shoot or score, either. You barely need another defender and, without question, you'd be hard-pressed to find a better winner or leader when it comes to basketball . Of course, you'll never hear Jerry Colangelo, the national team's managing director, say such a thing. Nor will you hear it from any member of USA Basketball , because they know what we all know: Iverson's talent and unwavering desire to try out for the Olympic team should far exceed any requirement for the World Championships, as well as the 2008 Games in Beijing. "The fact that we had the interview, that we talked about it and gave it a lot of consideration, is not disrespect at all to anyone, especially Allen Iverson," Colangelo said from Phoenix yesterday, before heading to New York today to announce the preliminary roster. "I have great respect for Iverson. He is a bona-fide superstar in this league. But when you put the team together, you stand up and pick your team. People always get left out, but that happens when you're assembling a team. "This is about molding a team, about having people who complement one another. When you look at the team we've assembled, the people we initially picked, we have enough players who wreak havoc offensively. It's about choices." Which brings us back to Kobe Bryant . Colangelo said he interviewed 36 players - and 28 accepted. What he didn't say was that he did not interview Bryant. He asked the three-time NBA champion if he would play and, 10 minutes later, Bryant accepted. Period. The coach of the team is Duke's Mike Krzyzewski, who happens to be the coach Bryant might have played for had he attended college, and is the same man who turned down the chance about 18 months ago to succeed Phil Jackson as the coach of the Lakers. This team is being built around Bryant - a star fixated on the roundball like no other. Iverson's 72d career 40-point game Friday, his 33.4 points-per-game average, may continue to solidify him as a future Hall of Famer, but they do nothing to make him anything other than an unfair casualty in these Olympics festivities, which is the other part of this continuing saga of untold stories. No one would dare blame Iverson for the team's bronze-medal finish in Athens. But while USA Basketball was very pleased with what he did in front of the cameras, they weren't too pleased with what they perceived he did behind them. If coach Larry Brown alienated Stephon Marbury and Carmelo Anthony, Iverson got blamed for it. If the team shot too many jumpers, the finger was pointed at Iverson. If James sauntered into film sessions or practices late, the perception was Iverson had something to do with that, too. And yes, let us not be fools here: Iverson himself was late a couple of times. For those who doubt whether Brown and Iverson had some sort of negative impact, ponder this: Billy King, the Sixers' president/general manager, served on the executive committee of USA Basketball for eight years. He's the guy who pushed for both Brown and Iverson on the 2004 Olympic team. King no longer serves on that committee. He was removed shortly after Team USA brought home bronze instead of gold. "It's not about having a star-studded team, it's the composition of the team that matters," Colangelo explained. "We looked at our last team and thought some changes were needed, so we've gone about the business of making them." In other words, you won't see Richard Jefferson starting over Shawn Marion. Or players like James or Amare Stoudemire riding the bench. You won't see any point guards like Marbury interested in quitting before the Games begin, demoralized before opening ceremonies. One would think none of this should have anything to do with Iverson, considering how well he's played over the last few years, especially since Team USA swears it's thinking about the future, about how a player's game will evolve over the next few years instead of where he is right now. Evidently, though, they thought about the past as much as the future. Commissioner David Stern would love to have Iverson on the roster, for global marketing purposes if nothing else, and still, the Sixers star was not invited. It doesn't matter what that tells the rest of us. Only what it tells Iverson. Stephen A. Smith | ONLINE EXTRA Could Kobe Bryant and Allen Iverson hav

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 With literally no room to breathe, a frustrated guard attempts to advance the ball up court. He's seen a full-court press before, but the man-to-man intensity of this one is starting to get to him. With all the passing lanes blocked, he goes for the crossover. Anticipating this move, junior guard Oliver Lafayette strips the ball away, sparks a fast break and shifts the momentum of the game, all of which translates to a UH win. This scenario became a familiar and welcome reality to the fans who attended last season's men's basketball home games. Beginning the day he put on a UH uniform, Lafayette has taken the challenge of guarding other squads' best scorers and has rarely failed to hold them below their season averages. Even though last season was Lafayette's first chance to play Division I ball, his accomplishments did not start and will not end there. "The best game I've ever played in would have to be when we won the national championship in (junior college),"Lafayette said. "We played (Kirkwood) for the national championship when I was at Brown Mackie. That was the best game I've ever played in." With the help of Lafayette, Brown Mackie won the National Junior College Athletics Association Division II championship 77-65 over Kirkwood. After his role in the championship game and leading the team in nearly all statistical categories, Lafayette was selected as the NJCAA Division II player of the year. After leaving Brown Mackie as the team's leading scorer, he shocked the college basketball world by combining for a total of 60 points in back-to-back games against Louisiana State and Arizona last season while becoming the Cougars' leading scorer with 15.7 points per contest. His 27 point second half explosion against LSU rides shotgun in Lafayette's favorite basketball moments. The Baton Rouge native still has a slight grudge against the purple and gold Tigers. "Playing against my home town at LSU \'c4\'ee you know, proving to them that I was one of the best players in the Louisiana area,'Lafayette said. "It's not that they didn't want to take me. It was that they didn't recruit me hard enough." With a little motivation, Lafayette accounted for more than one-third of Houston's points in an 84-83 victory over the NCAA Tournament runner-up. This marked the second year Houston had beaten the Tigers and was enough to scare them away from scheduling another game against UH for 2006-07. Lafayette also likes to keep up with things in the professional basketball world. He's already made some of his predictions about the teams to watch out for in the coming NBA season. "I think Phoenix is going to make a major comeback with the return of (Amare) Stoudamire. That's the team to watch. Chicago will be good with Tyrus Thomas and all those other young guys, especially Ben Gordon \'c4\'ee he's been stepping up. They'll be good." As far as favorite players go, his past and present are Michael Jordan and Kobe Bryant . "Growing up it was always MJ. Right now I like Kobe Bryant 's game," Lafayette said. "I like Dwayne Wade's game. But Kobe Bryant has the full package. He's got the shot. He defends the best player every night. He's just the total package," he said. The Cougars are a team that knows how to play solid fundamental basketball and distribute the ball, as they had three scorers, including Lafayette, average more than 10 points per game. Last year the team thrived off of fast breaks forced by defensive pressure. It also relied heavily on the three pointer, which occasionally got them into trouble. However, Lafayette said he the Cougars will be a better all around team this season. "I feel good. I feel good about the team. We've got a big squad. We've got more scorers on the team,"Lafayette said. "We just got to keep up the defense and we'll do good this year. We've got to do what we did last year. We've got to stay in the passing lanes, pick up the loose balls, and box-out. We did all that stuff last year. Now we've just got to get up on our scoring this year," he said. "Yeah, I'll get more 30-point games this year than I had last year. I've been working out everyday. "I've been working on my ball handling so I can start bringing the ball up the court. Then I've been working out in the gym so I can start shooting the ball better." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Mr 81 is coming to Hong Kong and he wants to play ball. Los Angeles Lakers superstar Kobe Bryant will visit Hong Kong this week as part of his 2006 summer Asia tour. Bryant scored an astonishing 81 points in a match against the Toronto Raptors last season. After the game, his teammates began calling him "81". The Philadelphia-raised phenomenon, who holds eight NBA and 30 Lakers records, will arrive in Hong Kong from Tokyo tomorrow for a two-day visit. He will make charity appearances on Wednesday morning, and visit sports shops in Mongkok to promote the new Kobe 1 shoes. The highlight of his visit will be at 4.30pm the same day. Bryant will take part in a basketball clinic for 400 fans, mostly Nike Youth League participants, at Hong Kong Polytechnic University's Shaw Sports Complex in Hunghom. The sponsors of the event said Bryant will take questions from fans who can show off their skills to the NBA star. He will leave for Taiwan on Thursday morning. Other stops on his tour include Seoul and Beijing. "I'm really looking forward to my trip, meeting kids, hosting clinics and having some fun playing the game of basketball ," Bryant said ahead of his Asian tour. "Having support from my fans in Asia has inspired me. And for me to get an opportunity to return that favour by flying to Asia and embracing them is exciting." Off the court, Bryant believes in helping the community. During his visit, he will participate in charity donation ceremonies. " Basketball is such a big part of my life, but it pales in comparison to what you can do once you step off the court," he said. "Once you're a basketball player, you're a role model, and there's a great opportunity to inspire, help and guide others. I enjoy that and am blessed to be able to do that." Outstanding local junior players who will meet the Lakers guard at the clinic include Lo Yi-ting and Lee Kiu-fung. "I've always admired his great moves, skill and his never-give-up attitude," said Kiu-fung. "Meeting him in Hong Kong will be a great opportunity for me to learn from one of the best players in the world." For Yi-ting, it is the second time he will be meeting an NBA star this summer, after he shook hands with LeBron James in Hong Kong last month. "Meeting two great players like LBJ and Kobe this summer is a dream come true," said Yi-ting. "Their visits have inspired my game. I think they have succeeded because of their great attitude. I want to ask him [Bryant] how he could score 81 points in an NBA game." Fact file Kobe Bryant was on fire last season: His 81 points versus Toronto was the second-highest in NBA history behind the legendary Wilt Chamberlain's 100-point game in 1962. Bryant became the first player since 1964, and the only NBA star besides Chamberlain and Elgin Baylor, to score 45 points or more in four consecutive games. Bryant also set single-season records for the Lakers for the most 40-point games (27) and most points scored (2,832). Career highlights: Three-time NBA champion: 2000, 2001, 2002 NBA scoring champion: 2006 (35.4 points per game) Career 40-plus point games (regular season): 67 His NBA records include: Most three-pointers made in one game (12), and most consecutive three-pointers (9), January 7, 2003, vs Seattle SuperSonics. Most free throws made (one quarter): 14 Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Credit: Reuters; Asian tour ... Kobe Bryant comes to Hong Kong this week. headline: NBA LACK APPEAL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 You pay really good money, sometimes a king's ransom, to go to the Garden for the chance to see a couple of NBA superstars, none of whom happens to play for the home team, incidentally. You can count them on one hand, too: Kobe Bryant , Dwyane Wade, Shaquille O'Neal and LeBron James. Steve Nash? Fine, we'll include him, too. But that's about it, even if the NBA is constantly trying to tell you that it's got an endless array of superstars worthy of your entertainment dollar. Trust us, the list of must-see pro basketball players is shorter than it's ever been. So what in the name of Michael Jordan is the league thinking when it suspends Kobe Bryant for his one and only appearance in New York this season? Doesn't anyone over at the NBA remember that it's in the entertainment business, first and foremost? Last night, long-suffering New York fans were denied the chance to see the new Bryant, the wondrous talent who now puts his team first, doesn't want to score 81 points, but certainly could still do it if the Lakers ever need it. At 7:36, when the public address announcer said, " Kobe Bryant is not with the team, due to an NBA suspension," the house booed as if it were watching the Knicks on a mail-it-in night. When there is not much to cheer about on the home front, and that still applies to a large degree, as much as Isiah Thomas wants to tell you differently, the one reason to rush over to the Garden is to see someone special such as Bryant. So thanks a lot, Stu Jackson. In ruling that Bryant deserved his one-game suspension for decking the Spurs' Manu Ginobili with an elbow in the Lakers' overtime loss on Sunday, the league's judge and jury said that when Kobe flailed his arms and gave one of the game's biggest floppers a smack in his face, it was "not a natural basketball act." Wrong. Sitting him out for a game in New York, that is "not a natural basketball act." Actually, it didn't matter if the Lakers were starting their eight-game, 15-day road trip in Charlotte, Milwaukee, or some other back-water town. He didn't deserve to sit, not for trying to make it seem like he got fouled on his last-second shot attempt. That was the smart basketball play, one that the great ones learn to do at an early age, and the Spurs didn't even complain to the league about the incident. Who did? The Knicks? "The unfortunate part is that we play here one time during the year . . . a single game and people come to see our team play," Phil Jackson said beforehand. "They miss out on an event that they probably paid good money to watch." First off, it's an event because Bryant makes it one. He knows what it means to perform on Broadway. He's the reason the Lakers are still one of the league's top draws, even if it's been a long time since O'Neal wore the purple and gold. Entering last night's game, only one other team had a better road attendance average. That was Cleveland, as if you need to know why. But even LeBron, at this stage of his career, doesn't quite grasp what it means to walk out on the stage that is the Garden. Not like Kobe does. Not like Michael did. A few seasons ago, LeBron held himself out of a game with a bum ankle. If that was Jordan or Bryant, the show goes on. Judging from the photo tip sheet listing the season-high 18 celebrities sitting at courtside, New York's in-crowd was ready to watch Kobe. For the Lakers, there was nothing positive about his absence. "Never," Lamar Odom said. " Basketball is easier when you've got Kobe Bryant on the court." He got the bad news when the Lakers were starting their morning shoot-around. "We were all out on the court and Kobe was sitting on the side, and we were like, why isn't he out here with us?" said Lakers reserve Maurice Evans. "Then Phil told us about the suspension. You could tell how disappointed Kobe was. He only plays in the Garden one time a season, so you know he wanted to play here, very badly." Since he was in town, Bryant asked the league for a sit-down to hear his case and watch the tape. But about two hours before tip-off, the league had its decision: We're not meeting and you're not playing. Here in New York? Like we said, it's not a natural basketball act. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant was suspended one game without pay by the NBA yesterday for striking San Antonio's Manu Ginobili in the face. With his Los Angeles Lakers in New York to play the Knicks, Bryant requested an "immediate hearing" with the league office to appeal the suspension. But his request was denied because it would have deviated from standard procedure, and Bryant was still slated to miss the game last night. "This is not the process that we use at the NBA. Certainly, in theory, given the fact that the Lakers were in New York, we could have heard an appeal. But again, we never have, as it's not part of our process," said Stu Jackson, the league's chief disciplinarian for on-court actions. "He does have the right to an appeal at a later date. If he were to win that appeal, he would get his money back." Bryant's absence left the Lakers without their leading scorer when they opened an eight-game road trip. "I've been waiting to play here. It's always been a fun place for me to play here, and I'm surprised. Shocked, by it, actually," Bryant said. "I unintentionally caught Manu Ginobili. What do you say? It's a basketball game. You unintentionally catch people with elbows every once in a while." The play occurred with seconds remaining in the Lakers' 96-94 overtime loss to the Spurs on Sunday. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant 's only Madison Square Garden appearance of the season was nullified yesterday by a suspension. Had the Knicks managed their payroll better, Bryant sightings at the Garden might not be so rare. In the summer of 2004, Bryant was a free agent and the Knicks were an eager -- though financially hamstrung -- suitor. Armed with only the midlevel salary-cap exception (worth about $5 million in the first year), Knicks officials flew to Southern California and made their pitch. The effort was as bold as it was futile. As Bryant's employer, the Los Angeles Lakers were allowed to pay him the maximum salary, about $130 million over seven years. Other teams with salary-cap room could offer Bryant about $102 million over six years. All the Knicks could offer was the Garden's mystique and New York's marketing appeal. It was enough to make Bryant listen. Had the Knicks been able to make a competitive offer, Bryant might be wearing blue and orange today. ''The reason why I even was interested in taking that meeting was because of this city,'' the 28-year-old Bryant said in an interview yesterday morning. ''Because I always loved this city. Always. Ever since I came here, growing up in Philadelphia, there's always so much energy and so much buzz in this city. How passionate they are about basketball , how knowledgeable they are about basketball .'' Bryant, a nine-time All-Star, excitedly ticked off the names of the Knicks legends Walt Frazier, Willis Reed, Earl Monroe and Dave DeBusschere and said, ''I just wanted to play here so bad.'' ''I know what it's like to have a team here that's successful and how the city rallies around it,'' he said. ''So I was very interested. And if they had more cap flexibility, it'd definitely be a place where I would end up. I might have picked the Knicks.'' Bryant heavily weighed an offer from the Los Angeles Clippers before eventually re-signing with the Lakers, who have again become one of the elite teams in the league. The Knicks, meanwhile, continue to founder without a true franchise player and a payroll that is by far the highest in the league. Isiah Thomas, the Knicks' president, might have changed the history of both teams if he had more than the lure of New York to offer that day. Bryant said Thomas's pitch focused on marketing opportunities and the city's other inherent advantages. ''He was a good salesman,'' Bryant said. ''But again, you don't have to sell much. I know, I watch basketball , I've been a student of the game. I would love to have played here. The buzz, the energy, I mean this is a basketball city, there's no question about it. I would love to have played here.''
