fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Charles Barkley in a match race with 67-year-old NBA referee Dick Bavetta? The mind boggles. Might somebody need medical attention after tonight's match race, suddenly the most intriguing portion of All-Star Saturday night? "If anything, for Charles," Allen Iverson said, laughing. "Dick told me he's going to whup Charles' butt," the Los Angeles Lakers' Kobe Bryant said. "He said he's ready, he's in the best shape of his life and he's going to smoke him." Reporters told Bryant that Barkley, retired since the 1999-2000 season, had been working with former Olympic track star John Carlos. "I saw he was doing situps, or something of that sort," Bryant said, rising to the moment. "Charles has a longer stride. It'll be close, man. It could be a tie." Or it could be a blowout. "I'm going with Dick all the way," Miami's Dwyane Wade said. "Dick Bavetta is going to kill him by a mile. That's for Chuck not putting me in his top five. I don't really appreciate that, Chuck, wherever you're at." Mellow Carmelo Denver's Carmelo Anthony doesn't mind being an injury-replacement All-Star. "I'm here, I'm happy to be here, regardless how I got here," he said. And about those rumors that he and Allen Iverson weren't getting along? "It's been great," Anthony said. "He came to Denver, I was suspended. When I got back, he got hurt. I'm just waiting for him to get healthy so we can go out there and really show people what we're about. We're fine. We're good. We have a long season ahead. We have plenty of time." Missing Andre Miller? "We all do, especially me," Anthony replied about the point guard, now with the Sixers. Pippen return? Former All-Star Scottie Pippen, retired since the '03-04 season, was letting it be known that he's considering a comeback. "When he came in and worked out with us in training camp last year, he looked like he was in tip-top shape, like he never left the game," Kobe Bryant said. "If he's really serious about coming back, I would love for him to be in the triangle [offense] with us. He'd help out tremendously with his leadership, his experience, his professionalism, obviously knowing how to execute with the system." What about Tim? What about retired guard Tim Hardaway and the homophobic statements he recently made in the days after former NBA forward John Amaechi revealed he is gay? Hardaway apologized, but was barred from All-Star events. "You have to be open-minded," Kobe Bryant said. "What the comments showed is that there's a struggle. There's a long way to go, but I'm sure we'll get there one day." All-Star notes Coach Phil Jackson, former player Chris Mullin and college broadcaster Dick Vitale led the list of 15 finalists for induction into the Basketball Hall of Fame. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 There are so many people to write about for the race for MVP, but I narrowed it down to four fantastic players this season. Chris Bosh is the face of the franchise because, in the Raptors, he is the guy that can make that critical 3-pointer, a mighty dunk, or a nasty block. Bosh is having a career-best 23.1 points per game, and over 10 rebounds, Bosh scored 61 points in the last three games. If Bosh wins MVP, it will be no shock to him or his teammates. Kobe Bryant , I think, is the best basketball player in the NBA because he is averaging a shocking 29.0 points per game. Some people think that Kobe is selfish; I doubt it, because Kobe plays each game like he can never take it back. He scored 99 points in a three-game stretch this season. That is crazy. Once you give LeBron James the ball, there is no stopping him. It's like he invented basketball - those flashy dunks, layups and steals. LeBron is averaging a no-surprise 26.7 points per game. Now I don't want LeBron to win, but it's a possibility. Though Carmelo Anthony did get suspended 15 games a few months ago, he still has a chance to win MVP, but it's slim. He is averaging an NBA-best 30.6 points per game. I think he gets these fantastic points from Allen ( The Answer) Iverson. If Carmelo wants to win MVP he's got to step up his game; 30 points each game won't work for MVP after you missed 15 games. Everyone is curious about MVP, but looks like we have to wait and see! Warsame Raghe, 10, Grade 5, North York Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant plays each game like he can never take it back. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NEW ORLEANS - Kobe Bryant wants to be remembered for scoring points and winning basketball games, not the suspension-yielding swipes he's taken at opposing players. If he keeps this up, he may get his wish. Bryant scored 50 points to lead Los Angeles to a 111-105 victory over the New Orleans Hornets on Friday night, making the Lakers star only the second player in NBA history with at least that many points in four consecutive games. ''Remember that suspension two weeks ago?'' Lakers coach Phil Jackson said, referring to a one-game suspension Bryant got for hitting Timberwolves guard Marko Jaric. ''I think this has motivated him. I don't know if we like to have suspensions all of the time work for us, but there were some things there that motivated him.'' Bryant, who also was suspended in January for hitting San Antonio's Manu Ginobili, had scoring totals of 65, 50 and 60 in his previous three games, all Lakers victories. ''The thing that was frustrating for me was people were talking about, 'He's a dirty player,' which, for me, is pretty insulting,'' Bryant said. ''To have people talking about something else besides that - it's a much better feeling.'' Indeed, people are now talking about Bryant's place alongside the man the Lakers' star first knew as the character Bombaata in the movie ''Conan the Barbarian.'' Only the late Wilt Chamberlain has had more 50-point games in a row than Bryant, hitting that mark in seven consecutive games during the 1961-62 season. Bryant, of course, has long since learned all about Chamberlain, the basketball legend. He's also studied up on Elgin Baylor and knows Michael Jordan, the only other players to hit at least 50 three straight times. ''It's a tremendous honor for me to be in the same [category] with those guys,'' Bryant said. Bryant made all 16 free throws he attempted. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant does not remind me of Michael Jordan. He reminds me of Kobe Bryant . "He reminds me of Michael Jordan," says LeBron James, who reminds me of LeBron James. I haven't seen other basketball players who remind me of Oscar Robertson, Jerry West, Wilt Chamberlain, Bill Russell or Larry Bird, either. Great players are like running backs. They're all unique, with their own style. When Magic Johnson popped onto the scene, every 6-foot-9 player capable of dribbling suddenly was anointed the next Magic. Huge point guards were going to be the fashion. Well, it didn't happen, hasn't happened, and probably won't happen. Clark Kellogg was a cinch to be the next Magic. He's been sitting behind a desk at CBS for years. Magic Johnson was a freak, one of a kind, which is still possible for someone to be. Which is what Bryant is. Why we always have to compare athletes, I don't know, but they've been on this Kobe-Mikey thing since Bryant broke into the NBA as a teenager. Right now, the way Bryant's going -- he's 28 and already in his 10th year as a pro -- he has exclusive membership in his own club. He's the best basketball player on Rock No. 3, has three championship rings, and has been going absolutely nuts. So crazy has he been, one can't help wondering if he's going to wear himself out. Certainly, his arm has to be tired. He scored 65, 50, 60 and 50 points in consecutive games before breaking his spellbinding spell with a miserable 43. Then, Tuesday, against the lowly Grizzlies, the Lakers star checked into the human being suite. He scored 23. Sinful. During that streak, he shot better than 52 percent from the floor. His second set of 60 and 50 games came on back-to-back nights, when he played all but four minutes. I won't say Lakers coach Phil Jackson is playing a dangerous game with his franchise player, because Jackson seems to have resigned himself to the fact that the only way his team's going to win is for Bryant to go off. L.A., slumping before Bryant's surge, now is the sixth seed in the Western Conference playoff chase, but Jackson realizes that, when Bryant is on, his team has a great chance. Still, if he shoots 26.9 percent, as he did against Memphis, odds are the Lakers are going down. And they did, to the worst defensive team -- worst team, period -- in the NBA. Memphis actually played a box-and-one on Bryant, a defense he said he hadn't seen since high school. But it wasn't defense. Defenses don't stop great players. Even Grizzlies coach Tony Barone admitted his side didn't "limit" Bryant. Every once in a while, scorers aren't going to shoot straight. Watching all this firsthand has been Luke Walton, son of Bill, and former star at University of San Diego High School and Arizona. Luke plays and plays well for the Lakers now, but he missed eight weeks with an ankle injury before returning March 15, which was one day before Bryant went on his run. The son of one of the greatest of all players has no problems with Bryant doing what he's doing. The Lakers have been winning. And he doesn't see his teammate as selfish, only as an amazing performer. "It's pretty awesome," Walton says. "It's incredible. I love the game of basketball , and to have watched it firsthand has been incredible." Walton isn't one of those who believe Bryant has been trying to do too much. To him, Bryant looks about as tired as a cheetah who hasn't had to run anywhere. "Not at all," he says. "Kobe was in his zone, that's all, scoring more than 50 a game, and he was having a lot of fun doing it and we were having a lot of fun watching him do it. "Before that, he was passing the ball a lot and getting us all involved. One thing about Kobe, he never gets worn down. He's the best-conditioned athlete I've competed with -- physically and mentally." Walton, as with every member of his basketball -playing family, is a student of the game. He has seen the great players. He grew up the son of a basketball junkie, and Bill remains every bit of that. Luke won't compare Bryant with anyone he's seen play. "Kobe is who he is," says Walton, who has developed tendinitis in the ankle but is playing. "He's not Michael Jordan, he's not Magic, he's just one of the best to have played the game. Everyone's all right with this. We're winning, and it's fun to watch." If Bryant is selfish, as some insist, Walton doesn't see it. "It's not like he's shooting a terrible percentage," Walton says. "We have proven we can win without him doing this, but he's been in that zone, and it's been successful. When something's working, you don't go away from it. "He gets a lot of criticism from the media, but he's been a great teammate. He's a superstar. He's stubborn about his ways. But he's a great teammate." Teammate? He's been an entire team, mate. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant Basketball player Bryant's trade demand apparently is back on. The Los Angeles Times reported Saturday the unhappy superstar, pictured right, told owner Jerry Buss he would like to be traded by the Los Angeles Lakers. Citing a source with knowledge of the situation, the newspaper reported Bryant asked to be traded in an "emotional" conversation with Buss while in Spain on vacation. Bryant again voiced concerns over the direction of the franchise, according to the report. Last month, Bryant admitted in an interview on 1050 ESPN Radio he had requested a trade. But he later backtracked on that demand after talking with coach Phil Jackson. Two weeks ago, Buss released a statement in which he said the team would build around Bryant. "We will continue to pursue every avenue possible to improve our team with him as the cornerstone," the statement said. A perennial all-star and two-time scoring champion, Bryant is frustrated with his team's back-to-back first-round playoff losses to the Phoenix Suns. The two defeats -- and a season where the Lakers did not qualify for the playoffs -- all have come in the three campaigns since superstar centre Shaquille O'Neal was traded to the Miami Heat, who won their first championship with O'Neal in 2006. Inevitably, the O'Neal-Bryant feud was revisited. Despite winning three straight championships as teammates, the superstar duo's relationship was strained and the pair did not talk to each other for an extended period, until recently patching up their differences. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant has reiterated his demand to be traded by the Los Angeles Lakers in a entry posted Sunday on his website. Bryant had initially appeared to backtrack on his request to leave the Lakers -- originally made on May 30 -- but he is now again firm in his desire to go. "Man, today is one of those surreal days for me and my family," Bryant wrote on KB24.com. "When you love something as much as I love the Lakers its hard to even imagine thinking about being elsewhere. "But, the one thing will never sacrifice when it comes to basketball is winning. That is plain and simply what it's all about. It's in my DNA. It's what pushes me to work as hard as I do. It's my daily passion and pursuit." Bryant, 28, last month hit out at the direction to Lakers were taking, using the latest failure in the playoffs to criticize a failure to build a championship-calibre team. Immediately after that interview -- given on ESPN radio -- Bryant was said to have reconsidered after talks with coach Phil Jackson, but he again blamed the Lakers' vision for his desire to leave. "The Lakers are pursuing a longer-term plan that is different from what (Lakers owner) Dr. Buss shared with me at the time I re-signed as a free agent." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The owners of the Los Angeles Lakers and Minnesota Timberwolves have begun talks for a trade that would involve sending Kevin Garnett to the Lakers, NBA sources said yesterday. A multi-team trade discussion is under way involving the Lakers, Timberwolves, Indiana Pacers and possibly a fourth team, with the Lakers getting Garnett and the Pacers getting Lamar Odom and teenage centre Andrew Bynum from the Lakers. Another minor player will probably be added to make the deal work financially. Another source said the Lakers are dealing directly with Minnesota in a swap that would not necessarily involve other teams. Jerry Buss and Timberwolves owner Glen Taylor spoke by phone for 20 minutes Friday. Buss reportedly ended the conversation by suggesting that Lakers' general manager Mitch Kupchak and Timberwolves vice-president of basketball operations Kevin McHale would continue the discussion yesterday. Such a move could address the concerns of Kobe Bryant , who has demanded to be traded for more than four weeks. Bryant met with Kupchak for about an hour Friday, a day after Garnett rejected a trade that would have sent him to the Boston Celtics. Bryant did not step down from his desire to be traded. However, Kupchak, Buss and Lakers' head coach Phil Jackson met later that day to discuss options based on the assumption they would still have Bryant, the Los Angeles Times has learned. Garnett, 31, has never formally asked for a trade while signalling his distress in recent years as the Timberwolves fell from top-notch status. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LOS ANGELES -- Kobe Bryant has skipped practice the past three days and is so unhappy with the Los Angeles Lakers that he has removed some items from his locker -- but he maintains he is ready to keep playing for the team. "If I'm here, I'm ready to strap it up," Bryant said. "My job is to play the game and get ready to play the game and that's what I'm doing." But that came after three days of missed workouts since Saturday in the wake of Lakers owner Jerry Buss's comments last week that he would look at trading superstar guard Bryant. "It's my job to play basketball . It's not my job to worry about what management is doing and this, that and the other," Bryant said. "I'm just going to get ready and let them do their job." Bryant has had discomfort in his right knee during training camp but Lakers coach Phil Jackson indicated physical ailments were not at the heart of Bryant sitting out. "He asked me about resting his legs but I think he's feeling all right," said Jackson, a nine-time NBA champion coach. "He sat it out by mutual agreement. I think that's about all I'll say." Jackson did note that Bryant has removed items from his locker, a possible hint that his days as a Laker are numbered. "There are things that are out of the locker. I will say that," Jackson said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 After weeks of talk, not much has changed, but this much is now certain: Kobe Bryant is leaving Los Angeles. There. That's pretty simple. He has passed the point of no return and the Lakers absolutely have to move him, whether it's this week, this month, this year or before the trade deadline in February. If they wait until next summer, it decreases the amount they can get in return, though in theory they could wait as long as February 2009 to get it done. OK, so he's a goner. That's a given. Even Phil Jackson said that the sooner it's resolved, the better for everyone involved, especially since Kobe hates all things L.A. at the moment. The best player on the planet is available, and Bulls GM John Paxson knows he'd be a fool not to try to steal him from Los Angeles. So Paxson has spent many an hour trying to do that. So far, the Lakers won't bite on a bad deal for them, and neither Paxson nor Bryant wants a deal that guts the Bulls. So Paxson remains patient and careful, but not so careful that including Luol Deng is a deal-breaker. Not that he's in a hurry to move Deng. Of course he's not. Nobody wants to see Deng go. But if Paxson can keep most of his team together and add Bryant, he will trade Deng ... or Kirk Hinrich, for that matter. Don't believe the nonsense that the Bulls have untouchables, because Paxson's mandate is to win an NBA title. Nowhere in his job description does it say he must win an NBA title with a home-grown team that fits Scott Skiles' system and personality. That might be Paxson's preference, but he will choose the shortest path that gets him there, and he knows he already has missed opportunities by passing on Kevin Garnett and Pau Gasol the last two years. Again, this isn't just Kobe Bryant we're talking about. It's a Kobe Bryant highly motivated and desperate to prove he can win a title without Shaquille O'Neal. There are at least six teams in the East today that think they can get out of the conference, including the Bulls, and we're fairly certain not one of the other five GMs is sitting at home hoping against hope that the Bulls trade Luol Deng and acquire Kobe Bryant . No, they're holding their breath and praying it doesn't happen. Still, Paxson's in no hurry, and he knows the acquisition makes no sense if he can't win with what's left. Bryant knows that as well, so you're left with a standoff, until the Lakers panic or a third team enters the fray and sends a ticket-seller to Los Angeles. In the meantime, Paxson will be patient and watch closely to see what Bryant does next, and if he doesn't wind up here, it'll be disappointing but not the end of the world. The Bulls have a nice young team and should compete in the East for a long time. The only certainties are that Kobe Bryant is leaving L.A. and Chicago is his top choice. It seems destined to happen. Stay tuned. Cows on parade Both the best player in basketball , Kobe Bryant , and the best player in baseball, Alex Rodriguez, are available, and people are lining up from Alsip to Zion to explain why they can't find use for either player in Chicago. It's no wonder we wind up the butt of America's jokes, not to mention the fodder for America's teams. Runnin' on empty E-mailer Ed in the Burg: "Want to know what deluded is? It's Lovie Smith still saying the Bears are a running team, and that they come off the bus running the football. Lovie, you may run off the bus, but the opposition jumps on that bus and runs over your team with it.'' The quote Chiefs coach Herm Edwards, on his favorite snack: "(In college) you didn't have a lot of money. You're living on your scholarship. When you ran out of money, if you had some Fig Newtons in your refrigerator, you never went hungry. So that's kind of been my deal, from college all the way to right now. There are some Fig Newtons in our refrigerator. So if it all goes bad, I know I can go back to those Fig Newtons.'' Just asking What will you remember most about the Darin Erstad era? Escape from N.Y. CBS' David Letterman: "It was such a nice day here in New York City, Alex Rodriguez opted out of his pants.'' Apocalypse now Lance Armstrong and Ashley Olsen? Best headline Sportspickle.com: "Chad Johnson jumps route, picks off pass intended for T.J. Houshmandzadeh.'' And finally ... ABC's Jimmy Kimmel: "The Red Sox win another World Series, the Patriots are undefeated, the Celtics have the strongest team in a long time, and now Boston wins the World's Largest Pumpkin growing contest: Ted Kennedy's head.'' fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 AP Basketball LOS ANGELES LAKERS coach Phil Jackson agreed to terms of a two-year contract extension believed to be worth about $US24million ($27.27m) yesterday, putting him under contract through the 2009-10 season. The Hall of Fame coach announced his decision before yesterday's game against Denver. He said it was likely to be his last contract. ''I was in my 50s in the last era, I'm in my 60s in this era, and maybe I can go on into my 70s,'' Jackson said. ''But I really don't think so. ''I'm losing a step as I go, mentally and physically. Being abreast of all these kids is not an easy task. I can hardly speak their language, but I'm trying.'' Jackson, 62, coached Chicago to six championships in the Michael Jordan era from 1989-98 before taking over the Lakers in June 1999. He coached LA to three championships, left in June 2004 and took a year off before returning to sign a three-year, $US30million contract -- the richest deal for an NBA coach -- in June 2005. Jackson and former Boston coach Red Auerbach are the only men to coach nine championships. Jackson said his contract extension wasn't connected to whether Kobe Bryant stayed with the Lakers. ''The decision to return as coach, and the decision to be asked to return as coach, both had very little to do with Kobe Bryant and very little to do with the talent here,'' Jackson said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant and the Los Angeles Lakers were playing like an inspired team. Bryant recorded 46 points, seven rebounds and five assists as the Lakers defeated the Toronto Raptors 121-101 at the Air Canada Centre Friday night. It was a busy day for the Lakers, who acquired former All-Star centre Pau Gasol from the Memphis Grizzlies earlier Friday. Los Angeles sent centre Kwame Brown, rookie guard Javaris Crittenton, guard Aaron McKie and first-round picks in 2008 and 2010 to Memphis for Gasol. The Grizzlies also received the draft rights to Marc Gasol, Pau's younger brother, while Los Angeles got back a second-round pick in 2010. Pau Gasol is on his way to Los Angeles where he must pass a team physical before he can join the team. In Friday's contest, Bryant led the way for the Lakers, putting on an MVP-type performance. He set the tone early by scoring 10 points in the first quarter and adding 11 in the second as the Lakers led by five at the half while shooting 54 per cent from the field. The Raptors were able to stay within three for most of the half thanks to Andrea Bargnani's 17 first-half points while Chris Bosh chipped in with 11. LANCERS HOST BROCK TODAY The Lancers men's and women's basketball teams host Brock today at the St. Denis Centre. The men, ranked 10th overall in the country, are on a seven-game winning streak and are first in the OUA west with a 12-4 record. They take on second-place Brock today at 2 p.m. Brock is ranked eighth in the country. The women's team, in third place in the OUA west with an 11-7 record, take on the fourth-place Brock women (10-8) at 4 p.m. SAINTS GAMES DELAYED The doubleheader basketball games scheduled for Friday at St. Clair College were delayed two hours due to poor weather. The originally scheduled games of 6 p.m. (women's basketball ) and 8 p.m. (men's basketball ) were push-ed two hours ahead. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Habib Habib is happy to talk about his game and his name. His game was on display last weekend, when he helped the Harvest City Reapers qualify for Hoopla -- the Saskatchewan High Schools Athletic Association basketball championships. Now, about the name . . . "I was teaching him exponents in math class and I said, 'I should call you Habib Squared,' '' Reapers senior boys basketball coach Joel Wells said. "He ended up handing in his test with 'Habib' and the exponent '2' beside his name.'' Habib is routinely asked about the repetitive nature of his name. In fact, he has posed some questions of his own. "I still haven't got an answer from my parents,'' Habib Omar Habib said with a chuckle. "I asked about it, but they just turned around and walked. I just said, 'OK.' "It's different. I like it.'' There is everything to like about Habib as a player. On Friday, for example, Habib had seven three-pointers as Harvest City defeated the visiting Neilburg Trojans 101-49 in to open a 1A boys qualifying tournament for Hoopla. The next day, Habib added 34 points -- including five three-pointers -- in an 86-55 victory over the Mortlach Mohawks. The latter victory propelled Harvest City into Hoopla. The Reapers' opener is Friday afternoon against the Fillmore Falcons. The winner advances to Saturday's provincial final. "Oh, I'm excited,'' the ever-smiling Habib said. "I can't sleep, actually.'' Habib has been giving opposing defenders sleepless nights despite being only 14. As a Grade 9 student, he is accustomed to playing against -- and scoring against -- opponents who are significantly older. "I thought it was going to be really hard,'' the 5-foot-10 Habib said. "It's still hard, but I expected it would be too old for me and I wouldn't get any minutes at all. I was worried about that stuff at first, but then I got to see how it was.'' Habib was introduced to the Reapers long before he actually joined the Regina Intercollegiate Basketball League team. "When he came here in Grade 7, I had him start practising with the senior boys because I could tell he was going to be something,'' Wells said. "I had to convince him at the beginning of this year that he could play at this level. It took some persuasion.'' Even so, Habib is already thinking several levels higher. "He has big dreams of the NBA,'' Wells said. "He's a big Kobe Bryant fan. We call him Little Kobe. He has Kobe Bryant pictures all over his desk.'' Habib also wears No. 8 -- which was Bryant's number with the NBA's Los Angeles Lakers before he switched to 24. Harvest City does not have a jersey with the latter number, but eight is enough for Habib -- who emulates Bryant in other ways. "I usually cut my hair the same way he does,'' Habib says with a chuckle. Does he also dunk like Kobe? "I'm grabbing the rim,'' he said, "but I'm still working on my vertical.'' Habib's life, and that of his family, began to ascend when he moved to Canada six years ago. The Habibs had previously resided in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. "Everything was going on with the war and stuff,'' Habib recalled. "It wasn't bad or anything, but it wasn't as fun as here.'' The hardships in Ethiopia prompted Habib's mother, Enanu, to move to Canada with her four children. Habib has two sisters (Ziggy, 24, and Kedy, 21) and a 16-year-old brother (Humed). Humed, a Grade 11 student, is also with the Harvest City basketball team. Habib attended Thomson School from Grades 3 to 6 before enrolling at Harvest City Christian Academy. He appreciates the educational and athletic experiences, along with all the opportunities his family has received in Canada. "I'm so grateful,'' Habib said. "It's not just because of my mom. It's God, too.'' n Hoopla is to begin today with opening-round games in 5A boys and girls. Competition in the 1A, 2A, 3A and 4A categories is to begin Friday. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LAKERS Lakers advance if ... Derek Fisher can keep Tony Parker from getting into the lane and creating wide-open shots for Tim Duncan, Manu Ginobili, Kurt Thomas and Michael Finley. Ginobili shot only 31 percent against the Lakers this season. As a team, the Spurs shot 43 percent. Time to Shine: Kobe Bryant . This year's MVP dominated on offense in the first two rounds against Denver and Utah. Now, his reputation as a one-on-one defender will get a severe test against Manu Ginobili and Michael Finley. 'X' Factor: Lamar Odom. The Lakers' No. 3 option on offense, Odom's athleticism and ability to handle the ball made him a hard-guard for the Jazz in the second round. Ditto for San Antonio, which will use Kurt Thomas, Robert Horry and Fabricio Oberto on him. Key stat: Kobe Bryant , right, shot 96 free throws in six second-round games against Utah. He shot 20 in four games this season against the Spurs. SAN ANTONIO Spurs advance if ... They control the tempo. They must keep the Lakers out of the open court and make them work for their points. In San Antonio's two wins during the season, L.A. averaged only 91.5 points. In the Spurs' two losses, the Lakers averaged 104.5 points. Time to Shine: Tim Duncan, bottom right. There were times in the opening rounds against Phoenix and New Orleans when Duncan looked his age. But he has always played well against Pau Gasol, and the Lakers don't have anyone else big enough (or good enough) to challenge him. 'X' Factor: Bruce Bowen is known for his defense, but he averaged 12.3 points against the Lakers this season. Bowen made 12 of his 15 three-pointers as the Lakers played off of him while trying to help on Tony Parker and Tim Duncan. Key stat: The Spurs know how to win, as evidenced by their four NBA championships since 1999. Season series: Tied, 2-2 Prediction: Spurs in seven. The Lakers are 8-2 in the playoffs, but they have not faced a team that defends like San Antonio. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Even if the 1980s short shorts that the Lakers wore for a Dec. 30 game against Boston have gone out of fashion, the same cannot be said for the Lakers/Celtics rivalry, set to be revived in a blockbuster NBA Finals beginning tonight. The teams last met in the Finals in 1987, but have combined to win 30 championships, counting the five by the Lakers during their years in Minneapolis. Between them, the Lakers and Celtics have retired numbers for 29 players, a who's who of NBA history. The fortunes of both franchises have been reversed over the past year, with Boston welcoming Kevin Garnett in a trade from Minnesota and the Lakers keeping Kobe Bryant despite his trade demand and summer of discontent. The Celtics beat the Lakers twice this season, but that was before Pau Gasol arrived in Los Angeles after a Feb. 1 trade with Memphis. Boston finished 66-16 in the regular season but needed two Game 7 victories just to reach the Finals. The greatest games between Magic Johnson and Larry Bird live on through NBA TV and ESPN Classic, but the Lakers-Celtics rivalry will be very much alive in these Finals thanks to three key sets of adversaries: KOBE BRYANT VS. RAY ALLEN There's no love lost between the two, ever since an October 2004 preseason game in which Bryant blocked Allen on a breakaway and glared at him afterward. Allen fired back, saying that Bryant would be "very selfish" trying to prove he could win without Shaquille O'Neal and predicting that he would be pleading for owner Jerry Buss to make a trade in another year or two. "We'll all be saying, 'I told you so,' when he says that," Allen added. Bryant responded: "Don't even put me and dude in the same breath." The feud between the two has since cooled, but Bryant and Allen have had fierce battles on the court, with Allen capable of running Bryant through screen after screen on the offensive end. PHIL JACKSON vs. RED AUERBACH Even in the last year of his life, Auerbach (right) took swipes at Jackson (left), charging that the Lakers coach "picks his spots" as far as coaching championship-ready teams. After Auerbach died in October 2006, Jackson recalled the words of Red Holzman, his coach and mentor in New York, after the Knicks beat Auerbach's Celtics in the 1973 playoffs: "There's sometimes you think good overcomes evil, and this is one of those times." With a Lakers victory in these Finals, Jackson would win his 10th championship as a coach, breaking the tie he shares with Auerbach. Jackson undoubtedly would take great satisfaction, considering he has mocked the NBA's Coach of the Year award for coming with a trophy of Auerbach on the bench. MITCH KUPCHAK VS. DANNY AINGE After working every angle to acquire Kevin Garnett from Minnesota, Kupchak could only watch last summer as Timberwolves general manager Kevin McHale pulled the trigger on a trade with Ainge, his former Celtics teammate. But Kupchak might have made the better deal in the end, when he added Gasol from Memphis for a package built around Kwame Brown's expiring contract. Even Grizzlies owner Michael Heisley is finally questioning whether his team got enough (or anything) in return for its star center. Kupchak also added Trevor Ariza in a trade from Orlando, giving the Lakers a defensive option against Paul Pierce in the Finals. Garnett and Gasol, meanwhile, will match up against each other in the series. Schedule Boston vs. L.A. Lakers All games 7 p.m., Ch. 4 Today: L.A. Lakers at Boston Sunday: L.A. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MVP PAUL PIERCE ~ Boston We hate to jump on the bandwagon - ABC certainly couldn't get enough of Pierce - but there's no question Pierce helped the Celtics add to the legend/hype of Celtics-Lakers. First, he was injured and carried off the floor in the third quarter. Then, he returned in the third quarter and sank back-to-back three-pointers. Pierce had a monster third quarter, scoring 15 points on 5-for-5 shooting to give the Celtics a four-point lead and some confidence headed into the fourth. NOT MVP KOBE BRYANT ~ L.A. With all due respect to Kevin Garnett's monster jam near the end of the game, it was Bryant's lackluster play and fourth quarter that sealed the Lakers' fate. The MVP went 1-for-6 in the fourth quarter and shot just 9-for-26 for the game. But at least he didn't demand a trade during the game. Still, with at least three games to go, there's no way you can count Bryant out for the rest of the series. 6TH MAN S. CASSELL ~ This is kind of like naming the best pinch-hitter on the 1927 Yankees. With a murderer's row of Ray Allen, Kevin Garnett and Paul Pierce, Cassell had to make the most of his 12 minutes in Game 1. The backup point guard did his job, providing a spark in the second and fourth quarters, the only time he got on the floor. Cassell went 4-for-9, but provided a key six points in the second quarter to help keep the Lakers' halftime lead to just five points. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LOS ANGELES - With the same purpose as one of his in-your-face jumpers, Kobe Bryant leaned into the microphone. "The series ain't over," he said. "It's far from over." Faced with long odds, Bryant and the Los Angeles Lakers returned to practice on Saturday for the first time since game 4 of the NBA finals, when the Boston Celtics stormed back from a 24-point deficit to win and take a 3-1 lead in this reborn rivalry. The Lakers have run out of time and tomorrows. It's either win game 5 today or pack away the sneakers for the summer. No team has ever come back from a 3-1 deficit in the finals, and even if the Lakers can stave off elimination and win at Staples Centre, they'll have to play games 6 and 7 in Boston, where the Celtics are 12-1 this postseason. Since the league switched to the much-debated 2-3-2 playoff format in 1985, no team has won the last two games on the road. Their climb is a steep one, and if the Lakers have any chance of mounting a comeback, Bryant, the league's MVP and the game's most transcendent player, most criticised personality and most unstoppable force, must be the one to lead them. Boston is one win from its 17th NBA title and first in 22 years, but the Celtics are wary of Bryant. They've done a decent job of containing him through four games - his only breakout was a 36-point performance in game 3 - but they know Bryant can single-handedly beat them if they're not careful. "We're up 3-1 and we know we have a lot of basketball to play because Kobe is on that team," Celtics coach Doc Rivers said. "He's the scariest player in the NBA in a lot of ways, so you're fearful of him all the time. A respectful fear." "One guy will not guard Kobe Bryant ," he said. "It's just too hard. It's too much work, and it takes all the energy out of that one guy." - AP Classification_140 Classification_140 Classification fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Language: ENGLISH Publication-Type: Newspaper Subject: BASKETBALL (91%); BASKETBALL TOURNAMENTS (90%); ATHLETES (79%); SPORTS & RECREATION (79%); SPORTS & RECREATION EVENTS (79%); SPORTS AWARDS (79%); TOURNAMENTS (76%) Organization: LOS ANGELES LAKERS (94%); BOSTON CELTICS (84%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (83%) Person: KOBE BRYANT (89%) Geographic: BOSTON, MA, USA (91%); LOS ANGELES, CA, USA (74%) Load-Date: June 15, 2008 fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor 0 lineWidth 12700 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 End of Document Page PAGE of NUMPAGES Superstars lining up to compete HYPERLINK https://advance.lexis.com/api/document?collection=news&id=urn:contentItem:4T3T-YWJ0-TX5N-00KM-00000-00&context=1519360 00 Superstars lining up to compete Geelong Advertiser (Australia) July 31, 2008 Thursday 1 - Main Edition Bookmark_143 Bookmark_143

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 AS the PA announcer at the Staples Center bragged on Sunday night: "This is not over yet!" The Lakers did just enough to send the NBA finals to Game Six - but Game Six is at the Banknorth Garden tonight and now is the chance for Boston to close the series out. Boston are 47-7 at home this season and as coach Doc Rivers pointed out, his side have earned the right to land the glory on home court over eight gruelling months. The Celtics are a best-priced 20-37 with Bet365 and that has to be about right given their dominance in front of their own fans. Kobe Bryant , MVP over the regular season, just hasn't produced for Los Angeles when it really mattered. His bare stats - 26.4 points per game compared to a play-offs average over 30 - confirms the drop as he has come up against a defence that has marshalled him superbly. Bryant isn't the only player suffering under the hoop in a series in which Boston's big players have been superb. Their form has been the main reason the free-scoring Lakers have struggled, and the LA side could find it tough again. Three of the five matches have yielded 192 points or fewer and the 10-11 available with several firms that it happens once again is worth latching on to. Boston are brawnier than ever on home soil, where nine of their 13 home contests in the playoffs have failed to hit the 190 mark. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Ten days ago, the Deseret News asked readers to project the 2008 U.S. Olympic men's basketball team, which was announced Monday by USA Basketball . In an online poll posted June 14, the names of the 33 NBA players comprising the U.S. Select men's roster -- used to determine the Olympic squad -- were listed, with poll participants invited to select the 12 they would chose to play at the Beijing Olympics. So, how did the 333 entries fare against the actual picks? Nine of the top 12 vote-getters were actual Olympic team selections (see accompanying box), with the top five being LeBron James, Deron Williams, Kobe Bryan, Carlos Boozer and Chris Paul. And the three erroneous picks? Instead of the Beijing-bound trio of Chris Bosh, Jason Kidd and Tayshaun Prince, poll participants preferred Amare Stoudemire, Paul Pierce and Lamar Odom. Actual '08 Olympic team selections (alphabetically): Carmelo Anthony, Carlos Boozer, Chris Bosh, Kobe Bryant , Dwight Howard, LeBron James, Chris Paul, Tayshawn Prince, Michael Redd, Dwyane Wade and Deron Williams. Deseret News online poll top 12 selections (votes received): LeBron James (292), Deron Williams (289), Kobe Bryant (288), Carlos Boozer (286), Chris Paul (283), Dwight Howard (269), Amare Stoudemire (207), Dwyane Wade (199), Paul Pierce (181), Carmelo Anthony (164), Lamar Odom (146) and Michael Redd (140). fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Sean Daniels has moved on from the farming accident that caused him to lose his left hand and forearm last October. In fact, he plans to return to the potato fields this fall. "It was just meant to happen," said the 17-year-old Daniels of the accident, in which he got his arm caught in a conveyor belt on the back of a potato truck. "It wasn't the job that caused it, it just happened." What's happened since that fateful October day has bordered on the whirlwind for Daniels, who soon will begin his senior year at Easton High School. His determination to overcome the accident and resume his interscholastic athletic career earned nationwide media attention last winter when he returned to the Easton basketball team and joined the Bears in the Eastern Maine Class D quarterfinals. Daniels then was fitted for a prosthetic arm this spring, and when he received the finished product he also got a big-time surprise - a trip to Loyola Marymount University in Los Angeles to join 600 other campers at the soldout Kobe Bryant Basketball Academy. "It was very shocking," said Daniels when he learned of the trip, which came about thanks to some behind-the-scenes work by his mother, Debbie Bartley, and the company that built his prosthesis. "I didn't really say much then or afterward. I was speechless. "I had looked up the camp on my own before, trying to figure out if I could ever swing it. Everybody knows I love basketball and that I'm a huge fan of the Lakers and Kobe Bryant , so for me it was the perfect gift." The recent cross-country trip necessitated Daniels' first-ever plane flight. "I was afraid at first," he said, "but I sat with an off-duty pilot, and he told me everything that was going on." Perhaps more nerve-wracking was his first day at the camp, as the talent pool was of high quality and his presence there was being filmed by a CBS news crew for a story about his case that's scheduled broadcast shortly after 8 a.m. today on the CBS Early Show. "It was different," said Daniels, who also was filmed by CBS in Bangor when he first received his prosthesis in mid-June and at home in Easton the next day. " I heard they talked to Kobe, too, and I'm wondering what he had to say." Perhaps the personal highlight of the trip was Daniels' opportunity to meet the Los Angeles Lakers' star. "I talked to him for a little bit," said Daniels. "He's a lot taller in person than I thought he was, and he was very nice, he showed a lot of respect for everyone. "Kobe has been my favorite player ever since he came into the league. He's an extremely talented guy, one of the best in basketball . He's someone that when he's on the court never gives up, and whether he's winning or down and out he fights to the end." Daniels did not wear his prosthesis while competing at the Bryant camp and does not plan to use it when on the court next winter, either, citing the heaviness of the device as well as the fact that he has grown accustomed to playing basketball without his left arm. "I remember when I first started playing again thinking about how much I wanted it, but once I got it and started using it, I didn't have that same feeling," said Daniels. "I got used to playing with just my limb, and the arm is pretty heavy. I figured I'd just get on with playing and just try to better my game the way it is right now." That decision got one of its first significant tests during the Bryant camp. "It really helped going out to L.A.," said Daniels, "because people were forcing me to go left and I had to figure out ways to go against it. I think I figured out some ways." Not that Daniels won't benefit in other ways from having his prosthetic arm available. "I'll be able to tie my shoes again," he said, "and there's an attachment that's like a claw I'll be able to use to do the dishes." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LA Lakers star Kobe Bryant wore his heart on his sleeve yesterday when he confessed that winning a gold medal for his country would mean more to him than any NBA title. The 29-year-old reigning NBA MVP already has three championship rings but says gold from Beijing would surpass anything he has achieved in the game. "A gold medal is different because you're playing for your country. There is more at stake I think," said Bryant. "When you're playing for an NBA championship you're obviously playing for a brand, you're playing for whatever motivates you, to prove people wrong or whatever it is. "You put on the USA uniform and you're playing for something that's bigger than all of that. You're representing your country. "It's one country up against the other one to prove who's best at this game. To me that holds more weight." Bryant has come to Macau off a stellar NBA season. He led the Lakers to the finals and picked up his first league MVP title after averaging 28.3 points per game. He was one of the star performers for Team USA during last week's training camp, and said their 120-65 blow-out of Canada last Friday had everyone pumped up. "We got up and down and moved the ball well, so it was great," said Bryant. "The offence that we had was so fluid and so versatile I don't think there were any problems whatsoever. The biggest challenge for us is finding that rhythm and cohesiveness on the defensive end." The Americans take on Turkey tomorrow and Lithuania on Friday and, with an abundance of all-star talent in the US squad, Bryant said he felt for coach Mike Krzyzewski. "The challenge that he's facing is to find what combination works best with what and at what time of the day," he said. "It's a position that I certainly don't envy." Bryant has been struggling with a right-hand finger injury and revealed yesterday it would require surgery - but the Olympics come first. "It's OK. It's a little sore but it's all right," said Bryant. "As soon as I get back we'll go in and talk about it, do it and then get ready for the season. I've heard it will be around three weeks, four week, five weeks. I heal pretty fast normally." And while basketball will be his main concern in Beijing, Bryant - who spent part of his youth in Italy - said he was hoping to catch some soccer during the Games, particularly a game involving Brazil. "They are all fabulous athletes," he said. "And I'm glad they are playing soccer not basketball ." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 SOME of the biggest and richest names in world sport will be at the Beijing Olympics, with the superstar mega-list headed by Roger Federer, Maria Sharapova, Lionel Messi, Yao Ming, LeBron James, and Michael Phelps. Some will be mucking in with their teammates at the Olympic Village although others are expected to stay in hotel suites more becoming of their status and financial clout. While the Olympics will attract more than 10,000 athletes, only a handful can claim to be truly global household names, and even fewer can boost of multi-million dollar bank accounts to boot. Perhaps the most recognisable face is Federer, the Swiss tennis machine who has dominated the sport for five years, although in China, home to 1.3 billion people, Houston Rockets centre Yao Ming is bigger. When it comes to money, the American basketball team carries perhaps the most clout. Players like James, Kobe Bryant , Carmelo Anthony, and Dwyane Wade are heavyweights of the game who have multi-million dollar salaries and all will be in Beijing. James, or `King James' as he is known, is one of the richest sportsmen on the planet and if you believe the reports, is well on his way to becoming the first billionaire athlete. Bryant and his teammates are equally flush, but it will be about face in Beijing after the Americans suffered the most humbling moment in their basketball history when they lost in the semi-finals in Athens. It ended their reign as champions over three Olympics and they are desperate to reclaim the mantle. ``We will be Olympic champions this year,'' James said. ``We have guys who don't want to lose -- Kobe ( Bryant ), Carmelo (Anthony), Dwyane (Wade) -- and the guys off the bench are very good. We've got so many great players.'' Federer has reigned as the No. 1 tennis player since 2004 -- although his position is now under threat from Rafael Nadal -- which makes him not only seriously rich, but a top draw in August. But he has indicated he will be one of those forgoing the Olympic village. Federer doesn't want distractions in his quest for a first-ever Olympic gold. ``It was quite difficult in Athens,'' he said. ``Every time I go to eat everyone taps on your shoulder.'' headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PHYSICALLY and metaphorically, they stand tall: Team USA - don't call them the Dream Team - features some of the highest-paid, most idolised and recognisable sporting stars in the globe. When it comes to basketball , people don't really talk about gold medal prospects. There is the US and there is everybody else. For most of those who play the Americans, it is a honour to share a court with the likes of LeBron James, Kobe Bryant and Dwyane Wade. Once it was felt that the Americans' undoubted superiority in talent led to a kind of hubristic arrogance. They were the team of champions just waiting to be undone by the champion team. If it was partly true, it didn't stop them winning most of the time. But in recent years, Team USA has been taught a little humility (just a little, this is basketball ). The different rules of the international game brought the US back to the field. There they faced zone defences of a type phased out in the NBA and referees less inclined to ignore blatant fouls if the resulting dunk made the highlight reel. They also paid the price for paying scant regard to their opposition. All that has changed and the result is a little frightening. Australia's Boomers will see just how scary it is when they face the US in Shanghai tonight, the final pre-games hitout for both teams. After a disappointing Athens Olympics four years ago, when Argentina won gold and the US bronze, the Americans got serious about Olympic basketball . They have kept the nucleus of their team together, put more effort into scouting their opposition and playing lead-up games, and avoided big-ego players not interested in playing as a team. On Sunday here, the US smashed Russia by 21 points a couple of days after beating Lithuania by 36 points. Australia will try not to be overawed but it will not be easy. "We are playing the most talented and athletic team in the world," Boomers coach Brian Goorjian said yesterday. ". . . I just hope we come out of it with our confidence intact." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant , LeBron James and the U.S. basketball arrived in Beijing amid near pandemonium yesterday after fans waited hours for a glimpse of their basketball heroes. Players left Beijing's airport by a side door and boarded a bus in a secure VIP area before driving into the city. Dozens of fans, most wielding cell phones or digital cameras, mounted a spiked iron fence to get a closer look, many shouting "Kou-bi-er" -- the local rendering of Kobe Bryant 's name -- in hopes of getting his attention. The Los Angeles Lakers guard waved and smiled, looking pleased and vaguely amused by the display. "I try to see American players every time they come to China, but this time is the most special because of the Olympics," said Manny Chen, a spikey-haired college student dressed in a USA jersey with Bryant's name it. Asked whom he would back in the U.S. team's opening game against Yao Ming and the rest of China's team Sunday, Chen answered without hesitation: "China, of course. "But America has the best chance of being the champion," he added. The Americans have something to prove after failing to win a major world title since the Sydney Olympics in 2000. The squad failed to impress in Tuesday night's warmup against Australia, winning 87-76 despite shooting just 3-of-18 on 3-pointers and 20-of-33 (61 per cent) at the foul line. "We just broke down. We gambled a couple of times and allowed them to make the extra pass," James said after the game. headline: WOWS 'EM BY SHOWING UP
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BEIJING - Olympic volunteers - usually a staid, workmanlike bunch - screamed, giggled and clicked their cameras Friday morning when "Koo-bee-eh" walked into the main press center. That is what they call Kobe Bryant here, and he is now expected to be every bit as Dreamy as erstwhile icons Michael Jordan and Magic Johnson. "I can feel it," Bryant said, about the adoration. "The people here are very passionate about basketball , very knowledgeable about basketball . They want to see great basketball . There's a lot of energy." The Redeem Team gave its first interviews yesterday, after a brief visit to the spartan Athletes' Village where they definitely will not be staying. While Mike Krzyzewski gave a very respectful, programmed opening speech to the media, Bryant laughed, conversed with teammates and paid absolutely no attention to his coach. But Kobe was a perfect diplomat when he sat down for breakout interviews, carefully praising opponents and promising to groom himself for the Opening Ceremony tonight. "I'm going to get a new haircut," he said. "I can't look all scruffy on national TV." Bryant figures to get plenty of air time, here in China and back home in America. The Americans haven't won a major world championship in eight years, but this team is supposed to be different because of him. He is the type of player and ego missing from these teams for too long - a star who theoretically will not allow his team to lose, even against the hottest outside-shooting international opponent. There are indications this will be a difficult task, beginning with the schedule. The U.S. has a preliminary game on Sunday against Yao Ming and China. The Americans have been placed into the Group of Death, Group B, along with the European champion, Greece, and the world champion, Spain, plus Germany and outclassed Angola. Armed with three point guards, the Americans are going with a smaller, more versatile lineup this time around. The most recent warmup game did not go particularly well, as the U.S. squeaked past Australia, 87-76, while hitting just 3-of-18 three-pointers. That kind of shooting night may well doom the Americans against a better team, whether or not Bryant mesmerizes. Asked why it has been so long since the Americans were able to cop a title, Bryant said that the bad spell shouldn't really surprise anyone. "Other countries have All-Stars, too," he said. Want more? fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BEIJING - The players representing USA Basketball in the Olympics are known as the "Redeem Team" back home, where they're expected to atone for the 2004 team's failings. No such designation applies in China, where the players are loved unconditionally. The NBA is big worldwide, and especially so in China. If Jazz guard HYPERLINK http://www.sltrib.com/olympics/ci_9684499 00 Deron Williams ever worried about being noticed while playing in one of the league's smallest markets, all he had to do was come to China. "The fans are crazy; they know you everywhere you go," Williams said Tuesday, after the Americans' 97-76 victory over Angola in pool play. "There's nowhere you can go in this city without being recognized." Or even outside the city of 17 million. When he traveled about 45 minutes to hit some golf balls, he was greeted immediately after walking into the driving range by cries of "Williams!" "Everybody's [called] their last name," Williams explained. "Except Kobe [ Bryant ]. Kobe's 'Kobe.' " Jazz forward HYPERLINK http://www.sltrib.com/olympics/ci_9684153 00 Carlos Boozer was welcomed with tears. A woman seeking his autograph began crying as he scribbled his name. "Literally crying," Boozer said, marveling at the memory. "I couldn't believe that. I felt like the Pope for a minute. I just play basketball ." And the treatment of the Jazzmen is nothing like the reception for Bryant. The Los Angeles Lakers' guard has joked about not being able to return home without a gold medal, but he should consider staying, regardless. He receives the loudest cheers in the Olympic Basketball Gymnasium every time he's introduced or makes a basket. Generally, the Americans are treated like the home team, although the Chinese fans applaud every good play by either team. It's all a commentary on the NBA's popularity in China. The league is marketing itself with merchandise stores and other avenues, while staffing offices around the country and staging exhibition games. NBA telecasts are watched by an estimated 450 million viewers -- about one-third of the population -- and some 300 million Chinese play recreational basketball . It helps that China has produced NBA players Yao Ming of Houston and Li Jianlian, and others are on the way. Boozer expects two or three of the Chinese players his team opposed Sunday to soon play in the NBA. The growth of Chinese basketball is undoubtedly spurred by the NBA's following, and it's no surprise to Roger Reid. The former Brigham Young coach landed a job with the Hangzhou Horses in China's professional league -- not necessarily because of his credentials with the Cougars, but because he had worked as a Phoenix Suns assistant. All Reid had to do to get his players' attention was say that's the way they do things in the NBA. " Basketball 's expanding in China, and it's already big," said Reid, now Southern Utah's coach. "And it's just going to get better." Perhaps no U.S. player is more popular with Chinese fans than Kobe Bryant , shown here going for a dunk against Angola at the Beijing 2008 Olympics on Tuesday, Aug. 12. headline: A STAR WHO'S NOT EXACTLY A PARAGON OF AMERICAN VIRTUE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobe Bryant is bigger in China than the Great Wall. How could a billion Chinese be so wrong? Not to question the taste of a country that loves everything American, from quarter-pound cheeseburgers to classic punk rock, but this 24/7, camera-clicking, name-chanting worship of Bryant by the local masses at the Olympics might be the strangest foreign love affair with a U.S. pop icon since the French decided Jerry Lewis was a comic genius. Sure, the man can hoop. But he's not exactly the finest example of American virtue. So why does China go absolutely gaga for Kobe? "I can't explain it," Bryant told me Wednesday. That makes two of us. The Chinese don't want to be like Michael Jordan or Brad Pitt. A nation with an insatiable appetite for U.S. culture adores the Los Angeles Lakers superstar to such an extent it would surprise nobody in Beijing if we woke up tomorrow and the giant portrait of Chairman Mao in Tiananmen Square was painted over, replaced with a mural of Kobe taking a great leap forward from the Nike swoosh. "Kobe might want to think about moving to China to live," said Olympic teammate Carmelo Anthony, who was kidding. We think. Back home, there are U.S. corporations who washed their hands of Bryant and walked away after he was accused of sexual misconduct in Colorado. Kobe can kiss his big Aristotle, insists Shaquille O'Neal. With baseball slugger Barry Bonds in exile, No. 24 for the Lakers just might now be No. 1 on America's list of love-him-or-hate-him athletes. "He's a polarizing figure in the United States," said New York Knicks coach Mike D'Antoni, working as an assistant for Team USA. "But, in China, he's off the charts. Everybody loves him." Like the smog, Bryant has been all over the Summer Games. At the opening ceremony, when hundreds of U.S. athletes marched into the Bird's Nest stadium, Chinese spectators had eyes only for Bryant. "The crowd was chanting 'Ko-be, Ko-be' and I wanted to get next to him, so I would be on TV," American 100-meter dash king Tyson Gay said. Gawkers wearing replica jerseys have stood in a downpour as heavy as a waterfall hoping to catch a glimpse of Bryant stepping off the team bus. While President Bush drew respectful applause at a U.S. women's hoops game, the arrival of Bryant to the arena incited such a surge of fans that Olympic volunteers had to lock arms in the aisle to prevent a riot. The historic occasion of Bryant munching fries in the Olympic Village was recorded for posterity by an intrepid YouTube videographer and has already received more than 6,000 hits. Please, somebody tell us: What drives this incredible Chinese obsession with Bryant? "It's the power of Nike. And it helps Kobe is a good-looking guy. He scored 81 points in a game. This is easy to understand. He has that American flair the kids in China want to copy," said Ma Jian, a 38-year-old Beijing resident who was the first basketball player to show the world China had game when he attended the University of Utah in the 1990s. Bryant is the unquestioned leader of the Redeem Team, which came to the Olympics with the sole purpose of re-establishing the USA as the world's basketball superpower. But, to be brutally honest, Bryant's shooting has been lousy and he has seemed helpless to stop teammates' excessive reliance on an attack based almost exclusively on dunks during early games in the tournament. With starting point guard Jason Kidd looking older than the Ming Dynasty and coach Mike Krzyzewski as of yet unable to establish a go-to guy on offense or find adequate minutes for the defensive energy supplied by Chris Paul and Tayshaun Prince, the USA again appears to be a loose collection of stars in danger of fading to black if Spain or Argentina shoots out the lights. These NBA stars promise their big jumpers will fall when the medals are on the line. If they don't? Bryant jokes that everybody on the team can change citizenship. What, Kobe worry? No way. He's the next emperor of China. "This feels good," said Bryant, enjoying the champagne-toast-and-rose-petal hospitality of his worshipers. "Like a home away from home." Lose in the Olympics and no doubt there would be Americans suggesting that, as punishment, Bryant should be forbidden to leave this city. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 For wisdom and solace during these bustling Olympics, some folks visit the temple of Confucius. Others seek refuge in the Palace of Heavenly Purity. Still others find respite at the Hall of Supreme Harmony. Me, I went to see Shaq. He's tucked away outside a remote gate of a city park, no markers on a tourist map, no signs on the street. He's surrounded not by incense, but by public toilets. He's staring not into the future, but into a parking lot. Those who visit offer him gifts not of gold and myrrh, but of projectile spit and empty water bottles. But Shaquille O'Neal doesn't seem to mind. Here, nobody can accuse him not hustling, because he is literally nailed to the ground. Here, nobody can exhort him to work on his wooden hands because, well, the dude actually has wooden hands. And here, finally, nobody can ever dare say that Kobe Bryant is bigger. Here, the self-proclaimed Superman really is a super man. All 15 metres of him. Welcome to the Forbidden Centre, the Great Brick Wall of China, the Temple of the Clang Dynasty. Here he can stand forever in his uniform, holding a ball, surrounded by fans, without ever having to shoot a free throw. Yeah, I still don't quite believe it, either, but in the heart of a Beijing is a giant statue of Shaquille O'Neal. It sits outside the east gate of Chaoyang Park, Shaq looking over the basketball and soccer fields that sprawl across a massive playground. Shaq always bragged that there would never be another Shaq but, well, the statue is exactly that. That's his dark bald head, his vacant stare, his Fu Manchu mustache. That's his name on the lower part of the front of his white jersey, and those are his baggy white pants that stretch down below his knees. That's also his number scrawled in red on the back of his jersey and -- guess what -- it's not his Laker 34. Forever and ever, O'Neal will wear No. 32, the number he's worn in Orlando, Miami and Phoenix. That statue is tall enough to see the freeway traffic over the trees, big enough to put a basketball hoop on his butt, and real enough to make you wonder why it's there. "I walk past him every day and I think, he is just so-so," said playground player Lei Shi, 26, as he walked to his game. "We're not sure why he's here." The answer can be found in an adjoining sign above the gate's entrance -- Li Ning Sport Park. This part of the area -- filled with basketball courts and ping-pong tables -- is sponsored by Li Ning, a Chinese sportswear giant, sort an Asian Nike. The company is named after its founder, Li Ning, the former Olympic gold-medal gymnast who lit the torch during the Beijing opening ceremony after floating fearlessly around the stadium. The acrobatics and subsequent lighting by a middle-aged man has been the most stunning sight of the Olympics. Wait, the second-most stunning sight. Li Ning commissioned the statue of Shaq two years ago after O'Neal signed a five-year endorsement deal. "We chose O'Neal based on his influence and expertise in basketball ," Li said at the time. "Our co-operation will bring everything possible to China's basketball fans." Well, he's got that last part right. He has given those fans nightmares. "In my dreams at night, Shaq is just that tall," said Chen Chen, 22. He has given them shattered beliefs. "Shaq is a good player but, look here, he is little fat," Chen said. He has also turned them into unwitting members of the feud. You see, Chen was interviewed as he walked past the Shaq statue wearing a Kobe Bryant jersey. "Wait," Chen said, worried that he was causing more disharmony. "I only bought the Kobe jersey because they didn't have the Shaq jersey." Kobe Bryant is indeed huge in China, not only the most popular player on the men's Olympic team, but probably the second-most popular basketball Olympian here next to Yao Ming. But, yep, in this park, Shaq is bigger, even if the fans don't want to admit it. "Kobe is younger, and that Shaq over there is fatter," said Tony Zhang, 24. "Kobe is today. Shaq is too old." Young athletes walk past him on their way to and from their basketball games. Almost every time, they look up at him. And almost every time, they shrug. "One good thing about this," said Minghas Li, "Shaq can't sing in real life, and here, he will not be able to sing." No, indeed, here in a city filled with sacred homage, the great god Shaq will spend the rest of eternity standing, staring, motionless, stiff arms, heavy legs, no explanations. Some would call it mystical. I would call it the 2004 NBA final. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kobemania still sweeping China He's from the glitz capital of the world, so he knows about fame. That happens when you're the one entertaining Jack Nicholson rather than the other way around. But Kobe Bryant can't explain his popularity at the Olympic Games for all the Lakers jerseys in China. Who could? "One night we had a big storm," Bryant said early Saturday on his 30th birthday. "It was raining and the bus was leaving our hotel to come here to the game and there's still people outside with Kobe signs and posters. No ponchos. Just chasing after the bus and screaming." Added captain Jason Kidd: "How would I describe it? Elvis Presley. Michael Jackson." Synchronized swimming goes hardcore Barred from wearing suits embedded with waterproof lights, the Spanish synchronized swimming team took a different tack: gangsta. They marched onto the deck at the Water Cube in costumes embroidered with a menacing cartoon character across the front and back, his cap backward and carrying a stick in a threatening manner. American coach Tammy McGregor, who studied fashion in college, designs most of the suits worn by the Americans. ''It needs to communicate what you're doing,'' she said. ''It needs to be visible from very far away." OK, but can he get the man in a chinlock? Henry Cejudo will appear Wednesday on ''The Tonight Show with Jay Leno'' to flaunt the gold medal he won in freestyle wrestling. ''I'm going to headlock Leno,'' Cejudo said Friday. ''That will be an easy headlock.'' Cejudo, 21, became the youngest American wrestler to win Olympic gold. Cejudo often watches Leno when he can't sleep, captivated by the Monday headlines in which Leno makes fun of errors in newspapers. ''Jay Leno is the man,'' Cejudo said. ''How many people get to go to Jay Leno? I mean, it's hard to get tickets.'' According to Jason Kidd, Kobe Bryant and the King of Pop are somewhat similar.
