fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Nike is undoubtedly the undisputed boss of sports apparel and footwear across the globe. With a whopping net worth of $115.6B, Nike is one particular brand that can be easily found in every athlete s wardrobe irrespective of whichever sports realm they belong to. With NBA uniform rights exclusively with Nike, Adidas took a dig with their throwback post on social media. The viral post that dealt with Adidas-styled jerseys for a few NBA teams dating back to October 2015, even caught the attention of a few basketball legends on social media. Adidas Takes A Direct Shot At Nike s After The Global Brand Fails To Come Up With Christmas Special NBA JerseysBefore Nike took over the uniform industry of the NBA in 2018, Adidas was entrusted with the responsibility of supplying jerseys to the basketball teams from early 2006 to 2017-18. Recently, Adidas took a dig at its competitor brand Nike for not coming up with Christmas special jerseys for the respective NBA teams. The post on social media by Adidas instantly became viral. Adidas throwback post read as"The day was great but better when we had these"The viral post featured colorful jerseys for the Chicago Bulls, San Antonio Spurs, Houston Rockets, and Golden State Warriors. During Adidas partnership with the NBA, the popular sports apparel brand used to produce holiday-themed uniforms to commemorate the Christmas Day matches. The variety of colors and the festive video not only excited the fans but also had them waiting for them during the Holiday season.Los Angeles Lakers Star LeBron James Reacts To The Viral Adidas PostThe throwback post by Adidas on X grabbed the attention of the NBA fraternity along with a few prominent faces in the world of basketball . Signature Nike athlete LeBron James reacted to the throwback post on X and wrote-"Not having Xmas day unis anymore really sucks! That was a great feeling walking into the locker room and seeing those. It was literally like receiving a (gift emoji)! Whomp whomp!"The criticism came from one of the renowned faces of Nike. It is next to impossible to deny the popularity of the Christmas-themed NBA jerseys back then. The Lakers star has been addicted to sports shoes for a long time.During a visit to the Cleveland Clinic Children s Hospital for Rehabilitation, James donated easy-entry sneakers and shoes to a group of children. For James sports should not be taken away from children. HYPERLINK https://time.com/4707678/lebron-james-cleveland-children/ 00 He said- Sport in general should never be taken away from any kid, he added. It creates fun, it creates laughter, it creates competitiveness, it creates brotherhood or sisterhood. To be able to have access to shoes that are very easy to take on or take off, you give kids another opportunity to live out their dream. Keeping the disabled patients in mind, LeBron James helped Nike to develop easy-entry shoes and sneakers for kids. The Lakers star s addiction to classy, yet comfortable sneakers needs no introduction. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Andrea Montolivo Chicago (USA), Dec 29 (EFE) - He signed the record of points and minutes in the NBA. He won four rings with three different teams. He played alongside his son in the Los Angeles Lakers. He won three Olympic gold medals. LeBron James will be 40 years old this Monday and he is still an unquestioned leader on the court and a living legend in the American basketball league. From Akron (Ohio) to the Olympus of the NBA. Chosen with the absolute number one pick by the Cleveland Cavaliers in the 2003 draft, LeBron James opened a legendary career that led him to be crowned champion in 2012 and 2013 with the Heat, in 2016 with the Cavs in a memorable Finals against the Golden State Warriors, and in 2020 with the Lakers on the 'bubble' in Orlando. A four-time regular season MVP, four-time Finals MVP, LeBron James averaged 23.5 points, 7.9 rebounds and nine assists per game this year with the Los Angeles Lakers. If his resume is stellar, so is his ability to maintain his excellence for more than 22 years in the NBA. He came to the NBA accompanied by the nickname 'The Chosen One' after a brilliant high school career at St. Vincent-St. Mary High School. The Cavaliers, who got the first pick in 2003, were able to acquire a player called to change NBA history. The longed-for first ring From his 25-point debut against the Sacramento Kings in late October 2003, LeBron showed impressive talent and power, but he had to wait and suffer before celebrating his first title. The first upset came in 2007, when he lost his first Finals to the San Antonio Spurs of Tim Duncan, Manu Gin\'f3bili and Tony Parker. After eight years in Cleveland, he announced his departure from the franchise to the Miami Heat, where he would form a stellar trio alongside Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh in an attempt to win the coveted ring. It was the 2010-2011 season and in his first attempt he was just one step away from the title. They were denied by MVP Dirk Nowitzki's Dallas Mavericks in 2011, but the following year LeBron and the Heat crowned their sports project with the first of their two rings. Rivalry with the Warriors Since 2011, LeBron reached the Finals eight consecutive times. He came up against giants such as the 2014 Spurs, who doubled him 4-1, and especially the legendary Warriors cycle. His rivalry with Steph Curry remains one of the most spectacular in NBA history. Last Christmas Day, LeBron and Steph squared off for the 53rd time. The sweetest victory on a sentimental level came in 2016, in an unforgettable Finals decided in Game 7 against the Warriors. Down 1-3 in the series, LeBron led the Cavs to a historic ring. No one had ever come back from 1-3 in the Finals. His block on Andre Iguodala in Game 7 of the series is modern NBA history. It was a liberation for LeBron, who delivered his home-state team its first-ever ring. He would stay in Cleveland until 2018, losing to the Warriors in the 2017 and 2018 Finals, before opening a new chapter in Los Angeles. With the Lakers jersey, he conquered his fourth title, in the Finals against the Miami Heat played in the Orlando 'bubble' during the coronavirus pandemic. Records and more records Although in recent seasons his Lakers have been far from being real ring contenders, LeBron James has continued to show a stellar level of basketball . In February 2023, he added to his legend by setting the new NBA record for points by bettering Kareem Abdul Jabbar's mark of 36,387 in a game in Los Angeles against the Oklahoma City Thunder, and just a few weeks ago he also set the American league record for minutes. He has won it all and he has won everywhere, from Cleveland to Miami, from Los Angeles to.... Las Vegas. It was in the Gaming City in December 2023 when he conquered with the Lakers the inaugural edition of the NBA Cup. This title was added to the three Olympic gold medals he won with the U.S. national team, the last of which this year in Paris alongside Kevin Durant and Steph Curry. Titles, records and family successes for LeBron, who this year crowned the dream of competing in the NBA with his son, Bronny James, born in 2004 and selected in the last draft by the Lakers. LeBron and Bronny became the first father-son duo to debut in a regular-season game in the American league. A historic 2024 closes for the James family, which before celebrating the arrival of the new year, prepares to celebrate the birthday of an absolute legend of world basketball . fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 In soccer we have great stars such as the Argentine Lionel Messi , the Portuguese Cristiano Ronaldo , the French Kylian Mbapp\'e9 , the Brazilian Neymar Jr. among others, just to mention those who are active. Precisely, today the 'King' LeBron is celebrating his 40th birthday, being one of the best players in the NBA today and with a story that has no end in sight. The U .S. national team legend is one of the most decorated players of all time, with accolades and all kinds of awards that further enhance his legacy. At 2.06 meters tall, James is the NBA' s all-time leading scorer with 41,131 points (as of Dec. 27), breaking the record set by American Kareem Abdul-Jabbar (38,387). His career began in 2003 with the Cleveland Cavaliers , but even before that he had already begun to shine with his own light. He was proclaimed Mr. Basketball three times in Ohio during his outstanding time at St. Vincent-St. Mary High School, so he was already considered by the media as a future NBA star. At just 18 years old, he signed a $90 million contract with Nike prior to his NBA debut. With the Cleveland Cavaliers he ratified what was his promising future. In his first season, he won the NBA Rookie of the Year award. LeBron showed his talent and power, but he had to wait and suffer a lot before celebrating his first title. The first disappointment came in 2007, when he lost his first Finals against the San Antonio Spurs of Tim Duncan, Manu Gin\'f3bili and Tony Parker. The "King" LeBron has won the NBA Regular Season MVP award four times. These were during the 2008/09, 2009/10, 2011/12 and 2012/13 seasons. After 8 years wearing the Cleveland jersey, he announced his departure to the Miami Heat, where he would form a stellar trio with Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh. In his first attempt, he fell one step short of the title (2010/11). The Dallas Mavericks said no, but the following year LeBron and the Heat (2012) crowned his sports project with the first of his two rings. In this case, the opponent was Kevin Durant's Oklahoma City Thunder: the Florida team won 4-1. Then, the series between the Miami Heat and San Antonio was more even. However, the Spurs won 4-3 and won their second championship. The third ring left an unmatched record and already wearing the Cleveland Cavaliers jersey again (2016), which fell 3-1 against the Golden State Warriors. Until then, no one had managed to reverse such a disadvantage in that instance, but LeBron made it possible: three consecutive victories to overcome by 4-3 an opponent that had won 73 games in the Regular Season. The fourth title was very special. In the midst of the coronavirus pandemic, LeBron James (with the Los Angeles Lakers) overcame the Miami Heat with a 4-2 win on the Disney bubble. With the U.S. basketball team, he has won three Olympic gold medals: Beijing 2008 , London 2012 and Paris 2024 , and a bronze in Athens 2004 , in addition to the bronze medal in the 2006 World Cup in Japan. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 King LeBron James celebrates his 40th birthday today , a symbol of the unique longevity of the Lakers star , who continues to explore multiple horizons as his career never seems to end. In his 22nd NBA season , LeBron James signed a new two-year contract in July\'a1 valued at approximately $100 million. And Lakers , where he has played since 2018, does, for pampering its star. This summer he selected in the draft his son, Bronny James to open the possibility of forming the first father-son duo to share the same court in the NBA . After two inconsistent seasons, the 17-title franchise fired coach Darvin Ham in June to hire JJ Redick , a former player with no previous coaching experience but a close friend of James. ALL-TIME LEADING SCORER The King left an indelible mark on the NBA by surpassing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar in February 2023 to become the all-time leading scorer before reaching the symbolic 40,000-point mark in March of this year. Entering his 22nd season, he equaled Vince Carter' s record and will surpass it if he decides to extend his career. His longevity also allows him to aspire to the record for most games played. He currently ranks fifth (1,520), not too far from Robert Parish's record (1,611), whom he could surpass if he extends his career for one more season. A four-time champion , LeBron James has not won a new ring since 2020 when he was crowned on the NBA bubble in Orlando during the covid pandemic. Following his run, LeBron James has made no secret of his desire to become an NBA franchise owner, especially in Las Vegas, which is in the plans to obtain a team in the event of an expansion that could occur after the signing of a new TV broadcasting rights deal. A partner in the Fenway Sports Group fund , which owns Liverpool of the English Premier League, James already owns stakes in that club, the Boston Red Sox and the Pittsburgh Penguins (NHL). fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LeBron James , owner of all-time records for points and minutes in the NBA , will celebrate his 40th birthday on Monday, December 30. With four championship rings obtained with three different teams and three Olympic gold medals, he is a living legend of American basketball . From Akron (Ohio) to the Olympus of the NBA . Chosen with the absolute number one pick by the Cleveland Cavaliers in the 2003 draft, LeBron James opened a legendary career that led him to be crowned champion in 2012 and 2013 with the Heat, in 2016 with the Cavs in a memorable Finals against the Golden State Warriors, and in 2020 with the Lakers on the Orlando 'bubble'. A four-time regular season MVP , four-time Finals MVP , LeBron James averaged 23.5 points, 7.9 rebounds and nine assists per game this year with the Los Angeles Lakers. If his resume is stellar, so is his ability to maintain his excellence for more than 22 years in the NBA. He came to the NBA accompanied by the nickname 'The Chosen One' after a brilliant high school career at St. Vincent-St. Mary High School. The Cavaliers, who got the first pick in 2003, were able to acquire a player called to change the history of the NBA. The longed-for first ring From his 25-point debut against the Sacramento Kings in late October 2003, LeBron showed impressive talent and power, but he had to wait and suffer before celebrating his first title. The first upset came in 2007, when he lost his first Finals against the San Antonio Spurs of Tim Duncan, Manu Gin\'f3bili and Tony Parker. After eight years in Cleveland , he announced his departure from the franchise to the Miami Heat , where he would form a stellar trio alongside Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh in an attempt to win the coveted ring. It was the 2010-2011 season and in his first attempt he was just one step away from the title. They were denied by MVP Dirk Nowitzki's Dallas Mavericks in 2011, but the following year LeBron and the Heat crowned their sports project with the first of their two rings. Rivalry with the Warriors Since 2011, LeBron reached the Finals eight consecutive times. He came up against giants such as the 2014 Spurs , who defeated him 4-1, and above all against the legendary Warriors cycle. His rivalry with Steph Curry remains one of the most spectacular in NBA history. Last Christmas Day, LeBron and Steph faced each other for the 53rd time. The sweetest victory on a sentimental level came in 2016, in an unforgettable Finals decided in Game 7 against the Warriors . Down 1-3 in the series, LeBron led the Cavs to a historic ring. No one had ever come back from 1-3 in the Finals. His block on Andre Iguodala in Game 7 of the series is modern NBA history. It was a liberation for LeBron, who delivered his home-state team its first-ever ring. He would stay in Cleveland until 2018, losing to the Warriors in the 2017 and 2018 Finals, before opening a new chapter in Los Angeles . With the Lakers jersey, he won his fourth title, in the Finals against the Miami Heat played in the Orlando 'bubble', during the coronavirus pandemic. Records and more records Although in recent seasons his Lakers have been far from being real ring contenders, LeBron James has continued to play stellar basketball . In February 2023, he added to his legend by setting the new NBA record for points by bettering Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's mark of 36,387 in a game in Los Angeles against the Oklahoma City Thunder, and a few weeks ago he also set the U.S. league record for minutes. He's won it all and won everywhere, from Cleveland to Miami, from Los Angeles to... Las Vegas. It was in the Gaming City in December 2023 when he conquered with the Lakers the inaugural edition of the NBA Cup . This title was added to the three Olympic gold medals he won with the U.S. national team, the last of which this year in Paris alongside Kevin Durant and Steph Curry. Titles, records and family successes for LeBron, who this year crowned the dream of competing in the NBA with his son, Bronny James , born in 2004 and selected in the last draft by the Lakers. LeBron and Bronny became the first father-son duo to debut in a regular-season game in the American league. A historic 2024 closes for the James family, which, before celebrating the arrival of the new year, prepares to celebrate the birthday of an absolute legend of world basketball . fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LeBron James has revealed he wants to end his basketball career with the LA Lakers - despite stressing that he understands how the 'business' side of the sport works. The NBA icon, who turned 40 on Monday, discussed his future with a group of reporters on his birthday as the Lakers prepare to face his former team the Cleveland Cavaliers on New Year's Eve. When asked if he envisages himself retiring in Los Angeles, LeBron insisted he would love for that to be the case. However, he then dropped a subtle hint that basketball politics could deny him that fairytale ending. 'I think that's the plan,' LeBron said about retiring as a Laker. 'I would love for it to end here, that would be the plan. 'I came here to play my last stage of my career and to finish it off here, but I'm also not silly or too jaded to know the business of the game as well. I know the business of basketball . 'But I think my relationship with this organization speaks for itself, and hopefully I don't have to go nowhere before my career is over.' As for how long his career could go on for, the four-time NBA champion believes he could spend at least another half-decade operating at the top level. 'It won't be because I can't play this game at a high level,' LeBron said when asked what could lead him to retirement. 'To be honest, if I really wanted to, I could probably play this game at a high level for another five to seven years. If I wanted to, but I'm not gonna do that.' James has cemented a legacy as arguably the greatest basketball player of all time in his remarkable 22-year career. To list some of his accolades; he is already the NBA's all-time leading scorer, has a place in the GOAT conversation with Michael Jordan, most minutes played, four NBA championships and three Olympic gold medals, plus 20 and likely soon to be 21 All-Star selections. Having turned 40, the Lakers great is also poised to become the first ever player to step onto an NBA court in his teens, 20s, 30s and 40s. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NBA Legend LeBron James turned 40 years old today. On Tuesday, he will take the floor against his first team, the Cleveland Cavaliers. While doing so, LeBron will also become the only player in NBA history to play in his teens, 20s, 30s, and now 40s. Over his legendary 22-year run in the league, James has carved out a legacy that is simply unmatched by anyone else. Today, we ll be taking a trip through memory lane as we look back at some of LeBron s greatest achievements. LeBron James : A Statistical AnamolyWhen LeBron first came into the league over 21 years ago, Kareem Abdul Jabbar held the record for the most points and minutes in the league. At the time, no one thought that Jabbar s records would ever be broken. However, James was simply too good to stick to the norms, and over the last 21 years, he has overtaken Jabbar in both points and minutes to become the all-time leading scorer in the NBA. That s not all, as LeBron is top five in all-time assists and has over 10K+ career rebounds. A stat that only he can boast about. LeBron is also the NBA s all-time leader in Playoff points and is second in assists. His success in the playoffs goes beyond that as James has also made eight consecutive NBA Finals appearances between 2011 and 2018. He also made the NBA Finals in 2007 and 2020 making it 10 total appearances, but has only been able to win four championships.However, it has to be said that the city of Cleveland would be irrelevant if it weren t for the little kid from Akron, Ohio. LeBron James Legacy Extends Beyond the CourtLeBron has already made careers on the NBA floor, but many don t know that he has also made lives off the court. LeBron set up the LeBron James Family Foundation through which he opened the I Promise School in 2018, and the I Promise Program in 2015 which aims to develop the city of Akron. Moreover, LeBron has also donated over $41 million to send over 1000 underprivileged kids to college since 2015.However, LeBron has another achievement that goes beyond the championships, his status as one of the greatest of all time, and his impact on the game of basketball . James made all his dreams come true when he shared an NBA court with his son Bronny James earlier during the 2024/25 season. When Does LeBron James Plan on Retiring?At 40, LeBron s time in the league is quickly running out, but he still wishes to share the court with his younger son, Bryce James. It ll be interesting to see whether James can beat Father Time and make his final dream come true. Here s what LeBron James told NBA reporters when asked about his retirement plans: I m not going to play that much longer, to be completely honest, he further added, One year, two years, whatever the case may be. I said the other night I m not playing until the wheels fall off. I m not. From the looks of it, the end of LeBron James legendary career is approaching fast. Hopefully, The King will be able to add another championship to his resume before he hangs up his boots for good. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MICHAEL JORDAN is generally acknowledged as the greatest player of all time but he is learning that life at the top is not always comfortable. Last night, as he failed again to lead the Chicago Bulls to the National Basketball Association's championship round, he found himself being blamed for the latest form of black-on-black violence in the United States: sports-apparel murder. In his seven years with the Bulls they have not reached the championship round. Yesterday in Detroit they lost the decider in the Eastern Conference best-of-seven play-off 93-74 to the Detroit Pistons, who now play the Portland Trail Blazers. The controversy could hardly have come at a worse time. Like virtually every other professional sportsman in the United States, Jordan endorses a sports shoe. His Nike Air Jordans, retailing for about Pounds 70, have become status symbols in the ghetto. More than one teenager has been killed for them. It is not just shoes that are at risk from this crime wave Chicago police estimate there are 50 apparel-related crimes a month in their city alone but Jordan, the most popular athlete in America, is taking most of the flak. He and Spike Lee, who directs the Air Jordan commercials, have been taken to task by Phil Mushnick, a New York Post sports columnist: 'While Spike Lee watches Michael Jordan dunk all over the world, parents around the country are watching their kids get mugged, or even killed, over the same sneakers Lee and Jordan are promoting . . . It's murder, gentlemen.' Mushnick is echoing a significant minority opinion, expressed around the country, that somehow endorsing basketball shoes sold at luxury prices leads inevitably to underprivileged teenagers committing crimes and that Jordan has a special responsibility to do something about it. Michael Wilbon, an Afro-American born in Chicago and a sports columnist for the Washington Post, takes a different view: 'White people think we all live in the ghetto. They have surreal expectations of what black people should do. Here's a guy who earns seven or eight million dollars with endorsements and they think he should be down in the ghetto eating at Sylvia's greasy spoon.' Nike blame the controversy on racism. 'People have this fixation with sneakers and aren't willing to look at the hard issues underlying crime: poverty, lack of education,' said Nike's Liz Dolan. 'That to me is racist.' Ted Ewanciw, public relations director at Pro-Serv, Jordan's agents, says that the player is troubled by the situation but feels that 'if you take away his shoe endorsements you won't take away inner-city murders'. That the blame should swirl around Jordan reflects his position as the most visible sportsman in the US. Not only does he endorse Nike shoes, he promotes the four aces of American consumer culture: Chevrolet, McDonalds, Coca-Cola and Wheaties. It is remarkable that, with race relations in the US at their most delicate for 30 years, an Afro-American is so popular. There are several reasons. First he is The Natural. As Wilbon explains, 'It's the child factor. He just reaches people . . . not in a weird Michael Jackson way but in a natural way.' Another reason is that compared to gridiron, where the players are encased in Darth Vader protection, or baseball, whose exponents are often a quarter of a mile from spectators, basketball places its players in intimate contact with the crowd. One can hear them talk and see them sweat. The fans can share Jordan's exploits close-up. And he is the greatest performer in the sport today. Unfortunately for Jordan his supporting cast in Chicago cannot come close to his ability. His skills can carry a team single-handedly only so far. Going up against the Pistons, a well-balanced team of bruisers, proved too harsh a challenge, particularly since Jordan, at 6ft 6in and 200lbs, is only average-sized. When he has scored more than 40 points this series his team won. Last night the Pistons, with a front line averaging more than 7ft in height, restricted him to 31 points. While the shoe fuss scarcely seems to have affected his concentration he has uncharacteristically shown frustration with his team-mates, berating them after one loss and allegedly throwing chairs round the changing-room. It would be understandable if the pressure was getting to this most accessible of superstars. The other night in Chicago he left the arena with four bodyguards. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 TO GRASP Michael Jordan 's impact, think in terms of altitude. Think, for example, of his leaps, which take his 6ft 6in frame over the National Basketball Association's seven-footers. He seems to have the ability to remain suspended in the air for seconds on end, hence his nickname, ''Air Jordan''. Even in a sport full of great sportsmen, he rules supreme - he can make some of the finest athletes in the world look slow. Think of his career scoring average of more than 32 points a game, which exceeds that of everyone else. Think of wealth, which is of such size he can help his employers, the Chicago Bulls, meet their payroll. Finally, think of esteem. In a poll published in the 1991 World Almanac, the Chicago Bulls star ranked first when schoolchildren named their hero. He also won in 1990. To many Americans, he is the sporting hero. At 28, Jordan is a basketball player who has become a phenomenon. Defined as a guard, he plays a position all of his own. He raises the event, devising moves in the air. ''Nobody has ever been better at the end of the game than Michael,'' the Bulls coach, Phil Jackson, says. ''Oscar Robertson was great, but this guy was a closer at both ends.'' Larry Bird, himself once heralded as the best player in the league, puts it simply: ''Maybe he's God disguised as Michael Jordan .'' Only one pinnacle has eluded Jordan. Though increasingly viewed as the best basketball player in the history of the game, he has never won an NBA title. Jordan alluded to the fact this week when he was made the NBA's Most Valuable Player for the second time. ''Although it means a lot to me now,'' he said, ''I think a world championship will certainly mean a lot to my team, as well as the city and the organisation. That's where my thoughts and goals are right now.'' This may just be his year. Jordan and the Bulls have reached the Eastern Conference finals - effectively the overall semi-finals - where they are playing the Detroit Pistons in a seven- game series. The Pistons, the reigning champions for the last two years, have been the Bulls' nemesis in each of those seasons, but the Bulls have a better league record this time and have taken a 2-0 lead. ''The last couple of years, we have come closer and closer without going the final step,'' Jordan said. ''They are beatable. We just have to find a way to beat them.'' Finding a way has become a theme of Jordan's life, since being cut from his high school team. The humiliation hurt but he responded by winning a scholarship to North Carolina. He led the college to a national championship, typically hitting the winning shot in a dramatic final. Jordan was the co-captain and star of the 1984 gold medal-winning US team just before his NBA career began. He also was the leading scorer on the US Pan-American team who won gold in Caracas. By then, he was beginning to come to national attention and became the Bulls' first pick of the 1985 draft, and the league's third pick overall. Many players come out of college highly regarded and then disappoint, but Jordan made an immediate impact. He was the Rookie of the Year, and the league soon grew accustomed to seeing him winning matches single-handed. As the accomplishments mounted - they now include five scoring titles and four nominations to the League's All-defensive team - so did his commercial value. He has landed endorsement contracts with Nike, Wilson Sporting Goods, McDonald's, Coca- Cola, Wheaties and Chevrolet. Crucial to his value has been a remarkably clean image. Other professional athletes crash their cars, get arrested for alcohol or drugs, spit on fans or hurl baseballs at them. ''I've spent a life building something positive, being viewed as something positive, and I know any mistake I make could damage that for the rest of my life,'' he said. ''People look for their role models to be almost flawless, and I guess I'm the closest thing to everything being viewed positive, very little being flawed in my life; and I want to maintain that. But it's a tough job. It's the biggest job I have today. Basketball is not the job I have. The job I have is away from pro basketball .'' His endorsements alone are worth millions. He signed an eight-year, $ 25.75m ( pounds 14.88m) contract with the Bulls in 1988, and he has since, more than once, allowed the Bulls to alter the contract, moving money from one year to another, enabling them to sign other players within the salary cap placed on NBA teams. Earlier in this year's play-offs, against the New York Knicks, Jordan did this: standing on the baseline, to the right of the basket, he faked around one defender, only to find two more. He faked past them as well, edging to the basket. In his way was Patrick Ewing, one of the NBA's top defenders. Jordan dunked over him. ''I am probably the most competitive person in the world,'' Jordan once said. ''I compete at everything.'' Once criticised for

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan , probably deep in a summer bunker somewhere on this globe, has been attacked recently, not by bogeys, but by a couple of former athletes. In a recent Sports Illustrated article, Hank Aaron, who was to baseball what Michael Jordan is to basketball , said that Jordan hasn't done enough for the black community. Jim Brown, who was to football what Michael Jordan is to basketball , also said in a radio interview a short while ago that Jordan hasn't done enough for the black community. Each implies that Michael Jordan has been too eager to take the money from White Corporate America, and run. Each says that Michael Jordan could provide a greater inspiration to disadvantaged blacks by speaking out more, being more visible in the black communities. Sharp criticism of a high-profile high-flyer like Jordan is not uncommon. The largest targets must dodge the most arrows. But when big-game hunters like Aaron and Brown are stalking you, it could mean not only that there is trouble, but that there is substance in their quivers, too. In this case, however, both are wrongheaded. Jordan, currently out of the country, was quoted in defense of himself in an article recently in The Chicago Sun-Times: "I think that kind of criticism is totally unfair. I've been trying to have people view me more as a good person than a good black man. I know I'm black. I was born black and I'll die black. That goes without saying. But because I want every kid to be viewed as a person rather than as a member of a certain race does not mean that I'm not black enough. "I've done a lot of things in the black community. If I'm guilty of anything, I'm guilty of not seeking publicity or keeping a record of everybody I've ever helped. I don't want any publicity for it anyway." Tim Hallam, the public relations director for the Chicago Bulls, said: "You can't believe the tremendous number of requests he gets, from sick kids, from hospitals, from schools and community centers, and you can't believe how much stuff he does. Stuff nobody knows about." There are numerous confirmed stories like these: Jordan spending time with a terminally ill white boy, bald from chemotherapy, and signing his Bulls jacket,and the boy later being buried in the jacket, as he had wished. Jordan meeting a little black girl in a wheelchair who was so nervous to meet her hero under the stands that she shook and cried while her parents were snapping a photograph. "Wipe your eyes, honey," Jordan said, "we can't have tears in the picture." Her parents later wrote Jordan a letter thanking him and telling him it was one of the few times in recent months that their daughter had smiled. If interested, one might discover the more public Jordan, in which his foundation has given money to black charities that include the United Negro College Fund and the Midwest Association for Sickle Cell Anemia. He has provided more than a dozen college scholarships to needy black students. And he has lent his name to numerous causes, including one in which his picture was shown on billboards in Chicago and other cities with, "Don't Be Stoopid, Stay in School," written five times like a blackboard exercise. But for Michael Jordan to be helpful to the community, white and black -- and why shouldn't he be as important to white kids as to blacks? Why shouldn't he visit handicapped white kids as well as black? -- his basic obligations, for now, are to do a good job as a basketball player and to be a good citizen. If he wants to be a social leader like Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. or a humanitarian like Albert Schweitzer or a philanthropist like Walter Annenberg, that's even better. What we see in Michael Jordan is first a man who has an honest job, a man of great talent, surely, but who by very hard work has honed and enlarged his skills. What we see in Michael Jordan , at this point, is someone who, unlike some other prominent athletes, has never been arrested, never had a drug problem, never had a streetcorner scuffle, never thrown a woman off a motel balcony, shows signs in his diction of a college education (or at least an awareness of expression), and in his positive demeanor of having listened to his parents. Michael Jordan says he wants to be known as "a good person." While he's hardly perfect, he seems well along in achieving his goal. That alone is enough for whites and blacks and any other colors to admire, respect and learn from. So let Michael Jordan enjoy his summer vacation, if hacking your way through woods and streams in pursuit of a little white ball is your idea of enjoyment. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 President Bush, or anyone else, ought not be upset with Michael Jordan for failing to attend a White House reception last week with the Chicago Bulls. Why should Jordan have traveled all the way to Washington so the President could enjoy such a rare and important photo opportunity? It seems only fair that Bush visit Michael, a busy man with domestic and global responsibilities, at Michael's house. As a habit, Jordan does not favor political alignments, but it is unlikely that he would refuse to open his home to the President, especially if Bush contacted Jordan's agent, David Falk of ProServ, to make an appointment. (White House staff should be aware that Jordan is currently engaged in rigorous two-a-day practices with the Bulls and prefers a relaxing round of golf during the middle of the day.) It's not as if the President is dying in the polls, but an election year is coming, the economy remains weak, the nomination of Clarence Thomas to the Supreme Court can't be helping, and who in this country currently has a higher approval rating than Michael Jordan ? Following his and the Bulls' ascension to the National Basketball Association championship last spring, Jordan has made the final leap from adroitly marketed basketball phenomenon to indisputable American icon. He's everywhere -- cereal boxes, fast-food joints, soft-drink bottles, basketball shoes, in the hearts and minds of millions -- except the White House. Maybe some day he'll run. NBC, realizing the consumer-friendly benefits of Jordan on the air, has recently become the Michael Jordan Channel. First there was a prime-time tribute to Jordan, with the comedian Billy Crystal as host. Then there was the hourlong show to announce the 1992 U.S. of N.B.A. Olympic team, climaxing with a live studio presentation of Jordan as the primary defender of that American birthright, the basketball gold medal. Next came a week's work as the host of "Saturday Night Live." Between television gigs, Jordan slipped down to his home state, North Carolina, to have a stretch of highway named after him. Which will be the first American city to take the next obvious step and rename the airport? One day last summer, sitting in his Roslyn, Va., office, Falk revealed that organizers of the Emmy Awards were after Jordan to appear on their show. "I'd love for him to do it," Falk said. "But I know what he's going to say when I bring it up to him. That's another day gone, when he'd rather be with his family or out on the golf course. I don't even know if I'm going to mention it to him. "You wouldn't believe all the requests that Michael gets. He gets asked to do so many charitable and community activities, more than one a day. If he did them it would be a full-time job. Beyond basketball and the community, he has his business obligations, and like all normal human beings, he wants to have enough time for himself." When Jordan opted not to appear at the White House with the Bulls, his decision created a controversy in Chicago as the Bulls opened their training camp. It was suggested in print and even by some of the Bulls, most notably Horace Grant, that Jordan should not be allowed to place himself above the team. The Bulls' coach, Phil Jackson, was asked to comment, and looked amused. He was likely thinking: Where does everyone think Jordan has been all these years? Larry Bird and Magic Johnson carried the N.B.A. out of its sickbed in the early 1980's and taught it to walk, tall and proud. Jordan is helping it fly. Doing roughly $15 million annually in endorsements, he is Star Trek, the Next Generation, where no athlete has commercially gone before. It apparently took stiffing the President for Chicago to notice that there are different rules for Michael Jordan , and have been since he took that city by storm in 1984. Jordan is no saint. He probably kept Isiah Thomas off the Olympic team. He has been manipulative within the Bulls' organization relating to personnel, just as the much-maligned Thomas has been in Detroit. He has hardly used his power to address prevalent black issues. But here, finally, is a black American athlete so successful in the art of overcoming racial stereotypes and marketing to mythical proportions that he can get away with telling the White House, sorry, he's busy. Maybe next year. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 'He's the greatestathlete I've everseen; he's just not a basketball play-er.'Bill Cartwright. A READER who doesn't understand the dynamics of professional basketball -- or all professional sports, for that matter -- would be inclined to say, "Hey, this Michael Jordan guy is a real dork." But that's exactly what Chicago Tribune sportswriter Sam Smith is trying to do in "The Jordan Rules" (Simon & Schuster, $22). He tells the story of how a pro basketball team overcomes the handicap of having the greatest individual virtuoso in history as its centerpiece player. Even a Michael Jordan must learn how to pay attention to team dynamics. But Smith had to put Jordan's name in the title. People wouldn't be lining up to discover, for example, "The Poop on Pippen." THE SPECTATOR Smith's book takes you through a year in the life of the NBA champion Chicago Bulls. Not too surprisingly, the dominant personality is that of Jordan, the best-known athlete in North America. Only occasionally, is Michael one of the boys. According to the book, he doesn't attend the team Christmas party. He has an extremely sharp tongue and is remarkably intolerant of lesser athletic mortals. He wants very much to lead the league in scoring, and he always knows his pertinent stats. He doesn't like assistant coach Tex Winter's offence because it isn't geared enough to him. Told his minutes will be cut (for his own good), he responds by squeezing off more shots when he is out there. He routinely calls for substitutions. He is so disrespectful of centre Bill Cartwright that he orders teammates not to throw the ball into the pivot during the last four minutes of the game. Not that Cartwright should feel insulted, because at one time or another, Jordan rails about the shortcomings of Scottie Pippen, Horace Grant, Will Perdue, Stacey King and the totality of the bench. In the midst of a frustrating ticket crunch in the 1991 NBA final (John Paxson couldn't even scrounge a ticket in Los Angeles for his father), Jordan flaunts his superstar ability to obtain everything he needs by laying upwards of 15 tickets out on the floor in front of his locker and elaborately stuffing them into envelopes while his left-out teammates silently fume. He refers to said mates as "the Supporting Cast" ("I wish I hadn't used that phrase," he would later say, "but, hell, everyone thinks that way, so why not?"). Is it any wonder his third-person nickname on the team was "The General"? But in the off-centre world of professional sports, all these indictments don't necessarily make him a bad guy. Michael Jordan is, without question, the most physically gifted instrument the Hoop God has ever placed on this earth. The game is so easy for him that he now sits astride the basketball world despite the fact that it is only his second-favorite sporting pastime. For if "The Jordan Rules" reveals nothing else, it gives us a portrait of a man who would play 36 holes of golf a day 365 days a year, if given a chance, and don't think for an instant that's a hyperbolic statement. Insofar as he does care about basketball , however, he wants to win, and he wants to win very badly. How best to accomplish that aim is the central question. Jordan just going out and being Jordan clearly isn't the answer. Kevin Loughery didn't have the answer. Stan Albeck didn't have the answer. Doug Collins didn't have the answer. But Phil Jackson did. The Bulls' head coach has been routinely identified as the "hero" of this book. Call him what you like, but he is clearly the most intelligent and insightful individual among the cast of characters. He respects Jordan's enormous abilities as only a former NBA player could, but at no point does he allow Michael to usurp his own authority. His basketball guru is Red Holzman, and the model he strives to emulate is the New York Knicks of 1968-74, a legendary outfit that proved it is possible for powerful personality to maintain individual identities while working for a common goal. The Knicks were the original "12 players, 12 cabs" team away from the court. There has been no more vivid example of athletic symbiosis on it. How could Michael Jordan not be in love with Michael Jordan ? On a given night, he truly can do whatever he likes. He can dribble by or jump over anyone. He can dunk in traffic or casually drill a three. Larry Bird and Magic Johnson, lesser physical beings, but in their primes better basketball players (indeed, Cartwright says, "He's the greatest athlete I've ever seen; he's just not a basketball player"), will never know the feelings of athletic omnipotence Jordan has experienced. Nor will anyone else know what it's like to be Michael Jordan off the court. He is the object of incessant public attention, and though everyone agrees he handles the crush amazingly well, one cost has been his lack of perspective. headline: JORDAN SEEKS OLYMPIC ANSWERS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. - Michael Jordan is the best basketball player in the world and he plays for the best team in the world. The Chicago Bulls are defending champions. They have nearly lapped the National Basketball Association with a 55-12 record. Jordan again is the leading scorer and so far superior to any other player in the league that the Most Valuable Player voting will be a joke if it is not one-sided. Jordan is at the height of his popularity - perhaps the most popular athlete on the planet. He is a fabulously successful businessman. If Michael Jordan endorses a product, it turns to gold. And he is paid accordingly. It would not be the least bit surprising to find out that Jordan's total value is quickly approaching $100 million. The public Michael Jordan , however, is not a happy man. The simple game of basketball that he has mastered gets very complicated when he leaves the court and walks into the locker room, or the board room. The business of basketball has become so overwhelming that Jordan still is not certain if he is going to play in the Olympics. He'll know more this week when he meets with his financial representatives and legal team in Washington. Jordan says he wants to play in the Olympics. ''I've never had any reservations,'' he said Tuesday night. But he does now, and, not surprisingly, they involved that ugly ''M'' word, money. Which is not to say that Jordan wants to be compensated for playing in the Olympics. He simply wants to make sure that the NBA is not getting rich from the Olympics. ''If no one is going to make money out of this, that's great,'' he said. ''When people start making money, then that's a different situation. USA Basketball (the governing body of U.S. Olympic basketball ) is a non-profit situation. I'd love to see them get all the money. I wouldn't want to see the NBA make tons and tons of dollars off a retainer fee. If we're (players) going to do it and give up our compensation for it, then everybody should.'' Jordan's reservations are primarily focused around one issue. The NBA is a member of USA Basketball , but since USA Basketball is a non-profit organization, it does not have a marketing department. Therefore, USA Basketball asked NBA Properties to market the Olympic effort and is paying NBA Properties a fee. The issue is the fee. How much is it? Jordan wants to know, and no one is saying. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The way MICHAEL JORDAN is telling it in the wake of reports that he ran up more than $100,000 in gambling debts, the worst that can be said about him is that his putting could use a little work. Jordan, the Chicago Bulls' star, said he has gambled on his golf game, but never on basketball . "I am no PETE ROSE," he said. "I can safely say this is not a Pete Rose matter." Rose, of course, was banned from baseball after Commissioner BART GIAMATTI concluded that he had bet on baseball games while serving as the Reds' manager. But as the National Basketball Association began looking into the discovery of several of Jordan's checks that were found among the effects of a slain North Carolina businessman and identified as payments of gambling debts, Jordan went out of his way on Friday to defend himself. "I wasn't involved in any point-shaving or betting on basketball games," he said. "I'm declaring that before anybody asks." Jordan added: "I have a right to be associated with whomever I chose." Bulls owner JERRY REINSDORF weighed in with his own comments on Friday. "If, in fact, Michael lost some money in a golf game or a card game, I don't think it's something to get excited about," he said. "I have a lot of confidence in Michael. Michael is a man of excellent judgment, and I assume he's done nothing he has to be ashamed of." headline: READERS' VIEW
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Jordan's Gambling Is His Business Michael Jordan 's fans stuffed the mailbag this week. Responding to last week's question, "What are your feelings about Michael Jordan ?" in light of recent revelations about his gambling habits, Post-Dispatch readers overwhelmingly said that it's none of their or anyone else's business. Some of the readers' responses follow: I am 64 years old. I enjoy poker on Saturday night, bingo two or three times a week and an occasional visit to the riverboat casinos. (I pay all my bills on time.) My second love is the NBA. Michael Jordan has provided me with countless hours of entertainment. He is truly an artist and an athlete beyond compare. He, like me, relaxes by gambling. Only difference: He has millions. Leave him alone. He is the greatest. Betty Leveque, Mehlville Media and parents have gone haywire in their focus of sports "role models" and their actions away from the playing field. I suggest a survey of the young teens who many feel are tainted by the actions of athletes and the allegations of improprieties to determine what effect, if any, such actions have upon our so-called fragile youth. The results would indicate that kids certainly emulate the athletic feats of sports figures but are not swayed in a positive or negative manner by off-the-field actions. Go ask any 8-year-old trying to imitate Michael Jordan 's cross-over dribble what they think about Jordan betting on his golf game. You'll probably get a shrug of the shoulders and a comment like, "I guess it's OK if it's his money. Now can I get back to shooting my jump shot?" We've survived the Black Sox scandal and Pete Rose (and we know there's always "juice" in the fight game) without damaging the psyche of the nation's youth. To anyone fretting over the "sins" of sports figures and the impact such may have upon our children . . . chill out, it ain't that big a deal. Greg Gibson, Overland - I read with horror the story about Michael Jordan 's gambling spree. The horror was not in Jordan's actions, but the fact that the story was printed in newspapers other than the "Enquirer," and it got the front page, no less. . . . I am not defending Michael Jordan 's actions. I think he is a great basketball player, but he is a man. Thomas Jefferson said that all men have the right to "life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness." It's incredible that a newspaper with the local readership in mind can ignore local events and focus on the private life of a basketball player. Jeffry J. Franz, St. Louis I do not have the pleasure of knowing Mr. Jordan personally, nor does 99.9 percent of the general public that follows his astounding play on the basketball court. Besides being the greatest basketball player of all time, Jordan is a class act and always seems to find time for his fans. It is none of our business as fans to know if a player likes to gamble, or what he eats for breakfast in the morning. I doubt anyone buys Air Jordans because they like the way Michael plays golf. So it is possible to be like Mike - without losing a million on the golf course. He's a great player and a charitable human being. Nothing's changed or tarnished. Stephen Einspanier, St. Louis Q. Are the Cardinals out of it? The Phillies were 24 games over .500, and the Cardinals still barely above .500, going into Friday night's games. Is all hope gone for Cardinals' fans? What must the Cards do to catch the Phillies? Send responses to: Readers Views, c/o Sports Department, St. Louis Post-Dispatch, 900 North Tucker Boulevard, St. Louis, Mo. 63101. All letters must be signed and include an address and phone number for verification. headline: MICHAEL JORDAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 James Jordan, the father of basketball star Michael Jordan , has not been seen in three weeks and his stripped car was found near Fayetteville, police said Thursday. Police said they did not know if foul play was involved. James Jordan, 51 or 52, was last seen July 22 at the funeral of a friend in Burgaw, 60 miles southeast of Fayetteville, and his family had not reported him missing, Sheriff Morris Bedsole said. James Jordan's red Lexus 400 was found Aug. 5 near a wooded area off a Fayetteville road, Bedsole said. Its rear window was smashed and its tires and stereo speakers were missing. The vehicle's vanity license plates also were gone. Police said there was no sign of blood at the car. It was not clear if a missing persons report was filed after officers determined Wednesday that the car belonged to James Jordan. Capt. Art Binder of the sheriff's department said it was not unusual for James Jordan to not contact his family for several days. But authorities said it was unusual for James Jordan to be gone for this long without notifying someone of his whereabouts. James Jordan attended the funeral of a friend, Willie J. Kemp, the afternoon of July 22. "He was just James Jordan like he's always been," Mrs. Willie Kemp said. Members of Michael Jordan 's security staff flew to Fayetteville from Chicago on Wednesday, officers said. The security staff is the only contact the sheriff's department has had with Jordan's family. Chicago Bulls spokesman John Diamond said Jordan had not contacted the team, but that was not unusual during the offseason. Michael Jordan grew up in Wilmington and visits Charlotte often to see his family and friends. James Jordan spends considerable time in Chicago and is often at his son's restaurant. He is easy going - quick with a joke, a cigarette always in hand. He and his wife owned a sporting goods store in Charlotte but sold it about two years ago, said store clerk Carriann Valentine. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 FAYETTEVILLE, North Carolina: Police are investigating the mysterious disappearance of James Jordan, father of basketball superstar Michael Jordan . Mr Jordan has not been seen since he attended the funeral of a close friend in Burgaw on July 22. His Lexus 400 car was found last week near woods off a Fayetteville road. Its rear window was smashed and its tyres and stereo speakers missing. The car's personalised number plates also were gone. Police said there was no blood or signs of a struggle at the car. No threats or ransom demands had been reported. A missing person's report was filed on Wednesday when officers determined the car belonged to James Jordan, police said. "It's not unusual for Mr Jordan not to let someone know where he's at," said Art Binder, chief of detectives in the sheriff's department. A member of Michael Jordan 's security staff flew to Fayetteville from Chicago on Thursday. In Chicago, John Diamond, a spokesman for the Chicago Bulls, said Jordan had not contacted the team, but that was not unusual for the off-season. "Obviously, we are concerned about the information that we've heard, but we don't want to jump to conclusions," Mr Diamond said. Mr Jordan and his wife, Deloris, had owned a sporting goods store in Charlotte but sold the business about two years ago. The family previously lived in Wilmington, where Michael Jordan grew up. He played for the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, the current National Collegiate Athletic Associaton champions, before signing with the Bulls. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WASHINGTON - Police believe the father of Michael Jordan , the US basketball superstar, was kidnapped before being shot dead, and are treating his death as murder, writes Phil Reeves. Authorities in the United States said James Jordan, 57, whose body was found floating in a creek in South Carolina, died of a gunshot wound to the chest from a .38 calibre weapon. Michael Jordan , whose huge success with the Chicago Bulls has earned him fans worldwide but whose image has been marred by gambling scandals, made no immediate public statement. His father's decomposed body was found in a river 11 days ago, but officials said it took some time to identify using dental records. Nine days ago, Mr Jordan's luxury red Lexus car was found about 50 miles away, near Fayetteville, in the neighbouring state of North Carolina. It had been stripped of its fittings and vandalised. Concern about Mr Jordan's whereabouts had been growing since the discovery of his car. He had last been seen attending a funeral of a friend in Burgaw, 60 miles from Fayetteville, on 22 July. His wife has told authorities that she last spoke to him four days later. The authorities said they had no suspects, nor had they determined any motive for the killing - but the FBI said a kidnapping investigation had begun. The death of his father is another blow to Michael Jordan , whose once-flawless career has recently been marred by off-the-court troubles, mostly related to gambling. His huge popularity - polls among youngsters in Britain, Europe and even China have placed him at or near the top - has earned him millions. He led the Chicago Bulls to three consecutive victories at the National Basketball Association finals, and was a member of the ''dream team'', the 1992 US Olympic basketball team. But a cloud descended this summer when a San Diego businessman and former friend published a book alleging that the basketball star refused to pay a $ 1.2m ( pounds 821,000) gambling debt run up on the golf course in 1991. Much of the debt has been settled, although his accuser recently claimed Jordan still owes him $ 100,000. Jordan began this season with a reprimand from the National Basketball Association for betting on golf games. And last year he was called to testify in a court case after a $ 57,000 cheque from his account was found in the possession of an alleged drug dealer and money launderer. Jordan admitted that he had lied when he originally said the money was a personal loan. In reality, it was payment for losses incurred during a poker weekend. headline: JORDAN, DAD SHARED CHARACTERISTICS, INTERESTS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 There's a lot of James Jordan in his son. Father passed on his looks to Michael Jordan - broad nose, prominent cheekbones, a smile that curves at the corners into dimples - his high spirits and sense of mischief. And now, after James Jordan's slaying, a world of Michael Jordan worshipers struggles to understand why anyone would kill the hero's father and just who was this man who helped raise a basketball legend. To those who knew him, he was more than Michael's dad - he was the uncle who persuaded Cynthia Jordan to stay in high school, the motorist who picked up hitchhikers, the longtime friend who drove July 21 from his home near Mint Hill, N.C., to the coast for friend Willie Kemp's funeral. "From the day we met him 24 years ago until the last time I saw him, he was just James Jordan," said Kemp's widow, Azella. "He enjoyed Michael, but he enjoyed other things. He loved the country, and he loved his family, and he loved his friends." So why is it that James Jordan's wife and no one else in his family thought it strange when they didn't hear from him for three weeks after the funeral? "She would be on one end of the country, and he's on the other end, and they would know where each other's basically supposed to be, but they didn't always talk," said the Rev. Lonnie Williams, a friend from Wilmington. Born a sharecropper's son, James Raymond Jordan grew up on hard work and hard times in the town of Wallace, north of Wilmington. He met his wife, Deloris, through her brothers and cousins with whom he played basketball . Eventually, his sense of humor and kindness won her over, and she accepted his marriage proposal in April 1957 while he was stationed in Virginia in the Air Force. The newlyweds settled in his hometown, and he began work as a forklift operator at the Wilmington GE plant. The fourth of their five children, Michael, was born Feb. 17, 1963, in Brooklyn, N.Y., where Jordan had gone to take vocational courses so he could win promotions - and he did, first to dispatcher, then to supervisor. They moved in 1970 to Wilmington, where Jordan built a split-level home. Their backyard quickly became the neighborhood playground. Other boys gathered under the basketball hoop to take on the three Jordan boys - James, Larry and Michael. The Jordans tried to raise their children colorblind and self-assured. They taught them the importance of family and of education. When niece Cynthia Jordan, 27, wanted to drop out of school, Uncle "Raymond" - as she called James Jordan - talked her out of it. And when friends needed money, he reached into his pocket. Then Michael Jordan became famous. He was just a freshman at North Carolina when his field goal in the 1982 NCAA basketball championship game against Georgetown University won the Tar Heels the national title. From North Carolina to the National Basketball Association Chicago Bulls, the enormity of his son's talents and popularity sometimes frightened James Jordan. "It's scary all of this could start in a little town like Wilmington, N.C. It scares the daylights out of you," James Jordan said in May. "To watch all that has happened to him, you say, 'How the hell is it possible?' You don't try to understand it. If I sat around and dwelled on the thought of what he's doing, it would scare me to death. You have to let it drift away. There's no way you can rationalize the whole thing." James Jordan's brother, Gene, said Friday: "Being Michael's father didn't change him." But when James Jordan pleaded guilty of accepting a $7,000 kickback in 1985, speculation was he needed the money because he spent too much time following his son to ballgames, from Chapel Hill to Europe to Hawaii. Jordan accepted the kickback from a contractor while he was in charge of inventory control at GE. He received a three-year sentence, suspended for five years, was placed on supervised probation for five years and was fined $1,000. Soon afterward, the family moved to the Charlotte area. It was here that Michael Jordan helped launch his parents into business. At his mother's urging, he formed the Michael Jordan Foundation to help charities such as United Negro College Fund, Midwest Association for Sickle Cell Anemia, Ronald McDonald Children's Charities. James Jordan and other family members owned and operated three sporting goods stores in Charlotte - Flight 23 By Jordan. James Jordan also ran JVL Enterprises in Rock Hill, which makes T-shirts, socks and shorts. When James Jordan wasn't at work, he followed his son around. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WASHINGTON - Federal agents investigating the violent death of James Jordan, the father of Michael Jordan , the world's most celebrated basketball star, yesterday questioned family members about the long delay in reporting the victim as missing, writes David Usborne. Officials announced on Friday that a body discovered floating in a South Carolina creek on 3 August had been identified as that of Mr Jordan. He had been shot in the chest. An FBI spokesman, Thomas Lusby, indicated that the death was being treated as a murder and a possible case of kidnapping. Through his sport, Michael Jordan had made his family extremely wealthy. Both he and his father were also renowned for their gambling. The Reverend Jesse Jackson wants the Attorney General, Janet Reno, to set up an investigation into the death. ''First it began to look like a simple robbery. Then it began to look like a gangland slaying. Then it began to look like a cover-up,'' said Mr Jackson. James Jordan had not been seen since 22 July, and the FBI expressed surprise that his family had not reported his disappearance earlier. Police in North Carolina, where Mr Jordan lived, disclosed only on Thursday that he was missing and that a luxury car discovered abandoned and stripped on 5 August had been his. Mr Lusby said he had received no clear explanation for the delay from the grieving family members, but said he hoped they would explain when they were ready. Michael Jordan arrived in North Carolina by jet on Friday from California, where he had been on holiday. In June Michael Jordan took the Chicago Bulls to a third straight National Basketball Association championship. headline: FOUR HELD IN JORDAN CASE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KEYWORD-HIT Four held over Jordan death FAYETTEVILLE, North Carolina: Investigators said yesterday they had charged four suspects with stealing parts of the car that belonged to basketball superstar Michael Jordan 's murdered father, James Jordan . But Cumberland County Sheriff Morris Bedsole said no suspects had been found yet in the killing of Jordan, whose decomposed body was found on August 3 in a creek just south of the border between North and South Carolina. QNP Authorities arrested the four and charged them with stealing parts of Jordan's car, found about 100km from where the body was found, Mr Bedsole said. ""We hope to arrest one more soon," Mr Bedsole said. He said he did not believe those charged in the car case were involved in Jordan's killing: ""Personally, I would believe the (murder) suspects are in this area." ""These people may have crossed paths," said Captain Art Binder, the county sheriff's department's chief of detectives. Mr Jordan's red Lexus car was found on August 5 severely vandalised and stripped of its tyres and stereo equipment. The investigation into Jordan's death has turned towards his finances. FBI officials believe Mr Jordan might have been kidnapped. Michael Jordan , the professional basketball star who is one of the world's best-known and wealthiest athletes, broke his silence on the case yesterday, issuing a statement through the Michael Jordan Foundation in Chicago. ""We will respect the request of law enforcement officials that we not comment regarding the circumstances surrounding his death while their investigations are continuing," he said. Mr Jordan's body was found floating in a creek in South Carolina with a single .38-calibre gunshot wound to the chest last week. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 AFAMOUS writer tells of finding himself in a lift (once up, once down) with celebrities. One was Charlton Heston - yes, his shoulders are at least four feet broad and he took up all the horizontal space; the other was the Chicago Bulls basketball star Michael Jordan , who, though but 6ft 6in - short by National Basketball Association standards - did the same thing with the vertical space: an illusion that many who have played against Jordan must know. Both had something more than mere physical presence: they had the unmistakable aura of kingship. In this respect, Jordan is the athletic king of kings, ''the first great athlete of the wired world'', as one profile put it: earnings from the game and endorsement deals $ 30m-plus a year, fame total, visibility complete, fun absolute. Bigger than Pele, Arnold Palmer, Muhammad Ali? Yessir. Pele was a star before television became the average man's access to the divine; Palmer played a sport that emphasises obsession, not communication; and Ali blotted his copybook with Vietnam politics and Islam. Michael Jordan does everything right; he is the darling of his time; and he does that latest thing, he performs and entertains at the same time. Not just in America, for he is as big in Belgrade, Athens and Paris as there. Wherever basketball is played, Jordan dominates. You'd think the world was this man's oyster. True. But adumbrations of a coming to grief have hung over Jordan for some years, starting with allegations about his ''compulsive'' gambling. Now that grief has struck close to home. On 3 August his father was found floating in a South Carolina creek with a hole in his chest. There have been hints in the media that Jordan's gambling and his father's death may be linked, and that this could be the beginning of the end of Michael Jordan . Riding on his broad shoulders, the Chicago Bulls have been champions for three years running. A guard, Jordan is the best defender in basketball ; on attack, no one has more moves in the air or a more consistent shot. Shaven head, bat ears, tongue hanging out (a trademark adopted from watching his father working on cars), alternating between shiftless grin and fierce glare, Jordan improvises within that microspace known as ''the paint'', a narrow cube under the basket thronged with bodies. He is not known as ''Air'' Jordan for nothing, and what he does up there, alone, in his huge hangtime (the time a player remains in the air), is something special and beautiful. The ball goes to him and one has expectations: of success, but also of fun: what will he do this time? On and off the court, Jordan has earned his respect, and his big bucks. As a black athlete, he's one of the few who is also a darling of the white world that runs sports and the media. He hasn't collapsed from an overdose of cocaine, or a dicky heart; he hasn't slept with 20,000 women and been shown to be HIV positive; he hasn't bullied Miss Black America candidates; he hasn't been arrested for bar-room brawls, or for hit-and-run accidents. All these things happened to other black athletes, and the sport in which Jordan competes is - though don't be fooled, competition between blacks and whites is as fierce in basketball as in baseball - the nearest thing to a level (racial) playing field America has. Now the senseless, ugly death of his father has brought up the old sense of Nemesis: that the mighty of this world can always be brought down by the merest accidental flings of the gods. The Jordan family is a close one, and Michael is not the product of a stereotypical, late-20th-century background. His grandfather may have been a sharecropper in rural North Carolina, there may have been the privations of pre-war black life in the South, but his father, James, worked his way out of that - though not without setbacks, including a three-year suspended sentence for taking kickbacks when he was head of inventory control at General Electric's plant in Wilmington, NC. Michael's mother, Deloris, was a bank manager, and Michael was educated at the University of North Carolina, a basketball powerhouse but also a place where Michael, a serious student, a member of a black Honour Society honed his human skills. The background, then, is black middle class, and Jordan may indeed have a passionate intensity, but it was not and is not that of the rebel or the loose cannonball; it was and is that of the now 30-year-old who knows what it takes to make it. That is why the flaws loom so large in the public mind, for a role model (which is what Michael is condemned, by the media and his worshipful public, to be) cannot have feet of clay. What are these ''flaws''? First, there are the public ones. There is the Michael Jordan who takes his Ferrari on the highways of Chicago at 100-plus miles an hour, and who knows he's immune from prosecution: the cops would ask for his autograph. There is the Michael Jorda

headline: REPORTS CITE KILLING OF HIS FATHER FOR SUPERSTAR'S DECISION TO RETIRE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan , the world's most famous athlete and basketball 's greatest player, will announce his retirement today, according to NBC and published reports. ''Dateline NBC,'' the Denver Post and the Chicago Sun-Times reported the news almost simultaneously last night. Jordan, NBA scoring champion the past seven years, leader of the three-time champion Chicago Bulls and two-time Olympic gold-medal winner, is expected to cite his father's slaying this summer as the reason for his retirement at age 30. ''It's time for me to move on to something else,'' the Sun-Times quoted Jordan as saying. ''I know a lot of people are going to be shocked by this decision and probably won't understand. But I've talked it over with my family and friends, and most of all I'm at peace with myself over the decision.'' His father James' death apparently robbed Jordan of his desire to play, the Post said. Jordan's father was shot to death as he napped in his car along a North Carolina roadside on July 23. His luxury sports car was later found stripped and his body was found in a South Carolina creek two weeks later. Two North Carolina teen-agers have been charged with murder and armed robbery. The elder Jordan shared in the successes and failures of his son. When the Chicago Bulls star was accused of excessive gambling during last season's NBA playoffs, James Jordan said a trip to an Atlantic City, N.J., casino was his idea. ''I took him with me. He just paid the bill,'' he said. At one point, when an angry Michael Jordan refused to speak with reporters over the gambling issue, his father acted as a spokesman. Dateline NBC producer Neil Shapiro said the show learned of Jordan's plans from ''authoritative sources close to the NBA.'' The Bulls have scheduled a news conference at 11 a.m. EDT today to make what they called a ''major announcement.'' Jordan threw out the ceremonial first pitch at last night's playoff opener between the Chicago White Sox and the Toronto Blue Jays at Comiskey Park in Chicago. He left after the seventh inning. Jerry Reinsdorf, owner of the White Sox and Bulls, said from the ballpark: ''I have nothing to say tonight. We'll have something to say tomorrow.'' In a copyright story, the Denver Post said Bulls coach Phil Jackson had confirmed that Jordan was retiring. The Post said Jackson would not say if Jordan had indicated his decision was irrevocable. According to the paper, a source close to Jordan said he has told friends he just doesn't have the fire to play any more. Jordan began his NBA career in 1984 after being named college player of the year at the University of North Carolina in both 1983 and '84. Jordan averaged 32.3 points per game during his nine NBA seasons, leading the league in scoring his last seven seasons, a record. He is also a three-time Most Valuable Player and is also a six-time all- league defensive player. He averaged 34.7 points in the playoffs and played in the NBA All-Star Game every year he's been in the league. Jordan led the Bulls to their first NBA title in 1991. The Bulls won their second straight title in 1992 and made it three in a row when by beating the Phoenix Suns in June. Jordan also found nothing but success in the Olympics. In 1984, before his rookie season in the NBA, he was coach Bob Knight's best player as the U.S. team went unbeaten through the basketball tournament in Los Angeles. It was the last time a team of collegiate players managed to win a gold medal for the United States. Jordan was back in 1992 when the Olympics allowed professional players into Olympic basketball for the first time. Jordan, the brightest star on the ''Dream Team,'' joined with Magic Johnson and Larry Bird to romp throughout the Barcelona Games. All of that team's stars subjugated their offensive talents for the success of the team, and none more than Jordan.
