fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 AS if hammering home a slam dunk from nowhere, Chicago Bulls star Michael Jordan announced his retirement Wednesday, ending the career of the one of the most stellar pro basketball players and most prominent sports celebrities in the world. The announcement forever freezes Mr. Jordan's career at its zenith, shocking hometown Chicago not so much with what it ends as with what it denies. Just a few months ago, Jordan led the Bulls to their third straight National Basketball Association (NBA) title. Fans hoped that Jordan, through his dazzling play on both ends of the court, would lift the Bulls in the coming season to another title and himself to an unsurpassed position in the firmament of NBA stars. ''Ending my NBA career does not mean I won't play basketball somewhere else,'' Jordan said in making his announcement. ''I am very solid with my decision not to play.....I have always stressed to people who have known me that when I lose the sense of not having to prove something as a basketball player it is time for me to move away from basketball .'' Jordan, who was once called the Babe Ruth of his sport by Bulls owner Jerry Reinsdorf, has hung up his jersey over someone who never donned the same red and black uniform but who was known always to be at the center court of the ball player's heart. On July 23 Jordan's father, James, was slain on a North Carolina roadside. The senior Jordan was known for helping his son put the dizzying pressure and esteem of pro sports in perspective. He frequently went on the road with the team, playing cards in a hotel room with his son safe from the brash klieg lights of media attention. By leaving the court at the acme of his career, Jordan hopes to avoid the slow decline in performance that has sullied the final seasons of many great players of basketball and other sports. In many ways, Jordan has etched an incomparable record with a combination of hard-driving finesse on both offense and defense: * Tied with Wilt Chamberlain with seven consecutive scoring titles. * The highest career scoring average for the regular season, with 32.3 points per game. * An average of 35.1 points in playoff games. * An all-defensive, first-team member in the past six seasons. * Three regular-season Most Valuable Player awards. * Two Olympic gold medals. Jordan became not only the most glittering star in the National Basketball 's galaxy but one of the world's most recognizable celebrities, with his trademark shaved head, roomy shorts, and tongue-out court efforts. Jordan the celebrity is partly the outgrowth of mega-endorsement contracts he has signed with Nike, McDonald's, and other corporations. These have turned him into a commercial messenger of the highest magnitude. Air Jordan sneakers and Nike's ''I like Mike'' are part of America's sidewalk vernacular. His status on and off the court helped propel the whole NBA to a new visibility. ''I still won't believe it,'' Reggie Miller, a guard for the Indiana Pacers, told WBBN radio. ''To me he's still playing for the black and red; I'm still kinda in denial if you want to say so.'' ''I'm stunned, totally stunned. I thought he'd play at least one year more, but it takes a lot of guts to quit at the top of your career,'' says Brett Hall, a Federal Express delivery man and a devout Bulls fan. ''A lot of fans won't be as interested in the Bulls like before - they'll be looking more at the [football] Bears now.'' headline: RIVER JORDAN WANTS TO GO WITH THE FLOW
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 We are stunned and want to know how Michael Jordan could quit at the peak of his career, how he could fly away from fame and a $ 40 million annual income when he was still the best basketball player in the history of mankind. How could the world's most famous athlete leave his basketball shoes, leave his identity, leave the applause, leave the paycheck, leave those tantalizing battles with Charles Barkley? How could Jordan just drop the ball, walk out of the arena and go home? You want to know how Michael Jordan could exchange a fantasy for the daily routine of a private citizen: just another guy who will mow the lawn, give his kids a bath, take his wife to the movies, hold his mother's hand, pick up a loaf of bread at the store, maybe even dump a load of laundry into the washing machine. Jordan was the American Dream, in living color in a baggy basketball uniform, gliding with ease through the currents of modern culture. When he played the game, Jordan held an ordinary basketball in his hands. But his popularity was so global, that basketball could have been the world in his hands. Michael Jordan , at 30, had everything. Almost. Everything except a life. One of his most famous shoe commercials showed Jordan alone in a gym, practicing foul shots, making a quiet plea into the soundtrack: "Can you imagine just being a basketball player? I can." But he knew that this basic request was impossible. Jordan - like Ali, DiMaggio and Pele - had transcended his sport and his own flesh and blood. He wasn't a player or a man; he was an icon. A patent. A trademark. Each time he breezed onto the court, we expected Jordan to be perfect. Only Jordan can understand the exacting nature of that pressure. Jordan won three NBA championships, one NCAA title, two Olympic gold medals. He led the NBA in scoring his last seven years. He averaged 32.3 points per game. He had nothing to prove. Jordan, the player, may be leaving so that Jordan, the person, can have a normal existence. And that is why he's flying away for now, though he left instructions to leave the doors to the gym open, just in case he decides to return after he's had some time to live for himself instead of others. With selfish hearts, we hope that one day he will get the urge to play again. Because there never will be another Michael Jordan . Unless Jordan comes back. And I think he will get the hunger to compete again. Jordan is leaving on his own terms. It is only fitting that he go out as a conqueror. We wouldn't want to see him staying too long, struggling to locate his youth, the rim suddenly out of reach. So many famous athletes - Willie Mays, Nolan Ryan, Steve Carlton, John Unitas, Muhammad Ali - stayed too long. But Jordan's youth also makes this a sad occasion. Old bones didn't stop Michael . Jordan 's skills were still so electric that he seemingly could throw on a switch and dominate a game on whim, like he did in leading the Chicago Bulls to three consecutive NBA championships. On the court, Michael defied gravity. But the demands of fame finally sucked the air out of him, took him down to the floor where he wants to try to walk like any other man. And if we can stop putting ourselves into his dream - who wouldn't want to be Michael Jordan , right? - we can understand his craving for anonymity. Jordan, it seems, recognized that his life - like the basketball that defined him - was hollow. Jordan had millions of fans. But the one he cherished most, his father, was gone. Jordan could drive the lane like no player before him. But it took four security guards to lead him to his car after games so he could drive away from the arena. Oddly, Jordan was safest on the basketball court, where no one could touch him except a hand-checking defender. But the simple act of checking into a hotel room required security measures normally associated with the protection of a president. Jordan was wealthy beyond imagination, but didn't have the means to buy privacy. He had the money to purchase any luxury item but could only shop at midnight, when the stores were closed and he'd arrange to browse by himself. Dining out became such a spectacle that he finally built his own restaurant, with a special sealed-off room for his family. He had his own line of expensive basketball shoes. But he became horrified to learn that inner-city kids were murdering one another to gain possession of them. Jordan tried to escape by playing golf, but some old golf-course wagers with a buddy became the juicy details of a sleazy book used to exploit him. Each day, busloads of grandmothers pull into Atlantic City to try their luck at the gaming tables. But when Jordan visited a casino on his own time during last season's playoffs, the innocent trip became a tabloid sensation. Jordan tried to accommodate the media, patient and more gracious than any sports star of his magnitude. But when his father James was shot

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Many young basketball players yesterday mourned Michael Jordan 's retirement. It was too soon, some said. They didn't understand. But Felipe Lopez, 18 years old, a star basketball player at Rice High School in Harlem, didn't take it so hard. He said he realized Michael Jordan was more than a basketball player -- he was a man. And like any other, he has the right to grow tired and move on with his life. "Really, we've got to understand that he is a human being, too," said Lopez, one of the top high school players in the country. "By losing his father, he lost his motivation." " Basketball is over some day," added Lopez, a shooting guard who has a chance to become the first guard in New York state history to score more than 2,000 points and pull down 1,000 rebounds. "Sometimes, you just move on." A Role Model Lost Jordan, 30, made his announcement at a news conference in Deerfield, Ill., a Chicago suburb. But the news touched the hearts of young basketball players throughout New York City. Many said they would miss the way Jordan mesmerized a crowd and revolutionized the game. "When you go to bed at night, you're looking forward to his highlights and numbers when you get up in the morning," said Scientific Mapp, 16, a point guard at Rice. But Gregory Glenn, a shooting guard at Walton High School in the Bronx, said the lesson from Jordan he will cherish most is that the best way to be like Mike is to be yourself. "You think of all his achievements and you know you can do it in your own way," Glenn said. "We look up to him not to be like him. We try to take some of the steps he took, and make our own path." Jaeme Cannon, 14, a member of the Gauchos, an elite New York amateur basketball team, said he started playing basketball because of Jordan. "No one will be able to fill Michael's shoes," he said. "I know many people who wear Michael Jordan sneakers and hats. Even my grandmother, who is 64 and knows nothing about basketball , likes red because it's Michael's color." For some high school players, Jordan's early retirement meant that there is more to life than basketball . "It's time to take care of his family, that's what he's trying to say," said Carlos Hines, 18, who is a co-captain at Walton High School. " Basketball isn't everything." Though Jordan denied his retirement was directly linked to the murder of his father this past July, some high school players said they believed that it would difficult for Jordan to continue playing without his father's presence. "When your friends come to the game, you get a boost, and that's probably the way it was for him," said Gary Bankas, 15, who also plays for Walton. "Every time he got the ball he probably wanted to score for his pops." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Leroy Wright and other Roosevelt High School students carried extracurricular reading with them to classes on Wednesday. It was the morning's sports section, bearing word that Michael Jordan was leaving basketball . In the Minneapolis school, in surrounding neighborhoods and beyond, Jordan's departure caused consternation and disappointment that crossed age, gender and racial lines. Wright planned to take the paper home and show his mother, because she likes Jordan as much as Leroy does. "This is a heartbreaker," Leroy, 18, said between classes. "I grew up watching Michael Jordan . When I think of basketball , I think of Michael Jordan ." A floor above, teacher Sue Feely's mass media class put aside talk of television and discussed Jordan's retirement at age 30. Everyone knew he was a superstar who led the Chicago Bulls to three titles in the National Basketball Association, but their appreciation reached past basketball . "He's my boy!" said Bonnie Kendrick, 17. "It's not just the way he played, it's the way he talks to people - and not just in commercials. "It's the way he feels about the black culture. We all need a black role model, and he's one." Temeka Plair, 17, sat beside Kendrick, and the two of them singled out Jordan's public-service announcements urging kids to finish high school. It was fast-break commentary, each rushing to finish the other's thoughts: "What he said was that everybody can do something if they put their minds to it. . . ." ". . . . and get an education, no matter how your parents treat you. . . ." In the front row, 16-year-old Anna Johnston said softly, " Michael Jordan has influenced a lot of kids with those stay-in-school commercials. You don't hear a lot about the importance of school anymore." Of course, Jordan represented more than a voice for education. He was a marketing dream and dynamo who earned millions hustling everything from hamburgers to sneakers. "A jacket, poster, books - I've got all kinds of things," said Duane Brooks, 17. "I watched Michael play in all those championship games, and he was all I cared about. I think I was his No. 1 fan." Several Roosevelt students predicted that Jordan - as Magic Johnson did - would return to the game. "I don't think he's gone," said Mike Neubert, 17. "A legend and idol like him won't just stop. He'll come back. But it may be a couple of years down the road." Robert Mestas, 17, a Roosevelt point guard regarded as one of the top basketball players in the state, has kept the sports sections detailing each of the Bulls' three championships with Jordan. "It's the best basketball that's ever been played, and I wanted memorabilia," he said. "I want to show it to my kids some day. I'll lie a little and say I'm the only one in the world to have them." Jordan's high-stakes gambling didn't dim Mestas' loyalty, nor that of other South Siders interviewed. "It had nothing to do with basketball ," Mestas said. "If any other person would have done it, it wouldn't have been news. People tried to mess with his mind." A few blocks away, at Hiawatha Lake Park, Marvin Johnson, 32, of Minneapolis, played hoops with four other men. Johnson, who once worked as an usher at Chicago Stadium during Bulls games, had seen Jordan play many times. "I used to love the way the dude dunked, with that tomahawk jam, when he seemed to just come sailing from midcourt with his tongue half out," Johnson said. "That wasn't the way most people do it." At McRae Park in Minneapolis, 5-year-old Christian Braye said that he wasn't much of a basketball fan, but that he did know about Jordan. "I see so much of him on the TV," Braye said. "He looks nice. But I like baseball." Darvine Gill, recreation coordinator at McRae, sensed how Jordan's retirement crushed her children. "It was a great disappointment to them," she said. "A big part of being a parent is not what kids say, but how they say it. And I know how they were saying Michael Jordan retired. I think his father's death [in a shooting] took its toll on him. You could see the change in his face. He was not the joyful person you had seen." Not far from the main building, in the warm afternoon sun, 11-year-old John Healey tossed a football to a friend. "[Jordan] is my favorite player," Healey said. "I like his jump shots and his layups and how he can slam. But it makes me a little upset that he's quitting and letting his teammates down." No, not everybody wants to be like Mike. "I don't want to be like him. I want to be like Duane," said Duane Brooks, the big Jordan fan. "But Michael is a cool person." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NEW YORK: The two teenagers charged with murdering the father of basketball superstar Michael Jordan now stand accused of robbing America of its greatest national hero. The random killing of James Jordan beside a North Carolina highway in August claimed millions of new victims this week - the US went into collective mourning when Michael, greatest basketball player of all time, arguably the world's greatest athlete, hero to a nation, announced his shock retirement. At the age of 30 Jordan, still shattered by his father's brutal death, said he no longer has the fire to continue in the NBA. With wife Juanita by his side and flanked by Chicago bulls management as well as some of the sponsors who contribute to his $50 million a year fortune, Jordan said he had "reached the pinnacle of my career" and had nothing left to achieve. And while he was at pains to point out that James Jordan's murder was not the sole reason for his retirement, he betrayed his innermost thoughts when he said: "I guess the biggest positive I can take out of my father not being here today is that he saw my last basketball game, and that means a lot. It is something that we have talked about a lot." He is the greatest athlete ever to play a team sport James Jordan was more than a father to Michael. He was his idol, mentor, and best friend. When he died a, large part of the incomparable Jordan died with him, and the part which remains no longer has the passion which characterised a spectacular career. Jordan once said he never relaxed in a game until he spotted his parents safely in their courtside seats. "At least that way I know I have at least one fan in the crowd," he said. Fittingly, he leaves the game he has completely dominated for a decade still at the top - having led the Chicago Bulls from the NBA cellar to three consecutive championships. But, he did not rule out a possible comeback if the competitive flame which flickers within is reignited. "I think the word retire means you can do whatever you want, and maybe someday down the road, that's what I'll desire to do," he said. He remains contracted to the Bulls and his financial security is guaranteed, with his estimated $35 million worth of annual corporate endorsements valid whether he plays or not. " Michael Jordan did not retire from Nike today," said Nike chief Phil Knight. "Our nine-year collaboration has always been more than an endorsement deal." Other companies including McDonalds and Gatorade also added their support for Jordan's decision. To have seen the artistry and grace of Jordan in full flight, slicing between defenders, or soaring above them in defiance of gravity, is to understand the loss Americans felt. "I used to say Michael Jordan was the Babe Ruth of basketball . Now I believe that Babe Ruth is the Michael Jordan of baseball," said Chicago Bulls chairman Jerry Reinsdorf. "He is the greatest athlete ever to play a team sport." Jordan's long-time friend and arch-rival Charles Barkley said he was disappointed "because we always said we wanted to retire together - we came in together and we wanted to leave together". "The events of this summer have changed everything and I want for Michael to be happy, that is my main concern," Barkley said. " Michael Jordan is the only person in this entire world that I've ever met who is as competitive as I am. That's why I'll miss playing against him." Bulls team-mate John Paxson, who played alongside Jordan for eight years, said team members spent an emotional time with their superstar team-mate before the announcement. "It was very emotional. A lot of things were said that people have probably been wanting to say for a long time, but the time was never right. The guys expressed how thankful they were to have played with the greatest player of all time," he said. Magic Johnson, the only modern-day player to rival Jordan in terms of talent and charisma, also paid tribute to his close friend. "I was always in awe of Michael's talent on the court but I have always been even more in awe of Michael Jordan the man. We are great friends and I hope whatever he chooses to do brings him happiness." Tom Wilson, president of Detroit Pistons, described Jordan as "the greatest player who ever played the game, and the most special athlete I've had the pleasure of watching." A look at the statistics sheets tell part, but not all, of the Jordan legend. Along with his three straight NBA titles, Olympic gold medals in 1984 and 1992 and a national collegiate title with North Carolina in 1982, Jordan holds just about every record worth mentioning in professional basketball . He was three times voted the NBA's most valuable player and three times the MVP of a playoff series and won seven straight scoring titles. He won rookie-of-the-year in 1985 and remained an All-Star for every year after that. He scored a career-best 69 points in one match against Cleveland in

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE reaction of fans to Michael Jordan 's retirement from basketball was nothing short of disgraceful. The man who set the standard for others to aspire to was widely criticised for his decision to quit basketball . It highlights the pressure star athletes are under. Chicago Bulls "fan" Jim Turgeon suggested Jordan had a responsibility to his fans to continue playing basketball . Another fan, Greg Barbieri, said it would be a shame to waste that talent. "What's he going to do with his life, sit around and feel sorry for himself?" Jordan deserves better than this. People like Mr Barbieri lose sight of the fact that despite his ability, Michael Jordan is human. Superstars are invincible in the public eye. Fans live out their dreams and fantasies watching the stars in action. The reality is that fans don't want to see Michael Jordan the human, they want to see Superman. They can see a human at any basketball stadium. Fans can simply not accept that their heroes are just the same as they are. Jordan said as much at his media conference: " Michael Jordan was once a basketball player but now he's a human being. A man. He's got other things he must achieve." "When I stepped on to the basketball court, there wasn't anything left for me to prove. "You (the media) don't have sympathy for normal people sometimes." Jordan walks away from basketball as the greatest player ever to step on to a court, taking with him three NBA championships and two Olympic gold medals. So far-reaching was his appeal that in a recent survey, Australian children voted him their favorite sporting icon. But as Los Angeles Laker James Worthy said, life is bigger than basketball . And there comes a point when a man has to move on. In Jordan's own words: "I've been on this rollercoaster for nine years. It's time to ride something else." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Deep in the heart of Illinois, a large city is crying. Around the globe, the sports community is saddened, mourning the loss of the world's greatest and most recognized superstar. After nine unprecedented years with the Chicago Bulls of the National Basketball Association, Michael Jordan has announced his retirement. For me, as for millions of people everywhere, the news of Jordan's retirement hit like a 300-pound sledgehammer to the stomach. Even now, just thinking about Jordan's retirement is shocking, and at times, unbelievable. However, Michael Jordan has left the game the only way conceivable -- he was still the best player. Going from a student who didn't even make a high school team, Jordan has shown what the epitome of determination and greatness is. Although not the most naturally gifted player basketball has seen, Jordan has worked his way into sports history. He is a legend who, through hard work and desire, became the best at his profession -- not only during his time, but ever. After being cut from his high school team, Jordan was motivated to prove to the coaches, the students, and to himself, that he could be the best. Jordan has not only worked to be the best, but he is, and will forever be the greatest. No sport has ever had such a competitor as Jordan. He worked so hard it looked flawless and even effortless. Jordan has set the NBA's standards for domination and excitement. So, don't cry Chicago. The sun will shine again. Just realize that nobody will ever have another Michael Jordan . You had the best. Mike Nguyen is an OAC student at Gloucester High School. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MEMO Madonna, Jacko and any other would-be superstars: if you want to see a really class act, take a look at Michael Jordan . Arguably the greatest sportsman of all time, Jordan recently stunned the world by announcing his retirement after leading the Chicago Bulls to a third consecutive US basketball championship. Doing what few great athletes have been able to do, he called it quits at the peak of his career - and did it with dignity. Jordan is at least as big an international star as Madonna or Jacko, yet has managed to resist their egomaniacal trappings. Not for him are the bodyguards, the indulgence, the retreat into fantasy; Jordan lives a relatively normal life as husband and father. As Oprah Winfrey points out tonight in her exclusive interview special, Jordan is just your average person - except that he can "fly". Anyone with a passing interest in the man should not miss Michael Jordan and Oprah (7.30, Channel Ten).The world's highest-paid interviewer is at the peak of her form and Jordan is fascinating. When he made his shock retirement annoucement, Sports Illustrated summed up world opinion with a cover story simply headed: "Why?" Jordan explains honestly and surprisingly eloquently his reasons for getting out of basketball and dispels a long list of myths that Oprah throws his way. No, his decision had nothing to do with money; nor fitness; nor fear that his skills had waned; nor his father's murder; nor the controversy over gambling debts. It was just the right time, explains Jordan. He had done it all and didn't want to go out on any lesser note. The hour-long interview includes the inevitable footage of Jordan in action, just enough as not to intrude, plus often-amusing messages from friends. Oprah doesn't spare her obviously warm feelings for the champ by shirking any questions. In fact, some might consider her almost ruthless in dealing with the gambling issue and his father's death. But, like I say, Jordan handles it all with class. He floats through it as he floated on the basketball court. A true superstar. headline: KEEP YOUR EYES ON THE BOUNCING BALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 As the teen and kids markets come under increasing scrutiny as one of the most fickle to market to, advertisers have found a way to capture their attention and keep it - basketball . Basketball has become a marketing phenomenon and an increasing number of advertisers have latched on to basketball stars for product endorsements and sponsorships. In the US, basketball players dominate an unprecedented number of advertising opportunities available to athletes, according to the New York Times, and ads during the Super Bowl were littered by basketball stars. In Australia, Tip Top, McDonald's, Gatorade, Nike, Reebok, Pepsi and Rexona, to name a few have used basketball to promote their products in the past 12 months. PepsiCo Australia uses Shaquille O'Neal in its ads and marketing director Mr Chris O'Donohue said Shaq was the sport's "next big thing". "We're making an investment in the future, teens already know him. So does he move our brand dimension? Yes," he said. " Basketball is one hot property. It's unique in being top of mind amongst young adults and teens. It's high impact, exciting, skilful, shows strength, the life of the court, it's masculine, has athleticism and even the sexual connotations, it's just great and identifies with young people." It has seen a surge in popularity in Australia - attendance at National Basketball League matches has grown by 372 per cent in the past 10 years and brings in an average crowd of 5,463. In 1993, 36 per cent of the population watched basketball on television, 12 per cent played the sport and 11 per cent attended matches, according to a Brian Sweeney & Associates study, "Australians and Sport". In its 1992 Youth Monitor survey, AMR:Quantum found 45 per cent of males aged between 10 and 17 regularly played basketball . When US sports drink Gatorade was launched here in November, advertising used US basketball star Michael Jordan . After just four weeks of distribution, Gatorade claimed market leadership with a 32.7 per cent share of the national sports drink market. Gatorade managing director Mr Michael Whitcomb said the company has used Jordan as a spokesman since 1991 which has been a "huge success". " Michael Jordan playing basketball is a very aerobic thing to do, so it is a great situation to demonstrate the benefits of a product like Gatorade," he said. "In Australia we found him to be an asset, four out of five teens rate Michael Jordan as their top personality. Basketball is growing in popularity so leveraging our association with him is a smart thing to do." Tip Top Bakeries is the latest advertiser to use basketball as a sales tool. An on-pack promotion called " Basketball Bingo" features 180 Australian basketball collector cards from all NBL teams. Instant cash prizes and holidays are offered to collectors and a $2,000 sports grant will be awarded in each State. One of the country's largest youth marketers, Coca-Cola, uses basketball in a different way. It sponsors the Sydney Kings and several other teams and is the official soft drink at NBL games. " Basketball is very much a hot sport for the core target market for Coke,"Coca-Cola spokeswoman Ms Kathy Walsh said. "It's a sport that youth recognise as their own and belongs to the teen market as no other sport does." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Bulls 104, Cavaliers 96 Scottie Pippen scored 31 points and Scott Williams had 13 of his 21 points in the final quarter last night as the Chicago Bulls began their quest for a fourth straight National Basketball Association title by beating the visiting Cleveland Cavaliers, 104-96. Game 2 of the three-of-five-game playoff series will be held Sunday at Chicago Stadium. The Cavaliers played without the heart of their inside game as John (Hot Rod) Williams, Brad Daugherty and Larry Nance watched from the bench in street clothes because of injuries. Price Struggles And Mark Price, Cleveland's leading scorer who averaged 17.3 points during the regular season, managed just 9 points. Gerald Wilkins led Cleveland with 23 points. Pippen, who became the Bulls' leader when Michael Jordan retired and then took up baseball, led a third-quarter surge and then had two spectacular baskets in the fourth quarter. He also finished with 12 rebounds. His 31 points were 1 short of his career playoff high. Luc Longley's dunk, Pippen's assist to Horace Grant for a layup after a length-of-the-floor dribble and Pippen's 3-point play highlighted an 11-4 run in the third quarter as the Bulls opened an 8-point lead. 11 for Pippen Pippen finished the quarter with 11 points and Chicago led, 76-71, with 12 minutes to play. Cleveland closed within 4 points in the final period, but Williams scored 8 straight for Chicago and the Bulls had an 88-79 lead with 6 minutes 44 seconds left. Cleveland got within 5, but Pippen slammed home a miss by Williams and then made a steal and a double-clutch dunk for a 92-83 lead with 4:51 left. He added another dunk and a driving basket as the Bulls surged ahead by 10 with 2:29 to go. Houston 114, Portland 104 Vernon Maxwell broke out of a shooting drought to score 10 consecutive points late in the game last night, leading Houston to a 114-104 victory over visitng Portland in their opening N.B.A. playoff game. The Rockets had a 97-95 lead with 5:20 to play when Maxwell -- who scored 24 points and hit five 3-pointers -- started his charge. He hit consecutive 3-point baskets and then two jumpers, giving Houston a 107-98 lead with 2:39 to play. Maxwell hit his first four baskets of the game, but then missed six of his next seven before heating up again. Houston defeated the Trail Blazers in four consecutive games in the regular season, including a 119-110 victory on April 14 that clinched the Rockets' second straight Midwest Division title. The second game of the three-of-five-game series will be played tomorrow at the Summit. Clyde Drexler led the Blazers with 26 points, including 12 in the third period when Portland got back into the game. Houston led, 64-55, at the half, but the Trail Blazers outscored the Rockets, 13-6, to start the third period, closing to 70-68 with 7:31 to go in the period. Houston, which got 26 points from Hakeem Olajuwon, took a 90-83 advantage into the final quarter. Portland, which hadn't led since the opening basket, came back to lead, 91-90, on Porter's basket with 8:42 to play. Suns 111, Warriors 104 Phoenix won Game 1 at home as Charles Barkley scored 36 points with 19 rebounds and Kevin Johnson added 24 points. The Suns secured the victory early by outscoring Golden State, 38-23, in the first quarter. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 At Robert Townson High School, on the southern edge of Sydney suburbia, the future of Australian sport is taking shape - it just doesn't look that Australian. Amid the 50-odd students shooting basketball hoops in the school hall yesterday, hardly a single Australian sporting logo was visible among the Michael Jordan T-shirts, NFL Superbowl sweaters, Nike trackpants and Air Jordan shoes. In the rolling suburbs outside Campbelltown, black American athletes like Michael Jordan and Shaquille O'Neal are the sporting heroes and American basketball is infinitely more popular than its Australian counterpart. "It's American; it's just better," explained 15-year-old Tracy Antrobus, as if the reason were self-evident. "We live in Australia and they (local teams)are Australian. They're white; they're not black." With 27 basketball teams and only one rugby team, Robert Townson High is emblematic of the way Australian kids are abandoning the Anglophile sporting pursuits of their parents. Yet the local basketball team, the Sydney Kings, has little to do with this phenomenon, which is almost entirely inspired by the highly successful marketing campaign of the US National Basketball Association. Two surveys last year indicated that the NBA's marketing blitz had turned Michael Jordan into the biggest sporting hero to Australian teenagers, just one example of the way in which black Americans have become role models for white Australian teenagers. "It's not just here, I see it everywhere," said Martin Lyons, a history teacher who doubles as the coach of the few remaining rugby diehards at the school. "The Americans are masters at advertising and promotion - the McDonald's culture. It's not just the sport; it's the style and the hip-hop music that goes with it. It's like Jon English said: 'When your dad listened to Black Sabbath, how do you shock your parents?'" The latest answer to that question seems to be Shaquille O'Neal, the 21-year-old star of the Orlando Magic basketball team who has filled the gap left by Jordan's retirement last year. A seven-foot rapper, actor and athlete, the Shaq is the ultimate embodiment of black American "homeboy" street style. "I love the way he jams and rips down the rings," said Mark Gossayn, referring to the Shaq's habit of hoisting himself up on the basketball hoop and tearing it out of its supports. In basketball movies like Shaq's just-released Blue Chips, such stunts are combined with the frenetic beat of hip-hop music. Most students at Robert Townson say their favourite music is rhythm-and-blues, a catch-all term that includes rap and swing beat. "The guys all want to be homeys," said 16-year-old Vicki Wong, using the black street slang for "homeboy". "They've all got their try-hard clothes on today." Royce Pinto, a 15-year-old wearing black Air Jordan sneakers, Nike socks and Chicago Bulls T-shirt and cap - turned backwards, of course - was trying harder than most. Asked who his favourite Australian basketballer was, he had to scratch for a name. "Leon Trimmingham," he said finally, naming the Sydney Kings' West Indian recruit. SPECTRUM: US eh? headline: BASEBALL INTEREST STRONGER
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KEYWORD-HIT Baseball interest stronger FORMER Basketball superstar Michael Jordan is not rejoining the Chicago Bulls, and he is not giving up his dream of playing top level baseball. Jordan, responding to published gossip that had him leaving minor league baseball's Birmingham Barons for the National Basketball Association, told reporters in Birmingham, Alabama yesterday that he would stay with the Double-A club at least until the end of the season. And the Bulls? ""I don't see myself being back in the game ( basketball ) at any time in the near future,'' Jordan said. The near future? ""Okay, the far future,'' he said. ""Either way you want to put it. I just don't like to say never. ""I don't like to close doors, but if you want me to say it: okay never. I will never play basketball again, except recreationally.'' On leaving the Barons? ""There's no truth to it,'' he said. ""It's nothing but rumours. I'm not going to throw a curveball at the fans. I told them I would be here, and I'm here.'' He did admit talking some time ago to Birmingham manager Terry Francona and his staff about quitting, but they urged him to stay, Jordan said. ""Sometimes I'm a little down,'' he said. ""The last time was probably about a month ago. I asked them to evaluate my progress, and I told them I didn't want to be just taking somebody's spot on the roster. ""They told me I had made good progress and that the potential was there. They told me everybody in baseball goes through what I've been through,'' he said. ""I didn't realise that. It's encouraging,'' he said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NEW YORK: Retired Chicago Bulls basketball star Michael Jordan may soon be leaping on to the silver screen for a career in the movies. Jordan, pursuing his dream of breaking into major league baseball with the Chicago White Sox, is reportedly set to star in an animated film being secretly developed by Warner Brothers. The film, codenamed the "Hare Jordan" project, would team the former NBA superstar with animated Loony Tunes cartoon characters similar to those who lived in "Toontown" in the hit movie Who Framed Roger Rabbit, according to a report in the trade newspaper Variety. "The studio is working on Looney Tunes character animation for the film - if the footage comes together nicely, the studio will forge ahead for a major holiday or summer release," Variety reported yesterday. The inclusion of Jordan, the most popular American athlete in history, would offer the movie instant box-office appeal. But it would not be the first time "Air Jordan" has appeared alongside cartoon characters. In 1992 he starred with Bugs Bunny in a popular Nike advertising campaign, and last year Jordan and "Marvin the Martian" teamed up for a Nike advertisement aired during the Super Bowl. Despite his retirement before last season, Jordan has continuing commercial arrangements with Nike, McDonald's and Gatorade. The report claims talks are already under way to involve all three in cross-promotion with the new movie. Warner Brothers and Jordan's agent, David Falk, have declined to comment on the report. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Call it Air Nostalgia. That could be the name for a growing category of products aimed at those who hold dear their memories of Michael Jordan -the basketball player. For them, CBS/Fox Video, Nike and phone card marketer Leading Concepts have created products, available just in time for the holidays. Despite a baseball salary of only $1,200 a month, Forbes notes that Mr. Jordan was still the top-earning athlete for 1994, citing his roughly $30 million in endorsements. For those who couldn't get enough of Nike spots from Wieden & Kennedy, Portland, Ore., chronicling Mr. Jordan's escapades with Bugs Bunny, not to worry; Warner Bros. is developing a feature-length motion picture starring Looney Tunes characters that mixes live action with animation, a la Walt Disney's ''Who Framed Roger Rabbit?'' The film could be ready by next summer. Quaker Oats Co.'s Gatorade brand, McDonald's Corp. and Nike-all Jordan sponsors-are being eyed as promotional partners. Sports marketing executives say Falk Associates Management Enterprises, Washington, which handles Mr. Jordan's marketing, is exploring film projects and TV specials about his life. The Jordan nostalgia season started in October with the retirement of his Chicago Bulls number in a slick, star-studded ceremony that Turner Network Television televised nationally. That broadcast marked the introduction of a commemorative phone card marketed by Leading Concepts and Phame PhoneNet, a unit of Falk. NBA Entertainment, Secaucus, N.J., created print and TV ads with an 800-number to order the card. For $23, consumers receive 23 minutes of Air Time. The two companies are finalizing plans for a series of Jordan phone cards, as well as cards featuring other Falk clients like NBA stars Muggsy Bogues and Patrick Ewing. Nike just introduced its new Air Jordan sneaker that lists Mr. Jordan's NBA career milestones on the outsole. The shoe is also featured in radio and TV spots from Wieden in Nike's nine biggest basketball markets. On Nov. 2, CBS/Fox Video released ''The Michael Jordan Gift Pack,'' bundling two best-selling videos, '' Michael Jordan : Air Time'' and '' Michael Jordan : Come Fly With Me,'' with a booklet about Mr. Jordan's career. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 IT was the fall of Michael Jordan 's retirement, his temporary retirement, as it turns out. He and some pals were tooling around California, just having fun, when they came upon a basketball court. Jordan, who had been bored playing against Barkley and Ewing, decided he wanted to play a game of hoops. "How is that done?" a corporate type asked. Like, maybe you hand somebody your business card. Or send a fax. Jordan grunted and said, "You just stand at the edge of the court and say, 'Next!' " The locals noticed a group of strangers standing by the court. One of the new people had a shaven head and a very athletic build. The shortest of the locals upped to Michael Jordan and said, "I've been wanting a piece of you for five years" -- or words to that effect. They played. The result was not important. Later, Jordan reported that it was the most fun he had playing basketball in years. How nice, to be able to do something for fun. For love. Michael Jordan 's father was murdered, and Jordan was in mourning, and he was already bored with basketball . David Stern says there was no secret suspension for gambling, and I believe the N.B.A.'s commissioner. You can't hide something like that. Jordan pursued this dream of becoming a major league baseball player in his 30's. He gave it his best shot and discovered that his long body was not prepared to hit home runs. Then baseball went away. Michael Jordan had too much pride in being a professional athlete to contemplate being a replacement player. So he left that sordid business to find something interesting to do. He isn't exactly going to stay home and make peanut butter and jelly sandwiches for his kids. So today, Jordan goes back to his real playground -- with the terse Jordanian news release: "I'm back." He will tap Reggie Miller in the chest -- figuratively in public, maybe even literally. Now, Miller and anyone who loves basketball is fascinated by the comeback. How nice for CBS, having its little college tournament with Jordan's return on NBC. Jordan needs action, needs the rush. And fans need it, too. Ever since the rumors began a few weeks ago, people have been saying: "He should have stayed retired." "He'll be as good as ever." "What happens when he gets bored again?" "Maybe he's lost a step." Ninety percent of Michael Jordan is better than almost every basketball player who ever lived. Suppose he's short of lungs? He'll have to play in spurts. Suppose his shot isn't falling? He'll concentrate on defense, passing and rebounding. Suppose the Bulls are not as good as they used to be? He'll make them better. Most great athletes are extremely competitive, or you never would have heard of them. Jordan is at the top of the list. He yaps at his teammates in practice. He plays in a seething rage when taunted or beaten by outsiders like the Knicks' John Starks. He must win, on the level of Ty Cobb, Bill Russell, Lawrence Taylor, Bob Gibson and Mark Messier. No mercy. He will demand the best from players around him. You think Scottie Pippen would have sat out the last seconds against the Knicks last spring if Jordan had been in that huddle? Jordan would have picked Pippen up by the shirt and thrown him back on the court. Pippen will play better, starting today. There was a time when Jordan resented the money and attention being paid to Toni Kukoc, slender, reticent, young, Croatian. Jordan and Pippen trapped Kukoc in the 1992 Summer Olympics. He was the enemy -- not politically but personally. Since then, Jordan has watched the guy build up his muscles, hit the open man, make clutch shots. Jordan now knows he will be playing with the tallest, best guard since Magic Johnson. B. J. Armstrong and Steve Kerr become 331/3 better at high noon today. The committee of gawky centers is now entrusted with making this a safer world for Jordan -- not a bad calling for these bigfeet. Can he win a championship? The Knicks in May? The Orlando Magic in early June? Barkley or Robinson or Malone or Kemp or Olajuwon in mid-June? Who's going to beat him? Remember, he's doing it for fun, because he loves it, because he needs it. That makes Michael Jordan even tougher. Next! Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Photo: Michael Jordan makes his return today. headline: JUST CUE IT: NEW JORDANADS ARE READY TO STORM THE AIRWAVES
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Madison Avenue copy-writers are working overtime _ just ''like Mike'' did in his first game back Sunday. Jordan's return has inspired some telling, albeit rejected, creative genius. The new, if rejected, Michael Jordan ad for Nike: What if the media left me alone? What if I wasn't so im-press-ive? What if Marv Albert had real hair, and what if I really was good buddies with Ahmad Rashad? What if I could dominate basketball , do some golf-ambling, lament the murder of my father, quit basketball , hit .202 for the White Sox, quit baseball, return to basketball _and avoid Bob Costas? OK, MJ, it's press time. Perhaps it was overkill when Sports Illustrated made you a cover boy, whiffing a pitch by about 10 years. But I seem to remember the same periodical featuring you on the cover a few times. You're an easy out; you're a tough interview. Until you broke a two- week media embargo Sunday, we'd seen more of your cars on TV (usually speeding away) than your own mechanic. We've built you up and torn you down. That seems to be the catch phrase. We have seen the enemy, and her name is Hana Storm. And now we've gone and reported that Reggie Lewis failed a cocaine test while in college. How rude. You're right, you did not ask for this media crush. Who does? Your talent, status and behavior have begged for it. Run if you must. Ignoring the press is no crime, but neither is reporting all sides of a public figure. It's called credibility, something the Bulls now have as a title contender. The new, if rejected, Michael Jordan ad for Gatorade: Like Mike. If I could ski like Mike. I want to ski, I want to ski, I want to ski Even the most jaded sports observers found themselves humming that stupid Gatorade jingle Sunday, but if there's one thing we know, it is that Michael Jordan bores easily. Jordan does what he likes, when he likes. He can hit a home run in pro baseball. He can shoot par in golf. Maybe he'll try skiing. Michael and Picabo do Gstaad. If Jordan can excite such a stir as to boost the stock market, he can also knock the world on its ear if, say, he decides to become a steer wrestler next season. We are at his mercy, and it's a delightfully uneasy feeling. Michael is the King in this play; the rest of us are Mr. Tree. New, if rejected, Michael Jordan ad for McDonald's: Over the gambling charges, through the reported murder plots, off the Nothing Left to Prove thing, around the More Time With My Family thing, under the Need a New Challenge explanation, nothing but silliness. And we reported it all. We still might not know the real reason why Jordan first quit basketball , but chances are it's one or more of the above. Jordan has played the press like an OJ lawyer, and it's left us in a dizzy state of both confusion and bliss. Confusion, because we never know what he's going to do or say next. Bliss, because that's what makes him interesting. When Jordan left the game, it was for the best. Today, his return is for the best. Can you argue both points? You bet. When Jordan left, the league was predictable. His private problems were too much for one man to handle in public. His return is a fun wildcard for the league to play with, and apparently he has learned to keep the media at arm's length. New, if rejected, Michael Jordan ad for Haines underwear: Wait 'til I get my Haines on the Bulls. Wait 'til I brief my NBA brethren on my status as the best player in the game. Wait 'til I (cotton) mesh with my teammates and undress the competition.23 We may have to wait until next season for this, but now that Jordan has gone back to what he does best, it will be an enjoyable wait. Jordan couldn't get a Haindle on baseball, but saw the perfect opportunity to extricate himself as gracefully as anyone could have hoped. Who says the baseball strike has hurt everyone? As an impetus to push Jordan back to basketball , the strike has been invaluable. It could just be Donald Fehr's greatest accomplishment. Morfit writes a weekly column for the Post Register. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan seems to be getting the hang of this new basketball thing. There was that silky smooth jumper quietly rippling through the net. There was the slam dunk, a loud reminder of his ability to soar over all opponents. And there was that no-look pass to Scottie Pippen. There were, simply, signs that the Jordan who dominated basketball for so many years may not be far from dominating again. Three days after missing 21 of 28 shots in his return to the National Basketball Association after a 21-month absence, Jordan made his first six shots tonight and scored 27 points in 26 minutes as the Chicago Bulls cruised past the Boston Celtics, 124-107. "I was a little more relaxed; there wasn't as much fanfare as the first game," he said of the 103-96 overtime loss at Indiana. "Hitting my first jump shot was nice." Jordan was perfect on his first six shots, all jumpers, and had a role in a Bulls victory for the first time since June 20, 1993, the day they won their third straight N.B.A. title. He made 9 of 17 shots and all 8 of his foul shots. He had three rebounds, three assists and two steals. His most eye-catching pass came when he looked away and fed Pippen for a driving layup that made the score 53-39 with 4:29 left in the first half. "That typified my relationship with Scottie," Jordan said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Jenny McCrorie, 86, retired nurse, Bismarck "Well, if he's well enough to do it I don't see why he shouldn't. He used to play and then he retired. Now he's playing again, and I did see him on TV Sunday. He looked fine. It didn't even show that he even left, and he's still on his toes. As long as his health holds up he can play and then he can retire and let somebody else play. My husband used to love watching him because he was a ballplayer himself. But now he's gone so I have to watch Michael Jordan play all alone now." Renelle Hafner, 21, student, Bismarck "I resent anyone who comes out of semiretirement to work again, but I hope he gives it all he's got. All I know is he should stick to one sport and pick one or the other because you can't do both. I know the younger generation really looks up to him. I know that from friends' kids, who all have Michael Jordan posters and shirts and everything else. He's showing them he's not a quitter. I think he'll do OK for the team, and you have to give him some time to get back into the swing of things." Jenny Richardson, 16, student, Bismarck "I don't think it's fair, because he thinks he can just leave whenever he wants. I don't think it's fair to the other players and I don't think any player should be able to do that. When a person retires, they retire for good. I still like him and if it were any other player I don't think people would like it, but they aren't as well-known as Michael Jordan . The only reason they let him back to play is because he'll do good for the team as the season goes on and with practice he'll get better." Wayne Jundt, 23, supervisor, Bismarck "I think he should of stayed retired because he's not going to be as good as when he left. I don't feel he will be quite the legend he was because he's gotten rusty and it showed on Sunday. It was only his first game so it might be too early to tell. Before he was hot and couldn't miss. Then he stopped playing and went to baseball, so now he's too mixed up. It's kind of hard to say how his team will do, but they won't do the three in a row again. Now that he's back there's going to be more people watching basketball ." Dan Miller, 22, cook, Bismarck "He shouldn't of left basketball and he'll probably be a better player than he was before because he only has himself to beat. The Bulls do have a lot of work to do and after Jordan left they went into a slump. I like basketball in general and I like the Magic and Shaquille. But I like the Bulls, too. After Jordan left, it put Shaquille and Hakeem in the spotlight. Now that Jordan's back the spotlight's back on him. I also noticed he's not wearing 23 again so now he'll have to make number 45 famous." Paul Hedren, 45, National Park Service, Williston "It's just fine, and he's been a stellar player in basketball . He's a better basketball player than he was in baseball where he was struggling. It's better to go back where he's a star and can excell because a sport like baseball's going no place and he was going no place in baseball either. There's no other sport that has a Michael Jordan . The Twins have Kirby Puckett and they love him dearly, but Michael Jordan transcends that and he's bigger than any player on a team because he's Mr. Basketball ." headline: KIDS AIR VIEWS ON JORDAN'S RETURN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Last week we asked you what you thought about Michael Jordan 's return to the Chicago Bulls. Here's what you had to say: Sara, Madison: ''I think it's really great that he's gone back to the Chicago Bulls, because they weren't doing well this year. I think he really loves the sport and couldn't give it up.'' Ben, 12, Madison: ''I don't think Michael Jordan is very good.'' Brandon Christien, Mount Horeb: ''I think it's pretty cool that Michael Jordan is playing basketball again.'' Derek Stewart: ''I really don't think it's that important about Michael Jordan coming back to the NBA because for one he's just an all star.'' Erin, 11, Madison: ''I think it's really cool that he's returning to the Chicago Bulls because I know that he just got over a big tragedy, so now that he's coming back it's just super great, and I hope he scores big in his next game.'' Nicholas Pfeiffer, 6, Madison: ''I think it's great.'' Andrea Fetherston, Verona: ''I think he should have stuck with what he had in the first place. I don't know why he went to baseball and then to basketball again, he should have stuck with what he wanted to do, but other than that, cool.'' Sean Raleigh, Sun Prairie: ''I don't think he should have come back because now the Bulls will be dominating again and that's kind of bad.'' Jeff, Barneveld: ''I think he's still the best in the NBA.'' Kevin, 9, Madison: ''I'm glad he's back, but he needs to score more points.'' Tony Walton, Mazomanie: ''He's just sore because of the baseball strike.'' Ryan Wherley, Sun Prairie, 12: ''I think it's great for the whole NBA since the league has so many good young players who are asking for so much money and I think it will be a boost for the league.'' Jonny Templin: ''I think that Jordan doesn't quite have his magic back, but he should have it back by next season.'' Greg, 11, Madison: ''I like that he's coming back because I've been waiting for him to come back for a while and I'm going to watch him play every single day.'' Marty Johnson, 9, Edgerton: ''I think that Michael Jordan needs more practice and in time he'll get better and score 50 more points. His body wasn't physically ready.'' fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Excuse me, but haven't we seen you somewhere before? The face is familiar. The bald head, the big grin. The uniform rings a bell. And the way you move. We've seen that before. Yeah, it seems like we know you. Wait a minute. Didn't you used to be Michael Jordan ? SHELTON Nah. Couldn't be. I mean, no offense, but Michael Jordan was the best basketball player of all time. I hope you don't mind my saying it, buddy; you kind of stunk up the joint out there. Take the last few minutes. Everyone knows that was the time that belonged to Michael. Advertisers fought for the fourth-quarter spots, because they knew that was the time he was going to turn the game into a personal highlight reel. You? To tell the truth, you play basketball a lot like the rightfielder for the Birmingham Barons. Not last year. This year. I don't want to be insulting, buddy, but you got in the way of a pretty good basketball game. Without you, the Bulls win. With you, the Magic wins 94-91. It's as simple as that. Remember what happened with 1:49 to play? The Bulls trailed the Magic, 88-86, and you were at the free-throw line with two shots to tie. And you missed them both. It was then the rest of us knew you were one of those lookalikes from the agency. Remember what happened with 10 seconds to play? You had spun around Nick Anderson and were dribbling upcourt, and the Bulls had a 91-90 lead. Now, we all know what happens if Michael Jordan , the real one, has the ball. He gets fouled, and he hits two free throws. It's money in the bank, victory in the standings. Everybody knows that. You? You look over the wrong shoulder, and you let Anderson catch you, dive and slap the ball away, and Penny Hardaway feeds it to Horace Grant for the winning bucket. Trust me, they're going to love that at Nike. Remember what happens in the final seconds, Mr. I-Wanna-Be-Like-Mike? You get the ball, you drive the left side of the lane, a step ahead of the defender. Michael slams the ball down like an exclamation point there, guaranteed. But you throw some lame pass behind Scottie Pippen and the Bulls lose. Shoot, Michael would have known not to pass it to Pippen of all people, because Pippen was 2-for-11 from the field. Come to think of it, that might not have been Pippen, either. You're Michael Jordan ? Right. That's like saying some kid who can't color inside the lines with his crayon is Rembrandt. That's like saying a guy who scares cats at a karaoke bar is Pavarotti. You're Michael Jordan ? Of course you are. And the Mona Lisa has a mustache. Look, Michael owned the playoffs. You owned 8-for-22 shooting stats, along with eight turnovers. You not only weren't the best player on the floor, you weren't the best player in your matchup with Anderson. Hey, we saw you at the end of the first half. You stood there with your palms up, wondering what the play was. When did Michael ever look confused on a basketball court? Get real. You had the ball stolen by Hardaway and Grant and Anderson, who isn't exactly, well, Michael Jordan on defense. You had a shot blocked by Dennis Scott. Dennis Scott. Look, I'll grant you, there are times you look enough like him. When you drive to the basket, for instance. But even then, the real Michael slams down the ball with enough force to make opponents' backbones shiver. Now you do this soft little finger-roll deal. And you couldn't hit a jump shot if they tripled the width of the rim. Oh, you fooled them for a while. You had big games in New York and Cleveland, and the Charlotte Hornets bought into the whole deal. How did you do that? Mirrors? But still, Jordan showed up every night. You have been grand one night, bland the next. I heard what you said. How you took the personal responsibility for this loss. How it's difficult because you're such a competitor. That may be true. But you aren't Michael Jordan . Admit it. At least, you aren't the one we remember. Gee. I wonder what he's doing these days? fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The Michael Jordan who exited from the playoffs in the second round this past week was clearly not the Michael Jordan we once knew, the money player who not only took the big shot but so often rattled it in. Old Mike would pick you clean at half court. New Mike cheats back and tries to swat the ball away from behind. Old Mike had an idea of his purpose before he took flight. New Mike waits until you can slide a credit card under his sneakers before making up his mind. But New Mike should be liked for a couple of reasons: He has only played 27 games in two years and will not collect Social Security for another 34, which means he may have some basketball left in him. And, also, New Mike is more of a regular guy and not the deity we so want him to be. He commits turnovers in the clutch, he loses games and -- this is important -- God no longer has an identity crisis. The truth about Jordan's comeback: We needed him -- the National Basketball Association, Midwest pork farmers, jaded sports columnists and every child with a life-size poster of Dennis Rodman on his wall -- more than he needed us. He came back as a basketball player. We wanted a celestial being. He got what he wanted. When we did not, we felt compelled to compare him with Old Mike instead of appreciating the wondrous player New Mike still is and will continue to become. Each time Jordan faltered, a new expert was called in to sidle up to the studio host and explain His Airness's demise, as if -- that's it, the ozone layer just disappeared: "Michael is just a jump shooter now. His timing is off. He may not be able to get it back." I have no official proof, but I'm positive my waitress at Michael Jordan 's restaurant was a step slower this season. She doesn't have the same explosiveness bringing nachos to my table she had in 1993. The History Channel did a nice piece on Sitting Bull, but for real in-depth chronicling, you needed SportsChannel's nightly take on Jordan. The greater insanity took over when he left the court. There were 15-cameraman pileups, near-fistfights between media members jockeying for position. At least three people hit the ground hard following him to and from practice as reporters hung on his every word. Children cut class to watch him walk 25 yards from a gym to the team bus. Select company used to include Larry Bird and Magic Johnson. Suddenly, it was expanded -- to Moses and Gandhi. Jordan's security team, numbering more than a half-dozen, whisked him all over during the playoffs. Once after a practice in Charlotte, N.C., a member of the Bulls' public relations staff inquired as to their whereabouts by asking, "Where's Jesus and His Apostles?" Note the first sentence of a Florida newspaper article about Jordan's impact before Game 1 of the Orlando series: " Michael Jordan is a lot like God." The Almighty could not be immediately reached for comment, but then, he never had to pass to Luc Longley trailing by 1. "When I hear the Second Coming and things of that nature, it bothers me," Jordan said after Game 6, a loss that ended the Bulls' season on Thursday. "I came back to play basketball because I love this game. That's it. I would like to uphold the standards of professionalism and show how much I care about basketball , but I'm not a savior." So Michael Miracle can sometimes be Michael Mediocre, too, and considering where the Jordan legend was headed, this is a good thing. He is a basketball player who happens to play the game so splendidly and gracefully we cannot let him get off that easily. With other athletes driving their sports cars into trees stone drunk, showing contempt for fans or just seeming not to care, we wanted more from Michael . Jordan must shoulder some of the blame, scripting his entire comeback as only Frank Capra could. Jordanmania was capsulized by his number change in Game 2, after he first said he would not wear his original 23 jersey because it was the last number his father saw him play in before his death in 1993. The high point was Jordan leaving No. 45 on the hanger and leading the Bulls to victory over the Magic. The nadir hit soon after, as the fines were levied and memorabilia collectors emptied the shelves of sporting goods stores for those precious and few No. 45s. On one hand, watching Jordan carve up Orlando in his old number was transcendental, worthy of at least one bad Superman cliche. But, too, it illustrated the hysteria behind his legend. "But Phil, what about all those parents who bought their kids No. 45 jerseys?" someone with a straight face asked Bulls Coach Phil Jackson last week. "What do you tell them after they spent all their money on that?" Jackson paused before answering. "Buy them books," he said. headline: IT'S ALL A BALL, BUGS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KEYWORD-HIT It'll be a ball, Bugs BASKETBALL superstar Michael Jordan will start shooting his first feature-length movie next month _ alongside cartoon characters Bugs Bunny and Daffy Duck. Warner Brothers announced yesterday that Jordan would play himself in the live action-animated feature Space Jam, which begins production next month and is scheduled to hit the screens late next year. QNP""My kids really wanted me to do it," the Chicago Bulls legend told a news conference in New York. ""If I can't play myself, I can't play no one else." He said Bugs Bunny was his favourite cartoon character. ""I don't mind playing second fiddle to him at all. Or fourth of fifth fiddle." Jordan, 32, returned to the National Basketball Association this year after an 18-month retirement, which included a stint as a minor league baseball player. It is understood his lacklustre baseball career is written into the script. He has acted opposite animated characters in ""Hare Jordan" commercials for Nike and in a Loony Tunes promotion with McDonald's. Jordan's off-court earnings from advertising contracts total more than $30 million a year. Yesterday he revealed he planned to continue with basketball for at least three more years. JORDAN and fellow stars . . . "I don't mind playing second fiddle.' headline: SELLS STOCK
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Basketball superstar Michael Jordan won't be the only investor in the newly formed golf company that bears his name. Michael Jordan Golf Co. of Winnetka, Ill., is raising some $ 3 million through a private placement of preferred stock, according to a Form D released this week by the Securities and Exchange Commission. The golf venture, which plans to open practice centers in the United States and abroad, has attracted money from about 30 investors so far. That includes 22 residents of Illinois who bought some $ 1.67 million of preferred stock. "We have had a very good response so far," said Charles Reeves, the president and chief executive of the golf company. "I think there is always interest in anything Michael does." Jordan, a 32-year-old guard with the National Basketball Association's Chicago Bulls, could have financed the entire venture himself. In addition to the reported $ 4 million salary he received from the Bulls last year, Jordan is well-paid to sponsor products such as Nike sneakers, Hanes underwear and GatorAde. However, Reeves and Steven McLennan, the former director of golf at Pebble Beach, planned to seek outside investors when they pitched the concept of a golf practice center that would make the sport more accessible to all people, particularly kids. Reeves declined to disclose who bought the company's preferred stock. The first center is slated to open in the Chicago suburb of Aurora this summer. The 31-acre site will include a driving range, classrooms, miniature golf, pro shops with items bearing the Jordan logo, and a putting green. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Opening arguments began today in the trial of a man accused of killing the father of the basketball star Michael Jordan . District Attorney Johnson Britt told jurors that he would show that the defendant, Daniel Andre Green, had fired the fatal gunshots. But a defense lawyer, Angus Thompson, insisted that Mr. Green was not even present when Mr. Jordan's father, James, was killed as he napped in his car. Mr. Britt said that the main prosecution witness, Larry Martin Demery, would testify that he was present when Mr. Jordan was killed. Mr. Demery, 20, has pleaded guilty to first-degree murder in the case. "Daniel Green, with Larry Demery standing beside him, pointed the gun inside the car," Mr. Britt told the jury. "The driver of the car woke up and made some statements. Daniel Green pulled the trigger and shot the driver of the car." But Angus Thompson, Mr. Green's lawyer, said in his opening statement that his client was not present when Mr. Jordan, 57, was killed along a highway in Lumberton in 1993. He said Mr. Demery had asked Mr. Green to help dispose of the body. Mr. Britt said that after going through Mr. Jordan's possessions "Daniel Green told Larry Demery, 'I believe we've killed Michael Jordan 's daddy.' " Judge Gregory Weeks ruled on Tuesday that jurors could not hear a videotape of Mr. Green giving a rap performance about the killing. But the judge ruled that the jury might be allowed to watch the home video, in which Mr. Green can be seen wearing two National Basketball Association rings, without the sound.
