fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 For the fourth year in a row, NBA star Michael Jordan is listed as the highest-paid athlete. Each year, Forbes business magazine ranks professional athletes by how much money they earned for the year. It's called the Super 40 list. Jordan slam-dunked all other contenders by earning $ 43.9 million in 1995. Heavyweight boxer Mike Tyson finished second with $ 40 million. Jordan, who resumed his basketball career in March with the Chicago Bulls, is one of seven basketball players to make Forbes' Super 40 list. Other basketball players on the roster: Shaquille O'Neal ($ 21.9 million), David Robinson ($ 9. 6 million), Patrick Ewing ($ 9.5 million), Grant Hill ($ 7.8 million), Hakeem Olajuwon ($ 7.8 million) and Charles Barkley ($ 7.1 million). Tyson is one of five boxers on the latest Super 40 list. There also are seven football players and seven baseball players on the list. Hockey has two athletes, tennis has five, golf has three and auto racing four. Atlanta can claim two Super 40 athletes. Boxing champ Evander Holyfield ranks 14th with $ 13 million in earnings. Greg Maddux, who helped pitch the Braves to the '95 World Series championship, earned $ 7.2 million for the year. That put him in 31st place on the list. The minimum dollar amount made by any athlete on the Super 40 list: $ 6.2 million. Total earnings were calculated by adding up an athlete's salary, winnings and endorsements. An endorsement is when someone is paid to lend his or her name and reputation to help promote or sell a product. Most of Jordan's earnings came from endorsements. Some of the products he was paid to endorse: Nike, Gatorade, McDonald's and Wilson. Tyson is the only athlete on the list who earned all of his money from competition and none from endorsements. Tennis star Steffi Graf, the only woman on the Super 40 list, made $ 7.5 million. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Chart: TOP 10 in '95 #1 Michael Jordan , 32, basketball , $ 43.9 million #2 Mike Tyson, 29, boxing, $ 40 million #3 Deion Sanders, 28, football and, in 1995, baseball, $ 22.5 million #4 Riddick Bowe, 28, boxing, $ 22.2 million #5 Shaquille O'Neal, 23, basketball , $ 21.9 million #6 George Foreman, 46, boxing, $ 18 million #7 Andre Agassi, 25, tennis, $ 16 million #9 Michael Schumacher, 26, auto racing, $ 15 million #10 Wayne Gretzky, 34, ice hockey, $ 14.5 million Chart: Yo, basketball fans! Got a question about basketball ? A terrific new book --"The Kids' World Almanac of Basketball " --probably has your answer. It's available at bookstores for $ 7.95 (paperback) or $ 16.95 (hardcover). Among the many things in the book: A complete history of the game (starting when goals were made by nailing a pe ach basket to a wall up to the present explosion in the popularity of women's basketball ). Both college and National Basketball Association (NBA) records and statistics. Profiles of the best men and women players. Information on the best coaches and their records. Lively accounts of basketball 's greatest moments. There's even a chapter to help kids improve their own basketball skills. --David Davidson Photo: Boxer Mike Tyson / Allsport Photo: Andre Agassi./ Associated Press Photo: mug of Deion Sanders./ Associated Press Photo: mug of Jack Nicklaus./ WILLIAM BERRY / Staff Photo: Michael Jordan ./ Associated Press Photo: Shaquille O'Neal./ Associated Press Classification_95 Classification_95 Classification fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Language: ENGLISH Subject: BASKETBALL (92%); BOXING (90%); PROFESSIONAL SPORTS (90%); SPORTS & RECREATION (90%); SPORTS & RECREATION EVENTS (90%); SPORTS CONTRACTS & SALARIES (90%); COMPANY EARNINGS (89%); WAGES & SALARIES (89%); BASEBALL (79%); TENNIS (79%); CELEBRITIES (78%); BASEBALL TOURNAMENTS (77%); AUTO RACING (66%) Company: NIKE INC (53%); NIKE INC (53%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (93%); CHICAGO BULLS (57%); ATLANTA BRAVES (55%); NIKE INC (53%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (93%); CHICAGO BULLS (57%); ATLANTA BRAVES (55%) Organization: NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (93%); NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (93%); CHICAGO BULLS (57%); ATLANTA BRAVES (55%); ATLANTA BRAVES (55%) Ticker: NKE (NYSE) (53%) Industry: NAICS316219 OTHER FOOTWEAR MANUFACTURING (53%); NAICS316211 RUBBER AND PLASTICS FOOTWEAR MANUFACTURING (53%); NAICS315239 WOMEN'S AND GIRLS' CUT AND SEW OTHER OUTERWEAR MANUFACTURING (53%); NAICS315228 MEN'S AND BOYS' CUT AND SEW OTHER OUTERWEAR MANUFACTURING (53%); SIC3149 FOOTWEAR, EXCEPT RUBBER, NEC (53%); SIC3021 RUBBER & PLASTICS FOOTWEAR (53%); SIC2339 WOMEN'S, MISSES', & JUNIORS' OUTERWEAR, NEC (53%); SIC2329 MEN'S & BOYS' CLOTHING, NEC (53%); CELEBRITIES (78%); PRODUCT ENDORSEMENTS (63%) Person: MICHAEL JORDAN (79%); SHAQUILLE O'NEAL (58%) Geographic: ATLANTA, GA, USA (73%); CHICAGO, IL, USA (57%) Load-Date: July

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PLAYGROUND sporting battles are likely to heat up this month with the beginning of a southside schools basketball competition at Carina. A primary school competition will run on Friday afternoons and a high school league on Wednesday afternoons at the newly-refurbished Clem Jones Stadium in Zahel St at Carina. Players from the Southern Districts Spartans and the Brisbane Blazers will hold clinics for more than 150 students during the first three weeks of the high school league before official games start on the fourth week. Games will be held between 1-3pm with teams from Christian Outreach College, Clairvaux McKillop College and Springwood, Rochedale, Holland Park, Cavendish Road, Mansfield, Camp Hill and MacGregor high schools. Details on the competing teams in the primary school competitions are still being finalised with the games running from 1.30-3pm. Southern Districts junior development officer Eric Bailey said the school competitions were a great way for students to get a fun introduction to the game. The former National Basketball League player said the clinics were designed to ease children into the game by arming them with some basic skills. "After this school competition, if they like the sport, they can join teams and participate in the more structured competition," Bailey said. A Spartans player missed out on a chance to play against the legendary Michael Jordan . Joe Hurst, with Bullets star Mike Mitchell and Gold Coast players Wayne Smith and Bett Johnstone, represented Queensland in the natio nal finals of the Gatorade three-on-three cham pionships in Sydney last week. The winning team at the nationals won a trip to the United States to take on Michael Jordan and his friends. Hurst's team made it to the quarter finals before losing to Western Australia by three points. Hurst said they had beaten WA earlier in the day and could have forced a shoot out if they had dropped a three-pointer on the buzzer. The team played seven 14-minute games throughout the day. "Every game was winnable," Hurst said. "I was a bit disappointed; we'll probably have another go next year, take it a little more serious." The New South Wales team, made up of former National Basketball League stars Dwayne McClain, Jerry Everett and Norman Taylor, will play against Michael Jordan . THE Southern Districts Basketball Association is offering expert coaching to young players, clubs or schools. Anyone needing coaching assistance now has access to advice from experienced players on the association's National Women's Basketball League team the Blazers, as well as their Continental Basketball Association Northern Conference Championship winners the men's and women's Spartans. Blazer's coach Bronwyn Marshall is a former Olympian and two of the side's stars Sandy Brondello and Jenny Whittle are expected to be in the Australian team for Atlanta Olympics later this year. The Blazers are keen to provide coaching assistance, particularly to any girls' teams. Former National Basketball League Most Valuable Player Joe Hurst and American forward Bernard Williams will also be on hand this season to provide coaching and run clinics. Phone Spartans on 3398 2790. THE Southern Districts Basketball Association has unveiled its new $ 40,000 renovations at its home at Carina and announced plans to spend another $ 10,000 on seating. The upgrading of the Clem Jones Stadium was completed quickly in preparation for the arrival of the National Women's Basketball League team the Brisbane Blazers. The extra seating is expected to be completed before the start of the NWBL and Continental Basketball Association seasons. The association is also formulating plans for a proposed further $ 200,000 upgrade later this year which will include a new administration block and changeroom facilities. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 A former convict was found guilty of murder today for shooting Michael Jordan 's father during a holdup as he awoke from a nap along a dark highway in his $40,000 luxury car. The defendant, Daniel Green, 21, could be sentenced to death in the 1993 slaying of James R. Jordan, 57. According to testimony, Green and his accomplice, Larry Martin Demery, 20, drove around in Jordan's red Lexus for four days after the slaying, picking up dates, and used the dead man's cellular phone to call friends and relatives. Green also made a video of himself dancing and rapping while wearing Jordan's jewelry, a watch and two National Basketball Association rings given to him by his son. Demery himself was the prosecution's star witness. He pleaded guilty to murder before testifying against Green and could get the death penalty. Green did not testify. Michael Jordan never appeared at the trial. A brother of the Chicago Bulls' superstar took the stand only long enough to identify some of their father's belongings. The jury deliberated for four and a half hours over two days before returning guilty verdicts on all charges related to the July 23, 1993 attack: first-degree murder, first-degree robbery and conspiracy. The jury will return Monday to consider whether Green's sentence should be life in prison or the death penalty. There was no immediate comment from Michael Jordan . At a Bulls practice just before the verdict came in, he said: "It's not going to be hard on me. It's going to be hard on my family." James Jordan, who lived in the Charlotte, N.C., area, had been driving home from Wilmington, Del., and had pulled over in his car -- a gift from Michael Jordan -- to take a nap in the middle of the night. Demery said he and Green -- who had been released from prison after serving two and a half years for assault -- were preparing to rob a motel, when the expensive car caught their attention and they changed their target. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Their names are a handful. Bradman the cricketer, Pele the master footballer, Jack Nicklaus the nearest thing yet to a perfect golfer, perhaps Muhammad Ali in his prime - all are performers who for a spell have not so much dominated, as transcended their chosen sport. In this list of legend must now be inscribed the name of Michael Jordan . For the casual watcher, like myself, basketball even at its highest levels can be strangely unsatisfying. The athleticism is unarguable, but scoring is too easy, as many as 100 times in a 60-minute game. The players seem to grow taller every season, apparently bred for no other purpose than to lob a round orange ball through a netted hoop fixed 10 feet above the ground. Reduced to their barest functions, most sports are faintly absurd - how is it that half the male population of the US is obsessed by whacking a small white ball with a piece of metal across otherwise attractive tracts of countryside, before attempting to roll it into a tiny hole? But basketball is more absurd than most. That is, until you witness the Chicago Bulls and Michael Jordan . This year's Bulls may be the greatest NBA team ever, basketball 's equivalent of the 1970 World Cup-winning Brazilians, or the pulverizing West Indian cricket teams of recent memory. In the regular season they won a phenomenal 72 of 82 games. Screaming Lord Sutch has as good a chance of becoming prime minister as has another team of denying the Bulls the 1996 NBA championship. And in a team of gods, Michael Jordan is Zeus. This has been his annus mirabilis; a record eighth year as NBA top scorer, a fourth Most Valuable Player Award, a season imprinted with the image of Jordan - leaping, twisting in midair, swooping, soaring, sometimes all of the above at once. Above all, he can raise his game to whatever level the moment requires. Take the second playoff game this week against the Orlando Magic, the Bulls' closest challengers in terms of ability. The first had been a 121-83 Bulls blow-out, basketball 's version of a 6-1 victory at football. The second was another story. Orlando led by 18 points at one stage in the second half - at which point Jordan had seen enough. From then on he scored at will, and the Bulls emerged winners by 93 to 88. Now he is demanding a $ 36m (pounds 24m) two-year contract to stay in Chicago, and such is the infatuation of the Windy City with Jordan that he almost certainly will get it. Indeed Jordan's colossal commercial pulling power means that even at $ 18m a year, he is a steal. Yet money is hardly the issue for Jordan. How many sportsmen voluntarily climb down from Olympus to the mudswamp? Michael Jordan did, in 1994, forswearing the Bulls for an apprentice's job in minor league baseball, on a farm team for the Chicago White Sox. I went to see him once, playing outfield for the Birmingham Barons one steamy mid-summer night in Alabama. The place was packed, of course, with every eye on the loping figure wearing No 42. But the spectacle was dreadful to behold. In the field he dropped a couple of easy fly balls and muffed a simple relay throw. At bat, he flailed in vain. That Jordan has come back to basketball a more human and appealing figure should not surprise. A sport in which even the best hitters fail seven times out of 10 has a way of teaching humility. In retrospect the decision, astounding at the time, was utterly explicable. Filial guilt undoubtedly played a part. James Jordan, whose ambition always was that his athletic genius of a son should play major league baseball, had been murdered the previous July. Burdened with personal tragedy, hounded by pseudo-scandals, winner of three NBA championships already and three times voted its most valuable player, Michael Jordan was also bored of basketball . Thus the flight of baseball fancy. He has returned mentally recharged, and a better player than ever. A fraction slower, some say, but wiser and tactically more astute and, when necessary - just like Bradman, Pele, or Nicklaus - still capable of lifting his game to a plateau of sustained excellence no other can reach. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Their names are a handful. Bradman the cricketer, Pele the master footballer, Jack Nicklaus the nearest thing yet to a perfect golfer, perhaps Muhammad Ali in his prime - all are performers who for a spell have not so much dominated, as transcended their chosen sport. In this list of legend must now be inscribed the name of Michael Jordan . For the casual watcher, like myself, basketball even at its highest levels can be strangely unsatisfying. The athleticism is unarguable, but scoring is too easy, as many as 100 times in a 60-minute game. The players seem to grow taller every season, apparently bred for no other purpose than to lob a round orange ball through a netted hoop fixed 10 feet above the ground. Reduced to their barest functions, most sports are faintly absurd - how is it that half the male population of the US is obsessed by whacking a small white ball with a piece of metal across otherwise attractive tracts of countryside, before attempting to roll it into a tiny hole? But basketball is more absurd than most. That is, until you witness the Chicago Bulls and Michael Jordan . This year's Bulls may be the greatest NBA team ever, basketball 's equivalent of the 1970 World Cup-winning Brazilians, or the pulverizing West Indian cricket teams of recent memory. In the regular season they won a phenomenal 72 of 82 games. Screaming Lord Sutch has as good a chance of becoming prime minister as has another team of denying the Bulls the 1996 NBA championship. And in a team of gods, Michael Jordan is Zeus. This has been his annus mirabilis; a record eighth year as NBA top scorer, a fourth Most Valuable Player Award, a season imprinted with the image of Jordan - leaping, twisting in midair, swooping, soaring, sometimes all of the above at once. Above all, he can raise his game to whatever level the moment requires. Take the second playoff game this week against the Orlando Magic, the Bulls' closest challengers in terms of ability. The first had been a 121-83 Bulls blow-out, basketball 's version of a 6-1 victory at football. The second was another story. Orlando led by 18 points at one stage in the second half - at which point Jordan had seen enough. From then on he scored at will, and the Bulls emerged winners by 93 to 88. Now he is demanding a $ 36m (pounds 24m) two-year contract to stay in Chicago, and such is the infatuation of the Windy City with Jordan that he almost certainly will get it. Indeed Jordan's colossal commercial pulling power means that even at $ 18m a year, he is a steal. Yet money is hardly the issue for Jordan. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Baggy shorts. The shaved head. The wagging tongue. The smile. Michael Jordan 's mannerisms have become almost as much a part of pop culture as Elvis' sneer. As sports columnists debate the champion Chicago Bulls' standing in NBA history, pop culture gurus can measure Jordan, 33, against such timeless icons as Elvis, Babe Ruth, James Dean and Marilyn Monroe. "There's no question Jordan is a pop icon," says Jack Christ, professor of pop culture at Ripon (Wis.) College. "I think he is comparable to the Beatles and Elvis." Fans "worship Michael Jordan with much the same intensity as they worship religious figures," says Radford University sports sociologist Steve Lerch. Pop icons are annointed for what they represent. John Wayne stands for patriotism, and "Marilyn Monroe is both sex goddess and earth mother," says Irv Rein, professor of communication studies at Northwestern University. Elvis and the Beatles represented youths rebelling against their elders. Lerch calls Jordan "the perfect embodiment" of more conservative values: competitiveness, hard work and discipline. The icon mantle doesn't fit Michael Jackson and Madonna, says Douglas Brinkley, author of The Majic Bus , 1993's Americana travelogue, in which he called Jordan "a black Elvis with wings." "They don't capture the entire country in the way that Elvis and Jordan do," he says. Says Christ, "It's no accident that Madonna calls herself 'Madonna' and hit big with a song about being 'like a virgin' . . . She has consciously tried to build a career out of popular iconography. It has worked to some extent: She is rich and famous." But if icons must act as role models, he says, "then Madonna is no icon at all or a dysfunctional icon." Getting paid millions for movies are Jim Carrey, Arnold Schwarzenegger and Tom Hanks (who tops the marketing industry's Q rating for likeability and recognizability). But they aren't role models like Jordan, whose rating compares to Hanks' and is No. 1 among athletes. Jordan's charisma, character and aura rival those of Bill Cosby, Robin Williams and Magic Johnson, says Henry Schafer of Marketing Evaluations, the Manhattan firm that figures Q ratings. "He's bigger than just a basketball star. He transcends all groups." Jordan also transcends race. Like John F. Kennedy and Martin Luther King Jr., "Jordan to me is an icon of reconciliation and good will every which way," Christ says. "Everybody likes him and nobody seems to notice any racial implications to anything he does." Unlike Ali at the height of his career, Jordan is non-threatening to the masses. Unlike Madonna, his character is beyond reproach (few fans even cared when the media made a fuss about his gambling on golf.) Media coverage has added to his legend, but Jordan "has worked hard to get where he is," Lerch says. Comparing personalities from different eras is subjective. "What would the Beatles or Elvis have been like if there had been MTV at the peak of their popularity?" Lerch ponders. "While Babe Ruth had endorsements, the limitations of the electronic media of his day made him much less visible than Jordan." And not everyone thinks Jordan's icon status is a slam dunk. Pop icons themselves have become a phenomenon as marketers use them to sell products, says Mark Gottdiener, chairman of the sociology department at the University of Buffalo and author of The Theming of America (due this fall.) "Recently I was in Chicago visiting friends. They suggested we go to Michael Jordan 's Restaurant. I asked what kind of food do they serve. That wasn't what was important. That's typical of the stature of a pop icon," Gottdiener says. "But I don't see him as being in the same league as Elvis." Fan Bob Allison, who maintains World Wide Web sites devoted to Jordan and the Beatles, agrees: Jordan "makes the league better by his presence, but he doesn't change the world. The Beatles' music touched everybody . . . It will be played long after (Jordan) retires to the history books. He will be a memory like Babe Ruth. The Beatles will remain in our ears." That's probably fine with Jordan, who recently told reporters he isn't "comfortable with the myth stage of my life. . . . It's every day under a microscope. Back in Babe Ruth's day, it wasn't like that." Even so, Jordan adds to the legend. At the end of Sunday night's title-clinching game, when he collapsed on the floor, hugging the basketball and sobbing, "The game turned from a simple victory, a city and basketball to a moral drama," says Rein. "The story line is redemption. He was redeeming his father (plus) he was redeemed. It made Ben Hur seem like small potatoes." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Eleven-year-old Kimberly Callair, a resident of Augusta and student at Oglethorpe County Middle School, has been chosen Georgia's recipient of the Michael Jordan Education Club award and will meet the basketball star at a ceremony next weekend in Chicago. Selected from fifth-and sixth-grade applicants across the state, Kimberly was judged on classroom performance in homework, attendance and behavior, as well as community service, for which she tutored a mentally handicapped student in reading. "The one-on-one tutoring done by Kimberly really boosted her self- esteem and confidence, as well as improved her own reading level," said Jeannelle Carlisle, Kimberly's sixth-grade reading teacher. Jordan Club applicants are required to write a one-page essay on overcoming an obstacle in life. For Kimberly, this meant a lifelong battle with a severe stutter. Kimberly, with 49 other award recipients - one from each state - will spend three days in Chicago. Their agenda includes a trip to the Chicago Aquarium, lunch at Michael Jordan 's restaurant and a brunch- awards ceremony and photo shoot with the basketball star. The Michael Jordan Education Club was established in 1989, introducing an awards program in Chicago in 1993. It became national in 1994. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 It's a toss-up as to which of the two stars has been winning the biggest applause from preview audiences -- Michael Jordan or Bugs Bunny. There's also debate over which scene in Space Jam triggers the loudest laughter. One prime candidate: the moment when the irrepressible Wabbit gives the startled basketball great a big soppy kiss. Neither of the two are the normal kind of Hollywood celebrity, but Space Jam, which zooms onto more than 2,000 North American screens Nov. 15, isn't your normal brand of Hollywood film. It's a $100 million Warner Brothers high-tech assault on the family-entertainment business long claimed by Disney. Southam Newspapers And Warner's big weapons are a revered basketball star and its own priceless Looney Tunes cartoon franchise. The film's big climax is a basketball game in which Jordan trades passes with such renowned members of the Warner animation stable as Bugs Bunny, Daffy Duck, Elmer Fudd, Sylvester the Cat and The Roadrunner. For Canadian filmmaker Ivan Reitman, the chance to preside over this zany blend of live action and animation was too tempting to refuse. But he admits some colleagues tried to warn him off the project, especially fellow director Robert Zemeckis. Roger Rabbit Zemeckis was at the helm of the 1988 Who Framed Roger Rabbit, which also paired off real people with cartoon creations, so Reitman phoned him for advice. "Bob, can you tell me what sort of trouble you went through when you made Roger Rabbit?" Reitman asked. "Is it something I should be doing?" "Don't touch it!" Zemeckis exclaimed. "It's the worst thing you could ever do. There's so much work you won't believe it!' But Reitman wasn't really listening, "so of course I went ahead and did it, and Bob was absolutely right!" Reitman has made his mark as the director of such big hits as Ghostbusters, Twins and Meatballs and also as the producer of Beethoven and National Lampoon's Animal House. In the case of Space Jam, he only has producer credits -- TV commercial specialist Joe Pytka is the listed director -- but Reitman says duties on this film went far beyond the normal producing function. "I was responsible for script and casting and supervising post-production and the voices for the animated characters. It was pretty well a full-time job." The idea for Space Jam emerged from the popular series of Nike commercials featuring Michael Jordan and Bugs Bunny. Reitman happened to be hitching a lift one day on a Warner Brothers aircraft and found himself sharing the flight with a studio executive. The executive said the pairing of Bugs and Jordan had proved so popular in the commercials that Warner wanted to take the partnership further. TEAMED Reitman was asked if he thought the two could be successfully teamed in a full-length feature and if the other characters from the studio's beloved Looney Tunes cartoons could also be incorporated into the action. He was hooked. Reitman worked with four writers to concoct a script in which Bugs and his Looney Tunes pals are in danger of being kidnapped by an obnoxious gang of midget space creatures called the Nerdlucks. Their megalomaniac leader, Swackhammer (the voice of Danny DeVito), wants Bugs, Daffy and the others to be feature attractions at his failing theme park back on the Nerdluck planet. Bugs challenges the puny aliens to a basketball game: if the Tunes win, they can stay on Planet Earth; if they lose, it's off to Swackhammer's planet. But hopes of Tune victory are dashed when the puny Nerdlucks reveal an awesome ability to absorb new skills and images. They absorb the hoop talents of major NBA stars like Charles Barkley and Larry Johnson and turn themselves into powerful new adversaries known as Monstars. Defeat for Bugs and his team seems certain until they recruit NBA great Michael Jordan for their side. Space Jam is arriving at a time when Warner has not only started making a major pitch for the family trade but has also launched a major new animation division. So Reitman stresses the project is a big gamble for the venerable studio. "They had to leap off a very tall bridge in terms of going ahead with this," Reitman says. "All animated films are by definition labour intensive and are therefore very expensive. At least half of this is a traditional animation film as good as they come, which involved hundreds and hundreds of people drawing away. But it's also a digital movie making heavy use of computer graphics -- AND it mixes traditional live action with animation. "Most important of all, this involved the Looney Tunes, one of the studio's cornerstone brand names. Understandably Warners wanted to make sure that if they were going to appear in something like this, it had to be good." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 for mild language. 1 hour, 27 minutes. At metro theaters. Ratings range from one to four stars. THE VERDICT "Space" is the place ---for kids and commercial pitchmen. 'Air,' hare make a perfect pair You may, for now, have to go to a movie theater to see it. You may find it advertised and critiqued in the movie section of your newspaper. You'll probably see it debut at No. 1 on the weekend box office chart when figures are released Monday. But "Space Jam," the new semi-animated extravaganza starring basketball great Michael Jordan and a plethora of Looney Tunes characters, isn't a movie. It is, gleefully and unabashedly, an 87-minute commercial. Understand that going in, and you may be charmed by this slick, TV-bright vehicle's hard sell. The negligible plot goes like this: Space aliens invade Looney Tunes Land (located at the center of the Earth, the entranceway marked by a huge Warner Bros. logo) and kidnap its famous denizens to serve as attractions at an interstellar amusement park. Head 'toon Bugs Bunny and his pals (including Daffy Duck, his backside also branded with the Warner label) scheme to win their freedom by challenging the diminutive spacelings to a basketball game. But when the aliens steal the talent from such National Basketball Association hotshots as Charles Barkley and Patrick Ewing (and store it in a Spalding basketball , which glows with magic!), the Looney Tunes players decide they need some help. They enlist the services of Jordan --- in his Air Jordans, of course ---to make the competition a little more equal. It should come as no surprise to find out that "Space Jam" director Joe Pytka made his mark in TV commercials, including Jordan's "Nothing but Net" ad for McDonald's. Pytka and the movie's four (count 'em!) scriptwriters manage to work in mentions of every product Jordan endorses (to their credit, many of the plugs are delivered with a wink). But the most prominent product placement is the sports giant himself. Loving, slow-motion shots of Jordan sailing into a layup, accompanied by soaring theme music and the screams of adoring fans, remind one of the self- promoting excesses of another famous Michael ---Jackson. But between the pitches for Gatorade and the beatific shots of Jordan, Bugs and company work their classic magic. Almost every Looney Tunes character, from Tweety and Sylvester to Foghorn Leghorn to the Roadrunner, gets screen time, and the cumulative effect bathes the audience in a wash of nostalgic good will. Kids 12 and younger will be unable to resist the film's neon glow and fast pace, while adults, when they're not feeling hustled, may get a chuckle or two out of the sly references to Disney and "Pulp Fiction." So load up the young 'uns in the Ford(trademark) or Chevrolet(trademark), drive on down to your nearest United Artists(trademark) theater chain and enjoy an afternoon of American commercialism at its most willful and good-natured. Afterward, you might stop by your local Blockbuster(trademark) and rent a real movie. May we suggest "Hoop Dreams"? fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Hamilton police investigating a burglary spate in the past week -- which included the theft of a card signed by basketball legend Michael Jordan -- were today interviewing a teenager. Central Hamilton business Mark One Comics suffered a double blow that cost $ 5000 at the weekend when it was burgled -- twice. Manager Chris Lander said little was taken from the Victoria St store in the first raid on Saturday night. But a hole bashed in the shop's wall caused substantial damage and forced the business to close for two days. It reopened today. Mr Lander said that on Sunday night "it looks like they came back to do their Christmas shopping" using the wall hole to gain entry. Basketball cards, boxer shorts and t-shirts were taken. The haul included a Michael Jordan -signed basketball card. Neighbouring business Opus CD, a classical music stockist, was also burgled twice in the past week. Boxes of CDs packed for posting overseas were torn apart. Owner Russell Armitage said he thought about sleeping on the premises but instead had security systems installed. Police said they were interviewing a 17-year-old and expected to lay charges. -------------------- CAPTION: HOLE LOOK: Mark One Comics manager Chris Lander, left, with Russell Armitage of Opus CD _ both have been burgled twice. headline: JAMMIN' WITH JORDAN IN SPACE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KEYWORD-HIT Jammin' with Jordan in space Basketball megastar Michael Jordan blasts on to the big screen this month in his first animated movie, Space Jam. Jordan is joined by wisecracking hero, Bugs Bunny, who gets himself and his Looney Tunes cohorts into a jam by facing off against the Nerdlucks, a grouchy gang of tiny space creatures who land on Earth. QNP The Nerdlucks are sent by their ruthless boss, Swackhammer (voice by Danny DeVito), to kidnap and export the Looney Tunes' characters to Moron Mountain, Swack-hammer's failing theme park on the Nerdlucks' boring planet. Bugs challenges Nerdlucks to a basketball tournament. If the Looney Tunes win, they remain on Earth. But if the aliens win, Bugs and company are headed into the hands of Swackhammer. The Nerdlucks, unfortunately for Bugs, absorb new skills instantly, turning them into formidable opponents. The Looney Tunes, fearing failure, kidnap Michael Jordan and transport him to Looney Tunes Land in a bid to save the game. TV Scene today is giving 50 readers the chance to win a double preview pass to see all the Space Jam fun on Wednesday, December 11 at the Greater Union Cinema 4, at 6.30pm. To be in the draw to win, put your name, address and daytime phone number on the back of an envelope and send it to: The Space Jam Competition, The Sunday Mail, Locked Bag 8884, GPO Brisbane 9003, to reach us by Thursday. headline: IRON MIKE HITS GOLD
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Oh, to be like Mike Mike Tyson, that is. Tyson earned $ 75 million this year, making him the undisputed champion of the Forbes Super 40 list of highest paid athletes, which was released yesterday. Although Tyson no longer wears the heavyweight boxing crown, his earnings from his three fights this year were enough to slam-dunk another Mike Michael Jordan from the top spot of the list. Tyson also made more than the man who knocked him out in their bout last month, Evander Holyfield, the sixth-place finisher with $ 15.5 million. Jordan of the Chicago Bulls, who led the list for four straight years, came in second with $ 52.6 million in salary and endorsement money. Last year, Tyson was No. 2 on the list with $ 40 million in earnings. Iron Mike was paid $ 25 million to dispatch Peter McNeeley in 89 seconds and earned the rest in signing bonuses. This year, Tyson had to work a little harder. It took him three rounds to whup Frank Bruno in March and one round to dispatch Bruce Seldon in September. He lasted 11 rounds before losing to Holyfield. No. 3 on the list, German auto racer Michael Schumacher, was paid $ 25 million to drive for Ferrari's Formula One team and made another $ 8 million in other income. He was No. 9 a year ago. Basketball behemoth Shaquille O'Neal, who signed with the Los Angeles Lakers in the summer, moved up from No. 5 to No. 4, with his total income computed at $ 24.4 million. Dallas Cowboys running back Emmitt Smith came in fifth with $ 16.5 million in earnings. The highest-ranking New York athletes on the list were Knicks center Patrick Ewing, who placed 10th with $ 12.4 million in earnings, and 14th-place finisher Wayne Gretzky of the Rangers, whose earnings tallied $ 11.5 million. Last year, tennis star Steffi Graf placed 30th on the list with $ 7.5 million in earnings. This year, no women made the list. But a male athlete who sometimes dresses in women's clothing was a big earner. Jordan's teammate Dennis Rodman made the list for the first time, coming in ninth with $ 12.9 million in earnings. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 -------------------- THE latest comic book character to jump to the big screen is The Phantom, starting at TopTown Cinema 4 this week. Starring Billy Zane (of Dead Calm fame), this movie was filmed in Australia, and The Phantom did better in that country than in the United States. America hype and hoopla come to the fore in Space Jam, a comedy starring champion basketball player Michael Jordan and a host of cartoon characters led by Bugs Bunny. It shot to the top of the US box office in its opening weekend, taking $ NZ42 million in the first three days. Courage Under Fire shows at Stratford's King's Theatre this weekend. Space Jam (G) Stars: Michael Jordan , Bill Murray, Looney Tunes. Director Joe Pytka. THE Looney Tunes are in trouble. A group of midget space-creatures have come to Earth to kidnap them and take them to an interplanetary theme park as the latest attraction. But Bugs Bunny throws down a challenge: if the much larger Looney Tunes win a basketball game against the aliens, they get to stay on Earth. If not, the aliens get their newest attraction. But the aliens turn snaky, and create huge creatures for their team filled with the essence of NBA stars. On the 'toons team? Only Michael Jordan ; NBA champ from the Chicago Bulls. On him, and a wide variety of cartoon characters, lies the fate of the world. Also screening: 101 Dalmatians (G), High School High (M), Ransom (R16), Sleepers (R16), Jingle all the Way (PG). FREEDMAN; Samuel G. Freedman is the author of "The Inheritance: How Three Families and America Moved From Roosevelt to Reagan and Beyond." By SAMUEL G. FREEDMAN; Samuel G. Freedman is the author of "The Inheritance: How Three Families and America Moved From Roosevelt to Reagan and Beyond." Body_118 Body_118 Body fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Five years before Michael Jordan made his acting debut opposite Bugs Bunny, in that exercise in synergy called "Space Jam," a small and serious film about inner-city basketball entitled "Heaven Is a Playground" quietly opened. It had no stars, no cartoons and no merchandising campaign. Rather, "Heaven Is a Playground" represented the decade-long effort of a first-time director to bring to the screen a classic book about one summer on the courts of Brooklyn. And "Heaven Is a Playground," antithetical in so many ways to "Space Jam," was a film that Michael Jordan was supposed to have made. For several years in the late 1980's, he was contracted to play a featured role in the drama, but financial problems delayed the production. Then in early 1990, with the money apparently in place, Mr. Jordan withdrew from the project. Whether he did so legally remains the subject of a suit in Cook County Circuit Court in Illinois. The producers of "Heaven" have sued Mr. Jordan, his agent David Falk and the sports agency Proserv, seeking $28 million in damages for breach of contract. On the orders of the presiding judge, the principals in the case have refused to publicly discuss any of the events at issue. Regardless of how the legal matter is resolved, the story of Mr. Jordan's two movies presents a parable of art and commerce and the choice one gifted athlete made between them. Put another way, "Heaven Is a Playground" strove toward a complex, almost literary understanding of basketball while "Space Jam" has giddily reduced it to entertainment product. "Heaven" had its genesis in 1973, when Michael Jordan was still in grade school and the most spectacular and volatile basketball star in the country was James (Fly) Williams, a product of Brooklyn playing for tiny Austin Peay University in Clarksville, Tenn. That fall, Rick Telander of Sports Illustrated magazine wrote a profile of Williams and his adoring fans in the slum of Brownsville. More important, Mr. Telander returned to the neighborhood for the entire summer of 1974 -- coaching a team of misfits, boarding with a self-appointed talent scout and absorbing the drama of talented and uneducated young men desperately trying to play their way out of the ghetto. The resulting book, "Heaven Is a Playground," fused acute sports journalism with urban-affairs reportage, setting its playground dreamers in a landscape ravaged by crime, drug abuse and gang violence. If anything, Mr. Telander suffered for being too prophetic. Published nearly 20 years before the 1994 documentary "Hoop Dreams" became a national hit by similarly chronicling the fleeting promise of deliverance offered by inner-city basketball , "Heaven Is a Playground" sold fewer than 5,000 copies in hard-cover and has tumbled in and out of print ever since. Still, on the strength of its cult following, the book attracted steady interest from the film industry. At various points in the late 1970's, the rights belonged to Nick Grillo, who later helped produce "The Hunt for Red October," and to George Clinton, impresario of the funk band Parliament-Funkadelic. Richard Wesley, an acclaimed playwright, wrote a screenplay as a starring vehicle for Richard Pryor. None of these efforts, however, ever went into production. So in the early 1980's Mr. Telander began writing the film with Randall Fried, a partner in pickup basketball games and the winner of a student Academy Award for his thesis film at the University of Southern California. Together the men created fictional equivalents to the book's major characters while preserving its grit and underlying tragedy. Their plot largely turned on the rise and fall of Truth Harrison, a mercurial college star who slips into cocaine addiction just when he is on the verge of signing a lucrative pro contract. That version of the project, like all its predecessors, failed to find studio support. Then, in 1986, Mr. Fried acquired the rights to "Heaven," enabling him to direct the film. And early the next year, he began pursuing Michael Jordan to play a leading role as Matthew Lockhart, a splendid and reclusive athlete who inexplicably had walked away from a college scholarship. The Michael Jordan whom Mr. Fried sought was not the global brand name of today. He was a wildly talented third-year player on a mediocre Chicago Bulls team who was sidelined the previous season with a broken foot. After several months

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WHEN you go to see an action comedy featuring the Looney Tunes characters and one-man basketball franchise Michael Jordan , you have to expect it to be commercial _ and Space Jam certainly makes no attempt to be anything else. At one point someone even manages to mention all of Jordan's product endorsements in a single sentence. Nevertheless, in between the numerous plugs for Jordan's shoes, there are some great laughs to be had, particularly at the expense of some of the NBA's biggest players. The story goes like this: A bunch of weird little aliens hit Looney Tunes Land with the intent of making Bugs and company slaves on their home planet (run by a tubby guy who sounds rather like Danny De Vito). The Toons challenge them to a basketball game, to which the aliens respond by heading above ground and "stealing" the talent of NBA heroes Charles Barkley, Patrick Ewing, Muggsey Bogues, Larry Johnson and Shawn Bradley, turning themselves into the giant basketball wizards, the "Monstars". Bugs decides to get help from "the world's greatest FILM REVIEW Space Jam (G) 1/2 Pacific Cinemas (Running time: 87 minutes) basketballer" Michael Jordan who's been languishing in his newfound career as as Major League baseballer, setting the scene for some silly basketball gags as "Air" Jordan tries to whip the Toons into shape before the big match. Space Jam has the above players starring as themselves (with some great jokes coming from Barkley and the boys as they struggle to catch and dribble a ball), as well as Larry Bird and Bill Murray. Jordan does well (but then he is playing himself!), assisted in Toon Land by a sycophantic sidekick played by Wayne Knight (Newman, of Seinfeld). Definitely one for the teens. _ Paula Weston Classification_184 Classification_184 Classification fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Language: ENGLISH Subject: BASKETBALL (95%); COMEDY FILMS (90%); PROFESSIONAL SPORTS (90%); ACTION & ADVENTURE FILMS (78%); BASEBALL (78%); SPORTS & RECREATION EVENTS (78%); MOVIE REVIEWS (67%) Company: BUGS&CO (67%) Organization: NATIONAL BASKETBALL ASSOCIATION (83%) Industry: COMEDY FILMS (90%); ACTION & ADVENTURE FILMS (78%); MOVIE REVIEWS (67%) Person: MICHAEL JORDAN (89%) Load-Date: March 9, 2002 fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor 0 lineWidth 12700 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 End of Document Page PAGE of NUMPAGES BROUGHT TO YOU BY HYPERLINK https://advance.lexis.com/api/document?collection=news&id=urn:contentItem:459P-KK10-0198-33JK-00000-00&context=1519360 00 BROUGHT TO YOU BY THE SOUTHERN STAR December 18, 1996, Wednesday Bookmark_187 Bookmark_187

headline: AFTER NBA
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan , a man who routinely does things no one else can, soon will face the inevitable: retirement. So will his sponsors. Like smart athletes who prepare for life after sports, the companies that pay the 34-year-old Jordan an estimated $ 40 million a year to sell their products are preparing for a Jordan after basketball . Some of the advertising already is shifting from Jordan-the-superstar-athlete to Jordan-the-average-guy. The ads for his cologne, launched in the autumn, focus on him off the court. Nike Inc.'s most-recent commercials have him pondering the number of times he missed the game-winning shot. "It's always interesting to see him fail a little, to realize he's human," said Bob Dorfman, a sports endorsement expert with the San Francisco office of Foote, Cone & Belding. Many of Jordan's ads this year are moving away from urging people to "be like Mike," instead saying "Mike's like us." Making The Switch In his cologne ads, Jordan is pictured quietly singing a children's song while driving his car; gently scolding his dog; and answering such questions as "what is your favorite shape?" from young children in a classroom. Sports play no role in the ads. "It would appear as if they are re-gearing him toward not playing basketball anymore," said Nova Lanktree, head of Lanktree Sports Celebrity Network Inc., one of the nation's top sports talent firms. Other athletes have made the successful switch. Golfer Arnold Palmer, years after being a successful competitor even on the Senior PGA Tour, makes millions by licensing his name. Palmer has agreements involving 800 licenses to use his name in 180 product categories, according to a recent Brandweek article. His estimated annual take from royalties is about $ 15 million. Bigger Than Basketball While Jordan already has some licensed products - his cologne, a line of formal wear and a prepaid phone card - it pales next to Palmer, who has his name on products ranging from chopsticks to the Palmer Home Collection of furniture, according to Brandweek. It's unlikely that Jordan will have any trouble making a switch from athlete-endorser, because he's already bigger than basketball , Lanktree said. He gets contracts now because he's Michael Jordan , not because he's Michael Jordan , Chicago Bulls star. He can look to Palmer as precedent - he's still doing commercials at the age of 67, almost 10 years since he won a seniors tournament and 25 years since he won a regular tour event Jordan has said he won't return if the Chicago Bulls don't re-hire coach Phil Jackson. Even if Jordan were to come back for another season, his career is near its end and the NBA had better be prepared, Dorfman said. "Football and basketball are going toe-to-toe (as far as attracting endorsement offers from Madison Avenue)," said Bob Williams, president of Chicago-based Burns Celebrity Inc., which hires sports figures to endorse products. "But I think the NBA, particularly without Jordan, faces the difficult task of holding its position during the next three to five years." There may not be a successor of Jordan's caliber on the horizon: * Shaquille O'Neal was injured and had a disappointing season for the Los Angeles Lakers. * Grant Hill's Detroit Pistons still are not among the league's top squads. * Dennis Rodman has played his way out of Madison Avenue's mindset. * And this season's Most Valuable Player Karl Malone couldn't lift his game to the challenge during the finals. NBA Commissioner David Stern has joked that he will fold the league when Jordan retires. Jordan seems to be preparing himself mentally for the day when stops getting paid for his unique athletic ability. "Expectations of the fans and the media - and to some degree from a marketing standpoint - all of that has played into this expectation, this immortal person thing," Jordan said last week. "I'm a basketball player. I'm a normal person." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 "It's like buying a piece of your hero," says Sydney perfume executive Jean-Marc Carriol about his company's latest product launch - Michael Jordan cologne. Mr Carriol, communications director for the perfume distributor Trimex, expects to sell $4 million worth of basketball hero Michael Jordan 's scent between now and Christmas. That's eight times the rate of sales generated by Australia's most popular men's fragrances. Michael Jordan joins the long list of rich and famous who are putting their names and faces behind perfumes and making millions. But not all celebrity fragrances are hits, according to author and perfume expert Mr Michael Edwards. "In recent years fragrances named after Cher, Sophia Loren and Michael Jackson have all faded. It proves that just using a name is not a guarantee of success," he said. "The product has to be good too, and the celebrity you are trading off has to be involved in the product." The failures haven't stopped an increasing number of celebrities signing endorsement deals with major perfume companies to manufacture fragrances bearing their name and image. Usually the celebrity is paid a royalty based on sales generated by the fragrance. In return, manufacturers demand their chosen stars participate in intensive marketing campaigns, effectively becoming the face, name and spokesperson of the perfume. Worldwide royalties for Michael Jordan from his new perfume are expected to hit $50 million in its first year. Other famous names which have cottoned on to the smell of money include Elizabeth Taylor, Priscilla Presley, Paloma Picasso, Isabella Rossellini , Jerry Hall and murdered fashion designer Gianni Versace. Sales of Versace perfumes, also distributed by Trimex, have increased 158 per cent over the past month compared with figures for the same period last year. "It's just walking out the door at the moment," said Mr Carriol. Last year Australia saw the launch of Moments by Priscilla Presley. When Presley visited Melbourne's Myer store on Bourke Street, 5,000 people showed up to catch a glimpse of the star, and buy her perfume. This year Trimex has launched a second Presley perfume, Indian Summer by Priscilla Presley. Sales of Presley perfume in Australia have reached $1.5 million over the past year, putting the brand among the nation's best sellers. The total Australian perfume market is worth $400 million a year. "The celebrity name has to be visible and known," Mr Carriol said. "The celebrity also has to have a reasonable link to what they are going to sell. You couldn't put Michael Jordan 's name on a nail polish. The image has to fit the product." Even the packaging of the Jordan perfume is designed to reflect the sports idol. On the base of the bottle is a rubber pad with a tread designed to look like the base of a basketball shoe. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 No Bull! Blokes can splash on a little Michael Jordan perfume before going through the hoops in the office. MICHAEL Jeffrey Jordan has long prided himself on his refusal to accept anything less than the best. Jordan is the undisputed king of sports endorsements. Jordan has given his name to deals with Nike for Air Jordan basketball shoes, Gatorade and Oakley for sunglasses. The latest venture for the famed Chicago Bull hoopster is in the giddy world of men's fragrance. The Michael Jordan cologne has just been launched in Australia to much fanfare. So why would someone bother to bring out a men's fragrance and name it after a basketball player from Chicago? The answer is simple. A recent survey clearly showed that Michael Jordan was the most popular sports icon in this country - his popularity was streaks ahead of any local football star. Even relegates Plugger to interchange bench. Clearly, Jordan's statistics on the basketball court are legendary. He's led the Bulls to five NBA championships and scored more than 25,000 career points to go with his Olympic gold medals from the 1984 Los Angeles and 1992 Barcelona Games. The cologne deal is expected to swell Jordan's bank balance by a whopping $50 million. When it was launched in the US last year there was such a demand for the product, retailers were forced to limit sales, just to keep it from going out of stock. It became the number one selling men's fragrance overnight and has remained a top seller ever since. Says Jordan of the fragrance: "It's not serious or contrived. It's relaxed and really makes you feel good." So the question remains, will a splash of MJ imbue the wearer with the charisma and the hoop-shooting ability of its namesake? My partner finds the cologne clearly distinctive, crisp and invigorating. "Very musky! It clearly leaves me with a warm feeling." (Her words, not mine). But the essence clearly didn't make me play like MJ down at McColl's gym at Glebe. In fact, it was time for me to retire the size 12 hightops forever. * The cologne is available in two sizes - a 50ml natural spray for $49.95 and a 100ml, $75. Michael Jordan cologne is distributed nationally through David Jones, Grace Bros, Target and selected perfumeries and pharmacies. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan spent part of his summer vacation in Maui being thrashed by large waves. He did not have a $33 million contract at the time, and even now, he laughs at the wisdom of his decision to body surf for the first time. But the ocean was inviting, his children were urging him on and, always paramount with Jordan, a challenge was calling. "I was scared to death, but I was out there," he said yesterday. "That's the highlight of the summer for me. People know how fearful I am of water. But there I was. No life jacket or nothing." He paused and added, "I could still stand up, though." Jordan was sitting in the suite of a Manhattan hotel. Realizing he can conquer the basketball universe for only so long, he is in New York promoting a new clothing and shoe line -- the Jordan Brand. The new business venture still falls under the Nike umbrella, but the shoes are quintessentially Jordan. He helped design them, and they feature the jump-man logo, Jordan's famous spread-eagle insignia. "It's a part of me; it's a part of the creative personality I have," he said. "When I walk away from the game, this is my means of staying in tune with the game. Not coaching, not commentary. Those are things that I chose not to do and I don't want to do." Jordan hawking sneakers does not have the same appeal as Jordan taking flight toward the basket. But here he is, at 34, with one foot in the United Center and another in a corporate boardroom, letting all the suits in on his vision for the company. In fact, a commercial is due out soon featuring Jordan leaving a game at halftime to attend to his corporate duties. It hardly seems real, Jordan preparing for life after basketball . "I really don't know how long I'm going to play," he said. "I couldn't give you a concrete time frame or whatever. I like to think that each and every year we keep winning championships and I keep playing. Knowing at some point and time that management is going to change direction and I don't feel it's favorable in my direction, then I won't have a problem walking away." He is reminded of the opinion that he is too competitive to stop playing, that he will play far beyond his prime and tamper with his legend. "I can give it up if I tell myself that's it," he said. "I'm not afraid to walk away. Some people think I am. That's someone's perception of me who doesn't know me. I never think about the negative. I think about what's happening now. I'm still playing. "We still got a chance to win a championship. That's how I look at it." Along those lines, Jordan tried to stay away from the business of basketball as much as possible this summer. "I got away from the game, I got away from the city a little bit, I got more into things like this," he said, surveying his own line of sneakers in front of him. "I did a lot of basketball camps this summer, staying in tune with the kids who love the game. I think that's fun." He also played a lot of golf and worked at a 35-and-older fantasy camp in Las Vegas, Nev., taking one day off to hash out the details of the one-year, $33 million contract offered by Bulls Chairman Jerry Reinsdorf -- the richest one-year salary in team sports history. Aside from that deal, Jordan said he did not think about the inner working of the Bulls very much. He knew the details would be taken care of. "I detached myself for a while, did things that I felt were important," he said. Establishing a balance between being the world's greatest basketball player as well as a purveyor of cologne, footwear, briefs and motion pictures has been a chore at times. "As you look at my career, those things haven't defined Michael Jordan ," he said. " Michael Jordan 's basketball skills defined him. That's the danger that these kids have today. A lot of these things have been given to them. If they don't use the tools to justify the things that have been given to them, then that can be very dangerous for the game of basketball ." Finally, he again spoke of retirement, of an adjustment period he is no longer afraid to confront. "Actually, I'm looking forward to it because of the way people have made it a challenge for me," he said. "It goes back to proving once again that I know what's correct for myself. And, you know, everyone else can speculate. I'm going to do what's right, and I'm going to make sure that you see that I have a choice." Without realizing it, Jordan sometimes speaks about his basketball career in past tense. But then he issues a declaration to his friend Patrick Ewing, the close-but-no-cigar Knicks, Charles Barkley and every other contender. "You had your opportunity to win when I was out of the game," Jordan said, referring to his brief run at professional baseball. "The next opportunity is when I leave the game. I don't see Patrick winning. I don't see Charles winning. "I don't see anybody winning but us. And whenever I step away, maybe that's their chanc

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Laura Baier hit her chip shot close enough to the hole to earn a "good job" and a handshake from Michael Jordan . That prompted Tyeisha Moore, who had gone earlier, to bound out of the crowd of youngsters, hand extended. "I did a good job, too." The ruse worked; Jordan also shook the Elgin girl's hand. It was Jordan's second annual golf challenge on Saturday, an event in which 40 kids get to putt, chip and hit full shots with the basketball legend at his golf center in Aurora. There were eight young winners for overall excellence, but perhaps the star of the day was Seann Estrella, 8, of Downers Grove. Immediately after Jordan had taken three unsuccessful putts from 50 feet (with a nasty sidehill break), Seann stepped up and drained one from the same spot. "This kid can putt," Jordan said. Funny, though. These kids weren't talking much about their golf games. It was all about seeing their hero, coming into contact with him and other MJ trivia. "His hands are huge," said Lindsay Horan of Naperville. "His shoulders are huge," said Laura Baier of Downers Grove. "He had chicken with green noodles and sauce for lunch," reported Cory Senz. Cory had a buddy who videotaped the dramatic footage of a plate of food making its way from the kitchen to Jordan, who had a quick private lunch in the pro shop after the event. Cory, 12, of Naperville, played in the event last year with his brother Ross. On Saturday, Ross, 13, made a repeat appearance - an amazing feat because thousands of kids signed up at Sears, which sponsored the event, and were whittled down to the final 40 through luck of the draw. The event also was an occasion for Jordan to accept a $ 10,000 donation from Sears for his youth golf program. It gives disadvantaged kids a chance to learn the game "that's very special to all of us," Jordan said. The day was pretty special to the young golfers, too. "This is a great opportunity for the kids," said Tom Senz, dad of Cory and Ross. "Michael's pretty wonderful with them." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE world's most famous sportsman is set to be upstaged by four Tyneside teenagers as basketball 's big guns lock horns in Paris this weekend. For Chicago Bulls superstar Michael Jordan will be asked to stay on the sidelines as the sharp-shooting quarter provide the half-time entertainment at the McDonald's Championship. Graeme Turnbull, Shaun Robison, Sarah Hindmarsh and Deborah Stabler won a dream ticket to Europe's showpiece NBA event after netting top marks in a local shoot-out. They jetted off today on their all-expenses paid trip to the Championships - played every two years and featuring the cream of world basketball . The 36,000-strong capacity crowd will court the youngsters at the half in the Bull's opening game. Shaun, 14, a pupil at Burnside High School in Wallsend, beamed: "I've been playing basketball for three years and I never dreamed about doing anything like this.'' Both Shaun and fellow pupil Graeme, also 14, had to hit the target from various points on the court to scoop their top prize. And their efforts were matched by England international and TY McGurk Tyne and Wear ladies' favourite Sarah, 15 and her 16-year-old partner Deborah. The Wearside pair admitted their trip to Paris still hadn't sunk in and Deborah revealed: "I don't even know the rules of basketball ! "I play netball and Sarah asked me to join her because of my shooting skills. We're a pretty good team and now she's giving me a crash course on the Bulls.'' fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan has taken it to the net thousands of times. Now he is on the Net. The Chicago Bulls superstar guard and SportsLine USA unveiled Jordan's official Web page last week, featuring photos, statistics and audio and video clips. Welcome to the club Michael. The Web already is home to hundreds of pages devoted to you and even more mentions of your name. An Alta Vista query turns up 83,000 Michael Jordan mentions; an Infoseek search, 9,800. Unofficial Jordan Web sites range from the commercial to the informational to the purely devotional. Most pages constructed by devotees across the country bespeak their authors' pure admiration and awe for Jordan. They don't mince words. "I strongly believe that Michael Jordan is the greatest basketball player of all time," starts one page ( HYPERLINK http://www.geocities.com/Colosseum/5058/ 00 http://www.geocities.com/Colosseum/5058/ ). "No, I know he is, and therefore I have set this page up to further glorify the man they call 'His Royal Airness.' " Tim Franklin's Jordan page ( HYPERLINK http://www.htc.net/nilknarf/Jordan.html 00 http://www.htc.net/nilknarf/Jordan.html ) meticulously lists Jordan's college and professional statistics, then he allows himself an editorial comment, "Simply Awesome aren't they?" One tribute ( HYPERLINK http://carver.holycross.edu/acamarra/jordan2.html 00 http://carver.holycross.edu/acamarra/jordan2.html ) greets visitors with a red silhouette of MJ flying toward the net. It features photographs and two dozen links to other Jordan sites. A Tarheel whose favorite athlete is - you guessed it - includes in his tribute photos of Jordan as a senior at the University of North Carolina and as a player at Laney High School ( HYPERLINK http://www.unc.edu/badgers/jordan.html 00 http://www.unc.edu/badgers/jordan.html ). Stop by "Hangin: A Filipino's tribute to Michael Jordan " ( HYPERLINK http://www.skyinet.net/users/mairj/jordan/index.html 00 http://www.skyinet.net/users/mairj/jordan/index.html ) to get a sense of Jordan's international popularity. The home page explains Jordan's nickname in the Philippines, Hangin (ha ngeen), the Filipino word for "air." It answers the question, "How popular is Michael Jordan in the Philippines?" and reveals that the nation saw its first live NBA basketball game in June 1991, Game 5 of the Bulls-Lakers championship series. Contrarians to Jordan's greatness are few and far between. The "Reasons to Hate Jordan" page ( HYPERLINK http://http:www.geocities.com/SouthBeach/Lights/6143/jordan.html 00 http:www.geocities.com/SouthBeach/Lights/6143/jordan.html ) acknowledges Jordan's superior play and importance to the game, but takes him to task for being a ball hog and showing off (Like "against the Lakers in the Finals, Jordan switched hands during the lay-up unnecessarily"). The author also thinks Michael's sneakers are getting uglier and his restaurant's too big. But don't worry, Michael, you'll soon have company; Shaq's getting his own "Why I Hate" page. Another Web site author describes the sad tale of his Jordan dream quashed in "A Michael Jordan Fan's Heartbreak" ( HYPERLINK http://members.tripod.com/mj23/mj.html 00 http://members.tripod.com/mj23/mj.html ). Michael Dequina, a member of Nike's Air Jordan Flight Club, woke up an August 1995 morning thrilled at the prospect of seeing one of his greatest idols during the filming of Space Jam at Blair Field in Long Beach. Despite a close call, Dequina never had the chance to shake Michael's hand or even get a look. Michael Jordan also is the subject of artistic musings on the Net. A short story by a fifth-grader named Joletta ( HYPERLINK http://www.kidpub.org/kidpub/The_Day_Michael_Jordan_Came_to_My_Hou 00 http://www.kidpub.org/kidpub/The_Day_ HYPERLINK http://www.kidpub.org/kidpub/The_Day_Michael_Jordan_Came_to_My_Hou 00 Michael_Jordan HYPERLINK http://www.kidpub.org/kidpub/The_Day_Michael_Jordan_Came_to_My_Hou 00 _Came_to_My_Hou se-Joletta_L.html) describes a chance meeting with Michael Jordan and his family that ends up with a limo ride and the Jordans spending two weeks with the family. Jordan also is the subject of digital artist Misako Tsunemi's attention ( HYPERLINK http://www.st.rim.or.jp/mtsune/MJ.html 00 http://www.st.rim.or.jp/mtsune/MJ.html ). Fans can find a large number of commercial stops, too. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan has lent his name and fame to everything from cereal to cologne to sneakers. Now, the basketball and business phenomenon from Chicago is bestowing the Jordan moniker on a strictly New York enterprise -- a steakhouse in Grand Central Terminal. In a licensing agreement with the Manhattan-based Glazier Group that was signed yesterday, Michael Jordan -- the Steak House will open in February on the northwest balcony of the terminal. Under the agreement, the terms of which prohibit disclosure of the financial arrangements, the restaurant will use Mr. Jordan's name and will be run by Penny and Peter Glazier. The deal comes as many prospective stores and restaurants are lining up to secure the 180,000 square feet of space in the refurbished Beaux-Arts landmark. The restaurant will be one of four fine-dining establishments on the balconies overlooking the main waiting room. Leases are being negotiated with prominent New York restaurateurs for the other three spots. Falk Associates Management Enterprises, a Washington group that represents Mr. Jordan, had been looking for more than a year for a suitable restaurant venture in Manhattan for the basketball star. The Glaziers, who own the Monkey Bar and Tapika in midtown Manhattan and Bridgewaters in the South Street Seaport, and had been planning a steakhouse in the balcony space, began negotiating with the group about nine months ago. None of Mr. Jordan's representatives would comment on the new venture. The 150-seat steakhouse, which will occupy 7,000 square feet as it wraps around the balcony, will have a spacious bar seating 60 people near the Vanderbilt Avenue entrance to the terminal, as well as a small retail shop selling mainly restaurant-related items like steak knives. "This is not about basketball and Michael Jordan the basketball star," Mr. Glazier said. "It's about fine dining and Michael Jordan the businessman. There will not be any sports memorabilia or photographs of Michael Jordan shooting baskets in the restaurant." And unlike the culinary repertoire at Michael Jordan 's in Chicago, a franchise restaurant that uses Mr. Jordan's name but in which he is not a partner, the steakhouse menu will not include the usual sports bar fare. Typical steakhouse main courses, including prime porterhouse and rib-eyes, plus chops, chicken and fish, will be in the $20 range, a notch below other Manhattan steakhouses like the Palm, Morton's, Sparks and Smith & Wollensky. "We wanted the prices to be a little lower," Mr. Glazier said, "because we envision this restaurant as a place that will attract families." Mrs. Glazier said that the restaurant will have an extensive list of wines, champagnes and spirits, but no beer on tap. " Michael Jordan has a good sense of style and a taste for fine food and wine," she said. "The restaurant will reflect that." So visitors to the steakhouse might plan on wearing their Sunday best instead of basketball jerseys to dine at tables set with white linen and French Christofle silver. The food will be prepared under the direction of David Walzog, who is the executive chef of Tapika, the Glaziers' restaurant on 56th Street and Eighth Avenue. The restaurant is being designed by the architect David Rockwell. All the details have been approved by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, which owns the terminal, and the Landmarks Preservation Commission. Because the terminal's landmark status prohibits any changes to the walls, floor or ceiling of the space, Mr. Rockwell has created freestanding polished wood panels to camouflage service areas and enclose two small private dining rooms. The color scheme will be burgundy and gold, with plush lounge chairs in the bar area that evoke trains like the 20th Century Limited. The Glaziers said they hope to become involved in more projects with Mr. Jordan once the steakhouse opens. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 He's just been named Sports Illustrated for Kids' Athlete of the Year. You've seen him in the movie "Space Jam" and watched him burn up the courts as a guard, leading the Chicago Bulls to many champsionships. You've probably even caught him selling underwear. His biggest fans know him as simply "Michael." But who is Michael Jeffrey Jordan, the multimillionaire athlete? And where did he come from? "Michael is a warrior on the court," says Jesse Kornbluth, a journalist who has spent hours interviewing "His Airness." "But he's a warrior with a tender side. Michael Jordan is about standing up for what you believe." Jordan's determination was tested when he first tried out to play basketball . At age 16 and 5-11, he was sure he had what it took to play and win for his North Carolina high school ---until he read the team roster. The name "Michael Jeffrey Jordan" wasn't there. Once past the disappointment, Jordan used the next summer to improve his game. "Michael hates to lose," Korn-bluth told News for Kids. So he lived on the courts, he drove himself hard, and he refused to give up. The next season, a 6-3 Jordan made the grade. Though no one seemed to recognize it then, Jordan was destined to become one of the greatest basket-ball players ever. Now 6-6 and weighing in at 216 pounds, No. 23 is all but unstoppable. He's helped the Chicago Bulls win the NBA championship five out of the last seven seasons. He's claimed the NBA top scoring title seven times in a row. And four times, he's been named the NBA's MVP ---most valuable player of the year. "I asked him once, why do you compete so hard? Why do you play every moment at maximum intensity?" says Kornbluth. "And he said, 'I don't want to be forgotten.' " Off the courts, 34-year-old Jordan is a smart business man, a husband to Juanita Jordan, and dad to three lucky kids ---Jeffrey, Marcus and Jasmine. He lives in a Chicago suburb and is as determined to keep his personal life as private as he is to slam dunk the ball. Our hat's off to "Air Jordan" ---one of the greatest athletes on earth. You'll find dozens of Jordan photos (everything from game day to public appearances), stats and NBA updates. One of the latest MJ biographies, the focus is on his return to the game after his brief (1993) retirement. "Airborne Again!" by Jesse Kornbluth (Aladdin, ages 12 and up, $ 4.99). Kornbluth reveals Jordan's mind-set just before his temporary retirement. " Michael Jordan : Beyond Air" by Philip Brooks (Children's Press, ages 9-12, $ 18). Career-centered biography for young readers on His Airness. " Michael Jordan " (Black American of Achievement series) by Sean Dolan, Nathan Irvin Huggins (Chelsea House Publishers, ages 12 and up, $ 8.95). A look at how Jordan has become an African-American role model. " For the Love of the Game: Michael Jordan and Me" by Eloise Greenfield, illustrated by Jan Spivey (HarperCollins, ages 5-9, $ 14.95). fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Naseem Hamed's earnings are only just the start of the biggest financial success story in British sport. Hamed's manager Brendan Ingle reckons the boxer will reach the nine-figure mark before he is finished punching for huge pay days. The World Boxing Organisation featherweight champion's income from in and out of the ring in 1997 - $ 12m (pounds 7.5m) has surpassed that of Lennox Lewis, $ 9.7m (pounds 6.1m), according to the American magazine Forbes. "I said three or four years ago that Naz will earn pounds 40m and all he's got to do now is keep winning," Ingle said. "And with the pay-per-view market and Frank Warren promoting him, there's nothing to stop him earning pounds 100m. Warren has done a fantastic job. The heavyweight champion Lewis should really be ahead, but Naz has passed him under Warren." A recent poll in a British newspaper put Lewis as the No 1 earner with Hamed in second place, but Hamed's commercial interests are now huge. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Has this been fun or what? Since they opened the season on Nov. 24, we've watched the Elgin Maroons become what is now a 29-1 supersectional qualifier. We wondered when they were 4-0 and heading into a weekend of playing St. Charles and West Aurora back-to-back if they could survive that test. They did. We wondered, when they were 8-0 going into an Upstate Eight Conference showdown at Waubonsie Valley if they could survive that test. They did. Four games after disposing of Waubonsie Valley, they won their own Christmas tournament and concluded 1997 with a sparkling 13-0 record. We wondered how long it could last. Larkin, East Aurora, Brother Rice, St. Charles, Schaumburg, Streamwood and Barrington all came and went. They were 20-0, the last remaining undefeated Class AA team in the state. Then the balloon popped. Waubonsie Valley came to town and dumped a 69-56 loss on the Maroons. How would they now react? Well, nine games later here we are at 29-1 and one game from Peoria and the state tournament. Magical? Yep. Pressure-packed? To be sure. Great basketball ? And how. But most of all it's been fun. As reporters we have a duty to stay objective. But I know that everyone on my staff, myself included, has had fun covering this team. Today, we're saluting the "Sweet 16" Maroons. The 16 Elgin High basketball players who have endured not just these 30 games but all the pressure that comes with being one of the most recognized teams in the state. We asked them a few simple questions about themselves and the season they've had to this point. Today, we share their answers with you and we hope you enjoy reading what's below.
