He's not arrogant, but the reason so many people are so hyped over him is he seems approachable, and not lost like Rodman or Shaquille." Mary Thelen, 48, instructional assistant, Bismarck "He is Mr. Basketball , and the whole game is compared to him -- he's the No. 1 name in basketball . Even for somebody who does not know anything about the game, I actually know something about Michael Jordan . He's the best player that ever was. I've watched games, and there's nobody who can do what he does. He has class, and portrays fairness. Obviously, I like the guy, and he seems to have a good personality. He's also a team man, who's out there to do a job. I also like Scottie Pippen and Phil Jackson." C.J. Bjork, 13 (in center with Nicole Paul and Nicole Eckenrade), Bismarck "Very important. He's, like, the most valuable player, and I like to watch the Bulls, because he's really good. I like Dennis Rodman, too, but he's kind of weird. (I do like his hair, because it's a different color every game.) Michael also has his own cologne, and it smells good. If he ever quit the Bulls, his fans would follow him to whatever he would do next. Now, I hope they win against the Utah Jazz, because the Bulls are a good team." Kimberly Thomas, 18, waitress, Mandan "We were just talking about him. He's done a lot for the game and, as a role model, he's influenced a lot of people in a positive way that's made people try to do their best. He's fun to watch, and he's one of the reasons I'd go to a pro game, because it can't be the same effect as seeing him on TV. There's other great players besides Jordan, like Shaq or Karl Malone. But, if Michael Jordan leaves, it will have a great effect on the Bulls being a favored team, because if you think of the Bulls, you think of Michael Jordan ." Levi French, 15, student, Mandan "Very important, and without him, nobody would watch basketball . He's the one that makes basketball work. I like to watch the Bulls, and I even have a Bulls jacket. I like Jordan and Pippen, because they're both hard workers and are good role models to have. Some people I know will probably quit following the Bulls if Jordan quits, and basketball won't be any fun to watch anymore, because then it will be only a bunch of punk rookies who get paid too much. I don't think Michael Jordan wants to retire." Ron Gerrick, 40, sales, Winnipeg, Manitoba "Since he's been playing the game, I'd say the NBA has a larger following in Canada because of him, and I go way back to Wilt Chamberlain. There were a few in between, but Jordan brought the game to a worldwide awareness. My son-in-law is a Bulls fan, or maybe he's a Jordan fan, but they seem to go hand in hand. There's probably more Bulls fans than there are Raptors or Grizzlies fans in Canada. My sport of choice is hockey, and we've always had superstars, but Jordan is definitely a basketball superstar." headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Every Friday morning in Carpentras, a city of 20,000 in south central France, an outdoor market takes over the center of the city as well as the main parking lot serving the business district. The merchants, itinerant peddlers who come from all over the region, sell everything from live birds to hand saws, with particular emphasis on food and clothing. A visitor to that market in May 1992, three months before Michael Jordan and the NBA Dream Team captivated the sports world in the Barcelona Olympics, plainly was surprised to find one of the items hanging in several clothing merchants' displays. It was a knockoff of a Bulls jersey, No. 23. At that moment it became as clear as the clothes on a Frenchman's rack that fashioning oneself after Michael Jordan had become a major reason basketball is in style around the world. The impact of No. 23 on basketball 's global growth is so great it almost is incalculable. "In France, more people know Michael Jordan than the players on the French national team," said Jean-Luc Thomas, basketball editor of the French sports daily, L'Equipe, which chose Jordan as its Champion of Champions for 1992. No basketball player previously had earned that distinction. In Tehran, where few Americans have gone for business or tourism in the 20 years since the Islamic revolution, the two English-language daily newspapers devote up to half their sports coverage to the NBA. The largest of the five Persian-language daily sports papers, Al Abrar, has a half-page of coverage of the NBA playoffs. "Most people here support Michael Jordan and the Bulls," Al Abrar reporter Pooria Jafereh said. In Kenya, basketball still is of such minor consequence the country does not compete in the African Basketball Championships. News of Jordan's October 1993 retirement was the lead sports story in Kenya's Daily Nation newspaper. Ratings for NBA telecasts in Spain dropped significantly in the nearly two seasons of Jordan's retirement. The chief of the English basketball association said his 8-year-old son would be "over the moon" when Jordan returned. Local color in the area near the Holmenkollen, Norway's legendary ski jump, now includes kids shooting hoops at red-and-black backboards outside their houses on the same wintry days people are soaring from the hill. In a German poll taken before the 1994 World Cup, young people were asked whether they would rather spend one day as Jordan or Lothar Matthaeus, then captain of Germany's defending soccer world champions. A stunning 35 percent in soccer-mad Germany preferred Jordan. "We hope out of the (1998) World Cup we can also have new stars who can match up with Michael Jordan and the NBA where marketing is concerned," said Sepp Blatter, secretary general of FIFA, soccer's governing body. Soccer remains the world's most popular sport, but basketball has made tremendous gains in the past decade, especially in Asia and Africa. The one constant attraction has been Jordan. When Jordan first went to Paris, he could stroll by himself down the Champs-Elysees, where only a few U.S. tourists recognized him. When he returned for the McDonald's Open last October, he needed bodyguards. "He is considered the best athlete in the world, not only in basketball ," said Boris Stankovic, president of the International Basketball Federation (FIBA). "If you have an athlete like that in your sport, the impact is tremendous." FIBA now has more member countries, 207, than any other sport but track and field. " Michael Jordan is an excellent example of how one player can give impetus to a whole movement," Stankovic said. "Our philosophy always has been that basketball is a team sport and that only a team could have that kind of impact on worldwide development, but Michael is something really special." headline: MICHAEL JORDAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Rayovac will spend more than 3 million dollars to get people in China to buy its batteries. The nation's No. 3 battery maker, with headquarters in Madison, is banking on its position as high value product and on the popularity of its high-profile pitchman, Chicago Bulls basketball superstar Michael Jordan , to lure the Chinese people to its batteries. ''From our research, we know that Michael Jordan is a very big icon in China,'' said Rayovac director of corporate communications John Daggett. National Basketball Association games are replayed in China, he said. ''Even though they've never seen a game live, he is a very big name in the Far East,'' Daggett said. The Chinese people buy more than 4 billion batteries a year, while Americans buy 2.8 billion, said Rayovac chairman and chief executive officer Dave Jones. Heavy-duty zinc-carbon batteries still dominate the market in China, but Rayovac will focus on selling its Maximum alkaline products. ''As they're Westernizing, and buying more and more electronic devices, they're making an upgrade into an alkaline (battery),'' Daggett said. The company has a contract with a distributor with extensive trade relationships in China. Batteries should be in the stores there by August, Daggett said, though he declined to reveal sales projections. Duracell and Energizer -- the two top-selling battery brands -- already are sold in China, but the market is very splintered, he said. Above the Rayovac logo on each package, Chinese lettering reads, ''powerful thunderbolt.'' ''We think it will tie in very nicely, especially when you add Michael into the equation,'' Daggett said. Rayovac shares closed Monday at $ 22.12, up 69 cents. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 It was class time at Beijing University, but at 10 this morning seven guys in shorts and T-shirts crowded around a large-screen colour TV in dorm room 511, a spare cement cubicle decorated with three metal bunk beds and yesterday's laundry. Nine student "shareholders" had bought the TV last month in anticipation of just such an occasion, and now hoots and jeers filled the air. "Aya, that Malone he plays dirty." "Nice ball. Go Rodman!" "They're losing with three minutes to go. I can't stand it. Hit me with a hammer!" Minutes later, half a world away, the Chicago Bulls clinched their sixth National Basketball Association championship with a swish of the ball passing through basket with only five seconds to play. The students jumped to their feet and began a refrain: "Chou Dan, Chou Dan, Chou Dan." Chou Dan, of course, is Michael Jordan . Sure, Bill Clinton, president of the world's most powerful nation, is about to visit China. But to the Chinese, Michael Jordan remains America's king. Clinton's state visit this month has brought out citizens' interest in all things American, from books to clothes to movie stars. But these all seem like passing fancies compared to the intense passion that Chinese, especially young Chinese, have developed for Michael Jordan and U.S. basketball . " Michael Jordan is much more famous than Clinton here," said Cheng Qian, 20, a Bulls fan and shareholder in the TV set who is a management major at Beijing University. The Chinese have named him kongzhong feiren -- "space flier." And, in the last week, businessmen, retired teachers, students and government officials have all paused to watch him, live on state-run television, as he led the Chicago Bulls to their 4-2 series victory over the Utah Jazz. When Beijing Meilande Information Co. recently asked 1,000 urban Chinese to identify the best-known Americans ever, Michael Jordan came in second, trailing Thomas Edison by just a few percentage points. Behind him were Albert Einstein, Mark Twain and Bill Gates. On the sidewalks of Jianguomenwai Avenue, street vendors sell Michael Jordan posters. In department stores, Michael Jordan books and calendars sit beside those featuring Hong Kong movie stars and Chairman Mao. "Of all American things, basketball is the most popular," said Li Fa, a junior, who could not join the crowd in 511 because he is a Jazz fan. "Everyone knows Michael Jordan ." State television began broadcasting prerecorded NBA games in the early 1990s, and recently switched to live broadcasts. Yesterday, Chinese viewers had three opportunities to see the final playoff game, which the Bulls won 87-86. The first was live at 7:30 a.m., then there was a taped game at 9 a.m. for the late risers, and finally a replay at 9 p.m. Chinese are hard pressed to say why they so adore the Bulls and Michael Jordan , who scored 45 points in the final game and was named Most Valuable Player. "I don't know -- because of his skill, and because he alone carries half of the Chicago team," said Li Qixing, 20, another of the TV's shareholders. U.S. basketball is certainly more colourful than the home-grown variant, where the People's Liberation Army's August 1 team (named for the day the army was founded) is the reigning champion. In skill and in entertainment value, Chinese players are no match for the likes of Dennis Rodman, who played the game with a green design dyed in his hair. Said Han Bai, a junior, sitting on his bunk bed in jeans and a T-shirt, "I couldn't accept this from a Chinese player, but he's an American, so we expect it." And U.S. sportscasts have other attractions. The students who forsook their studies to gather in room 511 on this steamy summer morning, ooohed when they saw Leonardo DiCaprio, star of the film Titanic, sitting at courtside. And, in a room full of male college juniors, the Jazz's cheerleaders, dancing in skintight black leotards, got the thumbs up as well. Heads nodded in approval as Cheng Qian opined, "In this way, the Jazz are better than the Bulls." Jordan is even more famous in China than U.S. President Bill Clinton. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 What others are writing about Michael Jordan 's future in the days after his sixth NBA title - one he won in the final seconds: %BC% HE STAYS %EC% * Bill Lyon, Philadelphia Inquirer: There isn't anything Michael Jordan does better than play basketball , and there isn't anything that fulfills him more. So, yes, he will be back. He will be back because the very first contract he ever signed included a clause called ''love of the game.'' It meant that, if moved by the spirit, he could play basketball , on his own, whenever, wherever, with whomever, he desired, and his employers couldn't do a fig about it. . . . He still loves the game. And vice-versa. * Steve Campbell, Albany Times Union: Jordan should come back for Jordan. Jordan owes it to himself to keep doing this for as long as he can. He owes himself the pleasure of pushing himself, of creating new challenges for himself. * Perry A. Farrell, Detroit Free Press: The one thing that won't change is Jordan's competitive nature. It will bring him back. %BC% HE GOES %EC% * Rich Hofmann, Philadelphia Daily News: Time is beginning to win the race . . . you saw it some in the Finals, and you saw it just minutes before Jordan concocted his exit for the ages. For Michael Jordan , it is the absolutely perfect time. So, go. Leave. Retire. Make history, one last time. Join Ted Williams. Join him in defining, once and forever, what a last day should be. * Dan Shaughnessy, The Boston Globe: Michael Jordan should walk away for good, leaving us with the perfect closing line, like Humphrey Bogart talking about the beginning of a beautiful friendship, or Clark Gable not giving a damn. Would ''Gone With the Wind'' or ''Casablanca'' be better movies if the directors had added another scene? fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Access to Jordan - to no avail Young campers have chance to ask, but don't Kids may say the darnedest things, but none of them at Michael Jordan 's basketball camp had much to say to the Bulls star about his future plans Friday. With fans eager to learn whether Jordan will be back and a horde of reporters waiting for a comment, only the young members of the camp at Elmhurst College were given access to His Airness. But not one camper asked about what he'll do in the wake of the Tim Floyd hiring. That would take a lot of nerve, said one 12-year-old boy, who added that maybe some of his peers would work up to that question. But on Friday, just what did the youngsters want to know about? Believe it or not, basketball . It was the young hoopsters who had the first crack at grilling Michael Jordan . They choked. With fans dying to know if Jordan will be back, and a horde of television and print reporters waiting for a comment, only the young members of his basketball camp were given access to MJ on Friday. But not one camper asked His Airness about his plans in the wake of the Tim Floyd hiring. Jordan has made a practice of not answering news media questions during his annual camp at Elmhurst College and so far, has kept mum on Floyd's hiring as director of basketball operations and possibly the next head coach. So what did the youngsters want to know about? Believe it or not, basketball . What was MJ thinking about when he made the game-winning shot against the Jazz in the NBA Finals? What does he focus on when the cameras are flashing while he's trying to shoot a free throw? "All positive things, all good things," Jordan answered to the two questions. He also told campers not to worry about going pro. "Don't think about being a professional basketball player," Jordan said. "Think about playing good basketball ." Several campers said they thought some of their peers would eventually ask Jordan about the future. But first they have to work up the nerve, said Grant Siders, 12, of Palatine. "I think they know he wants to be left alone," he said. "I think they're afraid to ask the question." They're afraid partly, Grant added, because they might not like the answer. Stephen Cagigal, 14, of Naperville said he wants to ask Jordan what his major was in college. A query about the future is silly, Stephen said, explaining Jordan likely won't answer it. Brett Olson, 10, of Arlington Heights, agreed. "It's his personal life," he said. "He'll do what he wants." Some said they just want to be sure Jordan has another camp next year. "I'm glad I'm here because this might be his last one," Tim Hull, 13, of Glen Ellyn, said. "But I want to come back next year." Many of the campers say they want to see Jordan back and predicted he'll play for the Bulls next season. Except Kevin Daniele, 13, of Glen Ellyn. "I hope he leaves on top," Kevin said. "He won't be able to win another championship with the team he's got now." Dozens of fans also attended the camp for a glimpse of Jordan and for hope of some good news. "I feel like this is a once-in-a-lifetime chance to see him," said Pam Gontarek, an Addison woman. "These moments are fleeting, and we should grab the opportunity to see him while we can." Suzanne Klinetop-Chung, an Oak Brook mother of a 10-year-old camper, said she had to keep reminding herself to keep things in perspective. "It's so easy to get caught up in the fame of Michael Jordan and get upset if you don't get perfect pictures," she said. "You have to remember that your son is getting a wonderful experience with a terrific athlete. "That's what the camp is all about." headline: TRYOUTS HOPE TO PLAY JORDAN IN MOVIE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Enough Michael Jordan basketball sneakers juked, squeaked and traveled Tuesday in the muggy gymnasium at Maplewood-Richmond Heights High School to pay the Bulls superstar's salary for a season. OK, we're exaggerating. There aren't enough Nikes in the world . . . But more than 500 starry-eyed Jordan wannabes - sneaker-sliding, jersey-tugging, tongue-flashing, rim-crashing local players with a few outsiders thrown into the mix - turned out for a casting call there for the soon-to-be movie " Michael Jordan : An American Hero" by the Fox Family Channel, a cable and satellite television channel. Carlos Y. Love, a physical education teacher at Sumner High School, was bald head, bulging shoulders and broad smile above the competition. He also stood a Jordanesque 6 foot 6 inches. He played his high school basketball at Roosevelt. By noon, the Fox Family Channel's president and chief executive officer, Rich Cronin, had dubbed Love a "finalist." For years, Love said, friends and strangers have pointed out the resemblance. He didn't really see it himself, though, until he ran into Jordan at a Chicago airport. He remembers the day: Dec. 19, 1986. "He said I favored him," Love said. Love's likeness might have been uncannily like Jordan's. But even Love, 31, acknowledged he's no Michael Jordan . Jordan's high-wire walk is a tough act to follow. "My hang time isn't Michael Jordan 's hang time," said a smiling Love, who is also a sometime model, actor and fitness trainer. "But nobody's hang time is." The movie will trace Jordan's life and basketball career, from his early years in North Carolina to his glory years with the Bulls. Jordans will be cast for three phases of his life: as a boy, a teen-ager and a man. Twenty to 30 boys and men from here will receive call backs. The competition is stiff. Tryouts in Chicago and St. Louis will be followed by casting calls in Los Angeles, New York and Charlotte, N.C. The roles should be cast in September with production beginning in October. If all goes well, the movie may air in March. The people who win the roles have to be able to play basketball and look the part. And players of all shapes and sizes and colors have shown up. In Chicago, the tryout drew an Asian American. And some people try to exaggerate their height by scrunching up their shoulders, Cronin said. "But we're also looking for someone who has acting talent, charisma and personality," he said. "You can't fake that." Some would-be Jordans had already lined up by the time casting director Julie Ashton arrived at 6:30 a.m. at the gym in Maplewood. They came from Bellefontaine Neighbors and Berkeley, from Webster Groves and Washington Park, from Fairview Heights and Florissant. There were even players from Ann Arbor, Mich. and Indianapolis. As Fox Family Channel officials circulated, the young men traveled from stop to stop on the gymnasium floor, as proud parents watched from the bleachers. The players showed off their moves. But they also read some lines and had their pictures snapped. Donte Murphy, 11, was at ease in front of the camera. The handsome youngster traveled from Kansas City with his parents. He previously did a national commercial, he said, for computers. He thought he'd make a good young Jordan. Said Donte, who's going into the seventh grade: "First, I think I'm a perfect age. I'm 11 years old. Second, I just want to be like Mike." A few others appeared to be long shots. Courtney White, who works at the Bellefontaine Habilitation Center, was a stocky 5 feet, 8 1/2 inches. He hadn't shaved his head or his goatee. "But I'll cut it off if I have to," said the good-natured White, 27. Another longshot was Jordan Davis, 14, though he had a name working in his favor. Davis, of Florissant, had braces and glasses. But he removed the glasses, to read a few lines in front of a camera. "I think I could make a good Michael Jordan when he was younger," he said. "People say my dad looks like Michael Jordan ." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Cruising along the Information Superhighway, an Internet search of " Michael Jordan + videos + books" produces 2,927,557 sites referring to the world's best basketball player. Some Web sites are official, such as the Chicago Bulls' site. Others less so, including one from an infatuated hacker who spells his hero's name "Micheal Jorden." Regardless, nearly 3 million sites are related to MJ. And Howard Stern calls himself the King of All Media? Michael Jordan 's basketball prowess and superstar status; his dabbling in baseball, golf, movies, merchandising; his early retirement and the August 1993 murder of his father James have demanded detailed description. Research a library data bank and you'll find some 50 books about him. (The Internet address Amazon.com is very helpful, with mini-reviews and book availability.) Of course, this space doesn't allow complete discussion of the MJ catalog. And since Jordan is an influential role model to kids who want to "Be Like Mike" - aside from his willingness to lay a golf wager, perhaps - many of the titles are children's books. With some exception, they are standard biographies, written for youngsters ages 4-12. How his first love was baseball. How he got cut from his Laney High School basketball team in Wilmington, N.C., and with hard work and a more than 5-inch growth spurt he later made the team. His game-winning jump shot as a North Carolina freshman for the 1982 NCAA championship. His gold medal performances in the 1980 and 1992 Olympics. And, of course, his six NBA titles with the Bulls. Young fans can start with Matt Christopher's "On the Court with Michael Jordan (Little Brown and Co., 1996). Amazon dubs Christopher "the number one sports writer for kids" with an "easy-to-read style ..." "On the Court" is intended for readers 9-12. An anonymous Amazon customer reviewer from Wheaton was impressed with Jordan's resilience following his father's death: "It tells you to go for your dreams and never give up even if something bad happens in your life." One interesting note about Sean Dolan's 64-page " Michael Jordan ," out of the " Basketball Legends" series (Chelsea House Publishing, 1994) is that it contains a "poignant introduction" by Coretta Scott King, wife of the late Dr. Martin Luther King." New York sportswriter Robert Lipsyte's " Michael Jordan : A Life Above the Rim" (Harpercrest, 1994) is a basic biography that also addresses basketball 's history and African-Americans' integration into it. It contains stories from one of MJ's best friends, former North Carolina teammate Buzz Peterson, and "info on Mike's new baseball career." Kirkus Reviews states: "Lipsyte provides not merely the facts but a sophisticated social and historical analysis of the game of basketball and Jordan's place in it." Geared for the young sports card collector is "Beckett Great Sports Heroes: Michael Jordan " (Dr. James Beckett, 1995), from the trading-card guide people. Along with biographical articles following his life and career (including articles by Daily Herald basketball writer Kent McDill) are a comprehensive listing of all of Jordan's basketball cards. His prized 1984-85 Star Extended Rookie card was listed at $ 2,500 when this was printed. Jordan himself wrote "I Can't Accept Not Trying" (Harper, San Francisco, 1994), a brief book (36 pages) that gives his viewpoints on goals, commitment, fears, teamwork, fundamentals and leadership. An Amazon customer comments, "Because this short volume is not meant to be a tribute to Michael Jordan , it is." A creative tribute is Eloise Greenfield's "For the Love of the Game: Michael Jordan and Me" (Harper Collins Juvenile Books, 1997). For kids ages 4-8, it's a 32-page extended poem "about heroes and human potential" accompanied by Jan Spivey Gilchrist's watercolor paintings and "narrated" by two African-American children. Booklist reviewer Susan Dove Lempke says, "This book will set children soaring." On to books for adults. Jordan had been soaring for seven years in the NBA when Chicago basketball writer Sam Smith came out with the controversial "The Jordan Rules: The Inside Story of a Turbulent Season with Michael Jordan and the Chicago Bulls" (Simon & Schuster, 1992). Not about former Detroit Pistons coach Chuck Daly's defensive strategy to clog the lane against Jordan, Smith instead offered a view of "Michael and the Jordanaires" during the Bulls' 1990-91 championship season. It pictured a selfish Jordan who ordered teammates not to pass the ball to center Bill Cartwright in crunch time; who didn't lift weights with the team because others could lift more; and who complained that Tex Winter's Triple-post offense didn't give him enough shots. "It's easy to come away from this season wondering how such a disparate bunch could win a title," summarized Smith, who followed up four years later with "Second Coming: The Strange Odyssey of Michael Jordan

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan has said time and time again that he reserves the right to change his mind. And he has proven on myriad occasions that what he says Wednesday doesn't mean a thing Thursday. But here is the most frightening proof yet that Jordan has thrown in the NBA towel: A high-ranking Nike official, speaking on the condition that he not be identified, told us Wednesday that top company executives were informed by Jordan around Christmas that they should not include him in their NBA strategy for 1999. He told them that he was done playing basketball , so Nike has little or nothing planned for Jordan in terms of basketball production this year. And this is the most clear-cut evidence yet that Jordan is a goner. But understand that none of this means anything once the Bulls' offer Jordan the maximum allowed by NBA law, $ 34.7 million, which translates into $ 22 million for a 52-game schedule. We still believe that if you place $ 22 million on a table in front of MJ and ask him to play three months of basketball , he's going to think seriously about taking it. So what if he's out of shape? He can play his way back into game condition under game conditions, while Scottie Pippen and Dennis Rodman and the best of the rest do the same. Sure, Indiana may run away with the regular-season title, but don't bet against Jordan Inc. once the playoffs begin. Yes, Bulls fans, it sounds very bad today. But Michael Jordan shifts about as often as the weather in Chicago, so if you don't like what you've heard, just wait five minutes. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Yes, the NBA has ended its lockout and is ready to begin its season next month. But there's just one order of business several young Geneva basketball players were concerned about Saturday: Is Michael Jordan coming back? About 160 young basketball players ventured to Western Avenue School Saturday afternoon to try out for the Geneva Park District's boys basketball league. Many said their dissatisfaction with the lockout would not deter their interest in professional basketball , but that might change if Jordan is not on the Bulls roster. "It matters because Michael Jordan is one of the best players in the league," said Tommy Evans, 8. "I would want to watch because Michael Jordan has all of these good shots." It is possible that free agents Scottie Pippen and Dennis Rodman might leave the Bulls, and Jordan's departure would be the last straw, the youths said. "They'll go from first to worst," said Brendan Cagney, 9. "They're losing all of their best players." Thomas Heffernan, 8, said he might keep watching NBA games, but he would not cheer for the Bulls if the team does not hang on to Jordan. The third- and fourth-graders also offered varying opinions about the recently ended lockout. "They're getting too much money," Elliott Bernhagen, 10, said. "I'd be happy if I was good and if I had fans and if I could get along in life, paying my bills, taxes and stuff." Others were not too upset about the shortened season, such as 8-year-old Ryan Hogan, who just kept busy with other activities so he did not really miss the Bulls. Some youths said they would continue watching with or without Jordan. "I like basketball ," said Bobby Fortier, 9. "The games get exciting." At least one young basketball fan would prefer Jordan retire. "I don't like Michael Jordan ," Tim Bozek said. The 9-year-old said he prefers to cheer for the Miami Heat, the Orlando Magic and the Phoenix Suns. "I don't care if the (Suns) take Pippen, just not Michael Jordan ." It's Your Turn. Let us know what you think. Our reader response line is (630) 587-8686. Be sure to leave your name, town and phone number. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 No one was happier with the salvation of the National Basketball Association season last week than Michael Jordan . No, not the world's greatest hoopster. The Michael Jordan who averages 17 points per game for the University of Pennsylvania, a very mortal No. 23 who, in the absence of the N.B.A., became fodder for America's sports announcers. "Maybe they'll stop killing me with the jokes," Jordan said on the telephone before Penn played Yale in an Ivy League game Friday. "One night I hear about a guy on ESPN starting his highlights, 'Jordan . . . from the top of the key . . . . airball.' With no N.B.A., when you're in a little slump and your name is Michael Jordan , the whole world knows." The professional players and owners finally reached agreement on Wednesday after an all-night bargaining session Tuesday night, in a deal that promises labor peace into the next century with the league having achieved its main goal of containing the contracts of high-salaried players. No one knows if Chicago's Michael Jordan will retire or not. As for Penn's Michael Jordan , the accidental lockout casualty from Philadelphia whose mother named him after her favorite cousin, he's elated at the N.B.A.'s return. "Not that I needed incentive to go out and perform well before, but without the jokes, maybe it will be easier." headline: AIR ALERT: MIKE'S LEAVING
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan , whose tremendous talent, fierce desire and enduring popularity made him the greatest player and biggest draw in basketball , is retiring from the sport he ruled for more than a decade. The 35-year-old hoops legend, who has talked of quitting since winning his sixth championship with the Chicago Bulls last year, is set to break the hearts of basketball fans around the nation with an announcement tomorrow, a source told the Daily News. Jordan's agent, David Falk, last night refused to confirm or deny a stampede of reports that the Bulls superstar will never step onto the court as a player again. "Until he announces whether he is retiring or returning, anything else is speculation," Falk told The Associated Press. Jordan had been in the Bahamas on vacation, but returned this week to Chicago, apparently in preparation for his announcement, which is sure to shake the sports world. News of Jordan's plans to hang up his sneakers while still near the top of his game followed last week's end of a six-month lockout by NBA owners that wiped out more than a third of the season. With a mini 50-game season set to begin early next month, team owners were counting on Jordan's return to help them rebuild the image of the game, which was tarnished by the big-money labor dispute. Despite his plans to quit, Jordan's impact will be felt for years to come on the court and in the record books. During his storied career, the Brooklyn-born basketball great won the MVP award five times, while leading the league in scoring 10 times and averaging 31.5 points a game. His retirement will mark the second time Jordan has quit in five years. In October 1993, he left basketball after the slaying of his father, saying he wanted to spend more time with his family. Instead, he tried his hand at playing minor-league baseball for the Double-A Birmingham Barons, a Chicago White Sox farm team, before giving up the diamond for the more familiar hard court in 1995. Fans around New York last night said they would miss Jordan, who brought a sense of style and excitement that the NBA has never seen. Hassoni Stanley, 26, of Manhattan, said she was grateful for the years of basketball memories No. 23 gave with the Bulls and in college at North Carolina. "I think he picked the perfect time," she said. "He's at the top of his career, and he doesn't want to wear it out." "Good for him," Kevin Pellon, 26, of Manhattan piped in. "He's done it all." But Francisco Gomez, 24, disagreed and predicted that the competitive Jordan would soon be back. "I think he's too good to retire," he said. "He has too much passion for the game. When he's at home watching those guys play his game, he's gonna go crazy. " Michael Jordan is the Jimi Hendrix of basketball he plays from his heart." Chicago native Tony Ayala, 33, noted that Jordan's departure could turn away fans. "The lockout already hurt the game," Ayala said. "This is going to hurt worse. Just by himself he sold a lot of tickets." "Nobody is in the wings to replace him," agreed Alex Gregg, 51, an upper East Side sports memorabilia dealer. "He will be missed. His appeal is across-the-board." Some fans, like Rick Robinson, recalled seeing Jordan in action at Madison Square Garden, where he would always turn up his game a couple of notches while playing the rival Knicks. "When Michael Jordan was on the court, he raised the level of play of the other nine men playing," said Robinson, 41. "He was and is a champion. "He is leaving the game the way you should with grace," he added. "This is the end of an era." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Just call them Generation MJ. After all, today's high school students virtually grew up with basketball icon Michael Jordan . They were entering kindergarten about the time he was starting his first year of professional basketball with the Bulls. They cheered him on to three NBA championships before they were teenagers. And when they were enduring confusing times in middle school, he was having struggles of his own - coping with the murder of his father and deciding to retire from basketball . He returned just as they were preparing for high school - giving them more inspiration and three more championships. As those students - now seniors - prepare for the next phase of their lives, it appears their role model is too. Two Mid-Suburban League basketball players said they won't be surprised if Jordan announces his retirement today. In fact, they said it probably is time and that he probably wouldn't maintain his current success. Karen Hagberg's earliest memory of Michael Jordan was when she was about 5. Her then-10-year-old sister brought home a huge poster with his picture on it. "My sister (still) is completely obsessed with that man," she said. "You walk into her room and it's a shrine to him." Hagberg, a 17-year-old center on Palatine High School's girls varsity basketball team, says her whole generation is pretty wild about MJ. "People look up to him," she said. "He's an awesome athlete, he's a good person and he's really cute. That helps with the girls." Rob Garnes, a senior at Rolling Meadows High School, said he's been impressed with Jordan since he saw him in his first slam-dunk contest. Jordan didn't win but his style won points with Garnes and his friends. "I like and respect him as a role model," Garnes said. "He brought basketball to a new level. People who were never interested in basketball now are into the game because of him." Both Hagberg and Garnes said their coaches often use Jordan to inspire the team, telling them they need leaders like MJ or that it takes commitment and determination to have the success Michael Jordan has. "He's always been someone I very much admired," Hagberg said. "He has a way of drawing us in." Who else can get a whole generation to pick up a book? Hagberg and Garnes - and most of their friends - are now reading "For the Love of the Game" by Michael Jordan , of course. What makes him so appealing, Hagberg said, is he's not one-dimensional, and in his classy way he has the ability to help people believe in their dreams. He also seems to bring a bit of luck, at least in Garnes' mind. Garnes touches a picture of Michael Jordan and Scottie Pippen before each basketball game. That ritual may have contributed to Garnes increasing his scoring average to 21.5 points a game. And even after Michael hangs up his shoes, Garnes will still be sporting Air Jordans. "Everyone who puts on a pair of his shoes thinks they're Michael Jordan ," he said. "Well, at least they make you feel a little bit special." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Sports marketers know one thing for sure: There is no "next" Michael Jordan sitting in reserve on Madison Avenue's endorsement bench. No other athlete comes close to packing Jordan's talent on the court and marketing charisma off of it. With Jordan expected to officially announce his retirement from pro basketball today, sports marketers are speculating that the "next" Michael Jordan will have to be something even more than the best athlete with the most charisma. The next superstar who shines in Jordan's marketing light will likely have to break some sort of barrier. Or represent something that touches consumers far beyond the sports world. Simply being the greatest is a start. But sports marketers insist it isn't enough anymore. "Whoever wants to be the next Michael Jordan will have to come with lots of extra bells and whistles," says Lesa Ukman, president of IEG Sponsorship Report . "Being a winner just isn't enough." Even the biggest names in sports today -- baseball home-run hero Mark McGwire, golf sensation Tiger Woods and basketball star Grant Hill -- pale next to Jordan's marketing dynamism. Lon Rosen, who represents Earvin "Magic" Johnson, describes the intangible marketing quality that Jordan uniquely possesses as his "otherness." Lacking that, what qualities or peculiarities might be necessary to create the sports world's next marketing wonder? Here's what marketer gurus project: * Female phenom. It would be prime time for a female athlete to emerge as a player -- and then a superstar -- in an all-male sports league such as the National Basketball Association or Major League Baseball. "If a woman broke that barrier, marketers would swarm her," says Cynthia Tripp, managing editor of the newsletter About Women & Marketing . * Global star. Picture this: a superstar, multilingual athlete who plays in the pro leagues in more than one country. Maybe a soccer star. Or a basketball idol. "The next mega-star may have to transcend international borders," says Jim Andrews, editor of IEG Endorsement Insider . * Young achiever. Sports fans are accustomed to 13- or 14-year-old tennis or skating stars. But what if a youngster in his early teens emerged as a pro baseball, basketball or even football star? Sports marketers say it could make Madison Avenue drool. * Tri-sport. In Bo Jackson and Deion Sanders, American sports fans have been treated to athletes who excel in pro football and baseball. But what if some super athlete emerged who starred in three team sports? And what if one of those sports was basketball ? That could make Madison Avenue buzz, says Sean Brenner, editor of Team Marketing Report . * Celebrity switch. Jordan has proven that a great athlete also can excel on TV and in Hollywood. But what about the other way around? What if a hot film star or rock star laced up his Nikes only to discover that he could be a superstar in a professional sport? * Jordan's genes. Perhaps the next Michael Jordan will have to be a Jordan offspring. Baseball superstar Ken Griffey Jr. has clearly topped his father's baseball accomplishments and earned his marketing wings. Jordan has two sons: 10-year-old Marcus and 12-year-old Jeffrey. Maybe one of them will reach for Dad's star. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Hearing that you'll never see Michael Jordan play competitive basketball again is like hearing that sunsets have been cancelled. That star-filled skies have been revoked. That babies are no longer allowed to smile. Hearing that Michael Jordan is going to retire today, no matter how much you expected it or prepared for it, is like taking a bounce pass to the chest. Los Angeles Times You are in awe of a guy for 16 years, from the tight-shorts days in North Carolina to the baggy-swagger years in Chicago, and yet when he quits, all you can do is gasp and utter one word. No. Not now. Not when the NBA is hitting the road three months late with a junker season sputtering with greed. Not when the only thing that can make us remember why basketball is so great is its greatest player ever. Not when he is leaving us with too many big mouths, small consciences, and Spree. The worst possible time for us. The best possible time for him. Michael Jordan leaves before soiling his $200 shoes with what will be the grubbiest season in NBA history. He leaves before scratching his image on a potentially mediocre Chicago Bulls team that probably even he could not have rescued into respectability. He leaves not with his head down, but with his hand in the sky. His right hand. The one that hung in the Salt Lake City air last spring at the completion of the 17-foot jump shot with 5.2 seconds remaining that gave the Bulls the victory that clinched Jordan's sixth NBA championship. Remember how he kept his hand out and his arm extended after the shot, calmly holding it there while all history erupted around him? Like he wanted children to learn from it, and the rest of us to remember it? As with darn near everything else he tried for 16 seasons at North Carolina and the NBA, it worked. His hand is hanging there still. It was a moment become a sculpture. With apologies to Ted Williams, it was perhaps the best last moment in the history of sports. Williams quit on a home run, but it didn't win a World Series. Michael Jordan didn't retire at the top. He retired while dancing across it. In that one second Jordan taught us much about grace, the last in a long line of lessons. By being cut from the high school basketball team, he offered a story of perseverance that is still being related to schoolkids today. By making the shot that won the national championship for North Carolina at the end of a quiet freshman season, he taught us about patience. How many great college players today would be deferential enough to sit so quietly during their first year, that the public would never even think about them until they hit the biggest shot in the biggest game of the season? With his 26 game-winning shots in the final seconds of NBA games, he consistently taught us about handling yourself under pressure. Then when he quit the game for most of two seasons between 1993 and 1995, he taught us even more. We've all heard the reports. He quit because the NBA was pressuring him to stop gambling. He quit before the NBA threw him out. Whatever the reason, instead of sticking around to fight his demons in what could have been an ugly battle, he simply walked away until they died a natural death. It was as classy as his baseball career was silly, but there was even a lesson in that. If you really want to do something, do it even if it others might think you are making a fool of yourself. Look at Michael Jordan . He did. His return to the NBA after that brief sabbatical was accompanied by one of the most famous faxes in sports history, the two-word missive that read, "I'm back." He hadn't lost his sense of the dramatic. He continued to prove that for three more full seasons, all championships. Even during the NBA lockout, he showed it. He attended one meeting when the players' case was still strong, but disappeared when they lost all leverage. And when the lockout was finally settled and the players were running around the streets of New York in abominable snowsuits with outrageous quotes? Michael Jordan is in the Bahamas, spokesmen said. Vacationing with family, reports said. Spotted playing golf, sources confirmed. Far away from the muck. Figuring out his future where a decent man should figure out his future, with family and peace. You know what I thought when I heard this? You really want to know? I thought, Michael Jordan is fooling us again. He is paying somebody to claim he is on vacation. He has hired a look-alike to sign his name at the golf shop. He is in the Bahamas, all right. He is jogging around the Bahamas. He is lifting weights on the beach, and running stairs at an old church, and playing hoops in a sweaty gym with nine hardened beach bums. He is planning on showing up in Chicago on Jan. 18, the first day that players can be signed, and announce that he is ready for one more year. Then, just like that, he isn't. How did you hear the news? I heard it late Monda

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan might be gone from the basketball arena. But he certainly isn't gone from the endorsement arena. His endorsement career, which earns him an estimated $ 40 million annually, even might temporarily jump with his announced retirement, sports marketers say. Major marketers from Nike to Hanes to Gatorade all are expected to feature Jordan in new ads that celebrate his accomplishments and cozy up to the public's anticipated interest in his announced departure. "Fifty years from now, heck, 50 years after he's dead, Michael Jordan will still be moving products," says Brian Murphy, publisher of Sports Marketing Letter. "He is the single most effective sports endorser of all time." And the single most wealthy. By Murphy's estimates, Jordan already has a total sports marketing income of nearly $ 375 million. That figure could double to $ 750 million over his lifetime, Murphy says. No athlete -- not Arnold Palmer, not Joe Montana, not Jack Nicklaus -- can come within shooting distance of that mark. Jordan not only reinvented basketball , he played a pivotal role in the invention of big-time sports marketing -- now a $ 250 billion annual industry. "He made sports marketing an easy sell," says David Carter, author of Keeping Score: An Insider's Look at Sports Marketing. "He's 100 miles ahead of every other athlete." With Jordan's retirement, however, an era of unbridled sports marketing money likely has run its last lap. "Everyone wants a Michael Jordan ," Murphy says, "but of the three billion people on this planet, the total number of Michael Jordans out there is one." With the possible exception of Muhammad Ali, Jordan is the most recognized athlete in the world, says Kevin Poston, president of Professional Sports Planning. Of all marketers, perhaps none will miss Jordan's daily appearance on the basketball court more than Nike, says Dave Zumbach, Reebok's vice president of U.S. marketing. "A lot of folks sitting in Beaverton, Ore., have to be wondering what's next?" Nike's stock price dropped 2 3/8 Tuesday to $ 42 a share. The market as a whole was down 145.21 points. Indeed, Jordan, who has earned an estimated $ 16 million from Nike over the last year, has a multiyear deal with Nike that stretches well into the next decade. But even something as seemingly simple as a shoe contract can get complex when Jordan is involved. Earlier this year, Jordan filmed a TV spot for AMF Bowling Centers. After some negotiations, he agreed to appear in bowling garb. But he refused to wear bowling shoes because Nike doesn't make any. After some negotiations, Jordan finally bowled for the cameras in a pair of black shoes made by a Nike division. "Mike made it very, very clear that he wears just one shoe brand on camera," says Merrell Wreden, vice president of marketing at AMF. "And that's Nike." headline: REMEMBERING A SPECIAL PLAYER
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 A perfect physical specimen. Impeccable fundamentals. A man whose competitiveness can't be quantified. Ever try to explain Michael Jordan to someone who wasn't a fan? Jordan exposes the flaws in words like they were so many NBA defenders. Still, words are all that's left. Here's what the sports world has to say: JERRY WEST, Hall of Famer: "This is a man who truly, as far as I'm concerned, is the modern-day Babe Ruth." WAYNE GRETZKY, Hockey's Jordan: "When you grow up, everyone has idols. You've got guys like Mickey Mantle and, of course, the Gordie Howes and the Michael Jordans, and you wonder, Is there anybody who is ever going to come along whom your grandkids or your kids can emulate or use as role models? You don't think somebody can come along and capture you again. But they do, and that's what makes sports so great. "You don't replace Michael Jordan . But somebody will come along and our kids will say, 'This guy is something special.' And we'll be arguing, 'Yeah, but he's no Michael Jordan .' " JAYSON WILLIAMS, Nets: " Michael Jordan is like, respectfully, Mark McGwire five times over. Mark McGwire did it for one year. Michael Jordan did it for 12 years." JOE DUMARS, Detroit Pistons: "There is no replacement for Michael Jordan . I don't see anybody replacing him. And no one should try and carry the torch." GRANT HILL, Pistons: "It was great to compete against him. It was great to watch him play. We are going to miss him, but he is leaving on his terms. He is leaving with championships. He is leaving with a ton of accomplishments. But the NBA will be all right. I think the league will survive without a dominant, Michael Jordan -type player." RONY SEIKALY, Nets: "I was very disappointed because the league needs him more than ever right now. To see him leave basketball , as an opponent it's great because it gives us a chance, because we don't always have to look at Michael and dream about having to face Michael. But as a fan, personally, I hate to see him leave." JOHN CALIPARI, Nets coach: "You're losing not only the guy that everyone in the world wants to see play basketball , but you're also losing that air of: You compete every practice. This isn't about money. This is about winning championships. (You lose) the competitiveness and the drive to perfection which is something every other player can look to." LLOYD DANIELS, NBA free agent: "He's leaving? That's a job for me right there." BRIAN LEETCH, Rangers: "He'll be missed, obviously. But, hey, he's probably got peace of mind. Everybody has a different way of making themselves happy. He's worked hard and done so much not only for this sport but for all of sports, and he's probably going to have fun now and play some golf." BONZI WELLS, Pistons rookie: "(Jordan's retirement) probably kept me from getting embarrassed on the court, although I would have liked to have been on the same court as him." LARRY BIRD, Hall of Famer and Pacers coach (smiling): "We want to wish he and his family well and we look forward to seeing him some time in late March when he makes his return again." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 If today is indeed the end, it's impossible to not flash back to favorite snapshots of Michael Jordan 's career. Like, I was there in Chicago Stadium when he made the first basket of the first game of his first Bulls season and in the Delta Center when he made the final shot of the final game of his final NBA Finals. In between were the slam-dunk victory during the 1988 all-star weekend, the change of hands while in full flight against the Lakers, the 3-point-basket spree against the Blazers, the night in Utah he was sick, and countless others. Yes, all were a privilege to witness in person; no, none represents my most enduring impression of Jordan. That came in the oddest of places - Sarasota rather than Chicago, baseball rather than basketball , sea level rather than air space. This was February 1994, the year Jordan sat out the entire basketball season during his first retirement. Instead of being a basketball icon, he was a 31-year-old outfield prospect in the White Sox training camp. The United Center and Chicago Stadium were a lifetime away. So were the crowds of 20,000 adoring fans. Harsh winter sunsets were replaced by harsher spring realities. It was 7 a.m. Eastern time. Hardly any media were awake at the time. I recall writing in a notepad, "There are no circuses at 7 o'clock in the morning." His Royal Airness had given way to Just Plain Michael. The entertainer had given way to the athlete. The Natural had given way to The Unlikely. Jordan was in the batting cage working with White Sox hitting instructor Walt Hriniak. They were repeating the basics, ball after ball, swing after swing, time after time. The teacher was meticulous, the process monotonous, the student methodical. I grew tired and frustrated just watching them attempt the impossible. What seemed to come so fluidly to Jordan in basketball was coming so robotically in baseball. It would've been the perfect time for him to throw down the bat, curse the heavens and retreat to the golf course on the way back to the hardcourt. But Jordan was just keeping on, piling sweat upon perspiration and blister upon callous. He just kept asking questions, absorbing knowledge and applying it the best he could. Michael Jordan was being Michael Jordan , except this was the one we rarely could see after the curtain dropped for practice at the Multiplex or Berto Center. Jordan wasn't born to become what he became. Oh, sure, he was blessed with the physical ability to create human flight patterns, to be extraordinarily agile at his size, to have remarkable instincts for the game but it takes so much more than just genes to transform great gifts into the greatest basketball player ever. The total package requires the physical ability to go with the athletic intelligence to go with superhuman competitiveness to go with impeccable work habits to go with unwavering concentration to go with the fear of failure to go with the desire for success to go with the passion for money to go with a tolerance for the distractions that accompany being the best. Combine them and you have what I saw that February morning in 1994. There Jordan was, a multimillionaire basketball player putting on a baseball uniform, carrying a bat into a cage at 7 a.m. and embracing instruction like a high school sophomore. Genius isn't necessarily portable. Einstein couldn't be counted on to play a mean harmonica. Hemingway couldn't be expected to hang spoons very well. Jordan couldn't automatically hit a curveball like he did a jump shot. But Jordan always could try harder than anybody else, which is what defined him as an athlete whether on the hardcourt or in the batting cage. If the Michael Jordan era is over, that's what I'll remember most about him. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 In the wake of Michael Jordan 's retirement, Citrus retirees offer their take on life after the game ends. There's one thing about Michael Jordan 's retirement from professional basketball that his fellow retirees in Citrus County can agree on: All of them would have liked to end their careers with the kind of nest egg that the departed Chicago Bulls star has stockpiled. "Yeah, I wouldn't mind retiring with what he's retiring with," said Henry Langley, who retired to Inverness after a 37-year career at Kingsbury Machine and Tool in Massachusetts. Langley was just one of several retirees musing on Jordan's retirement over libations in the clubhouse of the Inverness Golf and Country Club on Wednesday afternoon. Most of the retirees at the club weren't surprised by Jordan's announcement, made at a nationally televised news conference. And none of them had quite the same send-off when they ended their careers. "It wasn't the same," Langley said with a chuckle. "We had a party and I got a few mementos." Langley's friend Dave Pollardtook a far different approach five years ago when he worked his last shift at the Westinghouse plant in Lima, Ohio. "I didn't want a party," he said. "Most retirement parties are like a funeral. We had a cake, and about 60 or 70 people came in and said their goodbyes." For the first time, many retirees in Citrus County have something in common with perhaps the most recognizable sports figure in the world. RETIREJohn Clark, a player at the Beverly Hills Horseshoe Club, seemed glad that Jordan tossed 3-point shots instead of horse shoes. "If he ever played horseshoes, he'd beat the hell out of everyone," Clark said. So, what advice do Citrus County retirees have for the newest member of the ranks of retired workers? Watch your pennies? Clip coupons to make that pension go a bit further? The most common advice to the 35-year-old superstar was simply to stay active. Willie Eldridge, who retired last year after a lengthy career as principal of Inverness Middle School, said Jordan should "enjoy life - you're closer than you think" to the final curtain. Longtime retiree Bob Traugott offered these sage words: Move to Sugarmill Woods. "He can live on the golf course," he said, adding, "we'll put up basketball hoops on every driveway."+++John Clark, pitching horseshoes at the Beverly Hills Horseshoe Club Wednesday, has been retired for 10 years. Speaking on Michael Jordan 's retirement, Clark said, "If he ever played horseshoes, he'd beat the hell out of everyone.He was paid well to do a job and he did it well. I have a lot of respect for him."A patron of Coach's Pub & Eatery eats lunch while watching the televised press conference of Michael Jordan 's announcement of his retirement from profession basketball . Jordan said he is leaving the game to attend to the challenge of raising his children. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 At 9:02 a.m. Wednesday, the announcement everybody dreaded was made official. "I am here to announce my retirement from the game of basketball ." Michael Jordan , the man who single-handedly took the National Basketball Association to a level that was never seen by an American sport, will no longer be gracing arenas with his court presence. The questions surrounding the Michael Jordan mystery have finally been answered, though unfortunately it was an answer that none of us wanted to hear. U. California-Los Angeles His Airness has left the building. At a time when the NBA needs No. 23 the most, his refusal to save a league that leaned on him like Tito and Jermaine leaned on another Michael is to be commended. Though ESPN, the L.A. Times, every media outlet and even good friend Charles Barkley warned us all, it was still a shock. Jordan had nothing left to prove and simply could not compete against himself anymore. That's a shame for anyone who has ever seen Jordan set foot on the hardwood. Jordan was the premiere reason to watch the NBA. Even young NBA hoopsters with a seemingly "me-only" attitude toward the game are paying homage to Michael. Baller and part-time thug Allen Iverson said, "I didn't want to play this game until I saw Michael Jordan ." Since Jordan took the torch from Magic Johnson and Larry Bird and made it burn 10 times brighter, Mike has brought smiles to countless faces, including mine. I never thought that I could like a basketball player more than Magic Johnson. Growing up in Los Angeles while "Showtime" was at its peak, Magic was the man who made the Lakers run. He was the reason I fell in love with basketball , and he was the reason that I watched the NBA. But then Magic retired and Jordan replaced him. I take that back - he did more than replace Magic - he took the electricity of the game to a level reserved for papal visits. And he did it night after night in whatever arena he would play. Watching Jordan play is something indescribable. It's like watching Mozart compose a symphony or Da Vinci creating a new work of art. Michael Jordan is the best ever at what he does. When God created Michael Jordan , he created the perfect basketball player. From his days as a scrawny, quiet, Adidas-wearing hoopster at North Carolina University to a cigar puffing, effervescent yet ultra competitive dominator for the Bulls, Jordan was always the man. His legend began when he sank the game-winning shot for the Tarheels to defeat Georgetown and win a national title. His career came to a close in the same fashion - hitting the game-winning shot in game six of the NBA Finals against the Utah Jazz last spring. Jordan was always doing everything that could be done on a basketball court. He retires as the third-leading scorer in the history of the league. He had 10 scoring titles, five most valuable player awards, 11 all-star appearances, nine all-defensive first team appearances, and most importantly, six national titles. No other player has a resume with half of the accolades that Jordan earned. The knock on Jordan as a youngster was that he was a ball-hog, a selfish player who could not win titles by himself. After leading the league in scoring as a rookie, he poured in 63 points in a playoff game against the Larry Bird-led Celtics in the 1985-86 playoffs. The Bulls, however, lost and the critics said that he took too many shots. For the next four years, the league belonged to Los Angeles, Boston and Detroit. But as soon as the Bad Boy Piston era culminated, Jordan learned that his amazing physical game needed to be complemented by some sort of mental edge. He came into the 1990-91 season with a more team-oriented concept and soared by the Lakers' A.C. Green and Sam Perkins, switching hands in the air, and laying in one of the most memorable shots in history, picking up a ring and a legend. What Jordan was also picking up was the hopes and dreams of fans across the world. With the help of Nike, Gatorade, Hanes, Hebrew National, Rayovac, McDonald's and numerous other companies, Michael Jordan was quickly becoming the most famous man in the world. And he handled it with grace and dignity. He could easily have resented the constant attention and harassment he received, but he chose not to. He epitomized the way that an athlete should handle himself. Michael Jordan earned in excess of $ 47 million in 1998 from endorsements and Forbes estimated that he has been worth $ 10 billion to the world economy since the beginning of his playing career. Through it all, Jordan has been classy and accessible to the public. Even with allegations of off-court gambling, Michael came out of it shining like the sun in the Sahara Desert. It just showed that the competitive thirst of the man could never be quenched. Perhaps the only thing he didn't dominate in his tremendous career occurred when he first retired in 1994. He decided to do somethin

headline: RAPTOR GM GRUNWALD JOINS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Retired superstar 'the poster boy' for Canadian fans SPORTS REPORTER The accolades were flowing for the man many believe carried the fortunes of the NBA on his shoulders from the time he entered the league in 1984. Raptors GM Glen Grunwald yesterday called Michael Jordan the ''poster boy'' for the NBA, especially for Canadians. ''When everyone saw Michael Jordan they identified the NBA with him. Even if you weren't a basketball fan and didn't know much about (it) and you hadn't seen an NBA game, when you heard the name Michael Jordan you knew who that was,'' Grunwald told reporters at the Raptors office in Toronto. ''You knew that he represented a type of excellence that maybe doesn't exist in all sports all the time.'' The man believed to be among the greatest ever to play the game announced his retirement yesterday, leaving many to wonder what the future holds for the league this year and in the future. Grunwald compared Jordan to NHL star Wayne Gretzky in terms of helping elevate interest in the game. But Grunwald insisted that the loss of Jordan won't spell doom and gloom in terms of fan interest in the Raptors and Vancouver Grizzlies. ''We've got a foothold in Canada and basketball has been present in Canada for a long time and I think we're well under way here. We don't need Michael Jordan to succeed in Canada.'' With Jordan retiring, Miami Heat coach Pat Riley, who tried to vanquish the superstar as head of the New York Knicks and then in Miami, said it will gnaw at him knowing he never could beat the Jordan-led Chicago Bulls. ''When I lay my head down on the pillow at night, I'll know there was one guy I never had a chance to beat,'' Riley said outside the Heat's practice facility. ''It doesn't look like I'll get another chance.'' Since moving to the Eastern Conference in 1991, Riley's teams have been eliminated by the Bulls four times - twice with New York and twice with the Heat. In Washington, President Bill Clinton, his looming impeachment trial notwithstanding, went before prying cameras at the White House to say that he and his Chicago-born wife ''wish Michael Jordan well. We admire him, we like him very much and we thank him for years of thrilling exploits.'' NBA commissioner David Stern called yesterday a ''great day. The greatest basketball player has the chance to retire with grace and dignity.'' ''Michael is the most talented player I've ever seen,'' said retired Celtics great Larry Bird. ''Wilt (Chamberlain) was the most dominating, but Michael has the most talent in every aspect of the game, even defence,'' Bird added. ''All I can speak for is people in my era, and from what I've seen, he's definitely the greatest,'' said retired Los Angeles Lakers star Magic Johnson. ''He does everything well. He's great offensively and defensively,'' said Shaquille O'Neal of the Lakers. headline: WHAT THEY'RE SAYING ABOUT
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 "I thought of saying just two words: "I'm gone.' But I figured I owe the fans and certainly the media a little bit more than that. So that's one of my reasons for being here."- Michael Jordan "When I lay my head down on the pillow at night, I'll know there was one guy I never had a chance to beat. It doesn't look like I'll get another chance. That bothers me."- Miami Heat coach Pat Riley "I think it's appropriate that the sports fans around America take a day or two to ooh and ahh, and hold their breath again, and be glad again."- President Bill Clinton "The shattering retirement announcement will even drive Bill Clinton's sex scandals off America's front pages. If you thought boxer Mike Tyson, football legend Pele or racing driver Ayrton Senna were the most recognized names in the world - forget it."- The Sun newspaper, London "Jordan didn't just want to win, he wanted to crush you, take your will, humiliate you so that you wouldn't even bother to show up the next time. He studied every nugget of information about an opponent he could get his hands on."- Washington Post columnist Michael Wilbon "He never looked like he'd been sentenced by a judge to play basketball . I've seen too many athletes not go out atthe right time. They stay and struggle and lose some of the dignity they are entitled to."- former Bulls assistant coach Johnny Bach "For our own selfish reasons, we never wanted to see Michael Jordan retire." JORQUOTE.14- Former Bulls coach Phil Jackson "I'll miss him. But I won't miss guarding him or him guarding me."- Hawks guard Mookie Blaylock "This is a day that I hoped would never come. It has to be the toughest day in the history of the Chicago Bulls. It's a tough day for Chicago, it's a tough day for the NBA, it's a tough day for basketball fans all over the world and for Michael Jordan fans all over the world."- Bulls owner Jerry Reinsdorf "I disagree with Jerry (Reinsdorf). This is not a sad day. This is a great day, because the greatest basketball player in the history of the game is getting the opportunity to retire with the grace that described his play."- NBA commissioner David Stern "Jordan Retires! Shock Felt Around the World"- headline in Nikkan Sports, Tokyo "It'll be hard for him to watch games. headline: THE JORDAN BUSINESS EMPIRE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Don't cry for Michael Jordan . The basketball legend is leaving the NBA, but he will continue to score as one of the most sought-after pitchmen in sports. The Chicago Bulls star is the world's highest-paid athlete endorser, netting $ 50 million for touting such world-class companies as Nike, MCI WorldCom, Gatorade and McDonald's. "If you set out to create the perfect sports pitchman, you couldn't come up with anything better than Michael Jordan ," said Bob Williams, president of Burns Sports, a sports marketing firm. "The Michael Jordan brand remains extremely viable," he said. Commerce Secretary William Daley yesterday said Jordan has about a $ 10 billion affect on numerous arenas and the impact (of his resignation) is enormous. Jordan's contract with the Chicago Bulls paid him $ 33 million a year. He earns $ 33 million from commercial endorsements for such companies as Nike, MCI WorldCom, Gatorade, McDonald's and Hanes, making him the world's highest-paid sports pitchman. Thanks to Jordan's star power, the Warner Bros. movie "Space Jam" grossed $ 230 million Books based on Jordan's life, including blockbuster "Rare Air," have logged nearly $ 17 millionin sales. Michael Jordan cologne from glitzy designer Bijan has generated sweet revenues of $ 155 million The Bulls star recently launched his own line of footwear and apparel with long-time partner Nike. It's expected to bring in tens of millions. A Hanes line of Michael Jordan underwear, valued at $ 10 million a year, adds even more to the athlete's empire. Jordan has two restaurants, Michael Jordan 's The Steak House NYC, and another in Chicago, to put some more green into his pocket. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Basketball legend Michael Jordan confirmed his retirement from the game last night. Jordan, who will be 36 next month, ends his career as one of the most successful players in the sport's history. He led the Chicago Bulls to their sixth National Basketball Association title of the decade last season, having dropped extensive hints that it was his last year in the game. During the acrimonious six-month dispute between players and owners which has just ended, hopes rose that he would reconsider, but at an emotional news conference in Chicago, Jordan said he was hanging up his training shoes for good. "I thought about saying just two words - 'I'm gone' - but I thought I owed my fans a lot more than that," he said. Juanita, Jordan's wife, accompanied him for the announcement, along with Bulls owner Jerry Reinsdorf and National Basketball Association commissioner David Stern. Jordan said he looked forward to being a parent and would "live vicariously through my kids" whether or not they played basketball . "My life will take a change. Mentally, I'm exhausted, I don't feel I have a challenge. Physically, I feel great. This is a perfect time to walk away." But Reinsdorf admitted: "This is a day I hoped would never come. It's said for basketball fans all over the world." Jordan retired in 1993, but came back after an unsuccessful bid to play baseball. In addition to leading the Bulls to six NBA crowns, he won numerous Most Valuable Player awards and scoring titles and two Olympic gold medals, at the 1984 Los AngelesGame s and eight years later in Barcelona. His exit throws the Bulls' future into doubt, with stars such as Scottie Pippen and Dennis Rodman having said they would leave the club if Jordan quit. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 LONDON -- Michael Jordan and the National Basketball Association got the kind of world coverage Wednesday that American sports seldom receive outside North America. Jordan, the NBA's third-leading scorer of all time, announced his retirement from basketball on Wednesday. Associated Press And newspapers around the world reported -- and commented -- on the event. Several compared Jordan to such other universally known American exports as Coca-Cola or Mickey Mouse and noted his popularity -- particularly in places where his sport is hardly followed -- had nothing to do with basketball . "His popularity is unprecedented and without limit," said Italy's Gazzetta dello Sport in a front page editorial. "He's way better known than basketball itself because he goes beyond its limits: Jordan is myth, poetry." Jordan's retirement also made waves in China. "Flying Man Jordan is Coming Back to Earth," said the Beijing Morning Post's front page. Some papers likened the news of his retirement to the death of a close family member. One suggested Americans would rather keep Jordan and see U.S. President Bill Clinton leave. "America would willingly take Clinton's retirement in exchange for Jordan's," said Rome's La Repubblica newspaper. For a comparable retirement, you "have to go a long time back to when Pele was the god of soccer," said Australian National Basketball League general manager Bill Palmer. In countries like England, where the NBA has a tiny following, much of the coverage was about the millions Jordan has earned. Swiss papers took a similar approach and said he deserved every penny. In an era "when stock market coups and mergers allow the oddest of fellows to shamelessly amass fistfuls of dollars ...we don't begrudge the fact that Jordan has earned enough to buy a basketball stadium every day until the end of time," said a Swiss daily. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Here is a transcript of Bulls owner Jerry Reinsdorf's response after Michael Jordan announced his retirement Wednesday. "Well this a day that I think I hoped would never come. It has to be the toughest day in the history of the Chicago Bulls. It's a tough day for Chicago, it's a tough day for the NBA, it's a tough day for basketball fans all over the world and for Michael Jordan fans all over the world. Standing here in the United Center where Michael Jordan has given us so many wonderful moments, performances and championships it's hard to imagine games being played here without him. Michael is simply the best player who ever put on a basketball uniform. He has defined the Bulls, the city and the NBA for more than a decade. He will always represent a standard of excellence. His statue out in front of the building will greet everybody who ever enters this building again. When Michael retired in 1993, we retired his No. 23 and hung it in a position of prominence from the rafters. When Michael came back from baseball, he asked me to take it down. He felt funny about playing when his number was hanging up above and so we took it down. Today, Michael, we are returning your No. 23 to its rightful place of honor, where it will forever serve as a reminder of your dominance on this court and your dominance of this game. Someday, Michael, the White Sox might retire 45. And to my leader David Stern, the truth is that for what Michael's meant to the NBA, this number could very well be retired in every arena. Certainly, we in Chicago think that anybody who wears No. 23 with a basketball uniform would look like a pretender. Finally, Michael, I have one thing to present to you. For the last season, for the last game, for the last shot, I would like to present to you the 1998 world championship ring, which certainly means a lot to all of us. So, Michael, thank you very much for what you have meant to all of the world, there'll never be another one like you."
