fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Republicans haven't yet accused President Clinton of orchestrating Michael Jordan 's retirement to divert public attention from his impeachment trial, but it's certainly had that effect. The basketball star's exit has captured more public attention than the bombing of Iraq. And it has two things Americans love and can't possibly get from Washington's soap opera: an unsullied hero and a happy ending. Sure, there is sadness at the prospect of never seeing another performance of the greatest player in the history of basketball , perhaps the greatest in the history of team sports. But there is joy in seeing him go out on his own terms, still at the peak of his profession, with his last act the breathtaking winning shot that gave his Chicago Bulls their sixth title in his reign as lord of the championship rings. Jordan was in a better position to choose his own leave-taking than other athletes, many of whom hold on after their talents have eroded because they need the money or want to make one last attempt at an accomplishment that has eluded them. Michael has no shortage of money. He is an industry unto himself. His salary last year was more than $ 30 million. That's pocket change compared with what he makes from product endorsements. Estimates for that exceed $ 100 million. Nike, the sports shoe maker, reports that it sold more than $ 350 million in apparel bearing Jordan's name last year. The NBA, which rode Jordan's back to new heights as he displayed his acrobatic slam dunks around the country and around the world, will now be denied his star appeal as it tries to patch things up with fans, who are disenchanted after a six-month labor dispute closed the game down. The league's line so far is that things will really get exciting with Jordan gone because now everybody has a chance to win. Which is true. Jordan's competitiveness, intelligence and natural talent allowed him to dominate and capture all the sport's prizes. He stockpiled individual honors --- most valuable player, league's leading scorer, defensive player of the year --- while elevating the play of his teammates enough to beat teams that, player for player, were supposed to be better. Yes, Michael Jordan 's impact goes well beyond the sports arenas. But he introduced himself to the world on the court, and that is where he made us love him. In the end, what we will miss most are the unbelievable things he could do with a basketball in his hands and air beneath his feet. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MICHAEL'S TRYING to do what most of us, untouched by a divine talent, could never contemplate: he's trying to quit with his illusion of immortality intact. That feared defeat, that unwanted ending, that sad last act, is a vital part of the plot. It's also parallel to the narrative line we all live through our whole lives. We get to the end of things and we stick around for whatever is next. But that next story doesn't begin until you have an ending, a denouement, for the first. Michael is lopping off his tale's conclusion. Salon Magazine A BALLPLAYER, unlike an artist, cannot continue refining his art until the end of his life, and Jordan knew when the time had come to recognize that he had written the right ending for his career. He is going out at the top of his game, a champion who has nothing left to prove. Boston Globe HE IS arguably the world's most famous person, and not only famous but admired - a figure whose selling power extends far beyond sports. But his decision to leave the Chicago Bulls, who have pretty much owned the NBA championship in this decade, hardly came as a surprise. He'd said he didn't wish to play for any coach other than Phil Jackson, who has left the Bulls. And he can afford (at well over $ 70 million a year in salary and endorsements, boy, can he afford) to leave while still on top, making a dignified exit. The Washington Post THERE ARE no other Michael Jordans. This is an athlete whose thoughtful approach to fame offers the best guidance on what professional sports in general ought to be looking for. As Jordan so aptly demonstrated again Wednesday, the many aspirants to the throne of "His Airness" need to understand that it takes more than slam dunks to inspire lasting fan loyalty and admiration. LA Times JORDAN BECAME a potent symbol of sports merchandising. He rescued pro basketball from the doldrums and came to symbolize American culture that encircles the world. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MICHAEL Jordan must be the greatest basketballer of all time. Anyone who knows says so. There is no dissent, and so there is no doubt. That is, in itself, amazing. Imagine a sport without a "who's best" argument. Basketball , courtesy of Jordan, must be the only one, and is expected, now that he has retired, to stay that way. Again, amazing, even to consider that an individual has reached a peak in a sport that is never to be scaled again. I have no doubts either, not least because since Jordan almost every young athlete in almost every sport is measured for his or her vertical leap. Jordan astounded sports scientists, coaches and experts with his, so there had to be something in it. But I do recall some other doubts a while back when I wrote an innocent column questioning Jordan being voted "the most popular sportsperson in Australia". In Australia? Jordan didn't play basketball in Australia, didn't live here. Most popular in the world, OK, but not in Australia because he wasn't in Australia. So I nominated a few Australians instead, and a few South Australians. Well you wouldn't read about it. All hell broke loose. How dare I! Didn't I understand Jordan's greatness? Grown men who should have known better nearly cried. Yes, I did understand Jordan's greatness. It was so obvious as to be almost disconcerting. But what I did not understand, nor appreciate, was the blind acceptance of a different sport culture, foisted upon us. That is, the selling of Michael Jordan who didn't need to be sold, which turned out to be Michael Jordan selling American hamburgers and American sandshoes because he was Michael Jordan . Appreciate our own at home just as much, the contributors to our own sporting culture, was my argument. Certainly you would have to admit that of late we have done so on a State level, maybe a little too much. But my argument fell on deaf ears, which was my own private joke. It was Michael Jordan this, Michael Jordan that, by people who had never seen him play in the flesh, never been within a Chicago Bulls' roar of him. They were people who were chewed up by the American publicity machine, stripped by it of reason and perspective. Nothing has changed that view, not even the acknowledgment that if any athlete in the world deserved such hero-worship, Jordan did. But he is not and never will be the most popular sportsperson in Australia until, in his retirement, Michael Jordan comes here and plays basketball . Then, I agree with everyone else, we should be so lucky. headline: JORDAN EARNS VOTE AS THE BEST
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Jordan earns vote as the best Tracy Stockwell MUCH has been written and said about Michael Jordan and his career, following his retirement this week from basketball . He is often compared with Muhammad Ali, the self-proclaimed "Greatest". But is Michael the greatest athlete of all time? Better than Ali? No doubt they have both had and still have tremendous influence on sport not just in America, but worldwide. QNP Ali's influence, perhaps because of the times, was more at a social and political level. But even so, Ali was the media's dream with his quickness in and out of the ring. He was never short of something to say and his pre-fight rhymes and antics became as legendary as his boxing. Jordan, however, seemed more reserved in nature, except on the basketball court, and let his actions and image speak for themselves. Again, because of the timing of his career and the marketing of sport in the '80s and '90s, his image has been splashed all over publications and merchandise throughout the globe resulting in billions of dollars for the NBA, his sponsors and Air Jordan himself. But what is he really like? I remember him from a brief encounter at the 1984 Olympic Games in Los Angeles. One of my US swimming team-mates and I were attempting to hang a banner in the foyer of our accommodation in the Olympic Village and we weren't having much luck when much to our pleasure a young Michael Jordan and Patrick Ewing came to our rescue. Michael offered to help, commented on our sign and wished us good luck. Everyone knew he was a talent and was the third choice in the NBA draft that year. Two years later, I was in Indianapolis working for CBS television at the national swimming titles and we stayed in the same hotel as the Chicago Bulls had stayed the previous week during the NBA play-offs. A waitress saw the CBS sports logo and became animated while telling us how the highlight of her life had been serving Michael Jordan . I, like everyone else, followed Michael Jordan 's career, and I enjoyed watching him defy gravity, slam dunk with his tongue hanging out and glisten like chocolate while he won national championships, MVP awards and re-wrote the basketball record books. I think Michael Jordan is the greatest athlete of the 20th century, but I will always remember him as a polite and helpful 21-year-old Olympic team-mate. THUMBS UP to Australia's up and coming young tennis players Jelena Dokic, Alicia Molik and Lleyton Hewitt for their recent form. THUMBS DOWN to the corrupt members of the IOC and officials who were involved in bribery and to the ICC, who like the IOC, have been a little late in establishing or adhering to a code of conduct and guidelines that prevent corruption in the first place. headline: NOW MUST WIN WITH A PATCHWORK TEAM
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Tim Floyd can finally ditch those business cards with "director of basketball operations" on them and break out the real ones. Six months after he arrived in Chicago, the Bulls dropped the "in-waiting" tag and officially made Floyd their coach yesterday. "It's going to be a daunting task," he said. "I'm standing here in front of six trophies, you've got a Hall of Fame coach that you're replacing and the best basketball player in the world left your team two days ago." It'd be hard to find a job tougher than the one the former Iowa State coach is inheriting. Michael Jordan retired two days ago, Scottie Pippen is as good as gone and last season's championship team is all but dismantled. The Bulls have just four players under contract - Ron Harper, Toni Kukoc, Randy Brown and Keith Booth - with Harper the only starter. "I do recognize the tradition and I know there's a great deal at stake," he said. "But I'm looking forward to this task." Floyd, 44, has no NBA experience, but he does have a solid record as a college coach. Still, his appointment hardly came as a surprise. He'd been rumored to be Jackson's successor for the past two seasons. Floyd joined the Bulls in July, but the team made him director of basketball operations because Jordan swore he wouldn't play for anyone but Jackson. Floyd would be the Bulls' next coach, chairman Jerry Reinsdorf promised at the time, but only if Jackson turned the job down again when the lockout ended. Jackson did say no, and Jordan announced his retirement Wednesday. "He's stepping into the fire and he knows it," said Steve Kerr, one of Chicago's many free agents. "He's the coach who comes in when Michael Jordan leaves. I don't envy him. He's got a tough job ahead." Floyd said he's not worried about being seen as "the coach who ran Michael Jordan out of basketball ." "I would much preferred having a Michael Jordan on my team," Floyd said. "[But] somebody was going to have to be standing here today and moving into this position. I'm glad it's me." But with whom? Bulls General Manager Jerry Krause is trying to work out sign-and-trade deals for Pippen, Dennis Rodman and Luc Longley, sources said. Kerr still hasn't received an offer and doesn't expect one. "It's obvious that no matter what happens, we're not all going to be back, so we've gotten together a little bit and sort of recognized the fact that it's the end of something," Longley said. "And also thought about how great it's been. It has been. It's been a blast." Floyd hopes that will continue. "The same goal will remain in place, and that's to win a championship at some point," he said. "Whether it's a patchwork type of situation or whether it's a long-term process still remains to be seen. You just hope you can have a little fun along the way with that journey, regardless of what it is." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 With his retirement from basketball , the transformation of Michael Jordan from superstar athlete to venerable megabrand is complete. And Nike will be the first to take advantage of that. Starting this summer, Nike for the first time will extend the Jordan line of basketball shoes and apparel into other sports. The first product will be a cross-training shoe under the Jordan Brand, handled by Wieden & Kennedy, Portland, Ore. Baseball, football, running and tennis products also are being considered. ''Nike started as a running company and then grew into a number of other categories,'' said Larry Miller, president of the Jordan division at Nike. ''We think we have the same opportunity starting with basketball and expanding into other sports as a true sports brand.'' Life after Michael Jordan 's basketball career is as key an issue for Nike as it is for the National Basketball Association and its TV partners. Some 5% of all Nike revenue, about $370 million, comes from Mr. Jordan's line of basketball shoes and apparel. AN IMAGE TRANSCENDING SPORTS And like most of Mr. Jordan's business partners, Nike believes the superstar's brand image transcends sports and will be viable long after his basketball career has ended. ''We have the most recognized person in the world,'' Mr. Miller said. ''His appeal transcends sports, gender, race and age. We think that appeal is going to continue into the future.'' It will be a slightly different appeal, though. Nike expects to alter the ways in which it markets the Jordan Brand now that the Chicago Bull has hung up his uniform for the second -- and probably final -- time. ''We are looking at marketing more Michael the person,'' Mr. Miller said. ''Things like hard work and dedication, excellence and being prepared. It's not focusing on whether he can score 40 points -- it's the characteristics that made Michael able to score 40 points.'' Other marketing executives agree the Jordan brand has considerable staying power. ''His name as a brand will not only go well beyond his playing career but beyond his life span,'' said Tom George, senior VP-athlete marketing for sports marketing agency Advantage International. Some products, Mr. George said, won't perform as well over the long term. In particular, he pointed to licensed products such as Michael Jordan lunch boxes and backpacks, noting that fickle kids are constantly shifting their loyalties. BABY-BOOM LOYALTY Baby boomers are the demographic that most identifies with the 35-year-old Mr. Jordan, Mr. George said, and that could create new endorsement opportunities down the road in such areas as insurance and financial services. Mr. Jordan virtually singlehandedly created the modern sports marketing industry and transformed the role of celebrity endorsers. Forbes has estimated his annual endorsement income at $45 million, the highest of any pro athlete. And with multiyear deals from Nike, General Mills, MCI WorldCom, Quaker Oats Co.'s Gatorade, Rayovac Corp. and Sara Lee Corp.'s Hanes, Mr. Jordan will continue to be the frontman for a range of consumer product companies for at least the next 10 years. Analysts estimated his endorsements generate $500 million in sales for the companies he represents. ''Thirty years from now, he'll still be one of the top five sport endorsers,'' said Bob Williams, president of Burns Sports Celebrity Service. Leader: Nike thinks Michael Jordan 's CEO days will continue for some time. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan pretty much conquered the world of pro basketball before he retired this month. Suppose Hollywood comes courting now? Are there new realms for him to tame on the movie screen, perhaps as a superhero or suave adventurer? Certainly not, said most of the 95 readers who e-mailed. Jordan's star shines brightly enough from shooting hoops -- he doesn't have to jump through Hollywood's, too. And if he does seek screen time, let it be as himself, many added. Jordan "doesn't need to degrade his accomplishments by 'becoming' some fictional superman. His movie Space Jam was cute, but I just couldn't see him playing any movie roles right now.I'd just rather he remain a role model for kids in a positive/realistic light." -- Susan Owensby, Tulsa "If this legend were ever to star in another picture, it would most have to be as himself. A movie about this man desperately needs to be produced, with Michael starring." -- Frank McCullough, Gibson City, Ill. Several readers suggested starting with Jordan's established persona and pumping it up (if that's possible) into an even bigger bigger-than-life character: "When real life is better than fiction, why bother to create? Perhaps Michael Jordan could be the hero of the comic book basketball court who demonstrates courage and values both on the court and off." -- Dee Shear, Dexter, N.Y. "I would like to see him be a superhero against violence and drugs. Most kids think what Michael Jordan says is gospel. If he can get them thinking that drugs and violence are worth fighting against, we may have the ability to combat these terrible problems facing our kids." -- Beth Starkey, Chelsea, Mich. "They would be better off inventing a whole new character for Jordan. Someone standing for fitness, determination, bringing others together to fulfill a dream." -- Jeff Graff, St. George, Utah Still, there were a few who thought Jordan might try playing a James Bond sort of character, and a Spider-Man role had its advocates, too. One reader liked the idea of Jordan playing against type: "A villain in a Bond movie for starters. To see if he could convince us of playing someone other than the Mr. Clean that we are used to." -- David Rose, Overton, Texas And another proposed steering Jordan away from the superhero mold and into the buddy-action-thriller genre: "I think Michael Jordan should team up with Chris Tucker and Jackie Chan in Rush Hour 2 ." -- Ryan Jones, Vancouver, Wash. Talented or not, Jordan has had his glory days and it's time to just leave him alone and move on, several readers said. "Please stop talking about him. He played basketball . He did not find a cure for cancer." -- Thomas Jackett, Hubertus, Wis. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Phil Jackson and Michael Jordan orchestrated "The Last Dance." Now it's time to dance to a new and different beat! Best of luck to them and the Chicago Bulls. Growing up with Jordan Many articles have been written following Michael Jordan 's second retirement from professional basketball . For Jordan and his many fans, it is the end of an era. For me, his retirement also marks the end of my fascination of the sports world, a world I very often viewed through the eyes of a child. From now on, I will likely see the world of sports for what it is, an entertainment business. Growing up in Chicago's suburbs in the late 1970s and early 1980s, my friends and I lived and died by our teams: Sox, Cubs, Bears, Hawks and eventually, the Bulls. Every victory was precious and there were usually few victories, not unlike today. And then, Michael Jordan became a member of the Chicago Bulls. Very soon, Jordan would capture the imagination of basketball fans everywhere. Of course, there was also criticism. He can't drive with his left, he's got no jumper, he can't shoot the three, he doesn't make his teammates better, and he's never won a championship. As great as Jordan was, his critics were even greater in the late 1980s. As I grew into adulthood, so did Jordan, by answering his basketball critics as well as many who would criticize him about his personal life. His first retirement in 1993 occurred during a difficult time in his life, shortly after the death of his father. Jordan's competitive nature would not allow him to move to Florida or Arizona with other retirees, so he started his baseball career. Many expected Jordan to be the next Willie Mays or Rickey Henderson. While his baseball career did fall short, the Birmingham Barons sold a lot of No. 45 baseball jerseys. At that time, I was becoming disenchanted with sports. The baseball strike of 1994 was an insult to sports fans everywhere. Further, I became sickened as professional athletes rejected ridiculous amounts of money because they were able to get it elsewhere. Of course, Michael Jordan probably exacerbated the problem, which culminated in the recent NBA lockout. In my heart, though, I don't think Jordan played basketball for the money. Michael Jordan will be remembered as a great basketball player and as someone who set the standard of excellence for the sport. He was a great role model, competitive and hard-working in his craft and a gentleman off the court. As I made the journey from childhood to adulthood, Jordan has been a model of excellence, not just as a basketball player, but more importantly as a person. Frank Young Arlington Heights Worshipping false idols "Be like Mike" isn't for me. Who would want to be mobbed wherever they went? Today's young people don't aim to become president of the United States, rather they choose the grander pay scale of president of XYZ Corporation. High job satisfaction goes the wayside to precious financial gain. People are not content with being themselves. They want to blend in with their heroes. To find that right comfort level, they'll change mates, religions and sunglasses. Image is paramount. The battle cry: Defame decency, opt for opulence. When will we face being mortal? Anxiety can level off if we stopped guessing what everyone else thought about us and concentrated on what in our own hearts is best for each of us. Randy F. Gollay Buffalo Grove Appreciate toll of sport Phil Jackson and Michael Jordan can sit back and say, "I've been there. I've done that and I've experienced that. Thank you very much!" Society should appreciate that basketball is like any other sport, it demands your undivided physical and mental attention. The hype and holler of winning is great, but the toll on the players and their families is beyond belief. Phil Jackson and Michael Jordan orchestrated "The Last Dance." Now it's time to dance to a new and different beat! Best of luck to them and the Chicago Bulls. Richard J. White Elmhurst Good news, he's gone Best news I heard was that Michael retired. I hope he has time to take trips overseas to check on the children making shoes for Nike, like he said he was going to do. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan was made for slow motion. On the basketball court in real time, he was just too quick, a blur of movement whose actions were best monitored by the roar of his fans. Slow motion replays were the way to appreciate the way he could sprint, dive, throw, spin, feint and dance on air, seemingly beyond gravity's grasp. The faces to watch were those of his opponents, hands on knees, close to despair, after having seen yet again the giant basketballer flying out of nowhere, and the ball dropping through the hoop. The question they were asking themselves was obvious: How did he do that? Jordan, who has announced his retirement from the sport, was an artist. Artistry aside, sport is a business, a big business. Michael Jordan is also a business. Both the business of basketball and Michael Jordan have been rewarded handsomely by his outstanding abilities. Fortune magazine has estimated he brought in some $ US10 billion in tickets, TV revenues and merchandise sales. His salary last year was $ US33 million ($ NZ61.5 million). The average NBA basketball salary is $ US2.6 million. But money didn't lead countless thousands of youngsters to pin his action posters on their bedroom walls. To them, Michael Jordan was a dream, somebody they could try to emulate, the player who won the game on the last throw, as Jordan often did spectacularly, including his last game in the NBA championship. Through that throw, his beloved Chicago Bulls team won the championship for the third year in a row. Dreams are free, but Jordan provided inspiration. He also proved that huge amounts of money do not necessarily lead to corruption, although basketball has had its scandals. He was good for the sport, and the sport had certainly been good for him. But there is huge irony in the fact that one of the most professional yet "clean" players in the world has announced his retirement at the same time as the International Olympic Committee, which claims to represent the best of sporting ideals, is immersed in a grubby bribery scandal. For years, countries and cities bidding to host Olympic Games have known that having the best facilities and services was only part way along the path to success. Offering powerful inducements to some of the 100-odd committee members was considered part of the process, and their acceptance taken for granted. Already besmirched by allegations over the years, the committee should not survive the latest revelations. Progressively, all members should be replaced, and new inviolable rules instituted. How doubly ironic that some of those tired old would-be gods at the top of the Olympic movement have been benefiting from the sort of corruption that would lead to life banishment if athletes were to play similar games. Not surprisingly, similar accusations are now emerging with the Commonwealth Games Federation, and Wellington is in the forefront as it prepares to battle for the 2006 Games. Wellington committee head Kerry Prendergast shouldn't feel intimidated at the reaction her statements have provoked. She is right to raise the issue. On and off the court, Michael Jordan 's contribution has been immeasurable. In retirement he offers more. In contrast, the IOC's behaviour represents the worst, not just for sport, but for business and life itself. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WHEN one man's retirement leads to a 4 percent drop in the value of a company's shares, it is usually because that man is the owner, chief executive -- or Michael Jordan . When the story leaked that United Stated basketball hero Michael Jordan was going to retire, the market reacted quickly, resulting in a 4 percent drop in sports shoe and apparel company Nike's share value. Nike has had a long-term relationship with the Chicago Bulls player, selling Michael Jordan -branded shoes and sportswear, worn and advertised by Jordan. Massey University finance lecturer Paul Gronewoller said the initial share price drop was an over-reaction by investors to the pending announcement. "Generally, investors fall into two categories -- those who are informed and the uninformed. It is well documented that lesser informed investors can over-react to information they hear via the media." Dr Gronewoller said whether they were right will be determined by whether cashflow from sales is affected by Jordan's retirement. "I would expect the share price to recover. "I think Nike will continue to manufacture Michael Jordan products for years to come. He is still the greatest basketballer in the history of the game and his legend will continue. I don't see another basketball player filling Michael Jordan 's shoes in the next few years, to his image and his popularity will continue." He says parallels to Michael Jordan 's endorsement of products could be drawn with golfing greats who have retired. While Jack Nicklaus has returned from the professional circuit, his name is still successfully to sell product. Marketing lecturer Don Esslemont said the reaction by investors is linked to traditional marketing theory on cognitive processes that suggests there is an association between a personality and sales of the product that person endorses. "I expect the people that signed Michael Jordan up to sponsor Nike products were believers in this theory -- that having him sponsor their products would make people think more often about the Nike brand and would buy more product. "No doubt Michael Jordan would have sold more shoes for Nike and this would have been attributed to his image his brand, but there is no proof of a direct relationship between Michael Jordan and the success of Nike." Mr Esslemont said there is no reason to think Nike can't invest the resources that were used to pay Jordan in another creative way that will result in sales equal to those generated by Jordan. -------------------- CAPTION: MICHAEL JORDAN _ his retirement put the sharemarket through the hoops. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Kids know a championship basketball team when they see one, but they're hard pressed to find one locally. Talking to a group of Wood Dale kids as they play basketball at the park district recreational center Saturday, there is a consensus that without the three star players on the Chicago Bulls team, there is no hope of a championship. "The Bulls might not ever win another championship again," said 8-year-old Fred Weishaupt of Wood Dale. This year, Weishaupt will be rooting for the Orlando Magic. Michael Jordan retired for the second time, Scottie Pippen was traded to Houston and Rodman vacillates between retirement and free agency, leaving Chicago without three of its most recognizable sports stars. To Lisa Berg, 10, that's more than the Bulls can handle. "Unless they get someone as good as Pippen or Jordan, the Bulls will never win," she said. Nine-year-old Anthony Mele quickly interjects, "There will never be anyone as good as Jordan." Still, these kids are fans of the game. They spend their Saturdays practicing jump shots, dribbling and passing. For Mele, the Bulls reorganization isn't the end of his love affair with the game. He said, "I'll root for any team that's good." Then he thinks for a while, and adds, "Any team that has Shaq." But not everyone is so blinded by hoopla of the Bulls of old. Luigi Bomanno, 7, is the sole dissenter in the crowd. "I think the Bulls might win the championship again this year," he says to a crowd of nonbelievers. "They still have some of the old Bulls on the new team," he rationalizes. Ever the optimist, he says, "Somebody new might come along." His friends brush him off, arguing there's no way to replace all three Bulls greats, let alone Michael Jordan , the greatest player to ever grace the basketball court. Unfazed, Mele replies, "We don't need Michael Jordan ." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Playing for Keeps: Michael Jordan and the World He Made By David Halberstam Random House, 426 pp., $ 24.95 The world press turned out a torrent of turgid prose when Michael Jordan retired two weeks ago. You probably think you could not read another word about MJ, let alone 426 pages. Think again. David Halberstam renders a nuanced portrait of Jordan as a consummate athlete but goes beyond and lays out the circumstances by which a basketball player became a global icon so revered that a French newspaper could call him "God in person." Halberstam does not so much echo the hyperbole as explain how it came to be. The book, due Monday, is always about basketball first but also is a story of our times -- how, for instance, a sneaker company with its advertising millions found the perfect vessel for its hopes and sales in a black man from North Carolina. It is Jordan's story, but to tell it, Halberstam introduces us to the other stars in MJ's orbit: among them players Scottie Pippen and Dennis Rodman, coaches Phil Jackson and Dean Smith, general manager Jerry Krause, filmmaker Spike Lee and agent David Falk. The best novels make you care about the characters in them. This book is populated by characters you already care about. Halberstam takes full advantage, painting each one in revealing, delicious detail. This is a meticulously reported book. Based on that reporting, Halberstam makes judgments, some of them harsh. About Falk, for instance, Halberstam writes, "As he became more and more powerful, he left less and less doubt that his time was extremely valuable, certainly more valuable than yours, and quite possibly more valuable than anyone's except perhaps that of Michael Jordan himself." Jordan's time is so valuable that he gives little of it to Halberstam (as we learn in an author's note at book's end). There had been an unofficial understanding that Jordan would assent to two or three two-hour sessions. In the end, he did not grant them. Halberstam hazards a guess that Jordan, ever competitive, wanted to save good stuff for a book of his own someday. Still, Halberstam notes, Jordan never blocked access to his closest associates, who checked first to see if it was OK to talk to the writer. Jordan's imprimatur allowed Halberstam to dig. And he tells Jordan's stirring story from boyhood to brand name. The treatment is mostly admiring, though not entirely. You learn, for instance, that Jordan often taunted weaker teammates at practices. It was not until Jordan's return from his failed attempt at baseball, where he learned about limitations, that this "certain cruelty, a determination to destroy certain teammates in practice," disappeared. Still, his rage against Krause, the rumpled general manager, remained. "Jordan was skilled at verbal blood sport. . . . It was by no means Jordan at his most attractive, thought even those who took Jordan's side." Jordan emerges in all his complexity, as do his lessers. Halberstam writes as a reporter-historian- basketball buff. His narrative style is more Jordan jumper than skywalking dunk: swishing through the net, not rattling the rim. How good is this book? Well, let's put it this way: Michael Jordan is the David Halberstam of basketball . headline: MICHAEL JORDAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 When Michael Jordan retired, Michael Jordan started getting a lot of interview requests. The Penn guard who shares a name with the retired Chicago Bulls' superstar spoke to Xtra and MTV. Friends teased him, saying, "Boy, Mike, it must feel good to go out on top." He couldn't understand what all the fuss was about, and yet in a funny way, he could. After all, his name is Michael Jordan . He plays basketball . He wears No. 23. Get the idea? "I got a lot of interviews out of it," Jordan said. "A lot of people, because he was retiring, wanted to talk to me." His first reaction was, what for? "I have nothing to do with him," Jordan said, "except we share the same name." So basketball still has a Michael Jordan . Though he has a famous name, t his Jordan has his own style, tattoos and a story to go with it all. He came out of a tough Philadelphia neighborhood to become a solid player and student at Penn. Jordan, a junior point guard, leads the Quakers (10-3) with 14.7 points and 4.4 assists per game. Despite the name and number on his jersey, there's nothing spectacular in his game. A muscular 6-footer, with average quickness and an improving jump shot, Jordan does not appear destined for the kind of NBA career enjoyed by his namesake. And this Jordan isn't even much of a dunker. "I'm sub-par," he said. "On a good day, I can slam. It's all two points. I used to be infatuated with dunking, but now I take the layup." Michael Hakim Jordan, 21, was born a few years before the other Michael Jordan became famous. "I was born in '77, so my parents didn't even hear about him," Jordan said. "It's pure coincidence. My mom named me after her favorite cousin, because, I reminded her of him." The oldest of seven children born to Alice Jordan, he didn't want to meet the same fate as friends who had talent but never made it off the streets. Like the other Jordan, he wasn't destined for super-stardom right away. "He wasn't highly recruited," Penn coach Fran Dunphy said. "I think there's a lot of people who maybe kicked themselves for not paying more attention to him." Two years ago, Jordan won Ivy League freshman of the year honors. As a sophomore, he led Penn with 15.3 points and 4.9 assists as the Quakers finished second to Ivy League champion Princeton. He's more of a playmaking guard than a scorer, although Dunphy wants him to shoot more. There is no diamond earring, but he wears a tattoo on his left arm of a Herculean figure holding a basketball . His astrological sign - Cancer - is inscribed on his left arm above his name in old English letters, "MIKE." Pulling aside a sweat band on his right hand, Jordan reveals a small Chinese symbol, dulled and blurred by the years. He won't reveal its meaning - a small request for privacy that the other Jordan would envy. "I can't tell you that," he said. "Sorry." He boasts one Jordanesque statistic. Penn is 10-4 in his career and 4-0 this season when Jordan scores at least 20 points. "I think I've improved in my decision-making and just overall on my jump shot and my temper," Jordan said. "If I made a pass to someone and they dropped it, I'd get ticked off. But this year, I don't do that any more. I think I try too hard sometimes." Jordan has NBA aspirations, but his best shot probably is to impress a team as an unsigned free agent after next season. He's inspired by small guards who have made it big in the NBA - like the 76ers' Allen Iverson (6-foot) and Cleveland's Brevin Knight (5-10). His favorite player? " Michael Jordan ," he said. His most recent book is "To the Hoop: The Seasons of a Basketball Life." Body_115 Body_115 Body fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PLAYING FOR KEEPS Michael Jordan and the World He Made. By David Halberstam. Illustrated. 426 pp. New York: Random House. $24.95. ONE day I found myself on a boat wending its way up the Bosporus, which not only divides a city -- Istanbul -- but splits two continents as well. The guide for this morning tour for two was a young Turk who was doing his job by the numbers. At one point, leaning over the rail -- it was the Asian side, not the European side, if memory serves -- he asked what I do for a living in America. I told him. He came alive. "Have you ever met Michael Jordan ?" he asked. I said that I have, that in my line of work he's rather difficult to avoid. The guide said, "Oh my God!" On a safari in Africa, friends of mine saw children come out of huts without running water or electricity wearing Michael Jordan No. 23 basketball jerseys. In Paris, in beret, Jordan is hounded. When he retired on Jan. 13, the front page of a Chinese newspaper read: "Flying Man Jordan Coming Back Down to Earth." I was once in the Jerusalem home of a Nazi hunter for the Simon Wiesenthal Center. On his bookshelf, among his tomes of pursuit, were "The Jordan Rules" and "Rare Air," two excellent books on the life and times of one Michael Jeffrey Jordan. He will have to make room for another book. "Playing for Keeps" doesn't have the bite of Sam Smith's "Jordan Rules" or the glories of Walter Iooss Jr.'s photographs in "Rare Air" (not that it should, since it's not a picture book). What David Halberstam delivers -- and what the reader has come to expect from Halberstam -- is insight, balance, analysis, an assemblage of pertinent anecdotes and the kind of digging for information you would get from, say, Schliemann's band of archeologists, hip-high and burrowing deeper into Troy. Halberstam provides a sense of the impact Jordan has had here and abroad -- he was not only the consummate hoopster but a pitchman like few others. He was a high-wire act, the cavalry and Phineas T. Barnum on the stump all rolled into one. He could dazzle with his aeronautics, pull out a basketball game that appeared hopelessly lost or sell a pair of sneakers with a smile as genuine and appealing as rosy dawn. The "Michael Factor" meant a variety of things: he could elevate a struggling shoe company, Nike, to head-spinning heights; he could lead a team to six championships in the 1990's; he could make the difference of nearly nine million more television viewers during the National Basketball Association finals. And, as Halberstam says, citing a Forbes magazine estimate, Jordan's persona "helped generate $10 billion in revenues for the game, its broadcasters and for his varying corporate partners." Halberstam quotes some tough-minded folks who exult over Jordan. Harry Edwards is a black sociologist at the University of California, Berkeley. "Wary that the self-evident achievements of black athletes cast an imposing shadow over many black youths and pull them away from careers in other fields," Halberstam writes, Edwards "nonetheless talked about Jordan representing the highest level of human achievement, on the order of Gandhi, Einstein or Michelangelo. If, he added, he were in charge of introducing an alien being 'to the epitome of human potential, creativity, perseverance and spirit, I would introduce that alien life to Michael Jordan .' " And the novelist-lawyer Scott Turow follows that with: " Michael Jordan plays basketball better than anyone else in the world does anything else." Halberstam's achievement, however, is that he skillfully skirts hagiography and writes as credible reporter. He tempers the exuberance. He quotes Tex Winter, the wise old assistant coach of the Bulls: "I think Michael's a great, great player, but I'm not an idolater." Halberstam continues: "The two men had their own running debate on what constituted good basketball . 'There's no I in the word team,' Winter would tell him. 'There is in the word win,' Jordan would answer." But to Jordan's credit, he matured and learned that Winter was onto something, though it took Jordan seven seasons before he fully trusted his teammates -- the "supporting cast," as he has referred to them -- enough to help make them better, and finally gain a championship. Jordan's winning and crafty personality, his triumphant play, creative commercials, a canny N.B.A. marketing strategy and reception worldwide as a crossover superstar, on

On a safari in Africa, friends of mine saw children come out of huts without running water or electricity wearing Michael Jordan No. 23 basketball jerseys. In Paris, in beret, Jordan is hounded. When he retired on Jan. 13, the front page of a Chinese newspaper read: "Flying Man Jordan Coming Back Down to Earth." I was once in the Jerusalem home of a Nazi hunter for the Simon Wiesenthal Center. Barnum on the stump all rolled into one. He could dazzle with his aeronautics, pull out a basketball game that appeared hopelessly lost or sell a pair of sneakers with a smile as genuine and appealing as rosy dawn. Halberstam quotes some tough-minded folks who exult over Jordan. Harry Edwards is a black sociologist at the University of California, Berkeley. "Wary that the self-evident achievements of black athletes cast an imposing shadow over many black youths and pull them away from careers in other fields," Halberstam writes, Edwards "nonetheless talked about Jordan representing the highest level of human achievement, on the order of Gandhi, Einstein or Michelangelo. If, he added, he were in charge of introducing an alien being 'to the epitome of human potential, creativity, perseverance and spirit, I would introduce that alien life to Michael Jordan .' " And the novelist-lawyer Scott Turow follows that with: " Michael Jordan plays basketball better than anyone else in the world does anything else." Halberstam's achievement, however, is that he skillfully skirts hagiography and writes as credible reporter. He tempers the exuberance. He quotes Tex Winter, the wise old assistant coach of the Bulls: "I think Michael's a great, great player, but I'm not an idolater." Halberstam continues: "The two men had their own running debate on what constituted good basketball . 'There's no I in the word team,' Winter would tell him. 'There is in the word win,' Jordan would answer." Jordan, as drawn by Halberstam, is generous, loyal, thoughtful but flawed. He can be meanspirited (see his almost cruel riding of the Bulls' general manager, Jerry Krause). He can be hardheaded (as in his draft ideas and his resistance to some trades, like that of the bruising forward Charles Oakley to the Knicks for the bruising center Bill Cartwright, which Jordan later grudgingly acknowledged led to the Bulls' first three championships). And Jordan's "competitive rage" borders on the manic, whether he's playing golf and cards for high stakes or making bets with teammates about whose suitcase will come off the airport baggage conveyor first (Jordan bribes an attendant so he can win the wager). Jordan can also be so protective of his Nike-Gatorade-Coke-McDonald's-Wheaties-Hanes-etc. corporate-sponsoring image -- he is, Halberstam underscores, no Jackie Robinson, Muhammad Ali or Paul Robeson in the civil rights arena -- that he would not even support Harvey Gantt, an early civil rights leader, when he was running for a Senate seat in Jordan's home state of North Carolina against Jesse Helms, called by Halberstam "the nemesis of Carolina blacks (among many others)." Republicans buy sneakers, too, Jordan said. The faults in "Playing for Keeps" include an occasional excess of information regarding peripheral aspects of Jordan's life and lapses in which the language is less than carefully or thriftily wrought. One oversight occurs when Halberstam suggests that Jordan is an incarnation of the transformation in sports hype that allows players today to be promoted above team. He neglects to mention that Babe Ruth and Red Grange were there decades before, as was the famous Madison Square Garden marquee, "Geo Mikan vs. Knicks." But Halberstam over all has succeeded in lending perspective to the world Michael Jordan both made and inherited, as well as in portraying Michael Jordan the man and not the icon, guiding us, in effect, beyond the Bosporus. Ira Berkow is a sports columnist

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Editor's note: This column first appeared on June 20, 1984, after the Bulls drafted Michael Jordan with the third pick in the NBA draft: How unusual the first round of the NBA draft was Tuesday for the Bulls. They drafted North Carolina off-guard Michael Jordan with the third pick overall, and there weren't any boos, hisses or bahs. Normally, announcement of the Bulls' choice is the signal for the story to get gory. You hear the guy's name and immediately have to start scurrying the medical records or police blotters to match up with the scouting report. Remember when the Bulls wound up with Ronnie Lester? His knee had a surgical scar in the shape of a question mark. Remember Quintin Dailey? His scar was a conviction for aggravated assault on a college co-ed. With Orlando Woolridge, the question was whether it was worth spending a first-round pick on potential. With Reggie Theus, it was whether the UNLV wild could be extracted from him. With Sidney Green, it was who the hell is he? There was always something. Could the Bulls sign the guy with so-and-so as his agent? Does he have a work visa to play in this country? If so, does he need one considering his work habits? What fun it was picking apart this guy's character and that guy's ability. Questions, questions, questions. There always was a cloud of doom surrounding the Bulls' latest franchise player. Then Tuesday, and Michael Jordan . The guy is perfect. Well, not exactly perfect, because he isn't a 7-foot, shot-blockin', sky-hookin', reboundin' artist in the paint. If ever a professional sports franchise needed somebody to sprinkle it with a large dose of credibility, it's the Bulls. And if ever there was a basketball player who could provide it, he's Michael Jordan . As a member of the U.S. Olympic team, and possibly the best player on that team, and probably the team's leader, Jordan very well could be the best-known basketball player in America by the end of the summer. He could be a hero, and heroes have been in short supply at Chicago Stadium the past few winters. So as all-around solid as Jordan is, the question this year doesn't concern the Bulls' first-round draft choice. No, the question is on the other sneaker. As in, can this team become worthy of having a Michael Jordan on its roster? As of right now, the only blemish on Jordan's record is that he was drafted by the Bulls. Let's face it, this is a guy whose pedigree is more suited to the NBA penthouses of Boston, L.A. or Philly, and he winds up on skid row. Putting him in this sort of environment, on a team that lost 109 games the past two seasons, attracted fewer people than a John Glenn rally, and has a front office punctuated by chaos well, it's almost criminal, sort of the basketball equivalent of being traded to the Cleveland Indians. Unless, of course, the Bulls can build to Jordan's level and keep from wasting his talents. "He isn't a savior," general manager Rod Thorn admitted. "We have other problems to take care of besides just drafting Michael Jordan . He's a tremendous asset, but just him isn't enough. Addressing our other needs along with drafting him will help us improve." Thorn is in the process of addressing those other needs. Step 1 is waiting to see whether the Knicks sign Bulls free agent David Greenwood to an offer sheet. Once that happens, within the next 10 days or so, Thorn will see what he can wrangle as compensation. If that compensation is one of the Knicks' extra centers, the Bulls will have filled their biggest need. If it's not, they just might match the offer for Greenwood and then look to Golden State free agent Joe Barry Carroll or Atlanta's dangling Tree Rollins to fill the hole in the middle. Whatever, as good as Michael Jordan is, drafting him was only the start of making the Bulls a respectable NBA team. But he is an outstanding start, one that is worthy of being called a foundation. "He means a great deal to our franchise," Thorn said. "Anytime you put a great player with charisma, a leader type, on your team, it enhances the franchise. And with our team not having made the playoffs the past two years, that's important to us." So the next year or so will be pretty interesting. Will the Bulls build to Michael Jordan 's level? Or will he be dragged down to their level? The questions are different, but the answers are just as elusive. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 If you can't watch him on TV anymore, you can at least read about newly retired basketball superstar Michael Jordan , who according to David Halberstam, was ''nothing less than a kind of young American prince.'' Halberstam talks about the canonization of MJ and his book Playing for Keeps (Random House) on page C12. The other new book on the shelves is Roland Lazenby's Blood on the Horns -- The Long Strange Ride of Michael Jordan 's Chicago Bulls (Addax; $31.95), which focuses on the trials and tribulations of the ''world's most famous basketball team'' during its strife- ridden 1997-98 season. St. Michael will certainly help to sell Lazenby's book, though perhaps Sam Smith takes the bull by the horns more directly in The Jordan Rules -- The Inside Story of a Turbulent Season with Michael Jordan and the Chicago Bulls (Pocket Books; $7.50), which purports to ''reveal the team behind the man and the man behind the Madison Avenue smile.'' W.W. Norton is coming out with Michael Jordan and the New Global Capitalism by Walter LeFeber in July, a look at how worldwide advertising using Jordan spreads American cultural domination to smaller, poorer nations. A couple of bios written for kids portray Michael Jordan as Role Model extraordinaire: Chip Lovitt's unambiguously titled Michael Jordan (Scholastic; $5.50), revised, updated and complete with ''full-colour'' photos, and Bill Gutman's (sigh) Michael Jordan (Archway; $4.99), also updated to include Jordan's ''triumphant return'' to basketball from his sojourn in the hinterland of baseball. These books are getting a second airing thanks to Jordan's retirement. headline: HENDERSON KIDS PUT ON 'LIVING MUSEUM'
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 3/4 a3 local CHEYENNE -- Abraham Lincoln was shorter than he looks in pictures. William Shakespeare wore a retainer and had his blond hair tied up under his black beret. The United States' 16th president and the world's most famous playwright were two of the characters on display Wednesday at Henderson Elementary School as part of McKean's Magical Museum. The museum is like a "living wax museum," said Erin McKean, fifth-grade teacher. The museum's creator asked her students to portray characters to culminate their units on biography and United States history. The students chose their favorite historical characters and wrote one-minute speeches highlighting that person's life. Students memorized the speech, created a poster as a display backdrop and dressed the part to play their chosen character. The characters were on display for an hour, McKean said. The displays were activated by pushing a red button tacked onto the back of each student. When the button on Michael Curry's back was pushed, the 11-year-old brought Michael Jordan to life. Curry wore a Chicago Bulls jersey bearing the number 23 and held a basketball as he stood in front of a life-sized cutout of the basketball star. He chose to portray Michael Jordan because Jordan is his favorite basketball star. "He's always been my hero, and he's never greedy or stuck-up," Curry said. Students from other classes at the school toured the "museum" as groups or individually, allowing the students on display plenty of chances to give their speeches. "It's not embarrassing," said William Shakespeare, better known at the school as Brianna Best, 10. "Once you do your speech lots, you kind of get used to it." Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Larry Brinlee / staff Henderson Elementary student Dillon Conner, 10, left, listens to Michael Curry, 11, recite a short biography on basketball star Michael Jordan during the school's annual McKean Magical Museum Wednesday. The fifth-grade students chose their favorite American and portrayed them for other students as part of their history class. Curry chose Michael Jordan because he has always been his hero. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 KEVIN Steele breathes, eats and sleeps basketball . The six feet eight inches tall teenager will certainly be walking tall when he represents his country in America. But the 16-year-old, of Radley Way, Werrington, who plays centre, is hoping another dream comes true and he will meet his hoops idol Michael Jordan . He said: "It is every athlete's dream to represent their country and I am really looking forward to the games. I love basketball . "If I shook hands with Michael Jordan I would never wash my hands again. I am really looking forward to getting the chance to meet him." This raw enthusiasm is what makes the team special, according to UK Special Olympics team head coach Graham Lapper, of Eva Grove, Newcastle. He said: "They are all very spirited, enthusiastic and committed to basketball . They love their sport so much they would do anything for it." But basketball is more than just a sport for Kevin and his team mates Wayne Jones, 15, of Renfrew Close, Newcastle, and Bryan Dain, 21, of Lily Street, Wolstanton. It is about feeling like a winner. Wayne, Kevin and Bryan have minor learning difficulties and Wayne used to be withdrawn and quiet until basketball transformed his life. Coach Lapper said: "Wayne is a totally different lad now. He mixes with everyone and basketball has completely changed him. "He had never been away from home before and he went away with the team and he fitted in really well." Wayne said: "To go to the Olympics is a dream come true and I would not wish for anything else. I sometimes sit in a daze because I can not believe I am going." The games take place in Raleigh, North Carolina, in July. Athletes will be treated to a civic reception at the American Embassy in London. Coach Lapper said: "The lads will be playing in a 21,000 seater stadium and President Clinton may open the games. The Special Olympics is a mirror image of the Olympics with a torch and a parade. "It is an amazing achievement. Bryan, Wayne and Kevin will be representing their country at the highest level. "They have been picked because they are the best basketball players in the country and because of their great ambassadorial qualities." It costs GBP 1,000 to send each player to the games and Coach Lapper is appealing for sponsorship. Anyone interested in making a donation can contact him on 01782 659153. headline: THIS DAY IN SPORTS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 This was the date in 1982 a budding legend named Michael Jordan got the OK to make himself known to the world. North Carolina was down 62-61 in the NCAA championship game when Dean Smith called a timeout with 32 seconds left. Smith knew that Patrick Ewing-led Georgetown would concentrate on defending Carolina's top scorer, James Worthy. So Smith told his team to take the first open shot. As the Tar Heels' huddle broke, Smith tapped his freshman guard on the rump and said, "Knock it in, Michael ." Jordan did, on a left-side jumper with 16 seconds left, for the 63-62 final score and Smith's first national title. On the next play, Georgetown's Fred Brown froze and threw a game-ending turnover directly into Worthy's hands. Brown's mistake received the most attention but, with time, the real importance of that game has come into clearer focus. Also on this date 1976: Indiana caps its 32-0 season with a victory against Michigan, 86-68. 1981: The AIAW, in essence, has its last national women's basketball title game, with Louisiana Tech beating Tennessee. One more will be played the next year but will be overshadowed by the NCAA's first women's tournament. Says Tennessee coach Pat Summitt later, "I almost felt like we were stabbing people in the back that had made our dreams possible." 1987: Wrestlemania III sets the all-time attendance record for an indoor sports event, 93,173, at the Silverdome in Pontiac, Mich. 1997: Dean Smith coaches his final game, losing to eventual NCAA champ Arizona at the Final Four. Smith leaves with a record-27 NCAA tournament appearances. Quotes of the century "I knew that I would be going places, and I just wanted to know where I was when I got there." -- Michael Jordan , on why he majored in geography at North Carolina Sources: NCAA Final Four record book; Tennessee women's basketball media guide; Basketball Shorts Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PHOTO, B/W, Steve Taylor, Titan Sports Inc.; Duo: Andre the Giant, Hulk. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Pro basketball fans must suffer. We are suffering this year through a strike-shortened season. Utah Jazz fans, who comprise the majority of pro basketball fans in this area, had to endure losing to the Chicago Bulls and Michael Jordan in last year's NBA finals. Although Jordan's recent retirement gives hope to all the other teams, including the Jazz, all true basketball fans will suffer because they will miss watching him play. Writers suffer, too, when it comes to Michael Jordan . He can't be captured on paper. Not that authors haven't tried - more than 50 books have been published about him. The Idaho Falls Public Library has more books on Michael Jordan than on the rest of the NBA. The books are fairly evenly split between the Junior Department and the adult collection, showing his appeal to all ages. Congratulations to Michael Jordan for getting the best of a generation of writers as well as basketball players - from intellectual historians to sappy columnists to beat reporters - all of whom have tried to pin MJ on the page and all of whom failed. Perhaps it's Michael's grace or his indomitable will, but putting words to what he did on the court doesn't work. He beats them all. One on one you can't defend against or define Jordan, on the floor or with a word processor. David Halberstam is currently on the bestseller list with "Playing for Keeps: Michael Jordan and the World He Made." The heavy-hitting historian picks a good time to write about Jordan, on the occasion of his retirement. Although he is sometimes bogged down in his striving for significance, his vivid reconstruction of the winning shot in the final game of the sixth championship run is as good as a writer gets in capturing Jordan. Reading this section opens the wound for Jazz fans! "For the Love of the Game" is Michael Jordan 's attempt at autobiography, and while he is smart, largely using pictures rather than words, still photography remains the antithesis of Jordan in action. Bob Greene gives us "Hang Time: Days and Dreams with Michael Jordan ", arguably the single worst book about Michael Jordan . The book is more about Greene than Jordan, and in this work Greene exercises his ability to turn anything into schmalz. "The Jordan Rules" by Sam Smith is the ideal book for kids fascinated by Jordan. Chicago Tribune reporter Smith took a microscope to the Bulls' controversy-riddled 1990-91 championship season and emerged with a revealing glimpse of the pettiness that always threatens team chemistry. This is the definitive book about Jordan's early championship years. Suffering, missing Michael Jordan this NBA season, you can curl up with a book about him and experience, as much as possible, the uniqueness of the man. Library Corner is compiled by the staff of the Idaho Falls Public Library. headline: TRYING TO BE LIKE MIKE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 No amount of dribbling practice will raise actor Michael Jace to the level of basketball legend Michael Jordan . But, then again, Jace wasn't trying to mimic Jordan's athletic ability in his starring role in Fox Family Channel's " Michael Jordan : An American Hero." Rather, Jace, who has appeared in guest spots on NBC's "ER" and the HBO series "Arli$$," wanted to give TV audiences an accurate off-the-court picture of the superstar who has worked hard to keep his private life his own. Fox Family's movie about Jordan is TV's first drama about the all-star who has won legions of fans for his play as well as his image. "I was inspired by the strength of his will," says Jace. "American Hero" is based loosely on a book of the same name by Michael Murray, also the author of "Madonna: Innocence Lost" and "The True Story of the Menendez Murders." Jordan did not participate in the film or endorse it. In media interviews, Jordan has rejected as premature the idea of writing his auto-biography or lending his name to an authorized account of his life. Jace understands Jordan's view. "If I were in his position, I'd be leery of having someone do a biography of my life," says Jace. "So for me, it was really important in doing this that we in no way diminished his legacy. I wouldn't have taken this role if I thought that was going to happen." The movie delves deeply into Jordan's childhood relationship with his father, James Jordan (Ernie Hudson), with his wife, Juanita (Robin Givens), and his mother, Deloris Jordan (Debbie Allen). Jace hopes that after watching, audiences will "realize how important" Jordan's family, particularly his dad, has been to his far-reaching success. In segments of Jordan as a young boy (played by Cordereau Dye), he confronts his father about taunts from other boys at school and about discrimination on the playground basketball court. The father reminds his son that prejudice is ignorance and encourages him to rise above the hurtful jeers and mistreatment. "Jordan had an extremely strong connection to his dad" (who was shot and killed in a carjacking several years ago), Jace continues. "We approached the issue of racism to show children that any obstacle can be overcome. I hope this movie inspires kids to believe that if you put your mind to it, you can will yourself to do anything." Jace had to push himself into prime physical shape for the part, running at least 2 miles daily, adhering to a strict diet and playing basketball for "four to five hours a day." Despite all the training, Jace still can't do a 360-degree slam dunk, but he's not complaining. The role gave him insight into what makes Jordan tick. "In preparing, I went from being just a fanatical fan because of his ability on a basketball court to having a newfound respect for him as a person. "I think it's all intertwined," says Jace of the many aspects of Jordan that the film reveals. "The way he views life, the way he plays basketball and his upbringing. I don't see anyone playing the game the same way that Jordan did. I think that came from the way he has lived his life." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Who in the world today can't picture Michael Jordan ? His sky-borne frame is a global icon; his smiling face sells underwear and telecommunications; his intense looks of concentration brought millions of new viewers to the National Basketball Association. Scott Turow, the novelist, wrote, " Michael Jordan plays basketball better than anyone else in the world does anything else," and even though Jordan has retired from the game, no one has contradicted that assessment. His court moves are mimicked by so many young players that it's hard to imagine any of them wanting to be like anyone but Mike. But what about being Mike? In " Michael Jordan : An American Hero," an unauthorized yet always admiring biographical drama that will be shown at 8 tonight on Fox Family Channel, four actors are faced with that challenge. Cordereau Dye, 12, one of those actors, was found at an open casting call in Charlotte, N.C., one of five nationwide that involved 200 hopefuls. Mr. Dye is an eight-year veteran of the theater as well as a multi-sport athlete, so he felt not the slightest bit intimidated by the role, he said. "He was a regular kid," Mr. Dye said. "He showed that if you try hard, do your best, something will happen." Two additional actors, 5-year-old Dari G. Smith and 15-year-old Thomas Hobson, help build the portrayal of an ordinary childhood. Although the young Michael is tenaciously focused in everything he tries, he meets with opposition and small failures like everyone else. Mr. Dye said his favorite scene was an argument between Michael and his brother that showed the future star at his most insecure, competitive and human. Michael Jace, whose credits include "Boogie Nights" and "Forrest Gump," plays the adult Jordan, from his days at the University of North Carolina till his professional comeback at the end of the movie. Mr. Jace said he read reams of news clippings, interviews and biographies of the superstar, watched game film and soaked up as much about Jordan as he could from Jordan's colleagues and friends, including the sports broadcaster Ahmad Rashad, the brother-in-law of Debbie Allen, the actress and choreographer, who plays Jordan's mother. Mr. Jace said he thought the project had Jordan's tacit approval. And there was the physical regimen: a two-mile run every day, followed by five to six hours of basketball . "I wanted to be as close physically as possible," Mr. Jace said. "But no golf was incorporated." Other members of the cast are instantly recognizable. Ernie Hudson, whose film credits include both "Ghostbusters" movies and "Mr. Magoo," plays Jordan's father, a dispenser of sage advice and Jordan's best friend. Robin Givens plays Juanita, an independent, smart figure whom the athlete pursues through a sea of groupies and eventually marries; the singer Lou Rawls takes a small role as an insightful and supportive security guard during Jordan's days with the Chicago Bulls. The film touches on Jordan's early professional days, when the Bulls were chronic underachievers struggling to attract a crowd of 2,000 and when frustrated locker room brawls were common. The arrival of Phil Jackson, the New Age head coach who had such a profound effect on Jordan, is heralded by a surreal gathering on the court at which Jackson announces, "This is warrior music, gentlemen -- the official theme song of the Chicago Bulls." The waves of success are duly noted, and the film ends on the hopeful note of Jordan's return to basketball in 1995 after that first retirement. But the emphasis here is on the hard worker, the zealously private public figure, the work ethic, the determination. There is no final word offered on the life of Michael Jordan , now 36; that is a book that remains far from finished. Despite the retirement jokes and the obvious chasm he leaves in the sport, Mr. Jace said he hoped Jordan would not return to basketball . "If Jordan's the person I think he is," he said, "he'll do something significant." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Iverson is Pounds 44m star of the hoops ALLEN IVERSON grew up in a tenement slum where he waded through raw sewage. He hardly knew his father, his stepdad was jailed for drug dealing and, according to a cryptic Iverson, his mother "did whatever she had to do" to keep the family alive. To cap it all he was sentenced to five years in jail in 1993 for his part in a pool-room scrap in which nobody was seriously hurt. If you are looking for the ultimate rags to riches story, American basketball ace Iverson probably fits the bill. Because Iverson, now backed by a $71million (Pounds 44.4m), six-year contract with the Philadelphia 76ers, is the hot tip to replace retired legend Michael Jordan as the greatest hoops ace in the world. Certainly, the kids in Brixton who mobbed the 6ft playmaker on a recent promotional visit with shoe company Reebok, have no doubt that Iverson is - to use his U.S. nickname - The Answer To The Search For Jordan's Heir. BUT whatever the future holds for the street-wise kid from Hampton, Virginia, his presence in the NBA is the sort of story for which a Hollywood scriptwriter would pay top dollar. Ask Iverson to relive what it was like being brought up in the ghetto and he fixes you with a steely stare. Iverson, 24, said: "Coming home, no lights, no food, sometimes no water. Then, when there was water, no hot water. "Living in a house where the sewer was busted under the house and having to watch my sister walk around in her socks all day because the floor was wet from the sewage. The smell was making my sister sick. "That is why I will never talk about pressure on a basketball court. "Pressure to me is growing up where I grew up, going through the things I went through, being incarcerated. "That is why I just thank God I am where I am today." Where Iverson COULD be is in jail after he was caught up in a celebrated court case just before he was to go to a top college to continue his glittering basketball career. MORE than 40 youngsters were involved in a race fight that ended with Iverson being tried as an adult despite being only 17. He was accused of throwing a chair at a girl, even though the evidence against him was suspect, and was hammered with a five-year sentence. Iverson can now safely point out how ridiculous the charges were - but he admits the fear of the four months he spent in prison before his sentence was overturned and his record cleared, will remain with him for life. He said: "For me, to be in a bowling alley where everybody in the whole place knows who I am and to be cracking people upside the head with chairs and think nothing's going to happen? That's crazy. "And what kind of man would I be to hit a girl in the head with a chair? I wish at least they'd said I hit some damn man. "In prison there were no lights, sometimes no water, no heat, nothing to eat and I'm in jail. Part of me being in there was scared but I was never intimidated by anybody. "Still, I learn from my experiences, both good and bad. If I fall, I get back up. God puts those obstacles there for me to overcome." IVERSON is often described as his own worst enemy with a street-cred image that can strike fear into the heart of white, middle-class America. He has also been accused of surrounding himself with dubious friends, "boys" from his old neighbourhood who have sometimes dragged Iverson's name across the front pages for their brushes with the law over minor drug or gun charges. Iverson responded: "I love those guys and they care about me. When I was in jail, those guys were with me, they came to see me. "My friends were with me when I was struggling and they're with me now. They are my family." Iverson makes no apologies for the man he is. He said: "I guess the NBA needs a bad boy and apparently that is me. It is unfair, but life is unfair. "I always say if something hurts you it's cool to cry but never whine. God puts obstacles in my way to be overcome and I'm a man so I try to overcome them." HE added: "I don't cry about not making this team or that team, I just enjoy the fact that I am doing what I love to do. "I am what I am - a good person. People look at me and see the hairstyle, the jewellery and the street attitude but as long as my family and friends know I am a good person, that is all that matters."
