fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The combined forces of a star named Michael Jordan and the technology to beam his image around the world reflect a new kind of capitalism - one that breaks down national borders and turns the United States into what has been called the "capital of global arrogance." Are you reading this article because you saw an image of Michael Jordan on this page? Don't worry, you're not alone. Jordan has been affecting what many of us wear, eat, drink and, yes, even read, for some time now - even those of us who have never seen him defy gravity on a basketball court. We have laughed at Space Jam and been mesmerized by his hip commercials while sitting around in our Hanes underwear, devouring Big Macs and guzzling down Gatorade. Inspired by his Air Jordan Nike trademark, one of the most recognizable logos on the planet, we have vowed to be like Mike and "Just Do It." JORDAN For when Michael Jordan endorses, an awful lot of us buy. Sports figures, of course, long have been used to successfully sell commercial products, but Jordan has taken the marriage of athletic success and corporate power to dizzying heights. By mid-1998, Fortune magazine estimated that Jordan with his multitude of endorsements had at least a $ 10-billion impact on the economy - with about half that amount benefitting Nike, the athletic shoe company that launched His Airness. And that was only the U.S. economy. Thanks to the advent of such technologies as satellite dishes, the Internet, cable and fiber optics, Jordan hasn't just influenced the way Americans live. He has changed the lifestyles of people in nearly every nation in the world. Jordan, in fact, is a textbook study of how globalization, that buzzword of the '90s, works. Or doesn't work. That is the thesis of Michael Jordan and the New Global Capitalism, a unique foreign affairs primer by Walter LaFeber, a professor of history at Cornell University. Do you want to understand the New World Order? Study the history of basketball and the career of Michael Jordan , LaFeber says. The professor isn't joking. He believes, and argues convincingly, that the intersection of basketball , mass communications, multinational companies and a man named Michael Jordan is the perfect vantage point from which to view - and begin to understand - the cataclysmic economic changes the world is facing. Here's LaFeber's outline: The rise of basketball , since its invention in 1891, has paralleled the rise of mass communications. Saavy transnational companies such as Nike have seized upon these developments - and in particular on the extraordinary popularity of basketball megastar Michael Jordan - to sell their products worldwide. The resulting synergy has been the creation of a new global capitalism that is affecting cultures across the globe. Forget the 19th century robber barons and the wars of colonial conquest. By beaming the image and the idea of Michael Jordan across telecommunications networks of vast proportion, transnational companies have conquered from within, transcending the boundaries of individual countries. Not everyone, of course, has been happy with this state of affairs, warns LaFeber, particularly those who see this process of globalization as dominated by one country, the United States. For people as different as Islamic fundamentalist Osama bin Laden and the French cultural minister, globalization has meant nothing more than Americanization, a nefarious, repackaged version of that old '60s buzzword, American imperialism. This time around, though, it is American cultural imperialism that has these anti-globalists wringing their hands. For them, an America flexing its cultural as well as political and military muscles has become the "capital of global arrogance," says New York Times columnist Thomas Friedman, who offers his own detailed analysis of globalization and its backlash in The Lexus and the Olive Tree: Understanding Globalization. LaFeber calls it "soft power," a power that despite its name, packs a devastating punch to traditional societies. It is the power that has thrown thousands of cafes in Paris out of business as fast-food restaurants have moved in. It is the power that has convinced teenagers around the world that what they see on MTV is what is "cool" - not their own indigenous culture. It is the power of McDonald's, which, "blaring Michael Jordan 's endorsement," operates in 103 nations and feeds 1 percent of the world's population each day. Such power doesn't change only the buying habits in other countries. It also can modify the composition of those societies as native workers develop more loyalty to the multinationals they work for than to their own state, LaFeber says. "In earlier eras, a culture was transmitted across national boundaries by migration, travel or reading. Since leisure travel and literacy were often limited to the rich, the understanding - and exploitation - of other cultures

headline: MICHAEL JORDAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 best of the century One way to measure Michael Jordan 's greatness is simply to look at a photo of anguished, opened-mouthed faces. Utah fans, their expressions frozen, are cringing and grimacing as Jordan's final, championship-winning shot hangs in the air. They know the inevitable is a mere second away - the ball going in the basket as it did so often when he took a last-second shot. It was that kind of dominance, determination and unmatched skill that earned Jordan his selection as the top basketball player of the century by a six-member panel of experts assembled for the Associated Press. Oscar Robertson was second, followed by Wilt Chamberlain, Bill Russell and Magic Johnson. At No. 6 was Larry Bird, who led the Boston Celtics to three titles and whose friendly but intense rivalry with Johnson was credited with resurrecting the league in the 1980s. Rounding out the top 10 were Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, the greatest scorer in league history; Elgin Baylor, Jerry West and Julius Erving, Dr. J. DAVIS UP FOR BUCKS: Antonio Davis is ready to go. After suffering a mild concussion Tuesday against Cleveland, the Raptor centre was cleared by doctors to play tonight against Milwaukee. ''I feel fine,'' he said yesterday, still sporting a bandage over a three- stitch cut he also got. ''You mean TKO? Standing 8-Count? He's all right,'' said coach Butch Carter. CHRISTIE ON HOBBLE: Doug Christie didn't take part in a full Raptor practice last night and his availability this evening because of a sprained ankle will be a game-time decision. If Christie can't go, or has to play a limited a role, and with Tracy McGrady not available, it'll mean more minutes for Dell Curry, a former Buck. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 That's all, brother and sister. This century is about to -- as they say in the newspaper game -- have a "30" put on it. Which means it's finished. And I'm done with lists. Lists now can sit at the children's table. Lists officially have been wiped off my list. ESPN, counting down its list of the top 100 North American athletes of this century, has named Michael Jordan No. 1. Michael Jordan . The basketball player and failed baseball player and golfer. I guess, as they say in "Animal House," this list was a situation that absolutely required a really futile and stupid gesture on somebody's part. So, who else better for it than ESPN? Listen up here. Michael Jordan is not the greatest athlete of the 20th century. To call him the greatest basketball player might be palatable, although I'd have trouble getting it down. What Michael Jordan is, is ALIVE! What Michael Jordan is, is CURRENT! I demand a recount. Quite the promotional gimmick for ESPN. Jim Thorpe, dead. Jesse Owens, dead. Babe Didrikson, dead. Babe Ruth, dead. Jackie Robinson, dead. Wilt Chamberlain, dead. Sugar Ray Robinson, dead. Joe Louis, dead. Promotable Can't promote those people. Can't get any aw-shucks, don't-go-and-make-me No. 1 quotes from the deceased. Muhammad Ali, who finished third, can't be of much help with his Parkinson's Syndrome. But Michael has just been put into the larder. He's fresh. He remains very much Madison Avenue. Thorpe never wore Nikes. I don't know if Jim ever wore shoes. My understanding is that the vote by 48 distinguished panelists -- by "distinguished," I take it that most voters were from east of the Mississippi -- wasn't even close. Of course, this is the same group that made Wayne Gretzky No. 5. I don't know a thing about hockey and know Bobby Orr was the greatest hockey player. But then, Orr didn't retire last week, or maybe he would have finished higher than 31st. But Gretzky, a guy who skated for a living, ranked ahead of Thorpe (7) and Owens (6). Sure. Michael Jordan played basketball better than just about anyone who ever lived. Many say better than everyone else, and that's fine. But that's all he did great. Nothing wrong with that. He certainly belongs on the list. But, if you're going strictly by athleticism, all-around ability and accomplishment -- which, from what I gather, is what this panel was supposed to weigh -- then you can't make him the century's greatest athlete. Not over Thorpe or Jim Brown (4) or, for that matter, Bo Jackson (72). Ruth finished No. 2, and I would have had a hard time living with The Bambino, because all he did was play baseball, but I certainly would have taken him over Jordan. Because Babe was the century's first international star when the world was much larger and he remains an icon. Did Jordan dominate his sport more than the Babe? ESPN is 20, not old enough to drink, and apparently it simply forgot that sports did take place during this century's first 80 years. In trying to make a case against Ruth, ESPN tonsil Dan Patrick, whom I like very much, said: "Babe Ruth couldn't have made it today. The media would have crushed him." I don't think so. The media didn't crush Jordan when it had its chance. Sportswriters who followed Ali's globe-trotting have some tales to tell. Ali even brushed aside Vietnam and the draft. He's more popular today than ever. Besides, what does media-crushing have to do with naming the greatest athlete? The more I think about all this, the more I'm convinced that I would pick Jim Brown, another icon the media has failed to crush. Generally regarded as the greatest football player, and apparently without peer as a lacrosse player, Brown also set basketball records in high school that only were recently broken. Jim is in his 60s now and I'd wager that, if he worked out for a few months, he could play some in the NFL. His is the greatest athletic body I have seen. Only Wilt and Bo Jackson come close. Tell me, if you're going strictly on athletic ability -- and even if you're not, which apparently is what happened to this panel -- how could you possibly rank Jackson 72nd, or three behind Bonnie Blair? Jackson was a Pro Bowl running back and an All-Star baseball player. He had world-class speed. Former Chargers scout and general manager Ron Nay once told me Bo was the fastest player he'd ever put a watch on. Plus, don't forget, before his hip injury, he was Michael Jordan , the biggest name in sports. But this is more about what have you done for me lately. Jim Thorpe. Won the decathlon and pentathlon -- before the pentathlon involved guns -- in the 1912 Olympics. Probably was the first famous college football player and a charter member of the Pro Football Hall of Fame. Played major league baseball. I mean, how good was this guy? I can't imagine anyone living has seen more great athletes up close than Curt Gowdy. Curt went with Thorpe. If you're going by dominance in a

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan and Abe Pollin, the Washington Wizards' majority owner, moved closer to an agreement to have Jordan take over the team's basketball operations, a person with knowledge of the discussions said late last night. Jordan and Pollin were discussing the possibility of Jordan's coming on board, said a National Basketball Association official who requested anonymity. "It's no secret that Michael Jordan coming back to the N.B.A. is any capacity is something that would please all of us," the official said. He added, "I have yet to hear that an actual deal has been struck." Jordan's agent, David Falk, was on vacation and could not be reached for comment. The Wizards declined comment. Pollin did not immediately return a phone call asking for comment. The Wizards also said there would no announcement today. If he joined the Wizards as head of basketball operations, Jordan would oversee such duties as trade and the drafting of players. He would also be able to manage front-office personnel. The Washington Post reported today that Jordan and Pollin had reached an agreement in principle, citing unnamed sources. One component of the deal yet to be resolved is the amount of equity Jordan would assume from the minority owner Ted Leonsis and his partners. Leonsis, an executive at America Online, and two partners bought 100 percent of the Capitals and part of Washington Sports and Entertainment, which includes the Wizards, the Mystics of the Women's National Basketball Association and the MCI Center, from Pollin, the longtime owner, last year. Leonsis's group also has the right of first refusal to buy the rest of Washington Sports and Entertainment if the 76-year-old Pollin decides to sell. At 12-24 this season, they have not won a playoff game in 12 years and haven't had a sellout this season at the two-year-old downtown MCI Center, which Pollin spent $200 million to build so that a broader commercial life might return to the downtown. The Wizards' Mitch Richmond, Rod Strickland and Juwan Howard all have salaries that make them difficult to trade under the N.B.A.'s salary cap rules. Pollin invested $105 million in Howard alone during the summer of 1996. Last year, he unsuccessfully attempted to buy part of the in the Charlotte Hornets. He has said that he would not coach. The only holdup in the potential deal appeared to be Pollin, the league's senior owner, who recently sent a letter to employees saying he planned to retain majority ownership in the team. Pollin clashed with Jordan at a bargaining session during the long labor standoff in November 1998. The two began screaming at each other after Jordan told Pollin that if he could not make money, he should consider selling his team. "Abe's incredibly stubborn," the league official said. "He's also a smart and shrewd businessmen." headline: NBA OFFICIALS SAY THEY'D WELCOME JORDAN'S NEW ROLE
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The league office would be receptive to Michael Jordan returning to the NBA as a part-owner and director of basketball operations for the Washington Wizards. "We've made it clear that we think it'd be a terrific thing to have Michael Jordan involved in the NBA in whatever capacity he wants to be involved in. That's no secret," deputy commissioner Russ Granik said Friday. "That would be wherever and whatever he was interested in doing." A shroud of secrecy continued to hang over negotiations between current Wizards owner Abe Pollin, minority owner Ted Leonsis and Jordan. The Washington Post reported Friday that an agreement in principle had been reached for Jordan to take over as president of basketball operations. If negotiations continue to progress, Jordan could be running the team before the Feb. 24 trading deadline. "I think he'd be great in the job, I really do," Indiana Pacers general manager Donnie Walsh said. "He knows the players, he knows what it takes to win. In dealing with his own franchise he'll know why they are winning or not winning and what he needs to win." One major part of the deal yet to be resolved is the amount of equity in the franchise Jordan would assume from the minority ownership group. Leonsis, an executive at America Online, and two partners bought 100 percent of the NHL Washington Capitals and part of Washington Sports and Entertainment, which includes the Wizards, the WNBA's Mystics and the MCI Center, from longtime owner Pollin last year. Leonsis' group also has the right of first refusal to buy the rest of WSE when the 76-year-old Pollin decides to sell. No one was forthcoming with details about the deal Friday. "I have nothing to say about the details of any current discussions," Granik said. Pollin, Leonsis and Jordan's agent, David Falk, did not return phone calls. "We have no announcement scheduled and no comment," Wizards spokesman Matt Williams said. Still, the seemingly impending return of Jordan to the NBA was creating quite a buzz. No name in basketball commands attention like that of Jordan, whose mere presence would bring instant excitement to a moribund franchise. The Wizards, 12-24 this season, haven't won a playoff game in 12 years and haven't had a sellout this season at the 2-year-old downtown MCI Center. As head of basketball operations, Jordan would oversee the trading, signing and drafting of players and the hiring and firing of front-office personnel. Although he would be a newcomer to that end of the business, no one was expecting him to be a pushover in trade talks. "I don't think you're going to take advantage of Michael Jordan . Nobody has so far," Walsh said. "You're dealing with a basketball person who understands what you're talking about. Once you establish a relationship with him, you both know what each other are talking about. "It's the same as asking whether a great player can be a great coach, like Larry Bird. Guys who have been players in the league have ended up being good coaches and general managers. What's going to make Michael Jordan any different? I think he'll be a great general manager," Walsh said. headline: JORDAN WANTS OUT OF ENDORSEMENTS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 CHICAGO (AP) Michael Jordan is through endorsing products. One of biggest names in advertising says he no longer wants to be a corporate pitchman and has told his sponsors to gradually adopt new campaigns. "It's a stage you get past," he said in today's Chicago Sun-Times. The NBA great, now part owner of the Washington Wizards, has endorsed an array of products: Nike shoes and apparel, MCI telephone service, Hanes underwear and Ballpark Franks. In 1997, it was estimated he stood to earn $ 40 million a year in endorsements. "Now I don't want my name just used," Jordan said. "Endorsements are good for a while they give you a personality, a lot of credibility. And now I have that name. But I want to understand the business itself, see the value in something other than just endorsing." Jordan also told the Sun-Times he can't palm a basketball because of an injured finger. However, he says the injury was not a factor in his decision to retire in January 1999. He said the injury bothers his golf grip and the former minor-league baseball player said he can't hold a bat. But Jordan swears the injury did not influence his decision to retire after 13 seasons and six championships. "It didn't affect my retirement issue, although people doubt that," Jordan said. "It was ironic, the timing. But I was going to retire before this happened." Endorsements range from cereal to cologne Nike shoes and apparel MCI long distance telephone service Rayovac batteries Gatorade Ballpark Franks Hanes underwear Coach Leather products Wheaties Bijan fragrances WorldCom long-distance telephone service Upper Deck trading cards Oakley Sunglasses Wilson signature basketballs , watches and sports equipment CBS SportsLine Web site Chicago Chevrolet dealers NBA Entertainment/CBS-Fox home videos Michael Jordan 's Restaurant Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE ASSOCIATED PRESS * Who's who? fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 " Michael Jordan to the Max" @* @* @* Opens today Narrated by Laurence Fishburne. Produced by Don Kempf and Steve Kempf. Directed by James D. Stern and Don Kempf. An MVP.Com presentation. No MPAA rating, but suitable for general audiences. Running time: 45 minutes. Part A&E "Biography," part NBA infomercial and part worshipful celebrity tribute, " Michael Jordan to the Max" blows Chicago's favorite sports legend up to the size of Godzilla and even then that doesn't seem quite big enough for him. Michael Jordan has become so large and so beloved that not even script cliches and show-boating special effects can ding this spectacular, 45-minute, $ 8 million IMAX view of the Chicago Bulls star. Chicago-born Bulls fans Don Kempf and his brother Steve tracked Jordan's final three months as a Bull with their large-format cameras, then stalked him for an additional half-year after retirement. The fruits of their labors pay off with crisp basketball footage spanning a screen six or seven stories high, with a zillion megawatts of sound screaming from the scattered speakers. " Michael Jordan to the Max" begins as No. 23 dribbles toward the camera. Each bounce of the ball hits the floor with the sound of a bullet being fired. Accompanied by a slightly overblown score, the documentary whisks us through Michael Jordan 's life, reviewing his background, glimpsing his TV commercials with Spike Lee, celebrating his moments of glory and showcasing comments from his friends, co- workers, former coach Phil Jackson and media mouths who have their uncritical say on the basketball phenomenon. Not that "MJ to the Max" glosses over unpleasant truths. It delves briefly into the 1993 murder of Jordan's father, and how the sports star responded to the loss not by shaking his fist at the heavens and demanding to know why, but by being thankful for the 32 years he had to know his dad. If you listen closely, "MJ to the Max" pays as much attention to the importance of failure as it does success. "I have failed over and over and over in my life," Jordan says, "and that is why I succeed." His Airness remembers how being shut out of his varsity high- school basketball team filled him with so much pain and rejection that he rededicated himself to the sport. He would never feel that way again. As much a motivational film as a biography, "MJ to the Max" doesn't preach, but demonstrates the time-honored virtues of self- respect, perseverance, humility and optimism, all funneled through a charismatic guy who's made the most of his gifts. So what if "MJ to the Max" tosses in an occasional sports cliche ("It's as if everything he's learned all his life comes down to this moment!" the narrator burbles.) So what if the film employs that cheap and absurdly sensational "bullet-time" gimmick used in "The Matrix" where the image freezes, the camera swoops around to the other side of its subject, then motion continues? We're talking 'bout Mike here. 'Cause he can do it. " Michael Jordan to the Max" begins today at these theaters: - Marcus Imax, 1555 W. Lake St., Addison. Tickets: $ 8.50 adults, $ 6 matinees; $ 5.50 seniors and kids (12 and younger). Call (630) 889-1629. - Museum of Science and Industry Omnimax, 5700 S. Lake Shore Drive, Chicago. Tickets: $ 9 adults; $ 8 seniors; $ 7 children ages 3- 11. Call (773) 684-1414. - Navy Pier IMAX, 700 E. Grand Ave., Chicago. Tickets: $ 9 adults; $ 7 seniors; $ 6 for children 11 and younger. Call (312) 595- 0090. - Regal 20 and IMAX, Milwaukee and Aptakisic roads, Lincolnshire. Tickets: $ 8.50 adults; $ 5.50 for children 12 and younger. Call (847) 229-9100. - Six Flags Great America, 542 N. Route 21, Gurnee. Tickets: $ 5. Call (847) 249-4636. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 REVIEWS " Michael Jordan to the Max" Grade: B Imax documentary about Michael Jordan . Directed by James D. Stern and Don Kempf. Unrated. At Regal's Imax at the Mall of Georgia, Buford. 45 minutes.AJCThe verdict: It shoots; it scores. "Across the Sea of Time" Grade: B-. 3-D Imax film about New York City. Directed by Stephen Low. Rated G. At Regal's Imax at the Mall of Georgia, Buford. 51 minutes. The verdict: New York's history, way up close. Like Mount Everest, Michael Jordan has his own Imax film. It's the biggest format for the biggest sports story ever told. Part biography, part well-deserved pat on the back, " Michael Jordan to the Max" is a loving look at the Chicago Bulls superstar. It centers on the 1998 National Basketball Association playoffs, when a determined Jordan led his team to a sixth championship before retirement. For anyone unlucky enough not to have seen Jordan play ball in person, Imax is undoubtedly the next best thing. The $ 8 million movie starts with a slo- mo, head-on shot of Jordan working his way to the basket, growing nearly eight stories tall as his body fills the screen, each bounce of his basketball cracking like a cannon shot. There's a three-angle look at Jordan stealing the ball from Karl Malone in the closing seconds of the 1998 championship game and, of course, multiple looks at Jordan's final, most beautiful two-point basket to clinch the title for the Bulls. "Jordan" also details the star's North Carolina roots, his mostly embarrassing attempt at baseball (though the film is rarely at full hype, it does end this section with a Jordan home run), the loss of his father and his basketball philosophy. Such as his view of children and the sport: "Let them love the game," he says. First comes love, then comes training. Not too surprisingly, the film has a habit of ignoring Jordan's teammates. Nearly three-fourths of the way into "Jordan," there finally comes a brief, respectful homage to Scottie Pippen. Dennis Rodman, however, gets nothing. Oh, he's in the movie. It's hard to miss his colored hair during game footage. On such a big screen, it's also impossible to miss that he's a no- show at practice. New York's past in 3-D History comes alive visually in the 3-D Imax movie "Across the Sea of Time." The film, which focuses on New York, is full of big, gloriously detailed " stereoview" photographs depicting immigrant life at the turn of the century. They're similar to the flat, 2-D photos of hundreds of people jammed into Ellis Island or sunning at Coney Island that you've seen over and over again. But here, on Imax, they're in 3-D, and those past lives take on new meaning. Too bad all the provocative images get mucked up by two nagging inclusions: Donald Trump shows up in a cameo in a modern-day section, and a MetLife blimp (who wouldn't guess the company was a film sponsor?) conspicuously floats by in a view from the Empire State Building. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan was all smiles at the screening of " Michael Jordan to the Max" at Thursday's coming-out party at Navy Pier, but he ducked out of actually having to watch the movie. "I saw the film with my kids last night," he explained. "I couldn't watch it again today. Eight stories high - my head is too big!" Jordan, his wife Juanita and mother Delores were at the opening, a black-tie benefit for the Sickle Cell Disease Association. Eight hundred people attended the event held at the Crystal Garden and at the IMAX Theater at Navy Pier, many of them lining the red carpet leading to the entrance of the theater. "To the Max" opened in 50 IMAX theaters across the U.S. and Canada on Friday, including Navy Pier, the Museum of Science and Industry, Marcus IMAX in Addison, Six Flags Great America in Gurnee and the Lincolnshire Regal IMAX. "The kids loved it," Jordan said. "Jasmine (7) didn't even realize I played basketball ; she knows me as her dad, not as a Chicago Bull. The boys are playing basketball and they loved it." Jordan said he feels too young to be turned into an icon. "After all, I'm only 37 years old; this is like being some sort of older legend. I'm not as old as Ernie (Banks, who was in attendance, but didn't hear that). I get embarrassed being called a legend. Among the current and former Bulls in attendance were Elton Brand, Hersey Hawkins, B.J. Armstrong, John Paxson, Steve Kerr and Dave Corzine. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 THE inspiration struck Don Kempf while he was sitting in the back row of a giant-screen movie theatre at Chicago's Museum of Science and Industry. He decided he would use the massive IMAX screen for something other than a nature film. He wanted the world to have an eight-storey movie about one of his heroes: Michael Jordan . AP Three years later, the results of that epiphany have arrived in IMAX theatres in the United States -- an extreme close-up celebration of the world's most famous athlete, called Michael Jordan to the Max. "I don't think there's any other way that Michael Jordan should be shown," said Kempf, 33, who, with his 29-year-old brother, Steve, has formed a company called Giant Screen Sports. "Very few people have had the opportunity to see Michael Jordan that close." The Kempfs are already working on their next IMAX projects, including films about ice hockey superstar Wayne Gretzky and World Cup soccer. IMAX theatres' giant screens fill viewers' peripheral vision, immersing them in a setting. In the Jordan movie, the effect puts the audience on the basketball court with 20,000 fans clamoring all around. After the invention of IMAX techno logy more than 30 years ago, IMAX theatres were mostly in museums or amusement parks, and they tended to explore natural subjects, such as the wonder of flight and African elephants. But around the time the Kempf brothers were putting together their Jordan idea, IMAX Corp -- which makes the projection and sound systems and distributes many IMAX films -- was expanding, signing deals with commercial theatre chains. There are more than 200 IMAX theatres worldwide, 130 of them in the US. The makers of Michael Jordan to the Max believe they have hit on a winning formula. The film, which cost $12.2 million to make, follows Jordan through his final performances -- the 1998 National Basketball Association playoffs, when he led his ageing Bulls team to its sixth championship. But with non-IMAX old footage and the narration of actor Laurence Fishburne, the film also slows down to recount Jordan's life story, from his initial failure to make his high school team, through his father's murder. Capturing fast-paced sports footage presented some challenges not faced by IMAX filmmakers on savannahs or above volcanoes. IMAX cameras weigh about 60kg and are bulky. Rich Gelfond, one of the two IMAX chairmen and chief executives who bought the company in 1994 and have led its recent expansion, said the company was closely watching the fate of the Jordan movie. IMAX might even be able to show live sports events some day, he said. He also noted that two of the format's biggest stars this year had been Mickey Mouse and Michael Jordan . "Ten years ago the stars of IMAX movies were beavers," he quipped. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Just as Larry Miller settled into his new job at Nike Inc., he discovered selling Michael Jordan shoes and apparel would be much tougher than he'd imagined. In June 1998, when Miller arrived on the scene, the basketball superstar was making more money than any other athlete in corporate endorsements. Six months later, Jordan retired from the Chicago Bulls. "We lost the best marketing tool we could ever have: Michael Jordan playing basketball and slam-dunking on SportsCenter every night," Miller said. The Dallas Morning News Miller, president of Nike's Jordan brand, has spent the past 15 months trying to pump up the business despite Jordan's defection to the suit-and-tie world. It's a tricky balancing act: retain a retired athlete's image of athleticism and style while keeping the appeal fresh with a new generation of stars. Nike's launch of the Jordan brand represents a departure for the athletic shoe business. Stand-alone brands are common in such industries as automobiles and beer, but sneakers have always been identified as Nikes, Reeboks or Adidas. "Jordan is a great opportunity to introduce a brand that doesn't carry the Nike name," said Rick Burton, director of the University of Oregon's James Warsaw Center for Sports Marketing. "Now, a kid can say 'I'm wearing Jordans.' This is going to allow them to stretch their marketing in new ways." As a player, Jordan proved to be a marketing juggernaut, pitching Hanes underwear, Rayovac batteries, MCI WorldCom telephone services, Gatorade sports drinks and a half-dozen other products. Last year, Jordan decided to stop endorsing products that aren't tied to his business interests, said his agent, David Falk. Jordan has been a Nike fixture since he signed with the company in 1984. Repositioning the Jordan brand involved tweaking the marketing pitch, Miller said. "Michael is no longer playing basketball , we know that," he said. "We've got to go out and add other athletes who are relevant to the consumer." Jordan and Miller signed a half-dozen athletes they regard as worthy of the Jordan brand. Dallas Mavericks' star Michael Finley joins other players from the National Basketball Association, such as Ray Allen and Eddie Jones. Outside basketball , the Jordan brand recruited shortstop Derek Jeter of the New York Yankees, wide receiver Randy Moss of the Minnesota Vikings and boxer Roy Jones Jr. The Jordan brand's first major post-retirement test came a few months ago with the launch of Air Jordan XV, the latest version of a long line of signature basketball shoes. Previous Air Jordans were big sellers, so the expectations at Nike headquarters in Beaverton, Ore., were high. It turned out an initial production run of 200,000 pairs of the new Air Jordans in December. Miller's job was to sell them at $150 US a pair, a hefty price for the target market of 16- to 24-year-olds. And he had to do it at a time when the market for basketball shoes had been slumping for several years. But would a retired Jordan still pack marketing power? After all, nostalgia alone wouldn't be enough to sell shoes and clothing to a new generation of teenagers. Such retired sports stars as Joe DiMaggio, Jim Palmer and Joe Namath endorsed products long after their playing days ended, but they sold coffee makers, underwear and liniments. Jordan is trying to attract teens and young adults to his brand of basketball shoes and athletic apparel. "I know what (Jordan) represents," said David Carter, a principal in the Sports Business Group, a Los Angeles consulting firm. "The next generation of basketball fans are already embracing new heroes. He may not be what they're looking for." Nike, a company with revenue of $9 billion US, set up the Air Jordan brand as an independent business unit. The goal is to grow faster than Nike. Jordan brand sales hit more than $400 million US in 1999, and Miller says he wants to grow to 10 per cent of Nike's business. On his own from product design to promotion, Miller can't rely on the overpowering Nike marketing machine to create a market for Jordan brand products. "We don't have the same amount of money Nike has," Miller said. "We have to get more bang for our buck." Advertising began with print spots showing a mature Michael Jordan , posed with his six NBA championship rings on one hand. Some magazines so liked the image that they ran it for free. Television advertising followed. To the theme music of Overjoyed, the spot depicted a half-dozen other athletes sporting the Jordan brand before finishing with a serene Jordan in coat and slacks. The marketing campaign, Miller said, aimed to convey excellence, confidence and sophistication. "Michael has become much more than a basketball player," he said. "The things he represents to people is what we want to be as a brand." Although he's only 38, Jordan won't make a comeback. Last year, he bought a stake in the NBA's Washington Wizards,

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan won a court battle over the use of his name on a Chicago restaurant yesterday as a federal judge threw out a contract that gave rights to the name to two business executives. United States District Judge James Moran ruled that Gene Silverberg had materially hurt Jordan, now a executive with the Washington Wizards, by saying publicly that the basketball great had lost popularity and was a weaker restaurant draw. "There is something incongruous about a celebrity restaurant trashing the celebrity and then expecting him to appear and the restaurant to prosper," Moran said. He voided a contract that had given Silverberg and his brother, Joseph, exclusive rights to use the Jordan name on a restaurant in the six-county Chicago area. Restaurants using Jordan's name in New York and elsewhere are not affected by the lawsuit. "What this means is that Michael owns his name and no one is going to open a Michael Jordan restaurant without his permission," said Jordan's attorney, Fred Sperling. Jordan's name remains on the bankrupt and shuttered restaurant just north of Chicago's downtown district. The Silverbergs had sought to change the name to that of the Cubs slugger Sammy Sosa and open a new Michael Jordan 's on Michigan Avenue. But Jordan did not want that. A decision on whether to appeal Moran's ruling will be up to bankruptcy trustees. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 When Michael Jordan dominated on the basketball court, his arsenal included spectacular dunks, a fearsome fade-away jump shot and dogged defense. His court victory Thursday, in which he won back the exclusive right to open any Chicago-area restaurant that bears his name, relied on a weapon of a different sort: disparaging words. U.S. District Judge James B. Moran awarded Jordan the full use of his name because of shots clothing impresario Gene Silverberg took at him as Michael Jordan 's Restaurant was going under last fall. Silverberg, of the now-bankrupt Bigsby & Kruthers clothing retailer, had said during interviews that Jordan abandoned his namesake restaurant to focus on his new eatery, onesixtyblue, and charged that the basketball superstar's appeal had waned since he retired. Moran ruled those remarks violated the contract's provision that called for Silverberg and his brother, Joseph, to uphold Jordan's image. " Michael Jordan expected and was entitled to the protection and enhancement of his name," Moran said. Even though his remarks cost him the rights to use Jordan's name in city and suburban restaurant ventures, Silverberg was unrepentant afterward. "If I didn't mean them, I wouldn't have said them," he said. "If that's why we lost, I accept responsibility." Jordan was doing charity work and did not attend Thursday's hearing, but his agent, David Falk, praised the ruling while acknowledging there are no immediate plans to open another MJ- monikered restaurant. "This was not a fight Michael Jordan wanted to fight," Falk said. "This lawsuit was about defending the integrity of his name." Moran's ruling followed a weeklong trial last month in which Falk called Jordan "the most popular human being on the planet" whose name was marketing "magic in a bottle." Jordan's attorney, Fred Sperling, said the basketball star's name is his most valuable asset, and it should not be denigrated by his business partners. Silverberg admitted he took jabs at Jordan to pressure him into making more appearances at the LaSalle Street restaurant. But his attorneys claimed the remarks were protected because of an ongoing lawsuit over the opening of Jordan's other restaurant. Sam Tenenbaum, the Silverbergs' lawyer, said the brothers could appeal based on their contention that Jordan violated the contract first. As for Gene Silverberg, whose clothing empire crumbled earlier this year, he said he plans to become a clothing industry consultant. He also said he has no money left to open up the Sammy Sosa restaurant he pitched last year as a replacement for the Jordan eatery. "(My wife) Sandy owns the license, and it's available if Sammy wants it sold," he said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 CORAOPOLIS, Pa. (AP) -- Michael Jordan talked basketball again Friday. He even played it for a few minutes, too. The setting was a Five-Star Basketball Camp, which he credits with launching his NBA career.Associated Press Jordan didn't say anything especially provocative during his 45-minute talk, one of the few he has made in a basketball setting since he retired two years ago. He did say, upon questioning from the high school players, that: - Tim Duncan has the best skills of any big man now playing in the NBA; - Kobe Bryant became a complete player when he showed he could play defence, adding an element that many players neglect on their way to the NBA. - Vince Carter "needs to learn the defensive part of the game" to become a multidimensional player like Bryant. - When he was growing up, Jordan never set a time limit on how much he played. "I played until I was tired," he said. - He was angry when he initially was cut from his high school team, but he might not have become the player he did if that setback hadn't motivated him to become better. "I wasn't afraid to fail," he said. "It's part of life. I used to love constructive criticism. Sometimes you need it . . . I used to look in the mirror and say, 'He's right.' " Jordan told the campers, considered the elite group of those that attend the 25-year-old camp each summer, that he was a no-name in basketball until attending Five-Star at Robert Morris College in suburban Pittsburgh in 1980. Or, at least, he was known by a different name. "Nobody knew who Michael Jordan was," said Jordan, who was chosen as the 1980 camp's MVP. "They called me Mike." Most of Jordan's talk was technical, showing players how to move to their right or their left and perform a crossover dribble. Many of the players are among the most heavily recruited nationally, and some already have committed to major colleges. One camper, Todd Stewart, from Morgantown, W.Va., even managed to score on Jordan during his 1-on-1 game -- a memory Stewart can take with him as he transfers to Oak Hill (Va.) Academy this fall to pursue a Division I scholarship. "I was nervous," said Stewart, who might have been picked to oppose Jordan because he was wearing dark blue Duke basketball shorts, representing the archrival of Jordan's alma mater, North Carolina. "It was great just to be able to talk to him. I was scared I might fall down or something. I mean, he is Michael Jordan ." The visit to Five-Star was Jordan's first since attending the camp and was secured only after camp co-founder Howard Garfinkel made a personal visit to Jordan and "about 75 phone calls to his secretary." "You don't often get a living god lecturing in your camp," Garfinkel said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Faced with tragedy, the world's greatest basketballer bounced back, writes Sacha Molitorisz. On July 22, 1993, a 57-year-old American man went missing after attending a funeral service in North Carolina. Two weeks later, his body was found floating in a South Carolina creek, though authorities took until mid-August to identify his decomposed remains. He'd been murdered. Just another random act of violence in a gun-crazed country? Well, yes, except this time the deceased was James Jordan, father of basketball legend Michael Jordan , one of the world's best-known, most-loved, highest-paid sports stars. And, as Michael Jordan to the Max reveals, the father and son were very close. Suddenly the man who had earned three National Basketball Association (NBA) titles, two Olympic golds, a college championship and millions of dollars in endorsements was facing an entirely different, more painful challenge. Later that year, aged 30, Jordan retired. His retirement would have made a neat end to a remarkable career. Instead Jordan went on to have a bash at professional baseball (which his father had encouraged him to pursue), before returning to the Chicago Bulls in 1995 to win three more NBA championships. "If I could pass anything on to my kids, it would be to be optimistic about everything in life. And I consider myself lucky to have had my father for 30 years. Some kids never have a father," Jordan says in the 45-minute documentary. Sure, Jordan has had seven years to mull over the tragedy, and his eyes still moisten when he discusses his father's death, but his attitude reveals a rare, inspiring combination of optimism and determination. And then there's his talent - which makes for a plot that sounds more like a far-fetched, rags-to-riches yarn than a true story. First, Jordan missed selection for his high school basketball team, so he practiced relentlessly until he became exceptional. (And exceptionally tall.) Then, at college, his winning shot took the University of North Carolina to the NCAA title. Next he turned pro. After joining the Bulls, he found a soulmate in coach Phil Jackson. And he won, and won, and won ... until he tried baseball after his dad was killed, whereupon Sports Illustrated called him an embarrassment on its cover. Back to basketball then. And, finally, on to the 1998 NBA finals, which Jordan said would be his last. This time the Bulls were pitted against Karl Malone and the Utah Jazz. During game six of a best-of-seven series, the Jazz seemed to have stolen the momentum, until, with seconds left and the Bulls down by one point, Jordan stole the ball from Malone ... With a story like that, you'd have to be a fumbling fool of a film-maker not to make a good doco. The folks behind this IMAX exercise have weaved talking heads with career highlights and an amazing, Matrix-style sequence of Jordan's trademark slam dunk. While this may not be the best sports doco ever, it is engaging enough, and its subject is just about impossible to beat. Just ask Karl Malone. Michael Jordan to the Max Where IMAX Directors James D. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 MICHAEL JORDAN TO THE MAX. Giant Screen Sports, Imax, Auckland. Reviewer: Duncan Johnstone. THERE'S a good chance you never saw Michael Jordan play basketball live. Here's the next best thing. The man more synonymous with flight than the Wright brothers, has his own movie. And, appropriately for a player bigger than the game, the movie is larger than life. To see Jordan soar towards the hoop - including one sequence enhanced by special effects technology used for The Matrix - on the giant Imax screen is mindblowing. The camera work puts the viewer courtside, on the bench and on the court, following Jordan's quest to end his career with the 1998 NBA title. That mission is interwoven with Jordan's life story. Like any kid he had his sporting knockbacks at school. His initial love was baseball - something he would pursue professionally late in his career at the insistence of his father - but he quickly developed a basketball talent that led to college and NBA glory. He speaks candidly of his drive to become the best. Coaches, team-mates, opponents and marketing men also explain the legend. Jordan's professionalism was total. He reasoned that every time he played there was always someone in the crowd watching him perform for the first time. He challenged himself to make sure that person went home satisfied and with a memory of Michael Jordan . Now he has retired, no one will have that pleasure again. The best alternative is this 45-minute film, a two year effort which cost $ US9 million to make. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 A basketball -loving teenager who almost lost the power of speech after a Michael Jordan -style slam dunk left him badly injured was in court yesterday seeking millions in damages. Hui Yiu-wing, now 19, is said to have suffered severe brain injuries after he was hit on the head by a basketball post which collapsed as he hung his arm on to the ring in a Fanling public playground on June 29, 1994, when he was 12 years old. The now-defunct Regional Services Department, which operated the playground, admitted liability for the young man's injury but is contesting the claim for damages. It is understood Mr Hui is seeking about $ 6 million. Nicholas Pirie, counsel for the teenager, said: "It must be perfectly foreseeable that a young boy playing basketball would imitate what Michael Jordan does." After the accident, a public outcry caused the Regional and Urban Services departments to test all their basket stands. At least 16 others were found to be defective, Mr Pirie said. Mr Hui, then a secondary school student at an "excellent government school", had to suspend studies for nine months. He was admitted to the intensive care unit of the Prince of Wales Hospital and underwent brain surgery. "What's most striking is his language impairment . . . and personality change is likely to affect his initiative and judgment," Mr Pirie said of Mr Hui. The young man had to attend a practical school afterwards but his performance was poor. He failed to secure a stable job after leaving school and now works as a painter and decorator's apprentice. Mr Pirie asked the court to assess the plaintiff's future loss of income on the basis that he would have become a policeman or a fireman but for the tragedy. "The (claim) is not based on the fact that he could have been an Einstein or Bill Gates, or that he would have gone to medical school or become a lawyer," said Mr Pirie, who also told the court Mr Hui's parents had great hopes for him before the accident. The Court of First Instance hearing continues before Mr Justice Andrew Chung On-tak. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 What if Michael Jordan and LA Lakers star Kobe Bryant came from Humpty Doo? It sounds ridiculous, but the idea of world-class basketballers coming out of Darwin's rural hinterlands shouldn't be dismissed. And if Humpty Doo teenagers Matthew Gregor and Tim Cooper have any say in the matter, their hometown courts will be as revered as any of those found in the backstreets of Harlem. Model Gregor and Cooper, both 13, will represent the NT at the Sydney Youth Olympic Festival starting Wednesday. "My role model, like everyone else, is Michael Jordan and I dream of playing professionally one day," Cooper said. Australian Olympic Committee spokesman Bob Elphinstein said the AOC was excited at the prospects of harvesting sporting talent from rural areas. "Country towns have produced many champion athletes and Humpty Doo is obviously no exception," Elphinstein said. "We are very excited that kids from remote areas are getting a chance to compete at the elite level," he added. But, NT basketball coach Dean Wilson has warned his young squad -- a mix of 14 to 17 year-olds -- to expect some tough competition in the early rounds at the Penrith Basketball Stadium. "It's the best of the best of Australian junior basketball talent and while it will be a good experience for our guys, it'll also be very tough," Wilson said. He said the teams to beat would be Victoria and the Oceania Selection, a side comprising of the cream of South Pacific junior basketball talent. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan , beleaguered boss of the 9-34 Washington Wizards, is still wondering what could have and should have been with the Chicago Bulls. "I'm totally frustrated," Jordan said of rebuilding the Wizards. Jordan said he doesn't understand why the Bulls never offered him an ownership role, as the Wizards did. The Chicago Sun-Times "I'm not mad, but I'm puzzled," he said. For that matter, he still doesn't understand why the championship Bulls were broken up. If Scottie Pippen and Phil Jackson were still around, Jordan said, the Bulls would be going for their ninth championship this season. "We'd have been fine the last two years," he said. "Sure, I would have loved to keep playing." But Jordan, 37, ruled out following in the footsteps of Mario Lemieux, who has come out of retirement. "I'm up to 230, and I have a gut," Jordan said. "I have my days of 'What if?' That's just natural, especially with the young talent out there and the success they have, from the Kobe Bryants to the Vince Carters. Sure, I have 'What if?' questions with myself. But nothing to the point that drives me to getting in shape and playing basketball again. I've entered another walk of life. I'm content with that." The cutthroat competitor, the superhero with the dangling tongue and the inferno eyes, isn't handling failure well. It has reached the point where Jordan sometimes can't watch the Wizards in his arena suite, where the few thousand fans who show up frequently are booing. He has taken to staying in his office, where he can suffer alone while watching on his flat-screen TV. "I've never been this frustrated," Jordan said. "Never. It's a totally new experience. Instead of playing to win, these guys play not to lose. They are totally scared, and it's embarrassing to sit there and watch the game. I understand why the fans boo. You should see me." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BASKETBALL IN basketball , when you think of mental toughness and will-to-win, the name Michael Jordan always springs to mind. Kings coach Brett Brown reckons the Townsville Crocodiles' ageless import Robert Rose is in the same vein. Rose, even at 36 years of age, is an impressive force in the NBL. He looms as the man standing between the Kings and a top-four finish when they take on Townsville at the Super Dome tonight. "With Robert, the thing that has always impressed me is that he is as mentally strong a person as I have been around," said Brown. "You compare his sort of mental toughness with somebody like Michael Jordan . "Combine that with Robert's big-time talent, and it makes him one of the few people who can carry a team and win the game off his own back in this league." Sydney face a double dose of Rose, with the team travelling to Townsville to face the Crocs at the Swamp next Friday in the final round of the NBL regular season. The Kings (16-9) need both wins over Townsville as well as taking care of the Canberra Cannons at home next Saturday to sneak into fourth spot. Despite winning nine of their last 10 games, beating the Crocs twice won't be easy. After losing Australian Boomers swingman Sam Mackinnon for the season, the third-placed Crocs (19-6) believe they can still secure the minor premiership if they win their remaining three games. To prove it, they have produced a league best eight-game winning streak. This run includes last round's 126-63 blowout at home against the Brisbane Bullets. "I think we are a better team than last year. We are as good a chance as anyone this year," said Crocs coach Ian Stacker. "I think anyone can win it this year. It depends on who gets going on the night." He put the team's run of success primarily down to Rose, whose performances have him in contention for MVP honours. He has averaged 21 points per game to go with seven rebounds and six assists. "He is playing as well if not better than in '93 when he was MVP," Stacker said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan and Doug Collins, whose competitive natures clashed with the Chicago Bulls in the 1980s, are together again with the Washington Wizards. Their relationship, come September, is still undecided. Will Collins be coaching for Jordan, or will Jordan be playing for Collins? Yesterday, Jordan was the boss. The Wizards' part-owner and president of basketball operations hired Collins, an NBA analyst and former coach of the Bulls and Pistons, as the team's sixth head coach in 25 months. "Why? Why do you want to do this?" said Collins, anticipating the reaction. "It's easy -- because Michael Jordan called me on the phone and said, 'I need you. Can you come help me?' It was easy once he said that." Collins follows a trail littered with unsuccessful attempts to turn around the moribund Wizards, who haven't won a playoff game in 13 years. He replaces Leonard Hamilton, a rookie coach hand-picked by Jordan last summer after a successful college career at Miami. Hamilton resigned Wednesday night after the season finale. The Wizards finished 19-63, the third-worst record in the NBA, and set a franchise record for losses in a season. His replacement, Collins, coached Jordan in Chicago for three years before being fired after the 1988-89 season. "I think his coaching ability is desperately something we need to educate the young talent we have," Jordan said. Both men admitted they didn't get off to a great start in Chicago, but Jordan said they patched things up quickly. He said the widely perceived notion that he was responsible for getting Collins fired in 1989 was not correct. Jordan said Collins' hiring "is not depending upon whether Michael Jordan plays basketball again." "There's been a lot of conversation about Michael Jordan playing basketball again," Jordan said. "I've said, and I still hold true to my words, that there's a 99.9 percent chance that I won't." But, he added: "Three months from now, I can't tell where I'm going to be. Hopefully, I'll still be breathing. But if you ask me my opinion, as of right now, I'm not playing basketball next season, and Doug understands that." Pistons fire Irvine Two coaches fired [] The Detroit Pistons are looking for their sixth coach in nine years. Team president Joe Dumars fired George Irvine as the Pistons continue to search for ways to return to their glory years, which included consecutive titles in 1989-90. Five coaches have come and gone since Chuck Daly resigned after nine seasons in 1992. [] Randy Wittman was fired as coach of the Cleveland Cavaliers after a 30-52 season. Pick any of the following reasons: A 10-game losing streak, two seven-game slides and an embarrassing 35-point home loss to New York. headline: MICHAEL JORDAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Most sports careers follow an arc: lowly apprentice- ship, rising stardom, dizzy heights, peak of success, downward slide. The pattern's almost inevitable. The terminal phase is the hardest to cope with, but those who are smart (the Eric Cantonas) avoid it by getting out early, to set up a sportswear business, become an actor or, as it used to be, run a pub. The less smart soldier on, in pain and humiliation. The least smart temporarily retire - then make a comeback. No one expected a comeback from Michael Jordan , the former basketball star and, until Tiger Woods recently overhauled him, the biggest earner from sports advertising the world has ever known. He seemed too rich, too wise, too busy directing and funding his own basketball team, the Washington Wizards - and, at 38, too old. But the Wizards have been performing dismally, and American basketball is suffering from a slump in popularity. So the King of the Hoop has let it be known that he's contemplating a return to court. If he does, it will end in tears. The history of sport - and of pop music - is littered with examples of embarrassing comebacks. Bjorn Borg, for example, coolly dropped out at 26, with a string of Wimbledon titles to his name. But 10 years later a rush of blood to the head brought him back clutching his old racquet - and soon regretting it. Now he makes do with playing against fellow veterans. The disgraced Canadian sprinter Ben Johnson made a comeback, too, when his drug ban expired. But, surprise surprise, he never reached the highs to which illegal substances had once taken him. George Best had several years of comebacks. He'd have done better to stay in bed with his popsies. These days Best specialises in coming out of rehab into the Sky sports studio, where on good days he's an excellent panellist. That other wasted genius Paul Gascoigne made a comeback, for Everton reserves, only last week, before being taken off injured after half-an-hour. His best bet now would be to stay away but to keep the rumours going of an impending return. Jesus Christ has been doing that for 2,000 years, and look how famous he is. But not all comebacks are doomed. "Shoot me if I go near a boat again," Steve Redgrave said after picking up his fourth Olympic rowing gold. Fortunately, no one followed orders when he did, and he went on to win a fifth. Lester Piggott took to the saddle again in his mid-fifties, and still rode winners. George Foreman, whose greatest moment was against Muhammad Ali in 1974, entered the ring again in the 1990s, at 48. Perhaps he's still at it. Perhaps he'll get a shot at Lennox Lewis, or Lewis will get a shot at him - his hasn't been an ignominious return. For promoters, it's lucrative to have celebs put their gloves or boots back on. For audiences, it's ghoulishly fascinating, and sometimes inspiring. But what's in it for the comeback men themselves? What's Michael Jordan after? Though 1960s rock bands have been known to re-form and go on tour in order to boost the old boys' pensions, money isn't a factor with Jordan. Nor does he need the glory, he says. If it's only, as reported, that he wants to lose some weight, why not work out in private, in the gym? The obvious explanation is that he misses the buzz - the adrenalin and applause. Whether he plays or not, he'll always be a celeb. But when a man's celebrity is based on something he no longer does, he can feel very strange about it - exiled from himself and unentitled. Going back on court would be Jordan's way of confirming and vindicating himself: of showing the world there's still a brand to justify the brand-name. And who's to say he couldn't still do the business? Stanley Matthews went on playing football till he was 53. Though women have been known to make comebacks (think of swimmer Sharron Davies, or singer Debbie Harry), I suspect there's something male and middle -aged about the compulsion. Certainly, anxiety about waning powers drives many a man around Jordan's age to make some wild gesture - take up paragliding, begin affairs, go AWOL, self -destruct in workaholism or drink. At least shooting baskets is relatively healthy. It might save Jordan from something riskier. I confess to being fascinated by this business of comebacks, having made one of my own last Sunday when, after two decades of couch potato-dom, I ran in the London Marathon. My only previous shot at the event had been in 1981. We're talking modest and amateur here. This won't rank as one of the great sporting returns. Yes, it was gratifying to take five minutes off my 1981 time and to come home an hour and a half ahead of Steve Redgrave. But three hours 23 minutes still only made me 3,000th-odd. I couldn't run one mile at the pace the top runners go, let alone 26. No, the great thing about this comeback was that no one but me could care less how fast I ran. The sports writer Frank Keating slagged off marathon ent

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Well, it's hot right now to mock Michael Jordan . He hasn't exactly been Jerry West as architect of the Washington Wizards, has he? That team stinks, he's dumping salaries, he's firing coaches like old George Steinbrenner, and all along Jordan kept saying he was 99.9 percent sure he would not come back when all his friends knew it was precisely the other way around. Now, all sorts of people say they can't wait to see Michael Jordan come back after three years and get taken to school by Allen Iverson, Tracy McGrady and Ray Allen and all the kids. To which I say: please. The Kansas City Star If Michael Jordan comes back (and it looks like he will drag along his buddy Charles Barkley) he will be the most dominant player in the NBA again. Don't kid yourself. He might not score 37 a game, like when he was young and could fly. Might not be named to the NBA's all-defensive team, like he did nine times before. He might never win another dunk contest. He might get scorched by an Iverson crossover dribble on a Tuesday in Philly. But, at last check, basketball was still about winning. Ask yourself this question: who would you rather have with the ball and the clock running down than Michael Jordan ? Allen Iverson? Kobe Bryant? Jason Williams? Please. Jordan knows things. He knows how to get fouled. He knows how to work free for his shot. He knows how to sneak behind players and strip the ball clean. He knows how to jump at precisely the right instant and block a shot. He knows how to slide through and grab an offensive rebound. He knows how to find the open man. He knows how to get inside players' heads, move without the ball, work the refs. He knows how to calm his heartbeat when the game's on the line. He knows how to win. You think that goes away? Please. NBA ratings have taken a free fall since Jordan left because nobody - not Shaq, not Kobe, not Dirk, not Vince - plays like Jordan played. Maybe they can sky higher, dunk harder, shoot the three better. But they can't play like him. Basketball , the game, is dying out there. One pass, back your man down, send the double team, it's so bloody boring that NBA people, in absolute desperation, made zones legal. Nice move. Next, they will make fair-catching a punt illegal. And you're telling me that in this shriveling league, with this kind of lousy basketball going, that the kids are going to school Michael Jordan ? Are you insane? That's the whole problem with the NBA and its few remaining apologists - they believe basketball 's about athleticism and power and hard fouls and jumping ability. It's not. Basketball , when it's right, is about precision and teamwork and taking a charge and shooting mid-range jump shots and doing the little things and finding ways to win. Jordan learned that. Then, after the kooky baseball experiment, Jordan came back with a new weapon - that devastating turnaround jumper - and a kind of hunger that burned through opponents. You say he's too old? Please. Jordan's 38. John Stockton's 39. Karl Malone's going to be 38 in a month. Reggie Miller's almost 36. If you know how to play, you know how to play. You say he will have lost his skills? Please. Nobody knows more about coming back than Jordan. He missed a year and a half playing baseball, and in his second game back, scored 55 at Madison Square Garden. After one off-season of training, he was the best player in the world again. You say the Wizards are so bad that Jordan can't turn them around? Please. The Eastern Conference stinks. The Charlotte Hornets were turned around by Jamal Mashburn. Jamal Mashburn! Tell me Jordan can't make a difference. You say the game has passed Jordan by, and today's players are too good? Not even going to dignify that with a response. He's the best ever. He's got the biggest heart. He can singlehandedly make the NBA interesting again. Yet people continue to underestimate him. Don't get it. One friend says if Jordan comes back he will be "one of the 20 best players in the league, but no better." All I can tell you is, my friends can be awfully dumb. You never, ever, ever bet against Michael Jordan . fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Each year pro basketball teams get to pick new players from a group of college stars (and even some high school standouts) who have decided to go pro. And the National Basketball Association (NBA) uses numbered ping-pong balls to decide who gets to pick first, second, third, and so on. This year the numbered ping-pong balls fell in perfect order for the Washington Wizards, who finished the season with a 19-63 won-loss record. So on June 27, the Wizards and their president of basketball operations, Michael Jordan , will get to draft the very first player from this year's group of newcomers. Special to The Washington Post That means the Wizards will get the best player in the draft. Right? Well, maybe. But maybe not. Just because the Wizards can pick the very first player does not mean they will get the best player. Oh, sure, some great players have been the No. 1 pick. How about this for a list of former No. 1 picks who are now NBA all-stars: * Tim Duncan (No. 1 pick in 1997) * Allen Iverson (1996) * Shaquille O'Neal (1992) But some not-so-hot players have been picked No. 1, too: * Michael Olowokandi (1998) * Joe Smith (1995) * Derrick Coleman (1990) Sometimes the best player in the draft is not the first player picked. No one knows that better than Michael Jordan . When Jordan came out of college in 1984, he wasn't the No. 1 pick in the NBA draft. He wasn't even No. 2. The Chicago Bulls picked Michael Jordan with the third pick in the draft. Who was picked in front of Michael Jordan ? The first pick was Hakeem Olajuwon. A prime-time center and 12-time all-star who led the Houston Rockets to two NBA titles, Olajuwon was not a bad pick at all. The second pick in 1984 was Sam Bowie. You've never heard of Sam Bowie? He was a 7-foot-1 center from the University of Kentucky who spent most of his short NBA career on the bench or in the hospital. Bowie was always hurt. Believe me, he was no Michael Jordan . So right now, the pressure is on Jordan to make the right move with the No. 1 pick. Maybe he'll pick Duke star Shane Battier. Maybe he'll take some high school hot shot and hope that the kid becomes the next Kevin Garnett or Tracy McGrady. Or maybe he'll give up his No. 1 spot to a team willing to give one (or even more) of its proven players to the Wizards. But Jordan better score big with this No. 1 pick -- he'd better pick a Tim Duncan instead of a Michael Olowokandi -- or the Wizards will be worrying about ping-pong balls instead of basketballs for years to come. Fred Bowen is the author of sports novels for kids. headline: JORDAN IN COMEBACK
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan is making a comeback. The basketball superstar, who who retired three years ago after leading the Chicago Bulls to a sixth NBA championship, will play for the Washington Wizards. 'I am returning as a player to the game I love,' said the 38-year-old Jordan. Since the National Basketball Association does not allow players to have an ownership stake in any team, Jordan will relinquish his five to 10 per cent share of the Wizards. He will also resign as the team's president of basketball operations, a position he had held since January 2000. 'This will be great for the league,' said Philadelphia 76ers coach Larry Brown. 'You're talking about arguably the greatest player that has played our game, the greatest athlete in a team sport.' 'I don't expect to see anything less than a great player.' Jordan signed on for two years with the Wizards and said he would donate his entire salary for the upcoming season to relief and assistance efforts for victims of the September 11 attacks on New York and Washington. Due to the Wizards' dire salary cap situation, Jordan will reportedly earn just $ 1.3 million this season, a massive cut from his last year with the Bulls, when he earned more than $ 33 million. NBA Commissioner David Stern said: 'I am happy to welcome Michael Jordan , the player, back to the NBA, although as Commissioner I'm sorry to lose him in the boardroom. 'Michael has always brought joy to basketball fans around the world, and in these difficult times we can all use a little more joy in our lives.' Prior to Jordan's announcement, the lowly Wizards were scheduled to appear on national television only once. That will surely change with the return of the phenomenally popular Jordan, widely considered the best basketball player ever. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan 's comeback isn't the sort of return that will send biblical scholars and theologians on a long-awaited pilgrimage. If Jordan thinks his third coming is historic, not many pews are filled with followers. There will be no star in the east. If he's waiting for a visit from three wise men, Jordan must remember he's returning to the NBA, where it's difficult to find three wise men. They certainly aren't among those who pay exorbitant prices to watch the slop Mike left behind. There is so much controversy surrounding this. So many people who want Mikey to remain retired, so many who don't. To this I say: What difference does it make? If Michael Jordan wants to end his three-year hiatus and return to the game he played as well as anybody has, congratulations. Just as long as we remember he's a basketball player. If you put a sea out there in front of him, he isn't going to walk on it or part it. He's a 38-year-old basketball player coming back to play in a bad league he made worse by leaving. Already I'm hearing Jordan's return is just the tonic America needs at the present time. America doesn't need Michael Jordan . Michael Jordan needs Michael Jordan . Ahmad Rashad needs Michael Jordan . And the NBA, of course, needs Michael Jordan , because he's going to draw some lookie-loos, those who normally wouldn't even consider spending 24 seconds -- let alone an evening -- with professional basketball . The nice thing is that Jordan will donate his salary for the coming season to those relief agencies working with the victims of the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks. Not that I'm knocking the generosity, but he could have done that, easily, without playing again. "I'm happy to welcome Michael Jordan back to the NBA," gushes commissioner David Stern, who has discovered he isn't quite the marketing whiz he was cracked up to be since Jordan retired in 1999. Stern is driving that welcome wagon, you bet. The big concern among the Jordan worshipers who don't want him to return is that he could embarrass himself and forever smudge a brilliant career that saw him win five MVP awards and lead the Chicago Bulls to six NBA titles. But that shouldn't be a concern, because I don't believe it's going to happen. My guess is that Jordan is a pretty fair judge of his body and his talent. There's no chance he's going to be as good as he once was. Even His Airness knows gravity. Gravity is a monster. I'm convinced he's the type of athlete who isn't going to hang around if he can't play anymore. The last thing we want to see again is Willie Mays struggling to play center field with the Mets or Sugar Ray Robinson losing to stiffs. I don't believe that's going to happen with Jordan. He won't be da Vinci with the shakes. Maybe he needs his ego stroked, but it isn't going to be if he can't play anymore. Face it, ego or no ego, Jordan wouldn't be doing this if he didn't know he could without stumbling. He has sized up this wrinkled suit that the NBA has become and he probably sees himself still in a tux. If Jordan is half as good as he was the year he quit -- for the second time -- he'll be better than most players in the league. For one thing, he'll go after it. Say what you want about Jordan, but he always showed up. He never took a night off in a sport where vacations are frequent. If you came to see Jordan play -- and millions did -- you weren't disappointed. The understudy never went on. Jordan says the primary reason he's doing this is to teach the young Washington Wizards "and help them elevate their game to a higher level." This team he helped put together, as Washington's president, won 19 games last season. He has some serious teaching to do. "I know there are a whole lot of naysayers out there," Jordan said earlier this month. "Winning isn't always championships. What's wrong with helping kids find their way, teaching them the game?" Well, there's nothing wrong with it, although he could do that by merely practicing with the team. For certain, he can teach the players work ethic, about being professional, two things seriously lacking in the NBA. But this comeback isn't about teaching. Jordan misses the competition. As for the limelight, I'm not so sure. Jordan is too big a name to disappear overnight. His return with diminished skills isn't going to do much to enhance the legend. It's his life, his gig. If he wants to come back, that's OK with me. Are you retired? Are there people out there cautioning you about going back to work? Michael Jordan is bored. He isn't a good enough golfer to join the tour. He still can play basketball and get in his 18 holes. And if he finds he's lost too much, that he isn't close to Jordanesque, he always can re-re-retire. Won't be a problem with me, one way or the other. I won't think any less of him. I can remember the greatness. But I hope we're not watching Johnny Unitas in a Chargers uniform. We don't need
