fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Two retirements and two comebacks, and it all boils down to one thing: Michael Jordan is a basketball player. Not an executive. Not a spectator. Not a pitchman. Not a baseball player. Not a golfer. Not a homebody. Not a retiree. The Associated Press A basketball player who wants to play basketball again. Simple as that. "I'm doing it for the love of the game. Nothing else," Jordan said in the first week of September, in all but confirming his return. He made it official Tuesday, using similar language: "I am returning as a player to the game I love." He is 38 and will turn 39 in February, but he no doubt believes he can still step onto the court, size somebody up and beat him. His father, the late James Jordan, called it a "competition problem." In the three years since he retired from the Bulls, the problem with his competition problem was a level playing field. He played high-stakes golf matches against ordinary men who nonetheless could find themselves his equal if they could shoot anywhere near par. He talked trade with 28 other team executives whose savvy and smarts are calculated in terms of their knowledge of salary caps, trade exceptions and the other details that NBA executives must master. The competitive advantage he had enjoyed as a basketball player -- simply knowing that he was better -- was absent. Jordan had faded in the public spotlight, pitching sports drinks, batteries and long-distance service to a public far less enamoured of him than it was just three years ago. He sat in his luxury box at Wizards games, smoked cigars, scarfed down steaks and watched his once-impeccable body slowly grow softer as middle age drew nearer. The basketball -viewing public had become enamoured with a new generation of stars -- the smoothness of Kobe Bryant, the athleticism of Vince Carter, the dynamism of Allen Iverson. Jordan found himself becoming irrelevant, moving further away from what made him who he is, wondering if his departure from basketball was as premature as it was beginning to feel. He had said, after all, that he would have remained in the NBA if the Chicago Bulls had kept their dynasty intact. They didn't, and he left on someone else's terms. This will be his chance to make himself matter again. In a way, it's the ultimate competition: The present-day Michael Jordan vs. the memory of the old Michael Jordan . By the time training camps open Oct. 2, a total of 1,205 days will have passed since Jordan, in what has come to be known as his "Final Shot," stole the ball from Karl Malone, dribbled downcourt, placed a hand on the hip of defender Bryon Russell, shoved him away and sank a 20-foot jumper -- leaving his hand in an extended follow-through for several seconds -- to give the Chicago Bulls the last of their six titles. It was considered the perfect, storybook ending to a spectacular career. Jordan won the NBA championship in each of his final six full seasons, a scoring title in each of the final 10 seasons in which he played all or nearly all of the 82-game schedule. The only hiatus during that span were the 1993-94 and 1994-95 seasons after he tried to become a professional baseball player. Jordan had said at his first retirement news conference that he felt he had nothing left to prove. Traumatized by the murder of his father, tired of the media's scrutiny of his gambling, he said he simply wanted to stay home and watch the grass grow -- and cut it himself. But he quickly turned to a new form of competition, a sport he hadn't played since high school: baseball. For a year and a half, Jordan rode buses between small cities in the South, his companions a collection of fresh-faced hitting and pitching prospects who were awestruck in his presence. Jordan reported to spring training in 1995 along with other minor-leaguers and discovered the Chicago White Sox were splitting their roster in two -- those who said they would perform as replacement players because major-leaguers were on strike, and those who said they would not. Just five days after leaving spring training, Jordan began showing up at the Bulls practice facility in suburban Chicago. Within days, there was speculation that a basketball comeback was in the offing. With a two-word statement faxed to the Bulls office -- "I'm back" -- Jordan's first return was cemented. He scored 55 points against the Knicks in an electrifying performance at Madison Square Garden in his fifth game back, but Chicago was knocked out of the playoffs by Shaquille O'Neal and the Orlando Magic in the sixth game of the Eastern Conference semifinals. Jordan dribbled the ball off his foot and out of bounds late in Game 6 -- arguably the last major mistake he made in a big game. With a full training camp behind him the next season, Jordan's Bulls won an unprecedented 72 regular-season games to break the NBA record of 69. The first of three more titles followed, and last of which c

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 I have read so many comments and articles about how too much is being made of superstar Michael Jordan 's return to professional basketball ("Jordan giddy and confident: Wizard plans to play in all 82 regular-season games," Cover Story, Sports, Tuesday). Jordan is the greatest athlete ever to play any sport. He made us love the game of basketball , because he loves the game of basketball . For many, basketball has become a game of money and disloyalty. But Jordan stayed with the Chicago Bulls through thick and thin. Not only that, he was never really paid what he was worth. Did he complain? No, he just won championships! Other current NBA stars should take note of that. So stop criticizing Jordan and let him do what he does best: play basketball and help the nation and economy grow again. Derrick Montanye Wilmington, N.C. Quit picking on Jordan Do people who say Michael Jordan shouldn't come back because he will "tarnish" his image have any historical perspective at all? No one thinks any less of former San Francisco 49ers quarterback Joe Montana and defensive star Ronnie Lott or heavyweight boxing champion Mohammed Ali for squeezing every last drop out of the talent they possessed before letting go. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 He's back. Michael Jordan , six-time world champion, 10-time scoring leader in the National Basketball Association and maybe the greatest player ever, is returning to pro basketball and will play for the Washington Wizards. Special to The Washington Post No doubt Jordan's comeback will be fun and exciting for hoops fans. But let's get to the big question: How good will Michael Jordan be? I mean, no one can expect even Michael Jordan to be the high-flying, slam-jamming all-around superstar he was in his 20s. Heck, in those days, Jordan still had his hair. Three years ago, during his last season, Jordan was 35 and still pretty terrific. He even won his fifth Most Valuable Player award. But Jordan was not as good as he had been when he was younger. Now Michael Jordan is 38. I know that some older baseball players -- Barry Bonds (37), Roger Clemens (39) and Randy Johnson (38) -- have played great this year. But this is pro hoops. I can't think of a single NBA player who actually got better between the ages of 35 and 38. Another thing: Jordan will not be playing for the world champion Chicago Bulls. He'll be playing for the bottom-of-the-barrel Wizards. No more prime-time players such as Bulls teammates Scottie Pippen and Ron Harper. Instead, Jordan will be teaching NBA newcomers such as Kwame Brown and Courtney Alexander what it takes to win. OK, so he won't be the same old Michael Jordan . But will he be good enough to help the Wizards? Well, let's guess that after three years of sitting around and playing golf, Jordan will be 75 per cent of what he was during his last year as a pro. Can a player like that help the Wizards? No question about it. headline: MICHAEL JORDAN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 On Tuesday, hundreds of millions of people across the world will tune into a basketball game. It's not an international match, a final, or even a play-off. But as the NBA season begins, all eyes will be on Madison Square Garden, New York, where Michael Jordan , arguably the greatest basketball player of all time, will return from retirement to the professional game. Basketball has become big business. The best players, like Jordan in his prime, can earn eight-figure salaries, plus their income from sponsorship deals. It's a far cry from the wildest expectations of the sport's founder, Dr James Naismith, who nailed two peach baskets on to opposite walls of a YMCA gymnasium in 1891. Dr Naismith had been given 14 days to design an indoor game to provide "athletic distraction" for the college's football and lacrosse players during the winter. Inspired by a childhood pastime, he drew up 13 rules of basketball - 12 of which are still adhered to. During that first winter, the sport was played between teams of nine, with a simple leather football. Every time a basket was scored, the college janitor would have to climb his ladder to retrieve it. The sport spread rapidly, and the first professional league was founded only seven years later. Basketball was soon being played across the United States and Canada, before it arrived in Europe. Today, there are more than 300 million players worldwide. The game is played between two teams of five. Two or three points are awarded for a basket, depending on the player's proximity to the basket when the shot is taken. Single points can be picked up from "foul" shots, after an infringement. Matches are played in quarters, each consisting of 10 minutes. The NBA remains the biggest basketball league in the world. There are two British players competing in it - Michael Olowokandi at the LA Clippers and John Amaechi at Utah Jazz. The latter, who grew up in Manchester, is reportedly the highest-paid British sportsman. His five-year contract at Orlando Magic was worth pounds 37.7m before he opted to move to his current team at the end of last season. The UK, which boasts an estimated two million active players, has a professional league of its own - the BBL. There are 13 clubs in the premier division, which tends to be dominated by the "big three" of London Towers, Sheffield Sharks and Birmingham Bullets. Next week: Rugby Union The facts Michael Jordan 's professional comeback - with the Washington Wizards, against the New York Knicks - will be screened live on BBC1 this Tuesday at 11.05pm. HYPERLINK http://www.basketball-scotland.com 00 basketball HYPERLINK http://www.basketball-scotland.com 00 -scotland.com ), or the Basketball Association of Wales ( HYPERLINK http://www.bballwales.enta.net 00 www.bballwales.enta.net ). The English men's senior team play Slovenia in a European championship qualifier at Coventry Sky Dome, 24 November (0247 663 0693). For comprehensive NBA updates, scores and information, visit HYPERLINK http://www.nba.com 00 www.nba.com . There is a special section on the site for the UK ( HYPERLINK http://www.nba.com/uk 00 www.nba.com/uk ) and John Amaechi has a personal website ( HYPERLINK http://www.meech.org 00 www.meech.org ). Other useful websites are HYPERLINK http://www.bbl.org.uk 00 www.bbl.org.uk and HYPERLINK http://www.fiba.com 00 www.fiba.com , the sport's international body. Ball Balls range in price from around pounds 6 to pounds 50. Rubber balls are light, inexpensive and good for outdoor use. Synthetic leather is suitable for indoor or out. Match balls of leather composite are more solid, softer to handle and allow a better grip but won't last long in the back garden. Adult balls are a size seven, two sizes larger than a football. Baden is the most popular make in the UK but worldwide Spalding has the edge: their Infusion ball, pounds 39.99, available in the UK from November, is an all-surface, synthetic ball with an in-built two-inch pump to combat mid-game deflation. FIBA requires an all-tan ball at international level but national leagues often use the tan and white balls to increase visibility for spectators. Clothing and shoes Shoes, boots and half-boots designed for basketball give varying degrees of ankle support, flexibility and movement. Comfort is key, and look for a cushioned sole and heel and good traction. A reasonable pair will cost you pounds 50 to pounds 60; prices go up to pounds 140, but beware of paying just for names or high-tech gimmicks. Players usually wear a baggy vest and long shorts in breathable material such as mesh or soft polyester. For games, the vests

headline: BACK AT GARDEN
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The eyes and ears of basketball fans worldwide will be focused on Madison Square Garden tonight as the legendary but aging Michael Jordan returns to the game after a three-year absence. The biggest sports star of the late 20th century will reenter the National Basketball Association as an active player in an unfamiliar uniform, a new position - and a 38-year-old body. Fans are just hoping that body holds up. " Michael Jordan is such a fundamental principle of my childhood that, if he came back and didn't dominate the court I might have to throw out my old Air Jordans," Christian Lorentzen, a 25-year-old book designer from Crown Heights, Brooklyn, said yesterday. "I'd rather see him go out on top." "He was great when he played. He was unmatched," agreed venture capitalist Jon Yellen, 34, as he lunched at Michael Jordan 's The Steak House at Grand Central Terminal yesterday. "I would be really disappointed if he wasn't so good when he came out [this time]." Nearly 600 media outlets - about as many as the Garden would expect for a second-round playoff opener, and far more than the usual 150 for regular-season games - will be on hand for the New York Knicks' home opener against Jordan's Washington Wizards tonight. Reporters from Japan, Israel and France will be there, and the BBC will televise the game as well as a 90-minute pregame show throughout Europe. Celebrities expected to attend include former U.S. senator and New York Knicks star Bill Bradley, retired boxing great Joe Frazier, comedians Chris Rock and Billy Crystal, actors Matthew Modine and Michael Keaton, model Heidi Klum, ABC-TV's Diane Sawyer, MTV's Carson Daly and, of course, film director and longtime courtside denizen Spike Lee. At the NBA Store in midtown yesterday, former NBA star and current TNT hoops analyst Charles Barkley, clad in a blue Wizards jersey bearing Jordan's No. 23, held court on Jordan's chances for a successful comeback. Barkley predicted His Airness would score well under 25 points and said, "You can't recapture your past. You're at your best from [ages] 25 to 32." NBA spokesman Michael Bass said while tonight's game represented the return of "arguably the greatest basketball player of all time," it will be but one highlight of a night in which the heroes of the Sept. 11 terrorist attack will be honored. Each of the Wizards and Knicks will be introduced with a firefighter, police officer, emergency medical technician or member of the military, Bass said, while the coaching staff will be introduced with postal workers. Two New York City police officers will sing the national anthem, and Harry Connick Jr. and jazz trumpeter Branford Marsalis will perform "America the Beautiful." Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 AP Air Jordan gavels the close of the business day at the New York Stock Exchange yesterday. With his Chicago Bulls No. 23 now a Washington Wizards jersey, Michael Jordan returns to the NBA at Madison Square Garden tonight. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 The game was on the line and Michael Jordan had the ball, as he always used to, and as he will again. But his shot, a three-pointer from the right wing to tie the game, fell short, hit the rim with a flat note and was gathered in by an opponent. "My shot was pretty short and pretty flat," Jordan said later, after his comeback game had ended in his Washington Wizards losing 93-91 to the New York Knicks. "I had a good look and it came up short." It was disappointing but it may have been an appropriate response to the overblown expectations that have accompanied the greatest player ever on his return to the NBA. This season you can not only see that Jordan really is mortal, you can observe mortality reel him in. Jordan, in the familiar No 23 and the unfamiliar blue, black and gold of the Washington Wizards, fell short of what is expected of a living legend but performed pretty impressively for a 38-year-old millionaire who has spent the past 31/2 years golfing, gambling and tending his investment portfolio. He finished with 19 points, five rebounds, six assists and four steals, which add up to a respectable game, but missed 14 of his 21 shots, which add up to something less. "The game is a little bit different, my teammates are a little bit different, and obviously the outcome is a little different from what I wanted," he said. The second quarter had promised more. After missing his first shot, he had settled in the heart of the Washington offence, demonstrating that his court vision and his quick first step were unimpaired. Then he got off a fall-away jump shot from close to the right corner over the 6ft 6in Shandon Anderson, a rainbow over Madison Square Garden, and the ball fell through the heart of the basket. Soon the Wizards led 39-28, which turned out to be their biggest lead of the game. The second half was less happy. Jordan made only one of his last five shots and gave the ball away twice in the final three minutes. They may have been three minutes too far. Doug Collins, the Washington coach, gave Jordan some time on the bench in each quarter, but still had his superstar on court for 37 of the 48 minutes, more than anyone else on the team. According to his personal trainer, Jordan is at about 90% of his physical capacity. According to his coach, he has not practised for the past few days because his knee has been a little sore. "I thought he did fine," said Collins, who also coached Jordan in his second comeback with the Chicago Bulls. "He missed a few shots but he really hasn't practised since we played our last exhibition game on Friday. He had six assists, four steals, five rebounds. Now, if he makes three more shots, you'd say: 'Wow, what a great game.'" There is, no doubt, more to come. Jordan's claim to be the greatest ever rests on his extraordinary ability in all the dimensions of the sport. Even without the most spectacular part of his game - he no longer flies - he is still a special player. If his fitness holds up, he could play in this league for another decade. It is, of course, unlikely that he would want to. The NBA does not need him any more: much as it missed him, it has eventually found a new generation of stars. He does not need the game: he has already retired twice, which should give the lie to those who claim he is back because he so loves basketball . And this season his patience and his appetite will be tested as severely as his fitness and ability. The Wizards are required to place a disproportionate reliance on young and untested players. The regular season is long and repetitive - an 82-game qualifying tournament - and this team will not compete for any titles and may even fail to make the play-offs. Meanwhile opposing coaches will require Jordan to work hard on defence - harder than the Knicks worked him - and they will routinely run a second defender at him. This will be the true test of Jordan's love of the game. Even when he gets his fitness and his jump shot back, he is still going to be the somewhat faded star of a somewhat ordinary team. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WHO makes Britain's bestknown male celebrity starstruck? The answer came today when David Beckham revealed it was American R'n'B star Usher and basketball player Michael Jordan . In a Radio 1 interview today, Beckham, 26, says he was so overawed when he met Michael Jordan at a restaurant that he just sat there staring until wife Victoria berated him. The England captain said: "The most star-struck I have ever been was when I met Michael Jordan in a restaurant - even though he was sat at a table with Madonna. "I was just staring saying 'God - that's Michael Jordan !' "Madonna sent a bottle of champagne over and we had to say thank you. Michael Jordan stood up and shook my hand - and his hand covered the whole of my arm. I had met Madonna a couple of times before and Victoria had met her too. I'd like to swap places with Michael Jordan for a week." Beckham also said he idolised Usher. He said: "Usher asked if he could get a ticket for a game. To meet him was really good - but quite nerve-racking." He went on: "There are certain people I look up to. Sportsman-wise I have always looked up to people like Muhammad Ali, and football-wise Eric Cantona and Bryan Robson are my heroes." He also spoke about the way the public treats him now. "The reaction has been amazing. But it's always been like that really. Since the Greece game, it's been phenomenal." headline: TROPHY JOLT FOR BULLETS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BIRMINGHAM Bullets' interest in the BBL Trophy was ended by an 85-78 defeat at the hands of London Towers. Sheffield Sharks closed the gap at the top of the Northern Conference to just two points with a 84-75 victory over high-flying Leicester Riders at Ponds Forge. Jeff Monaco led the Sharks attack with 19 points, while Billy Singleton top-scored for the Riders with 21. Brighton Bears moved up to third place in the Southern Conference with a 88-79 victory over leaders Thames Valley Tigers. Albert White led all scorers with 32 points, while Shawn Jamison netted 25 for the Tigers. Edinburgh Rocks picked up their third Championship victory in an 88 -73 success over Derby Storm, Jon Gaines grabbing a game-high 21 points. Milton Keynes Lions confirmed their place as winners of Pool B. l Michael Jordan scored 21 points and helped hold Vince Carter scoreless in the second half as the Washington Wizards took their winning streak to six games with a 93-88 triumph over the Toronto Raptors. Jordan again got a lot of help from Richard Hamilton, who led the Wizards with 27 points. Carter enjoyed the upper hand early in the first regular season meeting between "Air Jordan" and "Air Canada." He scored 19 of his 23 points in the first quarter as the Raptors jumped out to an early 19-point lead. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 WIZARDS coach Doug Collins notices the difference between this reincarnation of Michael Jordan and the model he coached with the Chicago Bulls in 1986. Collins remembers the legend in the making, who he said could eat 20 Chicken McNuggets on the bus ride to the arena and go out and score 45 on anybody. The Washington Wizards-edition Michael Jordan is a different character to the Bulls legend. In the second quarter of a comeback victory against the Raptors in Toronto on Sunday, Jordan led a three-on-none fast break but relinquished the poster-perfect dunk to Richard Hamilton. MATP The Washington Wizards' reinvention of Michael Jordan is more Joe Clark than Joe Cool. He is a defensive stopper who is playing Yoda to the NBA's young Skywalkers. His deteriorating right knee has cut his scoring soliloquies to mere four and five minute bursts and forced him to play Scottie Pippen to Hamilton's MJ. But the Michael Jordan who goes to Madison Square Garden tonight to play the Knicks has confirmed the past month that this third go-round in the NBA is devoted to only one aspect of his legacy. "Me coming out of the office and down to the basketball court, something I felt I could help this team understand is that winning is an attitude," Jordan said after the Wizards rallied from 19 points down to defeat Toronto. "I want them to understand that if you put the effort on the basketball court, you can go home and have a great night." For his next trick, Mr Wizard will turn a collection of NBA draft-lottery leftovers coming off Washington's worst season since 1962 into a team riding the franchise's longest win streak in the past 20 seasons. The Wizards have won eight straight and 11 of their past 13 to erase a 2-10 start that peaked with their swingman/de-facto president of basketball operations lashing out at his teammates/employees. His now infamous declaration of "we stink" followed a 94-75 loss at Cleveland on November 27, after which Michael reportedly questioned whether his teammates "had his back." The loss to the Cavs came after an overtime defeat of Boston. The next night, though, Washington withstood a 40-point effort from Allen Iverson to beat the 76ers in Philadelphia, which Collins now calls the turning point of the season. Jordan made another declaration: "We felt like we had accomplished something winning one game against Boston and we hadn't. "When we have one win, we tend to relax. That's what happened in Cleveland. Me being a veteran, that starts to get frustrating. And hopefully (the team) can start to understand that." headline: SUIT OVER KID CUT FROM TEAM IS RIDICULOUS
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 When the judge throws Lynn Rubin's lawsuit out of court, he or she ought to throw Rubin out too. Then the judge should have a talk with Jawaan. Lynn Rubin of Union City, Calif., is suing his local school district because his son, Jawaan, was put on the junior varsity basketball team rather than the varsity. He is seeking $1.5 million in damages and wants the coach fired. Silly lawsuits are filed every day. But this one bears watching because of the lessons that can be learned. It seems that Jawaan's coach, Blake Chong, asked Jawaan, a sophomore, to try out for the varsity basketball team at New Haven Unified School District, but then placed him on the junior varsity team. Lynn Rubin was upset at the decision because his family had rearranged its schedule to accommodate the varsity practice times. Jawaan was upset too, and was later cut from the junior varsity team after confronting Chong. So Lynn Rubin sued for Chong's dismissal and the $1.5 million, an amount he calculated based on potential lost wages from Jawaan's possible professional career. Here's an opportunity for the justice system. Dismiss the lawsuit. Tell Lynn Rubin he should be ashamed of himself and dismiss him as well. Then ask Jawaan if he has ever heard of Michael Jordan . The Michael Jordan who, when he was cut from his high school basketball team, did not sue. The Michael Jordan who devoted himself to getting better so that he wouldn't be cut the following year. The Michael Jordan who made himself into the best basketball player who ever lived. Then tell Jawaan to go home and practice. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 JUANITA Jordan has taken the first steps toward ending her 13-year marriage to basketball legend Michael Jordan , filing for divorce. In a petition filed Friday in circuit court in Chicago, J. Vanoy Jordan, 42, of Highland Park cited "irreconcilable differences" in seeking a dissolution of her marriage. MATP She seeks permanent custody of the couple's three children, the 2323sq m "marital residence" in Highland Park and half the couple's property. "It is the parties' hope that the media respect the family's need and desire for privacy in this painful matter," Mrs Jordan's lawyer, Don Schiller, said yesterday, declining further comment. The six-page petition says irreconcilable differences have caused "the irretrievable breakdown" of Mrs Jordan's marriage to the 38-year-old basketball superstar and that future attempts at reconciliation "would be impractical and not in the best interests of the family." Michael and Juanita Vanoy Jordan were married on September 2 1989 in Las Vegas. Their children are Jeffrey, 13, Marques, 11, and Jasmine, 9. Jordan cited a desire to spend more time with his family when he retired from the Chicago Bulls the first time in October 1994 and joked about "driving the car pool". He maintained his Chicago residence when he returned to basketball as an executive with the Washington Wizards last season. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BASKETBALL legend Michael Jordan , who turns 39 next month, said he plans to continue with the Washington Wizards next season. Jordan has averaged 24.3 points, 6.5 rebounds and 4.3 assists a game this season for the Wizards, NBA division doormats who have risen to respectability on his shoulders. MATP Jordan said when he returned that he intended to play at least two seasons, but his latest confirmation is the first since he undertook the rigors of an NBA schedule. "My contract is two years," Jordan said. "The way I feel and with all I have to deal with, all the different problems, it's a different thought process at the end of the year." Jordan, who led Chicago to six NBA titles, has not given up on being a supreme player in the NBA. "I can still be as dominant in different ways," Jordan said. "It's a different challenge for me. I have got to a point where I really understand the game of basketball , but also I'm still learning. "As long as I can continue to do that and challenge myself, there's always going to be a place for me playing basketball ." Jordan has had problems on and off the court. He has had fluid drained from his right knee twice and played through a right wrist injury. And Juanita, his wife of 12 years, filed for divorce in Chicago. Jordan, though, said his personal problems will not affect his performance for the Wizards. "Once I'm on the court, I'm in my own little world," he said. "When I come off the basketball court, I have to deal with what I have to deal with." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 CHICAGO: Basketball great Michael Jordan and his wife Juanita are giving their marriage another try. The Jordans, who announced last month they were divorcing after 12 years of marriage, yesterday said they would attempt a reconciliation. Juanita Jordan also withdrew the divorce petition she filed on January 4 in Lake County Circuit Court. "We have decided to attempt a reconciliation and our efforts to do so will be greatly enhanced if the privacy of our personal lives is respected," the couple said in a statement issued by Juanita Jordan's attorneys. Attorneys for both sides confirmed the statement, and said there would be no further comment. "Juanita's and Michael's joint statement says everything they have to say," said Frederick Sperling, an attorney for Michael Jordan . In her original divorce petition, Juanita Jordan said past attempts at reconciliation had failed and future ones "would be impractical and not in the best interests of the family". But when he was asked about the pending divorce last month, Michael Jordan expressed some hope of a reconciliation. "That's something probably we'll work on," he said. "I don't think I want to sit here and go through that. We have kids, obviously, and we want to make sure that's the focus. "Outside of that, when the time comes, you guys will hear about it." When Jordan retired for the second time after the 1998 National Basketball Association season, he cited a desire to spend more time with his family, even joking that he would help with car pools. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 College- basketball star Jay Williams is the last person you'd expect to see coming out of the offices of John Wren, Omnicom Group president-CEO, or Brendan Ryan, president-CEO of Interpublic Group of Cos.' Foote, Cone & Belding Worldwide. Captains of industry, yes. Point guards, no. But the talented Mr. Williams is an unusual case. The Duke University basketball star is expected to be one of the top two or three picks in the June 26 National Basketball Association draft, as well as one of the top two or three choices for companies looking for the next Michael Jordan of endorsements. So when Mr. Williams' management team set up the traditional round of meetings with marketers, it went one step further. Call it the branding-or, to be exact, the re-branding-of a player. ''We wanted him to meet the people making the commercials, doing the marketing research and making the media buys,'' said Bill Sanders, director of marketing for BDA Sports Management, Mr. Williams' California-based agent. BDA even created a logo for Mr. Williams (see left). Mr. Williams recently completed a whirlwind tour that included meetings with Messrs. Wren and Ryan in New York, a trip to FCB's San Francisco office, as well as a meeting at the Playa del Rey, Calif., office of Omnicom's TBWA/Chiat/Day with Catherine Potter, who heads the agency's Nissan account. Mr. Williams' New York-based financial advisers, Melhado, Flynn and Associates-a Wall Street money-management firm where Mr. Williams interned last year-set up the meetings. ''I had already known Mr. Wren, and I had heard about Brendan Ryan,'' Mr. Williams said. ''I thought there might be some marketing things out there for me to do, and I was interested to see what they thought.'' The move appears to have paid off, with the agency executives saying they think Mr. Williams has plenty of endorsement potential. ''You know what impressed me most about him? He is just a remarkably likable guy,'' Mr. Ryan said. The reason for Mr. Williams' foray into the ad community is obvious. Athletes for years have been able to supplement their salaries-and in some cases, surpass them-via endorsements. According to Burns Sports and Celebrities, a Chicago sports-marketing firm, Tiger Woods earned $55 million last year in endorsements. Even in his three years of retirement before returning to the NBA this past season, Burns estimated Michael Jordan earned $35 million to $40 million in endorsement deals. (College athletes are barred from doing endorsements by NCAA rules.) On his tour, Mr. Williams also met with several marketers. NBC's morning news show ''Today'' has chronicled the meetings and will do a piece on Mr. Williams on June 25. Mr. Williams was known by his given name of Jason Williams throughout his star-studded career at Duke. But he changed his name to 'Jay' last month to avoid confusion with other athletes with less-than-stellar reputations-most notably Memphis Grizzlies point guard Jason Williams, whose past indiscretions include marijuana use and racial epithets screamed at an Asian fan sitting behind the team bench, and Jayson Williams, the former New Jersey Nets star and former NBC broadcaster facing trial on a charge of aggravated manslaughter. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 " Michael Jordan is going to retire from basketball ." "Yes, Michael Jordan is going to retire this time forever more." "And Michael Jordan is a great athlete." "Yes, Michael Jordan is the world's greatest athlete." "And Michael Jordan with his basketball career has done a lot for Chicago." "Yes, for several years Michael Jordan made the Chicago Bulls the most prominent sports team in the world." "And Michael Jordan is black." "Yes, Michael Jordan is...So?" Oh, an inconsequential observation today - but 50 years ago? In no way! On Aug. 28, 1945, Branch Rickey, general manager for the Brooklyn Dodgers, signed Jackie Robinson, an already established athlete in baseball, basketball , football and track in various minor leagues, to play professional baseball. But Robinson, age 26, 6 feet tall with broad shoulders, college educated, former lieutenant in the United States Army and a teetotaler Methodist was black. "I want you to be the first Negro player in the major leagues. Can you take it? Can you take everything that's going to come your way?" Rickey asked Robinson. "Do you want a ballplayer who's afraid to fight back?" Robinson questioned. "No, I want a ball player with guts enough not to fight back," Rickey responded. "You've got to do this job with base hits and stolen bases and fielding ground balls, Jackie. Nothing else." Robinson, a proud and volatile man with ebony skin, nodded, reached for Rickey's hand and then signed the contract. The rage of bigotry was instant. Sports pages throughout the United States supported the supposition that the blacks were generally inferior to whites. Bobby Feller, of the Cleveland Indians, claimed that Robinson could not hit big-league pitching because his shoulders were not built like white players. Alvin Dark, a Boston infielder, said, "Negroes don't think as quick as whites." Most sports enthusiasts customarily agreed. Neither Rickey nor Robinson was surprised. On the evening of Jan. 31, 1919, Jack Roosevelt Robinson, the youngest of six children of sharecroppers Jerry and Mallie Robinson, was born on a white-owned plantation near Cairo, Ga. Robinson's grandparents were slaves. The prejudice against blacks in Georgia at that time was so rampant that to escape the Jim Crow mentality, his mother moved the family to Pasadena, Calif. Because he was tough but fair-minded, Robinson was popular among both blacks and whites who knew him personally. Still he continued to suffer the sting of racism. As a kid, he was not accepted by the Boy Scouts, nor the local YMCA, nor allowed to sit with whites in the local movie house, nor sit at the counter in the local drugstore - although he may scrub its floors. In the Army he was court marshaled for refusing to sit in the rear of a military bus. After signing with Branch Rickey, Jackie caught a flight with his wife, Rachel, for spring training in the deep South, only to be bumped twice to make room for white passengers. After staying for a night in a "colored" motel, they decided to continue the trip on a bus - only again to be forced to sit in the back. Some of the initial exhibition games were canceled because of Robinson's skin color. Remembering his promise to Rickey, Robinson kept his cool. His prowess as a baseball player soon became obvious to the sports world. In spite of the continual abuse from sports officials, fellow and opposing teammates and the jeers of fans and with a body spiked by the shoes of both fellow teammates and those of the opposition, Robinson led the Dodgers to win the ever popular American World Series in 1955. With Jackie Robinson's self control and sports success, he not only opened the gates for other black athletes but stimulated the spark for civil rights in our self proclaimed land of equality. "So today which national sports heroes are black?" "Well, there's Chandler..." "No, Chandler is white." "Chandler of the Bulls?" "No, Chandler of the Bears oh, so what! Who cares?" - Based on "Jackie Robinson, a Biography," by Arnold Rampersad: Alfred A. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Hollie Grima, Australian basketball representative and Tasmanian female sports star of the year. When did you take up basketball and why? I was always tall and competing in athletics and playing netball until I was 14. It just seemed a natural progression. Most memorable sporting moment? Without doubt helping the Opals win the bronze medal at the world championships last year. Most admired sportsperson? Cathy Freeman. I managed to get her autograph when she was competing in the carnivals in Devonport a few years ago. Pet dislikes? Early mornings. I hate getting up early, which I often had to do at the AIS. Favourite music? Almost anything, but particularly rhythm and blues. Favourite movie? Chicken Run. Is there any other sport you wish you were good at? Not really, I'm very happy with basketball . What event would you like to win? An Olympic gold medal with the Opals. Best basketballer you have ever seen? It would have to be Michael Jordan on television, but live it would be Lisa Leslie. Best basketballer you have played with? Australian Opals player Lauren Jackson. Ideal job? Being a professional basketball player. Sporting fantasy? Winning a basketball game with a half-court shot. If you could spend one hour with anyone, who would it be? Michael Jordan . Who or what would you like to come back as in your next life? A cat. They just sleep around all day and get fed. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 225 N. Adams St., Lancaster, Wis. Michael Jordan played in his final National Basketball Association All-Star game Feb. 9. Jordan has announced that this season will be his last, ending a two-season return to the NBA and the career of arguably the greatest player of all time. As he prepares to leave the game, some people are quickly trying to find "the next Michael Jordan ." The truth is that no one in the NBA will become the next Michael Jordan . Though some players may remind us of Michael with their high flying dunks and skill, what he has done for the game of basketball will never be matched. You might read predictions of "doomsday" or decline in the "post Jordan era" of the NBA, but it is my opinion the NBA will be fine. The All-Star game was proof there are several young stars who are very able to carry the NBA to a bright future. Kevin Garnett, Steve Francis, Tim Duncan, Tracy McGrady and Allen Iverson are all younger than 30 and have great careers ahead of them. Kobe Bryant has the marketability and star power to be a great ambassador for the NBA, as Jordan has been for so many seasons. I would be remiss if I didn't mention Yao Ming, and the exposure he has given the NBA in his homeland of China. These are just a few signs that point to a great future for the NBA, albeit without "his Airness." During a special halftime ceremony during the All-Star game, Jordan said, "I leave the game in good hands." Yes he does. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan is not only the greatest basketball player in the history of the game - he is also a billion-dollar industry. American financial magazine Fortune once estimated that Jordan, since joining the NBA in 1984, had generated an astounding Dollars 10 billion in revenue. When he retires later this month, as he has promised, the NBA will lose its most brilliant star. Jordan has retired twice already, once in October 1993 and again in January 1999, but he made comebacks on both occasions. Now 40, his powers are inevitably fading - a relative assessment, because on his day he is still one of the best around, capable of outjumping and outshooting players 15 years his junior - and he says there is 'zero chance' of him making one last comeback. In the beginning, Jordan had to work hard to prove his ability. As a second-year student at Laney High School in Wilmington, North Carolina, he was once cut from the college basketball team. 'It was embarrassing not making that team,' Jordan has said. 'They posted the roster and it was there for a long, long time without my name on it. I remember being really mad, too, because there was a guy who made it that really wasn't as good as me.' That public ignominy was the event which supplied Jordan with his extraordinary mental strength: 'Whenever I was working out and got tired and figured I ought to stop, I'd close my eyes and see that list in the locker room without my name on it, and that usually got me going again.' Partly by sheer talent and partly because of this mental strength, no player has ever made so many crucial game-winning shots as Jordan. The first time, which brought him to the attention of the NBA, was as a North Carolina first-year student against Georgetown in 1982. Jordan nailed a 17-foot jump shot with 15 seconds left on the clock to lead his university to the state championship. 'The kid doesn't even realise it yet, but he's part of history now,' said Eddie Fogler, then a North Carolina assistant coach. 'People will remember that shot 25 years from now.' Two years later, after winning consecutive collegiate Player of the Year awards, Jordan entered the big time, the NBA. He was selected third in the 1984 draft by the Chicago Bulls, a marriage which was to become one of the most successful - and, thanks to Nike, certainly the most lucrative - in sporting history. It's no exaggeration to say that Michael Jordan revolutionised professional basketball . The coming of a player like Jordan had been foretold by the likes of Elgin Baylor in the Sixties, Julius Erving in the Seventies and Magic Johnson in the Eighties, all three black Americans whose athleticism and innovative ball skills had shaken up the one-paced sport that pro basketball was in the Fifties. But Jordan took basketball to a higher plane. At 6ft 6in he was not tall for an NBA player, but his physical attributes meant he could punch well above his height - his body fat was once measured at 4 per cent, compared with the average athlete's 7 to 8 per cent. Jordan could make shots no one else could dream of, thanks to his freakish ability to hang in the air, apparently defying gravity. In 1984, although he was not yet a big name in the NBA, Jordan's agent negotiated a trainer contract with Nike, reputedly worth Dollars 1 million a year. Until then, Jordan preferred to wear Adidas. The Air Jordan product line (see panel, right) was the major factor in establishing Nike's swoosh symbol as an icon of global culture - ironically, at first Jordan's footwear made him more famous than his ball skills. In 1985 he was named NBA Rookie of the Year, after averaging almost 30 points a game and playing in every one of the Bulls' 82 games. For the remainder of the decade, Jordan's ascent continued, with several milestones passed on the way: in 1986, he set a play-off record by scoring 63 points against the Boston Celtics (after the game, which the Bulls actually lost, Boston legend Larry Bird said: 'I think it's just God disguised as Michael Jordan . . .'). In 1987 he joined Wilt Chamberlain as the only player to score more than 3,000 points in a season. In 1988 he was voted the league's Most Valuable Player, the first of five such awards. And then, with the dawn of the Nineties, he really started to play. . . Former Bulls assistant coach Tex Winter once told Jordan, 'There's no "I" in the word team.' Jordan shot back with, 'There is in the word "win".' And with Jordan on side, the Bulls could not stop winning. In the Nineties he led Chicago to six NBA championships, in 1991-93 and 1996-98, an achievement which may never be equalled. Along the way, Jordan found the time to win two Olympic gold medals with the USA Dream Team, in '92 and '96, and star alongside Tweety Pie and Bugs Bunny in Disney's 1996 movie Space Jam . He also tried his hand at a pro baseball career, with the Chicago White Sox in 1994, but, for once, he di

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan 's fabulous career came to an end amid cheers and tears as his Washington Wizards were trounced 107-87 by the Philadelphia 76ers, who clinched home-court advantage in the first round of the play-offs. Perhaps the greatest player in NBA history, Jordan scored 15 points in the finale to his 15-year career, which included 10 scoring titles, six championships, five MVP awards, two comebacks and a seemingly endless reel of highlights. It was a day-long celebration in "The City of Brotherly Love", where the folks who perfected the art of booing showed Number 23 they could cheer long and loud, too. During the pre-game introductions, the Sixers turned the microphone over to Ray Clay, the former public address announcer of the Chicago Bulls who launched into his patented growl: "From North Carolina 6-6 number 23 Michael Jordan !!!" As Jordan shook hands with Clay, a two-minute standing ovation ensued. Jordan rocked back and forth, trying not to cry while staring at the sneakers that he turned into a status symbol. Jordan said leaving basketball was like losing a best friend after he and the sport had shared a great relationship. " Basketball has been my life," he said. "It's been like my best friend, but sometimes you have to move away from your best friend." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan wrote an open letter to the game of basketball , insisting his NBA playing days are "definitely over, but our relationship will never end." The full-page notice, which featured his Nike logo on top, appeared yesterday in newspapers across the U.S., including the Washington Post, The New York Times and the Los Angeles Times. Associated PressThe letter was a cross between a valedictory address, an Academy Awards acceptance speech and a love letter to the sport he began playing when he was 12. "Dear Basketball ," it began. "It's been 28 years since I saw you in the back of our garage. 28 years since our parents introduced us." The 40-year-old basketball great played his last NBA game Wednesday night, ending his career with the Washington Wizards after leading the Chicago Bulls to six titles while becoming the league's third-highest career scorer and perhaps the most celebrated figure in all sports. "In some respects, you've become my life," he wrote. "My passion. My motivation. My inspiration. Jordan recalled the time he was left off his high school varsity: "I was crushed. I was hurt. I think I even cried." He recounted his struggle to toughen his body and his days at North Carolina, a university career highlighted by his winning shot in the 1982 NCAA title game against Georgetown. Basketball , he says, discovered him then. "You found me in the corner and we danced," he wrote. He went on to thank fans, five coaches by name and 10 NBA teams he played against. "I love you, basketball ," he concluded. "I love everything about you and I always will. My playing days in the NBA are definitely over, but our relationship will never end." The letter was signed: "Much Love and Respect, Michael Jordan ." headline: JORDAN IS SLAM-DUNKED
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Michael Jordan had almost everything with the Washington Wizards: adulation from fans and the news media, a piece of ownership, the freedom to indulge in his competitive urges for two more seasons on the basketball court and power as a decision maker for a National Basketball Association team. The world's greatest player also had something he did not want: an old-school boss who didn't want him around anymore. In a move that stunned no one more than Mr. Jordan himself, Abe Pollin, the 79-year-old owner of the Wizards, told him in a brief and heated meeting that he did not want him to return with the club. Mr. Pollin gave Mr. Jordan no reason, but team officials, speaking on condition of anonymity, later said the decision had more to do with player resentment toward Mr. Jordan, management's dissatisfaction with a 110-179 mark during his tenure as an executive and a player, and Mr. Jordan's deteriorating relationships within the organization. As sports legacies go, hardly anyone remembers Willie Mays as a New York Met, or Joe Montana as a Kansas City Chief or O. J. Simpson as a San Francisco 49er. Michael Jordan , for one, would probably like to quickly forget he was ever a Wizard. Mike Wise HYPERLINK http://www.nytimes.com 00 http://www.nytimes.com Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Photo: Michael Jordan , no longer a Wizard. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 No matter what a sports team's owner thinks or does, people will always say it was his or her fault when a deal falls through. In the case of Michael Jordan 's no-return to the Washington Wizards' front office as president of basketball operations, the athlete still got his end of the bargain ("Wizards let Air out of legacy," Cover story, Sports, Thursday). The basketball player wanted another chance to show the world he is the greatest; he got that opportunity. Unfortunately, it is only as a player that his greatness shows. His skills at creating and running a winning team were exposed as less than ordinary. Commentary writer Ian O'Connor writes that Washington Wizards team owner Abe Pollin used Jordan as a sucker ("Like legendary Ruth before him, Jordan unceremoniously dumped," Keeping score, Sports, Thursday). Well, I believe it was a two-way street. Jordan's presence may have boosted ticket sales in Washington, but his lack of strong management ability was damaging to the overall team. To say what Pollin did was callous overlooks the fact that it was the right thing to do. Jordan doesn't belong running a team. He has demonstrated a weak work ethic and a poor judge of talent. That's not what any owner wants from an executive -- no matter how many tickets a team may sell. Jon Griswold Waterbury, Conn. A missed opportunity Who is Abe Pollin, anyway? The majority of the world -- rich and poor -- knows Michael Jordan . Even Iraqis probably love him. Jordan's fame alone is a good reason for Pollin and any other businessperson associated with the Washington Wizards to give Jordan the job of president of basketball operations. Japanese businessmen, Arab princes, even Bill Gates probably would pay money to eat dinner or just talk with the influential athlete. Think of the profits that could have been generated by Jordan's return. Perry W. Payne Jr. Washington Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 PHOTO, B/W, Luiz M. Alvarez, AP; Off guard: Washington Wizards' Michael Jordan , shown during a March 2 game against the Miami Heat in Miami, said he was shocked" that plans for his return as a Wizards front-office executive were rejected. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BEING a Michael Jordan aspirant is nothing unusual, but having the talent to become a quality basketball player like his hero should not pose a problem for Joshill Wallis. The Belgrave youngster has already notched up a national gold medal at the recent Victorian Primary School Sports Association championship in Darwin. Joshill and his fellow Victorians took out the top award in the Top End two weeks ago. "I have achieved a few things already in basketball ," he said. "I saw other kids play and then I practised, eventually getting better and better." Having played since he was six, Joshill progressed through the ranks before being selected for the championships from a four-day camp in March. He was chosen alongside 11 others from more than 300 hopefuls. Winning the gold was a great achievement for the Nunawading Spectre and Vermont Vulture player. "We went through the tournament undefeated," Joshill said. "I would love eventually to play in the National Basketball Association competition in America, as Coby Bryant is my favourite current player, although Michael Jordan is my real idol." Joshill is a nomination for the Free Press Leader junior Sports Star of the Year award. To make a nomination, phone 9762 2511 or e-mail HYPERLINK mailto:freepress@ldr.newsltd.com.au 00 freepress@ldr.newsltd.com.au . fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 It seems that there is nothing wrong with not knowing who the environment minister is, but try admitting you don't know whether Michael Jordan plays basketball or hockey. By Ernest Niedzialek I don't know who or what Bugs Bunny or the Looney Toons are. I have never seen an episode of the Power Rangers, The Simpsons, or South Park. I don't know what Christina Aguilera wore at the last award show, or what new body part Britney Spears revealed in her last video. I don't know who J. Lo is dating, or who Michael Jackson is allegedly molesting. I can never remember if Michael Jordan is a hockey player or a basketball player; or maybe I'm thinking of Tiger Woods. I also don't know what new model of Nike shoes either of them is sponsoring. Uttering such heretical statements to anyone, I always get the same reaction of flabbergast and utter shock - the same reaction many of you had, no doubt. Try telling someone things such as these yourself and you are likely to get a similar reaction. Apparently, I have been living under a rock. The rest of society lives under a rock too, though a different and far more socially acceptable one. Let me show you. Can you name the current minister of environment? Can you list Canada's top three trading partners and list our major imports and exports? Do you know how the Charter of Rights and Freedoms came about, state its purpose, or know what rights it guarantees? If you are like most people, you could not answer any of these questions. This is the political rock that most of us live under. Suddenly, my rock doesn't seem so bad. I asked dozens of people ranging from Grade 9 to 12 in my high school, as well as many homeowners of varying ages in my neighbourhood these very questions. None could answer more than one of these questions with any degree of accuracy. But very few had trouble naming three Britney Spears videos. Those same people, while ignorant about their own politics, have all heard about the governator of California. Not knowing about politics, a force which shapes our rights, freedoms, and daily lives, it seems, is perfectly acceptable. But to think that someone is not up to date on the latest celebrity gossip is unthinkable. So maybe Arnold Schwarzenegger has the right idea about raising the public's interest in politics. Maybe we need more celebrity influence in our politics. Maybe what John Tory needed in Toronto's last mayoral election was advertising that showed him wearing Nike shoes while drinking a can of Coca Cola. Those further back in the pack, however, might have had to resort to more drastic celebrity-inspired measures to get attention - such as illicit affairs or botched plastic surgery. Sure, it may not be the prettiest idea, but it would give a boost to the public's interest in politics. Ernest Niedzialek is a Grade 12 student at Neil McNeil Catholic Secondary School. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 toronto star wires David Anderson, left, is Canada's minister of the environment. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 basketball legend Michael Jordan will visit Hong Kong for the first time, as part of a marketing and promotion tour sponsored by sportswear company Nike. Apart from the territory, the Michael Jordan Asian Tour 2004 will include stopovers on the mainland, Taiwan and Japan, but no dates have been announced yet. "We have no official confirmation," said spokesman Daniel Chan, when asked about Jordan's itinerary. Jordan is widely considered the best basketball player of all time and is one of the most visible figures in the world today. He was destined for stardom from his early days and led the North Carolina Tar Heels to the American college championship in 1983. The third pick in the NBA draft, Jordan was named Rookie of the Year in 1985. It was a prelude to the success that would follow. The 41-year-old led the Chicago Bulls to six NBA championships, was a five -time NBA Most Valuable Player and was a starter on the NBA's All-Star team for 10 of his 16 years in the league. The guard was also a member of the 1984 and 1992 Olympic gold-medal winning US basketball teams. Jordan retired for the first time following the 1993-94 season and tried his hand at baseball for the Class AA Birmingham Barons of the Chicago White Sox. Jordan's attempt to play Major League baseball ended quickly and he came out of retirement for the 1994-95 season. He led the Bulls to three more championships before retiring again after the 1997-98 season. Jordan made one more comeback - this time with the Washington Wizards - in 2000-01, but he didn't even come close to the success he had with the Bulls. The Wizards missed the playoffs both years when Jordan was in their lineup. Jordan walked out of the game for the last time in 2002. Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 (Photo: Reuters); Farewell Michael Jordan quit basketball for good in 2002. During his career, he led the Chicago Bulls to six NBA championships. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Sports goods manufacturer Nike has revealed details Michael Jordan 's brief visit to Hong Kong as part of a four-leg "brand expansion tour" of Asia. Jordan, indisputably the greatest basketball player ever, will make his first ever visit to the territory on May 21 for a series of promotional activities. These involve a press conference at The Peninsula, the opening ceremony of the Jordan Exhibition at SportX, Ocean Terminal, Harbour City in Tsim Sha Tsui, and the Jordan Cup at the Convention and Exhibition Centre. According to a press release issued on behalf of Nike, Jordan "will promote the global reach of the game of basketball as the sport's most recognisable ambassador and inspire youth and fans by reinforcing the importance of excellence in every endeavour", although the prime reason for the visit, and particularly the press conference, is to plug the latest version of his Air Jordan basketball shoes, now in their 19th incarnation. The Jordan Exhibition also seems to be geared towards Nike's Jordan brand's new product line together with "a historical exhibition" of previous Air Jordan shoes. "If you aspire to excellence in everything you do, the sky is the limit," Jordan, now 41, was quoted as saying. "The game of basketball has taught me valuable life skills that have carried over to endeavours both on and off the court, which has helped me and the Jordan brand reach our levels of success. During my tour in Hong Kong and Asia, I want to pass along this knowledge and encourage youth around the world that positive energy and high aspirations will lead to excellence." All of this Jordan will attempt to achieve in the space of 24 hours after a two-day stop in Beijing and before jetting off to Taiwan and on to Tokyo, a feat almost as impressive as the six NBA titles and two Olympic gold medals he won during his playing career. MICHAEL JORDAN 'S HK ITINERARY: May 20, 2004 (Thursday) - Evening arrival. May 21, 2004 - Press conference (The Peninsula); Jordan exhibition opening ceremony (SportX, Ocean Terminal, Harbour City); Jordan Cup (Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre). MICHAEL JORDAN ASIA TOUR 2004 SCHEDULE: May 19-20 - Beijing. May 21 - Hong Kong. May 22 - Taipei. May 23 - Tokyo. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 While Michael Jordan is most often pictured in mid-flight, Hong Kong's ban on after-dark helicopter trips meant the basketball superstar had to use more down-to-earth means to enter the city last night. Fresh off a private plane from the mainland, Jordan arrived at the Peninsula hotel in a silver seven-seater van shortly after 8.45pm to the cheers of about 100 fans waiting outside. Wearing a grey and white T-shirt embossed with the Jordan logo, he spent about 10 seconds waving to assembled fans before heading into the hotel amid heavy security. Less than two hours later, he slipped out of the hotel and went with his entourage to Central's exclusive Kee Club. Police officers stood at both ends of Wellington Street when the group arrived. His bodyguards and club security guards cordoned off a segment of the street as Jordan, smoking a cigar, went inside. He is in town for just one day. Outside the hotel, 21-year-old Kaka Hui, a Polytechnic University student, had been waiting with her "No Mike, no life" banner since 5pm. "When I first arrived it was empty so I thought I had missed him, but more people arrived so I knew I hadn't. Now that I have caught a glimpse of him, I am happy." Earlier in the day, Jordan attended the final of the mainland high school basketball championships in Beijing. Minutes into the last period a huge scream went up as thousands of fans welcomed arguably the game's greatest player. Though there was no repeat of the frantic scenes that awaited Jordan at Dongdan stadium the day before, the event's host did have to give a warning to calm the audience: "If you don't calm down and sit down Michael will leave early." After presenting awards to the winning team, Shenyang No31 High School, and complimenting them on being "very competitive", Jordan left. His entire stay lasted only about 20 minutes and fans were left milling around forlornly clutching their "Michael is the King", or "23 fly forever" banners, their hero long gone. One 19-year-old admitted skipping classes for a week to travel from Taiyuan in Shanxi province to see Jordan, saying "my dream has come true". A Beijing boy who proudly sported Jordan's number 23 Chicago Bulls jersey said: "To see Jordan is a compulsory masterclass for everyone who plays basketball ." Graphic fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 (Photo: K.Y.Cheng); After flying in from Beijing, basketball legend Michael Jordan arrives at the Peninsula hotel (left) amid heavy security. He showed up later at the Kee Club in Central. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Always leave 'em wanting more, goes the saying. It was one Michael Jordan may have had in mind on his whistle-stop tour of Hong Kong yesterday. In Tsim Sha Tsui's Harbour City mall, 100 waiting fans chanted his name, hoping for a glimpse of their idol as he officiated at the invitation-only opening of an exhibition featuring Air Jordan sneakers. They were to be disappointed. He moved on without them catching so much as a glimpse of the world's most famous basketball star. Wong Kai-on, a 20-year-old waiter who took a day off work to get a glimpse of his idol, said: "I'm very disappointed. I wanted to get a picture of him. We were hoping he would come out after he heard us chanting." Inside, Jordan unveiled a life-size statue of himself, made by local artist Eric So. "He's got my ears. I love this," said Jordan, who signed a sneaker and the red basketball jersey adorning the statue. Next on his schedule was a match at the Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai between two Hong Kong teams drawn from a local league. The former Chicago Bulls and Washington Wizards legend arrived in time to catch the second half, then judged the teams in a slam-dunk contest watched by 2,000 fans. He had another autograph, and some words of advice, for the 17-year-old winner of the contest, So Yi-chun. "A perfect dunk is taking off from the free-throw line," he told him. Oh yes, and for a better dunk, jump higher. With that, he was gone. A group of fans unveiled a big banner reading: "Hong Kong loves Michael Jordan " as the basketball star left the court. But he was in too much of a hurry to see it. Even Jordan seemed to wish he had more time to spare before flying on to Taipei and Tokyo. "I love it, from the little bit of time we had here. "We had shrimp tempura, which was very, very good. "I want to come back and spend more time just to be able to visit the city and do some shopping." A case of leaving wanting more. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Name: So Yi-chun Grade: Form Five Age: 17 School: Jockey Club Ti-I College Sport: Basketball Statistics: Yi-chun was voted the Most Valuable Player of the 2003/04 inter -school Jing Ying (All Hong Kong) basketball competition and also excelled in the Nike Junior League last summer. He is a member of the Hong Kong junior squad and played for the Hong Kong schools team which won this year's inter -port competition in Foshan . He competes in commercial events like Adidas Streetball and Nike Battleground, and plays for Nan On club in the local First Division. NBA legend Michael Jordan judged Yi-chun's slam dunk the best at the Jordan Cup in Hong Kong recently. Soundbite: "When I was 12 I had a dream that Michael Jordan taught me to play basketball . I'll never forget how good he was when the Chicago Bulls won those championships. Like he says: 'Practice makes perfect, find your own way to improve yourself, and find competitors who can challenge you'." Academic performance: Yi-chun is waiting for his HKCEE results which will be out in August. Interests: He doesn't have much time for anything else. Basketball and school take up all his time. From his coach: "He has great potential and is a clever player. Compared to other players his age, his skills are versatile," said Ti-I coach Choi Fong -yue. "His shooting sense is good and he is quick." Ambition: Yi-chun hopes to win a university scholarship for Australia, where he would like to study sports marketing or sports physiotherapy. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Keith cock-a-hoop KEITH BUNYAN got his prized autographed book from Michael Jordan - when the NBA superstar popped in to use his aunt's loo. Watching Chicago Bulls legend Jordan as a kid inspired the Clark Eriksson Fury shooter to take up basketball . So when his hero was caught short playing golf at Muirfield, Bunyan's Aunt Georgie turned to blackmail to make her nephew's day. Bunyan, a key member of the Scotland team heading to the Commonwealth Games in Melbourne in March, said: "My aunt lives in Gullane and heard Michael Jordan was playing golf at Muirfield. She was there when he came off the course quite late at night. "At the time he had a contract preventing him signing autographs because it devalued his signature. "But when he asked if the clubhouse was still open so he could use the loo my aunt told him 'No - but my house is on the course.If you want to use the toilet you can, but you have to sign this book for my nephew'. "So that's how I got my autographed book! "I'd grown up watching Jordan and Magic Johnson on TV. I share my birthday with Magic - unfortunately I've not the same skill!" Modest Bunyan joined Scotland team-mates Mark Duncan, Scott Russell and Stuart Mackay to coach the Falkirk schools squad at the latest of the Clydesdale Bank's Road To Melbourne masterclasses at Grangemouth Sports Complex. Since quitting the Scottish Rocks after six years, Bunyan has been juggling his day job as an active schools co-ordinator in Falkirk with training and playing. He and the rest of the Scottish Commonwealth Games team have been backed all the way to Melbourne by the Clydesdale Bank. The Clydesdale this week pledged to extend their support to the 2010 Games in Delhi - while continuing to back Glasgow's bid to host the 2014 event. But Mackay, the tallest player in the squad at 6ft 10ins, reckons it's the trouser department where he could do with some extra support! The former US college star said: "I should be getting subsidies from the government because I have to go to specialist shops to get things like trousers. "I was fortunate to play in America for a high school and college. I stayed in Savannah, Georgia, and went to a small school there. I got a scholarship to Georgia College and was transferred to Armstrong. "I played division two basketball when I was there and that was hard enough! We'd play in front of about 6,000 people. "But the Commonwealth Games is the pinnacle of my career." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 How not to start NCAA tournament preparations: UCLA backup center Lorenzo Mata broke his nose in practice Wednesday night and will have to wear a mask Saturday against LSU. ... Florida football coach Urban Meyer has canceled a "Gator Gathering" in Atlanta set for Saturday and will call off spring practice Monday if Florida makes the finals. "I plan on being there Monday if we take care of our business," Meyer told the Gainesville Sun. The best college basketball team of all time? North Carolina '82, say ESPN.com users, who gave Michael Jordan & Co. the nod over the undefeated Indiana Hoosiers of 1976 in a contest pitting 64 of the best college teams against each other. ... College basketball loses another one early to the NBA: San Diego State junior forward Marcus Slaughter, who averaged 16.5 points and 11 rebounds a game, said he's heading to the pros. Count Kentucky coach Tubby Smith among those not surprised to see George Mason in the Final Four: "We were getting ready to play Georgia State earlier this season, and I was watching a film of them playing George Mason. I told one of my assistants: 'Man, I hope we don't have to play them. They're good.' " ... Pat Flannery, who coached Bucknell to a first-round tournament win over Arkansas, signed a two-year extension Thursday. headline: BASKETBALL
body: fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 BATHED in the spotlight that followed his every step, Dwyane Wade did not blink. He moved confidently in a zone of his own all night, driving his Miami Heat towards a championship and casting a chilling shadow on the dumbfounded, distracted Dallas Mavericks. Even when the Mavericks began pushing their way back into yet another game they had led, and back into an NBA finals they had led by 2-0, Miami's superstar was there to seal the outcome of game six. When the Mavericks' last chance - a three-pointer from Jason Terry to send the game into overtime - bounced off the rim, Wade reached up and grabbed it. Hearing the buzzer, he rocketed the ball almost 20 metres into the air, unleashing a scream into the stunned air of the Dallas stadium. Wade scored 36 points in a most-valuable-player performance, leading the Heat to a 95-92 victory. It was Miami's fourth straight triumph, and it gave them their first NBA championship. Wade gave coach Pat Riley, who had replaced Stan van Gundy in December, his first title in 18 years. It was Riley's fifth championship; he won four in the 1980s with the Los Angeles Lakers. Wade also delivered a fourth title to Shaquille O'Neal, who, after being traded from the Lakers to Miami, proved he could win again. Riley and O'Neal had each promised Miami a championship parade when they arrived. Because of 24-year-old Wade, whom Riley drafted as the fifth overall pick in 2003, they've got one. "He just took it to another level," Riley said. "Players like that are hard to come by. "He's making his legacy in his third year. "I've never had a player like this who can absolutely, at any time, beat five guys and make great plays. "Wade is probably one of the most respected players, and he proved that over the past four games." Surveying the wreckage of his team's collapse, Mavericks coach Avery Johnson said of Wade: "Some of that stuff you can't teach. He had a lot of will to win. "You've seen players like [ Michael ] Jordan , a lot of players in history who have those types of performances. We tried a lot of things, but he just had the desire to get it done." Ahead by 14 points in the first quarter and by nine in the second, the Mavericks saw Wade steal the championship out from under them. With just under 26 seconds to play, the Mavericks made a strange play that typified their confusion. Dallas's own superstar, Dirk Nowitzki, passed to centre Erick Dampier, who had rolled off a pick into the lane. Dampier bobbled the ball, and Wade collected it to make two free throws that gave the Heat their margin of victory. As the series progressed, Wade evolved from a young star into an unstoppable force. "That's what these events do," Riley said. "They make genuine stars out of players. And sometimes, to some kids, they become genuine heroes." The comparisons with Michael Jordan kept flying, much to Wade's discomfort. He grew up in the Chicago area, first admiring Jordan, then becoming his friend. "Dwyane has told me he would not like me speak of him in context with Michael Jordan out of respect for him," Riley has said. "I think it's time for Dwyane to take on his own persona." Wade is proud, but deflects such accolades. "The comparisons are flattering," he said. "But there will never be another Jordan. Hopefully, when I'm done playing, there will be someone comparing me to him." Riley compared Wade to a panther. Or a leopard. "Up in the tree, a lot of times they let certain prey go because it's not something they want to feast on," Riley said. Wade started slowly, as if sizing up his enemy. He did not score for the first 10 minutes, but then exploded, compensating for the foul trouble and general struggles that O'Neal endured. Wade was the one who had led the comeback in game three, with the Heat down by 13 and just six minutes, 34 seconds left in what became the turning point of the series. The Mavericks didn't recover in Miami. Wade only got better, scoring 42 points, 36 and 43 in the three Heat victories on their home court. When the Mavs returned home for game six, they resolved to show a different attitude. Terry called it "controlled anger", and it was one they felt from the threat of elimination, their lingering anger over Jerry Stackhouse's suspension for a flagrant foul on O'Neal in game four and their overall feeling that the league was slighting them and favouring Wade. However, in the end, the Mavs simply had no one to counter Wade, who seemed to be forging his legacy at every turn. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 VICTORIA (CP) -- Basketball star Steve Nash says he doesn't need his hometown to erect a statue in his honour. Instead, the humble, two-time National Basketball Association's most valuable player said Thursday he'd rather see children playing in a park. Nash was in his hometown of Victoria to participate in an inner-city park ceremony that included opening a new outdoor basketball court. Nash shrugged off comparisons to Canadian hockey legend Wayne Gretzky and American basketball superstar Michael Jordan , who both have had statues erected in their honour. "I'm not Gretzky or Jordan, so, no, I don't think we need a statue," said the Phoenix Suns guard. But many of the people who attended the ceremony begged to differ. "He's bigger than Michael Jordan ," said Max Cullen, 11, who came to the ceremony with two cousins and two other young friends. The boys wore Phoenix Suns jerseys in the hopes of scoring a Nash autograph. Chris Buckle, 12, clutched a basketball that looked like it had been bounced through one-too-many outdoor games. Hundreds were at Central Park to see Nash open the state-of-the-art court and run basketball drills for children who are members of the Steve Nash Youth League. Many of the children shouted Nash's name throughout the ceremony and chanted: S-T-E-V-E, he's a two-time MVP. Nash said he didn't expect the large turnout of people who ringed the new court for a glimpse at one of the world's most popular athletes. "I was expecting about 20 or so," he said smiling. The Steve Nash Foundation, which has had a hand in quietly resurrecting several Victoria area parks in the past, played a more open role in the Central Park redevelopment.
