fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Could Kevin Durant be the new Jordan? The NBA desperately needs a new superstar, and the 18-year-old might be the boy most likely. When Michael Jordan finally retired, in 1998 (let us discount his meaningless time on court with the Washington Wizards between 2001 and 2003), the NBA was left staring at a massive, superstar-shaped hole. So we'll find another superstar, they thought. We had Wilt Chamberlain, Larry Bird and Magic Johnson before Jordan. What's the problem? Well, the problem was that a player of Jordan's genius shows up less often than Halley's Comet. Wilt, Bird and Magic were brilliant stars but they couldn't dunk like Mike. Jordan made people gasp: how did he hang in the air for so long? More importantly, no one could sell Nikes like Mike. He was the first sportsman to become a genuine global brand. Throughout the 1990s, MJ was the NBA. Every season, or so it seemed, he performed miracles to lead the Chicago Bulls to the NBA Finals, the sport's showcase series. And then, somehow, Jordan played even better. He made clutch jump shots to order and never choked. No wonder we all wanted to be in his shoes. But the NBA didn't realise just how much basketball had come to rely on Jordan. Since he retired, the NBA's TV ratings' share has declined sharply. For the 1998 Finals, when Jordan's Bulls defeated the Utah Jazz, the ratings average (a percentage calculated by dividing the number of unique viewers by the number of estimated available viewers) was 18.7, a huge slice of the pie. For the 1999 Finals, the first post-Jordan series, the rating fell to 11.3, a staggering drop from the previous year. Millions of Americans switched off or changed channels, simply because they couldn't watch Jordan do his thing. Now, almost a decade on, there is no sign of Heir Jordan. The NBA is still facing up to life without its greatest-ever talent. Even the combined wattage of the NBA's current stars - genuinely great players like Kobe Bryant, LeBron James and Dirk Nowitzki - doesn't shine half as bright as one prime-time Jordan. The ratings decline is continuing. The 2006 NBA Finals, between the Miami Heat and the Dallas Mavericks, featured major stars like Shaquille O'Neal, Dwayne Wade and Nowitzki, yet only scored an 8.5 rating, one of the lowest figures in the last 30 years. If there is hope for another golden era for the NBA, then it lies in America's finely-honed college system. The current generation of college players is widely considered to be one of the most talented in 30 years. Two teenage prodigies, in particular, are set to take the NBA by storm next season, no doubt armed with lucrative footwear deals. That's the NBA's plan, anyway. We will hear much more about Kevin Durant, an 18-year-old forward for the Texas Longhorns, and Greg Oden, a 19-year-old centre for Ohio State. The pair were voted onto the Associated Press's prestigious All-America college team this week, making them only the third and fourth freshmen (first-year students) to be chosen since 1972, the year when freshmen first became eligible for selection. Durant and Oden are brilliant young players whose college careers are almost over before they begin. Rather than stay in school for another year or two, where they would develop their skills, they will be fast-tracked to the NBA, almost certainly as the first and second pick in this summer's draft, ready to be unveiled for the 2008 season. The vultures are hovering. The Boston Celtics were fined $30,000 recently after general manager Danny Ainge sat next to Durant's mother during a game, whilst Michael Jordan - yes, the very same - was fined $15,000 for alluding to Durant in an interview with The Charlotte Observer. Jordan, now co-owner of the Charlotte Bobcats, said he was interested in versatile players and added: 'The kid who may present that is the kid in Texas. [He] may have that because he has all the right signs.' (Team coaches and executives are barred from talking about students who have yet to declare for the NBA draft.) Both Durant and Oden exhibit 'all the right signs', but how strange it is that the NBA needs two unproven teenagers more than they need the NBA. Durant and Oden might be giants, but it's way too early to say if they will fill that Jordan-shaped hole. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Most collegiate walk-ons only see the court during mop-up time, their opportunity confined to the final 30-90 seconds of an already decided game. Jeffrey Jordan has much bigger aspirations, and he intends to fulfill them at Illinois. Jordan, a 6-foot-2 guard from Loyola Academy and the oldest son of basketball icon Michael Jordan , announced Friday that he will play for Illinois as a preferred walk-on. "Illinois is a great school with a strong basketball program." Jeffrey Jordan said in a prepared statement. "I look forward to receiving a great education at Illinois, and I also look forward to proving that I can play and compete at the Big Ten Level." Jordan didn't receive any Big Ten scholarship offers, though several schools, including Illinois and Northwestern, pursued him as a preferred walk-on, which guarantees him a roster spot. He ultimately chose Illinois over Valparaiso, which had offered a scholarship. Jordan told Illinois coach Bruce Weber of his intentions Wednesday. He visited Illinois on April 17 with his mom, Juanita, and played a pickup game with Illini players. "He's a quality kid who is very competitive and passionate about basketball ,"Weber said in a statement. "He's made great strides over the last year as a player and is an excellent student who should be a great fit here at the University of Illinois." "Coach (Homer) Drew and Valparaiso have a great program and a nice school," Jordan said in his statement. "But after I worked out at Illinois, (met) with the coaching staff and the players, and toured the campus with my mom, I just felt more comfortable (there)." Jordan, a two-time captain who twice earned All-Catholic League honors at Loyola, was ranked a three-star recruit by both Rivals.com and Scout.com. He led Loyola to consecutive Chicago Catholic League titles and a sectional title. Jordan had 9 points and 2 steals on Saturday at his father's Jordan Brand All-American Classic high school all-star game in NewYork. Though Jordan is not considered a difference-maker, his arrival should help Weber, who has taken heavy criticism for failing to sign and retain top scholarship recruits. Having Michael Jordan watching games at Assembly Hall will give the programincreased exposure. "Any time the greatest basketball player on the planet sends his son to a school, it's a shot in the arm for the head coach and the program," said Dave Telep, a national recruiting analyst for Scout.com. "If Illinois is good enough for Michael Jordan 's son, that sends a message to the rest of the guys around the country." Jordan joins scholarship recruits Demetri McCamey, QuintonWatkins, Bill Cole, Mike Tisdale and Rodney Alexander in Illinois' incoming class. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 He's retired now, but lots of folk still want to be like Mike. That's basketball icon Michael Jordan . Nike has just put out the 23rd edition of the former No23's Air Jordan shoes, and decided a shoe exhibition and party were in order at the swoosh label's Silvercord megastore. An all-star list of hipsters checked in on Friday to check out some 230 pairs of special editions and rare MJ-autographed sneakers and jerseys. Some folk were obviously b-ball fans, like Shawn Yue Man-lok, Pakho Chau Pak-ho and former Taiwanese national team player-turned-TV host Blackie Chen Chien-chou. "I have more than a thousand pairs of basketball shoes at home, and all my classic Air Jordans are on display here," Chen said proudly. "If I hadn't hurt my knee, I would've definitely been a basketball player instead of being in showbiz. Honestly, I'd rather be a 'baller than an artist." Other less in-the-hoops partygoers were merely there for the party. "Nope, I don't have a single pair of Air Jordans," said singer Stephanie Cheng Yung. "What drew me here today was, umm, because of the brand, and this collection. I do wear basketball shoes when I dance because it better protects the ankles, and I wear them to match different outfits." The exhibition continues until March 2. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 HUGH Greenwood has been leading a surreal life. Last week he was playing basketball at New York's Madison Square Garden in front of thousands of spectators and yesterday he was shooting hoops at Warrane Stadium in front of a couple of journalists and a floor cleaner. The 16-year-old is back home in Hobart to play for the AIS against the Hobart Chargers in the SEABL tomorrow night. Over the past few weeks Greenwood, the youngest member of the AIS basketball scholarship program, debuted for the AIS, won a bronze medal with the Australian Emus at the unofficial under-19s world championships in Germany and last week was in New York for the Michael Jordan Nike Camp. The camp is held annually by Chicago Bulls' NBA legend Michael Jordan for the best 16-year-old basketballers in the world, with Greenwood the only Australian invited. The whole New York experience left Greenwood in awe. ``We were picked up from the airport by stretch limo and stayed at the Grand Hyatt,'' he said. ``We got to meet Michael Jordan and have our photo taken with him which was so amazing because he is like a god.'' The culmination of the four-day camp was to play a match at Madison Square Garden -- arguably the world's most famous arena which seats 20,000 spectators. ``I was so nervous before the game because there was so many people there and they did it like an NBA game and we all had to run on to the court from backstage as we were introduced,'' he said. Greenwood also had the chance to impress during game time because the coach struggled to communicate with the other international players which allowed Greenwood to play virtually the entire game, which was streamed over the internet, at the point. ``I learned so much from the whole camp, it is nice to see where you sit against all the others,'' he said. ``It just shows how far you can go in the sport.'' And it wasn't all hard work in New York either. The camp participants were showered with freebies from the major sponsor with the latest Jordan-branded Nike gear including shoes, tracksuits and bags. ``When I went over there I had 11kg worth of luggage and when I came back I had 30kg,'' he said. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 By Quinton D. Howard In the beginning, the game did not include a 3-point line. Dunking was foreign, and passing was a given. There was less flash, and fundamentals combined with a team approach seemed to always prevail. In contemporary NBA basketball , we have a league where fundamentals complement the athletic, fast-paced style of play. Power forwards are knocking down 25-footers, point guards are jumping out the gym, and the traditional center position seems to have vanished. The NBA is the most competitive league of basketball in the world, the global standard. Basketball hopefuls attempt to emulate the style and moves of contemporary NBA superstars, and currently, there is a NBA great who is rewriting the rules to the game: Stephen Curry of the Golden State Warriors has been the highlight reel of the NBA as of late. His style is paralleled by none, but is Stephen Curry 's surge good or bad for the brand of basketball ? Last year, the world witnessed a primarily "jump-shooting" team, the Golden State Warriors, win the NBA title. Thus far during this 2015-2016 NBA season, Golden State has already posted a historic 24-consecutive (single-season) winning streak led by the unique superstar of Curry. The Davidson prospect was the MVP of the regular-season last year and is a MVP favorite this season. Curry's success as a basketball phenom has some credible analyst naming him the greatest shooter of all-time. With astronomical shooting percentages from the field and beyond the arc, Curry is currently leading the NBA in points per game. It is not "what" Curry is doing that is alarming, it is "how" he is doing it. This man is knocking down highly contested, long distance shots on a routine basis. Unlike most of his shooting predecessors, Curry can create his own shot, and his handle of the basketball is remarkable. The MVP is able to dribble the ball for 7+ seconds and score 30 feet away from the basket. As amazing as his highlights are, is this a style of basketball we want to see emulated? The premise is simple: the style of play that Curry exemplifies is not the way basketball should be played. He is licensed to play this style because he has been consistent and it has brought him and the Warriors success. It's his style and it has worked for him. However, specifically when teaching the game to young players, Curry's game-tape should not be referenced. The Golden State Warriors' point guard currently (thus far this 2015-2016 NBA season) attempts 20 field goals per game with 10 of them being from the 3-point line. Any basketball traditionalist can see the concern in these figures. A point guard should not be taking 20 shots per game, and half of a player's shots should not be from beyond the arc. The Warriors license the MVP to shoot any shot he desires, but it is not good basketball to be shooting 3-pointers on 2-1, 3-2 fast breaks, as Curry often does. It is not good basketball to be shooting contested 30-footers whenever you want. Stephen Curry is an amazing player and he embodies the entertainment aspect of the NBA, but the lunacy that clouds his game should not be mimicked. Young players should aspire to be great ball handlers and shooters like Curry, but the shot-selection and sometimes over dribbling should be left to the only individual it is working for (Curry). Imagine a high school player going into a basketball tryout playing like Curry. Even if the player was knocking down shots, how could his style of play be cohesive with a high school coach's offensive scheme? Stephen Curry is a once in a lifetime type of player. Now imagine the same high school player going into a high school tryout and emulating a style like Tony Parker. The Parker-like tactics would be far more coachable and easier to integrate with an offensive scheme, especially on a high school, AAU, or college level. The surge of Stephen Curry has been nothing short of amazing and it will be interesting to see how long he can sustain this level of play. We have never seen a player like Curry. It is vital to appreciate his uniqueness but what he is doing should not serve as a blueprint for basketball supremacy. Great shooting, great handling of the basketball , and great patience are all important aspects of the game that should be learned by up and coming players. Leave the entertainment part to the entertainers getting paid to do it. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Think you could be the next Stephen Curry ? Well, here s a good way to find out sign up for the online hoops class he ll be teaching this winter. The NBA s two-time MVP is going to teach, through video, the secrets that make Stephen Curry Stephen Curry . The course, which goes live in early 2018 and is offered by MasterClass, is touted to teach such things as shooting techniques, ballhandling, ball screens, balancing and other Curryesque things that, face it, you will not be able to do as well as he does. The enrollment fee is $90 for an individual class and $180 for a year of unlimited access to classes taught by others, such as Serena Williams. Curry will emphasize mechanics and share the details of his in-season and offseason workout routines. If I think about where I was when I was 13, if I had access to this type of curriculum, I would have been a much better player faster, he told ESPN. It took me a while to learn how to practice and do the drills the right way. I want people to see how I invest in my game, how I train, what I do in my workouts and what s my mental approach. So, when the opportunity with MasterClass came about to put a class together where I can show people my unique way of playing this game, it was natural fit. The goal is for everybody to get better and enjoy basketball more. I want them to push and challenge themselves and hopefully they see the game differently. Curry plans to offer occasional question-and-answer sessions, so the class might be worth it for those alone. (Please leave the coaching to Steve Kerr, though.) I get to assess my own game and see what I could have done better like when I get into a pick-and-roll situation, or a situation where I have to make quick decisions, you can freeze at that moment and explain everything that can possibly happen with all the different outcomes and why I chose to do what I did, versus what I could have done better, Curry said. I think hearing that from my perspective should be pretty interesting to people who either watch our games, or just play basketball themselves. One skill emphatically will not be taught, however. There is no dunking chapter, he cracked, so, if you re looking for that, then this is the wrong place to go. Who are Stephen Curry 's parents Sonya and Dell Curry? Dad Dell played in the NBA from 1986 until 2002 in various teams such as the Utah Jazz, Cleveland Cavaliers, Milwaukee Bucks, and Toronto Raptors. He was also a star guard on the Charlotte Hornets for 10 seasons. Since 2009 he has been a commentator on Hornets TV broadcasts. Mom HYPERLINK https://www.the-sun.com/entertainment/3528752/what-sonya-curry-net-worth/ 00 Sonya is an educator and founder of Christian Montessori School of Lake Norman in Charlotte, HYPERLINK https://www.the-sun.com/where/north-carolina/ 00 North Carolina . The pair met at Virginia Tech University while she was doing a recruiting visit to the school. Curry and Dell married in 1988 while he was playing in the HYPERLINK https://www.the-sun.com/topic/nba/page/21/ 00 National HYPERLINK https://www.the-sun.com/topic/nba/page/21/ 00 Basketball HYPERLINK https://www.the-sun.com/topic/nba/page/21/ 00 Association. Why are they getting divorced? The couple had been married for over three decades before announcing their split. Sonya and Dell released a statement to HYPERLINK https://people.com/sports/sonya-curry-dell-curry-to-divorce-after-33-years-of-marriage/ 00 People about their decision to file for divorce, saying: "After exploring a trial separation over the past year and much thoughtful consideration, we have decided to end our marriage. "As this comes with a great deal of sadness, our focus and desire is for our family's continued happiness. "We are so thankful for all the many blessings and successes! "We stay committed to and supportive of our children and grandchildren and will remain on connected paths. Adding: "We ask that our privacy be respected and prayer for our family as we move forward." Do they have any other kids? The couple have three children including HYPERLINK https://www.the-sun.com/who/stephen-curry/ 00 Stephen . His brother Seth is also a basketball player, while their sister Sydel is a former college volleyball player. Aside from their three children, the couple also share five grandchildren: Riley, Ryan, Canon, Carter, and a baby boy due in November 2021. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 In early August, NBA superstar Stephen Curry renewed his contract with the Golden State Warriors for a four-year, $215 million contract. But these days, this economic well-being is crossed by the family drama starring his father Dell Curry and his mother Sonya who accuse each other of committing infidelities during their marriage. Dell seems to have things clear because according to TMZ reported, he put name and surname to the lover: Steven Johnson, an athlete at the University of Virginia in the mid-1980s, the exact time when the couple Curry attended the same school. In turn, the former Charlotte Hornets player in the 1990s claimed that Sonya was living with Johnson in Tennessee and for that reason should not be entitled to alimony . As for Sonya, she also pointed out that her ex-husband, who played 16 years in the NBA between 1986 and 2002, had been unfaithful repeatedly during their marriage. Anyway, who is the mother of Stephen, Seth and Sydel Curry, accepted to be living a romantic relationship but that began after legally separated in March 2020. Dell and Sonya met at the University of Virginia known as Virginia Tech. There, the former NBA star was starting his basketball career while Sonya was doing the same in volleyball. At the same time, Steven Johnson was playing football and then joined the New England Patriots in 1988. Today, Steven is the president of Johnson Commercial Development, one of the largest commercial developers in the southeastern United States and, according to the Courier Herald, a major donor to the University of Virginia. In the midst of this conflict, Stephen expressed his family ties on Tuesday the 24th by dedicating a tender birthday message to his younger brother, Seth, who plays for the Philadelphia 76ers. "Happy birthday to my boy. He's one of them. it's amazing to see the way you do it for you and yours! I love you bro," the Golden State Warriors 30 wrote. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 by Trinity Bland and Shalika Oza November 9, 2021 Each era has its own face - a player who dominates not just in games but in how the game is played and perceived. In more ways than one, Golden State Warriors point guard Stephen Curry forced the game of professional basketball to change. Curry was an integral part of the league's culture shift, whether it was carrying himself on the court as a decent human being or making the Charles Barkleys who believed you couldn't win trying to hit 3-point shots look like clowns. When Curry made his NBA debut in October 2009, he entered with pressure being the son of former NBA player Dell Curry. The Warriors hadn't done well in years and were not contenders of any sort. The shift began when head coach Steve Kerr joined the organization. As a rookie coach, Kerr was the first to start his career with a 19-2 record. But the ball didn't stop rolling there as Kerr and Curry continued to soar. The day after Curry set the NBA record for most points in a single overtime, he became the first player in league history to be a unanimous MVP. Curry averaged 30.1 points, 6.7 assists and 5.4 rebounds in 2015 to 2016 while shooting 50.4 percent from the field, 45.4 percent from 3-point range and 90.8 percent from the charity stripe. He was the best player on the winningest team in NBA history, as the Warriors finished the regular season 73-9. It's the greatest individual season by a guard in league history. Across his 11 seasons in the league, he has attempted 5,739 3-pointers, converting them at a 43.5-percent clip. That would be a superb percentage in any one season, and even more so when maintained across an entire career. However, it's also the best 3-point percentage of any player in NBA history that has attempted at least 1,652 treys. The only players that have been more accurate from 3-point range than Curry across their careers attempted 4,000 fewer threes than he has. The Warriors' 2015 finals win cemented the start of the Golden State and Cleveland Cavaliers rivalry. The rivalry thrived so much that there is an entire Wikipedia article dedicated to the iconic 2015 to 2018 stretch. It should be recognized that this rivalry was because of NBA superstar LeBron James' "GOAT" status at the time. Having gone back to Cleveland in the 2014 offseason, the chatter around the league was the team's guaranteed spot in the finals. James' return to the team started off rocky, he proved the chatter right, bringing the Cavs to the 2015 finals. However, it wasn't going to be an easy route for James and it wasn't going to be easy for the Warriors either. On the way to winning the championship in 2014 to 2015, the Warriors had to go through quite the collection of individual studs. To win the title, Golden State were pioneers and defeated the four All-Stars who joined Curry on the All-NBA First-Team. In the first round, the Warriors swept Anthony Davis and the New Orleans Pelicans in four games but required six games to come from behind and defeat Marc Gasol and the Memphis Grizzlies in the second round. Then in the conference finals, Curry and Golden State made quick work of James Harden and the Houston Rockets in five games before vanquishing LeBron James and the Cleveland Cavaliers in six games to win the title. Curry stripped James of his "GOAT" status in a historic win that proved how the three-point is highly effective in winning, not just a circus shot meant for the audience. Curry also proved you can have fun on the court and still win the game. Anyone can tell James is laser-focused each game and fans who have watched him play for even just one season can tell he is strategic in the way he plays, a lot like many league legends. However, James doesn't look like he's having fun, which in turn, is debatably not fun for fans to experience. With Curry, there is no debate. He looks like he's actually having fun playing the game, which should be at the core of any player's mentality going into any game. There's a carelessness that comes with his playstyle that makes anything he does look easy. Whether Golden State wins the game or loses, Curry starts each game with an attitude of fun and passion. This makes all the difference for the rest of his teammates but it increases fan engagement and grabs the attention of other players to step up their game. We're looking at you, LeBron. And it's not just us who think Curry is the face of the league right now. James referred to Curry as the greatest shooter of all time. But we don't need James to tell us how Curry changed the game. The proof is in the numbers too. In the 2009-10 season, the league was averaging 18.1 3-point attempts per game. In the seasons since, that number has almost doubled. The league is currently averaging 35.9 3-point attempts per game. That number has grown exponentially since Curry entered the league - and not the slow and steady pace it was

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 What seemed like an unattainable mark is about to be smashed. Golden State Warriors guard Stephen Curry is only 10 three-pointers away from surpassing the historic Ray Allen as the NBA's all-time leading three-point scorer. Allen, in his 19-season career in the world's best basketball , made 2973 three-pointers, and Curry, 2964 so far. Curry led his team to victory Wednesday over the Portland Trail Blazers , scoring 22 points on 6-of-17 three-pointers. Read more: Bulls' Derrick Jones Jr. added to COVID-19 infected list The next game for the Warriors, will be on Saturday, December 11, when they visit the Philadelphia 76ers , being able to tie in case of scoring 9 three-pointers, or surpass Ray Allen in case of scoring 10. He can do it against Sixers Stephen Curry is already ranked as the best shooter of all time, and one of the reasons is that he has dazzled with his impressive shots from all angles, shapes and distances. The feat of becoming the greatest three-pointer of all time is within Curry's reach, as according to ESPN Stats & Info , the Warriors sharpshooter has 22 games with 10 three-pointers made. To put this in perspective, his teammate Klay Thompson , is the second in this aspect, with 5 games with 10 or more three-pointers. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Stephen Curry became the highest three-point scorer in NBA history. He did it tonight in the duel between the Golden State Warriors and the New York Knicks. After a couple of games where the 'Chef' had failed to break the record, it was until tonight, in the legendary Madison Square Garden, where he finally did. It was in the first minutes of the first quarter when Curry made his first three-pointer of the game, tying Ray Allen's record. But a couple of minutes later, he broke away from the paint and went to the outside of the perimeter, from where he connected the three-pointer that made history. Stephen Curry and the emotional celebration after his NBA history-making three-pointer Just after scoring the shot that made him the highest three-pointer in NBA history, Stephen Curry celebrated in a more than euphoric way. After scoring the historic triple, the 'Chef' took off all the pressure he had carried in recent days and which was noticeable on the court, especially in the last two games. The game was stopped to pay tribute to him properly, even though he was in rival territory. The fans surrendered to his feet, while he was congratulated by all his teammates. One of the most emotional moments was when Curry received the ball with which he scored the three-pointer and gave it directly to his father, Dell Curry. Another important moment of the night was when the '30 of the Golden State Warriors melted in a big hug with Ray Allen, who did the same a few years ago with Reggie Miller. The game resumed in New York, but after a timeout, Curry went to the bench and on the way he took the opportunity to embrace in a very emotional way with his mother, Sonya Curry. Stephen Curry , the best three-point shooter in NBA history Can Stephen Curry be considered the best three-point shooter in NBA history? As of tonight, there is no longer any doubt. While it took Ray Allen 18 seasons to reach 2,973 three-pointers, Curry broke that record in his 13th NBA season. That is, the 'Chef' needed five seasons less at the highest level of world basketball to impose its new record, which he got tonight. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Iman Shumpert made headlines in November this year when he was crowned champion of the renowned American TV show Dancing with the Stars, a programme that brings together great dancers with well-known figures from sport and entertainment. The man who won an NBA title with the Cleveland Cavaliers in 2016 has been without a team since last season when he played just two games for the Brooklyn Nets. This week he was the special guest on The Bootleg Kev Show podcast where he was lapidary on two of the best basketball players of the last decade. The 31-year-old American charged in particular against his former teammate, LeBron James, with whom he was champion of the league, whom he first attacked for leaving the Cleveland franchise in 2010: "The one who ruined basketball was not Kevin Durant with his signing with the Warriors, it was LeBron James before. It was LeBron when he went to play for the Miami Heat. And he knows it, Bron knows he ruined basketball . Personally, I loved the NBA because of the loyalty that was there, the loyalty that I thought was still there. But LeBron basically ended that, broke that code. It wasn't what he was supposed to do." That departure of The King generated a stir in the United States as it was understood as a betrayal of a team of which he was an idol for leaving to one in which he had much more likely to be crowned champion, as he did twice (2012 and 2013). Some time later, in 2014, he returned to the Cavaliers and in 2016 he was able to win the first ring in the history of the franchise to win back the love of his fans in Cleveland. But this wasn't the only thing that bothered Shumpert. The shooting guard is annoyed that LeBron exaggerates fouls to get the judges to call them: "The worst thing with him was that, we were asking him why he was flopping so much. And he told us this, literally: 'If other people make the free throws they make, I have to do it too. I get hit a lot, and I have to sell it to the refs because I'm so strong that if I don't do it, it looks like nothing happened. If I don't do that, nobody knows about the fouls I'm getting. Shumpert was a member of the Cavaliers team that defeated the Golden State Warriors, the franchise that featured Stephen Curry , the NBA's all-time leading scorer of three-pointers, in the 2015/16 season finals. Precisely, that ability for long shots of his opponent, also unleashed his fury: "We had two very different styles of basketball . And we knew that whoever could impose theirs was going to set the trend in all of basketball . That people would imitate whoever came out on top in our duels. We knew that kids were going to want to play the post, shoot fade-aways... or they were going to shoot from long range, to think it's okay to shoot that shit from almost the middle of the court". The conclusion of the guard then was blunt: "They (the Warriors) say they changed basketball , for me they ruined it. At least the basketball that I like." fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 "When I shoot nothing goes through my head, it's something that flows alone," confessed the Warriors point guard MADRID, 23 (EUROPA PRESS) The Golden State Warriors point guard Stephen Curry has celebrated his record of three-pointers (2,973) in the NBA held by Ray Allen since 2014, and established last December 14, 2021 in the emblematic Madison Square Garden in New York against the Knicks with the launch of a digital replicas of his Under Armour sneakers. Considered the best shooter in the American league, Curry already holds the record of 402 three-pointers scored in a single season (2015-16), and surpassed Allen's record total of three-pointers between official and friendly games in November. This season alone he's averaged five three-pointers per game, and he's managed to surpass Allen's regular-season record by more than 500 games to his credit, claiming it in just 786 games, as opposed to Allen's 1,300. For Curry, the shot that has transformed professional basketball in his era has become a mere extension of his talent. "When I shoot, literally nothing goes through my head that has anything to do with shooting or mechanics or anything like that. It's something that just flows naturally from the moment I'm dribbling, or I get a pass, to the moment I get the ball in my hand and I just know I'm going to shoot," he said. Behind this historic moment is a lifetime's work. Since he was a kid, Curry worked hard to hone his skills out of the zone. "Ever since I was a little kid, I was good at shooting. It was something that just came to me. I had a touch and an intuitive feel for the basket, so I just kept expanding my range. By the time I was nine or ten years old, I was standing at the three-point line in high school," he recalled. His favorite player at the time was Indiana Pacers guard Reggie Miller, a Hall of Famer who held the three-point record until it was broken by Ray Allen in 2011. "I watched it again recently, almost to feel the excitement and the energy that was in that arena. He catches the ball on the right wing. He bounces it hard and you can see him, he reacts a little bit, he's just as electrified as I am, but you can tell right away, as soon as the ball goes in, he knows exactly what three-pointer it is. He knew what that moment meant, and you can see it practically oozes out of his pores," he said. The crowd gave Stephen Curry a standing ovation as he embraced his teammates and family and took a second to let the record become a reality. In an emotional moment, Curry brought the ball over to his father, Dell Curry, a former NBA star in his own right. CURRY 9 FLOW Some 2,200 three-pointers have been made by Curry since he began his partnership with sports brand Under Armour, a collaboration that, among other things, has produced some of the most technically innovative basketball shoes of all time. Built around the revolutionary UA Flow technology, the Curry Flow 9 basketball shoe gives Curry a lightweight, traction-packed feel on the court, allowing him to sprint, stop, cut and jump more effectively and comfortably than his opponents. In addition to in-game, the Curry Flow shoe has actually improved Stephen's post-game performance as well. A lightweight, low impact shoe translates into less muscle wear and tear, ensuring faster recovery time. UA IMPACT SCHOLARSHIPS When you break a record like this, it's more than just a number. It's a reason to celebrate, which is why Under Armour has awarded three Impact Scholarships. One runs federation fees for 45 youth through a partnership with Every Kid Sports, another funds basketball development programs in each of the NBA's 30 cities in partnership with Good Sports, and the third provides 15 coach training workshops in NBA cities through Positive Coaching Alliance through the end of the 2021-22 season. Following the record-breaking event, Under Armour will continue the celebrations with basketball court renovations in three cities: Charlotte, North Carolina, where Curry grew up, Oakland, California, which has been his home for his entire professional career, and New York, where the record was officially broken. "We couldn't be more proud to support Stephen on this journey than we have been for the past nine years. No one in this sport works harder or more determined. Stephen is an inspiration to his fans, his teammates and all of us at Under Armour, and we look forward to continuing to work together to truly change the game," said Sean Eggert, senior vice president of Global Sports Marketing for Under Armour. FIRST DIGITAL GOOD To commemorate Stephen Curry 's achievement, Under Armour will release its first digital good in the form of 2.974 NFT, or non-fungible tokens, which are digital replicas of the game shoe Curry wore when he broke the record: the Genesis Curry Flow. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Christmas Day is a working day in basketball , with the NBA running its traditional slate of marquee games. This year the featured contest was the Golden State Warriors vs. the Phoenix Suns, the top two teams this season. The Warriors prevailed despite having three of their best scorers out. Their star, Stephen Curry , led them to first place in the NBA with 33 points, including five three-pointers. His Christmas Day total brought him to 2,999 career three-pointers, adding to the all-time record he set 10 days before. Most remarkably, Curry broke the record in more than 500 fewer games than Ray Allen, who held it before. Curry is the best shooter in the game's history. He has changed how basketball is played. Everyone agrees on that. I am in a minority in thinking that the change is for the worse. The background. Baskets made in the usual course of play count for two points. Baskets made from the free throw line when play is stopped count for one point. In 1979, the NBA introduced a three-point line, approximately 24 feet from the basket. (Other leagues had introduced the innovation in the 1960s.) A shot made from behind the line counts for three points instead of two. The 1979 season was the rookie year of Larry Bird of the Boston Celtics and Magic Johnson of the Los Angeles Lakers. How has the game changed? Bird, one of the best shooters ever, attempted about three three-point shots/game. Curry has averaged about ten/game in his career. This season, Curry has, on average, made 5.4 of his 13.5 three-point attempts/game. Curry makes more three-pointers in an average game than Bird even attempted. Indeed, Curry makes more three-pointers than both teams, on average, would attempt twenty-five years ago. Consider Michael Jordan, the consensus best player in NBA history, winner of six championships in eight seasons, including a two-year sabbatical when he did not play basketball . In his rookie season, he was 9-for-52 from three-point range and never shot better than 20 per cent from three-point range in his first four seasons. But the game was slowly changing in the 1990s, and by the end of his career Jordan had improved his three-point shooting to better than 35 per cent, a very impressive average from that distance. The master of the slam dunk learned to shoot from distance as well. Raymond J. de Souza: David Stern, Magic, Larry Bird and Toronto's victory( HYPERLINK https://fatherdesouza.com/articles/2020/1/10/david-stern-magic-larry-bird-and-torontos-victory 00 https://fatherdesouza.com/articles/2020/1/10/david-stern-magic-larry-bird-and-torontos-victory ) Raymond J. de Souza: A d\'e9j\'e0 vu Super Bowl for the history books( HYPERLINK https://nationalpost.com/opinion/raymond-j-de-souza-a-deja-vu-super-bowl-for-the-history-books 00 https://nationalpost.com/opinion/raymond-j-de-souza-a-deja-vu-super-bowl-for-the-history-books ) By the time Curry's NBA career began in 2009, the three-point revolution was ready. In 2010, already a fifth (22 per cent) of all shots taken were from behind the three-point line. In 2020, that percentage had risen to almost 40 per cent, or two-fifths of all shots. In 2015, six teams took more than one-third of their shots from three-point range; in 2020, it was 28 out of 30. Not only are players shooting more, like Curry they are getting better at it. While the percentage of two-point shots made has remained rather steady over time (just under 50 per cent), players have improved their three-point shooting over the years. It is now better than a third of attempts made (about 35 per cent). Curry turbo-charged the change. The thrilling contests of the 1980s, with the Celtics' half court offence and punishing defence going up against the Lakers' "showtime" fast break style, are no longer. Clashing styles of play have slowly disappeared. Instead, all teams now spread out as much as possible in order to pass the ball backward, outside the three-point line. It's a largely different game. Most fans seem to think it's better. I don't. I don't dispute that watching Curry is a treat, as excellence in any field is marvellous to behold. But the premise of the three-point shot, namely that a long distance shot should count for more than the effort and skill needed to get closer to the basket, is flawed. It would be like making a home run count for two runs instead of one. Or if a line was painted on the ice between the blue line and the face-off circles and goals scored from there counted for more. Or that a touchdown in football was worth nine points instead of six if it was scored on a play from behind the 25-yard line. Imagine if football offences deliberately moved the ball backward in order to attempt the nine-point touchdown. It happens in basketball all the time. I don't deny that the long distance shot has a certain excitement. So, too, do any number of other plays which are not favoured by an extra point.

(AP Photo/Ron Schwane) Body_121 Body_121 Body fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Team LeBron wins in namesake's homecoming CLEVELAND - Stephen Curry got another 3-point record. LeBron James got another All-Star win. Not a bad night in Ohio for the two All-Stars from Akron. Curry turned boos to oohs and aahs with the greatest long-distance shooting performance in All-Star Game history, then James made a turnaround jumper that gave Team LeBron a 163-160 victory over Team Durant on Sunday night. Curry made 16 3-pointers and scored 50 points, two off Anthony Davis' record. He was clearly hunting it, asking on the sideline during a sizzling third quarter how many points it would take. "It's pretty special, obviously being back in Ohio," Curry said, seconds after being handed the inaugural Kobe Bryant MVP Trophy. "The trophy is very special. Very humble, very blessed." On a night both players were among the greats of the game who were honored during a halftime ceremony celebrating the NBA's 75th anniversary team, Curry and James showed why they continue to stand out among today's best. Curry missed his final 3-point attempt that would have allowed him to surpass Davis. But with James' team needing a basket to reach the target score of 163 points, they couldn't afford to keep feeding Curry. So James pulled up from deep on the right side for the winning bucket, making him 5-0 in the format where the leading vote-getters in each conference draft teams. Giannis Antetokounmpo had 30 points, and James finished with 24 in his old home. Joel Embiid led Team Durant with 36 points. The All-Star Game's return to Cleveland was expected to be James' night. He is the Cavaliers franchise's greatest player, having led them to their only NBA championship in 2016. Instead, he was upstaged by Curry, who was born in the same Akron hospital. Curry finished 16 of 27 beyond the arc. He launched a few from the All-Star 2022 logo near midcourt, running backward without even waiting to watch one go in. He had been booed all weekend, smiling through the sounds during pregame introductions as Cleveland fans made clear they hadn't forgiven him for helping Golden State beat the Cavaliers three times in the NBA Finals from 2015-18. But the fans were enjoying his show too much to keep going as the game went on, though the boos returned when he was presented the MVP award. It was a thrilling finish to an All-Star Game played again befo

fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Sent to SAN FRANCISCO - Children and adults, and even some who didn't follow basketball before, love the show that Stephen Curry puts on every night on the court. And how could it not be a pleasure to see the "little guy" of 1.88 meters tall move all over the court and try unimaginable shots or from any corner of the court. Fortunately, the NBA Finals have the greatest shooter of all time back, who has already started to warm up in the series against Boston for his fourth championship ring. At 34 years of age, Stephen Curry continues to delight with his spectacular long-distance shooting. He has now made 12 three-pointers in two games, and on Sunday he reached 14 Finals duels with at least five three-pointers made. "You look back and see the work we've put in as a team over the last two years and it's paid off (injuries to several players including Steph). It's a pretty special Finals for me, and more so after what's happened since Game 6 of the 2019 Finals (when they fell 4-2 to Toronto)," the guard noted. Curry is in his 13th season in the League and has 20,644 lifetime points in the regular season, and to give us an idea of what his three-point shot means, Stephen has attempted more three-point shots (7,290) than two-point shots (7,230) in that span. Of the 20,644 he boasts, 9,351 points came from three-point shots, almost 47 percent of what he has scored in a regular season since he arrived in the NBA for 2009-10. Being so elusive and handling the ball superbly doesn't help much in defending him, and the current Celtics know that. "He's a player that in small spaces can create shots, we know that, he's hard to defend," said Dominican Al Horford. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 UConn women s basketball guard Azzi Fudd helped design the sneakers Stephen Curry wore in Game 3 of the NBA Finals. In a 116-100 loss to the Boston Celtics at TD Garden, Curry wore a custom pair of Curry 4 Flotros painted in blue, pink and yellow with a gender symbol and the phrase #RetireInequality. The heel of the shoe also featured the saying We re not done yet. The sneakers were made in a collaboration between Fudd, Curry, financial services company TIAA, DEZ Customz Art and Customs and custom sneaker artist Brad Hall. Fudd signed with Curry and his brand SC30 Inc. in December in a first of its kind NIL deal that involves both mentorship and sponsorship. The pair met at his camp in 2018 and have built a relationship since then. With the 50th anniversary of Title IX coming up later this month, TIAA has worked with athletes across several professional leagues, including Las Vegas Aces star A Ja Wilson, to raise awareness on the gender pay gap with a specific focus on how that affects retirement. According to the Retire Inequality website, women still retire with 30% less income than men. Fudd tweeted about the initiative in March. A couple pairs of the shoes will be made available to purchase on June 23 through the NTWRK app. All of the proceeds will go to the Women s Sports Foundation. \'a92022 Hartford Courant. (AP Photo/Steven Senne) Body_145 Body_145 Body fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Golden State, of course, favored to win title in 2023 The goal was enormous: Return to the NBA's mountaintop. And now with that monumental task complete, the NBA champion Golden State Warriors already have a new target: staying up there for a while. The victory cigars hadn't been extinguished after the title-clinching win in Boston, the last celebratory bottles of Mo\'ebt & Chandon hadn't been emptied, and the topic - can the Warriors win it again next season? - was already coming up. They have been installed by FanDuel Sportsbook as the favorites for the 2023 NBA title, and with Finals MVP Stephen Curry , Klay Thompson and Draymond Green all back it would be foolish to think that their chance at a fifth championship in nine years isn't very real. "It still has not been proven, that when we're whole, that anybody can stop it," Green said. That's true, and that's why it makes sense for the Warriors to carry the burden of being favorites into next season. They know what it takes; they have as many championships in the last eight seasons - four - as the rest of the league combined. The last run that was better than this was put together by Michael Jordan and the Chicago Bulls, winning six in an eight-year span of the 1990's. Warriors coach Steve Kerr - now a nine-time champion - played for the Bulls for some of that run, and has led Golden State to all four of these titles. "They are all unique. They are all special," Kerr said. "I think this one may have been the most unlikely just from the standpoint of where we've been the last couple years. A lot of unknowns." Indeed, there were questions. The Warriors answered them all. No, the core wasn't too old. Yes, Thompson would come back from more than 900 days on the sidelines with injuries. And absolutely, Curry can still be unstoppable in the biggest moments. They took that core and fortified it with a new group of talent. Among those on that list: 27-year-old Andrew Wiggins, the 2014 No. 1 pick who has come into his own and was nothing less than a star in the NBA Finals; Jordan Poole, who turns 23 this weekend and will celebrate as a champion who has blossomed after facing off against Curry in practice all the time; and Jonathan Kuminga, the 19-year-old who got into 86 games and is raved about by teammates. "And we ain't done," Thompson said early Friday while appearing on Green's podcast, The Draymond Green Show. "That's the beautiful thing about it. We got these young bucks behind us and we got the same squad coming back? That's scary for the NBA." Green concurred. "It is very scary," he said. They earned that championship moment, after going through an NBA Finals loss in 2019 to Toronto and then two seasons with a combined 54-83 record, a million miles away from being the team that the Warriors demand they be. The Warriors had the NBA's worst record in 2019-20 in large part because of roster turnover. The Warriors lost in the play-in tournament last sason, but seeds were planted for something great. "You don't want to see us next year," Curry said when the 2020-21 season ended. Prophetic words. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Soccer and basketball stars Neymar and Stephen Curry exchanged messages on social media on Monday after the Brazilian star celebrated a goal for Paris Saint-Germain the same way the American celebrated his points in the NBA last season. Stephen Curry commented on Twitter on a post that showed a photo of Neymar doing the celebration known as "night night", in which the player puts his hands together and makes the gesture of who is sleeping. "I'm watching, Neymar," wrote the Golden State Warriors star. Neymar replied to the phrase posted by Stephen Curry with the name of the celebration. "Night night, star," published the PSG and Brazilian national team player. Neymar and Curry have been friends for a long time, since the time the forward wore the jersey of Barcelona. The Brazilian has attended Golden State Warriors games, just as the basketball star went to Paris to watch a PSG game. The two have shared interviews and had several special encounters as well. Last Saturday, Neymar scored two goals in PSG's 5-2 win over Montpellier in Ligue 1. The Brazilian plays again on Sunday, at 3:45 pm, against Lille. The post Neymar and Curry interact on networks after goal celebration: 'Night night, star' appeared first on ISTO\'c9 Independente. Curry has a new children's book "I Have a Superpower." (Photo by Jordan Strauss/Invision/AP, File) This image released by Penguin Random House shows "I Have a Superpower" by Stephen Curry and illustrated by Geneva Bowers. (Penguin Random House via AP) Golden State Warriors Stephen Curry tosses his cap in the air after his graduation ceremony at Davidson College on Wednesday, Aug. 31, 2022, in Davidson, N.C. (AP Photo/Chris Carlson) Body_67 Body_67 Body fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 NEW YORK - On nearly every basketball court around the world -from NBA arenas to elementary school gyms - you'll see players of all sizes regularly attempting long 3-point shots. There's one man largely credited with transforming basketball from a must-see above the rim game to box office-long range shooting: Stephen Curry . But the Golden State Warriors megastar who broke the NBA's all-time 3-point record last season is now aiming at a different target: children's books. "This is our first project out and (we want) to hopefully reach the next generation of kids that are dreaming big, are going to be the next leaders and game changers," said Curry of "I Have a Superpower" illustrated by Geneva Bowers. "We want them to be able to set their sights on what they want to accomplish in the world and really believe that it's possible." The father of three says the book was partly inspired by his own children's love of reading. "You kind of have your kids in mind when you're talking about a book, and then you realize that this is kind of a universal opportunity to reach the next generation," explained the four-time NBA champion and reigning Finals MVP. A member of the NBA's prestigious 75th Anniversary team and executive producer of the "Holey Moley" game show, Curry spoke with The Associated Press about empowering children and the upcoming NBA season. Answers have been edited for clarity and brevity. What do you want young people to learn from "I Have a Superpower?" We're all unique and we all have something to offer the world. And if you have a work ethic, if you have a belief and you feel like whatever that requires is already inside of you, then good things will happen. What would you tell parents who might say it's easy for you to tell kids "just work hard?" You can always go back to when I started playing basketball when I was 5 years old - I first started playing organized basketball at 9. I was undersized, scrawny and never, ever talked about as being the greatest at anything. And (I) had to go through those experiences of trying and failing I think that's something that most people will hopefully be able to learn from, connect with and be inspired by. You and your wife, Ayesha, are building a media brand with TV shows and books. Why choose the media space to make an impact? There are a lot of different mediums to story-tell and to inspire. And publishing and writing this book is another example of ways to connect and kind of broaden your horizons my wife showed me how it's done, so now I got to follow in her footsteps a little bit. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Giannis Antetokounmpo surprised NBA fans by declaring that, for him, Stephen Curry is the best basketball player in the world today. The Greek, who twice won the league's Most Valuable Player (MVP) award and was recently chosen as the No. 1 for the 2022-2023 season by ESPN, assured that, to be in that position, an athlete has to take his team to the last instance of the tournament in which he competes. "The best player in the world is the last person standing. He's the person who takes his team to the Finals, to the finish line, and helps them win that game. That's the way I look at it, and I think Stephen Curry is the best player in the world," he said. Antetokounmpo and his jealousy for Curry's Warriors Similarly, the center confessed that he felt jealous to see the Warriors players (reigning champions) at the ESPY Awards. "I was jealous to see Golden State at the parade and the ESPY Awards. Now I know that feeling. You know they're taking something away from you that was yours," the Milwaukee Bucks basketball player expressed during NBA Media Day. Antetokounmpo finished third in the voting for the MVP of the U.S. competition last season 2021-2022, in which he averaged 29.9 points, 11.6 rebounds and 5.8 assists. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Meridian Editor Stephen Curry is undoubtedly one of the best basketball players in history, a sure member of the Hall of Fame and is currently considered by many the best player in the world, while many fans and basketball coaches approve this theory, there is another group that does not, however; the Golden State Warriors coach, Steve Kerr, considers the "Chef" is the modern Michael Jordan. Read also: NBA: Stephen Curry 's Warriors far from their championship version After the great performance of the Warriors star point guard in the victory over the Washington Wizards, the praise was not long in coming back from injury and showing an impressive game with ease seems to be something you don't see every day, only players like LeBron James, Kevin Durant and Curry make it very easy. But for Kerr there is no other player more impactful than Curry, his style of play resembles the greatness of the legend of the Chicago Bulls, "causes emotion in people and has inspiring qualities," admitted the coach of the reigning champions. Read also: NBA: Draymond Green could be close to leaving the Warriors Curry has had a regular season due to the injury he suffered in his left shoulder, and despite his great numbers he has not been able to lead the team 100% to place at the top of the Western Conference standings, however, he has done a great job in his game and has tried to improve the balance of his franchise, meanwhile, he remains Steve Kerr's best card for each game. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Meridiano Newsroom As is customary every time an NBA team is champion they usually visit the White House, this time, the Golden State Warriors received an invitation from the U.S. government to visit the White House after their championship last season. Read also: NBA: Steve Kerr puts Stephen Curry on par with Michael Jordan The team attended to take some pictures and share with the members of the said government, remembering that in the previous occasions the San Francisco organization did not attend after achieving their championships due to the mandate that was in the hands of Donald Trump, and that did not interest at all their star Stephen Curry . U.S. President Joe Biden described Curry as one of the best athletes today and gave a great welcome to the entire team that was victorious last season. Biden acknowledged that the Warriors had had a tough time the past two years when they did not win any trophies, but recognized the work the team is constantly doing to move forward and achieve success. Stephen Curry spoke about the arrival of Brittney Griner, the women's basketball star who was imprisoned in Russia, and thanked the government for making it possible for her to return to her country and to be present at the ceremony. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Washington, Mar. 24 - U.S. President Joe Biden appointed NBA player Stephen Curry and Ecuadorian-born American chef Jos\'e9 Garc\'e9s as members of the Sports, Fitness and Nutrition Advisory Council, the White House announced Friday. Curry and Garces are part of the group of 27 people that Biden wants to have as members of this body. The council is an advisory body to the president charged with promoting physical activity and healthy eating for all Americans, regardless of their background or abilities. Jos\'e9 Garc\'e9s is described by the White House as an entrepreneur and innovator, known for his knowledge of Spanish and Latin American cuisine, which he has promoted from his restaurants, such as Amada and Volv\'e9r. Curry will also be joined by his wife, Ayesha Curry, who is an entrepreneur, chef and writer. Other sports figures appointed to the council include former basketball player Tamika Catchings, "one of the most decorated athletes of all time," with four Olympic gold medals, a WNBA (Women's Basketball Association of America) championship and five times named Defensive Player of the Year and ten All Stars. Also on the list is Chloe Kim, the young American snowboarding prodigy who made history at the 2018 PyeongChang Olympics in South Korea by taking gold at age 17 in the halfpipe event; and Elana Meyers, who won five Olympic medals in the also bobsled winter sport. Also, there is former baseball player Ryan Howard, who spent most of his career with the Philadephia Phillies and set a milestone as the athlete who hit the fewest 100 and 200 home runs; and Chaunte Lowe, the 12-time U.S. high jump champion and four-time Olympian, who trained for her fifth Games while undergoing chemotherapy and recovering from a double mastectomy. In 2022, Biden appointed Spanish chef Jos\'e9 Andr\'e9s and basketball player Elena Delle Donne as co-chairs of the council. The Golden State Warriors player is part of the group of 27 people who make up that body, including his wife, Ayesha Curry, who is a businesswoman, chef and writer . The council is an advisory body to the president charged with promoting physical activity and healthy eating for all Americans, regardless of background or ability. The four-time NBA ring winner is not the only athlete appointed to the council. Also on the list is Chloe Kim, the young American snowboarding prodigy who made history at the 2018 PyeongChang Olympics in South Korea by taking gold at age 17 in the halfpipe event. Elana Meyers , who won five Olympic medals in the winter sport bobsled, is another of Biden's advisors. Also, there is former baseball player Ryan Howard , who spent most of his career with the Philadephia Phillies and set a milestone as the athlete who hit the fewest 100 and 200 home runs, and Chaunte Lowe, the 12-time U.S. high jump champion and four-time Olympian , who trained for her fifth Games while undergoing chemotherapy and recovering from a double mastectomy. On the other hand, Biden included in the select group the Ecuadorian-born U.S. chef Jos\'e9 Garc\'e9s, described by the White House as an entrepreneur and innovator, known for his knowledge of Spanish and Latin American gastronomy, which he has promoted from his restaurants, such as Amada and Volv\'e9r. In 2022, the U.S. president had appointed professional basketball player Elena Delle Donne , a three-time WNBA All-Star Game selection, two-time MVP and Olympic gold medalist in Rio 2016, and U.S. naturalized Spanish chef Jos\'e9 Andr\'e9s as co-chairs of the board. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Meridiano Newsroom The Western Conference semifinal series between the Golden State Warriors and the Los Angeles Lakers will be one of the most watched in recent years, as it figures to be more like an NBA Finals than a semifinal. It is almost certain that all basketball lovers will be watching this series, even basketball legends, one of them will be Magic Johnson, former Lakers player, who will undoubtedly be in the front rows to appreciate this great show. Magic spoke about what this series will be like, and without giving much favoritism as to who will be the winner, he spoke about the audience that will be watching these games. "Everyone will be tuning in to the Lakers vs Warriors series and the ratings will be through the roof," Magic said on his social network on twitter. Curry and LeBron have marked one of the greatest rivalries of all time, they faced each other in 4 consecutive finals where the "Chef" took the glory three times while James did it once with a historic comeback. Now they will return to give fans a new show, which will serve to continue to know the greatness of both NBA stars. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 It is common knowledge that LeBron James opened the door to retirement after the Los Angeles Lakers' 0-4 loss to the Denver Nuggets in the Western Conference Finals . But there is a possibility that this is a strategy by LeBron to get the Lakers to make big moves to have a winning team next season or to force a trade to none other than the Golden State Warriors, where he would play with the other ace of this basketball era, Stephen Curry . The interest between LeBron and the Warriors could be mutual . LeBron is best friends with Draymond Green and has already said that Stephen Curry is the current player he would most like to play with. Being even more precise, in a recent interview he mentioned that, if not playing for the Lakers, he would like to play for the Warriors or return to the Miami Heat, where he already won two championships. In The Shop cycle, he was full of praise for another key Warriors player like Green: "I like the way Draymond talks to the guys. I'd love to have a pissing match with Draymond. I love it when someone curses me out if I'm not doing my job. I would love that with Draymond." LeBron James is chasing a fifth ring, at 38 years old the possibilities are starting to dry up and he's starting to feel like Los Angeles isn't the place to win it. Alternatively, if LeBron goes to Golden State for the final chapter of his career, complemented by the defensive prowess of Draymond and the offensive power of Curry and Klay Thompson, not only would the chance of a fifth ring for his account increase, but also the possibility of, if only for a short time, putting on a show with few precedents. Will LeBron James and Stephen Curry have their "Last Dance" together? fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry has added another golden trophy to his collection after being named the 2022-23 Kareem Abdul-Jabbar Social Justice Champion. The NBA announced on Tuesday that the four-time champion was honored, selected by Abdul-Jabbar and a panel of activists and league executives. Curry has been a member of several initiatives encouraging people to vote, including serving as a co-chair of former First Lady Michelle Obama's "When We All Vote" and participating in the social media campaign for the National Basketball Social Justice Coalition s Freedom to Vote." The nine-time All-Star also uses his Underrated brand to empower underprivileged athletes and help create equal opportunity for women. Through the platform, he hosts basketball and golf tournaments and he pledged $6 million to revive the men's and women's golf programs at Howard University. LEGACY: HYPERLINK https://www.usatoday.com/story/opinion/columnists/2022/05/08/colin-kaepernick-kareem-abdul-jabbar/02/ 00 NBA great Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's fight for social justice has always been about sacrifice He founded the non-profit Eat.Learn.Play. with his wife, Ayesha, in 2019 with a focus on serving the community of Oakland. The organization has provided more than 25 million meals and 500,000 books to families in need and hosted 10,000 kids at summer sports camps. As an athlete, I consistently leverage my platform to amplify advocacy and address the pervasive issue of systemic racism," Curry said in a statement. "I firmly believe that we must be vocal both on social media and in real life, taking tangible actions to effect real change in our society and for generations to come." HYPERLINK https://www.instagram.com/reel/CmjzKnFhsfo/?utm_source=ig_embed&utm_campaign=loading 00 View this post on Instagram HYPERLINK https://www.instagram.com/reel/CmjzKnFhsfo/?utm_source=ig_embed&utm_campaign=loading 00 A post shared by Wardell Curry (@stephencurry30) After starting his Hall of Fame NBA career, Curry went back to school and graduated from Davidson College in September. He majored in sociology and wrote his final thesis on gender equality in sport. The NBA will make a donation of $100,000 to Curry's charity of choice, the University of San Francisco Institute for Nonviolence and Social Justice. The four runner-ups of the award each receive $25,000 to a charity of their choice. Memphis Grizzlies forward-center Jaren Jackson Jr. selected The Girls Opportunity Alliance/The Obama Foundation; San Antonio Spurs guard Tre Jones wanted to honor the Children's Bereavement Center of South Texas; Boston Celtics forward Grant Williams chose VisionC; and Phoenix Suns guard Chris Paul wanted to support the Social Change Fund United. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Rangabali The trailer of Telugu-language comedy film Rangabali came out on Tuesday. Directed by Pawan Basamsetti, the film stars Naga Shaurya, Yukti Thareja, Satya and Brahmaji. The two-minute-22-second-long trailer introduces us to an unemployed young man (Naga Shaurya). He soon falls in love with a girl (Yukti Thareja), who happens to be a doctor. The boy's father has a medical shop and he frequently wreaks havoc by giving wrong medicines to customers. The teaser also shows glimpses of fight sequences. Rangabali will hit cinemas on July 7. Five Nights At Freddy's Universal Pictures has unveiled the first trailer of its upcoming horror film Five Nights At Freddy's. Directed by Emma Tammi and produced by Jason Blum under the banner of his Blumhouse Productions, the movie stars Elizabeth Lail, Piper Rubio, Kat Conner Sterling, Mary Stuart Masterson and Matthew Lillard. In the two-minute-26-second-long trailer, we see a security guard at a video game parlour called Freddy's, which was a hotspot in the 1980s. He goes there with his young daughter. However, he soon learns that the place is haunted and that animatronic bears take in children. Five Nights At Freddy's will arrive in theatres and simultaneously be streaming on Peacock on October 27. 72 Hoorain Bollywood Hungama on Wednesday shared the official trailer of the upcoming film 72 Hoorain. Directed by Sanjay Puran Singh Chauhan, the film stars Pavan Malhotra and Ameer Bashir in lead roles. The two-minute-22-second-long trailer shows two suicide bombers who were brainwashed to believe in the legend of 72 hoorains. After executing a devastating bombing attack in Mumbai, they are both seen trapped in what seems like purgatory rather than heaven. 72 Hoorain will hit theatres on July 7. Stephen Curry : Underrated Apple TV released the official trailer of its upcoming sports documentary Stephen Curry : Underrated on Tuesday. The coming-of-age journey chronicles one of the most influential and unexpected players in the history of basketball - Stephen Curry . The one-minute-eight-second-long trailer blends archival footage and interviews that document Curry's rise from an undersized college player at a small-town Division I college to a four-time NBA champion. Stephen Curry : Underrated will be streaming on Apple TV+ from July 21. fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Stephen Curry continues to prove that golf, beyond being his main hobby after basketball , is also another sport that he dominates with solvency. A large number of American athletes are devoted to golf and its practice, and proof of this is the countless number of athletes or former athletes who play it: Michael Jordan, Stephen Curry , Gareth Bale, Patrick Mahomes, Canelo Alvarez, etc., and non-athletes such as Barack Obama, Jack Nicholson or Bill Gates . To show who is the best of them all, they come together in the most prestigious golf tournament at 'celebrity' level, the American Century Celebrity Golf Championship held at Lake Tahoe, in Nevada, but near California. There, at an altitude of 1,900 meters, they play a 54-hole event divided into several days with a par of 72 strokes . This year, the event was attended by another handful of American sports stars in addition to Curry: Patrick Mahomes, Ray Allen, Aaron Rodgers, Travis Kelce, Josh Allen, Kyle Lowry, Tony Romo, Canelo Alvarez, Dell Curry and Doug Pederson, among a total of 93 . Before 2004, the scoring in this tournament was the same as in classic and professional golf. From that edition, the way of scoring points was changed: the eagle adds 6, 8 for the hole in one, 3 for the birdie, 1 for the par, 0 for the bogey and -2 for the double bogey or worse. Therefore, Stephen Curry , with his eagle on the last hole, came from behind to beat Mardy Fish . In addition, the NBA point guard also achieved several records, such as making the first hole one in history and being the first active athlete to win the title since 2000, 'Al' del Greco. This is the first time Curry has won the trophy, but there are true cracks of the clubs that have repeated on several occasions. Rick Rhoden with eight and Dan Quinn with five stand out. Independent Digital News and Media Limited All Rights Reserved fLayoutInCell 1 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 75 82 Length: 662 words Byline: Lindsey Bahr Highlight: Stephen Curry : Underrated is a portrait of a man the greatest three-point shooter of all time who has felt inferior playing the game he loves since he was a scrawny kid playing on his local under-10 team Body_5 Body_5 Body fLayoutInCell 1 lineColor lineWidth 25400 lineStyle 0 fFlipH 0 fFlipV 0 shapeType 20 Stephen Curry : Underrated is easily one of the most inspirational movies of the year. This is a portrait of a man the greatest three-point shooter of all time who has felt inferior playing the game he loves since he was a scrawny kid playing on his local under-10 team. It s easy to roll your eyes at supermodels who say they were teased when they were kids or famous actors who look like the captain of the football team insisting they were outcasts in high school. In the rear-view mirror, when things have gone so well after that origin story, it just always rings a little false. But in this documentary, directed by Peter Nicks and streaming on HYPERLINK https://www.independent.co.uk/topic/apple 00 Apple TV+ Friday, the filmmakers put you in Curry s shoes. They show you video of him on that under-10 team, indeed looking scrawnier than his counterparts and with the posture of someone who s already self-conscious about it. They have Reggie Miller on hand to read the draft report which says Curry is far below HYPERLINK https://www.independent.co.uk/topic/nba 00 NBA standard in regard to explosiveness and athleticism, extremely short for a shooting guard position, and cautions do not rely on him to run your team. Ouch. And they close in on his face at some of the biggest moments of his career, both in college and in the NBA, to show a reaction that s neither smug nor nonchalant or overly celebratory: It s authentic astonishment. And as if to upend expectations even more, the present timeline of Stephen Curry : Underrated is not focused on a season or chasing a title though there is plenty of basketball , including the moment he breaks the three-point record. It s almost more about college deciding he wanted to play at school, finding the right school for himself and, many years and championships later, studying to finish what he started, while juggling his career, his contracts and his family. In 2009, Curry decided to leave Davidson College a year early, without graduating, to pursue professional basketball . But he made a promise his mother, Sonya, that he d go back and finish at some point. Curry and Davidson, like most healthy athlete-school agreements, seemed to choose one another at exactly the right time. When he got in, feeling good about himself, he went to tell his friends: They d never even heard of the small liberal arts college in HYPERLINK https://www.independent.co.uk/topic/north-carolina 00 North Carolina . That was a bit disappointing but what would have been even worse is if he d accidentally sabotaged the whole thing by not responding to coach Bob McKillop, who started to worry whether his recruit was being pursued by other schools when Curry went dark. From high school to college on into the pros, something that struck many about Curry is how he makes a lot of mistakes but never seems to wallow in them. When he goes up against Michigan for the first time in college, he describes how everything that could go wrong went wrong. Later, his coach said he was going to start the next game. McKillop saw in his perseverance a toughness that was rare in players. His parents, Dell and Sonya, helped shepherd that discipline but always because he wanted it not some horrifying reversal in which the parents are the drivers of something. You don t need to know much about basketball or care about Steph Curry to watch this film, though many probably will. But much like the Michael Jordan doc The Last Dance, this beautifully constructed (and much more economical) narrative operates on its own terms, with a beautiful score guiding the viewer through his life. As he says in a zen sort of way, he s just, Trying to find the space to survey my life ... Let my mind think about how I got here. Stephen Curry : Underrated, an Apple TV+ release streaming Friday, is rated PG-13 by the Motion Picture Association for brief, strong language. Running time: 110 minutes. Three stars out of four. - MPA Definition of PG-13: Parents strongly cautioned.
